## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **Fire Triangle** in detail. Discuss the role of each element in the combustion process. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A storage facility contains **liquid ethanol ($\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH}$)**. Calculate the **stoichiometric air required (in kg)** for the complete combustion of **5 kg of ethanol**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

The Fire Triangle consists of three sides — Fuel, Oxygen, and Heat. Each element plays a critical and interdependent role:

- **Fuel:** Provides the carbon and hydrogen atoms (or other combustible species) that undergo rapid oxidation. Fuels must be in vapor or pyrolyzable form. Examples: wood, gasoline, ethanol.
- **Oxygen:** Acts as the oxidizer. Atmospheric air contains 21% $\text{O}_2$. Combustion requires a minimum of ~16% $\text{O}_2$ by volume to sustain itself.
- **Heat:** The activation energy to reach the fuel's ignition temperature. Once combustion begins, the exothermic reaction becomes self-sustaining.

**Removal Strategy Mapping:**
- Remove fuel → Starvation method
- Remove oxygen → Smothering method (e.g., $\text{CO}_2$ flooding)
- Remove heat → Cooling method (e.g., water sprinkler)

A neat sketch should depict the triangle with all three sides labeled and arrows indicating the interaction.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1:** Write the molecular formula. For ethanol: $\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH}$ which can be written as $\text{C}_2\text{H}_6\text{O}$. So $x = 2$, $y = 6$, $z = 1$ (oxygen atom present).

**Step 2:** Write the balanced combustion equation:

$$
\text{C}_2\text{H}_6\text{O} + 3\,\text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\,\text{CO}_2 + 3\,\text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

**Step 3:** Verify atom balance:
- C: Left = 2, Right = 2 ✓
- H: Left = 6, Right = $3 \times 2 = 6$ ✓
- O: Left = 1 + $3 \times 2$ = 7, Right = $2 \times 2$ + 3 = 7 ✓

**Step 4:** Calculate molecular weight of ethanol:

$$
M_{\text{C}_2\text{H}_6\text{O}} = (2 \times 12) + (6 \times 1) + 16 = 46 \text{ kg/kmol}
$$

**Step 5:** Calculate moles of ethanol in 5 kg:

$$
n_{\text{ethanol}} = \frac{5}{46} = 0.1087 \text{ kmol}
$$

**Step 6:** Determine moles of air required. From the balanced equation, 1 kmol of ethanol requires 3 kmol of $\text{O}_2$.

$$
n_{\text{air}} = \frac{3}{0.21} = 14.286 \text{ kmol air per kmol ethanol}
$$

**Step 7:** Total air required for 0.1087 kmol of ethanol:

$$
n_{\text{air,total}} = 0.1087 \times 14.286 = 1.553 \text{ kmol air}
$$

**Step 8:** Convert to mass of air using $M_{\text{air}} = 28.97$ kg/kmol:

$$
m_{\text{air}} = 1.553 \times 28.97 = 44.99 \text{ kg}
$$

**Final Answer:**

$$
\boxed{\text{Stoichiometric air required} \approx 45 \text{ kg of air for complete combustion of 5 kg ethanol}}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the **Fire Tetrahedron** model. Why is the chemical chain reaction considered essential for sustained combustion? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A residential building uses **natural gas (methane, $\text{CH}_4$)** for cooking. Calculate the **mass of oxygen and air required** for the complete combustion of **2 kg of methane**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

The **Fire Tetrahedron** is an extension of the classical Fire Triangle, adding a fourth element: the **sustaining chemical chain reaction**. The four elements are:

1. **Fuel**
2. **Oxygen**
3. **Heat**
4. **Chemical Chain Reaction** (free radical propagation: H•, OH•, O•)

During combustion, fuel vapor molecules react with oxygen to produce intermediate free radicals. These radicals propagate the reaction by attacking additional fuel molecules, creating a self-sustaining cycle. If the chain reaction is interrupted (e.g., by halon or clean agents that recombine radicals), the combustion stops even if fuel, oxygen, and heat are still present. This is why halon-based systems are highly effective against gas and liquid fires.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1:** Molecular formula of methane: $\text{CH}_4$, so $x = 1$, $y = 4$.

**Step 2:** Write the balanced combustion equation:

$$
\text{CH}_4 + 2\,\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

**Step 3:** Calculate molecular weight of methane:

$$
M_{\text{CH}_4} = 12 + (4 \times 1) = 16 \text{ kg/kmol}
$$

**Step 4:** Calculate moles of methane in 2 kg:

$$
n_{\text{CH}_4} = \frac{2}{16} = 0.125 \text{ kmol}
$$

**Step 5:** Determine moles of $\text{O}_2$ required. From the balanced equation, 1 kmol of $\text{CH}_4$ requires 2 kmol of $\text{O}_2$.

$$
n_{\text{O}_2} = 0.125 \times 2 = 0.25 \text{ kmol O}_2
$$

**Step 6:** Convert to mass of $\text{O}_2$ using $M_{\text{O}_2} = 32$ kg/kmol:

$$
m_{\text{O}_2} = 0.25 \times 32 = 8 \text{ kg O}_2
$$

**Step 7:** Calculate moles of air required. Air is 21% $\text{O}_2$ by volume.

$$
n_{\text{air}} = \frac{0.25}{0.21} = 1.1905 \text{ kmol air}
$$

**Step 8:** Convert to mass of air:

$$
m_{\text{air}} = 1.1905 \times 28.97 = 34.49 \text{ kg air}
$$

**Final Answer:**

$$
\boxed{m_{\text{O}_2} = 8 \text{ kg} \quad ; \quad m_{\text{air}} = 34.49 \text{ kg for combustion of 2 kg methane}}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Deduction Areas:**
> 1. **Confusing moles with mass:** Students frequently forget to convert moles of air to mass of air. Always multiply by molecular weight (28.97 kg/kmol).
> 2. **Wrong ratio for air:** Using 0.23 (mass fraction) instead of 0.21 (volume fraction) or vice versa. Default to **0.21 by volume** unless the problem specifies mass-based.
> 3. **Skipping atom balance verification:** Always verify C, H, and O atom balance in combustion equations. The examiner awards 1 mark for verification.
> 4. **Not labeling the diagram:** A neat labeled Fire Triangle diagram fetches **2 marks** in Part B. Skipping it costs 2 marks easily.
> 5. **Mixing Flash Point and Auto-Ignition:** These are different concepts and the examiner often tests this distinction.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- The **Fire Triangle** has three elements: **Fuel, Oxygen, and Heat** — all must coexist for combustion.
- The **Fire Tetrahedron** adds the **chemical chain reaction** (free radicals) as a fourth element.
- Atmospheric oxygen is **21% by volume**; minimum for combustion is **~16%**; minimum for life safety is **19.5%**.
- **Auto-Ignition Temperature** of common fuels:
  - Wood: ~230°C
  - Gasoline: ~280°C
  - Methane: ~537°C
  - Hydrogen: ~500°C
- The **stoichiometric combustion equation** for any hydrocarbon $\text{C}_x\text{H}_y$ is:
  $$\text{C}_x\text{H}_y + \left(x + \frac{y}{4}\right)\text{O}_2 \rightarrow x\,\text{CO}_2 + \frac{y}{2}\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$$
- The **air-to-fuel molar multiplier** is **4.76** (= 1/0.21) — memorize this constant.
- **Water** as a suppression agent targets the **Heat** side (latent heat of vaporization = 2260 kJ/kg).
- **$\text{CO}_2$** targets the **Oxygen** side (smothering effect).
- **Halon / Clean Agents** target the **Chain Reaction** side (Fire Tetrahedron approach).
- **Heat transfer mechanisms** in a fire: Conduction, Convection, Radiation.
- The **Fire Triangle** model is the conceptual basis for all **NFPA, IS 3808, NBC 2016, and KTU 2024 fire protection curriculum**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fire Tetrahedron. Basic Concept of Fire Fighting with Water
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT836 - DESIGN OF FIRE PROTECTION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Fire Tetrahedron. Basic Concept of Fire Fighting with Water

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Fire Tetrahedron — Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fire Tetrahedron (KTU 2024 Module 1 Definition)**
> The *Fire Tetrahedron* is a four-sided geometric model used in fire protection engineering to represent the four interdependent elements that must be simultaneously present for combustion to initiate and self-sustain. The four elements are: **(1) Fuel (Combustible Material)**, **(2) Oxidizing Agent (typically Oxygen)**, **(3) Heat (Activation Energy)**, and **(4) Uninhibited Chemical Chain Reaction (Self-Sustained Reaction)**.

The fire tetrahedron is the modern extension of the classical **Fire Triangle** (Fuel–Heat–Oxygen). It was introduced in the mid-20th century after fire scientists recognised that removing any of the three classical sides does not always extinguish a fire — a fourth, *uninhibited chain reaction* of free radicals (H•, OH•, O•) must also be interrupted. This led to the understanding that **dry chemical powders** and **halogenated agents** extinguish fire not by cooling or smothering, but by breaking the chain reaction in the flame's vapour phase.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1)**
> Students must be able to **(a)** identify and label the four sides/vertices of the tetrahedron, **(b)** explain how removing each side leads to fire extinguishment, and **(c)** map the four fire-extinguishment methods (cooling, smothering, fuel removal, chain-breaking) onto the corresponding side they neutralise.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Four-Legged Stool"

Imagine a four-legged wooden stool holding up a burning candle:
- **Leg 1 (Fuel):** The wax — the very substance that *can* burn.
- **Leg 2 (Oxygen):** The air in the room — the *oxidiser* feeding the flame.
- **Leg 3 (Heat):** The match flame that initially *energised* the wax vapours.
- **Leg 4 (Chain Reaction):** The invisible "fire-carriers" — the excited free radicals that hop from molecule to molecule keeping the flame alive once it is lit.

> If you **saw off any one leg**, the stool (combustion) collapses. This is exactly the design principle behind every fire-extinguishing strategy taught in **OESFT836** — you do not have to fight all four sides, you only need to *break* one.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Quantity | Value / Standard |
|---|---|
| Atmospheric Oxygen (normal) | **20.9 % (v/v) in air** |
| Minimum Oxygen for combustion (most organics) | **≈ 15 % (v/v)** |
| Specific Heat Capacity of Water, $c_w$ | **4.186 kJ/(kg·K)** |
| Latent Heat of Vaporisation of Water, $L_v$ | **2260 kJ/kg** at 100 °C |
| Auto-ignition Temperature of paper | **≈ 218–246 °C** |
| Minimum Energy of Ignition (hydrogen) | **0.02 mJ** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Elements — Structured Logic

### 2.1.1 Fuel (Combustible Material)
- Any material capable of undergoing oxidation with the release of heat.
- **Physical states:** solid, liquid, vapour/gas.
- **Fire Point:** The lowest temperature at which a liquid produces enough vapour to sustain combustion *after* the ignition source is removed. (Lower than auto-ignition, higher than flash point.)
- **Fire Loads** are quantified as: $\text{Fire Load (MJ/m}^2\text{)} = \dfrac{\text{Total Calorific Value of Combustibles (MJ)}}{\text{Floor Area (m}^2\text{)}}$

### 2.1.2 Oxidising Agent
- Most commonly the **oxygen in air (≈ 20.9 %)**.
- Other oxidisers: nitrates, peroxides, chlorine, fluorine, pool chemicals.
- **Critical Oxygen Index (COI):** fires are extinguished by reducing local O₂ below the *Limiting Oxygen Concentration (LOC)* — typically **15 %** for hydrocarbon fires.

### 2.1.3 Heat (Activation Energy)
- The energy required to bring the fuel–air mixture to its **ignition temperature**.
- Heat is the *initiator* and the *propagator* of the chain reaction.
- Once combustion starts, the **exothermic reaction itself** becomes the heat source — this is why a fire can become self-sustaining.

### 2.1.4 Uninhibited Chemical Chain Reaction
- Combustion is a **free-radical chain reaction**: H• + O₂ → OH• + O•, OH• + CO → CO₂ + H•, etc.
- The reaction proceeds in three steps:
  1. **Initiation** — bond breaking (needs activation energy).
  2. **Propagation** — radicals regenerate themselves (self-sustaining).
  3. **Termination** — radicals combine to form stable products.
- **Halons, dry chemical powders (ABC), and clean agents** extinguish fire by acting on this fourth side — they donate or absorb free radicals, terminating the chain.

## 2.2 The Four Methods of Extinguishment Mapped to the Tetrahedron

| Side Removed | Method | Mechanism | Typical Agent |
|---|---|---|---|
| Heat | **Cooling** | Absorbs heat below fire point | **Water**, foam |
| Oxygen | **Smothering** | Displaces O₂ below 15 % | Foam blanket, CO₂, inert gas |
| Fuel | **Fuel Removal** | Cuts off combustible supply | Shut-off valve, isolation |
| Chain Reaction | **Chemical Inhibition** | Breaks free-radical chain | Dry chemical, Halon |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board-Valuation Insight (2024 Scheme):**
> When a question asks *"How does water extinguish fire?"* — the examiner expects a **two-part answer**:
> 1. **Primary mechanism (≈ 70 % weightage):** *Cooling* (high specific + latent heat of water).
> 2. **Secondary mechanism (≈ 30 % weightage):** *Smothering/Steam Smothering* — the steam generated displaces oxygen locally.
> Mentioning both is what differentiates a 7-mark answer from a 14-mark answer.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Boundary / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sensible heat absorbed by water | $Q_s = m \cdot c_w \cdot \Delta T$ | kJ | $c_w = 4.186$ kJ/(kg·K) |
| 2 | Latent heat absorbed during vaporisation | $Q_l = m \cdot L_v$ | kJ | $L_v = 2260$ kJ/kg @ 100 °C |
| 3 | **Total cooling capacity of water** | $\boxed{Q_t = m\left(c_w\,\Delta T + L_v + c_s\,\Delta T_v\right)}$ | kJ | $c_s \approx 2.01$ kJ/(kg·K) for steam |
| 4 | Volumetric water flow rate (discharge) | $Q_v = A \cdot v$ | L/min or m³/s | $A$ = nozzle orifice area, $v$ = velocity |
| 5 | Discharge through nozzle (orifice) | $Q_v = C_d \cdot A \sqrt{2gh}$ | m³/s | $C_d \approx 0.97$–$0.99$ (smooth nozzle) |
| 6 | Fire Load Density | $q = \dfrac{\sum (M_i H_i)}{A_f}$ | MJ/m² | $H_i$ = calorific value of material $i$ |
| 7 | Limiting Oxygen Concentration | $\text{LOC} \approx 15\%$ (most HC) | % v/v | Fires self-extinguish below this |
| 8 | Required water for cooling | $m = \dfrac{Q_{req}}{c_w \Delta T + L_v}$ | kg | Use $\Delta T$ = ambient to 100 °C → ≈ 60 °C |
| 9 | Expansion ratio of water → steam | $V_{steam} / V_{water} \approx 1700 : 1$ | – | At atmospheric pressure |
| 10 | Discharge density (sprinkler design) | $D_d = \dfrac{Q_{total}}{A_{coverage}}$ | L/min/m² | TAC/NBC clause reference |

> [!NOTE]
> **Convention Note on Vertical Bars in Tables:**
> Per KTU-premier-engine protocol, all absolute-value / magnitude expressions inside tables use $\vert$ or $\mid$ to avoid breaking markdown syntax. Example: $\Delta T \mid_{max}$.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The fire tetrahedron is the **conceptual backbone** of *all* fire-protection system design:
- **Selection of extinguishing agent** (water vs. foam vs. CO₂ vs. clean agent) is dictated by *which side* of the tetrahedron we are trying to break.
- **System layout (sprinklers, hydrants, gas flooding)** is dictated by the *fuel load* and *fire load* of the protected enclosure.
- **Fire investigation** uses the tetrahedron to determine the *origin* and *cause* — the *heat* and *ignition source* side points to the cause; the *fuel* side reveals the spread path.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Total Heat-Absorption Capacity of Water

> **Problem:** A **Class A** wood-pallet fire has an estimated heat-release rate (HRR) of **$Q_{req} = 8500$ kJ** in its incipient phase. Determine the **minimum mass of water** required to absorb this energy, assuming water is delivered at **27 °C** ambient and is heated to **100 °C** before vaporising.

### Step 1 — Identify the energy path
Water absorbs heat in three stages:
1. Heating liquid water from $T_a$ to $100$ °C → **sensible heat**.
2. Phase change at 100 °C → **latent heat**.
3. (Optionally) superheating steam — usually ignored in textbook problems.

### Step 2 — Write the governing equation
$$Q_{req} = m \cdot c_w \cdot (100 - T_a) + m \cdot L_v$$

Substituting numerical values:

$$Q_{req} = m \cdot (4.186)(100 - 27) + m \cdot (2260)$$

$$Q_{req} = m \cdot [4.186 \times 73 + 2260]$$

$$Q_{req} = m \cdot [305.58 + 2260]$$

$$Q_{req} = m \cdot 2565.58 \;\;\text{kJ/kg}$$

### Step 3 — Solve for the mass
$$m = \dfrac{Q_{req}}{2565.58} = \dfrac{8500}{2565.58}$$

$$\boxed{m \approx 3.31 \text{ kg of water}}$$

### Step 4 — Convert to volume
$$V = \dfrac{m}{\rho_w} = \dfrac{3.31}{1000} = 3.31 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3 = \boxed{3.31 \text{ L}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Only about **3.3 L** of water is *theoretically* required to absorb the incipient-phase energy. In practice, KTU/TAC design codes mandate a **safety factor of 2–3** due to inefficient heat transfer, radiation losses, and partial vaporisation.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Discharge Rate Through a Fire-Fighting Nozzle

> **Problem:** A **fire hydrant branch** delivers water to a smooth-bore nozzle of diameter **$d = 25$ mm** under a head of **$h = 40$ m** of water column. Determine the discharge rate. (Take $C_d = 0.98$, $g = 9.81$ m/s².)

### Step 1 — Apply the standard orifice equation
$$Q_v = C_d \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{2 g h}$$

### Step 2 — Compute the cross-sectional area
$$A = \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi (0.025)^2}{4} = 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

### Step 3 — Compute the velocity term
$$\sqrt{2 g h} = \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 40} = \sqrt{784.8} = 28.01 \text{ m/s}$$

### Step 4 — Multiply
$$Q_v = 0.98 \times 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \times 28.01$$

$$Q_v = 1.348 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

Convert to **L/min** (1 m³/s = 60 000 L/min):

$$Q_v = 1.348 \times 10^{-2} \times 60\,000 = \boxed{808.8 \text{ L/min}}$$

> [!TIP]
> This value is consistent with the standard **KTU/TAC hydrant-class flow of ≈ 900 LPM at 7 bar** for an institutional building design.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Why Water is the "Gold-Standard" Agent

| Property | Water | CO₂ | Dry Chemical (ABC) | Halon 1301 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Specific Heat (kJ/kg·K) | **4.186** | 0.84 | – | 0.71 |
| Latent Heat (kJ/kg) | **2260** | 571 (sublimation) | – | – |
| Mechanism | Cooling + Smothering | Smothering | Chain-Breaking | Chain-Breaking |
| Side of tetrahedron attacked | Heat (+ partially O₂) | Oxygen | Chain Reaction | Chain Reaction |
| Suitable for Class C (electrical) | No (conductive) | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Cost per kg | **Very Low** | Moderate | Moderate–High | Very High (banned) |
| Environmental impact | None | Low | Low | High (ODP) |
| Storage stability | **Indefinite** | Indefinite | Hygroscopic issues | Indefinite |

> **Conclusion of Matrix:** Water remains the **primary, most economical, and most universally deployed** agent for **Class A** and (with additives) **Class B** fires precisely because it is the only common agent that **simultaneously** removes the *heat* side of the tetrahedron and, via generated steam, partially *smothers* the *oxygen* side.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Fire Tetrahedron — Mermaid Block Diagram (3D-Style Functional Mapping)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["VERTEX A : FUEL"] --> B["EDGE 1 : Fuel to Heat"]
    A --> C["EDGE 2 : Fuel to Oxygen"]
    B --> D["VERTEX B : HEAT"]
    C --> E["VERTEX C : OXYGEN"]
    D --> F["EDGE 3 : Heat to Chain"]
    E --> G["EDGE 4 : Oxygen to Chain"]
    F --> H["VERTEX D : CHAIN REACTION"]
    G --> H
    H --> A
    EXT1["AGENT : WATER --> COOLING --> removes VERTEX B HEAT"] -.-> D
    EXT2["AGENT : FOAM --> SMOTHERING --> removes VERTEX C OXYGEN"] -.-> E
    EXT3["AGENT : ISOLATION --> FUEL REMOVAL --> removes VERTEX A FUEL"] -.-> A
    EXT4["AGENT : DRY CHEMICAL --> INHIBITION --> removes VERTEX D CHAIN"] -.-> H
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The four **vertices** (A, B, C, D) represent the four elements.
- The four **edges** represent the *interaction* between two adjacent elements.
- The four **dotted arrows (EXT1–EXT4)** show *which* extinguishing agent *breaks* which vertex/side of the tetrahedron.

---

## 4.2 Water-Based Fire-Fighting Mechanism — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SRC["WATER SOURCE : Tank / Reservoir / Municipal Supply"] --> PUMP["FIRE PUMP : Electric or Diesel"]
    PUMP --> RISER["RISER MAIN"]
    RISER --> VALVE["OUTLET VALVE : Hydrant Landing"]
    VALVE --> HOSE["HOSE LINE"]
    HOSE --> NOZZLE["NOZZLE : Jet / Spray / Fog"]
    NOZZLE --> W1["PHASE 1 : Heating 27 C to 100 C - SENSIBLE HEAT"]
    W1 --> W2["PHASE 2 : Phase Change to STEAM - LATENT HEAT 2260 kJ per kg"]
    W2 --> W3["PHASE 3 : Steam displaces Oxygen - SMOTHERING"]
    W1 --> S1["TETRAHEDRON SIDE REMOVED : HEAT side"]
    W2 --> S1
    W3 --> S2["TETRAHEDRON SIDE REMOVED : OXYGEN side partial"]
    S1 --> RESULT["FIRE EXTINGUISHED"]
    S2 --> RESULT
    NOZZLE -.-> SPRINKLER["ALTERNATE PATH : AUTOMATIC SPRINKLER HEAD"]
    SPRINKLER --> W1
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The flow is **Source → Pump → Riser → Valve → Hose → Nozzle → Fire** (with Sprinkler as the automated alternate path).
- Three sequential **cooling phases** (sensible heat → latent heat → steam smothering) are mapped to the **Heat** and **Oxygen** sides of the fire tetrahedron.

---

## 4.3 Classification of Fire & Agent Selection Matrix (Functional)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F["FIRE INCIDENT"]
    F --> CA["CLASS A : Solid Fuels wood paper cloth"]
    F --> CB["CLASS B : Flammable Liquids"]
    F --> CC["CLASS C : Flammable Gases"]
    F --> CD["CLASS D : Metals"]
    F --> CE["CLASS E : Electrical"]
    CA --> A1["AGENT : WATER or FOAM"]
    CB --> A2["AGENT : FOAM or DRY CHEMICAL"]
    CC --> A3["AGENT : DRY CHEMICAL or CO2"]
    CD --> A4["AGENT : SPECIAL DRY POWDER"]
    CE --> A5["AGENT : CO2 or CLEAN AGENT"]
    A1 -.->|"Removes HEAT side"| T["FIRE TETRAHEDRON"]
    A2 -.->|"Removes OXYGEN side"| T
    A3 -.->|"Breaks CHAIN reaction"| T
    A4 -.->|"Removes FUEL side via isolation"| T
    A5 -.->|"Removes OXYGEN side"| T
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. Students frequently describe water as "removing *only* the heat" — **incomplete.** Always mention **both cooling (primary) AND steam-smothering (secondary)**.
> 2. Students often confuse **fire point** with **flash point** — the question setter will deduct a mark if the distinction is missing.
> 3. When asked "which side does Halon act on?", many write "Oxygen" — this is **wrong**. Halons act on the **Chain Reaction** vertex.
> 4. Avoid the trap: "Water extinguishes *all* classes of fire" — water is **NOT** used for Class C (electrical) and Class D (metal) fires.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### **Q1.** *With the aid of a neat sketch, describe the **Fire Tetrahedron**. Mention how it is an improvement over the classical fire triangle.* **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 model question]** — **3 Marks**

**Model Answer:**

The fire tetrahedron is a four-sided geometric model representing the four elements that must coexist for combustion to occur:

1. **Fuel** — combustible material (solid, liquid, or gas).
2. **Heat** — energy at or above the ignition temperature.
3. **Oxygen** — typically ≥ 15 % by volume in air.
4. **Uninhibited chemical chain reaction** — free-radical propagation in the flame.

Removal of any *one* of these sides leads to **fire extinguishment**.

**Improvement over the fire triangle:** The classical fire triangle (Fuel–Heat–Oxygen) fails to explain why some agents (e.g., dry chemical powder, Halon) extinguish a fire *without* significantly cooling it or displacing oxygen. The fourth side — *uninhibited chain reaction* — explains this: these agents act by **interrupting the free-radical chain**, terminating the combustion process at the chemical level. Hence, the tetrahedron is a more complete and accurate model of combustion.

---

### **Q2.** *Explain with two reasons why **water** is the most widely used fire-fighting medium.* **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 model question]** — **3 Marks**

**Model Answer:**

Water is the most widely used fire-fighting medium because:

1. **Exceptionally high cooling capacity:** Water has a specific heat of **4.186 kJ/(kg·K)** and a latent heat of vaporisation of **2260 kJ/kg**. To vaporise 1 L of water absorbs ≈ **2594 kJ** of heat — far more than any other commonly available agent. This effectively **removes the Heat side of the fire tetrahedron**.

2. **Smothering action of generated steam:** When water vaporises, it expands by a factor of **≈ 1700** at atmospheric pressure, locally displacing oxygen and partially **removing the Oxygen side** of the tetrahedron. *(Other supportive points: abundant, cheap, non-toxic, indefinitely storable, easily piped.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

> *(As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern — answer **any ONE** of the two sub-questions per slot.)*

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **Q.A (a).** Draw and explain the **Fire Tetrahedron** in detail. Discuss the role of each of its four sides and how each contributes to the start and continuation of combustion. **[7 Marks]**
>
> **Q.A (b).** Water is the most common fire-fighting agent. Explain the **physical and chemical principles** behind its action on a Class A fire, with reference to the fire tetrahedron. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution to Q.A (a):

The fire tetrahedron is a 3D pyramid having **four sides** and **four vertices**. (Neat sketch expected — see Section 4.1.)

| Side / Vertex | Role in Fire Initiation | Role in Fire Continuation |
|---|---|---|
| **Fuel** (vertex) | Provides combustible vapours/gas | Continuously feeds the flame |
| **Heat** (vertex) | Supplies the activation energy (ignition) | The exothermic reaction itself becomes the heat source — the fire becomes self-sustaining |
| **Oxygen** (vertex) | Acts as the oxidiser in the exothermic reaction | Sustains the reaction at > 15 % concentration |
| **Chain Reaction** (vertex) | Begins as the heat initiates bond breaking, producing free radicals (H•, OH•, O•) | Free radicals propagate the reaction — H• regenerates in every cycle, making combustion self-perpetuating |

All four vertices must be present **simultaneously** for a fire to start *and* continue. The tetrahedron helps designers understand that extinguishment can be achieved by attacking *any* one of the four.

#### Model Solution to Q.A (b):

Water extinguishes a Class A (solid-fuel) fire by **two principal mechanisms**, both of which act on the fire tetrahedron:

**1. Cooling (Primary mechanism — Heat side):**
- Water has a very high **specific heat (4.186 kJ/(kg·K))** and an exceptionally high **latent heat of vaporisation (2260 kJ/kg)**.
- Heat absorbed per kg of water vaporised = $c_w \cdot \Delta T + L_v \approx 2566$ kJ/kg.
- This cools the burning solid below its **fire point**, removing the *Heat* vertex of the tetrahedron.

**2. Smothering (Secondary mechanism — Oxygen side):**
- 1 L of water → 1.7 m³ of steam at atmospheric pressure.
- The steam blanket locally **displaces oxygen** around the fire, pushing the O₂ concentration below the **Limiting Oxygen Concentration (LOC ≈ 15 %)**.
- This partially removes the *Oxygen* vertex.

**3. Emulsification / Penetration (for liquid fuels):**
- Water sprays can break liquid fuel into fine droplets, emulsifying it and preventing vapour release.

Water is **not used** for Class C (electrical) — it is conductive; nor for Class D (metals) — it reacts violently with alkali/alkaline-earth metals.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

> **Q.B (a).** Compare the **Fire Triangle** and the **Fire Tetrahedron**. Why is the tetrahedron considered a more complete model? **[7 Marks]**
>
> **Q.B (b).** A Class A fire has an estimated heat-release of **12 000 kJ** in its incipient phase. Calculate the **minimum mass and volume of water** required for extinction, given initial water temperature of **30 °C**. State the tetrahedron side(s) acted upon. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution to Q.B (a):

| Aspect | Fire Triangle | Fire Tetrahedron |
|---|---|---|
| Number of elements | 3 | 4 |
| Elements | Fuel, Heat, Oxygen | Fuel, Heat, Oxygen, **Chain Reaction** |
| Models combustion as | Chemical oxidation | **Free-radical chain reaction** |
| Can explain Halon/dry-chemical action? | No | **Yes** |
| Geometric form | 2D triangle | 3D pyramid (tetrahedron) |
| Modern use | Educational introduction | **Standard in KTU 2024 & fire codes (TAC, NBC)** |

The tetrahedron is more complete because the **chain-reaction** side explains the extinguishing action of agents that work *without* significant cooling or oxygen displacement — namely **dry chemical powders, Halons, and clean agents**. These agents donate or absorb free radicals (H•, OH•), terminating the chain and removing the fourth vertex.

#### Model Solution to Q.B (b):

**Step 1 — Energy path of water:**
- Sensible heat: from 30 °C → 100 °C, $\Delta T = 70$ K.
- Latent heat: vaporisation at 100 °C.

**Step 2 — Total heat absorbed per kg of water:**

$$q_{total} = c_w \cdot \Delta T + L_v = 4.186 \times 70 + 2260 = 293.02 + 2260 = 2553.02 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 3 — Mass of water required:**

$$m = \dfrac{Q_{req}}{q_{total}} = \dfrac{12000}{2553.02}$$

$$\boxed{m \approx 4.70 \text{ kg}}$$

**Step 4 — Volume of water required:**

$$V = \dfrac{m}{\rho_w} = \dfrac{4.70}{1000} = 4.70 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3 = \boxed{4.70 \text{ L}}$$

**Step 5 — Tetrahedron side(s) acted upon:**
- **Primary:** **Heat side** (cooling — sensible + latent heat).
- **Secondary:** **Oxygen side** (steam displacement, partial).
- **In a real design**, apply a safety factor of **2–3** to account for radiation losses and incomplete heat transfer.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Quick-Revision Checklist (Module 1 — Fire Tetrahedron & Water Fire-Fighting):**

- 🔥 **Fire Tetrahedron** has 4 vertices: **Fuel, Heat, Oxygen, Chain Reaction** — all must coexist.
- 🔺 The classical **Fire Triangle** is a subset (3 vertices) and *cannot* explain Halon/clean-agent action.
- ❄️ **Water** extinguishes by **Cooling (Heat side — primary)** + **Smothering (Oxygen side — secondary)**.
- 📊 Key water constants: $c_w = 4.186$ kJ/(kg·K); $L_v = 2260$ kJ/kg; expansion ratio water → steam ≈ **1700 : 1**.
- 🚫 **Water is NOT** used for **Class C (electrical)** and **Class D (metals)** fires.
- 🧪 **Halon & dry chemicals** act on the **Chain Reaction** vertex — *not* on oxygen.
- 📐 **Limiting Oxygen Concentration (LOC)** ≈ 15 % v/v — below this, most fires self-extinguish.
- 🛢️ **Fire Point ≠ Flash Point** — fire point is the lowest temperature at which combustion *sustains after* removal of the ignition source.
- 🏗️ In **KTU design problems**, always use **safety factor = 2 to 3** for the computed water quantity.
- 🧯 Four methods of extinguishment = four sides of the tetrahedron: **Cooling / Smothering / Fuel Removal / Chemical Inhibition**.
- 📐 Discharge through nozzle: $Q_v = C_d A \sqrt{2gh}$ — $C_d \approx 0.97$–$0.99$ for smooth bore.
- 🔢 **Fire Load Density** $q = \dfrac{\sum M_i H_i}{A_f}$ (MJ/m²) — governs water-storage tank design.
- 🏛️ For a typical institutional hydrant, design flow ≈ **900 LPM at ≈ 7 bar** — consistent with the worked nozzle problem (Section 3.2).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Carbon Dioxide
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT836 - DESIGN OF FIRE PROTECTION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Carbon Dioxide

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Carbon Dioxide (CO₂)** is a colourless, odourless, electrically non-conductive chemical compound (chemical formula CO₂, molecular weight 44.01 g/mol) that is stored under high pressure as a liquefied gas and discharged as a fire suppression agent. It extinguishes fire primarily through the mechanisms of **oxygen displacement (smothering)** and **cooling (heat absorption during rapid expansion/vaporisation)**. CO₂ is classified as a **"clean agent"** fire suppressant under **NFPA 12** (*Standard on Carbon Dioxide Extinguishing Systems*), leaving no residue, causing no collateral damage to equipment, and is therefore suitable for protecting high-value electrical, electronic, and flammable-liquid hazards.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Carbon Dioxide systems are introduced as the *foundational gaseous suppression system* in fire protection engineering. Mastery of CO₂ properties, extinguishing mechanism, system architecture, and design calculations is mandatory before progressing to Halon alternatives, clean agents (FM-200, Novec 1230), and inert gas systems (IG-01, IG-541).

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — Plain English Explanation

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine blowing out a candle. The flame dies not because the wax is gone, but because the moving air **carries the oxygen away** from the flame's base. A Carbon Dioxide system does the exact same thing — but in a *controlled, room-scale* manner. When a fire breaks out inside an enclosed hazard (e.g., a server room or generator enclosure), the CO₂ system floods the room with CO₂ gas until the **oxygen concentration drops from 21% to about 12–15%** — below the threshold required for combustion. The fire simply *chokes to death* because it can no longer find oxygen to react with.
>
> The secondary effect is like putting out a hot pan with a cold lid: the liquid CO₂ vaporising inside the piping absorbs enormous amounts of heat (latent heat of vaporisation ≈ **571 kJ/kg** at 0°C), cooling the fire zone simultaneously.

## 1.3 Key Physical & Chemical Properties (Board Exam Essentials)

| Property | Value (SI) | Value (Imperial) |
|---|---|---|
| Molecular Weight | **44.01 g/mol** | — |
| Boiling Point (1 atm) | **-78.5 °C** (194.7 K) | **-109.3 °F** |
| Critical Temperature | **31.0 °C** (304.1 K) | **87.8 °F** |
| Critical Pressure | **7.38 MPa** | **1070 psia** |
| Gas Density (0 °C, 1 atm) | **1.87 kg/m³** | **0.114 lb/ft³** |
| Liquid Density (0 °C, 35 bar) | **~900 kg/m³** | **~56 lb/ft³** |
| Latent Heat of Vaporisation | **571 kJ/kg** | **245 BTU/lb** |
| Expansion Ratio (Liquid → Gas at 21 °C) | **≈ 1 : 450** | — |
| OEL (Occupational Exposure Limit) | **0.5% (8-hr TWA)** | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why these numbers matter in design:** The expansion ratio (1:450) tells you that **1 m³ of liquid CO₂ produces 450 m³ of gas** at room temperature — this is the heart of *Total Flooding* calculations. The 31 °C critical temperature means CO₂ cannot exist as a liquid above 31 °C, regardless of pressure — this is why high-pressure CO₂ cylinders in hot climates (e.g., Kerala summer ambient 35–40 °C) must be stored below critical temperature for reliable liquefied discharge.

## 1.4 Classification of CO₂ Applications

- **Total Flooding Systems:** Designed to fill an enclosed space with CO₂ to achieve a uniform minimum design concentration.
- **Local Application Systems:** Discharge CO₂ directly onto a specific hazard (e.g., paint dip tank, printing press) — does not require a tight enclosure.
- **Hand-Hose Line Systems:** Manually operated hoselines for fire brigade or trained personnel use.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** A common exam mistake is treating "CO₂ is non-toxic" as "CO₂ is safe for occupied spaces". CO₂ becomes **dangerous to humans at 4% (1-hour exposure) and lethal above 10%**. NFPA 12 mandates a **30-second pre-discharge alarm** and **lockout/evacuation procedures** for all total-flooding systems protecting normally-occupied areas.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mechanism of Fire Extinguishment — Structured Logic Steps

The CO₂ system extinguishes a fire through **three simultaneous physical mechanisms**. Understanding *why* each step happens is the key to KTU conceptual questions.

**Step 1 — Oxygen Displacement (Smothering)**
- Combustion requires ambient O₂ ≥ **15%** (most Class A/B fires).
- CO₂ displaces air, diluting the O₂ partial pressure.
- Design concentrations are set so that the *final* O₂ level is below the combustion threshold (typically 12–14%).

**Step 2 — Cooling via Latent Heat Absorption**
- Liquid CO₂ stored at ~57 bar expands rapidly upon discharge through the nozzle.
- The phase change (liquid → gas) absorbs ~**571 kJ/kg** of latent heat from the fire zone.
- This cools the fuel surface below its **fire point**, halting pyrolysis and flame propagation.

**Step 3 — Thermal Radiation Shielding (Minor)**
- The dense CO₂ cloud (denser than air, **ρ_CO₂ ≈ 1.5 × ρ_air**) forms a blanket over the fire, partially blocking radiant heat feedback to the fuel.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why CO₂ is considered a "Clean Agent":** Unlike water, foam, or dry chemical, gaseous CO₂ disperses evenly, leaves zero residue, is electrically non-conductive (dielectric strength ≈ **30 kV/cm**), and causes no damage to electronics, documents, or precision machinery. This is why CO₂ is *the* suppressant of choice for **electrical, telecom, and data-centre hazards**.

## 2.2 Standard Design Concentrations (NFPA 12 Reference Table)

| Fuel / Hazard | Minimum Design Concentration (%) |
|---|---|
| Class A (paper, wood) | 34 |
| Class B — Heptane | 34 |
| Class B — Gasoline | 36 |
| Class B — Propane | 35 |
| Class B — Ethanol | 43 |
| Electrical Equipment | 50 |
| Deep-Seated Class A | 65 |
| Hydraulic Fluid (phosphate ester) | 34 |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are KTU board-exam essentials. Memorise the variables, units, and the *one* critical constant (specific volume S).**

| # | Formula | Variables & Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $W = 2.303 \cdot \dfrac{V}{S} \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{100}{100 - C}\right)$ | $W$ = CO₂ mass (lb); $V$ = room volume (ft³); $S$ = specific volume of CO₂ at storage conditions (ft³/lb); $C$ = design concentration (%) | **Total Flooding — quantity of CO₂ required** |
| 2 | $N_c = \dfrac{W}{m_{cyl}}$ | $N_c$ = number of cylinders; $m_{cyl}$ = mass per cylinder (lb) | Cylinder quantity sizing |
| 3 | $Q_{avg} = \dfrac{W}{t_{dis}}$ | $Q_{avg}$ = average discharge rate (lb/s); $t_{dis}$ = discharge time (s) | Pipe flow sizing (typically $t_{dis} \le 60$ s) |
| 4 | $Q_{peak} = K \cdot Q_{avg}$ | $K$ = peaking factor (NFPA 12 chart, typically 2.0–3.5) | Pipe sizing for first half of discharge |
| 5 | $\Delta P = \dfrac{f \cdot L}{D} \cdot \dfrac{\rho v^{2}}{2}$ | Darcy-Weisbach: $f$ = friction factor; $L$ = pipe length; $D$ = diameter; $v$ = velocity; $\rho$ = density | Pressure drop in CO₂ distribution piping |
| 6 | $v = \dfrac{Q_{peak}}{A} = \dfrac{4 Q_{peak}}{\pi D^{2}}$ | $A$ = pipe cross-section; $Q$ in consistent units | Velocity check (limit $v \le 400$ ft/s to prevent excess noise/erosion) |
| 7 | $P_{N} = P_{cyl,min} - \sum \Delta P$ | $P_N$ = nozzle inlet pressure; $P_{cyl,min}$ = minimum cylinder pressure during discharge | Nozzle pressure availability check |
| 8 | $V_{inj} = \dfrac{Q_{peak}}{C_d \cdot A_{orifice} \cdot \sqrt{2 \rho \Delta P}}$ | $C_d$ = discharge coefficient ($\approx 0.65$); $A_{orifice}$ = nozzle orifice area | Nozzle orifice sizing |
| 9 | $t_{min} = \dfrac{60 \cdot V_{room}}{Q_{avg} \cdot S_{gas}}$ | Minimum discharge time for concentration hold | NFPA 12 compliance check |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Storage Constants for KTU Exams:**
> - High-Pressure CO₂ at **70 °F (21 °C)**: $S = 0.685$ ft³/lb
> - High-Pressure CO₂ at **0 °F (-18 °C)**: $S = 0.604$ ft³/lb
> - Low-Pressure (300 psi) CO₂ at **0 °F**: $S = 0.305$ ft³/lb
>
> The choice of $S$ depends on storage temperature — a *very common exam trick* is to vary the storage temperature and expect a recalculated $W$.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

CO₂ system design is fundamental in:
- **Data Centres & Server Rooms** (Class C fire protection without equipment damage)
- **Marine Engine Rooms** (offshore platforms, naval vessels)
- **Paint Booths & Dip Tanks** (local application)
- **Electrical Substations & Transformer Yards**
- **Pharmaceutical & Aerospace Cleanrooms** (no residue contamination)
- **Printing Industry** (ink, solvent hazards)
- **Aircraft Hangars** (engine test cells)
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Quantity of CO₂ for Total Flooding

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Note:** The NFPA 12 *exact* formula is $W = 2.303 \cdot (V/S) \cdot \log_{10}[100/(100-C)]$. The factor 2.303 arises from the conversion of natural logarithm (ln) to base-10 logarithm. Below is the *physical derivation* that justifies this equation.

### 3.1.1 The Underlying Physics

Consider a room of volume $V$ containing air at atmospheric pressure. CO₂ is added until the **mole fraction** of CO₂ equals $C/100$. Because CO₂ is added from a storage cylinder where it occupies a *much smaller volume per unit mass* than at room conditions, we cannot simply use the ideal gas relation $PV = nRT$ in the simple form. The *exact* formulation must account for the **cumulative pressure rise** as gas is added.

### 3.1.2 The Derivation (Exhaustive)

Let $V$ = room volume (ft³), $P$ = absolute pressure (psia), $T$ = room temperature (°R), and $C$ = design concentration (% by volume).

**Step 1 — Initial Condition (no CO₂):**
The room contains pure air.
$$V_{air,0} = V, \quad P_{0} = P_{atm}$$

**Step 2 — Infinitesimal CO₂ Addition:**
Add a small amount of CO₂ with mass $dW$ from the cylinder. This amount, at storage conditions, occupies a volume $dV_s = S \cdot dW$ (where $S$ is the specific volume at storage).

**Step 3 — Apply Ideal Gas Law to Final Mixture:**
After addition, the total pressure rises to $P + dP$, and the CO₂ mole fraction is $C + dC$. Using Dalton's Law:
$$P_{CO_2} = \left(\dfrac{C}{100}\right) P, \quad P_{air} = \left(\dfrac{100 - C}{100}\right) P$$

**Step 4 — Relate $dW$ to Pressure Change:**
The differential mass of CO₂ corresponds to a partial pressure rise:
$$dW \cdot S = V \cdot \dfrac{dP_{CO_2}}{P_{CO_2,storage}}$$

Substituting $P_{CO_2} = (C/100) P$:
$$dW \cdot S = V \cdot \dfrac{d(C/100 \cdot P)}{P_{CO_2,storage}}$$

**Step 5 — Integrate from $C = 0$ to $C = C_{design}$:**
For an isothermal, isobaric room (the displaced air escapes — typical assumption for total flooding of *non-pressure-resistant* enclosures):
$$W \cdot S = V \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{100}{100 - C}\right)$$

**Step 6 — Convert to Base-10 Logarithm:**
Since $\ln(x) = 2.303 \cdot \log_{10}(x)$:
$$\boxed{W = \dfrac{2.303 \cdot V}{S} \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{100}{100 - C}\right)}$$

This is the **NFPA 12 exact formula** for total flooding CO₂ mass.

## 3.2 Analytical Worked Example — KTU Board Style

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Example Problem (modelled on KTU 2024 Scheme Part B pattern):**
>
> *A battery-charging room has dimensions 6 m × 4 m × 3 m. Ambient temperature is 25 °C. The CO₂ system is stored at 21 °C (70 °F) in high-pressure cylinders. The design concentration for electrical hazards is 50%. Determine: (a) the mass of CO₂ required, (b) the number of 50-lb cylinders needed, and (c) the average discharge rate for a 60-second discharge.*

### Solution — Step by Step

**Step 1 — Compute Room Volume:**
$$V = 6 \times 4 \times 3 = 72 \; \text{m}^{3}$$
Convert to ft³ (1 m³ = 35.315 ft³):
$$V = 72 \times 35.315 = 2542.7 \; \text{ft}^{3}$$

**Step 2 — Identify Specific Volume $S$:**
For high-pressure CO₂ at 70 °F (21 °C): $S = 0.685 \; \text{ft}^{3}\text{/lb}$.

**Step 3 — Apply the Total Flooding Formula:**
$$W = \dfrac{2.303 \cdot V}{S} \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{100}{100 - C}\right)$$

$$W = \dfrac{2.303 \times 2542.7}{0.685} \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{100}{50}\right)$$

$$W = 8547.2 \times \log_{10}(2)$$

$$W = 8547.2 \times 0.30103$$

$$\boxed{W = 2573.2 \; \text{lb} \;\; (\approx 1167 \; \text{kg})}$$

**Step 4 — Number of 50-lb Cylinders:**
$$N_c = \dfrac{W}{m_{cyl}} = \dfrac{2573.2}{50} = 51.46$$

Round up to nearest whole cylinder:
$$\boxed{N_c = 52 \; \text{cylinders}}$$

**Step 5 — Average Discharge Rate:**
$$Q_{avg} = \dfrac{W}{t_{dis}} = \dfrac{2573.2 \; \text{lb}}{60 \; \text{s}} = 42.9 \; \text{lb/s}$$

Convert to SI: $42.9 \; \text{lb/s} \times 0.4536 = 19.46 \; \text{kg/s}$.
$$\boxed{Q_{avg} \approx 19.5 \; \text{kg/s}}$$

**Step 6 — Peaking Factor Check (NFPA 12 Chart):**
For 52 cylinders discharging simultaneously into a common manifold, the peaking factor $K \approx 2.2$. The peak flow for pipe sizing is:
$$Q_{peak} = 2.2 \times 42.9 = 94.4 \; \text{lb/s} \;\; (\approx 42.8 \; \text{kg/s})$$

**Step 7 — Pipe Sizing Check (Velocity ≤ 400 ft/s):**
Assume a 2-inch (0.167 ft) ID pipe:
$$A = \dfrac{\pi D^{2}}{4} = \dfrac{\pi (0.167)^{2}}{4} = 0.0219 \; \text{ft}^{2}$$

Using $\rho_{CO_2,gas} \approx 0.114 \; \text{lb/ft}^{3}$ at 21 °C:
$$v = \dfrac{Q_{peak}}{\rho \cdot A} = \dfrac{94.4}{0.114 \times 0.0219} = 37{,}830 \; \text{ft/s}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Velocity is absurdly high!** This shows that 2-inch pipe is far too small for 42 kg/s CO₂ flow. In practice, CO₂ systems are *two-phase flow* (liquid + gas), with much higher effective density — but the lesson holds: **pipe sizing is critical**, and large diameter mains (4–6 inch) with parallel runs are typical for high-mass CO₂ systems.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Design Decision Tree

| Design Parameter | Decision Criterion | KTU Exam Frequency |
|---|---|---|
| Total Flooding vs. Local App. | Is hazard fully enclosed & ≤ 1500 m³? | High |
| High-Pressure vs. Low-Pressure | Total quantity > 2000 lb → Low Pressure | High |
| Cylinder Quantity | $N_c = \lceil W/m_{cyl} \rceil$ + 1 spare | High |
| Discharge Time | ≤ 60 s for total flooding | High |
| Pre-Discharge Delay | 30 s minimum for occupied areas | Medium |
| Pipe Material | Schedule 40/80 black steel (ASTM A53) | Medium |
| Nozzle Type | 180° / 360° radial; $C_d$ = 0.65 | Medium |
| Safety Provisions | Pneumatic horn + visual alarm + abort switch | High |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 System Architecture — Block-Level Functional Flow

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Note:** All node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters; labels contain only raw uppercase text with no markdown formatting.

```mermaid
graph TB
    A1[DETECTION PANEL] -->|Fire Signal 4 Wire Loop| A2[CONTROL UNIT]
    A2 -->|Initiate Sequence| A3[ACTUATION VALVE]
    A3 -->|Pneumatic Pilot Pressure| A4[MASTER CYLINDER VALVE]
    A4 -->|Opens All Cylinders Via Manifold| A5[CO2 CYLINDER BANK N EQUAL TO 52]
    A5 -->|High Pressure Liquid CO2| A6[MANIFOLD COLLECTOR 2 INCH SCH 80]
    A6 -->|Pilot Line To Other Cylinders| A5
    A6 -->|Main Flow Path| A7[SELECTOR VALVE FOR HAZARD ZONE]
    A7 -->|Liquid Plus Gas Two Phase| A8[DISTRIBUTION PIPING 4 INCH MAIN]
    A8 -->|Branch Lines 2 INCH| A9[MANIFOLD HEADER]
    A9 -->|Final 1 INCH Branches| A10[DISCHARGE NOZZLES]
    A10 -->|Atomised CO2 Cloud| A11[PROTECTED HAZARD ROOM 72 M3]
    A2 -->|Pre Discharge Signal T Minus 30s| A12[ALARM HORN AND STROBE]
    A12 -->|Evacuate Personnel| A13[SAFETY PERSONNEL]
    A2 -->|Hold Or Abort| A14[EMERGENCY ABORT SWITCH]
    A2 -->|Ventilation Shutdown| A15[MAKE UP AIR DAMPER CLOSE]
    A2 -->|Power Cut To Hazard| A16[EQUIPMENT SHUNT TRIP]
```

## 4.2 Detection-Actuation-Distribution Sequence

```mermaid
graph LR
    S1[STAGE 1 DETECTION] -->|Smoke Or Heat Detected| S2[STAGE 2 ALARM VERIFICATION 30 SEC]
    S2 -->|Confirmed Fire| S3[STAGE 3 PRE DISCHARGE ALARM T MINUS 30]
    S3 -->|Delay Countdown| S4[STAGE 4 ABORT WINDOW PERSONNEL EVACUATE]
    S4 -->|No Abort| S5[STAGE 5 CYLINDER DISCHARGE 60 SEC]
    S5 -->|CO2 Floods Room| S6[STAGE 6 HOLD TIME 10 MIN MINIMUM]
    S6 -->|Manual Reset After Fire Out| S7[STAGE 7 VENTILATION RESET]
```

## 4.3 Functional Component Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph STORAGE_SUBSYSTEM
        C1[CO2 CYLINDER 50 LB]
        C2[CO2 CYLINDER 50 LB]
        C3[CO2 CYLINDER 50 LB]
        C4[MANIFOLD HEADER]
    end
    subgraph ACTUATION_SUBSYSTEM
        V1[ACTUATION CYLINDER]
        V2[MASTER VALVE]
        V3[SOLENOID ACTUATOR 24 VDC]
    end
    subgraph DISTRIBUTION_SUBSYSTEM
        P1[MAIN PIPE 4 INCH]
        P2[BRANCH PIPE 2 INCH]
        P3[TERMINAL PIPE 1 INCH]
        N1[180 DEGREE NOZZLE]
        N2[360 DEGREE NOZZLE]
    end
    subgraph DETECTION_SUBSYSTEM
        D1[IONISATION SMOKE SENSOR]
        D2[PHOTOELECTRIC SENSOR]
        D3[HEAT DETECTOR 68 DEGREE C]
    end
    C1 --> C4
    C2 --> C4
    C3 --> C4
    V1 --> V2
    V3 --> V2
    V2 --> C4
    C4 --> P1
    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> P3
    P3 --> N1
    P3 --> N2
    D1 --> V3
    D2 --> V3
    D3 --> V3
```

## 4.4 Nozzle Coverage Schematic

```mermaid
graph TB
    ROOM[PROTECTED ROOM 6 M X 4 M X 3 M] --> N1
    ROOM --> N2
    ROOM --> N3
    ROOM --> N4
    N1[180 DEG NOZZLE COVERAGE 5 M RADIUS] -.->|AIMED AT WALL| ROOM
    N2[180 DEG NOZZLE COVERAGE 5 M RADIUS] -.->|AIMED AT WALL| ROOM
    N3[180 DEG NOZZLE COVERAGE 5 M RADIUS] -.->|AIMED AT WALL| ROOM
    N4[360 DEG NOZZLE COVERAGE 3 M RADIUS] -.->|CEILING CENTRE| ROOM
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: List any three physical properties of Carbon Dioxide that make it suitable as a fire extinguishing agent. State the relevant standard.**

**Model Answer:**
Carbon Dioxide (CO₂) is governed by **NFPA 12 – Standard on Carbon Dioxide Extinguishing Systems**. The three key physical properties are:

1. **High density (1.5 × air):** Forms a blanket that displaces oxygen from the fire zone.
2. **Latent heat of vaporisation (571 kJ/kg):** Absorbs large quantities of heat during the liquid-to-gas phase change, providing a cooling effect.
3. **Electrical non-conductivity:** Dielectric strength ≈ **30 kV/cm**, making it safe for energised electrical equipment.

*(1 mark each for the three properties + identifying the standard)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define "Total Flooding System" as applied to CO₂ fire protection. Why is a 30-second pre-discharge delay mandatory?**

**Model Answer:**
A **Total Flooding System** is a fixed CO₂ extinguishing system designed to discharge a predetermined quantity of CO₂ into an enclosed hazard volume to achieve a uniform minimum design concentration throughout the space (typically 34–65% depending on fuel).

A **30-second pre-discharge delay** is mandatory because:
1. CO₂ is an **asphyxiant** — exposure above 4% becomes hazardous to life.
2. NFPA 12 requires personnel evacuation and a manual abort opportunity for occupied spaces.
3. The delay allows warning horns, strobes, and door interlocks to activate before CO₂ release.

*(2 marks for definition + 1 mark for delay justification)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`

**Q: A library archive room of dimensions 10 m × 6 m × 4 m is to be protected by a high-pressure CO₂ total flooding system. The design concentration for Class A fire (paper/books) is 34%. The cylinders are stored at 21 °C (70 °F).**
**(a) Calculate the mass of CO₂ required, and the number of 50-lb cylinders needed. (7 marks)**
**(b) With a discharge time of 60 s, calculate the average and peak discharge rates. Comment on the velocity in a 2-inch schedule-40 distribution pipe and suggest a corrective action. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Mass and Cylinder Quantity**

**Step 1 — Volume of room:**
$V = 10 \times 6 \times 4 = 240 \; \text{m}^{3} = 240 \times 35.315 = 8475.6 \; \text{ft}^{3}$

**Step 2 — Specific volume (high-pressure, 70 °F):**
$S = 0.685 \; \text{ft}^{3}\text{/lb}$

**Step 3 — Apply NFPA 12 formula:**
$W = \dfrac{2.303 \times V}{S} \times \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{100}{100 - C}\right)$

$W = \dfrac{2.303 \times 8475.6}{0.685} \times \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{100}{66}\right)$

$W = 28{,}496 \times \log_{10}(1.5152)$

$W = 28{,}496 \times 0.1805$

$\boxed{W = 5143.5 \; \text{lb} \approx 2333 \; \text{kg}}$

**Step 4 — Cylinder Quantity:**
$N_c = \lceil 5143.5 / 50 \rceil = \lceil 102.87 \rceil = \mathbf{103 \; cylinders}$ (plus 1 spare recommended = 104).

*(7 marks: 2 for formula application, 2 for arithmetic, 2 for cylinder count, 1 for SI unit conversion)*

**Part (b) — Discharge Rate and Pipe Velocity**

**Step 1 — Average Discharge Rate:**
$Q_{avg} = W / t_{dis} = 5143.5 / 60 = 85.7 \; \text{lb/s} = 38.9 \; \text{kg/s}$

**Step 2 — Peaking Factor (NFPA 12 for 103 cylinders):** $K \approx 2.3$.
$Q_{peak} = 2.3 \times 85.7 = 197.1 \; \text{lb/s}$

**Step 3 — Velocity in 2-inch (0.1723 ft ID) Pipe:**
$A = \pi (0.1723)^{2} / 4 = 0.0233 \; \text{ft}^{2}$
Using gas density $\rho = 0.114 \; \text{lb/ft}^{3}$:
$v = 197.1 / (0.114 \times 0.0233) = 74{,}200 \; \text{ft/s}$

**Step 4 — Comment:** This velocity is *physically impossible* (> Mach 200) because the actual flow in CO₂ distribution piping is **two-phase (liquid + gas)** with much higher effective density (~30–50 lb/ft³). The correction is to use **two-phase flow models** and select a larger pipe (typically 4–6 inch main with parallel runs) to keep equivalent velocity below 200 ft/s and pressure drop within 10% of cylinder pressure.

*(7 marks: 2 for $Q_{avg}$, 2 for $Q_{peak}$, 2 for velocity analysis, 1 for corrective action)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`

**Q: (a) Explain with neat sketches the three mechanisms by which CO₂ extinguishes a fire. Compare CO₂ with water sprinklers for protection of a data centre. (7 marks)**
**(b) Discuss the health hazards of CO₂ exposure. With reference to NFPA 12, list the safety provisions required for a CO₂ total-flooding system in an occupied area. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Mechanisms and Data Centre Comparison**

The three extinguishing mechanisms are:
1. **Oxygen Displacement (Smothering):** CO₂ reduces ambient O₂ from 21% to <15% by volume, arresting combustion. This is the *dominant* mechanism, contributing ~70% of effectiveness.
2. **Cooling Effect:** Latent heat of vaporisation of liquid CO₂ (571 kJ/kg) cools the fuel below its fire point.
3. **Radiation Shielding:** Dense CO₂ cloud partially blocks radiant heat feedback to the fuel surface.

**Comparison — CO₂ vs. Water Sprinkler for Data Centre:**

| Criterion | CO₂ System | Water Sprinkler |
|---|---|---|
| Residue / Damage | None | Severe equipment damage |
| Electrical Safety | Non-conductive | Conductive (short-circuit risk) |
| Detection-to-Suppression | 30–60 s (delayed) | 10–30 s (faster) |
| Personnel Hazard | Asphyxiation risk | Slip/scald risk |
| Re-activation cost | Low (vent & reset) | Very high (replace equipment) |
| Suitability for Data Centre | **Excellent** | **Poor** |

*(7 marks: 3 for mechanisms, 4 for comparison table)*

**Part (b) — Health Hazards and Safety Provisions**

**Health Hazards of CO₂:**
- **4% concentration** for 1 hour: Headache, dizziness (OEL exceeded)
- **6%** for 30 min: Visual distortion, tremors
- **>10%**: Loss of consciousness within minutes
- **>20%**: Death within 30 minutes (asphyxia, cardiac arrest)
- Additionally, discharge at high pressure can cause **frostbite** (CO₂ solidifies as "dry ice" snow at -78.5 °C).

**NFPA 12 Safety Provisions for Occupied Areas:**
1. **30-second pre-discharge alarm** (audible horn + visual strobe).
2. **Manual abort switch** at each entrance, with re-arming delay to prevent cycling.
3. **Time-delay device** (countdown timer) to allow personnel evacuation.
4. **Self-contained breathing apparatus (SCBA)** provisions for first responders.
5. **Discharge warning signs** at all access doors ("CO₂ – DO NOT ENTER DURING DISCHARGE").
6. **Door interlocks / lockouts** to prevent entry during/after discharge without ventilation.
7. **Ventilation interlock** — mechanical supply/exhaust must shut off to maintain concentration.
8. **Cylinder bank lockout** to prevent unauthorised actuation.

*(7 marks: 3 for health hazards, 4 for safety provisions)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Mistakes in CO₂ Design Questions (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> 1. **Forgetting the 2.303 factor** in $W = 2.303 \cdot (V/S) \cdot \log_{10}(100/(100-C))$ — losing **2 marks** instantly.
> 2. **Using the wrong specific volume $S$** — examiners frequently vary storage temperature (0 °F vs 70 °F) to test attention. Memorise: $S = 0.685$ (HP, 70 °F), $S = 0.604$ (HP, 0 °F), $S = 0.305$ (LP, 0 °F).
> 3. **Mixing units** — V must be in ft³, $W$ in lb, $S$ in ft³/lb. Forgetting m³ → ft³ conversion is a **3-mark error**.
> 4. **Skipping the cylinder count rounding** — always round **up** to the next whole cylinder; never round down.
> 5. **Omitting the 1-cylinder spare** recommended by NFPA 12 — this is the *quality* differentiator between a 13-mark and 14-mark answer.
> 6. **Conflating "low pressure" with "low concentration"** — low-pressure systems use refrigeration to maintain 300 psi at 0 °F; they are not lower-concentration systems.
> 7. **Forgetting the 30-second pre-discharge delay** in any safety-related question is a guaranteed mark loss — it is **mandatory by NFPA 12**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Print this section. Read it the night before the exam.**

- **CO₂ is a clean agent** under **NFPA 12**; extinguishes via smothering (O₂ dilution), cooling (latent heat 571 kJ/kg), and radiation shielding.
- **Critical Temperature 31 °C** — above this, CO₂ cannot be liquefied regardless of pressure. *Critical for Kerala storage conditions.*
- **Expansion ratio 1:450** — 1 m³ liquid → 450 m³ gas at 21 °C.
- **Design concentrations** — Class A 34%, electrical 50%, deep-seated 65% (memorise the 34/50/65 trio).
- **Total Flooding formula:** $W = 2.303 \cdot (V/S) \cdot \log_{10}[100/(100-C)]$ — *memorise the 2.303 factor!*
- **Specific volume $S$** depends on storage: 0.685 (HP, 70 °F), 0.604 (HP, 0 °F), 0.305 (LP, 0 °F).
- **Discharge time** ≤ 60 seconds for total flooding (NFPA 12).
- **Pre-discharge delay** = 30 seconds (mandatory for occupied areas).
- **Cylinder sizing** — round up $N_c$ to next whole cylinder; add 1 spare.
- **Pipe sizing** uses two-phase flow; velocity limit 200–400 ft/s; pressure drop ≤ 10% of cylinder pressure.
- **Health hazards** — 4% (1-hr), 6% (30-min), >10% (lethal) — *memorise these thresholds.*
- **System types** — Total Flooding, Local Application, Hand Hose Lines.
- **Advantages** — No residue, non-conductive, effective on Class B/C, no equipment damage.
- **Disadvantages** — Asphyxiation hazard, no cooling of deep-seated fires (Class A smouldering), high-pressure storage required.
- **Application hierarchy** — Battery rooms → Server rooms → Electrical panels → Paint booths → Engine rooms → Flammable liquid storage.
- **Safety chain** — Detection → Alarm verification → Pre-discharge alarm (30 s) → Manual abort window → Discharge (≤ 60 s) → Hold time (≥ 10 min) → Ventilation reset.
- **Comparison anchor:** CO₂ vs Halon — both clean agents, but CO₂ is non-ozone-depleting and unlimited supply (Halon banned under Montreal Protocol).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Powders
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT836 - DESIGN OF FIRE PROTECTION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Powders

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fire Extinguishing Powders** are finely divided, free-flowing solid particulate extinguishing agents, composed primarily of inorganic salts (phosphates, bicarbonates, or specialized metallic compounds), which extinguish fire primarily by chemically interrupting the combustion chain reaction (negative catalysis) and secondarily by smothering, cooling, and radiation shielding effects. They are the most versatile dry chemical agents, effective on Class A, B, C, and specialized Class D fires.

As per **IS 14609:2018** and aligned with **IS 15105** (Powder-Based Extinguishing Systems), powders used in fire protection are standardized dry chemical mixtures manufactured to precise particle-size distributions, moisture limits, and flow characteristics.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of it like "sand for chemistry class" — but smarter!**
> Imagine sprinkling a very fine, specially engineered dust onto a small campfire. The dust doesn't just smother the flames — it chemically "talks" to the fire at the molecular level and tells the chemical chain reaction to STOP. Even after the dust settles, the chemistry keeps working to prevent re-ignition. That's exactly what fire-extinguishing powder does — it's not just a blanket; it's a molecular firefighter in dust form!

**Why this analogy works:**
- Just as flour thrown on a candle flame can extinguish it (physical smothering), powders do MORE — they chemically attack the **free-radical chain reaction** in the flame zone.
- The fine particle size (typically **10–75 µm**) ensures maximum surface area for chemical interaction with flame radicals like **H•, OH•, and O•**.

---

## 1.3 Classification of Powders (KTU Syllabus Highlight)

| Powder Type | Active Chemical | Fire Class | Typical Color Code |
|-------------|----------------|------------|--------------------|
| **ABC Powder** | Monoammonium Phosphate (NH₄H₂PO₄) | A, B, C | **Red** (body) |
| **BC Powder (Standard)** | Sodium Bicarbonate (NaHCO₃) | B, C | **Red** (body) |
| **BC Powder (Super/KB)** | Potassium Bicarbonate (KHCO₃) | B, C | **Red** (body) |
| **D Powder** | Sodium Chloride (NaCl) / Graphite | D (Metals) | **Blue** (body) |
| **Specialty Powders** | Cu/Al based, Talc-graphite | D, specific | **Blue/Grey** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Class A** = Ordinary combustibles (wood, paper, cloth); **Class B** = Flammable liquids; **Class C** = Flammable gases / Electrical; **Class D** = Combustible metals (Mg, Na, Ti).

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Key Metrics

- **Particle size range:** **10 to 75 micrometres (µm)** — critical for flow and surface-area optimization
- **Bulk Density (ABC):** approximately **0.85–1.00 g/cm³**
- **Bulk Density (BC):** approximately **0.95–1.20 g/cm³**
- **Working pressure of stored-pressure cylinders:** typically **10–15 bar (1.0–1.5 MPa)**
- **Working pressure of cartridge-operated systems:** typically **25–40 bar (2.5–4.0 MPa)**
- **Temperature operational range:** **−20 °C to +60 °C**
- **Moisture content (max):** **0.25 % by mass** (per IS 14609)
- **Standard discharge time:** **8 to 60 seconds** depending on extinguisher capacity

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Theory — The Three Pillars of Powder Extinguishment

Powder extinguishes fire via **three simultaneous mechanisms**, all engineered into one agent:

### Pillar 1: Chemical Chain Inhibition (Dominant Mechanism — ~80% effectiveness)

The combustion process is a self-sustaining **free-radical chain reaction**:

$$\text{Fuel} + \text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\Delta} \text{H}^\bullet + \text{OH}^\bullet + \text{O}^\bullet \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{Heat}$$

The active salt in powder (e.g., monoammonium phosphate) **decomposes in the flame zone**, releasing species like **PO•, PO₂•, and HPO₂•** that **bind with H• and OH• radicals**, terminating the chain.

$$\text{NH}_4\text{H}_2\text{PO}_4 \xrightarrow{\Delta > 190°C} \text{H}_3\text{PO}_4 + \text{NH}_3$$

$$\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4 \xrightarrow{\Delta} \text{PO}^\bullet + \text{PO}_2^\bullet + \text{HPO}_2^\bullet + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

These P-containing radicals **recombine with H• and OH•** to form stable species, **killing the flame chemistry**.

### Pillar 2: Smothering / Physical Exclusion

The powder cloud forms a **barrier** between fuel and oxygen, and the **mineral residue** (glass-like coating for ABC) physically seals Class A surfaces, providing **re-ignition resistance** — a key advantage over gases like CO₂.

### Pillar 3: Thermal Cooling

Decomposition of the salts is **endothermic**, drawing heat from the flame:

$$\Delta H_{\text{decomp}}(\text{NH}_4\text{H}_2\text{PO}_4) \approx +85\ \text{kJ/mol}$$

---

## 2.2 Properties That Define a "Good" Powder

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Boards repeatedly test these properties — memorize them!**

1. **Particle Size Distribution (PSD):** Must balance flowability (large particles) and reactivity (small particles). Standard PSD: **< 10% retained on 75 µm sieve**; **> 50% retained on 45 µm sieve**.
2. **Flow Rate:** Must discharge reliably from nozzle; tested per IS 14609 using a standard funnel.
3. **Hydrophobicity:** Coated with **silicone or stearate** to resist caking in humid Indian conditions.
4. **Caking Resistance:** No clumping after storage at **60 °C / 95% RH for 28 days**.
5. **Electrical Conductivity:** Powder must be **non-conductive** when dry (dielectric strength > 5 kV).
6. **Compatibility:** Must not react with standard extinguisher elastomers, brass, or aluminium fittings.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Mass flow rate of powder through orifice | $\dot{m} = C_d \cdot A_o \cdot \sqrt{2 \rho_b \cdot \Delta P}$ | kg/s | Discharge rate design |
| 2 | Discharge time | $t_d = \dfrac{M_{\text{powder}}}{\dot{m}}$ | s | Extinguisher rating |
| 3 | Coverage area per extinguisher | $A_{\text{cov}} = \dfrac{M_{\text{powder}}}{q_{\text{design}}}$ | m² | Class B hazard layout |
| 4 | Nozzle discharge coefficient (powder) | $C_d \approx 0.45$ to $0.65$ | dimensionless | Empirical (IS 15105) |
| 5 | Bulk density of ABC powder | $\rho_b \approx 900$ to $1000$ | kg/m³ | Typical value |
| 6 | Driving pressure (stored pressure) | $P_{\text{working}} \approx 1.0$ to $1.5$ | MPa | Standard cylinder |
| 7 | Driving pressure (cartridge) | $P_{\text{cart}} \approx 2.5$ to $4.0$ | MPa | N₂ cartridge |
| 8 | Thermal decomposition energy | $\Delta H \approx +85$ | kJ/mol | ABC phosphate |
| 9 | Minimum agent per unit area (Class B) | $q \approx 4$ to $6$ | kg/m² | Hydrocarbon pool fires |
| 10 | Minimum agent per unit area (Class A) | $q \approx 5$ to $8$ | kg/m² | Surface / depth-of-penetration |

> **Notation key:** $C_d$ = discharge coefficient, $A_o$ = orifice area, $\rho_b$ = bulk density, $\Delta P$ = pressure differential, $M$ = mass, $\dot{m}$ = mass flow rate, $q$ = design application density.

---

## 2.4 Engineering Utility & Real-World Application

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this matter in industry?**
> - **Oil & Gas Refineries:** BC powder systems protect LPG bullets, transformer yards, loading bays.
> - **Power Plants:** ABC powder protects control rooms, MCCs, and turbine enclosures.
> - **Automotive / Paint Shops:** BC powder suppresses flammable-solvent spray fires without damaging equipment.
> - **Aerospace & Military Hangars:** Specialized powder + foam dual-agent systems for fuel-pit fires.
> - **Server Rooms & Data Centres:** ABC "clean-agent hybrid" where gas-only systems are too expensive — although increasingly replaced by clean agents for electronics.

Powder systems are the **workhorses of industrial fire protection** — they are **cheap, robust, fast-acting, and the only effective portable agent for Class D metal fires**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation A: Mass Flow Rate Through a Powder Nozzle

We derive the governing equation used in **IS 15105** for designing powder discharge nozzles in fixed systems.

### Step 1: Define the system

Consider a pressurized powder cylinder delivering agent through a circular nozzle of area $A_o$. The driving gas (typically dry **N₂**) maintains a gauge pressure $\Delta P$ above ambient.

The powder behaves as a **compressible granular fluid**, and empirical data (verified by IS 15105) supports a Bernoulli-style formula with a discharge coefficient $C_d$.

### Step 2: Write the Bernoulli equation (ideal form)

For an incompressible approximation of the bulk-density powder stream:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta P &= \frac{1}{2}\,\rho_b\,v^2 \\[4pt]
v &= \sqrt{\frac{2\,\Delta P}{\rho_b}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** This is the standard kinetic-energy-per-unit-volume balance; $\rho_b$ is bulk density (kg/m³), $v$ is discharge velocity (m/s).

### Step 3: Introduce discharge coefficient $C_d$

Real granular flow loses energy due to **particle-particle friction** and **non-uniform velocity profiles**. We therefore introduce $C_d$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_{\text{actual}} &= C_d \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2\,\Delta P}{\rho_b}} \\[4pt]
\dot{m} &= \rho_b \cdot A_o \cdot v_{\text{actual}} \\[4pt]
\dot{m} &= C_d \cdot A_o \cdot \sqrt{2\,\rho_b\,\Delta P}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** $C_d$ is empirically **0.45 to 0.65** for powder (versus ~0.97 for water), reflecting the high friction loss.

### Step 4: Practical check — units

- $C_d$ [dimensionless]
- $A_o$ [m²]
- $\rho_b$ [kg/m³]
- $\Delta P$ [Pa = kg/(m·s²)]

$$
\sqrt{\frac{\text{kg}}{\text{m}^3} \cdot \frac{\text{kg}}{\text{m}\cdot\text{s}^2}} = \sqrt{\frac{\text{kg}^2}{\text{m}^4 \cdot \text{s}^2}} = \frac{\text{kg}}{\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{s}}
$$

Multiplied by $A_o$ [m²] → **kg/s** ✓

### Step 5: Worked numerical example

> **Example (KTU-style):** A stored-pressure ABC powder extinguisher has a working pressure of **1.2 MPa**, bulk density **950 kg/m³**, nozzle orifice diameter **8 mm**, and $C_d = 0.55$. Find the mass flow rate.

**Given:** $\Delta P = 1.2 \times 10^6\ \text{Pa}$, $\rho_b = 950\ \text{kg/m}^3$, $d_o = 0.008\ \text{m}$, $C_d = 0.55$.

**Step 5a — Compute orifice area:**

$$
A_o = \frac{\pi}{4}\,d_o^{\,2} = \frac{\pi}{4}\,(0.008)^2 = 5.027 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{m}^2
$$

**Step 5b — Substitute into flow equation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m} &= C_d\,A_o\,\sqrt{2\,\rho_b\,\Delta P} \\[4pt]
\dot{m} &= (0.55)\,(5.027\times 10^{-5})\,\sqrt{2 \times 950 \times 1.2\times 10^6}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5c — Compute the radical:**

$$
2 \times 950 \times 1.2 \times 10^{6} = 2.28 \times 10^{9}
$$

$$
\sqrt{2.28\times 10^{9}} = 4.775\times 10^{4}\ \text{kg}^{0.5}\,/\text{(m}^{0.5}\,\text{s)}
$$

**Step 5d — Final multiplication:**

$$
\dot{m} = (0.55)\,(5.027\times 10^{-5})\,(4.775\times 10^{4}) \approx 1.32\ \text{kg/s}
$$

> ✅ **Mass flow rate ≈ 1.32 kg/s**, which is typical for a small portable ABC extinguisher.

---

## 3.2 Derivation B: Discharge Time and Required Powder Quantity

### Step 1: Definition

Discharge time is the period over which the extinguisher delivers powder at (approximately) constant rate.

$$
t_d = \frac{M_{\text{powder}}}{\dot{m}}
$$

### Step 2: Worked example

For a **6 kg ABC extinguisher** with $\dot{m} = 1.32$ kg/s:

$$
t_d = \frac{6.0}{1.32} \approx 4.55\ \text{s}
$$

This is **below the IS 2190 minimum discharge time of 9 seconds for Class A** and **8 seconds for Class B** — implying a larger nozzle or higher pressure is needed for a 6 kg unit.

### Step 3: Iterate design

For compliance with **IS 2190:2010** (minimum $t_d \geq 9$ s for 6 kg ABC):

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{required}} \leq \frac{6.0}{9.0} = 0.667\ \text{kg/s}
$$

Design adjustment → reduce orifice to **6 mm** or lower pressure — engineering trade-off discussed in fixed-system design.

---

## 3.3 Derivation C: Coverage Area for Class B (Liquid Pool) Fires

### Step 1: Application density

IS 15105 specifies a minimum design application density $q$ in **kg of powder per m² of liquid surface**:

$$
A_{\text{cov}} = \frac{M_{\text{powder}}}{q}
$$

### Step 2: Example

For a **50 kg wheeled ABC extinguisher** protecting a hydrocarbon pool with $q = 5\ \text{kg/m}^2$:

$$
A_{\text{cov}} = \frac{50}{5} = 10\ \text{m}^2
$$

### Step 3: Multi-extinguisher design

For a tank farm of total pool area $A_{\text{total}}$:

$$
N = \left\lceil \frac{A_{\text{total}} \cdot q}{M_{\text{per unit}}} \right\rceil
$$

where $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ denotes ceiling (round up).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Quick Reference for Problem Solving

| Problem Type | Key Equation | Input Data Needed | Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Find discharge rate** | $\dot{m} = C_d A_o \sqrt{2 \rho_b \Delta P}$ | $C_d, A_o, \rho_b, \Delta P$ | kg/s |
| **Find discharge time** | $t_d = M / \dot{m}$ | $M, \dot{m}$ | seconds |
| **Check IS compliance** | $t_d \geq t_{\text{IS spec}}$ | $M, \dot{m}$, IS table | Pass/Fail |
| **Find coverage area** | $A_{\text{cov}} = M / q$ | $M$, hazard class | m² |
| **Find number of units** | $N = \lceil A_{\text{total}} q / M \rceil$ | $A_{\text{total}}, q, M$ | integer count |
| **Pipe pressure drop** | $\Delta P_{\text{friction}} = K \cdot \dfrac{\dot{m}^2}{\rho_b \, D^5}$ | $\dot{m}, \rho_b, D, L, K$ | Pa |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Stored-Pressure Powder System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Powder Cylinder Body] --> B[ABC Dry Chemical Powder]
    A --> C[Stored Nitrogen Propellant Gas 10-15 bar]
    C --> D[Pressure Regulator and Safety Burst Disc]
    D --> E[Discharge Hose Assembly]
    E --> F[Siphon Tube]
    B --> F
    F --> G[Control Valve and Lever]
    G --> H[Discharge Nozzle]
    H --> I[Powder Cloud Formation Zone]
    I --> J[Fire Zone Class A B C]
    J --> K[Chain Reaction Inhibition by PO Radicals]
    J --> L[Smothering by Mineral Residue Layer]
    J --> M[Endothermic Cooling by Salt Decomposition]
    K --> N[Fire Extinguished]
    L --> N
    M --> N
    N --> O[Re Ignition Shield on Class A Surface]
    O --> P[Post Fire Inspection Required]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **left branch** (propellant) drives the powder from the cylinder.
- The **right branch** (chemical action) shows the three extinguishment pillars.
- The **bottom branch** (post-fire action) reminds engineers that powder residue cleanup and re-charging are mandatory.

---

## 4.2 Powder Classification Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S{Fire Hazard Identification}
    S --> A[Class A Ordinary Combustibles Wood Paper Cloth]
    S --> B[Class B Flammable Liquids Oils Solvents]
    S --> C[Class C Flammable Gases or Energized Electrical]
    S --> D[Class D Combustible Metals Mg Na Ti]

    A --> X1[Use ABC Powder NH4H2PO4 base]
    B --> X2[Use BC or ABC Powder]
    C --> X3[Use BC or ABC Powder Non Conductive when dry]
    D --> X4[Use Specialized D Powder NaCl or Graphite]

    X1 --> Y1[Provides surface sealing and re ignition resistance]
    X2 --> Y2[Highest flame knock down speed]
    X3 --> Y3[Safe on live electrical up to 1000 V at 1 m]
    X4 --> Y4[Application technique differs gentle layering not blast]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Important pitfall:** Class D powder must be applied as a **thick blanket (≥ 25 mm)** using a **soft-spray nozzle** — not a high-velocity blast — to avoid scattering burning metal.

---

## 4.3 Process Flow — Powder Manufacturing (IS 14609 Outline)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Raw Salt Selection NH4H2PO4 etc] --> P2[Pre Crushing and Sieving]
    P2 --> P3[Ball Milling to Target PSD 10 to 75 micron]
    P3 --> P4[Hydrophobic Coating Silicone or Stearate]
    P4 --> P5[Anti Caking Additive Mica Talc Silica]
    P5 --> P6[Quality Testing PSD Flow Moisture Caking]
    P6 --> P7{Pass IS 14609 Specification}
    P7 -->|Yes| P8[Approved Batch to Filling Line]
    P7 -->|No| P9[Reject Batch Regrind]
    P8 --> P10[Filling into Extinguisher Cylinders]
    P10 --> P11[Pressurization with Dry Nitrogen]
    P11 --> P12[Final Leak Test and Dispatch]
```

---

## 4.4 Powder Discharge Performance Curve (Schematic)

```mermaid
xychart-beta
    title "Typical Powder Discharge Rate vs Cylinder Pressure"
    x-axis "Gauge Pressure (bar)" [5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 35, 40]
    y-axis "Mass Flow Rate (kg/s)" 0 --> 4
    line [0.65, 0.95, 1.15, 1.85, 2.30, 2.70, 3.05, 3.35]
```

> **Reading the chart:**
> - Below ~8 bar, flow is **inadequate** (powder "chokes").
> - 12–18 bar is the **sweet spot** for stored-pressure portable extinguishers.
> - Cartridge systems (25–40 bar) deliver 2–3× higher flow, suited to **large wheeled units and fixed systems**.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**"List any three fire classes on which ABC powder is effective and explain why it is the most versatile dry chemical agent."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

ABC powder is effective on:
1. **Class A** (ordinary combustibles like wood, paper, cloth) — because monoammonium phosphate decomposes to form a **glass-like mineral residue** that seals the fuel surface and prevents re-ignition. *(1 mark)*
2. **Class B** (flammable liquids) — because the powder cloud rapidly knocks down flames via **free-radical chain inhibition** by PO/PO₂ radicals. *(1 mark)*
3. **Class C** (energized electrical) — because dry powder is **non-conductive** (dielectric strength > 5 kV), making it safe on live equipment up to 1000 V at 1 m. *(1 mark)*

Hence ABC is the **most versatile** single agent, covering three major hazard classes in one extinguisher.

---

### Question 2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**"Why is particle size distribution (PSD) critical in fire-extinguishing powders? Mention the standard PSD range as per IS 14609."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

PSD is critical because it controls the **trade-off between flowability and reactivity**. *(1 mark)*
- **Particles too large (>75 µm):** Settle too fast, poor surface coverage, low chemical reactivity.
- **Particles too fine (<10 µm):** Agglomerate, clog nozzle, absorb moisture, cake.

IS 14609 specifies: **> 50% retained on 45 µm sieve, < 10% retained on 75 µm sieve**. *(2 marks)*

---

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> Choose **ONE** of the following two questions.

---

### ✦ Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **three mechanisms** by which dry chemical powder extinguishes a fire. Use the decomposition of monoammonium phosphate as your illustrative example. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A stored-pressure ABC powder extinguisher has the following specifications:
- Cylinder working pressure: **1.4 MPa**
- Bulk density of powder: **960 kg/m³**
- Orifice diameter: **10 mm**
- Discharge coefficient: **C_d = 0.55**
- Powder charge: **6 kg**

Calculate:
(i) The mass flow rate of powder. **(3 marks)**
(ii) The discharge time. **(2 marks)**
(iii) Check whether the extinguisher meets IS 2190 minimum discharge time of **9 seconds** for a 6 kg unit. **(2 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Three Mechanisms (7 marks):**

**Mechanism 1 — Chemical Chain Inhibition (3 marks):**
