Combustion is sustained by free radicals **H•, OH•, O•**. ABC powder releases **PO/PO₂ radicals** from decomposition of monoammonium phosphate:

$$
\text{NH}_4\text{H}_2\text{PO}_4 \xrightarrow{\Delta > 190°C} \text{H}_3\text{PO}_4 + \text{NH}_3
$$

$$
\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4 \xrightarrow{\Delta} \text{PO}^\bullet + \text{PO}_2^\bullet + \text{HPO}_2^\bullet
$$

These P-radicals **recombine with H• and OH•**, terminating the chain reaction. This is the **dominant mechanism (~80% effectiveness)**.

**Mechanism 2 — Smothering / Surface Sealing (2 marks):**

The mineral residue forms a **glass-like coating** on solid fuels (Class A), physically separating fuel from oxygen and providing **re-ignition resistance** that gases like CO₂ cannot.

**Mechanism 3 — Endothermic Cooling (2 marks):**

Decomposition is **endothermic**:

$$
\Delta H \approx +85\ \text{kJ/mol}
$$

Heat drawn from the flame zone reduces flame temperature below auto-ignition threshold.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**(i) Mass flow rate (3 marks):**

$$
A_o = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.010)^2 = 7.854 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{m}^2
$$

$$
\sqrt{2\,\rho_b\,\Delta P} = \sqrt{2 \times 960 \times 1.4\times 10^6} = \sqrt{2.688 \times 10^9} = 5.184 \times 10^4
$$

$$
\dot{m} = (0.55)\,(7.854\times 10^{-5})\,(5.184\times 10^4) \approx 2.24\ \text{kg/s}
$$

**(ii) Discharge time (2 marks):**

$$
t_d = \frac{6.0}{2.24} \approx 2.68\ \text{s}
$$

**(iii) Compliance check (2 marks):**

Required minimum $t_d \geq 9$ s. Actual $t_d \approx 2.68$ s → **FAILS** IS 2190.

**Design correction required:** Reduce orifice to ~6 mm OR reduce working pressure to ~0.2 MPa to achieve compliant discharge time. The engineer must iterate orifice/pressure to meet both **discharge time AND reach distance** specifications.

---

### ✦ Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** Compare **ABC, BC, and D type powders** with respect to their active chemical, fire classes covered, mechanism, and typical applications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A fixed dry-powder suppression system is to protect a **hydrocarbon pool fire of 30 m²** area. The design application density is **5 kg/m²** for Class B. Available extinguisher modules are **25 kg wheeled units** with discharge time of **30 seconds**.

Calculate:
(i) Total powder mass required. **(2 marks)**
(ii) Number of wheeled units needed. **(2 marks)**
(iii) The required mass flow rate per unit to deliver the charge in 30 s, and verify the nozzle size if $C_d = 0.55$, $\rho_b = 950$ kg/m³, $\Delta P = 1.2$ MPa. **(3 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Comparison Table (7 marks):**

| Parameter | ABC Powder | BC Powder | D Powder |
|---|---|---|---|
| Active chemical | Monoammonium phosphate (NH₄H₂PO₄) | Sodium bicarbonate (NaHCO₃) or Potassium bicarbonate (KHCO₃) | Sodium chloride (NaCl) or Graphite |
| Fire classes | A, B, C | B, C | D only |
| Mechanism | Chain inhibition + residue sealing + cooling | Mainly chain inhibition | Smothering + heat absorption |
| Re-ignition resistance | Excellent (Class A glass seal) | Poor | Moderate |
| Application | Offices, vehicles, warehouses | Oil refineries, transformers, paint shops | Magnesium, sodium, titanium machining |
| Colour code | Red (body) | Red (body) | Blue (body) |
| Cost | Higher | Lower | Specialized, expensive |

*(Award 7 marks for full comparative coverage; partial credit for missing comparisons.)*

---

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**(i) Total powder mass (2 marks):**

$$
M_{\text{total}} = A \cdot q = 30 \times 5 = 150\ \text{kg}
$$

**(ii) Number of units (2 marks):**

$$
N = \left\lceil \frac{150}{25} \right\rceil = 6\ \text{units}
$$

**(iii) Mass flow rate per unit (2 marks):**

$$
\dot{m} = \frac{25}{30} \approx 0.833\ \text{kg/s}
$$

**Verify nozzle size (1 mark):**

Rearranging the discharge equation for $A_o$:

$$
A_o = \frac{\dot{m}}{C_d \sqrt{2\,\rho_b\,\Delta P}} = \frac{0.833}{0.55\,\sqrt{2 \times 950 \times 1.2\times 10^6}} = \frac{0.833}{0.55 \times 4.775\times 10^4}
$$

$$
A_o = \frac{0.833}{2.626\times 10^4} \approx 3.17 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{m}^2
$$

$$
d_o = \sqrt{\frac{4 A_o}{\pi}} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 3.17\times 10^{-5}}{\pi}} \approx 6.36\ \text{mm}
$$

**Conclusion:** A **6–7 mm orifice** is suitable for the wheeled unit.

---

> [!WARNING]
> ### ⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls
> 1. **Forgetting to convert MPa → Pa** in the discharge equation — loses 1 mark immediately.
> 2. **Using $C_d \approx 1.0$ (water value) for powder** — examiner deducts 1–2 marks; powder $C_d \in [0.45, 0.65]$.
> 3. **Mixing up Class A vs Class B application densities** — Class A uses 5–8 kg/m², Class B uses 4–6 kg/m².
> 4. **Ignoring the IS 2190 minimum discharge time** requirement (9 s for ≤6 kg, 15 s for >6 kg).
> 5. **Wrong units for bulk density** — kg/m³ in SI, not g/cm³ (without conversion).
> 6. **Confusing stored-pressure vs cartridge-operated working pressures** — these are 1.0–1.5 MPa vs 2.5–4.0 MPa.
> 7. **Forgetting the ceiling function** when computing number of extinguishers — partial credit only.
> 8. **Writing "PO" instead of "PO• / PO₂•"** as radicals — minor deduction but shows conceptual weakness.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — "Powders"**

- ⭐ **Powders are the MOST VERSATILE** fire agents — covering A, B, C, D (with separate D powder).
- ⭐ **Active ingredients:** ABC → Monoammonium phosphate; BC → Sodium/Potassium bicarbonate; D → NaCl/Graphite.
- ⭐ **Dominant mechanism:** Chemical chain inhibition (~80%); secondary = smothering + cooling.
- ⭐ **PSD standard:** > 50% on 45 µm sieve, < 10% on 75 µm sieve (per IS 14609).
- ⭐ **Bulk density (ABC):** 900–1000 kg/m³.
- ⭐ **Working pressure:** Stored = 1.0–1.5 MPa; Cartridge = 2.5–4.0 MPa.
- ⭐ **Discharge coefficient for powder:** $C_d \approx 0.45$–$0.65$ (NOT 1.0).
- ⭐ **Discharge time (IS 2190):** ≥ 9 s for ≤ 6 kg unit; ≥ 15 s for 6–25 kg; ≥ 30 s for wheeled.
- ⭐ **Application density:** Class B ≈ 4–6 kg/m²; Class A ≈ 5–8 kg/m².
- ⭐ **Particle size = 10–75 µm** — controls flow AND reactivity.
- ⭐ **Decomposition of NH₄H₂PO₄** releases PO/PO₂ radicals that bind H• and OH• — KILL THE FLAME.
- ⭐ **Re-ignition resistance** is the unique ABC advantage for Class A.
- ⭐ **D powder must be applied as a SOFT BLANKET** — never blast.
- ⭐ **Non-conductive** when dry → safe on live electrical.
- ⭐ **Standards to memorize:** IS 14609 (powder spec), IS 15105 (systems), IS 2190 (extinguishers), NFPA 17 (systems).
- ⭐ **Key equation:** $\dot{m} = C_d A_o \sqrt{2\rho_b \Delta P}$ — practice it 5 times before the exam.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Foams
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT836 - DESIGN OF FIRE PROTECTION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Foams

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Firefighting Foams

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Firefighting Foam (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):** A firefighting foam is an **aggregated mass of air-filled bubbles** formed by the mechanical agitation of a mixture of **water, foam concentrate (foam compound), and air** in definite proportions. It has a **density lower than that of water, oil, and most flammable liquids**, which enables it to float on the surface of hydrocarbon fuels and form a continuous, cohesive blanket that **smothers, cools, and suppresses flammable vapours** during a fire incident.

A foam blanket performs **three simultaneous fire-extinguishing mechanisms**:

1. **Smothering (Exclusion of Oxygen):** The foam blanket physically blocks atmospheric oxygen ($O_2$) from reaching the fuel surface.
2. **Cooling:** The water content of the foam bubbles absorbs heat via vaporisation. The latent heat of vaporisation of water is **$2,260 \text{ kJ/kg}$**.
3. **Fuel-Vapour Suppression:** The aqueous film (in AFFF type) seals the fuel surface and prevents the re-ignition of vapours.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Foams are classified as the **primary extinguishing medium for Class B fires** (flammable liquids) in the KTU 2024 fire protection design curriculum. They are also used for **Class A fires** (ordinary combustibles) at low expansion.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **bowl of soapy water bubbles** floating gently on the surface of a swimming pool. Now picture the same bubble mass, but instead of air, each bubble is filled with a fire-suppressing vapour, and the liquid being protected is not water but **kerosene in a bulk storage tank**. The bubbles cling together to form a single floating "lid". This floating lid is exactly what firefighting foam is.

> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of foam as a **fire blanket made of millions of tiny bubbles**, similar to a thick layer of shaving cream or the foam on top of a freshly poured cappuccino. When poured over a burning oil pan, this bubble blanket immediately:
> - **Covers the fire** (like a lid on a candle),
> - **Cools it down** (the water in the bubbles evaporates), and
> - **Stops the vapours** from coming out (like sealing a jar).

The key intuition is that the foam is **lighter than the burning liquid**, so it **floats on top**, automatically sealing the fire without any human intervention. This is why foam is the **gold standard** for oil tank fires, petrochemical storage yards, aircraft hangars, and refineries.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (KTU High-Yield)

The following physical constants and standard metrics are mandatory for KTU 2024 board examination numericals:

| Parameter | Value / Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Density of water | $\rho_w = 1,000 \text{ kg/m}^3$ or $1 \text{ kg/L}$ | Base metric for foam solution density |
| Latent heat of vaporisation of water | $L_v = 2,260 \text{ kJ/kg}$ | Heat absorbed during foam water phase change |
| Specific heat of water | $c_w = 4.186 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$ | Used in cooling calculations |
| Specific heat of foam (typical) | $c_f \approx 3.5 - 4.0 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$ | Depends on foam type |
| Expansion Ratio (ER) | $3:1$ to $1000:1$ | Low/Medium/High expansion |
| Standard application density | $4.1 \text{ L/min/m}^2$ to $6.5 \text{ L/min/m}^2$ | NFPA 11 for hydrocarbon spills |
| Proportioning rate (typical) | $1\%, 3\%, 6\%$ | Concentrate-to-water ratio |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Concept:** Foam is **NOT a chemical extinguishing agent** like $CO_2$ or dry chemical powder. It is a **physical extinguishing medium** that works primarily through **smothering and cooling**.

---

## 1.4 Why Foams? (Engineering Justification)

Foams are preferred over water for flammable liquid fires because:

- Water **sinks** below the burning hydrocarbon (since oil density $\approx 800 \text{ kg/m}^3$ < water density) and may even **spread the burning liquid**, causing catastrophic boil-over.
- Foam **floats** on the fuel, forming a stable, persistent barrier.
- Foam is **self-healing** — when disturbed, it re-forms to seal any break in the blanket.
- Foam is **electrically non-conductive** (when properly proportioned), making it safer near electrical hazards than water jets.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favourite:** In the KTU 2024 scheme, examiners frequently test the **three-fold mechanism of foam extinguishment** and the **limitations of water on Class B fires** as a 3-mark short answer.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Composition of Firefighting Foam

A firefighting foam is formed by combining **three essential elements** in carefully controlled proportions:

| Element | Typical Proportion | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Water** | $\approx 94\% - 99\%$ | Carrier liquid; provides cooling via latent heat of vaporisation |
| **Foam Concentrate** | $1\% - 6\%$ (by volume of water) | Surfactant that reduces surface tension and stabilises the bubble |
| **Air** | Atmospheric / inducted | Inflates the foam solution to form bubbles |

### Step-by-Step Foam Generation Process (Bulleted Logic)

1. **Water is pressurised** through a fire pump and delivered to a proportioner.
2. **Foam concentrate is injected** at a precise ratio (typically **$1\%$, $3\%$, or $6\%$**) into the water stream at the proportioner.
3. The resulting **foam solution** (water + concentrate) is then passed through a **foam-making device** (e.g., aspirating nozzle, foam chamber, or in-line eductor).
4. Inside the foam device, the solution is **mechanically agitated with air** to produce a homogeneous, stable foam.
5. The finished foam is **discharged onto the fire surface**, where it spreads, forms a blanket, and extinguishes the fire.

> **"Why" and "How" Explanation:** The foam concentrate contains **surfactants** (surface-active agents) that reduce the surface tension of water from $\approx 72 \text{ mN/m}$ down to $\approx 15-20 \text{ mN/m}$. This allows the water to spread out as a thin film and form stable, long-lasting bubbles. Without the surfactant, the bubbles would rapidly collapse under the influence of gravity and heat.

---

## 2.2 Classification of Firefighting Foams (KTU 2024 Taxonomy)

The KTU 2024 syllabus requires a thorough understanding of the following foam families:

### 2.2.1 Protein Foam (P)

- **Base Material:** Hydrolysed animal protein (typically from hooves, horns, fish scales).
- **Mechanism:** Forms a thick, cohesive blanket with good burn-back resistance.
- **Expansion Ratio:** Low to medium ($6:1$ to $10:1$).
- **Application:** Hydrocarbon fuel fires (gasoline, diesel, kerosene).
- **Limitation:** Poor fluidity; cannot be used on polar solvents (alcohols, ketones).

### 2.2.2 Fluoroprotein Foam (FP)

- **Modification:** Protein foam + fluorinated surfactants.
- **Advantage:** Improved fuel tolerance, faster knockdown, and better compatibility with dry chemical agents.

### 2.2.3 Aqueous Film-Forming Foam (AFFF)

- **Base Material:** Synthetic fluorosurfactants (PFAS-based historically).
- **Mechanism:** Spreads an **aqueous film** on the fuel surface with surface tension $\approx 15-18 \text{ mN/m}$, lower than most hydrocarbon fuels ($\approx 24-28 \text{ mN/m}$).
- **Expansion Ratio:** Low to medium ($6:1$ to $10:1$).
- **Application:** Fast knockdown of hydrocarbon pool and jet fires.
- **Note:** Modern formulations are shifting toward **F3 (fluorine-free) foams** due to environmental concerns.

### 2.2.4 Alcohol-Resistant AFFF (AR-AFFF)

- **Mechanism:** Contains a **polysaccharide polymer** that forms a protective membrane when it contacts polar solvents.
- **Application:** Fires involving alcohols, ketones, esters, and other water-miscible fuels.

### 2.2.5 Film-Forming Fluoroprotein Foam (FFFP)

- **Combines:** Fluoroprotein film formation with protein foam stability.
- **Application:** Rapid knockdown with excellent post-extinguishment security.

### 2.2.6 Class A Foam

- **Designed for:** Class A fires (wood, paper, cloth, vegetation).
- **Concentration:** $0.1\% - 1\%$.
- **Mechanism:** Reduces water surface tension, allowing better penetration into porous solids.

### 2.2.7 High-, Medium-, and Low-Expansion Foams

| Type | Expansion Ratio | Primary Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Low-Expansion** | $3:1$ to $20:1$ | Hydrocarbon pool fires, spill fires |
| **Medium-Expansion** | $20:1$ to $200:1$ | Flammable liquid fires in pits, dike areas |
| **High-Expansion** | $200:1$ to $1000:1$ | Volume-filling for enclosed spaces (basements, aircraft hangars) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Favourite Question:** *"Compare protein foam and AFFF in terms of mechanism, expansion ratio, and application."* This is a classic 7-mark Part B sub-question.

---

## 2.3 Key Foam Properties (Critical for Numerical Problems)

| Property | Definition | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|
| **Expansion Ratio (ER)** | $\text{ER} = \frac{\text{Volume of finished foam}}{\text{Volume of foam solution consumed}}$ | $3:1$ to $1000:1$ |
| **25% Drainage Time** | Time for $25\%$ of the foam solution to drain out of the foam mass | $3 - 30$ minutes (varies with type) |
| **Application Density** | Foam solution rate per unit area of fire | $4.1 \text{ L/min/m}^2$ (NFPA 11) |
| **Burn-back Resistance** | Ability to resist re-ignition from a localised heat source | Tested via standard test fires |
| **Viscosity** | Affects proportioning and flow rate | $5 - 50 \text{ cP}$ at $20°\text{C}$ |

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Markdown Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **every KTU 2024 board-relevant formula** for foam design. Memorise these symbols and units — they appear in **$90\%$ of numerical problems**.

| S.No. | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{ER} = \dfrac{V_{\text{foam}}}{V_{\text{solution}}}$ | Expansion Ratio | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $V_{\text{solution}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{foam}}}{\text{ER}}$ | Foam Solution Volume | Litres (L) |
| 3 | $V_{\text{concentrate}} = V_{\text{solution}} \times \dfrac{P_r}{100}$ | Foam Concentrate Volume | Litres (L) |
| 4 | $V_{\text{water}} = V_{\text{solution}} - V_{\text{concentrate}}$ | Water Volume | Litres (L) |
| 5 | $Q_{\text{solution}} = A \times q$ | Solution Flow Rate | L/min |
| 6 | $Q_{\text{concentrate}} = Q_{\text{solution}} \times \dfrac{P_r}{100}$ | Concentrate Flow Rate | L/min |
| 7 | $t = \dfrac{V_{\text{concentrate,total}}}{Q_{\text{concentrate}}}$ | Discharge / Application Time | minutes (min) |
| 8 | $V_{\text{concentrate,total}} = A \times q \times t \times \dfrac{P_r}{100}$ | Total Concentrate Required | Litres (L) |
| 9 | $Q = m \cdot c \cdot \Delta T$ | Heat Absorbed by Foam (Cooling) | kJ |
| 10 | $\dot{Q}_{\text{cooling}} = \dot{m}_w \cdot L_v$ | Rate of Heat Absorption by Vaporisation | kW |
| 11 | $\dot{m}_w = \rho_w \cdot \dot{V}_w$ | Mass Flow Rate of Water | kg/s |
| 12 | $\Delta P_{\text{eductor}} \geq 2.5 \, \Delta P_{\text{nozzle}}$ | Eductor Pressure Drop Rule | bar / kPa |

> **Notation Key:**
> - $V_{\text{foam}}$ = volume of finished foam (L)
> - $V_{\text{solution}}$ = volume of foam solution (L)
> - $V_{\text{concentrate}}$ = volume of foam concentrate (L)
> - $V_{\text{water}}$ = volume of plain water (L)
> - $P_r$ = Proportioning rate ($\%$) — typically $1\%$, $3\%$, or $6\%$
> - $A$ = Fire area or hazard area (m²)
> - $q$ = Application density (L/min/m²)
> - $t$ = Application time / duration (min)
> - $L_v$ = Latent heat of vaporisation of water = $2,260 \text{ kJ/kg}$
> - $m$ = Mass of foam solution (kg)
> - $c$ = Specific heat capacity (kJ/(kg·K))
> - $\Delta T$ = Temperature rise (K or °C)

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Foam systems are designed and installed in:

1. **Petrochemical storage tank farms** (fixed foam pourers, foam chambers).
2. **Refineries and process plants** (subsurface and semi-subsurface injection systems).
3. **Aircraft hangars** (high-expansion foam flooding).
4. **Truck loading bays and marine terminals** (medium-expansion foam monitors).
5. **Warehouses storing flammable liquids** (low-expansion foam sprinkler systems).
6. **Helidecks and offshore platforms** (foam monitors + water spray).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Foam design calculations under KTU 2024 follow **NFPA 11** (Standard for Low-, Medium-, and High-Expansion Foam) and **IS 15519** (Indian Standard for foam fire extinguishing systems). KTU students should be familiar with both.

---

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anti-Truncation Mandate:** Every algebraic transition is written explicitly. **No step is skipped.** This is the level of detail expected in KTU 2024 board examination full-mark solutions.

---

## 3.1 Derivation: Relationship Between Foam Volume, Solution Volume, and Expansion Ratio

### Statement:
Prove that the volume of foam solution required to produce a given volume of finished foam is equal to the finished foam volume divided by the expansion ratio.

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

Let:
- $V_f$ = Volume of finished foam (litres)
- $V_s$ = Volume of foam solution consumed (litres)
- $\text{ER}$ = Expansion Ratio (dimensionless)

By the **definition** of expansion ratio, it is the ratio of the finished foam volume to the foam solution volume:

$$\text{ER} = \frac{V_f}{V_s}$$

**Step 1:** Cross-multiply to obtain:

$$V_f = \text{ER} \times V_s$$

**Step 2:** Rearrange to solve for $V_s$:

$$V_s = \frac{V_f}{\text{ER}}$$

**Step 3:** This is the **fundamental relationship** used in all KTU foam design calculations. The water and concentrate together constitute the foam solution.

> **Conversion Logic Explanation:** The expansion ratio tells us how much a given volume of solution is "puffed up" by the inducted air. For example, a 1 L of solution at ER = $8:1$ produces 8 L of finished foam. Therefore, to produce 8,000 L of finished foam at ER = $8:1$, we need $8,000 / 8 = 1,000$ L of foam solution.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Total Foam Concentrate Required for a Given Fire Scenario

### Statement:
Derive the expression for the **total volume of foam concentrate** required to extinguish a hydrocarbon fire covering an area $A$ for a duration $t$ at a given application density $q$ and proportioning rate $P_r$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1:** The application density $q$ is defined as the **volume flow rate of foam solution per unit fire area**:

$$q = \frac{Q_s}{A}$$

where $Q_s$ is the solution flow rate in L/min and $A$ is in m².

**Step 2:** Solve for the solution flow rate:

$$Q_s = q \times A$$

**Step 3:** The concentrate flow rate is the solution flow rate multiplied by the proportioning ratio:

$$Q_c = Q_s \times \frac{P_r}{100} = q \times A \times \frac{P_r}{100}$$

**Step 4:** The total concentrate volume required for the application duration $t$ (in minutes) is:

$$V_{c,\text{total}} = Q_c \times t = q \times A \times \frac{P_r}{100} \times t$$

**Step 5:** The total water volume required is the total solution volume minus the concentrate volume:

$$V_{w,\text{total}} = Q_s \times t - V_{c,\text{total}} = q \times A \times t - q \times A \times \frac{P_r}{100} \times t$$

$$V_{w,\text{total}} = q \times A \times t \times \left(1 - \frac{P_r}{100}\right)$$

> **Conversion Logic Explanation:** At a $3\%$ proportioning rate, every 100 L of foam solution contains 3 L of concentrate and 97 L of water. This is why $V_w$ = $V_s \times (1 - P_r/100)$.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Solved KTU-Style Numerical Examples

### Worked Example 1: Basic Foam Solution Calculation

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Pattern]**
> A hydrocarbon pool fire is to be extinguished using a foam system with an expansion ratio of $8:1$. The system is designed to deliver $24,000 \text{ L}$ of finished foam. Calculate the volume of foam solution required.

**Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- $V_f = 24,000 \text{ L}$
- $\text{ER} = 8:1$

**Step 1:** Apply the fundamental relation:

$$V_s = \frac{V_f}{\text{ER}}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute values:

$$V_s = \frac{24,000}{8}$$

$$\boxed{V_s = 3,000 \text{ L}}$$

**Step 3:** If the proportioning rate is $3\%$, the concentrate volume is:

$$V_c = V_s \times \frac{P_r}{100} = 3,000 \times 0.03 = 90 \text{ L}$$

**Step 4:** The water volume is:

$$V_w = V_s - V_c = 3,000 - 90 = 2,910 \text{ L}$$

---

### Worked Example 2: Full Foam System Design

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Pattern]**
> A foam pourer system is to be designed for a circular hydrocarbon storage tank of diameter $20 \text{ m}$. The system uses AFFF with expansion ratio $7:1$, proportioning rate $3\%$, and application density $6.5 \text{ L/min/m}^2$. The application time is $55$ minutes. Calculate: (a) Total foam solution required, (b) Total foam concentrate required, (c) Total water required, (d) Total finished foam volume produced.

**Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- Tank diameter $D = 20 \text{ m}$
- Expansion ratio $\text{ER} = 7:1$
- Proportioning rate $P_r = 3\%$
- Application density $q = 6.5 \text{ L/min/m}^2$
- Application time $t = 55 \text{ min}$

**Part (a): Total Foam Solution Required**

**Step 1:** Calculate the fire area (tank cross-sectional area):

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4} \times D^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \times (20)^2$$

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 400 = 100\pi \approx 314.159 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2:** Calculate the solution flow rate:

$$Q_s = q \times A = 6.5 \times 314.159$$

$$Q_s = 2,042.04 \text{ L/min}$$

**Step 3:** Calculate total solution volume for 55 minutes:

$$V_{s,\text{total}} = Q_s \times t = 2,042.04 \times 55$$

$$\boxed{V_{s,\text{total}} = 112,312.2 \text{ L} \approx 112.31 \text{ m}^3}$$

**Part (b): Total Foam Concentrate Required**

**Step 4:** Apply the proportioning formula:

$$V_{c,\text{total}} = V_{s,\text{total}} \times \frac{P_r}{100}$$

$$V_{c,\text{total}} = 112,312.2 \times 0.03$$

$$\boxed{V_{c,\text{total}} = 3,369.37 \text{ L} \approx 3.37 \text{ m}^3}$$

**Part (c): Total Water Required**

**Step 5:** Apply the water balance:

$$V_{w,\text{total}} = V_{s,\text{total}} - V_{c,\text{total}}$$

$$V_{w,\text{total}} = 112,312.2 - 3,369.37$$

$$\boxed{V_{w,\text{total}} = 108,942.83 \text{ L} \approx 108.94 \text{ m}^3}$$

**Part (d): Total Finished Foam Volume**

**Step 6:** Apply the expansion ratio:

$$V_{f,\text{total}} = V_{s,\text{total}} \times \text{ER}$$

$$V_{f,\text{total}} = 112,312.2 \times 7$$

$$\boxed{V_{f,\text{total}} = 786,185.4 \text{ L} \approx 786.19 \text{ m}^3}$$

---

### Worked Example 3: Heat Absorption (Cooling) Calculation

> **[KTU University Exam - June 2024 Pattern]**
> A foam system delivers $100 \text{ kg}$ of foam solution per second onto a fire. Calculate the rate of heat absorption by the water content, assuming $70\%$ of the water evaporates. Take $L_v = 2,260 \text{ kJ/kg}$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Mass flow rate of water (assume solution is essentially water):

$$\dot{m}_w = 100 \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step 2:** Effective mass flow rate of water that evaporates (70\%):

$$\dot{m}_{\text{evap}} = 0.70 \times 100 = 70 \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step 3:** Apply the latent heat equation:

$$\dot{Q} = \dot{m}_{\text{evap}} \times L_v = 70 \times 2,260$$

$$\boxed{\dot{Q} = 158,200 \text{ kW} = 158.2 \text{ MW}}$$

> **Conversion Logic Explanation:** The foam solution is $\approx 97\%$ water at $3\%$ proportioning, so we treat it as water for thermal calculations. The evaporation fraction is the fraction of the water content that actually vaporises to absorb heat.

---

## 3.4 Application Density Reference Matrix (NFPA 11 / IS 15519)

| Hazard Type | Application Density (L/min/m²) | Application Time (min) |
|---|---|---|
| Hydrocarbon spill (low-expansion) | $4.1$ | $55$ |
| Hydrocarbon spill (medium-expansion) | $6.5$ | $55$ |
| Hydrocarbon spill (high-expansion) | $6.5$ | $55$ |
| Polar solvent spill (AR-AFFF) | $6.5$ | $55$ |
| Aircraft hangar (high-expansion) | $5.0 - 6.5$ | $10 - 25$ |
| Fixed roof tank (subsurface) | $4.1$ | $55 - 65$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Pro Tip for KTU Board Exams:** Memorise the **$4.1 \text{ L/min/m}^2$ for $55 \text{ min}$** standard for hydrocarbon spills. It appears in nearly every numerical problem.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
> *"Define firefighting foam and list the three essential elements required for its formation."*

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Allocation: 1 + 2):**

A firefighting foam is an **aggregated mass of air-filled bubbles** formed by the mechanical mixing of a foam solution with air. It has a density lower than most flammable liquids, allowing it to **float on the fuel surface** and form a smothering blanket.

The three essential elements required for foam formation are:
1. **Water** — acts as the carrier fluid and provides the cooling effect.
2. **Foam Concentrate** — a surfactant-based liquid that reduces surface tension and stabilises bubbles.
3. **Air** — entrained by the foam-making device to inflate the solution into a foam mass.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
> *"State the three extinguishing mechanisms of firefighting foam with brief explanations."*

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Allocation: 1 + 1 + 1):**

1. **Smothering:** The foam blanket physically **excludes atmospheric oxygen** from reaching the burning fuel surface, interrupting the combustion triangle.
2. **Cooling:** The water content of the foam absorbs heat as it evaporates. The latent heat of vaporisation is **$2,260 \text{ kJ/kg}$**, which removes substantial thermal energy from the fire.
3. **Fuel-Vapour Suppression:** The foam blanket and, in the case of AFFF, the **aqueous film** seal the fuel surface and prevent the release of flammable vapours, thereby preventing re-ignition.

---

## PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** with two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. Students answer **ONE** of the two alternative questions provided below.

---

### ❖ Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat labelled block diagram, explain the **working of a foam fire protection system** for a hydrocarbon storage tank, listing all major components. Mention the role of the foam proportioner. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A foam pourer system is to be designed for a **square hydrocarbon storage tank** of side $15 \text{ m}$. The system uses AFFF with an **expansion ratio of $8:1$**, a **proportioning rate of $3\%$**, an **application density of $6.5 \text{ L/min/m}^2$**, and an **application time of $55$ minutes**. Calculate:
- (i) Total foam solution required
- (ii) Total foam concentrate required
- (iii) Total water required
- (iv) Total finished foam volume **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A:

**(a) Block Diagram & Working Explanation (7 Marks):**

**Block Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    D1[Water Tank] --> D2[Fire Pump]
    D2 --> D3[Proportioner]
    D4[Concentrate Tank] --> D3
    D3 --> D5[Solution Manifold]
    D5 --> D6[Foam Pourer on Tank]
    AR[Air] --> D6
    D6 --> D7[Foam Blanket on Fuel]
    D7 --> D8[Fire Suppression]

    classDef src fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000000
    classDef eq fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107,color:#000000
    classDef pr fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000000
    classDef res fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,color:#000000

    class D1,D4,AR src
    class D2,D3,D6 eq
    class D5,D7 pr
    class D8 res
```

**Working Explanation (Sub-part marks):**

1. **Water from a dedicated source** is pressurised by a **fire pump** to the required system pressure. *(1 mark)*
2. The pressurised water passes through a **foam proportioner**, which accurately injects foam concentrate at a pre-set ratio (typically $3\%$ or $6\%$). *(1 mark)*
3. The **role of the foam proportioner** is to ensure a precise and constant ratio of concentrate to water, regardless of fluctuations in water flow or pressure. This guarantees the foam quality meets design specifications. *(2 marks)*
4. The resulting **foam solution** is routed through a manifold to the **foam pourers** mounted on the tank shell. *(1 mark)*
5. At the pourer, the solution is mixed with **atmospheric air** through an aspirating device, producing finished foam. *(1 mark)*
6. The finished foam flows down the inside of the tank shell, **spreads over the burning fuel surface**, and forms a continuous blanket that smothers, cools, and suppresses vapours. *(1 mark)*

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks — 1.75 marks each part):**

**Given:**
- Tank side $L = 15 \text{ m}$
- $\text{ER} = 8:1$
- $P_r = 3\%$
- $q = 6.5 \text{ L/min/m}^2$
- $t = 55 \text{ min}$

**Step 1: Calculate Fire Area**

$$A = L^2 = (15)^2 = 225 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2: Calculate Solution Flow Rate**

$$Q_s = q \times A = 6.5 \times 225 = 1,462.5 \text{ L/min}$$

**Step 3: Calculate Total Foam Solution (i)**

$$V_{s,\text{total}} = Q_s \times t = 1,462.5 \times 55 = 80,437.5 \text{ L}$$

$$\boxed{V_{s,\text{total}} = 80,437.5 \text{ L} \approx 80.44 \text{ m}^3}$$

**Step 4: Calculate Total Foam Concentrate (ii)**

$$V_{c,\text{total}} = V_{s,\text{total}} \times \frac{P_r}{100} = 80,437.5 \times 0.03$$

$$\boxed{V_{c,\text{total}} = 2,413.13 \text{ L} \approx 2.41 \text{ m}^3}$$

**Step 5: Calculate Total Water (iii)**

$$V_{w,\text{total}} = V_{s,\text{total}} - V_{c,\text{total}} = 80,437.5 - 2,413.13$$

$$\boxed{V_{w,\text{total}} = 78,024.37 \text{ L} \approx 78.02 \text{ m}^3}$$

**Step 6: Calculate Total Finished Foam (iv)**

$$V_{f,\text{total}} = V_{s,\text{total}} \times \text{ER} = 80,437.5 \times 8$$

$$\boxed{V_{f,\text{total}} = 643,500 \text{ L} = 643.5 \text{ m}^3}$$

---

### ❖ Question B (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024, Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Explain the **classification of firefighting foams** based on expansion ratio. Compare **protein foam and AFFF** in terms of composition, mechanism, and application. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A high-expansion foam system is to be installed in an **aircraft hangar of floor area $1,200 \text{ m}^2$ and average height $12 \text{ m}$**. The system uses foam with **expansion ratio $500:1$** and **proportioning rate $3\%$**. The application density is **$5 \text{ L/min/m}^2$** of foam solution, and the **application time is $20$ minutes**. Calculate:
- (i) Total volume of foam solution required
- (ii) Total volume of foam concentrate required
- (iii) Total volume of finished foam produced
- (iv) Is this finished foam volume sufficient to fill the hangar to a height of $10 \text{ m}$? Show by calculation. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B:

**(a) Classification & Comparison (7 Marks):**

**Classification Based on Expansion Ratio (3 marks):**

| Type | Expansion Ratio | Application |
|---|---|---|
| Low-Expansion Foam | $3:1$ to $20:1$ | Hydrocarbon pool fires, spill fires, fixed pourer systems |
| Medium-Expansion Foam | $20:1$ to $200:1$ | Diked areas, pits, transformer bunds |
| High-Expansion Foam | $200:1$ to $1000:1$ | Volume filling of enclosed spaces (hangars, basements) |

**Comparison of Protein Foam and AFFF (4 marks):**

| Parameter | Protein Foam (P) | Aqueous Film-Forming Foam (AFFF) |
|---|---|---|
| **Composition** | Hydrolysed animal protein (keratin-based) | Synthetic fluorosurfactants + hydrocarbon surfactants |
| **Mechanism** | Forms a thick, cohesive blanket; primary action is smothering | Forms an aqueous film with surface tension $\approx 15-18 \text{ mN/m}$ (lower than fuel), spreading rapidly across the fuel surface |
| **Expansion Ratio** | $6:1$ to $10:1$ (low to medium) | $6:1$ to $10:1$ (low to medium) |
| **Knockdown Speed** | Slow | Fast |
| **Burn-back Resistance** | Excellent | Moderate |
| **Application** | Hydrocarbon fires only | Hydrocarbon fires (AR-AFFF for polar solvents) |

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Given:**
- Floor area $A_f = 1,200 \text{ m}^2$
- Hangar height $H = 12 \text{ m}$
- $\text{ER} = 500:1$
- $P_r = 3\%$
- $q = 5 \text{ L/min/m}^2$
- $t = 20 \text{ min}$

**Step 1: Calculate Total Foam Solution (i)**

$$V_{s,\text{total}} = q \times A_f \times t = 5 \times 1,200 \times 20$$

$$\boxed{V_{s,\text{total}} = 120,000 \text{ L} = 120 \text{ m}^3}$$

**Step 2: Calculate Total Foam Concentrate (ii)**

$$V_{c,\text{total}} = V_{s,\text{total}} \times \frac{P_r}{100} = 120,000 \times 0.03$$

$$\boxed{V_{c,\text{total}} = 3,600 \text{ L} = 3.6 \text{ m}^3}$$

**Step 3: Calculate Total Finished Foam (iii)**

$$V_{f,\text{total}} = V_{s,\text{total}} \times \text{ER} = 120,000 \times 500$$

$$\boxed{V_{f,\text{total}} = 60,000,000 \text{ L} = 60,000 \text{ m}^3}$$

**Step 4: Sufficiency Check (iv)**

Required volume to fill the hangar to $h = 10 \text{ m}$:

$$V_{\text{required}} = A_f \times h = 1,200 \times 10 = 12,000 \text{ m}^3$$

Compare with produced foam:

$$V_{f,\text{total}} = 60,000 \text{ m}^3 \gg 12,000 \text{ m}^3$$

$$\boxed{\text{Yes, the system produces sufficient foam to fill the hangar to 10 m.}}$$

The foam produced ($60,000 \text{ m}^3$) is **5 times** the required volume ($12,000 \text{ m}^3$), confirming a generous design margin. ✓

---

## ⚠️ KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING / PITFALL CALLOUT

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU 2024 Foam Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the 60-second → 1-minute conversion:** Many students multiply application density by time in **seconds** instead of **minutes**, leading to answers that are off by a factor of 60. Always verify units before substituting.
>
> 2. **Confusing Foam Solution with Finished Foam:** Students often use the application density value directly with the expansion ratio, double-multiplying. Remember: **Application density is always based on foam SOLUTION flow rate, not finished foam volume.**
>
> 3. **Incorrect Area Calculation:** For circular tanks, use $A = \pi D^2 / 4$. For square tanks, use $A = L^2$. Do NOT use $A = \pi D^2$.
>
> 4. **Wrong Proportioning Rate:** Forgetting that $3\%$ means $0.03$ (decimal) in the formula, not $3$. A common mark-loser.
>
> 5. **Ignoring Unit Conversions:** $1 \text{ m}^3 = 1,000 \text{ L}$. Always show the conversion explicitly in the final answer.
>
> 6. **No Block Diagram in 7-mark Theory Questions:** A 7-mark theory question on foam systems without a labelled diagram will typically receive a **1-2 mark deduction** at KTU valuation. Always include a functional block diagram.

---

## 📋 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 — Foams (Module 1)**

- ✅ **Definition:** Foam = aggregated mass of air-filled bubbles formed from **water + concentrate + air**.
- ✅ **Three Elements of Foam:** Water, Foam Concentrate, Air.
- ✅ **Three Extinguishing Mechanisms:** Smothering, Cooling, Fuel-Vapour Suppression.
- ✅ **Expansion Ratio (ER):** Ratio of finished foam volume to solution volume; ranges from $3:1$ (low) to $1000:1$ (high).
- ✅ **Three Foam Categories by ER:** Low ($3-20:1$), Medium ($20-200:1$), High ($200-1000:1$).
- ✅ **Protein Foam (P):** Animal protein base, thick blanket, good burn-back, hydrocarbon only.
- ✅ **AFFF:** Synthetic fluorosurfactant, aqueous film ($\approx 15-18 \text{ mN/m}$), fast knockdown.
- ✅ **AR-AFFF:** For polar solvents (alcohols, ketones); uses polysaccharide polymer membrane.
- ✅ **Proportioning Rate:** Typically $1\%$, $3\%$, or $6\%$ concentrate by volume of water.
- ✅ **Application Density (NFPA 11):** $4.1 \text{ L/min/m}^2$ (low-expansion hydrocarbons) to $6.5 \text{ L/min/m}^2$ (medium-expansion).
- ✅ **Standard Application Time:** $55$ minutes for hydrocarbon spills; $10-25$ minutes for high-expansion hangar flooding.
- ✅ **Key Formula — Concentrate:** $V_c = V_s \times P_r/100$.
- ✅ **Key Formula — Finished Foam:** $V_f = V_s \times \text{ER}$.
- ✅ **Key Formula — Solution Flow:** $Q_s = q \times A$.
- ✅ **Key Formula — Total Concentrate:** $V_{c,\text{total}} = q \times A \times t \times P_r/100$.
- ✅ **Latent Heat of Vaporisation:** $L_v = 2,260 \text{ kJ/kg}$ (water).
- ✅ **Proportioning Methods:** In-line inductor, Balanced pressure, Around-the-pump, Bladder tank, Premixed.
- ✅ **Design Standards:** NFPA 11 (USA) and IS 15519 (India).
- ✅ **Limitation:** Foam is **NOT suitable** for Class C (electrical), Class D (metal), and Class K (cooking oil) fires in its standard form.
- ✅ **Modern Trend:** Shift toward **F3 fluorine-free foams** due to PFAS environmental concerns.

> 🎯 **Final KTU 2024 Tip:** For any foam design problem, always start by listing **Given Data**, calculate **Area**, then **Solution Flow Rate**, then **Total Solution Volume**, then **Concentrate Volume**, and finally **Finished Foam Volume**. This systematic sequence is what top-scoring KTU candidates follow.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inert Gases Halons. Need for Halon Replacement and Halon Substitutes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT836 - DESIGN OF FIRE PROTECTION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Inert Gases Halons. Need for Halon Replacement and Halon Substitutes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Definition of Halons (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Halons** are a class of **brominated halogenated hydrocarbons** in which one or more hydrogen atoms of a hydrocarbon molecule have been replaced by **bromine (Br)**, often in combination with other halogens such as **fluorine (F)** and **chlorine (Cl)**. They are short-hand numbered according to the Halon numbering system: the first digit represents the number of carbon atoms, the second the number of fluorine atoms, the third the number of chlorine atoms, and the fourth the number of bromine atoms (with trailing zeros omitted).

> [!NOTE]
> **Halon Numbering Convention**
> A four-digit code **BCFA** where:
> - B = number of carbon atoms
> - C = number of fluorine atoms
> - F = number of chlorine atoms
> - A = number of bromine atoms
> - Hydrogen atoms fill the remaining valencies.

The three commercially significant Halons used in fire protection are:

| Halon Designation | IUPAC Chemical Name | Chemical Formula |
|---|---|---|
| **Halon 1211** | Bromochlorodifluoromethane | $\text{CBrClF}_2$ |
| **Halon 1301** | Bromotrifluoromethane | $\text{CBrF}_3$ |
| **Halon 2402** | Dibromotetrafluoroethane | $\text{C}_2\text{Br}_2\text{F}_4$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Historical Note for KTU Exams:** Halon 1301 was the most widely used total-flooding agent for protecting high-value electronic and aviation assets; Halon 1211 was predominantly used in portable extinguishers; Halon 2402 had limited shipboard and military applications.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a campfire. To put it out, you can either **smother it** (cut off oxygen, like $\text{CO}_2$ or inert gases do) or **break the chain of gossip** running around the fire. Halons work in this second, cleverer way. Combustion is a self-sustaining **chain reaction** of free radicals (especially $\text{OH}^\bullet$, $\text{H}^\bullet$, and $\text{O}^\bullet$). When Halon molecules enter the flame, the **bromine radical ($\text{Br}^\bullet$)** is released and acts like a "gossip-killer," intercepting these radicals and stopping the chain reaction almost instantly.

> [!NOTE]
> A **clean agent** is one that leaves no residue upon discharge, is electrically non-conductive, and does not damage sensitive equipment. Halons were the original "clean agents" and remain the gold standard against which all replacements are benchmarked.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Boiling point of Halon 1301** ≈ **−57.75 °C** (allows storage as a super-pressurized liquefied gas)
- **Vapour density of Halon 1301** ≈ **5.1** (≈ 5.1 times heavier than air at standard conditions)
- **Ozone Depletion Potential (ODP)** of Halon 1301 = **10** (reference scale, where CFC-11 = 1)
- **Global Warming Potential (GWP, 100-yr)** of Halon 1301 = **6,900** (relative to $\text{CO}_2$ = 1)
- **Atmospheric Lifetime** of Halon 1301 ≈ **65 years**
- **Minimum Extinguishing Concentration (MEC)** of Halon 1301 ≈ **5 %** by volume for heptane fires

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These metrics (**ODP, GWP, Atmospheric Lifetime, MEC, NOAEL/LOAEL**) are the **five-metric screening tool** the KTU examiner expects you to memorise and reproduce for any clean-agent comparison question.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Mechanism of Fire Suppression by Halons

Halons suppress fire primarily through a **chemical (kinetic) flame inhibition** mechanism, not by oxygen displacement. The mechanism proceeds in four structured steps:

- **Step 1 — Thermal Decomposition (Pyrolysis):** When the Halon molecule enters the flame front, the relatively weak **C–Br bond** (bond dissociation energy ≈ **234 kJ/mol**) breaks preferentially over the stronger C–F bond (≈ 485 kJ/mol). The bromine radical is liberated:

$$\text{CBrF}_3 \xrightarrow{\Delta} \text{CF}_3^{\bullet} + \text{Br}^{\bullet}$$

- **Step 2 — Radical Scavenging:** The released $\text{Br}^{\bullet}$ is extraordinarily efficient at abstracting hydrogen from fuel radicals, producing **HBr**, which is the actual flame-quenching species:

$$\text{Br}^{\bullet} + \text{RH} \rightarrow \text{HBr} + \text{R}^{\bullet}$$

- **Step 3 — Catalytic Cycle:** HBr then reacts with the highly reactive **hydroxyl radical ($\text{OH}^{\bullet}$)** — the main heat-release carrier in a flame — regenerating $\text{Br}^{\bullet}$ in a self-propagating catalytic cycle:

$$\text{HBr} + \text{OH}^{\bullet} \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{Br}^{\bullet}$$

- **Step 4 — Chain Termination:** The $\text{Br}^{\bullet}$ recombines with other radicals, terminating the chain branching reactions and collapsing the flame tetrahedron (Fuel + Heat + Oxygen + Chain Reaction).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why bromine and not chlorine?** Bromine is roughly **100 times more efficient** than chlorine at catalysing radical recombination. This is why Halons (Br-containing) outperform CFC extinguishing agents despite the same family of chemistry.

### 2.2 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

- **Aviation:** Aircraft cargo bays and engine nacelles (e.g., legacy Boeing/Airbus systems).
- **Telecommunications & Data Centres:** Server rooms, switchgear, and UPS rooms where water damage is unacceptable.
- **Military Ground Vehicles and Submarines:** Where space and weight optimisation are critical.
- **Museums and Archives:** Protection of irreplaceable artefacts.
- **Marine Engine Rooms:** Halon 1301 and Halon 2402 were standard before the Montreal Protocol.

### 2.3 The Need for Halon Replacement

The phase-out was driven by four scientific and regulatory pillars:

- **Pillar 1 — Stratospheric Ozone Depletion:** The bromine atom is ~40–60 times more destructive to stratospheric ozone than chlorine. Halons have an ODP of up to **10**, meaning a single kilogram of Halon 1301 destroys the same ozone as **10 kg of CFC-11**.
- **Pillar 2 — Global Warming Potential:** With a GWP of **6,900** (Halon 1301), a single kilogram released is equivalent to **6,900 kg of $\text{CO}_2$**.
- **Pillar 3 — Long Atmospheric Lifetime:** Halon 1301 persists ~65 years, ensuring that every kilogram released damages the climate for decades.
- **Pillar 4 — Regulatory Mandate:** The **Montreal Protocol (1987)**, signed by 197 countries, mandated the complete phase-out of Halon production for fire protection by **1 January 1994** (developed nations) and 2010 (developing nations).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favourite Question:** *"Explain the environmental reasons for the phase-out of Halons."* — Always cite all four pillars: Ozone Depletion, Global Warming, Atmospheric Lifetime, and Montreal Protocol obligations.

### 2.4 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield Table)

| Parameter | Symbol | Formula / Definition | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Ozone Depletion Potential | $\text{ODP}$ | $\text{ODP}_x = \dfrac{\text{Ozone destroyed per mass of } x}{\text{Ozone destroyed per mass of CFC-11}}$ | Dimensionless |
| Global Warming Potential | $\text{GWP}_x$ | $\displaystyle \text{GWP}_x = \frac{\displaystyle \int_0^{TH} a_x \cdot [x(t)]\, dt}{\displaystyle \int_0^{TH} a_{CO_2} \cdot [CO_2(t)]\, dt}$ | Dimensionless |
| Atmospheric Lifetime | $\tau$ | Time for a pulse emission to decay to $\text{e}^{-1}$ | Years |
| Design Concentration | $C_d$ | $C_d = 1.2 \times \text{MEC}$ (typically) | \% by volume |
| Minimum Extinguishing Concentration | $\text{MEC}$ | Volumetric fraction needed to extinguish | \% by volume |
| Volume Calculation | $V_{agent}$ | $V_{agent} = \dfrac{V_{room} \cdot C_d / (100 - C_d)}{1 + (\mu \cdot T_d)}$ | $\text{m}^3$ or kg |
| NOAEL (No Observed Adverse Effect Level) | $\text{NOAEL}$ | Highest concentration with no toxic effect | \% by volume |
| LOAEL (Lowest Observed Adverse Effect Level) | $\text{LOAEL}$ | Lowest concentration producing toxic effect | \% by volume |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The agent volume formula above is simplified; the full NFPA 2001 expression incorporates temperature, specific volume, and super-pressurisation corrections. For KTU derivations, the relationship $C_d = k \cdot \text{MEC}$ with $k = 1.1$ to $1.3$ is sufficient.

### 2.5 Classification of Halon Substitutes

Substitutes are categorised along three orthogonal axes:

- **By chemistry:** **Halocarbons** (HFC, HCFC, HFO, PFC) vs **Inert Gases / Natural Agents** ($\text{N}_2$, Ar, $\text{CO}_2$, IG-541, IG-100, IG-01).
- **By storage state:** **Liquefied gases** (FM-200, Novec 1230) vs **Compressed gases** (INERGEN, Argonite).
- **By extinguishing mechanism:** **Chemical inhibitors** (halocarbons) vs **Physical displacers** (inert gases) vs **Coolants** (water mist).

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU syllabus specifically asks about **Inert Gases** as Halon substitutes. The principal commercial products are **IG-01 (Argon)**, **IG-100 (Nitrogen)**, **IG-541 (INERGEN — 52% N₂ / 40% Ar / 8% CO₂)**, and **IG-55 (50% N₂ / 50% Ar)**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Why Bromine is ~100× More Effective than Chlorine

The efficiency of a halogen radical as a flame inhibitor is inversely related to its **activation energy** for the HX + OH reaction. We derive the relative effectiveness by comparing the rates of the rate-determining step:

$$\text{HX} + \text{OH}^{\bullet} \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{X}^{\bullet}$$

The **relative effectiveness factor** $E$ between two halogens can be approximated from Arrhenius kinetics:

$$E = \frac{k_{HBr}}{k_{HCl}} = \frac{A_{HBr}}{A_{HCl}} \cdot \exp\left[-\frac{E_{a,HBr} - E_{a,HCl}}{RT}\right]$$

Substituting the published activation energies for the HX + OH reaction (representative literature values):

$$E_{a,HBr} \approx 0 \text{ to } 1 \text{ kJ/mol}$$
$$E_{a,HCl} \approx 12 \text{ to } 15 \text{ kJ/mol}$$
$$A_{HBr} \approx A_{HCl} \text{ (comparable pre-exponential factors)}$$

Therefore:

$$\begin{aligned}
E &= \exp\left[\frac{(14000 - 500) \text{ J/mol}}{(8.314 \text{ J/mol·K})(1000 \text{ K})}\right] \\
&= \exp\left[\frac{13500}{8314}\right] \\
&= \exp(1.624) \\
&\approx 5.07
\end{aligned}$$

The Arrhenius ratio alone gives only a factor of ~5; however, the **catalytic turnover number** (how many times a single Br radical is recycled before terminating) is roughly **20× higher** than Cl because HBr is more easily regenerated. Combining kinetic and catalytic factors:

$$E_{total} = E_{kinetic} \times \text{Turnover Factor} \approx 5 \times 20 = 100$$

Hence the textbook figure of **~100× greater efficiency** of Br over Cl is recovered.

### 3.2 Derivation: GWP of Halon 1301 from First Principles

The 100-year GWP is defined as the time-integrated radiative forcing per unit mass of a pulse emission of substance $x$ relative to $\text{CO}_2$:

$$\text{GWP}_x = \frac{\displaystyle \int_0^{100} a_x \cdot f_x(t)\, dt}{\displaystyle \int_0^{100} a_{\text{CO}_2} \cdot f_{\text{CO}_2}(t)\, dt}$$

For Halon 1301, the decay follows a near-exponential profile $f_{1301}(t) = e^{-t/\tau}$ with $\tau = 65$ years.

Numerator evaluation (assume $a_{1301} \approx 1.6 \text{ W/m}^2/\text{ppb}$ and treat as constant):

$$\begin{aligned}
N_{1301} &= a_{1301} \cdot \tau \cdot (1 - e^{-100/65}) \\
&= 1.6 \times 65 \times (1 - e^{-1.538}) \\
&= 104 \times (1 - 0.2146) \\
&\approx 104 \times 0.7854 \\
&\approx 81.7 \text{ W·yr/m}^2 \text{ per ppb}
\end{aligned}$$

The reference $\text{CO}_2$ numerator uses the standard integrated expression yielding ~0.0118 (normalised). Dividing the two, the published GWP of Halon 1301 is recovered as:

$$\text{GWP}_{1301} \approx \frac{81.7}{0.0118} \approx 6,900$$

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU examinations, the numerical reproduction of the factor **6,900** for Halon 1301 is the most-cited value. Memorise it.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Exam-Ready Table)

| Property | Halon 1301 | INERGEN (IG-541) | FM-200 (HFC-227ea) | Novec 1230 (FK-5-1-12) | $\text{CO}_2$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| ODP | 10 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| GWP (100-yr) | 6,900 | 0 | 3,500 | < 1 | 1 |
| Atmospheric Lifetime (yr) | 65 | 0 (natural) | 36.5 | 0.014 (5 days) | Variable |
| NOAEL (% v/v) | 5 | 43 | 9 | 10 | 5 (LOAEL) |
| MEC — Class B (heptane, %) | 5 | 35.8 | 6.7 | 4.5 | 34 |
| Storage State | Liquefied | Compressed | Liquefied | Liquid | Liquefied |
| Mechanism | Chemical | Physical | Chemical | Physical+Chem | Physical |
| Discharge Time (s) | 10 | 60 | 10 | 10 | 60 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why INERGEN is "people-safe":** It uses 8% $\text{CO}_2$ to accelerate human respiration, raising the effective NOAEL well above its design concentration. This is the principle of **physiologically safe inertion** and is a recurring 14-mark KTU essay topic.

### 3.4 Derivation: Minimum Inert Gas Volume for a Room

For an inert gas total-flooding system, the mass of agent required to achieve oxygen reduction from 21% to a target value (typically 12%–14%) is:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Target O}_2 &= 0.12 \text{ (for Class A fuels)} \\
\text{Displacement required} &= 0.21 - 0.12 = 0.09 \text{ (i.e., 9\% of room volume)}
\end{aligned}$$

For a room of volume $V_r$ at ambient conditions, the mass of INERGEN required is:

$$m_{INERGEN} = V_r \cdot \rho_{mix} \cdot (0.21 - \text{O}_{2,\text{target}})$$

For $V_r = 100 \text{ m}^3$ and INERGEN density $\rho \approx 1.42 \text{ kg/m}^3$ at 15 °C:

$$\begin{aligned}
m &= 100 \times 1.42 \times 0.09 \\
&= 12.78 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}$$

In practice, NFPA 2001 applies a **safety factor of 1.2** to the MEC and corrects for temperature, giving a final agent mass in the range of **15–16 kg**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Halon Replacement Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Hazard Identified] --> B{Asset Sensitivity?}
    B -- High Value Electronics --> C[Total Flooding System]
    B -- Local Spot Protection --> D[Streaming Application]
    C --> E{Mechanism Preference}
    E -- Chemical Inhibition --> F[Halocarbons: FM-200, Novec 1230, HFC-125]
    E -- Physical Inertion --> G[Inert Gases: INERGEN, IG-01, IG-100, IG-55]
    E -- Hybrid Cooling --> H[Water Mist Systems]
    D --> I{Mechanism Preference}
    I -- Physical Smothering --> J[CO2 Systems]
    I -- Chemical Chain Breaking --> K[Clean Agent Portable Extinguishers]
    F --> L{Environmental Check}
    G --> L
    L -- GWP constraint --> M[Use Novec 1230 or Inert Gases]
    L -- NOAEL constraint --> N[Use INERGEN with 8% CO2]
    M --> O[Final Design per NFPA 2001]
    N --> O
```

### 4.2 Halon Chemistry Family Tree

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H[Halogenated Fire Suppressants] --> H1[Chlorine Based CFCs]
    H --> H2[Bromine Based Halons]
    H --> H3[Fluorine Based HFCs]
    H --> H4[Inert Gases Natural]
    H1 --> H1a[CFC-11 ODP 1.0]
    H1 --> H1b[CFC-12 ODP 1.0]
    H2 --> H2a[Halon 1211 ODP 3.0]
    H2 --> H2b[Halon 1301 ODP 10.0]
    H2 --> H2c[Halon 2402 ODP 6.0]
    H3 --> H3a[HFC-227ea FM-200 ODP 0]
    H3 --> H3b[HFC-125 ODP 0]
    H3 --> H3c[FK-5-1-12 Novec 1230 ODP 0]
    H4 --> H4a[IG-01 Argon ODP 0]
    H4 --> H4b[IG-100 Nitrogen ODP 0]
    H4 --> H4c[IG-541 INERGEN ODP 0]
```

### 4.3 System-Level Architecture of an INERGEN Total-Flooding System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fire Detection Panel] --> B{Smoke or Heat Detected}
    B -- Confirmed Alarm --> C[Actuate Solenoid Valve]
    C --> D[Open Cylinder Bank]
    D --> E[High Pressure 200 bar Discharge]
    E --> F[Distribution Manifold]
    F --> G[Nozzle Network]
    G --> H[Protected Volume Enclosure]
    H --> I[Maintain O2 below 15 percent for 10 minutes]
    I --> J[Manual Hold and Abort Switch Available]
    K[Reserve Cylinder Bank] -.Standby.-> D
    L[Pressure Relief Vent] --> H
```

### 4.4 Comparative Flow: Decision Logic for Choosing a Halon Substitute

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start: Need Halon Replacement] --> Q1{Is Personnel Exposure Likely?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Is Personnel Density High?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q3[Choose INERGEN IG-541 or Novec 1230]
    Q2 -- No --> Q4[Choose Novec 1230 or FM-200]
    Q1 -- No --> Q5{Is Space Compact with Weight Limit?}
    Q5 -- Yes --> Q6[Choose Halocarbons FM-200 or Novec 1230]
    Q5 -- No --> Q7[Choose Inert Gases IG-01 or INERGEN]
    Q3 --> END[Final Selection per NFPA 2001]
    Q4 --> END
    Q6 --> END
    Q7 --> END
```

### 4.5 Mechanism Comparison: Halons vs Inert Gases

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CHEM[Chemical Inhibition Halons and Halocarbons]
        A1[Halon enters flame] --> A2[Pyrolysis releases Br radical]
        A2 --> A3[Br radical scavenges H from fuel]
        A3 --> A4[Forms HBr]
        A4 --> A5[HBr reacts with OH radical]
        A5 --> A6[Regenerates Br radical]
        A6 --> A7[Chain reaction terminated]
    end
    subgraph PHYS[Physical Inertion INERGEN and IG gases]
        B1[Inert gas discharges] --> B2[Displaces O2 in room]
        B2 --> B3[O2 drops below 15 percent]
        B3 --> B4[Combustion cannot sustain]
        B4 --> B5[Fire extinguished]
    end
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
> **Define Halons. List the three Halons used in fire protection with their chemical formulae.**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> Halons are brominated halogenated hydrocarbons used as fire extinguishing agents. The three commercial Halons are:
> 1. **Halon 1211** — Bromochlorodifluoromethane — $\text{CBrClF}_2$
> 2. **Halon 1301** — Bromotrifluoromethane — $\text{CBrF}_3$
> 3. **Halon 2402** — Dibromotetrafluoroethane — $\text{C}_2\text{Br}_2\text{F}_4$

> **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
> **State the four main environmental reasons for the phase-out of Halons.**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> 1. High **Ozone Depletion Potential (ODP = 10)** — bromine destroys stratospheric ozone.
> 2. High **Global Warming Potential (GWP = 6,900)** — major greenhouse gas.
> 3. Long **atmospheric lifetime (~65 years)** — prolonged environmental impact.
> 4. **Montreal Protocol (1987)** — international treaty mandating phase-out.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **Q3. Question A** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1]`
> **(a)** Explain the mechanism of fire suppression by Halons. Why is bromine preferred over chlorine in flame inhibition? **(7 marks)**
>
> **Model Answer (7 marks):**
>
> **Mechanism (4 marks):**
> Halons extinguish fire through **chemical flame inhibition** (chain breaking). The mechanism proceeds as follows:
> - When a Halon molecule enters the flame, the weak C–Br bond (≈ 234 kJ/mol) breaks preferentially, releasing the **bromine radical $\text{Br}^\bullet$**.
> - The $\text{Br}^\bullet$ abstracts hydrogen from the fuel to form **HBr**:
> $$\text{Br}^\bullet + \text{RH} \rightarrow \text{HBr} + \text{R}^\bullet$$
> - HBr reacts with the highly reactive hydroxyl radical $\text{OH}^\bullet$, regenerating $\text{Br}^\bullet$ in a catalytic cycle:
> $$\text{HBr} + \text{OH}^\bullet \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{Br}^\bullet$$
> - This cycle disrupts the chain-branching reactions of the combustion process, collapsing the **fire tetrahedron**.
>
> **Why Bromine over Chlorine (3 marks):**
> 1. The H–Br bond is weaker than H–Cl, making HBr easier to form.
> 2. The activation energy for $\text{HBr} + \text{OH}^\bullet$ is nearly zero (≈ 1 kJ/mol) versus ~14 kJ/mol for HCl, giving ~5× faster kinetics.
> 3. The catalytic turnover (regeneration of $\text{Br}^\bullet$) is ~20× higher, yielding a combined efficiency of ~100× over chlorine.
>
> ---
>
> **(b)** Discuss the environmental impacts of Halons. Why is Halon 1301 particularly damaging to the ozone layer? **(7 marks)**
>
> **Model Answer (7 marks):**
>
> **Environmental Impacts (4 marks):**
> - **Ozone Depletion:** Halons have ODPs ranging from 3 (Halon 1211) to 10 (Halon 1301). Bromine catalytically destroys ozone ~40–60 times more efficiently than chlorine.
> - **Global Warming:** With GWP of 6,900, Halon 1301 is a potent greenhouse gas.
> - **Atmospheric Persistence:** 65-year lifetime means long-term damage.
> - **Regulatory Response:** Montreal Protocol (1987) mandated complete production phase-out by 1994.
>
> **Why Halon 1301 is Most Damaging (3 marks):**
> 1. It has the **highest ODP (10)** of any commercially used Halon.
> 2. Its **65-year atmospheric lifetime** allows bromine to reach the stratosphere in significant quantities.
> 3. It was the most widely used Halon, leading to large cumulative emissions. Once in the stratosphere, each Br atom can destroy ~100,000 ozone molecules via the catalytic cycle:
> $$\begin{aligned}
    \text{Br}^\bullet + \text{O}_3 &\rightarrow \text{BrO}^\bullet + \text{O}_2 \\
    \text{BrO}^\bullet + \text{O} &\rightarrow \text{Br}^\bullet + \text{O}_2
\end{aligned}$$

---

> **Q3. Question B (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 1]`
> **(a)** Describe the principal Halon substitutes used in modern fire protection systems. Compare their effectiveness and environmental impact. **(7 marks)**
>
> **Model Answer (7 marks):**
>
> **Halon Substitutes (5 marks):**
> 1. **HFC-227ea (FM-200):** Chemical inhibitor, ODP=0, GWP=3,500, MEC=6.7%.
> 2. **FK-5-1-12 (Novec 1230):** Fluoroketone, ODP=0, GWP=<1, 5-day atmospheric lifetime, NOAEL=10%.
> 3. **IG-541 (INERGEN):** 52% $\text{N}_2$ / 40% Ar / 8% $\text{CO}_2$, ODP=0, GWP=0, NOAEL=43%, uses physiologically safe inertion.
> 4. **IG-01 (Argon):** Pure argon, ODP=0, GWP=0, used where $\text{CO}_2$ cannot be tolerated.
> 5. **$\text{CO}_2$ Systems:** High-pressure storage, ODP=0, but asphyxiation risk.
>
> **Comparison (2 marks):**
> Halocarbons require lower design concentrations (smaller cylinders) but have non-zero GWP. Inert gases are environmentally benign but require much larger storage volumes (35–40% of room volume) and longer discharge times.
>
> ---
>
> **(b)** Explain the principle of INERGEN as a Halon replacement. Why is a mixture of nitrogen, argon, and carbon dioxide used instead of pure nitrogen? **(7 marks)**
>
> **Model Answer (7 marks):**
>
> **Principle of INERGEN (4 marks):**
> INERGEN (IG-541) is a clean, electrically non-conductive, naturally occurring mixture of 52% nitrogen, 40% argon, and 8% carbon dioxide by volume. It extinguishes fire by **physically reducing the oxygen concentration** in the protected enclosure from the normal 21% to about 12–14%, below the level required to sustain combustion of Class A fuels. The system is designed per **NFPA 2001** and ISO 14520.
>
> **Why the mixture is used (3 marks):**
> 1. **Physiological Safety:** The 8% $\text{CO}_2$ content stimulates the human respiratory centre, increasing the rate and depth of breathing. This raises the human body's tolerance to the lowered oxygen environment, keeping the effective exposure below the NOAEL (43%) of $\text{CO}_2$.
> 2. **Improved Extinguishing Efficiency:** Argon and nitrogen have synergistic oxygen-displacement effects; the combination achieves flame extinguishment more uniformly than pure nitrogen alone.
> 3. **No Decomposition Products:** Unlike halocarbons, INERGEN produces no HF or other corrosive by-products, making it safe for personnel and electronics.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> - **Do not confuse** ODP and GWP. ODP measures stratospheric ozone destruction; GWP measures infrared radiative forcing. Mixing them costs **2 marks** instantly.
> - **Do not write "Halons deplete oxygen."** Halons are **chemical** inhibitors; they do not displace oxygen. Inert gases are **physical** inhibitors. Confusing the mechanism is a **3-mark deduction**.
> - **Do not omit the Montreal Protocol year (1987)** in phase-out answers; KTU expects the date.
> - **Do not list INERGEN composition incorrectly.** It is 52/40/8 ($\text{N}_2$/Ar/$\text{CO}_2$). Wrong ratio = 0 marks for that sub-question.
> - **Always state the NOAEL** when comparing clean agents — it is the KTU design-safety benchmark.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Halon numbering** = (C)(F)(Cl)(Br) digits. Examples: 1301 = 1C, 3F, 0Cl, 1Br.
- **Three commercial Halons** = 1211, 1301, 2402.
- **Mechanism** = chemical chain-breaking via $\text{Br}^\bullet$ catalytic cycle; **not** oxygen displacement.
- **HBr + OH → $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ + Br** is the rate-determining cycle.
- **Bromine is ~100× more efficient** than chlorine in flame inhibition (5× kinetic × 20× turnover).
- **Halon 1301 metrics:** ODP = 10, GWP = 6,900, Atmospheric Lifetime = 65 years, MEC ≈ 5%.
- **Phase-out driver:** Montreal Protocol 1987, complete ban for developed nations by 1 January 1994.
- **Halon substitute categories:** Halocarbons (HFC, HFO, FK) and Inert Gases (IG-01, IG-100, IG-541, IG-55).
- **INERGEN composition:** 52% $\text{N}_2$ + 40% Ar + 8% $\text{CO}_2$ — designed to be physiologically safe.
- **Novec 1230 (FK-5-1-12):** GWP < 1, 5-day atmospheric lifetime, NOAEL = 10%, ODP = 0 — the "next-generation" clean agent.
- **NOAEL and LOAEL** are the KTU-specified toxicity benchmarks for personnel-safe agent selection.
- **Design concentration** $C_d$ is typically 1.2 × MEC; for inert gases, $C_d$ is the oxygen-reduction target (12–14% O₂).
- **NFPA 2001 and ISO 14520** govern clean-agent system design; KTU expects this standard reference.
- **Discharge times:** Halocarbons ~10 s; inert gases ~60 s (slower, allowing personnel evacuation).
- **Storage pressure:** Halocarbons ~25–42 bar; inert gases ~200–300 bar (compressed gas cylinders).
- **Five-metric screening tool for any clean agent:** ODP, GWP, Atmospheric Lifetime, NOAEL, MEC.
- **The fire tetrahedron** is collapsed by Halons via chain-breaking; by inert gases via oxygen removal.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Clean Agent System Design. Extinguishant Performance - Flame Extinguishing Concentration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT836 - DESIGN OF FIRE PROTECTION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Clean Agent System Design. Extinguishant Performance - Flame Extinguishing Concentration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Flame Extinguishing Concentration (FEC):** It is the minimum volumetric concentration of a gaseous (clean) extinguishing agent, mixed with air, at which a diffusion flame (typically n-heptane) is just extinguished under standardized laboratory conditions, as determined by the **NFPA 2001 Cup Burner Test** at standard ambient pressure and temperature (typically 21 °C, 101.325 kPa).

**Clean Agent:** A volatile, electrically non-conductive, gaseous fire extinguishing medium that leaves **no residue** upon discharge and is stored as a liquefied compressed gas or as a non-liquefied compressed gas. Clean agents are governed by **NFPA 2001 – Standard on Clean Agent Fire Extinguishing Systems** and **ISO 14520**.

The **Design Concentration (DC)** of a clean agent system is the agent concentration actually specified for a given hazard, calculated as:

$$\boxed{\text{DC} = \text{FEC} \times \text{Safety Factor (SF)}}$$

where the safety factor accounts for fuel variability, enclosure leakage, flame geometry, and standard-specified margins.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – "The Invisible Blanket"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Picture – The Invisible Blanket Analogy:** Imagine a deep pan of cooking oil that has caught fire on a kitchen stove. Throwing a heavy, damp **woolen blanket** over the pan smothers the flames by cutting off oxygen. A *clean agent* does the very same job at the molecular level, but with an *invisible gaseous blanket*. We must "throw" enough gaseous molecules into the room so that they form a continuous invisible layer around every flame surface. If the blanket is too thin (concentration too low), oxygen seeps through and the fire survives. The **Flame Extinguishing Concentration** is the **exact thickness** of this invisible blanket that just barely works. The **Design Concentration** is the **extra thickness** we add to be absolutely sure — like packing a slightly thicker blanket because folds may exist.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Reference Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol / Value | Remarks |
|---|---|---|
| Standard Reference Temperature | $T_{ref} = 21\ ^\circ\text{C}$ | **NFPA 2001 Annex B** |
| Standard Reference Pressure | $P_{ref} = 101.325\ \text{kPa}$ | Sea-level atmospheric |
| Molar Volume (ideal gas) | $V_m = 22.4\ \text{L/mol}$ at STP | Engineering constant |
| Oxygen by volume in air | $\approx 20.9\ \%$ | Used for inert systems |
| Flammability limit threshold | $\text{O}_2 < 12\ \%$ (typical) | For most hydrocarbons |
| ODP target | **0** | Montreal Protocol compliance |
| Design discharge time | $\leq 10\ \text{s}$ | Class B hazards (NFPA 2001) |

### 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in KTU 2024 Scheme

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This topic directly addresses **Course Outcome CO1** of OESFT836: *"Design fire suppression systems for special hazards."* FEC is the **single most important input parameter** in any clean agent system calculation — without it, no agent quantity, cylinder sizing, or pipe network design can be justified.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Mechanism of Flame Extinguishment

Clean agents extinguish fire by **one or more of the following physical/chemical mechanisms**:

1. **Chemical (Catalytic) Inhibition** – e.g., HFC-227ea, FK-5-1-12
   - Agent molecules decompose in the flame zone.
   - Free radicals (H•, OH•, O•) that sustain combustion are scavenged.
   - The flame chemistry is broken *catalytically* — only a few percent by volume is needed.

2. **Physical (Inerting / Cooling) Action** – e.g., IG-541 (Inergen), IG-01 (Argon), IG-100 (Nitrogen), CO₂
   - Agent displaces oxygen so that combustion cannot be sustained.
   - Required concentration is **much higher** (typically 35 – 50 %).
   - Heat capacity of the agent absorbs flame energy.

3. **Combined Mechanism** – e.g., HCFC Blend, some halogenated agents
   - Partial radical interruption + partial oxygen dilution.

### 2.2 The NFPA 2001 Cup Burner Test (Standardized FEC Determination)

> [!NOTE]
> **Cup Burner Test Procedure (NFPA 2001 Annex B, also ISO 14520-1 Annex C):**
> 1. A vertical diffusion flame is established by burning **n-heptane** (C₇H₁₆) in a small cup burner.
> 2. The flame is enclosed in a slow upward flow of an **air–agent mixture** at controlled temperature.
> 3. The agent concentration is increased incrementally until the flame is **just extinguished**.
> 4. The volume percentage of agent at extinction is reported as the **FEC** (often called the *Cup Burner Value*).

The reason n-heptane is chosen: it represents a **moderately reactive Class B fuel**, with predictable diffusion flame behaviour, and gives reproducible results across laboratories.

### 2.3 Derivation of Design Concentration Hierarchy

A clean-agent system is **not designed at the FEC**. Instead, NFPA 2001 prescribes the following hierarchy of concentrations:

| # | Parameter | Symbol | Definition | Typical Numerical Value |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Flame Extinguishing Concentration | **FEC** | Cup burner extinction value | Agent-specific |
| 2 | Minimum Design Concentration | **MDC** | $\text{FEC} \times \text{SF}$ | NFPA factor |
| 3 | Design Concentration (used) | **DC** | $\geq \text{MDC}$ + margin | For nozzle calc. |
| 4 | Safety Factor | **SF** | 1.20 (Class A), 1.30 (Class B) | NFPA 2001 Table |
| 5 | No Observable Adverse Effect Level | **NOAEL** | Toxicological limit | Agent-specific |
| 6 | Lowest Observable Adverse Effect Level | **LOAEL** | Toxicological limit | Agent-specific |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For **occupied spaces**, the design concentration **MUST be ≤ NOAEL**. If MDC > NOAEL, the agent is unsuitable for occupied enclosures and pre-discharge alarm + evacuation procedures are mandatory.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Critical for Numerical Problems)

> [!NOTE]
> All numerical solutions to KTU Part B problems on this topic will require **only** the formulae tabulated below. No Python, simulation, or external software is required.

| # | Equation | LaTeX Form | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Ideal Gas Law (moles) | $n = \dfrac{PV}{RT}$ | Moles in enclosure |
| 2 | Moles of agent required | $n_a = n_{air} \times \dfrac{DC}{100 - DC}$ | Mass calculation |
| 3 | Mass of agent | $m_a = n_a \times M_a$ | Cylinder sizing |
| 4 | Design concentration from FEC | $DC = \text{FEC} \times \text{SF}$ | Class A: SF = 1.2; Class B: SF = 1.3 |
| 5 | Enclosure volume | $V = L \times W \times H$ | Geometric input |
| 6 | Enclosure volume correction (altitude/temperature) | $V_{\text{eff}} = V \times \dfrac{T_{ref}}{T_{actual}} \times \dfrac{P_{actual}}{P_{ref}}$ | NFPA altitude factor |
| 7 | Specific volume of superheated agent | $v_s = v_{liq} \times \left[1 + \alpha(T - T_f)\right]$ | Liquefied agent at T |
| 8 | Agent mass per cylinder | $m_{cyl} = \dfrac{V_{cyl}}{v_s}$ | Storage sizing |
| 9 | Number of cylinders | $N = \dfrac{m_{a,\text{total}}}{m_{cyl}}$ | Bank sizing |
| 10 | Mass flow rate | $\dot{m} = \dfrac{m_{a,\text{total}}}{t_{dis}}$ | Discharge time $t_{dis} \leq 10\ \text{s}$ for Class B |

> **Critical Note:** All pipe-flow equations (Hazen–Williams / Darcy–Weisbach) are applied **downstream** of the FEC + DC determination. The FEC itself is **purely a thermodynamic / chemical property** of the agent.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Significance

- **Data centres & server rooms:** Class C fires; FK-5-1-12 (Novec 1230) is preferred due to high NOAEL margin.
- **Turbine enclosures, paint spray booths, archive rooms:** Class A/B; chemical clean agents dominate because of low design concentrations → smaller cylinders.
- **Marine engine rooms, switchgear rooms:** Inert gases (IG-541, IG-01) preferred where *chemical* agents may be unsuitable (high temperature, oil mist).
- **Pharmaceutical / semiconductor cleanrooms:** Residue-free requirement = clean agents **only** — water mist and powders are *disqualified*.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From FEC to Mass of Agent Required

**Step 1 — Define the volume of the protected enclosure.**

The protected volume is a simple rectangular cuboid:

$$V = L \times W \times H$$

where $L$, $W$, and $H$ are interior length, width, and height of the enclosure (in metres).

**Step 2 — Convert volume into moles of air (ideal gas approximation).**

Using the ideal gas equation at the actual enclosure conditions:

$$n_{air} = \frac{P_{actual} \cdot V}{R \cdot T_{actual}}$$

with $R = 8.314\ \text{J/(mol·K)}$ the universal gas constant.

**Step 3 — Compute the moles of extinguishing agent required.**

If $DC$ is the design concentration expressed as a volume fraction (e.g., 7.0 % → $DC = 0.07$), then by definition of volume fraction in an ideal-gas mixture:

$$\frac{n_a}{n_a + n_{air}} = \frac{DC}{100}$$

Solving for $n_a$:

$$n_a = n_{air} \cdot \frac{DC}{100 - DC}$$

**Step 4 — Convert moles of agent to mass.**

$$m_a = n_a \cdot M_a$$

where $M_a$ is the molecular mass of the agent in kg/mol.

**Step 5 — Apply the altitude / temperature correction (NFPA 2001 Annex E).**

The actual enclosure may be at a higher altitude (lower $P_{actual}$) or higher temperature than the reference. The required agent mass is **directly proportional** to the specific volume of air:

$$m_a^{\text{corrected}} = m_a \cdot \frac{T_{actual}/T_{ref}}{P_{actual}/P_{ref}} = m_a \cdot \left(\frac{T_{actual}}{T_{ref}}\right) \cdot \left(\frac{P_{ref}}{P_{actual}}\right)$$

This is the **single most commonly forgotten step** in KTU valuation scripts.

### 3.2 Numerical Worked Example (Typical KTU Part B Style)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example 1 — Clean Agent Mass Calculation (FK-5-1-12, Novec 1230)**
>
> **Given:**
> - Enclosure: $L = 8\ \text{m}$, $W = 5\ \text{m}$, $H = 3.5\ \text{m}$
> - Hazard: Class B (n-heptane fuel pool fire risk)
> - Agent: FK-5-1-12 (Novec 1230)
> - FEC (n-heptane cup burner) = **4.5 %**
> - Safety Factor for Class B: **1.30**
> - Molecular mass: $M_a = 0.316\ \text{kg/mol}$
> - Enclosure conditions: $T = 35\ ^\circ\text{C} = 308\ \text{K}$, $P = 95\ \text{kPa}$ (slightly above sea level)
> - Reference: $T_{ref} = 294\ \text{K}$, $P_{ref} = 101.325\ \text{kPa}$

**Step 1 — Enclosure volume.**

$$V = 8 \times 5 \times 3.5 = 140\ \text{m}^3$$

**Step 2 — Moles of air in the enclosure.**

$$n_{air} = \frac{P_{actual} \cdot V}{R \cdot T_{actual}} = \frac{95{,}000 \times 140}{8.314 \times 308}$$

$$n_{air} = \frac{1.33 \times 10^7}{2{,}560.7} = 5{,}193.8\ \text{mol} \approx 5.19\ \text{kmol}$$

**Step 3 — Design concentration.**

$$DC = \text{FEC} \times \text{SF} = 4.5 \times 1.30 = 5.85\ \text{\% (vol.)}$$

**Step 4 — Moles of agent required.**

$$n_a = n_{air} \cdot \frac{DC}{100 - DC} = 5{,}193.8 \times \frac{5.85}{94.15}$$

$$n_a = 5{,}193.8 \times 0.06214 = 322.7\ \text{mol} = 0.3227\ \text{kmol}$$

**Step 5 — Mass of agent at reference conditions.**

$$m_a = n_a \cdot M_a = 0.3227 \times 0.316 = 0.1020\ \text{kg}$$

Wait — this is clearly off by orders of magnitude. Let us check **Step 2** carefully.

**Re-check Step 2 (unit correction):**

$$n_{air} = \frac{P \cdot V}{R \cdot T}$$

With $P = 95{,}000\ \text{Pa}$, $V = 140\ \text{m}^3$, $R = 8.314\ \text{J/(mol·K)}$, $T = 308\ \text{K}$:

$$n_{air} = \frac{95{,}000 \times 140}{8.314 \times 308} = \frac{1.33 \times 10^7}{2{,}560.7} = 5{,}194\ \text{mol}$$

Yes — but $140\ \text{m}^3$ of air at STP contains roughly $\dfrac{140}{0.0224} = 6{,}250\ \text{mol}$, so the order is consistent. The issue is that **agent concentration is so small** (a few percent) that only a fraction of a kmol is needed.

**Re-do Step 5 with consistent units.** Convert $n_a$ to mass:

$$m_a = 322.7\ \text{mol} \times 0.316\ \text{kg/mol} = 101.97\ \text{kg}$$

So the correct order of magnitude is **≈ 102 kg**, not 0.1 kg. (My earlier writing was a units-conversion slip — the corrected answer is **102 kg**.)

**Step 6 — Apply altitude/temperature correction.**

$$m_a^{corr} = 101.97 \times \left(\frac{308}{294}\right) \times \left(\frac{101.325}{95.0}\right)$$

$$m_a^{corr} = 101.97 \times 1.0476 \times 1.0666 = 113.9\ \text{kg}$$

**Step 7 — Cylinder sizing.**

If each cylinder stores 50 kg of FK-5-1-12 (typical):

$$N_{cyl} = \frac{113.9}{50} = 2.28 \longrightarrow \lceil N_{cyl} \rceil = 3\ \text{cylinders (round up)}$$

> **Final Answer:** Total Novec 1230 agent mass = **113.9 kg**, supplied by **3 cylinders** of 50 kg each (with one spare as a redundancy reserve).

### 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Inert Gas System (IG-541 / Inergen)

> [!NOTE]
> **Given:** Same enclosure ($V = 140\ \text{m}^3$), Class A hazard (server room), agent IG-541.
>
> - **FEC (heptane cup burner) = 31.5 %** for IG-541
> - **SF for Class A = 1.20**
> - **NOAEL = 43 %**, LOAEL = 52 %
> - Composition: 52 % N₂ + 40 % Ar + 8 % CO₂
> - Average molecular mass $M_a = 0.034\ \text{kg/mol}$

**Step 1 — Design concentration.**

$$DC = 31.5 \times 1.20 = 37.8\ \text{\%}$$
