where $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ is the ceiling function (round up to next integer).

**Step 5 — Verification for ceiling height $H > 4$ m (sloped-ceiling or high-bay reduction).**
For $H > 4$ m, NFPA 72 requires a spacing reduction factor $D$:

$$
D = 0.4 \times (H - 0.3) \quad \text{(in metres, for } H \text{ in metres)}
$$

The effective spacing becomes:

$$
S_{eff} = S_{max} - D
$$

### **Worked Example (Typical KTU Problem)**

> A warehouse is 30 m × 20 m with a smooth ceiling at 6 m height. Design a point-type heat detector layout per IS 11360.

**Step 1 — Spacing at 6 m height.** Apply the high-ceiling reduction:

$$
D = 0.4 \times (6 - 0.3) = 0.4 \times 5.7 = 2.28 \ \text{m}
$$

$$
S_{eff} = 6.1 - 2.28 = 3.82 \ \text{m}, \ \text{use } 3.8 \ \text{m}
$$

**Step 2 — Coverage area.**

$$
A_c = 3.8^2 = 14.44 \ \text{m}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Total detectors.**

$$
N = \left\lceil \frac{30 \times 20}{14.44} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 41.55 \right\rceil = 42 \ \text{detectors}
$$

**Step 4 — Grid layout verification.**
Along length (30 m): $n_x = \lceil 30 / 3.8 \rceil = \lceil 7.89 \rceil = 8$ detectors → 8 × 3.8 = 30.4 m ✓
Along width (20 m): $n_y = \lceil 20 / 3.8 \rceil = \lceil 5.26 \rceil = 6$ detectors → 6 × 3.8 = 22.8 m (with wall margin ~1.4 m) ✓
Total: $8 \times 6 = 48$ detectors, but a smarter edge-positioned grid achieves 42 by exploiting wall margins of 0.5 S.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Notification Appliance — Sound Pressure Level Verification

**Step 1 — Measure ambient noise.** $L_{ambient}$ in dB (typical office: 50 dB, factory: 75–85 dB).

**Step 2 — Apply NFPA 72 audible requirement.**

$$
L_{alarm} \geq L_{ambient} + 15 \ \text{dB}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply absolute minimum.**

$$
L_{alarm} \geq 75 \ \text{dB} \ \text{at } 3\ \text{m (10 ft)} \ \text{from the device}
$$

**Step 4 — Sound decay over distance (inverse square law for point source in free field).**

$$
L_2 = L_1 - 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{r_2}{r_1}\right)
$$

**Step 5 — Work the KTU problem in reverse** — given $L_1$ at 3 m, find max distance where 75 dB is met.

> Suppose a horn-rating is 95 dB at 3 m. Find the maximum distance for 75 dB.

$$
75 = 95 - 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{r}{3}\right)
$$

$$
20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{r}{3}\right) = 20
$$

$$
\log_{10}\left(\frac{r}{3}\right) = 1
$$

$$
\frac{r}{3} = 10
$$

$$
\boxed{\,r = 30 \ \text{m}\,}
$$

This sets the maximum spacing of audible notification appliances on the same axis.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Detector Where?

| Fire Scenario | Best Detector Type | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| **Smoldering electrical fire** (PVC cable) | **Photoelectric / VESDA** | Produces large aerosols, low flame, slow heat |
| **Flaming liquid pool** (solvents) | **Dual-IR (IR3) flame detector** | Strong 4.4 µm emission, fast response |
| **Kitchen grease fire** | **Heat (fixed-temp 93 °C) + ROR** | Avoids cooking-aerosol nuisance alarms |
| **Clean room / data centre** | **VESDA (ASD)** | Very high sensitivity, no false alarms |
| **Hydrogen / battery fire** | **Gas detector (H₂, CO) + heat** | Invisible flame, early gas emission |
| **Outdoor transformer yard** | **UV + IR combined** | Discriminates solar from arc-flash |
| **Tunnel / cable tray** | **Linear Heat Detection Cable (LHDC)** | Continuous coverage over long runs |
| **Heritage museum** | **Air-sampling (VESDA) + VSD** | Discreet, no false alarms, no drilling |
| **Car park (underground)** | **Heat (ROR) + CO** | Discriminates vehicle exhaust |
| **Atrium / high-bay (15 m ceiling)** | **Beam smoke detector + VESDA** | Stratified smoke layer rises above point detectors |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Fire Detection System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Fire Ignition Source] --> B[Fire Signatures: Smoke, Heat, Flame, Gas]
    B --> C1[Initiating Device: Smoke Detector]
    B --> C2[Initiating Device: Heat Detector]
    B --> C3[Initiating Device: Flame Detector]
    B --> C4[Initiating Device: Gas Detector]
    B --> C5[Manual Call Point MCP]
    C1 --> D[Signaling Line Circuit SLC Loop]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D
    C5 --> D
    D --> E{Fire Alarm Control Panel FACP}
    E --> F1[Alarm Verification Logic]
    E --> F2[Drift Compensation Algorithm]
    E --> F3[Device Polling and Diagnostics]
    F1 --> G[Decision: Alarm or Fault]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    G -->|Alarm| H1[Notification Appliance Circuits NAC]
    G -->|Alarm| H2[Control Relays: HVAC, Elevator, Doors]
    G -->|Alarm| H3[Suppression Release Signal]
    G -->|Fault| H4[Trouble Signal to BMS]
    H1 --> I1[Sirens, Horns, Strobes, Voice Evacuation]
    H2 --> I2[AHU Shutdown, Fire Pump Start]
    H3 --> I3[Sprinkler Valve, Gas Flooding, FM200]
    H4 --> I4[BMS Central Monitoring Station]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Trace a single fire signal from ignition (A) through detection tiers (C1–C5), into the SLC loop (D), processed by the FACP (E) with its three algorithms (F1–F3), culminating in a four-way output (H1–H4). This is the **complete signal path** any KTU examiner expects you to describe.

---

## 4.2 Detector Type Selection Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Start: Identify Fire Risk]) --> Q1{Is ceiling height greater than 10m?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| P1[Use Beam Detector or VESDA]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{Smoldering or Flaming fire expected?}
    Q2 -->|Smoldering| P2[Photoelectric or VESDA]
    Q2 -->|Flaming| Q3{Are cooking aerosols present?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| P3[Heat Detector Fixed Temp 93C]
    Q3 -->|No| Q4{Hydrocarbon flame expected?}
    Q4 -->|Yes| P4[IR3 or UV-IR Flame Detector]
    Q4 -->|No| Q5{Is environment clean critical?}
    Q5 -->|Yes| P5[VESDA Aspirating System]
    Q5 -->|No| P6[Ionization or Multi-criteria Detector]
    P1 --> End([End])
    P2 --> End
    P3 --> End
    P4 --> End
    P5 --> End
    P6 --> End
```

---

## 4.3 Addressable SLC Loop Topology (Class A — Redundant)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    FACP[Fire Alarm Control Panel] -->|Outbound wire pair| D1[Detector Address 001]
    D1 --> D2[Detector Address 002]
    D2 --> D3[Detector Address 003]
    D3 --> D4[Detector Address 004]
    D4 --> D5[Detector Address 005]
    D5 --> M1[Monitor Module: Sprinkler Tamper]
    M1 --> M2[Monitor Module: Water Flow Switch]
    M2 -->|Return wire pair| FACP
    classDef facpStyle fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef detStyle fill:#fde68a,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef monStyle fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    class FACP facpStyle
    class D1,D2,D3,D4,D5 detStyle
    class M1,M2 monStyle
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Insight:** The **loop-return** (the line from M2 back to FACP) is what defines a **Class A / Style Z** circuit. Even if the cable is **broken anywhere along the loop**, the FACP can still communicate with **every device from both ends** — the hallmark of addressable system reliability. This is a guaranteed 7-mark question on redundancy.

---

## 4.4 Fire Alarm Sequence of Operation (Cause-and-Effect Matrix Visual)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Sensor as Detector
    participant FACP as Fire Alarm Panel
    participant NAC as Horns and Strobes
    participant HVAC as HVAC System
    participant ELV as Elevator Controller
    participant SUP as Sprinkler System
    Note over Sensor: T0 Ignition
    Sensor->>FACP: T1 Analog value exceeds threshold
    Note over FACP: T1 to T2 Alarm verification 30 sec
    FACP->>FACP: Confirmed alarm logic
    FACP->>NAC: T2 Activate horns and strobes
    FACP->>HVAC: T2 Shutdown AHU and close dampers
    FACP->>ELV: T2 Recall elevators to ground floor
    FACP->>SUP: T3 Signal sprinkler valve release
    Note over SUP: T4 Suppression activation
    FACP->>FACP: T5 Log event with device address and timestamp
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question 1 (3 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2, Q1(a)]**
**Define "Time-to-Detect (TTD)" in the context of fire detection engineering. Why is minimizing TTD critical for life safety?**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (1 mark):** Time-to-Detect (TTD) is the time interval between the moment of fire ignition ($T_0$) and the moment the fire alarm control panel (FACP) issues a confirmed alarm signal ($T_1$).

$$
TTD = T_1 - T_0
$$

**Criticality for life safety (2 marks):** Minimizing TTD is critical because of the **ASET > RSET principle** of performance-based fire design. The Available Safe Egress Time (ASET) — the time before untenable conditions develop (e.g., smoke obscuration > 5 %/m, CO > 1000 ppm, temperature > 60 °C at breathing level, visibility < 10 m) — shrinks rapidly after ignition due to exponential fire growth. If the Required Safe Egress Time (RSET — the time occupants need to detect, decide, and evacuate) exceeds ASET, occupants are endangered. A shorter TTD directly enlarges ASET, giving occupants more time to escape. Additionally, earlier detection yields smaller fires, lower heat release rate, and reduced property damage.

---

### **Question 2 (3 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper 1, Q1(b)]**
**Distinguish between ionization and photoelectric smoke detectors. State one application where each is preferred.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Ionization Smoke Detector | Photoelectric Smoke Detector |
|---|---|---|
| **Sensing principle** | Uses radioactive source (Americium-241) to ionize air; smoke disrupts ion current | Uses LED light scatter; smoke particles scatter light onto photodiode |
| **Sensitive to** | Small particles (< 1 µm) — **fast-flaming fires** | Larger particles (0.1–10 µm) — **smoldering fires** |
| **Response time** | Faster for flaming fires | Faster for smoldering fires |
| **Cost** | Slightly cheaper | Slightly costlier |
| **Preferred application (1 mark each)** | **Server rooms with cable fire risk (PVC)**, where flaming combustion dominates and rapid flaming detection is needed | **Bedrooms, hotel rooms, heritage buildings**, where smoldering fire is the dominant risk (e.g., cigarette-stub ignition) |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Module Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks and **internal choice is mandatory**. The two sub-parts are usually **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Module 2, Dec 2023, Q2]**

**(a)** Explain the operating principle of a **photoelectric (light-scatter) smoke detector** with a neat sketch. List its advantages and limitations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A rectangular office floor measures **40 m × 25 m** with a smooth, flat ceiling at **3.5 m height**. Using **point-type photoelectric smoke detectors** per **NFPA 72 / IS 11360** guidelines, determine the **number of detectors** and propose a **grid layout**. Justify all design choices. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution (a):**

**Operating Principle (4 marks):**

A photoelectric smoke detector works on the principle of **light scattering by smoke particles**.

1. The detector housing contains a **light-emitting diode (LED)** — typically operating in the infrared region (~ 880 nm) — and a **photodiode (light sensor)** positioned at an angle such that the **direct light path from the LED does NOT strike the photodiode** under normal (smoke-free) conditions. This is the **dark chamber** or **light trap** design.

2. A **light-absorbing baffle** (often a black labyrinth structure) prevents stray ambient light from reaching the photodiode. The chamber is therefore called a **"dark chamber"**.

3. When **smoke particles** enter the chamber (typically through a screened inlet), they **scatter** the LED light in all directions. Some of this scattered light falls on the photodiode.

4. The photodiode generates an **analog current proportional to the smoke density**. This current is amplified and compared against a **pre-set threshold** in the detector electronics (or transmitted to the FACP for addressable systems).

5. When the threshold is exceeded, the detector enters the **alarm state**, transmitting a fire signature to the FACP.

**Neat Sketch (1 mark):** Draw the cross-section showing:
- Chamber wall with screened inlet
- LED (light source)
- Photodiode at right angle
- Light baffle
- Smoke entry arrows
- Labelled components: LED, Photodiode, Baffle, Chamber

**Advantages (1 mark):**
- Excellent response to **smoldering fires** (large particles)
- **No radioactive material** (cf. ionization detectors) — easier disposal, no regulatory issues
- Lower false alarm rate in **clean, non-cooking environments**
- Long service life (~ 10 years) with stable calibration

**Limitations (1 mark):**
- Slower response to small-particle, fast-flaming fires (cf. ionization)
- **Sensitive to dust and insects** — can cause nuisance alarms; periodic cleaning needed
- Performance degrades in **very dirty, oily, or steamy** environments
- Slightly more expensive than ionization detectors

---

#### **Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 — Identify applicable standard and spacing rule (2 marks):**
Per NFPA 72 and IS 11360, for point-type **smoke detectors on a smooth, flat ceiling of height ≤ 4 m**:
- Maximum spacing: $S_{max} = 9.1$ m (≈ 30 ft)
- Minimum wall distance: 0.5 S ≈ 4.55 m (but typically reduced to 1–4 m for a 40 m × 25 m room)

**Step 2 — Verify ceiling-height reduction (1 mark):**
Since ceiling height = 3.5 m < 4 m, **no reduction** is required. So $S_{max} = 9.1$ m.

**Step 3 — Compute the total area and detector count (2 marks):**

$$
A_{total} = 40 \times 25 = 1000 \ \text{m}^2
$$

$$
A_c = S^2 = 9.1^2 = 82.81 \ \text{m}^2
$$

$$
N = \left\lceil \frac{1000}{82.81} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 12.08 \right\rceil = 13 \ \text{detectors}
$$

**Step 4 — Propose a grid layout (2 marks):**
Choose a uniform grid: along 40 m length, use 5 detectors spaced 8 m apart (with 4 m wall margin each side, total 4 × 8 + 2 × 4 = 40 m ✓). Along 25 m width, use 3 detectors spaced 8 m apart (with 4.5 m wall margin each side, total 2 × 8 + 2 × 4.5 = 25 m ✓).

**Grid layout: 5 × 3 = 15 detectors** (slightly more than the minimum 13 to ensure clean alignment and meet wall-margin requirements).

| Wall | W1 | D1 | D2 | D3 | D4 | W2 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Row 1** (y = 4.5 m) | Wall | 4 m | 8 m | 8 m | 8 m | 4 m → 40 m ✓ |
| **Row 2** (y = 12.5 m) | Wall | 4 m | 8 m | 8 m | 8 m | 4 m |
| **Row 3** (y = 20.5 m) | Wall | 4 m | 8 m | 8 m | 8 m | 4 m |

**Total: 15 detectors. Justification:** The grid provides uniform coverage, satisfies the 0.5 S wall-margin rule (4.5 m ≥ 0.5 × 8 = 4 m ✓), and gives a small engineering margin to handle cable routing and ceiling obstructions (e.g., AC diffusers, light fittings).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [ALTERNATIVE CHOICE]
**[KTU University Exam – Module 2, June 2024, Q3]**

**(a)** With a **neat functional block diagram**, describe the operation of an **Aspirating Smoke Detection (ASD) system** such as VESDA. State two distinct advantages over point-type detectors. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **data centre** of size **20 m × 15 m × 4 m** (L × B × H) is to be protected by an ASD system. The required sensitivity is **0.05 % obs/m**. The pipe network has **8 sampling holes** of equal size. The transport time from the most remote hole to the detector must not exceed **60 seconds**. The pipe diameter is **21 mm (internal)**. Verify whether the proposed design is acceptable, given a transport velocity requirement of **1 m/s minimum** through each sampling hole. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution (a):**

**Block diagram (2 marks):** A VESDA-type ASD system has the following functional blocks:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Protected Area: Data Centre] -->|Air drawn by suction| B[PVC Sampling Pipe Network]
    B --> C[Sampling Hole 1]
    B --> D[Sampling Hole 2]
    B --> E[Sampling Hole N]
    C --> F[Inlet Manifold]
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G[Two-Stage Filtration: Filter 1 and Filter 2]
    G --> H[Laser Detection Chamber with 680 nm laser]
    H --> I[Photodiode and Signal Processing]
    I --> J{Four Alarm Thresholds: Alert, Action, Fire 1, Fire 2}
    J -->|Any threshold exceeded| K[FACP: Fire Alarm Panel]
    L[High-Efficiency Aspirator Blower] -->|Provides suction| B
    L --> M[Exhaust to safe location]
```

**Operation in words (3 marks):**

1. A **high-efficiency aspirator blower** continuously draws air from the protected zone through a **network of PVC pipes** (typically 21 mm or 25 mm diameter) fitted with **calibrated sampling holes** spaced at regular intervals.

2. The air passes through a **two-stage filter** to remove dust and contaminants, protecting the laser chamber.

3. The cleaned air enters a **laser-based detection chamber** where a **680 nm laser diode** illuminates any smoke particles. The scattered light is captured by a **photodiode** and processed.

4. The **signal processor** compares the obscuration level against **four programmable alarm thresholds** — *Alert* (e.g., 0.01 % obs/m), *Action* (0.1 %), *Fire 1* (1 %), *Fire 2* (5 %). This **multi-level warning** allows staged response: investigate, dispatch, evacuate, suppress.

5. The detector transmits the alarm and the smoke density reading to the **FACP** for action.

**Two distinct advantages over point-type detectors (2 marks):**
1. **Sensitivity 100–1000× higher** (down to 0.001 % obs/m) than spot detectors (typically 0.05–4 % obs/m). This is critical in data centres where any smoke is a major event.
2. **Continuous air sampling and multi-level warning** — gives building managers lead time of **minutes to hours** before the fire becomes flaming, allowing investigation and controlled response.
3. *(Optional third advantage for discussion)* Detection is independent of ceiling height, air stratification, or smoke dilution — works equally well in high bays, atriums, and concealed spaces.

---

#### **Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 — Pipe cross-sectional area (1 mark):**

$$
A_{pipe} = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.021)^2 = 3.464 \times 10^{-4} \ \text{m}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Total pipe length estimate (1 mark):**
For a perimeter loop around the data centre ceiling, approximate pipe length:
$L_{pipe} \approx 2(20 + 15) = 70 \ \text{m}$ (perimeter only; branch lines add more; assume 100 m total for a single loop with branches).

**Step 3 — Volumetric flow rate at the detector (1 mark):**
A typical VESDA detector aspirates at 30–120 L/min. Take 60 L/min = $1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ m³/s.

$$
Q = 1.0 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 4 — Velocity in main pipe (1 mark):**

$$
v_{pipe} = \frac{Q}{A_{pipe}} = \frac{1.0 \times 10^{-3}}{3.464 \times 10^{-4}} = 2.89 \ \text{m/s}
$$

This exceeds the 1 m/s minimum ✓

**Step 5 — Velocity through each of the 8 sampling holes (1 mark):**
By continuity, flow divides among 8 holes. Assuming uniform holes, each carries $Q/8$:

$$
v_{hole} = \frac{Q/8}{A_{hole}}
$$

For a typical 2 mm sampling hole:

$$
A_{hole} = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.002)^2 = 3.14 \times 10^{-6} \ \text{m}^2
$$

$$
v_{hole} = \frac{1.25 \times 10^{-4}}{3.14 \times 10^{-6}} = 39.8 \ \text{m/s}
$$

This is well above the 1 m/s transport velocity requirement ✓ (note: very high hole velocity is actually undesirable for representative sampling, indicating that **more or larger holes** are needed for better balance — but the design as proposed is *acceptable* for transport time).

**Step 6 — Transport time (1 mark):**
For 100 m of pipe at 2.89 m/s:

$$
t = \frac{L}{v} = \frac{100}{2.89} = 34.6 \ \text{s} \leq 60 \ \text{s} \ \checkmark
$$

**Conclusion:** The proposed ASD design is **acceptable** — the transport time (34.6 s) is well within the 60 s limit, and the main pipe velocity (2.89 m/s) exceeds the 1 m/s minimum.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students typically lose marks on Fire Detection questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "smoke detector" with "fire detector"** — strictly, smoke/heat/flame sensors are *initiating devices*; the *FACP* declares "fire". Lose 1 mark for imprecision.
> 2. **Forgetting wall-margin and ceiling-height reduction rules** — examiners expect mention of **0.5 S wall margin** and **spacing reduction for H > 4 m** in detector layout problems. Lose 2 marks.
> 3. **No justification of detector choice** — when asked "which detector?", you must justify with the fire signature, environment, and cost. Generic answers score 4/7, not 7/7.
> 4. **Skipping the time-line (T0 → T5)** in sequence-of-operation questions — examiners expect explicit time-stamps and identification of *which* tier does *what*. Lose 2 marks for vagueness.
> 5. **Confusing Class A vs Class B wiring** — Class A (loop) is redundant; Class B (radial) is not. A 14-mark addressable system question will fail without this distinction.
> 6. **Not citing the standard** (IS 2189, IS 11360, NFPA 72) — always quote the relevant clause number. The KTU board values *standard-aware* answers.
> 7. **Mixing up ionization and photoelectric sensitivities** — recall: **ionization is for flaming**, **photoelectric is for smoldering**. Reverse this, lose 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Fire Detection (Module 2)**

- ✅ **Fire Detection = identifying the by-products** of combustion — **smoke, heat, flame, gas** — and transmitting an alarm to the FACP.
- ✅ **Time-to-Detect (TTD)** is the key performance metric. Minimize it to maximize **ASET** relative to **RSET**.
- ✅ **Four-tier hierarchy:** Initiating Devices → Signaling (IDC/SLC) → FACP → Notification & Control Outputs.
- ✅ **Conventional vs Addressable:** Conventional = zone-level, binary; Addressable = device-level, analog, intelligent.
- ✅ **Class A loop wiring** provides single-fault tolerance — a hallmark of addressable systems.
- ✅ **Detector types and their preferred fires:**
  - **Ionization** → small-particle flaming fires
  - **Photoelectric** → smoldering fires (large particles)
  - **VESDA / ASD** → clean rooms, data centres (very high sensitivity)
  - **Beam smoke** → high bays, atriums (> 10 m ceiling)
  - **IR3 / UV** → outdoor hydrocarbon fires
  - **LHDC** → tunnels, cable trays
  - **Multi-criteria** → modern, low false-alarm environments
- ✅ **Critical standards:** IS 2189 (installation), IS 11360 (heat detectors), NFPA 72 (US), EN 54 (EU).
- ✅ **Smoke obscuration equation** (Beer–Lambert): $I = I_0 e^{-\sigma L}$ — applies to all optical smoke detection.
- ✅ **Detector count formula:** $N = \lceil A_{total} / S^2 \rceil$, with **wall margin = 0.5 S** and **ceiling height reduction for H > 4 m**.
- ✅ **Spacing rules (smooth ceiling):** Smoke = **9.1 m**, Heat = **6.1 m**, Spacing reduction $D = 0.4 (H - 0.3)$ for H > 4 m.
- ✅ **Notification:** $L_{alarm} \geq L_{ambient} + 15$ dB; minimum **75 dB at 3 m**.
- ✅ **FACP functions:** polling, alarm verification (30 s), drift compensation, four alarm thresholds, BMS integration, event logging.
- ✅ **Drift compensation** is essential for long-term reliability — addresses dust-induced baseline shift.
- ✅ **Alarm verification** is the FACP's second-confirmation mechanism to suppress nuisance alarms.
- ✅ **Multi-sensor (multi-criteria) detectors** combine smoke + heat + CO for **lowest false-alarm rate** — used in modern smart buildings.
- ✅ **Always cite the standard and clause number** in your KTU answers — it shows professional depth.
- ✅ **Fire Detection is the *first link* in the life-safety chain** — without it, suppression and evacuation cannot activate in time.

---

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module 2

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# MODULE 2 — SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Automatic Fire Detection System (AFDS) — Formal Definition

An **Automatic Fire Detection System (AFDS)** is an integrated network of *sensing devices*, *control equipment*, *notification appliances*, and *interfacing wiring/power supplies* that continuously monitors a defined *protected premises* for the physical signatures of incipient combustion, processes the signal, and initiates appropriate pre-programmed fire-response actions (alarm, evacuation, suppression release, HVAC shut-off, fire-fighter's lift homing, etc.) **without human intervention**.

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Scheme — Working Definition
> Per **IS 2189: 2008 (Code of Practice for Installation of Automatic Fire Detection and Alarm System)**, an AFDS is a *system* — not a single gadget. It includes the **Fire Alarm Control Panel (FACP)**, **initiating devices (detectors, manual call points)**, **notification appliances (sounders, beacons)**, and **ancillary services (power, wiring, controls)**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Always-Awake Watchman"

Imagine a hospital ward. A *human* watchman sleeps, takes breaks, can be bribed, smokes on duty, or simply cannot smell the first wisps of smoke. An AFDS is like an **immortal, tireless, hyper-sensitive watchman placed in every room** — sniffing the air (smoke), feeling the temperature (heat), and watching for the flicker of flame (optical). The instant the watchman notices the *very first symptom* of fire, it shouts, calls the brigade, locks the doors, and can even trigger sprinklers — all in **seconds**, before the human occupants have even registered a smell.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "Automatic"?** Human detection (by smell, sight, or touch) of a fire typically occurs at a *fully developed* stage when survival of occupants is at risk. AFDS detects the **incipient (pre-flashover)** stage, where toxic gases and heat have not yet overwhelmed the occupants. **Early detection = early warning = life saved.**

## 1.3 The Need and Importance of an AFDS — Why is it Indispensable?

A modern building is a **complex, enclosed, high-energy-density environment**. The combination of *electrical wiring, IT loads, kitchen appliances, stored combustibles, and synthetic furnishings* ensures that *fire risk* is a constant, statistical reality. Manual vigilance cannot be relied upon, and so an AFDS is required for the following engineering, social, legal, and economic reasons:

| # | Aspect | Reason for AFDS Necessity |
|---|--------|---------------------------|
| 1 | **Human Physiology** | Humans sleep, are incapacitated, or are intoxicated. Toxic gases (e.g., **HCN, CO**) cause unconsciousness in **2–4 minutes**, before any visible smoke. |
| 2 | **Time to Flashover** | A typical upholstered chair reaches **flashover in ~3–5 minutes**. Detection at the *smoldering stage* (0–60 s) gives 4–5 extra minutes. |
| 3 | **Life Safety** | ASET (Available Safe Egress Time) > RSET (Required Safe Egress Time). AFDS **widens ASET** by early detection. |
| 4 | **Property Protection** | Direct fire loss in India is >₹25,000 crore/year. Early detection reduces average loss by **60–80 %**. |
| 5 | **Code Compliance** | **NBC 2016 (Part 4, Fire & Life Safety)**, **TAC**, and **local fire service rules** mandate AFDS for buildings above 15 m or with occupancy > 300. |
| 6 | **Integration with BMS** | AFDS can interface with HVAC dampers, smoke extractors, lifts, and access-control doors for a holistic **Fire Command & Control**. |
| 7 | **Business Continuity** | Insurance premiums drop by **30–50 %** with certified AFDS. |
| 8 | **Suppression Pre-Action** | Pre-action sprinklers, water-mist, and clean-agent systems need **reliable detection signals** before discharge. |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Hint:** Whenever the question asks *"Why AFDS?"* — always answer with the triplet: **(i) Life Safety, (ii) Property Protection, (iii) Statutory Compliance.** Examiners love this triplet.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# MODULE 2 — SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Principle of Detection — Fire Signatures

A fire, from the moment of *ignition*, produces a unique bouquet of physical and chemical "fingerprints." The AFDS exploits these. A detector is, fundamentally, a **transducer** that converts one or more of these signatures into an electrical signal.

The classical **fire tetrahedron** (Fuel + Heat + Oxygen + Uninhibited Chain Reaction) tells us what signatures to look for:

| Fire Signature | Detector Category | Phenomenon Exploited |
|----------------|-------------------|----------------------|
| **Solid particulates (aerosol, smoke)** | Smoke Detector (Ionization, Photoelectric, Aspirating) | Light scattering or ionization current change |
| **Convected hot gases (hot air)** | Heat Detector (Fixed Temp, Rate-of-Rise) | Thermal expansion, melting, bimetal deflection |
| **Electromagnetic radiation (IR, UV, Visible)** | Flame Detector (IR, UV, IR/IR, Visual) | Photon emission from C–H and OH* radicals |
| **Combustion gases (CO, CO₂, HCN)** | Gas Detector (Electrochemical, MOS) | Redox reaction on sensor element |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A detector activates when the **measured signal exceeds a pre-set threshold** (fixed threshold logic) **OR** when the **rate of change of the signal exceeds a pre-set rate** (rate-of-rise logic). Modern addressable systems also use *drift compensation* and *fuzzy logic* to suppress false alarms.

## 2.2 Classification of Fire Detectors — The Big Picture

The IS 2189 / EN 54 / NFPA 72 family of standards classifies detectors as follows:

```
FIRE DETECTORS
├── (A) HEAT DETECTORS
│     ├── A1. Fixed-Temperature
│     │      ├── Fusible-link type
│     │      ├── Bimetallic-strip type
│     │      ├── Glass-bulb (frangible) type
│     │      └── Continuous-Line / Pneumatic type
│     ├── A2. Rate-of-Rise (RoR)
│     │      ├── Pneumatic / Diaphragm type
│     │      └── Thermoelectric (thermistor) type
│     └── A3. Rate-Compensated (Combination Fixed + RoR)
│
├── (B) SMOKE DETECTORS
│     ├── B1. Ionization Chamber
│     ├── B2. Photoelectric (Light-Scattering)
│     ├── B3. Beam (Projected-Beam) Detector
│     └── B4. Aspirating Smoke Detection (ASD / VESDA)
│
├── (C) FLAME DETECTORS
│     ├── C1. UV (1.0–3.5 × 10⁻⁷ m)
│     ├── C2. IR (4.4 × 10⁻⁶ m for CO₂ band)
│     ├── C3. IR/IR (dual band)
│     └── C4. UV/IR (combined)
│
├── (D) GAS DETECTORS
│     ├── D1. CO (Carbon Monoxide)
│     ├── D2. CO₂ (NDIR)
│     └── D3. Multi-gas (Electrochemical / MOS)
│
└── (E) MULTI-CRITERIA / MULTI-SENSOR DETECTORS
      (Heat + Smoke + CO + Optical all in one housing)
```

> [!NOTE]
> For this module, we focus exhaustively on **Category (A) — Heat Detectors**, as per the KTU syllabus directive.

## 2.3 Heat Detectors — Deep Dive

A **heat detector** is a device that responds to an *abnormal increase in temperature* (absolute) or an *abnormal rate of temperature rise* (differential) caused by a fire. It is the **simplest, most rugged, and least expensive** of all detector types, but also the **slowest** because it requires a substantial thermal disturbance to actuate.

### 2.3.1 Type 1 — Fixed-Temperature Heat Detector

**Operating Principle:** When the air temperature at the detector reaches a pre-calibrated threshold (e.g., **57 °C, 68 °C, 79 °C, 93 °C** per IS 2175), the detector's sensing element undergoes a **one-way, irreversible** physical change (melting, breaking, or bi-metallic snap) and triggers the alarm.

**Sub-Types:**

1. **Fusible-Link Type** — A low-melting alloy (e.g., Wood's metal, melting at ~70 °C) holds a spring-loaded contact. On melting, the spring releases, closing the alarm circuit.
2. **Bimetallic Strip Type** — Two metals with different thermal expansion coefficients (e.g., **Invar + Brass**) are bonded. On heating, the strip bends and *snaps* (creep-action) into a contact, latching the alarm.
3. **Glass-Bulb (Frangible) Type** — A small glass bulb filled with a coloured heat-sensitive liquid sits across a sprinkler orifice or a detector contact. When the bulb breaks, the alarm circuit closes (also commonly used in sprinkler heads).
4. **Continuous-Line (Pneumatic / Hydrastep) Type** — A thin, sealed copper or stainless-steel tube runs along the protected area. The tube is filled with a gas (air, N₂, or CO₂) at a reference pressure. Heating causes the gas to expand; the resulting pressure rise is sensed at the *terminal pressure switch* and the alarm triggers. **Used for cable tunnels, conveyor belts, transformer yards.**

### 2.3.2 Type 2 — Rate-of-Rise (RoR) Heat Detector

**Operating Principle:** The detector has **two thermally isolated chambers** (or thermistors) that sense the ambient temperature. The control electronics continuously compute the time-derivative $dT/dt$. If $dT/dt \geq 8.3 \, ^\circ\text{C/min}$ (i.e., **15 °F/min**, the IS 2175 threshold), the alarm is raised. The detector uses a *pneumatic* (air-bleed vent) or *electronic* (dual thermistor bridge) technique to maintain a *thermal lag* between the two sensing points, so that only a rapid rise — and not the slow daily cycle — is detected.

### 2.3.3 Type 3 — Rate-Compensated (Combination) Heat Detector

A hybrid that combines **fixed-temperature** AND **rate-of-rise** functions in a single housing. It is the **most popular** for general indoor applications. Typical *UL 539* ratings: **57 °C fixed + 8.3 °C/min RoR**.

### 2.3.4 Spacing & Location (NBC / IS 2189)

| Parameter | Typical Value |
|-----------|---------------|
| Maximum spacing (smooth ceiling, $\text{SR} \le 0.083$) | **9 m** for 57 °C; **7.5 m** for 68 °C and above |
| Minimum spacing from wall | **150 mm** |
| Maximum distance from wall (one-wall location) | **4.5 m** |
| Mounting height range | **Up to 9 m** (heat) — beyond this, use beam or ASD |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations form the analytical core of Module 2 for Heat Detectors. Each will be derived and applied in Sections 3 & 5.

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Symbols |
|---|---------|-------------|-----------------|
| 1 | $\tau = \dfrac{RTI}{\sqrt{u_g}} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{T_g - T_0}{T_g - T_{det}}\right)$ | **Heskestad–Schleich Activation Time Equation** for heat detector | $\tau$ = time to activation (s); $RTI$ = Response Time Index $\big((\text{m}\cdot\text{s})^{1/2}\big)$; $u_g$ = ceiling-jet gas velocity (m/s); $T_g$ = gas temperature (K); $T_0$ = ambient (K); $T_{det}$ = detector activation temperature (K) |
| 2 | $\Delta T_{max} = 16.9 \cdot \dfrac{Q^{2/3}}{H^{5/3}}$ | **Alpert's near-field ceiling jet temperature** ($R/H = 0$) | $\Delta T$ in °C; $Q$ = HRR in **kW**; $H$ = ceiling height in **m** |
| 3 | $\Delta T = 5.38 \cdot \dfrac{(Q/R)^{2/3}}{H}$ | **Alpert's far-field ceiling jet temperature** ($R/H \ge 0.18$) | $R$ = radial distance from fire axis (m) |
| 4 | $u_{g,0} = 0.96 \cdot \dfrac{Q^{1/3}}{H^{1/3}}$ | **Alpert's near-field velocity** | m/s |
| 5 | $u_g = 0.20 \cdot \dfrac{(Q/H)^{1/3}}{(R/H)^{5/6}}$ | **Alpert's far-field velocity** | m/s |
| 6 | $RTI = \dfrac{m \cdot c_p}{h_{ref} A_{ref} \sqrt{u_{ref}}}$ | **Definition of RTI** | $u_{ref} = 1.5 \text{ m/s (canonical)}$ |
| 7 | $L = 0.25 H \quad \text{to}\quad 0.5 H$ | **Detector minimum operating height above fire** for fast fires | $H$ = ceiling height (m) |
| 8 | $\text{Sensitivity} = \dfrac{1}{RTI}$ | Inverse measure (faster detector ⇒ higher sensitivity) | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Constants to Memorise for KTU Exam:**
> - $u_{ref} = 1.5 \text{ m/s} \;(= 5 \text{ ft/s})$
> - RoR threshold: $8.3 \;^\circ\text{C/min} \; (\equiv 15 \;^\circ\text{F/min})$
> - Standard fixed-temp ratings: **57, 68, 79, 93 °C**
> - Specific heat of brass ≈ $380 \text{ J/(kg·K)}$

# MODULE 2 — SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Heskestad–Schleich Heat-Detector Response Equation

The activation time of a heat detector is governed by the **lumped-capacitance energy balance** on its sensing element.

### Step 1 — Lumped-Capacitance Energy Balance

Let the detector's sensing element be a small body of mass $m$ and specific heat $c_p$, exposed to the ceiling-jet gas at temperature $T_g$ and velocity $u_g$. The net convective heat gain raises the element's temperature $T_d(t)$:

$$m \, c_p \, \frac{dT_d}{dt} = h \, A \, (T_g - T_d)$$

where $h$ is the convective heat-transfer coefficient and $A$ is the projected area of the element.

### Step 2 — Heskestad's Heat-Transfer Correlation

For a small cylindrical element in cross-flow, the Nusselt number is:

$$Nu = 0.037 \, Re^{0.8} \, Pr^{0.33}$$

For a Re evaluated using the ceiling-jet velocity $u_g$ and a characteristic element diameter $D$:

$$h = \frac{Nu \cdot k_g}{D} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; h \propto u_g^{0.8}$$

To linearise and make the analysis tractable, Heskestad proposed an *equivalent* linearised heat-transfer coefficient, expressed relative to a **reference velocity** $u_{ref}$:

$$h \cdot A = h_{ref} \, A_{ref} \, \left(\frac{u_g}{u_{ref}}\right)^{1/2}$$

The square-root dependence (not 0.8) is a *Heskestad idealisation* that yields a closed-form analytical solution. The reference velocity is fixed by convention at:

$$u_{ref} = 1.5 \text{ m/s} \quad (\equiv 5 \text{ ft/s})$$

### Step 3 — Definition of the Response Time Index (RTI)

By definition:

$$RTI \;\equiv\; \frac{m \, c_p}{h_{ref} \, A_{ref} \, \sqrt{u_{ref}}}$$

Units: $\dfrac{\text{kg} \cdot \text{J/(kg·K)}}{\big(\text{W/(m}^2\cdot\text{K)}\big) \cdot \text{m}^2 \cdot \sqrt{\text{m/s}}} = \big(\text{m·s}\big)^{1/2}$

So the energy balance becomes:

$$\frac{m \, c_p}{h_{ref} \, A_{ref} \, \sqrt{u_{ref}}} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{u_g}} \cdot \frac{dT_d}{dt} = (T_g - T_d)$$

$$\frac{RTI}{\sqrt{u_g}} \cdot \frac{dT_d}{dt} = (T_g - T_d)$$

### Step 4 — Integration to Activation

Assume $T_g$ is approximately *quasi-steady* (valid for $t$ much shorter than the time-scale of fire growth). Integrating from $T_d(0) = T_0$ to $T_d(\tau) = T_{det}$:

$$\int_{T_0}^{T_{det}} \frac{dT_d}{T_g - T_d} = \int_{0}^{\tau} \frac{\sqrt{u_g}}{RTI} \, dt$$

$$-\ln(T_g - T_d) \Big|_{T_0}^{T_{det}} = \frac{\sqrt{u_g}}{RTI} \, \tau$$

$$\ln(T_g - T_0) - \ln(T_g - T_{det}) = \frac{\sqrt{u_g}}{RTI} \, \tau$$

$$\boxed{\; \tau \;=\; \frac{RTI}{\sqrt{u_g}} \, \ln\!\left(\frac{T_g - T_0}{T_g - T_{det}}\right) \;}$$

This is the **Heskestad–Schleich equation** — used worldwide in fire-engineering calculations and built into tools like **DETACT-T₂** and **DETACT-QS**.

### Step 3.5 — Worked Numerical Example (Required for KTU Numericals)

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Pattern:** A typical 7-mark numerical on heat detectors follows the format below. Master this template.

**Problem:** A corridor ceiling is at $H = 4 \text{ m}$. A fire produces a **Heat Release Rate (HRR)** of $Q = 1000 \text{ kW}$. A heat detector with $RTI = 25 \, (\text{m·s})^{1/2}$ and activation temperature $T_{det} = 57 \, ^\circ\text{C}$ is located at a horizontal distance $R = 3 \text{ m}$ from the fire axis. Ambient temperature $T_0 = 27 \, ^\circ\text{C}$ (300 K). Calculate the time to activation. Use Alpert's ceiling jet correlations.

**Solution:**

**Step (a) — Check the geometric regime:**

$$\frac{R}{H} = \frac{3}{4} = 0.75 \ge 0.18 \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \text{Use FAR-FIELD correlations.}$$

**Step (b) — Compute ceiling jet temperature rise** (Alpert's far-field):

$$\Delta T = 5.38 \cdot \frac{(Q/R)^{2/3}}{H}$$

$$\frac{Q}{R} = \frac{1000}{3} = 333.33 \text{ kW/m}$$

$$\left(\frac{Q}{R}\right)^{2/3} = (333.33)^{2/3} = \left[(333.33)^{1/3}\right]^2 = (6.93)^2 \approx 48.03$$

$$\Delta T = 5.38 \cdot \frac{48.03}{4} = 64.6 \;^\circ\text{C}$$

$$T_g = T_0 + \Delta T = 27 + 64.6 = 91.6 \;^\circ\text{C}$$

**Step (c) — Compute ceiling jet velocity** (Alpert's far-field):

$$u_g = 0.20 \cdot \frac{(Q/H)^{1/3}}{(R/H)^{5/6}}$$

$$\frac{Q}{H} = \frac{1000}{4} = 250 \text{ kW/m} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; (250)^{1/3} \approx 6.30$$

$$\frac{R}{H} = 0.75 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; (0.75)^{5/6} \approx 0.781$$

$$u_g = 0.20 \cdot \frac{6.30}{0.781} = 1.61 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step (d) — Apply the Heskestad–Schleich equation:**

$$\tau = \frac{RTI}{\sqrt{u_g}} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{T_g - T_0}{T_g - T_{det}}\right)$$

$$\tau = \frac{25}{\sqrt{1.61}} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{91.6 - 27}{91.6 - 57}\right)$$

$$\tau = \frac{25}{1.269} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{64.6}{34.6}\right)$$

$$\tau = 19.70 \cdot \ln(1.867) = 19.70 \cdot 0.6244$$

$$\boxed{\; \tau \;\approx\; 12.3 \text{ s} \;}$$

**Step (e) — Validation:** A 12.3-second response is reasonable for a 1 MW fire directly below a 4 m ceiling. The detector activates well before flashover, demonstrating the efficiency of a properly designed AFDS.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Pitfall (KTU Examiner's Watch):** Students often plug $T_g - T_0$ and $T_g - T_{det}$ in *Kelvin* without conversion. The equation is dimensionally valid in **either** °C **or** K, but the *ratio* inside the log is the same because the **differences** are absolute. The pitfall is using *non-difference* temperatures in the numerator — this causes dimensional error.

## 3.2 Analytic Comparison Matrix — Detector Selection by Fire Type (NBC 2016 / IS 2189)

| Fire Type / Material | Recommended Detector | Reasoning |
|----------------------|----------------------|-----------|
| Smoldering wood, paper, cloth (Type A — Class A) | Photoelectric Smoke / ASD | Large visible aerosols, low heat |
| Flammable liquid pool, oil (Type B — Class B) | Heat (RoR) or Flame (IR) | Fast flame, little smoke |
| Electrical switchgear room | Smoke + Heat (multi-criteria) | False-alarm sensitive; a/c flow |
| Kitchen (deep fryer, 200 °C+) | Heat (Fixed 93 °C) | Smoke detectors false-alarm |
| Cable tunnel, transformer yard | Line-type heat (pneumatic / fibre) | Wide-area, robust, ATEX |
| Storage of polyurethane foam | Aspirating Smoke (VESDA) | Very early, pre-combustion |
| Vehicle parking garage | Heat (Rate-compensated, 57 °C) | Combustion-engine exhaust false alarms |
| LPG / CNG bottling | Gas detector (IR / catalytic) | Heavier-than-air, explosive |
| High-ceiling (> 9 m) atrium | Beam smoke / ASD + flame | Heat slow at high H |

# MODULE 2 — SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Mark Distribution (Verified with 2024 Scheme ESE Pattern):**
> Module 2 weightage ≈ **15 % to 20 %** of full paper. Heat Detectors + Detection Principle = **guaranteed 14-mark question**.

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1 — *3 Marks*
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Similar Pattern]**

**"Define an Automatic Fire Detection System (AFDS). List its four major components."**

**Model Answer:**

An **AFDS** is a pre-engineered, self-acting network of sensors, control equipment, and notification devices that continuously monitors a protected premises for the physical signatures of incipient fire (smoke, heat, flame, gas) and triggers pre-programmed fire-response actions (alarm, suppression, evacuation) without human intervention, in compliance with **IS 2189: 2008** and **NBC 2016 (Part 4)**.

**Four major components:**

1. **Initiating Devices** — Detectors (heat, smoke, flame, gas) and manual call points.
2. **Fire Alarm Control Panel (FACP)** — The "brain"; receives signals, processes logic, and issues commands.
3. **Notification Appliances** — Sounders (≥ 65 dB(A) at 3 m), hooters, strobes, voice evacuation speakers.
4. **Power Supply & Wiring** — 24 V DC supply with **battery backup (≥ 4 hours standby + 15 min alarm)** per IS 2189.

---

### Question A.2 — *3 Marks*
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Similar Pattern]**

**"Distinguish between a Fixed-Temperature heat detector and a Rate-of-Rise heat detector. State one application where each is preferred."**

**Model Answer:**

| Criterion | Fixed-Temperature | Rate-of-Rise (RoR) |
|-----------|-------------------|---------------------|
| **Actuation logic** | Trips when $T \ge T_{set}$ (e.g., 57 °C) | Trips when $dT/dt \ge 8.3 \;^\circ\text{C/min}$ |
| **Response speed** | Slower (waits for full heating) | Faster (reacts to *gradient*) |
| **False alarms** | Low (slow thermal events ignored) | Moderate (sudden HVAC changes may cause trip) |
| **Suitability** | Slowly developing fires, kitchens | Rapidly developing fires, normal offices |
| **Preferred application** | Kitchen exhaust duct, paint shop | Office, classroom, server room (general) |

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Module Internal Choice (KTU Pattern)

> **Each question is internally optional.** Examiners offer **(a) and (b)** sub-parts. Both sub-parts are **compulsory**.

---

### CHOICE A — *14 Marks*

**Q. (a) Explain with a neat block diagram the working principle of an automatic fire detection system. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A fire produces four signatures — **smoke, heat, flame radiation, combustion gases**. Each *initiating device* (detector) continuously monitors one or more of these. The signal is conditioned, threshold-compared, and sent to the **FACP** via the **SLC (Signalling Line Circuit)** loop. The FACP runs a *decision algorithm* (fixed threshold, rate-of-rise, multi-criteria fuzzy logic, drift compensation). On confirmed fire, the FACP simultaneously:

- Activates **sounders & beacons** for occupant notification (≥ 65 dB(A) at 3 m).
- Sends a trip signal to **AVC / sprinkler pre-action valves**.
- Closes **HVAC dampers** to prevent smoke spread.
- **Homes the fireman's lift** to the designated floor.
- Auto-dials the **Fire Station / Brigade**.

> *[Insert Block Diagram from Section 4.1 — 2 marks]*

The system operates on **24 V DC** from a regulated power supply backed by sealed lead-acid batteries providing ≥ 4 hours standby + 15 min alarm. Modern addressable systems (Apollo, Notifier, Bosch) use *polling* on the SLC, with each detector having a unique address; the FACP then displays the *exact room and floor* of the fire. **(7 marks distributed: 2 for diagram, 3 for explanation, 1 for compliance, 1 for power/architecture.)**

---

**Q. (b) Classify fire detectors in detail. Discuss the working of any two types of heat detectors. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Classification (3 marks)** — as per **IS 2189 / EN 54 / NFPA 72** — see Section 4.2 tree:

- **Heat Detectors** — Fixed-Temperature, Rate-of-Rise, Rate-Compensated, Line-Type.
- **Smoke Detectors** — Ionization, Photoelectric, Beam, Aspirating (VESDA).
- **Flame Detectors** — UV, IR, IR/IR, UV/IR.
- **Gas Detectors** — CO (electrochemical), CO₂ (NDIR), combustible (catalytic).
- **Multi-Sensor / Multi-Criteria** — Combined smoke + heat + CO.

**Working of two heat detectors (2 × 2 = 4 marks):**

1. **Fusible-Link Fixed-Temperature Detector:** A low-melting alloy (e.g., **Wood's metal, mp ≈ 70 °C**) holds a spring-loaded contact. On reaching the alloy's melting point, the spring releases, closing the alarm circuit — *one-way, irreversible*. Standard ratings: **57 °C, 68 °C, 79 °C, 93 °C**.
2. **Bimetallic Strip Rate-Compensated Detector:** Two metals with different coefficients of thermal expansion (e.g., **Invar** and **Brass**) are bonded. Heat causes the strip to bend. A *creep-action* design ensures a *snap* action at a precise temperature, simultaneously indicating both a fixed-temperature AND a rate-of-rise condition. Used widely in modern ceiling-mounted detectors.

---

### CHOICE B — *14 Marks*

**Q. (a) With a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a pneumatic continuous-line heat detector. Where is it preferred? (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Construction (3 marks):**

A **continuous-line pneumatic heat detector** (e.g., *Harshaw / Spirig / Protectowire*) consists of a **3 mm OD copper or stainless-steel tube** filled with air, nitrogen, or CO₂ at a reference pressure. The tube is hermetically sealed at one end and connected to a **pressure-sensing diaphragm switch** at the other end. The tube is laid in a *zig-zag* pattern across the protected area (e.g., cable trays, conveyor galleries).

**Working (3 marks):**

Under normal conditions, the gas pressure is stable. When a *local hot spot* develops along the tube, the gas at that point expands, generating a **pressure wave** that propagates to the diaphragm switch. The switch trips, signalling the FACP. Modern versions use a *restoration* pump to re-establish reference pressure after the alarm condition, allowing **continuous service**. Some variants (*Fibre-Optic Linear Heat Detection, LHD*) use a fibre with a Bragg grating to detect distributed temperature.

**Preferred applications (1 mark):**

- Cable tunnels, switchgear rooms, transformer yards
- Conveyor belt galleries in coal-handling plants
- Car parks, parking decks, large warehouses
- Petrochemical storage tank farms (ATEX-rated versions)

---

**Q. (b) A heat detector installed on a 5 m ceiling has an RTI of 30 (m·s)¹ᐟ² and a fixed temperature rating of 68 °C. The fire HRR is 1500 kW and the detector is located 4 m horizontally from the fire centre. Ambient temperature is 30 °C. Estimate the activation time. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

> *(This is the same template as Section 3.5 with new numbers — solving here in exam-style step form)*

**Step 1 — Geometric check:**

$$\frac{R}{H} = \frac{4}{5} = 0.80 \ge 0.18 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{FAR-FIELD correlations.}$$

**Step 2 — Ceiling-jet temperature rise (Alpert's far-field):**

$$\Delta T = 5.38 \cdot \frac{(Q/R)^{2/3}}{H}$$

$$\frac{Q}{R} = \frac{1500}{4} = 375 \text{ kW/m}$$

$$(375)^{2/3} = (375^{1/3})^{2} = (7.211)^{2} \approx 52.0$$

$$\Delta T = 5.38 \cdot \frac{52.0}{5} = 56.1 \;^\circ\text{C}$$

$$T_g = 30 + 56.1 = 86.1 \;^\circ\text{C}$$

**Step 3 — Ceiling-jet velocity:**

$$u_g = 0.20 \cdot \frac{(Q/H)^{1/3}}{(R/H)^{5/6}}$$

$$(Q/H)^{1/3} = (1500/5)^{1/3} = (300)^{1/3} \approx 6.694$$

$$(R/H)^{5/6} = (0.80)^{5/6} \approx 0.829$$

$$u_g = 0.20 \cdot \frac{6.694}{0.829} = 1.615 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 4 — Heskestad–Schleich activation time:**

$$\tau = \frac{RTI}{\sqrt{u_g}} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{T_g - T_0}{T_g - T_{det}}\right)$$

$$\tau = \frac{30}{\sqrt{1.615}} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{86.1 - 30}{86.1 - 68}\right)$$

$$\tau = \frac{30}{1.271} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{56.1}{18.1}\right)$$

$$\tau = 23.60 \cdot \ln(3.10) = 23.60 \cdot 1.131$$

$$\boxed{\; \tau \;\approx\; 26.7 \text{ s} \;}$$

> The detector triggers in ~27 s, comfortably before flashover, validating the AFDS design.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes that COST MARKS in Heat-Detector Questions:**
>
> 1. **Unit confusion in $Q$:** Alpert's correlations require $Q$ in **kW** and $H$ in **m** for $\Delta T$ in **°C**. Using $Q$ in MW gives an answer off by a factor of $\sim 5$ — full marks lost.
> 2. **Forgetting the $R/H$ check:** Students blindly use the near-field equation for any geometry. ALWAYS check $R/H \ge 0.18$ first.
> 3. **Inverting the log argument:** The numerator must be $T_g - T_0$ and denominator $T_g - T_{det}$. Inverting makes the log negative — physically impossible.
> 4. **Ignoring the $\sqrt{u_g}$ factor in RTI equation:** A common error is writing $\tau = RTI \cdot \ln(\cdot)$ — wrong, missing the $\sqrt{u_g}$ in the denominator.
> 5. **Mistaking RTI for "response time":** RTI is a *property* of the detector (lower = faster), NOT the time itself. The actual time $\tau$ depends on $u_g$ and $T_g$.
> 6. **Saying "heat detectors detect smoke":** WRONG. Heat detectors ONLY sense temperature. Saying so loses full conceptual marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **AFDS = Detector + FACP + Notification + Power + Wiring** (5 components — remember all).
- The 4 fire signatures → **smoke, heat, flame, gas** — match each to its detector type.
- **Fixed-Temp** heat detectors trip on $T \ge T_{set}$ (57/68/79/93 °C); use fusible, bimetal, or frangible bulb.
- **Rate-of-Rise** heat detectors trip on $dT/dt \ge 8.3 \;^\circ\text{C/min}$ (15 °F/min).
- **Rate-Compensated** = Fixed + RoR in one housing (most common indoor type).
- **Continuous-line (pneumatic / LHD)** preferred for tunnels, conveyors, transformers.
- Maximum spacing of heat detector on smooth ceiling: **9 m** (57 °C) or **7.5 m** (≥ 68 °C).
- **Heskestad–Schleich activation time** equation (master it — high-yield KTU question):

$$\tau = \frac{RTI}{\sqrt{u_g}} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{T_g - T_0}{T_g - T_{det}}\right)$$

- **Alpert's ceiling-jet correlations** — use the **far-field** version when $R/H \ge 0.18$:

$$\Delta T = 5.38 \cdot \frac{(Q/R)^{2/3}}{H} \quad ; \quad u_g = 0.20 \cdot \frac{(Q/H)^{1/3}}{(R/H)^{5/6}}$$

- **Near-field** ($R/H < 0.18$): $\Delta T = 16.9 \cdot Q^{2/3}/H^{5/3}$, $u_g = 0.96 \cdot Q^{1/3}/H^{1/3}$.
- **RTI** units: $(\text{m·s})^{1/2}$. Lower RTI = faster detector. Standard values: 5 (fast), 25 (medium), 100 (slow).
- Reference velocity for RTI: $u_{ref} = 1.5 \text{ m/s}$ (5 ft/s).
- **Standards to cite in exam:** **IS 2189:2008**, **IS 2175:1988**, **NFPA 72**, **EN 54-5**, **NBC 2016 Part 4**.
- **Three pillars of AFDS necessity:** Life Safety + Property Protection + Statutory Compliance.
- Modern addressable systems: each detector has a *unique 8-bit address*; FACP shows the exact location.
- **Power backup** (per IS 2189): 24 V DC battery, **4 h standby + 15 min alarm** minimum.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fixed Temperature, Rate of Rise, Thermistor Rate of Rise and Rate Compensated Type Detectors. Smoke Detectors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT836 - DESIGN OF FIRE PROTECTION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Fixed Temperature, Rate of Rise, Thermistor Rate of Rise and Rate Compensated Type Detectors. Smoke Detectors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Fire Detectors — Classification Context

Under **NFPA 72** (National Fire Alarm and Signaling Code, also adopted by KTU referenced standards like IS 2189 and NBC 2016), automatic fire detectors are classified by the physical phenomenon they sense. In Module 2, we focus on **Heat Detectors** (Fixed Temperature, Rate-of-Rise, Thermistor, Rate Compensated) and **Smoke Detectors** (Ionization, Photoelectric, Beam, Aspirating).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Memorize Verbatim):**
> A *Heat Detector* is an **automatic fire-sensing device** that responds to the **thermal energy** (convective or conductive heat transfer) released by a fire, whereas a *Smoke Detector* is an **automatic fire-sensing device** that responds to the **aerosol particulates (smoke)** generated by combustion or pyrolysis.

---

## 1.2 Fixed Temperature Heat Detector

### Formal Definition
A **Fixed Temperature (FT) Heat Detector** is a non-restorable or restorable (depending on construction) device whose sensing element is calibrated to generate an **alarm signal when the ambient temperature at the detector reaches a pre-set threshold** (typically **57 °C, 68 °C, 74 °C, or 93 °C** per IS 2175 / EN 54-5 Class A1, A2, B, C, D, E).

### Conceptual Analogy 🧠
Imagine a **chocolate bar in a parked car on a hot summer day**. The chocolate does not "feel" the *speed* at which the temperature rose — it only melts once the cabin temperature crosses its melting point. A Fixed Temperature detector behaves identically: it has **no memory of how fast** the temperature rose, only whether it has **crossed a fixed threshold** or not.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The triggering element is usually a **eutectic solder**, a **bimetallic strip**, or a **glass bulb containing a heat-sensitive liquid** (the Frangible Bulb design). When the bulb liquid expands beyond a calibrated pressure/temperature, the bulb shatters, releasing a spring-loaded plunger to close the alarm circuit.

---

## 1.3 Rate of Rise (ROR) Heat Detector

### Formal Definition
A **Rate of Rise Heat Detector** is an automatic device that initiates an alarm when the **rate of increase of ambient air temperature exceeds a predetermined threshold**, regardless of the *initial* ambient temperature. The standard trip threshold is **8.3 °C/min (15 °F/min)** as per IS 2175 and NFPA 72.

### Conceptual Analogy
Picture the **accelerometer in your smartphone**. It does not care if your phone is stationary on a table or already moving at a constant velocity in a car — it fires the "step counter" *only* when it senses a sudden **change in motion (acceleration)**. A Rate of Rise detector is the thermal equivalent: it ignores a slow drift in temperature but triggers when the temperature *accelerates* upward.

> [!NOTE]
> The sensing mechanism is typically a **pneumatic (air-filled) chamber coupled with a vented diaphragm**. Rapid air expansion due to fast temperature rise deflects the diaphragm, closing electrical contacts. Slow temperature increases allow air to leak through a calibrated vent, equalizing pressure and preventing false alarm.

---

## 1.4 Thermistor Rate of Rise Detector

### Formal Definition
A **Thermistor Rate of Rise Detector** uses a **Negative Temperature Coefficient (NTC) thermistor** as the sensing element. Its resistance falls exponentially with temperature. The detector's electronic circuitry continuously computes the derivative $\dfrac{dT}{dt}$ and compares it with the configured **ROR threshold** (commonly **8 °C/min to 15 °C/min**).

### Working Principle
The bridge circuit measures instantaneous resistance, the microcontroller samples at fixed intervals (e.g., every **4 s**), applies a digital filter, and computes the temperature slope:

$$\frac{dT}{dt} \approx \frac{T(n) - T(n-1)}{\Delta t}$$

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of it as a **smart fitness watch measuring heart-rate acceleration**. A simple thermometer gives you current pulse — but the smart watch continuously *differentiates* the signal to detect exertion peaks. The thermistor ROR detector is the **"smart watch"** of the heat-detection world — replacing mechanical diaphragms with **solid-state electronics**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always mention the term *"electronic differential measurement"* in any 7-mark answer on thermistor detectors. It is a high-frequency examiner keyword.

---

## 1.5 Rate Compensated Detector

### Formal Definition
A **Rate Compensated Detector** combines the principles of both fixed temperature and rate-of-rise detection in a **single dual-element mechanical design**. It uses **two matched metal shells with different coefficients of thermal expansion**. The differential expansion (which is proportional to $dT/dt$) and the absolute expansion (proportional to $T$) are *both* mechanically summed in a single diaphragm assembly, so the device triggers when **either** a fixed threshold **or** an excessive rate is breached.

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine a **modern home security alarm with dual triggers**: it fires when the *front door opens* (fixed event) **OR** when motion sensors detect rapid movement inside (rate event). A rate-compensated detector is this **"OR-gate" philosophy** built into one mechanical body.

---

## 1.6 Smoke Detectors

A **Smoke Detector** senses the presence of **aerosol particulates** produced by combustion. The three principal sub-classes are:

| Sub-class | Sensing Mechanism | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Ionization Chamber** | Reduction of ion current by smoke aerosols | Fast-flaming fires |
| **Photoelectric (Optical)** | Light scattering by smoke particles | Smoldering fires |
| **Beam (Projected Beam)** | Obscuration of light beam across a gap | Atria, warehouses |
| **Aspirating (VESDA)** | Active air sampling + laser scattering | Clean rooms, data centers |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** A smoke detector is the **"nose"** of the fire alarm system. Just as our nose detects odor molecules via chemoreceptors, the smoke detector's chamber detects particulates via either **electrical current disruption** (ionization) or **light scattering** (photoelectric).

### Ionization Chamber Detector — Formal Definition
Contains a small radioactive source (**Americium-241**, activity ≈ **0.9 µCi / 33 kBq**) that ionizes air between two electrodes, creating a steady **microampere current**. When smoke aerosols enter, they attach to ions, **reducing the current**. A threshold drop (typically **50%**) triggers the alarm.

### Photoelectric Detector — Formal Definition
Contains a **light-emitting diode (LED)** and a **photodiode** arranged at an angle (typically **90°**). In clear air, light from the LED does not strike the photodiode. When smoke scatters photons into the photodiode's field of view, the photodiode voltage rises above a preset threshold, generating an alarm.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fixed Temperature Detector — Operational Physics

The triggering physics is governed by **conductive heat transfer** from the surrounding hot gas to the detector's sensing element. The lumped-capacitance model gives:

$$T(t) - T_{\infty} = (T_0 - T_{\infty}) \exp\!\left(-\frac{hA}{\rho V c_p}\,t\right)$$

where:
- $T(t)$ = sensing element temperature at time $t$
- $T_{\infty}$ = surrounding gas temperature
- $T_0$ = initial element temperature
- $h$ = convective heat transfer coefficient (W/m²·K)
- $A$ = surface area, $\rho V c_p$ = thermal mass of element

The detector trips when $T(t) \geq T_{set}$, i.e., the **fixed temperature threshold**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why doesn't the detector trip exactly at $T_{set}$?**
> Because the detector has **thermal inertia**, there is a finite *time lag* between the surrounding gas reaching $T_{set}$ and the internal element reaching $T_{set}$. This delay is quantified by the **Response Time Index (RTI)** — a critical KTU parameter.

### Response Time Index (RTI)

The RTI is defined as:

$$\tau \, \sqrt{u} \;=\; \text{RTI} \;=\; \text{const.}$$

where:
- $\tau$ = time for the detector to reach alarm temperature minus the gas time constant
- $u$ = gas velocity at the detector (m/s)

$$\boxed{\;\tau \;=\; \frac{\text{RTI}}{\sqrt{u}}\;}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RTI Categories (per NFPA 72):**
> - **RTI < 50 m$^{1/2}$·s$^{1/2}$** → **Fast Response** (sprinkler / spot detector equivalent)
> - **RTI = 50 – 80** → **Special Application**
> - **RTI > 80** → **Standard Response**

---

## 2.2 Rate of Rise Detector — Operational Physics

The pneumatic chamber behaves as a **thermally insulated, vented gas volume**. Applying the ideal gas law at constant volume with a slow leak:

$$\frac{dP}{dt} \;=\; \frac{nR}{V}\,\frac{dT}{dt} \;-\; k_{\text{leak}}\,(P - P_0)$$

For *fast* temperature rises, the leak term is negligible, so the pressure rise is **directly proportional to $dT/dt$**:

$$\Delta P \;\approx\; \frac{P_0}{T_0}\,\Delta T$$

The diaphragm deflects when $\Delta P > \Delta P_{\text{trip}}$, equivalent to $dT/dt > 8.3\,°C/\text{min}$.

---

## 2.3 Thermistor Rate of Rise — Solid-State Equivalent

For an NTC thermistor, the **Steinhart-Hart equation** governs the resistance-temperature relationship:

$$\frac{1}{T} \;=\; A + B \ln(R) + C \,[\ln(R)]^3$$

with $T$ in Kelvin. The detector circuit typically linearizes this via a **Wheatstone bridge** and uses the differential $\Delta T / \Delta t$ computed over a rolling time window.

---

## 2.4 Rate Compensated — Combined Trigger Logic

The dual-element shell has inner shell coefficient $\alpha_1$ and outer shell $\alpha_2$. The differential displacement $x$ at the diaphragm is:

$$x \;=\; L\,(\alpha_1 - \alpha_2)\,\Delta T \;+\; L\,\alpha_1\,T_0$$

This gives a **displacement proportional to both the absolute temperature (fixed-T component) and its rate (ROR component)**.

---

## 2.5 Smoke Detector — Physical Principles

### 2.5.1 Ionization Chamber — Current Equation

Steady-state current in clean air:

$$I_0 \;=\; n_e\,e\,v_d\,A_c$$

where:
- $n_e$ = ion density (ion pairs / m³)
- $v_d$ = ion drift velocity (m/s)
- $A_c$ = electrode collection area (m²)

When smoke enters, ions attach to particles (charge neutralization), reducing $n_e$. The alarm fires when:

$$I \;<\; 0.5 \, I_0 \quad \text{(50% obscuration trip)}$$

### 2.5.2 Photoelectric — Scattering Equation (Mie Regime)

For a smoke particle of diameter $d_p$ in the Mie scattering regime ($d_p \approx \lambda$), the scattered intensity at angle $\theta$ is:

$$I_{\text{scat}}(\theta) \;=\; \frac{I_0 \, \lambda^2}{8\pi^2 r^2}\,\bigl[i_1(\theta) + i_2(\theta)\bigr]\,N\,V$$

where $i_1, i_2$ are Mie intensity functions and $N$ is the particle number density.

### 2.5.3 Obscuration (% per foot) — KTU Favorite

The **percent obscuration per foot** is a standard smoke measurement:

$$\%\,\text{Obs/ft} \;=\; \left(1 - 10^{-\,\sigma \, c}\right)\times 100$$

where:
- $\sigma$ = specific extinction coefficient (m²/g)
- $c$ = smoke mass concentration (g/m³)

A typical **alarm threshold** is **1.5 %/ft to 2.5 %/ft** for spot-type photoelectric detectors.

### 2.5.4 Beam Detector — Beer-Lambert Law

Across a beam path of length $L$ (typically **3 m to 100 m**), the received power is:

$$\frac{P_r}{P_t} \;=\; \exp(-\sigma \, c \, L)$$

Alarm is generated when $P_r / P_t < 0.85$ (i.e., **15% obscuration**).

### 2.5.5 Ceiling Jet Temperature — Alpert's Correlation (Crucial for Detector Spacing!)

For a fire of **heat release rate $Q$** at a ceiling of height $H$, the maximum ceiling-jet temperature rise above ambient at radial distance $r$ is:

$$\boxed{\;\Delta T_{\max} \;=\; \frac{5.38}{H}\,\left(\frac{Q^2}{r^2\,H}\right)^{\!1/3} \;=\; \frac{5.38\,Q^{2/3}}{r^{2/3}\,H^{5/3}}\;}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the single most-asked formula in KTU Fire Protection Design papers.** You will use it to compute the radius of detector activation around a fire plume.

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Units / Notes** |
|---|---|---|
| Detector activation time (lumped) | $T(t)-T_\infty = (T_0-T_\infty)\,e^{-hAt/\rho V c_p}$ | °C, time in s |
| RTI activation time | $\tau = \dfrac{\text{RTI}}{\sqrt{u}}$ | s ; RTI in m$^{1/2}$·s$^{1/2}$ |
| Fixed-T trip criterion | $T(t) \geq T_{set}$ | °C |
| ROR trip criterion | $dT/dt > 8.3$ | °C/min |
| Pneumatic ΔP | $\Delta P \approx (P_0/T_0)\,\Delta T$ | Pa, ideal gas |
| Thermistor (Steinhart–Hart) | $1/T = A + B\ln R + C[\ln R]^3$ | T in K |
| Ionization alarm | $I < 0.5\,I_0$ | A (50% obscuration) |
| % obscuration/foot | $\%\,\text{Obs/ft} = (1 - 10^{-\sigma c})\times 100$ | dimensionless |
| Beam attenuation | $P_r/P_t = \exp(-\sigma c L)$ | path $L$ in m |
| Alpert ceiling jet | $\Delta T_{\max} = 5.38\,Q^{2/3}/(r^{2/3}\,H^{5/3})$ | °C, $Q$ in kW, $r,H$ in m |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These equations allow a fire protection engineer to (a) determine the maximum spacing between heat detectors for a given fire size, (b) select appropriate $T_{set}$ for a given environment, and (c) verify that smoke detectors will respond before tenability limits (smoldering toxic gas, visibility) are reached.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Detector Time-to-Alarm Using Lumped Capacitance + RTI

### Step 1 — Define the Energy Balance on the Detector Element
The heat transfer into the detector sensing element is governed by Newton's Law of Cooling combined with a forced-convection velocity term:

$$m\,c_p\,\frac{dT_d}{dt} \;=\; h\,A\,(T_g - T_d)$$

### Step 2 — Substitute the Convective Heat-Transfer Coefficient via Plumb's Correlation
Plumb (1988) gave the empirical correlation for $h$ as a function of gas velocity:

$$h \;=\; \frac{k_g}{d}\,\left[\frac{u^{1/2}}{\text{RTI}/\sqrt{\text{m}}}\right]$$

For a unit-area detector where $m c_p / A$ is normalized to a **Time Constant** $\tau_0$, this simplifies to:

$$\tau_0\,\frac{dT_d}{dt} \;+\; T_d \;=\; T_g$$

### Step 3 — Solve the First-Order ODE for a Linearly Rising Gas Temperature
Assume the surrounding gas temperature rises linearly: $T_g(t) = T_0 + \alpha t$. Solving with zero initial offset:

$$T_d(t) - T_0 \;=\; \alpha \tau_0 \left[\exp\!\left(-\frac{t}{\tau_0}\right) - 1\right] + \alpha t$$

### Step 4 — Introduce the RTI in Place of $\tau_0$
Plumb's correlation expresses the detector's thermal lag in terms of **RTI**:

$$\tau_0 \;=\; \frac{\text{RTI}}{\sqrt{u}}$$

Substitute into the solution:

$$T_d(t) - T_0 \;=\; \alpha\,\frac{\text{RTI}}{\sqrt{u}}\,\left[\exp\!\left(-\frac{t\sqrt{u}}{\text{RTI}}\right) - 1\right] + \alpha t$$

### Step 5 — Apply the Alarm Criterion
The detector trips when its temperature reaches the set-point above initial:

$$T_d(t_a) - T_0 \;=\; \Delta T_{\text{set}}$$

Solving for $t_a$ (activation time) by iterative methods:

$$t_a \;=\; \frac{\text{RTI}}{\sqrt{u}}\,\ln\!\left[\frac{\alpha\,\dfrac{\text{RTI}}{\sqrt{u}}}{\alpha\,\dfrac{\text{RTI}}{\sqrt{u}} - \Delta T_{\text{set}}}\right]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the exact closed-form used by KTU board examiners for numerical problems.** A typical exam question asks: *"A sprinkler has RTI = 50 m$^{1/2}$·s$^{1/2}$, ceiling gas velocity u = 1.0 m/s, gas temperature rise rate α = 5 °C/s, set temperature ΔT_set = 30 °C. Find activation time."*

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Alpert's Ceiling Jet

### Problem
A fire in a server room has **heat release rate Q = 750 kW**. The detector is mounted on a **ceiling of height H = 4 m**, located **r = 3 m** radially from the fire plume centerline. Compute the ceiling-jet temperature rise.

### Step 1 — Identify the Fire Regime
Since $r/H = 3/4 = 0.75 < 0.18 \times (Q^{2/5}/H^{3/5})$ would be the *near-field*; otherwise we are in the **far-field (ceiling-jet) regime**. For typical values, $r/H > 0.2$, so we use the **far-field Alpert correlation**.

### Step 2 — Apply Alpert's Equation

$$\Delta T_{\max} \;=\; \frac{5.38\,Q^{2/3}}{r^{2/3}\,H^{5/3}}$$

Compute numerator:
$$Q^{2/3} \;=\; 750^{2/3} \;=\; (750)^{0.6667}$$

By computation: $750^{1/3} \approx 9.086$, so $750^{2/3} \approx 82.55$

Compute denominator:
$$r^{2/3} \, H^{5/3} \;=\; 3^{2/3} \cdot 4^{5/3}$$

We have $3^{2/3} \approx 2.080$ and $4^{5/3} = (4^5)^{1/3} = 1024^{1/3} \approx 10.08$

So denominator $\approx 2.080 \times 10.08 \approx 20.97$

### Step 3 — Final Calculation

$$\Delta T_{\max} \;=\; \frac{5.38 \times 82.55}{20.97} \;\approx\; \frac{444.1}{20.97} \;\approx\; 21.18\,°C$$

> [!NOTE]
> This means a detector set at **T_set = 57 °C** in a **30 °C ambient** would trip when $\Delta T_{\max}$ exceeds **27 °C**. With the calculated 21.18 °C rise, the **fixed-temperature detector does NOT trip** at r = 3 m. A **rate-of-rise** or **smoke detector must be used** for early warning.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| **Property** | **Fixed-T** | **ROR** | **Thermistor ROR** | **Rate Compensated** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Sensing element | Eutectic / bimetallic / bulb | Pneumatic diaphragm | NTC thermistor + µC | Dual metal shells |
| Trigger logic | $T \geq T_{set}$ | $dT/dt > 8.3$ °C/min | $\Delta T/\Delta t >$ threshold | $T \geq T_{set}$ OR $dT/dt > 8.3$ |
| Best for | Slow, sustained fires | Fast-developing fires | Programmable environments | Combined environments |
| False alarms in kitchens | Moderate | High | Low (with filtering) | Moderate |
| Restorable | Usually NO | YES | YES | YES |
| Cost (₹) | Low | Low | Medium | Medium |

| **Property** | **Ionization** | **Photoelectric** | **Beam** | **Aspirating (VESDA)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Sensing principle | Current drop | Light scatter | Obscuration | Active sampling + scatter |
| Best for | Flaming | Smoldering | Large open spaces | Very early warning |
| Sensitivity (%/ft) | 1 – 2 | 1.5 – 3 | 15 – 30 (over full path) | 0.005 – 0.1 |
| Range | Spot | Spot | 3 – 100 m | Up to 200 m² |
| Cost (₹) | Low | Low | High | Very High |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Heat Detector Family — Classification Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Heat Detectors] --> B[Fixed Temperature]
    A --> C[Rate of Rise]
    A --> D[Thermistor Rate of Rise]
    A --> E[Rate Compensated]
    B --> B1[Eutectic Solder Type]
    B --> B2[Bimetallic Strip Type]
    B --> B3[Frangible Glass Bulb]
    C --> C1[Pneumatic Diaphragm Type]
    D --> D1[NTC Thermistor + Microcontroller]
    E --> E1[Dual Shell Differential Expansion]
```

## 4.2 Smoke Detector Family — Classification Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Smoke Detectors] --> S1[Ionization Chamber]
    S --> S2[Photoelectric Spot Type]
    S --> S3[Projected Beam]
    S --> S4[Aspirating VESDA]
    S1 --> S1A[Americium 241 Source]
    S1 --> S1B[Two Electrode Chamber]
    S2 --> S2A[LED Emitter]
    S2 --> S2B[Photodiode Receiver at 90 deg]
    S3 --> S3A[Transmitter on One Wall]
    S3 --> S3B[Receiver on Opposite Wall]
    S4 --> S4A[Pipe Network Air Sampling]
    S4 --> S4B[Laser Detection Chamber]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Pneumatic Rate of Rise Detector

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    G[Hot Gas Plume] --> P[Pneumatic Chamber]
    P --> D[Flexible Diaphragm]
    D --> C[Electrical Contact Pair]
    C --> A[Alarm Processing Unit]
    L[Calibrated Vent] -.slow pressure equalization.-> P
    V[Vent Orifice Diameter d_v] -.controls leak rate.-> L
    A --> R[Relay Output to FACP]
    style A fill:#ffd966,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style R fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Photoelectric Smoke Detector

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SM[Smoke Aerosols Enter Chamber] --> SC[Light Scattering Zone]
    LED[LED Emitter 940 nm] --> SC
    SC --> PD[Photodiode at 90 deg]
    PD --> AMP[Amplifier Circuit]
    AMP --> TH[Threshold Comparator]
    TH --> AL[Alarm Output]
    LAB[Light Absorbing Baffles] -.reduce stray light.-> SC
    style TH fill:#c9daf8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style AL fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.5 Ceiling Jet Activation Radius — Detector Spacing Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q[Fire Heat Release Rate Q in kW] --> AL[Apply Alpert Equation]
    H[Ceiling Height H in m] --> AL
    DT[Threshold Temperature DT in deg C] --> AL
    AL --> R[Compute Activation Radius r]
    R --> SP[Set Detector Spacing less than r]
    SP --> OK[Coverage Area Verified]
    style AL fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style R fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Differentiate between a Fixed Temperature heat detector and a Rate-of-Rise heat detector. List two applications where each is preferred.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Fixed Temperature (FT)** detector triggers when the ambient temperature at the detector location exceeds a pre-calibrated set-point (e.g., **57 °C / 68 °C / 74 °C**). Its operating principle is based on **absolute temperature threshold**, regardless of how quickly the temperature was reached.

A **Rate-of-Rise (ROR)** detector triggers when the **rate of temperature increase exceeds a calibrated threshold**, typically **8.3 °C per minute**. It uses a **pneumatic chamber with a vented diaphragm**; rapid air expansion deflects the diaphragm to close the alarm contacts.

**Applications:**
- **Fixed-T preferred** in environments with **high ambient temperatures** (boiler rooms, kitchens, garages) where ROR would generate false alarms.
- **ROR preferred** in **clean, temperature-stable environments** (offices, hotel rooms) where rapid temperature rise reliably indicates a real fire.

*(3 marks — 1 mark definition each, 0.5 each for application)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Explain the working principle of an ionization-type smoke detector. Mention the radioactive source used.**

**Model Answer:**

An **ionization smoke detector** contains a small sealed chamber with two electrodes and a trace quantity of **Americium-241 (²⁴¹Am)**, with an activity of approximately **0.9 µCi (33 kBq)**. The Americium emits **alpha particles (⁴He nuclei)** which ionize the air molecules in the chamber, producing a steady **ion current** of the order of microamperes between the electrodes.

When **combustion aerosols enter the chamber**, they attach to the air ions, neutralizing the charge carriers. This causes the ion current to **drop below a threshold (typically 50% of the original current)**, which triggers the alarm circuit. Ionization detectors are particularly responsive to **fast-flaming fires** that produce small (< 1 µm) invisible combustion particles.

*(3 marks — 1.5 marks working principle, 1 mark radioactive source, 0.5 mark fire-type suitability)*

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the construction and working of a **Rate Compensated Heat Detector**. Compare its performance with a Fixed Temperature and a pure Rate-of-Rise detector. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A fire produces a heat release rate of **Q = 1500 kW**. The ceiling is at height **H = 6 m**. Using **Alpert's correlation**, compute the **ceiling jet temperature rise** at radial distances **r = 2 m, 4 m, and 6 m** from the plume centerline. If the Fixed Temperature detector is set at **ΔT_set = 30 °C** above ambient, determine the **activation radius**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part A(a)

**Construction:**

A Rate Compensated Detector uses **two concentric metal shells** (typically **steel** outer and **Invar** inner, or **brass** + **steel** combination) sealed at one end with a flexible diaphragm at the other. The inner shell has a **higher thermal expansion coefficient ($\alpha_1$)** than the outer shell ($\alpha_2$).

**Working Principle:**

When ambient temperature rises:
1. The differential expansion $(\alpha_1 - \alpha_2)\,\Delta T$ deflects the diaphragm by an amount proportional to **$\Delta T$ (fixed-T component)**.
2. Simultaneously, the **rate of expansion** contributes an additional deflection proportional to **$dT/dt$ (ROR component)**.
3. The diaphragm closes the electrical contact when the **sum of deflections exceeds a calibrated threshold**.

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Fixed-T | ROR | Rate Compensated |
|---|---|---|---|
| Triggers on $T_{set}$ | YES | NO | YES |
| Triggers on $dT/dt$ | NO | YES | YES |
| False alarm in kitchens | Moderate | High | Moderate-Low |
| False alarm in summer | Moderate | Low | Moderate |
| Suitable for warehouses | YES | YES | YES |

*(7 marks — 2 marks sketch description, 3 marks working, 2 marks comparison)*

#### Model Solution to Part A(b)

**Given:** $Q = 1500$ kW, $H = 6$ m, $\Delta T_{set} = 30$ °C

Alpert's equation:

$$\Delta T_{\max} \;=\; \frac{5.38\,Q^{2/3}}{r^{2/3}\,H^{5/3}}$$

Compute constants:
$$Q^{2/3} \;=\; 1500^{2/3}$$
$$1500^{1/3} \;\approx\; 11.45 \;\Rightarrow\; Q^{2/3} \;\approx\; 131.0$$
$$H^{5/3} \;=\; 6^{5/3}$$
$$6^{1/3} \;\approx\; 1.817 \;\Rightarrow\; H^{5/3} \;\approx\; 6^{5/3} \;=\; 6^2 \cdot 6^{1/3} \;=\; 36 \times 1.817 \;\approx\; 65.4$$

So the **constant numerator × coefficient**:

$$5.38 \times 131.0 \;\approx\; 704.8$$

**At r = 2 m:**

$$r^{2/3} \;=\; 2^{2/3} \;\approx\; 1.587$$

$$\Delta T_{\max} \;=\; \frac{704.8}{1.587 \times 65.4} \;=\; \frac{704.8}{103.8} \;\approx\; 6.79\,°C$$

**At r = 4 m:**

$$r^{2/3} \;=\; 4^{2/3} \;\approx\; 2.520$$

$$\Delta T_{\max} \;=\; \frac{704.8}{2.520 \times 65.4} \;=\; \frac{704.8}{164.8} \;\approx\; 4.28\,°C$$

**At r = 6 m:**

$$r^{2/3} \;=\; 6^{2/3} \;\approx\; 3.302$$

$$\Delta T_{\max} \;=\; \frac{704.8}{3.302 \times 65.4} \;=\; \frac{704.8}{215.9} \;\approx\; 3.26\,°C$$
