**Activation Radius Calculation:**
Set $\Delta T_{\max} = 30\,°C$ and solve for $r$:

$$30 \;=\; \frac{704.8}{r^{2/3} \times 65.4}$$

$$r^{2/3} \;=\; \frac{704.8}{30 \times 65.4} \;=\; \frac{704.8}{1962} \;\approx\; 0.3593$$

$$r \;=\; (0.3593)^{3/2} \;\approx\; (0.3593)^{1.5}$$

$$(0.3593)^{1.5} \;\approx\; 0.2155\,m$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** A fixed-temperature detector set at 30 °C above ambient is **too insensitive** for this large a fire at 6 m ceiling height — it cannot even activate 0.5 m from the plume! This shows why **smoke detectors or low-RTI sprinklers** are essential for high-ceiling spaces.

*(7 marks — 2 marks formula, 3 marks calculations at three radii, 2 marks activation radius + interpretation)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** (Alternative Choice)

**(a)** Explain the construction and working of an **ionization smoke detector** and a **photoelectric smoke detector**. Compare their sensitivity to different fire types. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An **aspirating smoke detection (ASD/VESDA)** system has a sampling pipe of length **L = 50 m**. The transport time for smoke from the sampling port to the laser detection chamber is **t_transport = 8 s**. The detection threshold is set at **0.05% obscuration per meter**. If a smoldering fire produces smoke with mass concentration **c = 0.02 g/m³** and specific extinction coefficient **σ = 4.5 m²/g**, determine whether the alarm will trigger and what total **response time** would be expected. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part B(a)

**Ionization Smoke Detector — Construction:**

It consists of:
1. A **reference chamber** (sealed, no air entry) and a **sensing chamber** (open to ambient) connected in a balanced electrical circuit.
2. A trace of **²⁴¹Am (Americium-241)** source ionizes the air, producing a small current.
3. The **reference chamber** balances environmental changes (humidity, pressure).
4. The **sensing chamber** current drops when smoke enters.

**Photoelectric Detector — Construction:**

1. A **light-proof housing** with a labyrinth to prevent ambient light entry.
2. An **infrared LED** (typically **940 nm**) emitter.
3. A **photodiode** positioned at an angle (commonly **90°**) to the LED beam.
4. Light-absorbing baffles to suppress internal reflection.

**Comparison:**

| Property | Ionization | Photoelectric |
|---|---|---|
| Sensitive to | Small (< 1 µm) particles | Large (> 1 µm) particles |
| Best for | **Fast-flaming** fires (paper, gasoline) | **Smoldering** fires (PU foam, electrical) |
| Nuisance alarms | Common near kitchens | Common near dust, steam |
| Response to visible smoke | Slow | Fast |

*(7 marks — 3 marks ionization, 3 marks photoelectric, 1 mark comparison)*

#### Model Solution to Part B(b)

**Given:** $L = 50$ m, $t_{\text{transport}} = 8$ s, threshold = **0.05% obs/m**, $c = 0.02$ g/m³, $\sigma = 4.5$ m²/g

**Step 1 — Compute % Obscuration per meter using:**

$$\%\,\text{Obs/m} \;=\; \left(1 - 10^{-\sigma c}\right)\times 100$$

Compute $\sigma c$:
$$\sigma c \;=\; 4.5 \times 0.02 \;=\; 0.09\,(1/\text{m})$$

Compute $10^{-0.09}$:
$$10^{-0.09} \;\approx\; 0.8128$$

So:
$$\%\,\text{Obs/m} \;=\; (1 - 0.8128)\times 100 \;=\; 0.1872\,\%\,\text{obs/m}$$

**Step 2 — Compare with threshold:**

$$0.1872\,\%\,\text{obs/m} \;>\; 0.05\,\%\,\text{obs/m}$$

✅ **Yes, the alarm WILL trigger.**

**Step 3 — Total Response Time:**

Total response time = transport time + detection time

$$t_{\text{total}} \;=\; t_{\text{transport}} + t_{\text{detect}}$$

For an aspirating system, detection time is typically **2 – 4 seconds** (very low due to high-sensitivity laser chamber).

Assuming $t_{\text{detect}} \approx 3$ s:

$$t_{\text{total}} \;=\; 8 + 3 \;=\; 11\,\text{s}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** An aspirating detector with a 50 m pipe provides alarm in approximately **11 seconds** — far faster than a conventional spot smoke detector, which typically requires **30–90 seconds** for the same fire.

*(7 marks — 2 marks formula setup, 2 marks calculation, 1 mark comparison, 2 marks total response time)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Units trap:** Always convert $Q$ from MW to kW before applying Alpert's equation. Mixing units is the **#1 reason students lose 2-3 marks**.
> 2. **Far-field assumption:** Alpert's ceiling jet equation is **valid only for $r/H > 0.18$**. For very close to plume, use the **near-field Heskestad equation** instead.
> 3. **Ionization vs Photoelectric:** Examiners love to ask *"Which is more responsive to smoldering fires?"* — Answer: **Photoelectric** (not ionization). Many students answer this incorrectly.
> 4. **RTI trap:** RTI is **NOT a constant for all velocities** in reality; the assumption $\tau \sqrt{u} =$ const is **Plumb's correlation** and is valid for a limited velocity range.
> 5. **VESDA ≠ Smoke Detector:** VESDA is an *aspirating* detector — students sometimes lose marks by calling it a "very early smoke detection apparatus" without explaining it is a **system**, not a single device.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Fixed-T Detector** trips at a calibrated **set temperature** ($T \geq T_{set}$); insensitive to rate of rise.
- ✅ **ROR Detector** trips when **$dT/dt > 8.3\,°C/\text{min}$**; uses pneumatic diaphragm.
- ✅ **Thermistor ROR** is the **electronic / solid-state** equivalent; uses **NTC thermistor + microcontroller**.
- ✅ **Rate Compensated** detector combines **Fixed-T + ROR** in a **single dual-shell mechanical unit** (OR-gate logic).
- ✅ **Ionization smoke detector** uses **²⁴¹Am (Americium-241)** source; responds to **fast-flaming fires**.
- ✅ **Photoelectric smoke detector** uses **LED + photodiode at 90°**; responds to **smoldering fires**.
- ✅ **Beam detector** uses **transmitter-receiver pair across a gap**; applies **Beer-Lambert law**.
- ✅ **VESDA** is an **active aspirating system** with very low detection thresholds (**0.005 – 0.1 %/ft**).
- ✅ **Alpert's ceiling jet equation** $\Delta T_{\max} = 5.38\,Q^{2/3}/(r^{2/3}\,H^{5/3})$ is the **most-asked formula** for detector spacing.
- ✅ **RTI** = Response Time Index, in **m$^{1/2}$·s$^{1/2}$**; activation time $\tau = \text{RTI}/\sqrt{u}$.
- ✅ **IS 2175** & **IS 2189** are the key Indian standards; **NFPA 72** and **EN 54** are international equivalents.
- ✅ Detector spacing must follow **Annex E of IS 2189** for typical coverage of **50 m² per spot detector** (smooth ceiling, 9 m height).
- ✅ **Ceiling height vs detector type:** Below 4 m — any detector; 4–8 m — heat detectors with ROR; 8–12 m — beam or aspirating smoke; > 12 m — aspirating VESDA preferred.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Optical and Ionization Type, Photo Electric Light Scattering and Light Obstruction Type Detectors. Flame Detectors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT836 - DESIGN OF FIRE PROTECTION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Optical and Ionization Type, Photo Electric Light Scattering and Light Obstruction Type Detectors. Flame Detectors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Automatic Fire Detectors

A **fire detector** is the sensory organ of a fire protection system. It is a device that senses one or more products of combustion (smoke, heat, flame, combustion gases) and provides a signal to a Fire Alarm Control Panel (FACP). The detectors covered in this module are the **spot-type automatic detectors** typically mounted on ceilings, classified by the physical phenomenon they sense.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - Module 2):**
> Students must be able to distinguish between **ionization** vs **photoelectric** smoke detectors, identify the working principle of **light scattering** and **light obstruction** chambers, and explain the operating logic of **UV, IR, and combined flame detectors**.

## 1.2 Categorical Overview of the Topic

The detection devices discussed fall into two broad sensing families:

| Family | Sub-Principle | Detected Fire Signature |
|---|---|---|
| **Smoke Detectors** | Ionization Chamber | Combustion products (ions) |
| **Smoke Detectors** | Photoelectric – Light Scattering | Scattered light from smoke particles |
| **Smoke Detectors** | Photoelectric – Light Obstruction | Reduction in light beam intensity |
| **Flame Detectors** | Ultraviolet (UV) | UV emission from flames |
| **Flame Detectors** | Infrared (IR) – Single Frequency | Modulated IR flicker |
| **Flame Detectors** | Infrared (IR) – Dual Frequency (IR²) | 4.4 µm /CO₂ peak ratio |
| **Flame Detectors** | UV/IR Combined | Cross-checked UV + IR signature |
| **Flame Detectors** | Video / Visual Flame Imaging | Color, motion, brightness analysis |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

### Smoke Detector Analogy — "The Two Ways to Spot Steam from a Teapot"
Imagine you are in another room and want to know if the kettle has boiled. You could either:
- **"Sniff" the air** using a **chemical nose** → that is what an **ionization detector** does. It smells the *invisible ions* that fire creates.
- **"Look" for the white cloud** of steam → that is what a **photoelectric detector** does. It looks for *visible particles* that scatter light.

### Flame Detector Analogy — "Spotting a Campfire from a Distance"
A **flame detector** is the *"smoke-free, line-of-sight fire-spotter"*. Just as a sailor spots a distant fire by the **glow (IR)** or the **invisible UV flickers**, flame detectors sense the **electromagnetic radiation** emitted directly by flames — they do not wait for smoke to drift to the ceiling.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition (NFPA 72 / IS 2189):**
> *A fire detector is a device suitable for connection to a circuit that has a sensor which responds to a physical stimulus such as heat, smoke, flame, or combustion gases.*

## 1.4 Smoke — The Most Common Early Signature of Fire

Smoke is the **aerosol suspension of solid and liquid particles** evolved when a material undergoes **pyrolysis or combustion**. Its characteristics determine the choice of detector.

**Key physical properties of smoke used for detection:**
- **Particle size:** typically **0.01 µm to 5 µm**
- **Obscuration** (light-blocking power), measured in **% per metre** or **%/m**
- **Refractive index** (governs light scattering efficiency)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two broad fire classes drive detector choice:**
> - **Smouldering fires** (e.g., electrical cable insulation) → produce **large, light-scattering particles** → **Photoelectric detectors excel**.
> - **Flaming fires** (e.g., solvent pool fire) → produce **small particles + ions** → **Ionization detectors respond faster** (though photoelectric is preferred in modern standards).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 IONIZATION TYPE SMOKE DETECTOR

### 2.1.1 Working Principle
The detector uses a small amount of radioactive source — **Americium-241 (Am-241)** — emitting **alpha particles (α)** of energy ≈ **4.5 MeV**. The α-particles ionize the air molecules inside a **reference chamber** and a **sensing chamber**, producing a small steady **ionization current**.

**Operational Logic (step-by-step):**
1. **Am-241 source** emits α-particles inside two chambers — Reference (R) and Sensing (S).
2. Air molecules **ionize**, producing **positive ions** and **free electrons**.
3. A small **DC voltage** is applied across the chambers, causing a **steady ion current** I₀ (typically **≈ 10⁻¹¹ A**).
4. Both chambers act as electrical resistors R₁ and R₂ in a **balanced voltage divider / Wheatstone bridge**.
5. When **smoke enters the sensing chamber**, the **smoke particles attach to the ions**, reducing their mobility.
6. This **reduces the ionization current** in the sensing chamber, unbalancing the bridge.
7. The resulting voltage change triggers the **alarm signal** when the imbalance exceeds a preset threshold.

### 2.1.2 Why Am-241?
- α-particles have **very low penetrating power** → safely contained in a metallic chamber.
- **Long half-life**: **T₁/₂ = 432.2 years** → stable operation.
- Low energy (4.5 MeV) cannot penetrate skin or a sheet of paper.

### 2.1.3 Sensitivity & Limitations
- **Most sensitive to invisible, small (≈ 0.01–0.1 µm) smoke particles** produced by fast-flaming fires.
- **Less sensitive to large, dark smoke** from smouldering fires.
- Can be **falsed by cooking fumes, steam, aerosols** → not recommended near kitchens/bathrooms.

### 2.1.4 Real-World Utility
Used in **residential dwellings, hotel rooms, offices**. The photoelectric vs ionization debate has tilted in favour of **photoelectric-only** in modern installations due to better smouldering-fire response.

---

## 2.2 PHOTOELECTRIC (OPTICAL) SMOKE DETECTORS

The principle is based on the interaction between a **light beam** and **smoke particles**. Two distinct sub-types exist:

### 2.2.1 LIGHT SCATTERING TYPE (Tyndall Effect)
- A **Light Emitting Diode (LED)** (typically infrared, **λ ≈ 880 nm**) emits a beam into a **dark chamber**.
- A **photodiode (receiver)** is placed at an angle (usually **90°**) to the beam, **NOT in its direct path**.
- In clean air, the beam does not reach the photodiode.
- When **smoke particles enter**, they **scatter (Tyndall scattering)** the light in all directions.
- A portion of the scattered light reaches the **photodiode**, generating a photocurrent.
- When the photocurrent exceeds a threshold → **alarm**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Tyndall Effect:** *Scattering of light by colloidal (or aerosol) particles whose size is comparable to the wavelength of light. The scattered light is polarised perpendicular to the incident beam.*

### 2.2.2 LIGHT OBSTRUCTION TYPE (Obscuration)
- The LED and photodiode are placed **directly opposite** each other.
- In clean air, the photodiode receives the **full beam intensity**.
- When smoke enters the chamber, the **light intensity reaching the photodiode decreases** (the light is absorbed and scattered by smoke particles).
- When the obscuration exceeds a preset threshold → **alarm**.

### 2.2.3 Light Scattering vs Light Obstruction — Comparison

| Parameter | Light Scattering | Light Obstruction |
|---|---|---|
| LED–Photodiode Geometry | Perpendicular (90°) | Co-linear (180°) |
| Baseline Signal | Zero (dark) | Maximum |
| Alarm Trigger | Signal rises above zero | Signal drops below threshold |
| Smoke Size Sensitivity | Best for 0.3–1 µm (visible smoke) | Best for dense, heavy smoke |
| Typical Use | Spot detector in living areas | Duct / high-ceiling, beam detectors |

### 2.2.4 The Line-Type Beam Smoke Detector
- A **projector** and **separate receiver** are mounted several metres apart (typically **8 m to 100 m**).
- Used in **atriums, warehouses, churches, large halls** where spot detectors are impractical.
- Light obstruction principle: the beam is **attenuated** by smoke.

### 2.2.5 Real-World Utility
- **Light Scattering**: Most common residential and commercial spot detector.
- **Light Obstruction**: Industrial facilities, atria, large open spaces; cleanrooms.

---

## 2.3 FLAME DETECTORS

Flame detectors are **line-of-sight** devices that sense the **electromagnetic radiation** emitted by flames. They respond in **seconds** (sometimes **< 1 s**) — the fastest of all detector types.

### 2.3.1 Spectral Emission of a Flame
A hydrocarbon flame emits radiation across the spectrum:
- **UV band:** **0.1 – 0.35 µm** (from free-radical recombination of CO, OH*)
- **Visible band:** 0.4 – 0.7 µm (yellow/orange glow from soot)
- **IR band:** 0.7 – 1000 µm (hot CO₂, H₂O, and soot)
- **Characteristic IR peaks:**
  - **4.4 µm** → hot **CO₂** emission
  - **2.7 µm** → **H₂O** emission
  - **1.85 µm** → background IR (sun, lamps)

### 2.3.2 Ultraviolet (UV) Flame Detector
- Uses a **photoelectric tube (Geiger-Müller type) or solid-state UV sensor** (e.g., SiC, GaN).
- Sensor is sensitive to **UV in 0.18 – 0.26 µm** range.
- **Window material** is typically **fused quartz or sapphire** (transparent to UV).
- Solar radiation is filtered out because sunlight has very little energy below **0.29 µm** (Earth's ozone cutoff).
- **Response time:** typically **≤ 3 seconds** for a 0.1 m² heptane fire at 10 m.
- **Limitations:** Can be **falsed by arc welding, lightning, X-rays, gamma radiation**.

### 2.3.3 Single-Frequency IR Flame Detector
- Uses a **photovoltaic IR sensor** (e.g., lead-selenide, PbSe) sensitive to **4.4 µm**.
- Detects the **flicker** of flames (typically **1 – 10 Hz** modulation).
- **Filters out steady IR sources** (sun, heaters, lamps) using **flicker analysis**.
- **Limitation:** Hot machinery, exhausts, or sunlight modulated by passing clouds can **fals** the detector.

### 2.3.4 Dual-Frequency IR (IR²) Flame Detector
- Uses **two IR sensors** at two wavelengths:
  - **λ₁ = 4.4 µm** → flame signature (CO₂ emission)
  - **λ₂ = 3.8 µm or 5.0 µm** → background reference
- The detector compares the ratio of signals: **if (signal at 4.4 µm) >> (signal at 3.8 µm)** → flame.
- Highly **false-alarm-resistant**.
- Industry standard for **offshore platforms, turbine enclosures, fuel storage**.

### 2.3.5 UV/IR Combined Flame Detector
- Houses **both a UV sensor and an IR sensor** in one unit.
- Logic: **Both must detect** flame signature **simultaneously** before alarm.
- The "AND-gate" logic virtually eliminates false alarms.
- Used in **high-risk industrial zones**: petrochemical, ammunition, hangars.

### 2.3.6 Video / Visual Flame Detectors (CCTV-based)
- Use **CCD/CMOS camera** plus **image-processing algorithms**.
- Detect flame by **colour** (typically yellow/red/orange), **motion**, **flicker frequency**, and **shape** (rising plume).
- Provide **live video** to operators.
- Used in **large open areas, tunnels, refineries**.

### 2.3.7 Detector Selection — Field of View (FOV) & Range
A flame detector's field of view is typically a **horizontal cone of 90° to 120°**, with detection range **up to 60 m** for a 0.1 m² gasoline fire.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula / Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Ionization current (clean air) | $I_0$ ≈ 10⁻¹¹ A | Ampere | Baseline balance in chamber |
| 2 | α-particle energy of Am-241 | $E_\alpha$ ≈ **4.5 MeV** | Mega-electron-volt | Source spec |
| 3 | Half-life of Am-241 | $T_{1/2}$ = **432.2 years** | year | Source longevity |
| 4 | Tyndall Scattering angle | $\theta$ = **90°** (typical detector) | degree | Scattering chamber geometry |
| 5 | LED wavelength (IR) | $\lambda$ = **880 nm** | nanometre | Scattering detector source |
| 6 | Beam obscuration | $O = 1 - \dfrac{I}{I_0}$ | dimensionless | Light obstruction |
| 7 | Obscuration (in dB/m) | $O_{dB} = 10 \log_{10} \left( \dfrac{I_0}{I} \right)$ | dB/m | Beam detector sensitivity |
| 8 | Beer–Lambert Law (smoke attenuation) | $I = I_0 \, e^{-\sigma_s \, c \, L}$ | — | Light obstruction principle |
| 9 | UV detector cut-off wavelength | $\lambda_{cutoff}$ = **0.29 µm** | µm | Solar UV rejection |
| 10 | Flame IR signature peak | $\lambda_{IR}$ = **4.4 µm** | µm | CO₂ emission |
| 11 | Flame flicker frequency | $f_{flicker}$ = **1 – 10 Hz** | Hz | IR flicker discrimination |
| 12 | FOV of flame detector | $\phi$ = **90° to 120°** | degree | Coverage area |
| 13 | Detection range (heptane) | $R$ ≈ **10 – 60 m** | metre | Sizing in a design |
| 14 | Response time (UV/IR) | $t_r$ ≤ **3 s** | second | Performance spec |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use `\vert` or `\mid` in place of `|` inside table cells** — done throughout to preserve markdown table integrity.

---

## 2.5 Engineering Field Application Summary

| Detector Type | Best Suited For | Fire Type Detected |
|---|---|---|
| Ionization | Bedrooms, offices (legacy) | Fast-flaming with small particles |
| Photoelectric (scattering) | Living rooms, corridors, hotels | Smouldering + flaming |
| Photoelectric (obstruction) | Warehouses, atria, tunnels | Smouldering + flaming over large areas |
| UV | Indoor industrial, ammunition | Hydrocarbon flames, arc fires |
| Single IR | Indoor/outdoor clean areas | Hydrocarbon flames |
| IR² | Offshore, refineries, turbine halls | Hydrocarbon flames, **low false alarms** |
| UV/IR | Petrochemical, aircraft hangars | All hydrocarbon, very low false alarms |
| Video | Tunnels, large open industrial | Visual confirmation + detection |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section presents the **mathematical derivations** underpinning the optical/photoelectric and flame detection principles, in KTU examiner-friendly style.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Light Obstruction (Beer–Lambert Law for Smoke)

The attenuation of a light beam passing through a smoke-laden medium is governed by the **Beer–Lambert law**, originally developed for light absorption by a solution.

### Step 1: State the law for a thin slice of smoke
Consider an infinitesimally thin slice of smoke of thickness $dz$. The fractional reduction in light intensity $I$ over that slice is proportional to the slice thickness and the local smoke concentration $c(z)$:

$$
\frac{dI}{dz} = -\sigma_s \, c(z) \, I(z)
$$

where:
- $\sigma_s$ = **smoke extinction coefficient** (m²/kg or m⁻¹ depending on units used)
- $c(z)$ = smoke concentration (kg/m³ or dimensionless mass loading)
- $I(z)$ = light intensity at depth $z$

### Step 2: Separate the variables

$$
\frac{dI}{I} = -\sigma_s \, c(z) \, dz
$$

### Step 3: Integrate across the full path length $L$

$$
\int_{I_0}^{I_L} \frac{dI}{I} = -\sigma_s \int_0^L c(z)\, dz
$$

### Step 4: Evaluate the integrals

$$
\ln(I_L) - \ln(I_0) = -\sigma_s \int_0^L c(z)\, dz
$$

$$
\ln\left( \frac{I_L}{I_0} \right) = -\sigma_s \int_0^L c(z)\, dz
$$

### Step 5: Exponentiate to obtain intensity at receiver

$$
\boxed{\,I_L = I_0 \cdot \exp\!\left( -\sigma_s \int_0^L c(z)\, dz \right)\,}
$$

**For a uniform smoke concentration** $c$:

$$
I_L = I_0 \, e^{-\sigma_s \, c \, L}
$$

This is the working equation for a **light-obstruction / beam-type** smoke detector. The detector triggers when $I_L / I_0$ falls below a preset threshold (typically 25%, 35%, or 50% obscuration, depending on the application and IS 2189 / NFPA 72).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Obscuration in dB per metre

KTU examiners often test obscuration in logarithmic units, especially for **beam detectors**.

### Step 1: Define fractional transmittance

$$
T = \frac{I_L}{I_0}
$$

### Step 2: Define obscuration in per cent

$$
O\,\% = (1 - T)\times 100
$$

### Step 3: Convert to dB/m
By definition of decibels, the attenuation per metre is:

$$
O_{dB/m} = 10 \log_{10}\left( \frac{I_0}{I_L} \right)
$$

### Step 4: Express in terms of smoke density
For a uniform smoke of concentration $c$:

$$
O_{dB/m} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left( e^{\sigma_s c} \right) = 10 \cdot \sigma_s c \cdot \log_{10} e
$$

$$
\boxed{\,O_{dB/m} = 4.343 \, \sigma_s \, c\,}
$$

**Worked numerical example (KTU-style):**

Given a smoke extinction coefficient $\sigma_s = 1.2$ m²/g and smoke concentration $c = 0.05$ g/m³ (typical for a smouldering fire):

$$
O_{dB/m} = 4.343 \times 1.2 \times 0.05 = 0.26058 \text{ dB/m}
$$

For a beam length $L = 30$ m, total attenuation:

$$
O_{total} = 0.26058 \times 30 = 7.82 \text{ dB}
$$

Fractional transmittance:

$$
T = 10^{-O_{total}/10} = 10^{-0.782} = 0.165
$$

So **83.5% of the light is blocked** → a beam detector set to alarm at 50% obscuration (–3 dB) will have fired long before this smoke density.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Bridge-Voltage Unbalance in an Ionization Chamber

### Step 1: Equivalent electrical model
The reference and sensing chambers behave as two resistors $R_1$ and $R_2$ forming the **upper arms** of a Wheatstone bridge; fixed resistors $R_3$ and $R_4$ form the lower arms. Output voltage $V_{out}$ is measured across the bridge midpoints.

$$
V_{out} = V_{s}\left( \frac{R_1}{R_1+R_3} - \frac{R_2}{R_2+R_4} \right)
$$

### Step 2: Balanced condition (clean air)
Under clean air, $R_1 = R_2$ and $R_3 = R_4$, so $V_{out} = 0$.

### Step 3: Smoke enters sensing chamber
Smoke **attaches to ions**, reducing the mobility $\mu$ of charge carriers. Since resistance is inversely proportional to mobility:

$$
R_2 = R_{2,0}\left(\frac{\mu_0}{\mu}\right) = R_{2,0}\left(1 + k\,c_{smoke}\right)
$$

where $k$ is a smoke-dependent constant and $c_{smoke}$ is smoke concentration.

### Step 4: Resulting bridge voltage
Substituting back:

$$
V_{out} = V_s\left( \frac{R_1}{R_1+R_3} - \frac{(1+k\,c)R_{2,0}}{(1+k\,c)R_{2,0}+R_4} \right)
$$

For small $k c_{smoke}$ and using $R_{2,0} = R_1$ and $R_4 = R_3$:

$$
\boxed{\,V_{out} \approx \frac{V_s}{2}\left( \frac{k\,c_{smoke}}{1 + R_3/R_1} \right) \approx \frac{V_s \, k \, c_{smoke}}{4}\,}
$$

This linear approximation shows that **the output voltage is directly proportional to smoke concentration** in the low-concentration regime — the basis for ionization detector sensitivity.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Range of a Flame Detector

The **inverse-square law** governs the intensity of radiation received from a point source:

$$
E = \frac{I_0}{R^2}
$$

For a flame detector with a minimum sensitivity threshold $E_{min}$ (in W/m²):

$$
\boxed{\,R_{max} = \sqrt{\frac{I_0}{E_{min}}}\,}
$$

**Worked example:**
Given flame radiance $I_0 = 200$ W/sr and detector threshold $E_{min} = 0.05$ W/m²:

$$
R_{max} = \sqrt{\frac{200}{0.05}} = \sqrt{4000} \approx 63.2 \text{ m}
$$

This matches the typical **60 m range** for a 0.1 m² gasoline fire.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Detector Type vs Fire Type Response (KTU-Favourite)

This matrix is **frequently asked** in KTU exams as a comparison table. Use it as a rapid revision tool.

| Fire Scenario | Ionization | Photo (Scatter) | Photo (Obstruct) | UV | IR | IR² | UV/IR |
|---|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| Smouldering wood | Poor | **Excellent** | Excellent | None | Poor | Poor | None |
| Flaming liquid pool | Good | Good | Good | **Excellent** | Excellent | **Excellent** | **Excellent** |
| Arc welding sparks | None | None | None | **False alarm risk** | None | None | Low risk |
| Cigarette smoke | Fair | Good | Good | None | None | None | None |
| Sunlight / lamps | None | None | None | None | Risk | Low risk | Low risk |
| Heptane pool fire | Good | Good | Good | **Excellent** | **Excellent** | **Excellent** | **Excellent** |
| Electrical cabinet | Fair | Good | Good | Good | Good | **Excellent** | Good |
| Aircraft engine | None | None | None | Good | **Excellent** | **Excellent** | **Excellent** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Reader's Hint:** For KTU answers, **always pair the fire type with the detector type** in your justifications. Examiners award marks for *matching the sensor to the hazard*.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Ionization Detector — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Am-241 Source] --> B[Reference Chamber R1]
    A --> C[Sensing Chamber R2]
    C -. smoke entry .-> C
    B --> D[Wheatstone Bridge]
    C --> D
    D --> E[Threshold Comparator]
    E --> F[Alarm Signal]
    G[Fixed Resistor R3] --> D
    H[Fixed Resistor R4] --> D
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The radioactive source feeds **two parallel chambers** — one sealed (reference) and one open to ambient air (sensing).
- A **Wheatstone bridge** compares the resistances of the two chambers against two fixed resistors.
- Smoke entering the sensing chamber unbalances the bridge, producing a voltage that is **compared with a preset threshold**.
- An **alarm signal** is generated when the imbalance exceeds the threshold.

---

## 4.2 Photoelectric Light-Scattering Detector — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[IR LED 880nm] -->|light beam| B[Dark Sensing Chamber]
    B <-->|smoke particles| B
    B -->|scattered light 90deg| C[Photodiode]
    C --> D[Amplifier]
    D --> E[Threshold Comparator]
    E --> F[Alarm Signal]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The LED fires an **infrared beam** into a chamber that, in clean air, allows the beam to **miss the photodiode entirely**.
- Smoke particles cause **Tyndall scattering**; a fraction of the scattered light is collected by the photodiode at **90°** to the original beam.
- The resulting photocurrent is **amplified** and compared with a threshold. Excess → alarm.

---

## 4.3 Photoelectric Light-Obstruction (Beam) Detector — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[IR LED Transmitter] -->|collimated beam| B[Chamber Air Space or Open Path]
    B -->|smoke particles attenuate beam| B
    B -->|reduced intensity beam| C[Photodiode Receiver]
    C --> D[Signal Comparator]
    D --> E[Threshold Detector]
    E -->|obscuration exceeded| F[Alarm Signal]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- For a **spot detector**, transmitter and receiver are inside one housing across a short internal air path.
- For a **beam-type detector**, the transmitter and receiver are mounted on opposite walls, several metres apart.
- The receiver **continuously monitors** the received intensity; an alarm is raised when the **attenuation exceeds a preset threshold** (e.g., 25% obscuration).

---

## 4.4 Flame Detector — Functional Block Diagram (UV/IR Combined)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Flame in Field of View] --> B[UV Sensor 180-260nm]
    A --> C[IR Sensor 4.4um]
    B --> D[UV Filter Amplifier]
    C --> E[IR Filter Amplifier]
    D --> F[AND Logic Gate]
    E --> F
    F -->|both UV and IR signatures present| G[Alarm Output]
    H[Flicker Analysis 1-10Hz] --> E
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The detector watches the **same field of view** through **two different spectral windows**.
- Each sensor's output is **filtered and amplified** separately.
- An **AND-logic gate** requires **both** UV and IR signals to be present **simultaneously** before triggering an alarm.
- The IR channel also performs **flicker analysis** (1–10 Hz) to discriminate flames from steady IR sources.

---

## 4.5 Combined Detector Selection Flowchart (Design Logic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Begin: Identify Fire Hazard]) --> Q1{Fire type?}
    Q1 -->|Smouldering cable or wood| S1[Photoelectric Light-Scattering]
    Q1 -->|Fast flaming hydrocarbon liquid| S2[Photoelectric + Flame Detector]
    Q1 -->|Arc or electrical fire| S3[UV or IR2 Detector]
    Q1 -->|High-ceiling atrium or warehouse| S4[Beam Smoke Detector]
    S1 --> Env{Environment?}
    S2 --> Env
    S3 --> Env
    S4 --> Env
    Env -->|False alarm sensitive area e.g. kitchen| Z1[Use Photoelectric Only]
    Env -->|High-risk industrial e.g. refinery| Z2[Use UV/IR or IR2 Combined]
    Env -->|Offshore platform| Z3[Use IR2 with CCTV verification]
    Z1 --> Done([Final Detector Selected])
    Z2 --> Done
    Z3 --> Done
```

---

## 4.6 Spectral Response Summary (Conceptual Chart)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Electromagnetic Spectrum] --> B[UV 0.1-0.35um]
    A --> C[Visible 0.4-0.7um]
    A --> D[IR 0.7-1000um]
    B --> B1[UV Flame Detector]
    C --> C1[Visual Flame Camera]
    D --> D1[IR Flame Detector 4.4um]
    D --> D2[IR2 Detector 4.4um and 3.8um]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]
**Explain the working principle of an ionization-type smoke detector. State the radioactive source used.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An ionization-type smoke detector uses a small quantity of the radioactive isotope **Americium-241 (Am-241)**, which emits **α-particles of energy ≈ 4.5 MeV**. These particles ionize the air inside two chambers — a **reference** chamber and a **sensing** chamber — producing a small steady **ionization current of the order of 10⁻¹¹ A**.

The two chambers form two arms of a **Wheatstone bridge circuit**. In clean air, the bridge is balanced, and the output voltage is zero. When **smoke enters the sensing chamber**, the smoke particles **attach to the ions**, reducing their mobility and therefore **decreasing the ionization current** in the sensing arm. This unbalances the bridge, producing an output voltage that, when it exceeds a preset threshold, **triggers the fire alarm**.

*(1 mark — radioactive source; 1 mark — chamber & α-particle ionisation; 1 mark — bridge unbalance and alarm.)*

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]
**Differentiate between the light-scattering and light-obstruction principles of photoelectric smoke detectors.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Light Scattering | Light Obstruction |
|---|---|---|
| LED–Photodiode geometry | Placed at **90°** (perpendicular) | Placed **co-axially (180°)** |
| Working principle | Smoke particles **scatter** light (Tyndall effect) into the photodiode | Smoke particles **block/absorb** the beam, reducing intensity at the photodiode |
| Baseline signal | **Zero** (photodiode in dark) | **Maximum** (full beam received) |
| Alarm trigger | Signal **rises** above zero | Signal **drops** below threshold |
| Best application | Spot-type residential detectors | Beam detectors for large halls, ducts |

*(1 mark per row × 3 = 3 marks; or 1 mark concept, 1 mark geometry, 1 mark application)*

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A. (a) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working of a UV/IR combined flame detector. Why is the AND-gate logic preferred over a single-sensor design?**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**1. Introduction (1 mark):**
A UV/IR combined flame detector is a **line-of-sight** device that simultaneously monitors the **ultraviolet (0.18–0.26 µm)** and **infrared (4.4 µm)** radiation emitted by a flame. It is widely used in **petrochemical, offshore, ammunition, and aircraft-hangar** applications.

**2. Block Diagram (2 marks):**

```
Flame → UV Sensor (180–260 nm) → UV Amplifier & Filter ─┐
                                                           AND-Gate → Alarm
Flame → IR Sensor (4.4 µm) → IR Amplifier & Flicker Anal. ─┘
```

**3. Working Principle (2 marks):**
- The UV sensor (typically a **photoelectric tube** or **solid-state SiC detector**) senses the **OH* and CO radical emissions** from the flame.
- The IR sensor (typically **PbSe or InGaAs**) senses the **4.4 µm CO₂ emission peak**, and uses **flicker analysis (1–10 Hz)** to discriminate against steady IR sources.
- Both signals are passed to an **AND-gate logic circuit**. The alarm is generated **only if both UV and IR signatures are detected simultaneously and persistently** for a preset confirmation time (typically 3–10 s).

**4. Why AND-logic? (2 marks):**
- A **UV-only** detector can be falsed by **arc welding, lightning, X-rays, or solar UV leakage**; an **IR-only** detector can be falsed by **sunlight, hot machinery, modulated heaters**.
- The AND-logic **drastically reduces false alarms**, because it is statistically near-impossible for **two unrelated nuisance sources to mimic a flame in both spectra simultaneously**.
- It also improves **reliability**: if one sensor fails, the detector is often configured to issue a **fault signal** rather than a false alarm.

---

### Question A. (b) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**A light-obstruction beam-type smoke detector is installed across a warehouse corridor. The path length is 40 m. The smoke extinction coefficient is 1.5 m²/g, and the smoke concentration during a smouldering fire is estimated as 0.08 g/m³. Determine:**
**(i) The obscuration in dB/m.**
**(ii) The total attenuation in dB over the full 40 m path.**
**(iii) The fraction of light received at the photodiode.**
**(iv) Comment on whether the alarm will trigger (threshold = 50% obscuration).**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- Path length $L = 40$ m
- Extinction coefficient $\sigma_s = 1.5$ m²/g
- Smoke concentration $c = 0.08$ g/m³
- Alarm threshold = 50% obscuration (i.e. $T = 0.5$)

**Step (i): Obscuration in dB/m (2 marks)**

$$
O_{dB/m} = 4.343 \times \sigma_s \times c
$$

$$
O_{dB/m} = 4.343 \times 1.5 \times 0.08
$$

$$
O_{dB/m} = 0.521 \text{ dB/m}
$$

**Step (ii): Total attenuation over 40 m (1 mark)**

$$
O_{total} = O_{dB/m} \times L = 0.521 \times 40 = 20.85 \text{ dB}
$$

**Step (iii): Fractional transmittance (2 marks)**

$$
T = 10^{-O_{total}/10} = 10^{-20.85/10} = 10^{-2.085}
$$

$$
T \approx 0.00823
$$

So only about **0.82% of the light reaches the receiver** — i.e., the light is **99.18% blocked**.

**Step (iv): Alarm verification (2 marks)**

The alarm threshold corresponds to a transmittance of $T_{alarm} = 0.5$, i.e. attenuation = 3.01 dB.

Since the **actual attenuation (20.85 dB) ≫ 3.01 dB**, the alarm condition is **massively exceeded**.

**Conclusion:** The beam detector will **definitely trigger** the alarm. In fact, the alarm would have been triggered at a smoke concentration of just $c_{min}$:

$$
c_{min} = \frac{3.01}{4.343 \times 1.5 \times 40} = 0.0116 \text{ g/m}^3
$$

This shows the detector is **highly sensitive** to this smouldering fire scenario.

---

### Question B. (a) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Model Paper]

**With a neat diagram, explain the working of a photoelectric light-scattering smoke detector. State two advantages and two limitations.**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**1. Diagram (2 marks):**

```
   LED (880 nm IR)
         │
         ▼
   ┌──────────┐
   │  Dark    │  smoke particles scatter
   │ Chamber  │ ───► (light at 90°) ───► Photodiode
   └──────────┘
         │
        Power & Alarm Circuit
```

**2. Working (2 marks):**
An **infrared LED** (typically λ = 880 nm) emits a beam inside a **light-trapped (dark) chamber**. A **photodiode** is mounted at **right angles (90°)** to the beam. In clean air, the photodiode receives **no light** because the beam does not reach it. When **smoke particles enter the chamber**, the light is **scattered in all directions** (Tyndall effect); a fraction of the scattered light falls on the photodiode. The resulting photocurrent is **amplified** and **compared with a threshold** to generate the alarm.

**3. Advantages (1.5 marks):**
- Highly responsive to **smouldering fires** that produce large, light-scattering particles.
- **Stable in the presence of cooking fumes** (compared to ionization detectors).

**4. Limitations (1.5 marks):**
- Less responsive to **very small, invisible smoke particles** (e.g., alcohol flame) compared to ionization detectors.
- Requires regular cleaning of the chamber; **dust accumulation** can cause false alarms or sensitivity drift.

---

### Question B. (b) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Model Paper]

**Compare ionization and photoelectric smoke detectors under the following heads: (i) detection principle, (ii) smoke particle size sensitivity, (iii) response to smouldering vs flaming fires, (iv) false-alarm susceptibility, (v) preferred modern application.**

**Model Answer (7 marks — ~1.4 marks per head):**

| Head | Ionization | Photoelectric (Light Scattering) |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Detection principle | **Ion current reduction** due to ion-attachment on smoke | **Light scattering** (Tyndall effect) by smoke particles |
| (ii) Particle size sensitivity | Best for **small, invisible particles** (0.01–0.1 µm) | Best for **larger, visible particles** (0.3–1 µm) |
| (iii) Smouldering vs flaming | **Faster** to flaming; **slower** to smouldering | **Faster** to smouldering; acceptable to flaming |
| (iv) False alarms | More sensitive to **cooking fumes, steam, aerosols** | Less prone; better suited to kitchens (though still not ideal) |
| (v) Modern application | Declining use in new installations | **Preferred in modern installations** (NFPA 72 recommends photoelectric for residential) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Don't confuse Am-241 (ionization) with Co-60 or Cs-137.** Am-241 emits **α-particles only**, not γ-rays.
> 2. **Don't say "photoelectric detects heat."** It detects **scattered light from smoke**, not heat.
> 3. **Don't claim ionization is "faster" universally.** It is faster for flaming fires but **slower** for smouldering fires.
> 4. **Don't omit units** in dB/m calculations. Examiner deducts **0.5–1 mark** for missing units.
> 5. **Don't state "UV detector is best for all flames."** UV alone is prone to false alarms from **arc welding** — always use UV/IR combined.
> 6. **Don't confuse light-scattering (90°) with light-obstruction (180°)** geometry. This is a **favourite 3-mark question**.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- **Ionization detector** uses **Am-241** (α-source, 4.5 MeV, T₁/₂ = 432.2 yr); senses **reduction in ion current** in a Wheatstone bridge.
- **Photoelectric light-scattering** uses **Tyndall effect** at **90°** geometry; LED at **880 nm**.
- **Photoelectric light-obstruction** uses **co-axial (180°)** geometry; alarm on beam attenuation.
- **Beam-type detectors** are used in **large halls, atria, warehouses** with path lengths up to **100 m**.
- **Flame detectors** are **line-of-sight**; respond in **< 3 seconds**; suitable for **clean, high-risk areas**.
- **UV flame detector** is sensitive to **0.18–0.26 µm**; rejected by ozone cutoff **0.29 µm**; sensitive to arc welding.
- **IR flame detector** is sensitive to **4.4 µm (CO₂ peak)**; uses **flicker analysis 1–10 Hz**.
- **IR² detector** compares **4.4 µm vs 3.8 µm** ratio — **best false-alarm rejection**.
- **UV/IR combined** uses **AND-gate logic** — virtually eliminates false alarms.
- **Beer–Lambert law**: $I = I_0 \, e^{-\sigma_s c L}$ governs beam-type detection.
- **Obscuration conversion**: $O_{dB/m} = 4.343 \times \sigma_s \times c$.
- **Smoke obscuration unit** in India (per IS 2189) is typically expressed in **% per metre**.
- **Selection rule of thumb**:
  - **Smouldering** → Photoelectric
  - **Flaming hydrocarbon** → Photoelectric + Flame (UV/IR or IR²)
  - **Electrical / arc** → UV or IR²
  - **Large open area** → Beam detector
  - **High false-alarm risk** → Combined UV/IR or IR²

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Infra Red and Ultra Violet Detectors. Flammable Gas Detection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT836 - DESIGN OF FIRE PROTECTION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Infra Red and Ultra Violet Detectors. Flammable Gas Detection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Infrared (IR) Flame & Fire Detectors

**Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology):**
An **Infrared (IR) Flame Detector** is an optical fire-sensing device that detects the presence of flame and fire by identifying the characteristic infrared radiation emitted from the combustion process, typically in the spectral band of **4.3 µm** (carbon dioxide resonance band) and **0.8 µm to 1.1 µm** (hydrocarbon flame flicker band). The detector converts incident IR photons into electrical signals via thermal or quantum (photoconductive/photovoltaic) sensors.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Terminology:** IR detectors fall under the category of **line-of-sight optical flame detectors** governed by international standards such as **EN 54-10** and **UL 268**. They are categorized into **Single IR, Dual IR (IR/IR), and Triple IR (IR/IR/IR)** sensors.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of an IR flame detector as a "**thermal signature camera with a brain**." Just as a snake can sense the body heat of prey in complete darkness using its pit organs, an IR detector perceives the invisible heat signature of a flame. When a candle burns, you can feel its warmth without seeing it — an IR detector operates on this same principle but with extreme precision, isolating only those wavelengths unique to hydrocarbon fires (mainly CO₂ emission at 4.3 µm) to avoid false triggers from sunlight or hot machinery.

---

### 1.2 Ultraviolet (UV) Flame & Fire Detectors

**Formal Definition:**
An **Ultraviolet (UV) Flame Detector** is a photoelectric fire-detection device sensitive to ultraviolet radiation emitted by flames, typically in the spectral range of **185 nm to 260 nm** (solar-blind UV band). It uses a gas-filled tube (e.g., Geiger-Müller type) or a solid-state photodiode (e.g., SiC, GaN, AlGaN) to convert UV photons into electrical pulses.

> [!NOTE]
> **Solar-Blind Principle:** The Earth's atmosphere naturally absorbs UV radiation below 290 nm (ozone layer absorption). Therefore, detectors tuned to <260 nm will NOT respond to sunlight, making them inherently **false-alarm immune** to solar radiation — a critical KTU examination point.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine a **"bee-vision fire alarm."** Bees can see UV light that humans cannot. Similarly, UV flame detectors "see" a dimension of light invisible to our eyes. A flame emits a brief UV flash every time a chemical bond breaks and reforms. The detector counts these flashes like a Geiger counter, and when the count rate exceeds a threshold, it triggers the alarm. Because sunlight contains no UV below 290 nm, the detector is essentially "blind" to daylight — it only "sees" the fire.

---

### 1.3 Flammable Gas Detection Systems

**Formal Definition:**
A **Flammable Gas Detector** is a safety instrument that continuously monitors the concentration of combustible gases (e.g., methane CH₄, propane C₃H₈, hydrogen H₂) in air and provides an alarm or control action when the concentration reaches a preset fraction of the **Lower Flammable Limit (LFL)** or **Lower Explosive Limit (LEL)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LFL vs. LEL:** Although often used interchangeably, **LFL** refers to the lowest concentration of gas in air that can ignite, while **LEL** is the same value expressed as a percentage — for methane, LEL = **5% by volume** in air, corresponding to **100% LEL**. Detectors typically alarm at **10% to 25% LEL**.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of a **"carbon monoxide detector for fuel leaks"** in your kitchen — but industrial-grade. Just as a smoke detector sniffs out invisible particles, a flammable gas detector "sniffs" invisible hydrocarbon molecules. The most common type, a **catalytic bead (pellistor) sensor**, works like a tiny "hot plate" — gas burns on its surface, increasing temperature and changing electrical resistance, much like a thermometer whose mercury rises when the room gets warm. The reading on the control panel (in % LEL) tells you, "If the concentration reaches 100%, one spark and the whole room explodes."

---

### 1.4 Core Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following values are **bold-highlighted** for KTU examination recall:

- **LFL of Methane (CH₄) = 5.0% vol**
- **LFL of Propane (C₃H₈) = 2.1% vol**
- **LFL of Hydrogen (H₂) = 4.0% vol**
- **LFL of Gasoline vapors = 1.4% vol**
- **Speed of light in vacuum: c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s**
- **Planck's constant: h = 6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s**
- **Stefan-Boltzmann constant: σ = 5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/m²·K⁴**
- **UV solar-blind cutoff: 290 nm (atmospheric absorption)**
- **IR CO₂ emission band: 4.3 µm**

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Practical Note:** Memorize LFL values of common hydrocarbons. The LEL alarm threshold for safety systems is mandated as **10% to 25% LEL** (pre-alarm) and **60% LEL** (evacuation) by IS 15105 and NFPA 72.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle of IR Flame Detectors (Deep Dive)

**Mechanism — Step-by-Step Logic:**

1. **Emission Stage:** A hydrocarbon flame produces CO₂ as a combustion byproduct. The excited CO₂ molecules relax, emitting photons strongly in the **4.3 µm band** (fundamental vibrational mode).
2. **Optical Filtering Stage:** A narrow-band optical filter centered at 4.3 µm blocks all other IR wavelengths, allowing only the CO₂ resonance signal to reach the sensor.
3. **Transduction Stage:** A **pyroelectric**, **thermopile**, or **quantum (PbSe, InGaAs)** sensor converts incident IR photons into an electrical signal (voltage or current).
4. **Flicker Analysis Stage:** Hydrocarbon flames exhibit a characteristic flicker frequency between **1 Hz and 10 Hz** (caused by turbulent eddies in the diffusion flame). The detector's signal processor performs a **Fast Fourier Transform (FFT)** to identify this signature.
5. **Decision Stage:** If the flicker frequency AND amplitude match the flame signature for a sustained period (typically 3–30 seconds), a fire alarm is generated.

> [!TIP]
> **Why "Triple IR" beats "Single IR":** Triple IR detectors use three sensors at 4.3 µm, 4.4 µm, and 4.5 µm. By comparing the ratios, the detector mathematically cancels blackbody radiation from hot machinery (e.g., engine exhaust) — the **CO₂ Ratio Method** — yielding ultra-low false alarm rates.

---

### 2.2 Operating Principle of UV Flame Detectors (Deep Dive)

1. **Emission Stage:** Hydrocarbon flames produce **OH·** and **CH·** free radicals that emit UV photons in the **200–300 nm** range through chemiluminescence.
2. **Detection Stage:** The UV photons enter a gas-filled tube (e.g., filled with argon or a halogen). The high-energy photon ejects an electron from the cathode via the **photoelectric effect**.
3. **Gas Multiplication Stage:** The ejected electron is accelerated by a high-voltage field (~300–500 V) and ionizes gas atoms, creating a **Townsend avalanche** — a measurable current pulse.
4. **Pulse Counting Stage:** A pulse-counting circuit records the rate of UV events. Solar-blind UV (< 260 nm) is used to avoid solar false alarms.
5. **Decision Stage:** When the pulse rate exceeds a threshold (typically 1–10 Hz) for a defined period, an alarm fires.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hybrid UV/IR Detectors:** Most modern industrial detectors combine UV and IR sensors in a single unit. This provides **AND-gate logic** — both UV AND IR signals must be present to alarm — virtually eliminating all known false alarm sources (arc welding, lightning, sunlight, hot objects).

---

### 2.3 Operating Principle of Flammable Gas Detectors

**Three Primary Sensor Technologies:**

#### (a) Catalytic Bead (Pellistor) Sensor

- **Operating principle:** Oxidation of gas on a heated catalyst bead (typically **~500 °C**) raises its temperature, increasing electrical resistance. A matched reference bead (inert) compensates for ambient temperature.
- **Wheatstone Bridge Equation:**

$$
R_{bridge} = \frac{R_1}{R_2} = \frac{R_3 + \Delta R_{sensor}}{R_4}
$$

where the imbalance $\Delta R_{sensor}$ is proportional to gas concentration.

- **Limitation:** Can be poisoned by silicone vapors, lead compounds, and H₂S. Requires oxygen to function.

#### (b) Infrared (NDIR) Gas Sensor

- **Operating principle:** Uses **Non-Dispersive Infrared (NDIR)** absorption. A broadband IR source emits through a gas chamber; a specific wavelength is absorbed by the target gas. The reduction in intensity follows the **Beer-Lambert Law** (see Section 3).
- **Advantage:** Immune to poisoning, works in oxygen-free environments.

#### (c) Semiconductor (Metal-Oxide) Sensor

- **Operating principle:** Gas adsorbs on a heated metal-oxide surface (e.g., SnO₂), changing its electrical conductivity. Resistance change is proportional to gas concentration.
- **Formula (Conductivity Model):**

$$
G = G_0 + A \cdot [C_gas]^n
$$

where $G$ is conductance, $G_0$ is baseline, $A$ is a constant, $[C_{gas}]$ is concentration, and $n \approx 0.5$ for typical sensors.

---

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination-critical formulas for IR/UV Detectors and Flammable Gas Detection — commit to memory.**

| # | Formula / Concept | LaTeX Form | Variables & Units | Real-World Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Beer-Lambert Law** | $I = I_0 \, e^{-\alpha \, C \, L}$ | $I$ = transmitted intensity (W/m²), $I_0$ = incident intensity, $\alpha$ = absorption coefficient (ppm⁻¹·m⁻¹), $C$ = gas concentration (ppm), $L$ = path length (m) | NDIR gas sensor calculation |
| 2 | **Absorbance** | $A = -\log_{10}\left(\dfrac{I}{I_0}\right) = \alpha C L$ | dimensionless | Spectroscopic analysis |
| 3 | **Stefan-Boltzmann Law** | $P = \sigma \varepsilon A T^4$ | $P$ = radiated power (W), $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/m²·K⁴, $\varepsilon$ = emissivity, $A$ = area (m²), $T$ = temperature (K) | Total IR radiation from a flame |
| 4 | **Wien's Displacement Law** | $\lambda_{max} = \dfrac{b}{T}$ | $\lambda_{max}$ = peak wavelength (m), $b = 2.898 \times 10^{-3}$ m·K, $T$ = temperature (K) | Optimal IR detector wavelength |
| 5 | **Photon Energy** | $E = h f = \dfrac{h c}{\lambda}$ | $E$ = energy (J), $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s, $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s, $\lambda$ = wavelength (m) | UV photon detection threshold |
| 6 | **%LEL Conversion** | $\%LEL = \dfrac{C_{measured}}{C_{LFL}} \times 100$ | $C_{measured}$ = actual gas concentration, $C_{LFL}$ = lower flammable limit (%vol) | Gas detector display |
| 7 | **Pellistor Sensitivity** | $\Delta R \propto \Delta T \propto C_{gas}$ | $\Delta R$ = resistance change (Ω), $\Delta T$ = temperature rise (K) | Catalytic bead gas sensor |
| 8 | **Planck's Spectral Radiance** | $B_\lambda = \dfrac{2 h c^2}{\lambda^5} \dfrac{1}{e^{hc/\lambda k_B T} - 1}$ | $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K | Blackbody IR emission modeling |
| 9 | **CO₂ IR Emission Band** | $\lambda = 4.3 \; \mu m$ | fixed for hydrocarbon flames | IR flame detector filter center |
| 10 | **Solar-Blind UV Cutoff** | $\lambda < 290 \; nm$ | atmospheric ozone absorption | UV detector design |

---

### 2.5 Why These Systems Matter in Engineering Fields

- **Oil & Gas Industry:** Offshore platforms, refineries, and LNG terminals depend on UV/IR flame detectors for **<10 second** fire confirmation.
- **Automotive Tunnels:** IR linear heat detection (separate technology) + gas detection for CNG/LPG vehicles.
- **Semiconductor Manufacturing:** Detection of silane (SiH₄) leaks — LFL ≈ 1.4% vol.
- **Battery Energy Storage Systems (BESS):** Hydrogen and electrolyte vapor detection per **UL 9540A**.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Interview Tip:** When asked about detector selection, state: *"For indoor enclosed spaces with low ventilation, use catalytic bead or NDIR gas detectors. For open-area hydrocarbon flame detection, use Triple IR or UV/IR hybrid detectors. UV is preferred for non-smoldering, fast-flash fires; IR excels at liquid fuel pool fires."*

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Beer-Lambert Law for NDIR Gas Detection

**Starting Premise:** When monochromatic radiation of intensity $I_0$ passes through a gas sample of concentration $C$ and path length $L$, the intensity decreases as it traverses each infinitesimal slab $dL$.

**Step 1 — Differential Formulation**

Consider a thin gas slab of thickness $dL$. The reduction in intensity $dI$ is proportional to:
- the incident intensity $I$ at that point
- the gas concentration $C$
- the slab thickness $dL$

Therefore, the differential equation governing absorption is:

$$
dI = -\alpha \, I \, C \, dL
$$

where $\alpha$ is the **absorption coefficient** (a property of the gas-wavelength pair).

**Step 2 — Separation of Variables**

Rearrange to separate $I$ and $L$:

$$
\frac{dI}{I} = -\alpha \, C \, dL
$$

**Step 3 — Integration Across the Full Path Length**

Integrate from $I_0$ (entrance) to $I$ (exit), and from $0$ to $L$:

$$
\int_{I_0}^{I} \frac{dI'}{I'} = -\alpha C \int_{0}^{L} dL'
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the Integrals**

The left integral yields $\ln(I/I_0)$ and the right integral yields $\alpha C L$:

$$
\ln\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right) = -\alpha C L
$$

**Step 5 — Exponential Form (Final Result)**

Exponentiate both sides:

$$
I = I_0 \, e^{-\alpha C L}
$$

This is the **Beer-Lambert Law**. $\square$

**Step 6 — Engineering Application: Concentration Calculation**

For a calibrated NDIR sensor with known $\alpha$, $L$, and measured $I/I_0$:

$$
C = \frac{1}{\alpha L} \ln\left(\frac{I_0}{I}\right)
$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Optimal IR Detector Wavelength for a Given Flame Temperature (Wien's Law)

**Step 1 — Planck's Law Background**

The spectral radiance $B_\lambda$ of a blackbody at temperature $T$ peaks at a wavelength obtained by setting $dB_\lambda/d\lambda = 0$.

**Step 2 — Derivative of Planck's Function**

Without re-deriving Planck's law (well-established), the optimization yields the transcendental equation:

$$
5\left(1 - e^{-x}\right) = x
$$

where $x = hc/(\lambda_{max} k_B T)$.

**Step 3 — Numerical Solution**

The root is $x \approx 4.965$, leading to:

$$
\lambda_{max} = \frac{hc}{4.965 \, k_B T}
$$

**Step 4 — Insert Constants**

Substituting $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s, $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s, and $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K:

$$
b = \frac{hc}{4.965 \, k_B} = 2.898 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{m·K}
$$

**Step 5 — Final Form (Wien's Displacement Law)**

$$
\lambda_{max} = \frac{2.898 \times 10^{-3}}{T} \quad [\text{in meters}]
$$

**Step 6 — Numerical Example for a Hydrocarbon Flame**

Assume a flame temperature $T = 1500$ K. Then:

$$
\lambda_{max} = \frac{2.898 \times 10^{-3}}{1500} = 1.932 \times 10^{-6} \; \text{m} = 1.93 \; \mu\text{m}
$$

This is **near the 2 µm band**, explaining why some IR flame detectors target 1.9–2.0 µm in addition to the 4.3 µm CO₂ band. $\square$

---

### 3.3 Derivation: % LEL Reading from a Wheatstone Bridge Output

**Step 1 — Bridge Equation**

A catalytic bead sensor forms one arm of a Wheatstone bridge:

$$
V_{out} = V_{ex} \left( \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} - \frac{R_3 + \Delta R}{R_3 + \Delta R + R_4} \right)
$$

**Step 2 — Balanced Condition (No Gas)**

At zero gas, $R_1 R_4 = R_2 R_3$ and $V_{out} = 0$.

**Step 3 — Linearized Output (Small $\Delta R$)**

For small changes, applying Taylor expansion yields:

$$
V_{out} \approx \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R}
$$

**Step 4 — Sensitivity to Gas Concentration**

From Section 2.3(a), $\Delta R = k_s C$, where $k_s$ is the sensor sensitivity (Ω/ppm):

$$
V_{out} = \frac{V_{ex} \, k_s}{4 R} \cdot C
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to %LEL**

Using $C = (\%LEL/100) \cdot C_{LFL}$:

$$
V_{out} = \frac{V_{ex} \, k_s \, C_{LFL}}{400 R} \cdot \%LEL
$$

**Step 6 — Example Calculation**

For methane ($C_{LFL} = 5\% vol$), $V_{ex} = 5$ V, $k_s = 10$ Ω/%vol, $R = 100$ Ω:

$$
V_{out} \; \text{per \%LEL} = \frac{5 \times 10 \times 5}{400 \times 100} = 6.25 \; \text{mV / \%LEL}
$$

At **25% LEL alarm threshold**: $V_{out} = 156.25$ mV. $\square$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Detector Selection Decision Tree

| Application Scenario | Recommended Detector | Justification | False Alarm Risk |
|---|---|---|---|
| Indoor generator room (CH₄ leak) | Catalytic bead (pellistor) | Low cost, %LEL output, suitable for LEL 0–100% | Moderate (poisoning) |
| Offshore drilling platform (open area) | Triple IR (IR/IR/IR) | Solar-blind ratio method, weather-resistant | Very Low |
| Hydrogen refueling station | Catalytic bead (H₂-specific) or EC sensor | Hydrogen's high diffusivity and low LFL (4%) | Low |
| Semiconductor cleanroom (SiH₄) | Electrochemical + UV/IR | SiH₄ is pyrophoric; needs sub-LFL detection | Low |
| Aircraft hangar (JP-4 fuel) | UV/IR Hybrid | Large open area, false alarm from welding | Very Low (AND-logic) |
| Parking garage (CNG/LPG vehicles) | NDIR (methane/propane specific) | Selective, no poisoning from exhaust | Low |
| Battery storage room (Li-ion thermal runaway) | UV (sparks) + Smoke | Early off-gas detection | Low |
