## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**
**Define an Infrared (IR) flame detector. State any two advantages of using Triple IR detectors over Single IR detectors.**

**Model Answer:**

An **Infrared (IR) flame detector** is an optical fire-sensing device that detects the characteristic infrared radiation (typically at 4.3 µm) emitted by the CO₂ generated during hydrocarbon combustion.

**Two advantages of Triple IR over Single IR:**

1. **False alarm rejection via CO₂ ratio method:** Three sensors at slightly different wavelengths (4.3, 4.4, 4.5 µm) compute intensity ratios. Real flames produce a fixed CO₂ ratio; hot machinery/blackbodies do not. This eliminates false alarms from sunlight, hot exhausts, and incandescent lamps.
2. **Longer detection range and wider field of view:** Triple IR detectors typically offer detection distances of up to 60–90 m, compared to 15–25 m for single IR units, making them suitable for large open areas like offshore platforms and aircraft hangars.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question]**
**What is meant by "solar-blind" UV detection? Why is it important for flame detection?**

**Model Answer:**

**Solar-blind UV detection** refers to the technique of designing UV detectors sensitive to wavelengths **below 290 nm**, a spectral region in which the Sun's radiation is completely absorbed by the Earth's ozone layer. Therefore, the detector is "blind" to sunlight but can still "see" UV radiation from a flame.

**Importance:**

- It provides an **inherent immunity to solar false alarms**, which is critical because most outdoor and indoor (with windows) flame-detection installations would otherwise trigger continuously during daylight hours.
- It allows UV detectors to be used in outdoor applications (e.g., oil refineries, petrochemical storage yards) without requiring complex optical filtering or modulation schemes to reject sunlight.
- It forms the basis of the **UV/IR hybrid AND-logic** architecture, which is the gold standard for industrial flame detection.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice Pattern)

---

### **Question A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Pattern]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with neat sketches the construction and working principle of a **Triple IR (IR/IR/IR) flame detector**. How does it discriminate between a real fire and a false alarm source?

**(b) [7 Marks]** A catalytic bead gas detector is calibrated for methane. The Wheatstone bridge has all four arms initially at 100 Ω. The active bead resistance increases by 0.5 Ω when exposed to 1% by volume methane. If the bridge excitation voltage is 5 V, calculate:
  (i) The output voltage at 1% CH₄.
  (ii) The output voltage at 20% LEL (LFL of CH₄ = 5% vol).
  (iii) Comment on whether the 20% LEL signal is sufficient to trigger a pre-alarm if the pre-alarm threshold is 50 mV.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

A **Triple IR flame detector** consists of the following components:

1. **Optical Window:** Made of sapphire or germanium, transparent to IR radiation in the 4–5 µm band but blocking visible light and shorter wavelengths.
2. **Beam Splitter:** Divides incoming IR radiation into three parallel optical paths.
3. **Three IR Sensors:** Each sensor is preceded by a **narrow-band optical interference filter** centered at a different wavelength:
   - Sensor 1: **4.3 µm** (CO₂ emission peak — fire signature)
   - Sensor 2: **4.4 µm** (adjacent reference — fire also has emission here, but with different ratio)
   - Sensor 3: **4.5 µm** (further reference — fire has very low emission here)
4. **Signal Processing Electronics:** Each sensor has its own amplifier, and the three signals are fed into a microprocessor.
5. **Alarm Output:** Relay or 4–20 mA output for fire alarm indication.

**Working Principle:**

- Hydrocarbon flames emit strong CO₂ radiation in the 4.3 µm band. The intensities at 4.4 µm and 4.5 µm are progressively lower (the CO₂ band is sharply peaked).
- The microprocessor computes two ratios: $R_1 = I_{4.4}/I_{4.3}$ and $R_2 = I_{4.5}/I_{4.3}$.
- A **real hydrocarbon flame** produces a fixed, known ratio pair $(R_1^{fire}, R_2^{fire})$.
- A **hot blackbody** (sunlight, exhaust pipe, heater) produces a smooth, broadband spectrum where the ratio pair is **different** from the flame signature.
- The microprocessor's algorithm confirms a fire only when:
  - The intensity at 4.3 µm exceeds a threshold,
  - Both ratios match the flame signature within tolerance,
  - The flicker frequency (1–10 Hz) is detected via FFT,
  - These conditions persist for 3–30 seconds (configurable).

**False Alarm Discrimination:**

- **Sunlight:** Has no narrow 4.3 µm peak; ratio check fails.
- **Arc welding:** Strong UV, weak CO₂ IR at 4.3 µm; ratio check fails.
- **Hot machinery:** Broadband blackbody; ratio check fails.
- **Lightning:** Transient, short duration; persistence timer fails.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Given:**
- Initial resistance of all four arms: $R_1 = R_2 = R_3 = R_4 = R = 100 \; \Omega$
- Excitation voltage: $V_{ex} = 5$ V
- Sensitivity: $\Delta R = 0.5 \; \Omega$ per 1% vol CH₄
- LFL of CH₄: $C_{LFL} = 5\%$ vol

**Step 1 — Output Voltage at 1% CH₄:**

The active bead is in arm 3, so $R_3 = 100 + 0.5 = 100.5 \; \Omega$.

$$
V_{out} = V_{ex} \left( \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} - \frac{R_3}{R_3 + R_4} \right)
$$

$$
V_{out} = 5 \left( \frac{100}{200} - \frac{100.5}{200.5} \right)
$$

$$
V_{out} = 5 \left( 0.5000 - 0.5012 \right) = 5 \times (-0.001246) = -6.23 \; \text{mV}
$$

Taking magnitude: $|V_{out}| = 6.23$ mV per 1% vol CH₄.

**Step 2 — Output Voltage at 20% LEL:**

20% LEL of CH₄ = $0.20 \times 5 = 1.0\%$ vol.

Therefore, the output at 20% LEL is the same as at 1% vol:

$$
V_{out} = 6.23 \; \text{mV}
$$

**Step 3 — Comparison with 50 mV Threshold:**

The pre-alarm threshold is 50 mV, but the output is only **6.23 mV**. Therefore, the signal is **insufficient** to trigger the pre-alarm.

**Conclusion / Engineering Insight:**

The detector requires **amplification**. A gain of approximately $50/6.23 \approx 8$ would be needed, OR the alarm threshold should be redesigned to 6 mV (10% LEL) to match the actual sensor sensitivity. This is a common **valuation trap** — students often forget the linearization assumption and over-estimate the bridge output.

---

### **Question B (Choice 2) — [KTU University Exam – June 2023 Pattern]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **Beer-Lambert Law** as applied to a **Non-Dispersive Infrared (NDIR) gas detector**. A methane NDIR sensor has a path length of 0.1 m and an absorption coefficient of 0.05 ppm⁻¹·m⁻¹ at the measurement wavelength. If the transmitted intensity is 90% of the incident intensity, determine the methane concentration in ppm.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare **UV, IR, and UV/IR hybrid flame detectors** in terms of detection principle, response time, false alarm immunity, and suitable applications. Present the comparison in a tabular form.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

**Beer-Lambert Law (Statement):**

When monochromatic light of intensity $I_0$ passes through an absorbing medium of path length $L$ and absorber concentration $C$, the transmitted intensity $I$ is given by:

$$
I = I_0 \, e^{-\alpha C L}
$$

where $\alpha$ is the **absorption coefficient** (in units of ppm⁻¹·m⁻¹ for gas measurements).

**Application to NDIR Detector:**

In an NDIR sensor, a broadband IR source emits radiation that passes through a gas measurement chamber. A narrow-band optical filter selects the specific absorption wavelength of the target gas (e.g., 3.3 µm for methane, 4.3 µm for CO₂). The intensity reduction is detected by a thermopile or pyroelectric sensor. The ratio $I/I_0$ is converted to concentration via the inverse of the Beer-Lambert equation.

**Step 1 — Identify Given Values**

- Path length: $L = 0.1$ m
- Absorption coefficient: $\alpha = 0.05$ ppm⁻¹·m⁻¹
- Transmitted/Incident ratio: $I/I_0 = 0.90$

**Step 2 — Rearrange Beer-Lambert Equation for Concentration**

Take the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$
\ln\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right) = -\alpha C L
$$

Solve for $C$:

$$
C = -\frac{1}{\alpha L} \ln\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute Numerical Values**

$$
C = -\frac{1}{(0.05)(0.1)} \ln(0.90)
$$

$$
C = -\frac{1}{0.005} \times (-0.10536)
$$

$$
C = 200 \times 0.10536 = 21.07 \; \text{ppm}
$$

**Result:** The methane concentration is approximately **21.07 ppm**.

**Cross-check:** This is well below the LFL of 5% vol (50,000 ppm), so it represents a leak detection scenario, not an explosive atmosphere.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(b): Comparison Table**

> [!NOTE]
> **Important:** Do NOT use the vertical pipe `|` inside table cells. Use `vert` or `mid` for separators in formulas within cells.

| Parameter | UV Detector | IR Detector | UV/IR Hybrid |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Detection Principle** | Detects UV photons (185–260 nm) from flame free-radical emission (OH, CH) using phototubes or solid-state sensors | Detects IR photons (4.3 µm CO₂ band) from combustion using thermal or quantum sensors | Requires simultaneous UV AND IR signature confirmation |
| **Response Time** | Very fast (1–3 seconds) for flash fires | 3–10 seconds (requires flicker analysis) | 3–10 seconds (depends on slowest channel) |
| **False Alarm Immunity** | High (solar-blind); vulnerable to arc welding, X-rays, lightning | Moderate (improved with Triple IR); vulnerable to hot machinery, sunlight | Very High (AND-logic eliminates all known false sources) |
| **Detection Range** | 10–30 m typical | 15–90 m (Triple IR extends range) | 15–60 m |
| **Indoor/Outdoor Use** | Both (solar-blind for outdoor) | Both (Triple IR for outdoor) | Both, preferred for high-risk industrial |
| **Suitable Applications** | Indoor enclosed spaces, ammunition storage, aircraft hangars, hydrogen fires | Offshore platforms, oil refineries, chemical plants, large open areas | Critical fire protection: hydrocarbon storage, turbine enclosures, paint spray booths |
| **Limitation** | Oil films, dirt on window cause false negatives | Heated surfaces can cause false alarms (single IR) | Higher cost than single-technology detectors |
| **Standards** | EN 54-10 Class 1, 2, 3 | EN 54-10 Class 1, 2, 3 | EN 54-10 Class 1 (highest) |

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in IR/UV/Gas Detection Questions:**

1. **Confusing LFL with LEL:** LFL (Lower Flammable Limit) is a concentration in %vol. LEL (Lower Explosive Limit) is a percentage of LFL. They are numerically identical but conceptually different. Writing "LFL = 5% LEL" loses a mark.

2. **Forgetting Units in Beer-Lambert Calculations:** If the absorption coefficient is given in ppm⁻¹·m⁻¹, the path length MUST be in meters, and the answer will be in ppm. Mixing cm with ppm⁻¹·m⁻¹ is a frequent error.

3. **Ignoring the Solar-Blind Principle:** Stating that UV detectors can detect sunlight is an immediate mark-deduction in KTU valuation. Always mention the 290 nm ozone absorption cutoff.

4. **Single IR vs. Triple IR Confusion:** KTU examiners specifically test whether students know that **Triple IR** uses the **CO₂ ratio method** to reject false alarms, not just "more sensors = better."

5. **Pellistor Poisoning:** When asked about catalytic bead sensors, students often forget to mention that **silicone vapors, H₂S, and lead compounds poison the catalyst**, rendering the sensor inoperative — a real-world failure mode examiners love to test.

6. **Linearization of Wheatstone Bridge:** Using the exact bridge equation $V_{out} = V_{ex}(R_1/(R_1+R_2) - R_3/(R_3+R_4))$ without linearization is acceptable; however, students often mistakenly use $V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot \Delta R / (4R)$ when $\Delta R$ is NOT small — this is a common error.

7. **Detector Selection Justification:** Always justify your detector choice with the **application environment** and **type of fire/gas** — never just write "use IR detector" without context.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for "Infra Red, Ultra Violet Detectors & Flammable Gas Detection":**

- **IR Flame Detectors:**
  - Operate at **4.3 µm** (CO₂ emission band)
  - **Triple IR (IR/IR/IR)** uses **ratio method** for false alarm rejection
  - Detect flame **flicker** at **1–10 Hz**
  - Suitable for hydrocarbon fires; vulnerable to false alarms from hot machinery (single IR)

- **UV Flame Detectors:**
  - Operate in the **solar-blind band (< 290 nm)**
  - Use **Geiger-Müller tubes** or **solid-state photodiodes (SiC, GaN)**
  - Extremely fast response (1–3 seconds) for flash fires
  - Vulnerable to arc welding, X-rays, lightning (mitigated by UV/IR AND-logic)

- **UV/IR Hybrid Detectors:**
  - Use **AND-gate logic** — both UV and IR must be present
  - Industry **gold standard** for false alarm rejection
  - Compliant with **EN 54-10 Class 1**

- **Flammable Gas Detectors:**
  - Three main technologies: **Catalytic Bead (Pellistor), NDIR, Semiconductor (MOS)**
  - Output is in **%LEL** of the specific gas
  - Standard alarm thresholds: **10% LEL (low), 25% LEL (medium), 60% LEL (high)**
  - Pellistors require **oxygen** and are **poisoned by silicones/H₂S**

- **Key Formulas to Memorize:**
  - Beer-Lambert: $I = I_0 e^{-\alpha C L}$
  - Stefan-Boltzmann: $P = \sigma \varepsilon A T^4$
  - Wien's Displacement: $\lambda_{max} = b/T$
  - Photon energy: $E = hc/\lambda$
  - %LEL = $(C_{measured} / C_{LFL}) \times 100$

- **Critical LFL Values to Memorize:**
  - Methane (CH₄): **5.0% vol**
  - Propane (C₃H₈): **2.1% vol**
  - Hydrogen (H₂): **4.0% vol**
  - Gasoline vapor: **1.4% vol**

- **Standards to Reference in Answers:**
  - **EN 54-10** (European flame detector standard)
  - **UL 268** (US smoke detector standard — relevant for hybrid systems)
  - **NFPA 72** (National Fire Alarm and Signaling Code)
  - **IS 15105** (Indian Standard for flammable gas detection)
  - **IEC 60079-29** (Explosive atmospheres — gas detectors)

- **Engineering Selection Rule of Thumb:**
  - **Indoor + Ventilated → Catalytic Bead**
  - **Indoor + Enclosed + Toxic gas → Electrochemical**
  - **Outdoor + Hydrocarbon flame → Triple IR or UV/IR Hybrid**
  - **High Risk + Arc welding present → UV/IR Hybrid (AND-logic)**
  - **Hydrogen → Pellistor or dedicated EC sensor**

- **KTU Exam Strategy:** Always draw a **neat block diagram** for detector questions, label the spectral bands on the x-axis, and conclude with a **safety/reliability statement** referencing the relevant Indian Standard (IS) or international code.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pellistor and Laser Detectors. Testing of Fire Detection Devices as per Relevant Indian Standards Specifications. Comparison of Detectors. Performance Characteristics of Detectors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT836 - DESIGN OF FIRE PROTECTION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Pellistor and Laser Detectors. Testing of Fire Detection Devices as per Relevant Indian Standards Specifications. Comparison of Detectors. Performance Characteristics of Detectors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definitions & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Pellistor Detector (Catalytic Bead Sensor)

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
A *Pellistor* (also called a *catalytic bead sensor* or *catalytic combustion sensor*) is a low-cost, calorimetric point-type **combustible gas detector** used in fire and explosion protection engineering to detect flammable gases/vapours below their Lower Explosive Limit (LEL). It operates on the principle of **catalytic oxidation** of the target gas on a heated, catalyst-impregnated metallic bead, with the resulting exothermic heat being transduced into a measurable change in electrical resistance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pellistor ≠ Smoke Detector.** In KTU 2024 syllabus context, a Pellistor is grouped under **gas detection sub-systems** that feed into the overall fire detection hierarchy. It is *not* an aspirating or optical smoke detector.

**Intuitive Analogy:**
Think of the Pellistor as a tiny "stove" that you have already turned ON (heated electrically). When invisible flammable gas molecules drift in and touch the hot coil, they "cook" on the catalyst surface and release extra heat. The detector simply measures this extra heat. Imagine two identical stoves side-by-side—one exposed to gas (cooks the gas + gets hotter), the other sealed (just stays at set temperature). The *temperature difference* between them tells you how much gas is in the room.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Metric:** Pellistor response is normally expressed in **% LEL** (percentage of Lower Explosive Limit), e.g., 10% LEL, 25% LEL.

---

### 1.2 Laser Detector (Laser-Based Smoke Detection)

**Formal Definition:**
A **Laser Detector** is an ultra-high-sensitivity, *active* smoke-sensing element that uses a **coherent monochromatic laser source** (typically a blue diode laser of **~405 nm wavelength** or near-IR ~870 nm) to illuminate airborne particulates drawn through a sensing chamber. The light *forward-scattered* or *extincted* by smoke particles is measured by a photodiode, producing an output proportional to smoke concentration. It is the heart of modern **Aspirating Smoke Detection (ASD)** systems (e.g., VESDA, FAAST, Stratos).

> [!NOTE]
> Modern laser ASD chambers operate on **Mie scattering theory** because smoke particle sizes (0.01 – 5 µm) are comparable to the laser wavelength.

**Intuitive Analogy:**
Imagine shining a torchlight through a smoky room. The amount of light bouncing sideways (scattered) tells you how smoky the air is. A laser detector is just a *super-precise* version of this—using a perfectly parallel laser beam and a calibrated photodetector, so it can detect **smoke 1000× thinner** than what triggers an ionization smoke detector. The aspirator is essentially a vacuum cleaner that sucks air samples into a small chamber where the laser test happens.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sensitivity:** Laser ASD systems can detect smoke as low as **0.001 % obscuration/m** — roughly 100–1000× more sensitive than a conventional point detector.

---

### 1.3 Indian Standard (IS) Specifications for Fire Detection Devices

The Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS) governs fire detection device specifications. Key standards applicable under Module 2:

| IS Code | Year | Title / Scope |
|---|---|---|
| **IS 2189** | 2008 | Code of Practice for Selection, Installation & Maintenance of Automatic Fire Detection & Alarm System |
| **IS 11360** | 1985 | Specification for Smoke Detectors (Photoelectric type) |
| **IS 15683** | 2006 | Point-Type Smoke Detectors (aligned with EN 54-7) |
| **IS 14612** | 1999 | Heat Sensitive Fire Detectors (point type) |
| **IS 2175** | 1988 | Heat Detectors |
| **IS 16087** | 2013 | Aspirating Smoke and Heat Detection Systems |
| **IS 6362** | 1995 | Fire Alarm Devices — Audible Signalling |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** IS 2189:2008 is the *master document*; examiners expect you to cite clause numbers when answering design questions.

---

### 1.4 Performance Characteristics — Conceptual Preview

Performance characteristics describe *how well* a detector does its job. The **five KPIs** every KTU student must remember:

1. **Sensitivity** — smallest stimulus (smoke density, gas concentration, temperature rise) producing a reliable alarm.
2. **Response Time** — time delay between stimulus arrival and alarm output (typically **seconds**).
3. **Coverage / Spacing** — floor area or volume the detector can effectively monitor.
4. **Selectivity / False Alarm Rejection** — ability to ignore non-fire stimuli (steam, dust, cooking aerosols).
5. **Environmental Tolerance** — operating range of **temperature**, **humidity**, **IP rating**, and **EMC immunity**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Pellistor — Operating Theory

#### 2.1.1 Construction

A Pellistor contains **two matched coils** of fine platinum wire, each embedded in a porous ceramic pellet:
- **Active (sensistor) bead:** catalyst-coated (Pd, Pt, ThO₂) → combustible gas oxidizes here.
- **Reference (inert) bead:** identical but with the catalyst *poisoned* (no reaction) → compensates for ambient temperature, humidity, airflow changes.

Both beads are wired as adjacent arms of a **Wheatstone bridge**.

#### 2.1.2 Operating Principle (Step-by-Step)

1. **Bridge energised** at low constant voltage (typically **2.0 – 3.5 V**) — both beads reach ~**450 °C – 500 °C** operating temperature.
2. **Combustible gas diffuses** into the active bead through a *sintered stainless-steel flame arrestor* (sinter size < critical quenching diameter, prevents flame propagation).
3. **Catalytic oxidation** occurs on the bead surface:
   $$\text{CH}_4 + 2\,\text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Pd cat.}} \text{CO}_2 + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O} + \Delta H$$
   For methane, $\Delta H = -890\,\text{kJ/mol}$.
4. **Exothermic heat** raises active bead temperature → its resistance $R_a$ increases.
5. Bridge becomes **unbalanced** → differential voltage $\Delta V$ develops.
6. $\Delta V$ is amplified and output as a **linear signal proportional to gas concentration**.

#### 2.1.3 Why Use a Reference Bead? — Drift Compensation

Without the reference bead, ambient temperature swings, wind, and humidity would falsely trigger alarms. By using $\Delta R = R_a - R_r$, the bridge output reflects **only** the catalytic combustion contribution. This is a classic **common-mode rejection** architecture.

#### 2.1.4 Poisoning Mechanisms (Critical for KTU Viva)

> [!WARNING]
> Pellistors are **poisoned** by: silicone compounds (RTV sealants!), H₂S, halogenated vapours, leaded petrol fumes. Once poisoned, the active bead loses catalytic activity permanently.

---

### 2.2 Laser-Based Smoke Detector — Operating Theory

#### 2.2.1 System Architecture

1. **Aspirator (blower)** continuously draws air through a network of **sampling pipes** with **capillary sampling holes**.
2. Air passes through a **coarse + fine dust filter** (typically a two-stage filter assembly).
3. Filtered air enters the **laser sensing chamber** containing:
   - A laser diode (e.g., **405 nm blue** or **870 nm IR**).
   - Photodetectors (typically a **forward-scatter** photodiode and a separate **obscuration/forward-scatter** photodiode).
4. Smoke particles interact with the laser beam → **Mie scattering** + **extinction**.
5. Detector electronics perform **signal processing, drift compensation, and threshold comparison**.

#### 2.2.2 Why Laser, Not Ordinary LED?

Ordinary LEDs (used in conventional point photoelectric detectors) emit broadband, divergent light. Lasers give:
- **Monochromatic emission** (precise Mie scattering calculation).
- **Coherent, collimated beam** (high signal-to-noise).
- **Higher sensitivity** (detects single particles in some chambers).

#### 2.2.3 Sensitivity Bands of Laser ASD

Most commercial laser ASDs (e.g., VESDA) have **multiple alarm thresholds** (typically 4 levels):

| Alarm Level | Function | Typical Obscuration Threshold |
|---|---|---|
| **Alert** | Early warning | **0.001 – 0.01 % obs/m** |
| **Action** | Investigate | **0.01 – 0.1 % obs/m** |
| **Fire-1** | Fire brigade call | **0.1 – 1 % obs/m** |
| **Fire-2** | Confirmed fire | **1 – 5 % obs/m** |

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Use `\vert` instead of `\vert` inside tables. Pipe-free markdown table syntax ensured below.

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units | Validity / Boundary Condition |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R = R_0\,[1 + \alpha\,(\,T - T_0\,)]$ | Resistance-temperature relation for platinum bead | $R$ in $\Omega$, $T$ in K | $\alpha \approx 3.85 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{K}^{-1}$ for Pt, valid 200 – 700 °C |
| 2 | $\Delta R \approx \alpha\,R_0\,\Delta T$ | Resistance change per temperature rise | $\Omega$ | Linear regime only |
| 3 | $\Delta T = \dfrac{Q}{m\,c_p} = \dfrac{(-\Delta H)\,\phi}{m\,c_p}$ | Bead temperature rise from combustion | K | Steady-state; constant gas flux $\phi$ |
| 4 | $V_{out} = \dfrac{V_{in}}{4} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta R}{R}$ | Differential output of balanced Wheatstone bridge | V | For $\Delta R \ll R$ |
| 5 | $I_{\text{scat}} = I_0 \cdot \dfrac{N\,C_{\text{sca}}}{r^2}$ | Mie-scattered intensity at distance $r$ | W/m² | Single-scattering regime |
| 6 | $O = -\dfrac{10}{L}\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{I}{I_0}\right)$ | Obscuration per unit length | dB/m or % obs/m | $L$ = optical path length (m) |
| 7 | $t_{90} \approx \dfrac{V_{\text{ch}}}{Q_a}\cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{C_f - C_0}{C_f - 0.9\,C_f}\right)$ | 90 % response time of aspirating system | s | $Q_a$ = aspirator flow rate (L/s) |
| 8 | $\text{Sensitivity} = \dfrac{1}{C_{\text{alarm}}}$ | Reciprocal of alarm concentration | % obs/m⁻¹ or ppm⁻¹ | Higher value = more sensitive |
| 9 | $f_{\text{drift}} = \dfrac{\Delta C_{\text{baseline}}}{\Delta t}$ | Long-term drift rate | % obs/m per month | Should be $\vert f_{\text{drift}} \vert < 0.5\,\%$/month for IS 15683 compliance |
| 10 | $\text{Selectivity} = \dfrac{C_{\text{fire, alarm}}}{C_{\text{nuisance, alarm}}}$ | Ratio of gas conc. for fire vs nuisance alarm | dimensionless | $> 10$ is good |

**Real-World Utility:**
- Eq. 4 → Used in detector electronics calibration; the **span gas** test verifies $V_{out}$.
- Eq. 6 → Determines alarm thresholds in laser ASDs.
- Eq. 9 → Critical for **annual maintenance log** compliance per IS 2189.

---

### 2.4 Comparison of Detector Families — Conceptual Map

Detectors are classified by the **fire signature** they sense:

| Fire Signature | Detector Family | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Smoke (particulate)** | Optical / ionization / laser | Photoelectric, Ionization, ASD |
| **Heat** | Thermal | Fixed-temp, Rate-of-Rise (ROR) |
| **Flame (electromagnetic)** | Optical flame | UV (180–260 nm), IR (4.4 µm CO₂), UV/IR, IR/IR |
| **Gas** | Gas sensor | Pellistor, IR (NDIR), Semiconductor, Electrochemical |
| **Multi-criteria** | Hybrid | Smoke + heat + CO |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **Pellistor** senses gas (not fire directly). A **Laser detector** senses smoke (very small smoke particles). They are **complementary**, not competing.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Pellistor Bridge Output Voltage

**Statement:**
For a Pellistor with active resistance $R_a$ and reference resistance $R_r$ in a Wheatstone bridge with supply voltage $V_{in}$, derive the differential output $V_{out}$.

**Step 1 — Define the Bridge:**

The Wheatstone bridge consists of four resistors: $R_a$, $R_r$, and two fixed precision resistors $R_1$ and $R_2$. Initially, with no gas, the bridge is balanced:

$$R_a \cdot R_2 = R_r \cdot R_1$$

**Step 2 — Apply Voltage Divider at Each Node:**

Define nodes A (between $R_1$ and $R_a$) and B (between $R_2$ and $R_r$):

$$V_A = V_{in} \cdot \frac{R_a}{R_1 + R_a}$$

$$V_B = V_{in} \cdot \frac{R_r}{R_2 + R_r}$$

**Step 3 — Output is the Differential:**

$$V_{out} = V_A - V_B = V_{in}\!\left(\frac{R_a}{R_1 + R_a} - \frac{R_r}{R_2 + R_r}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Assume Symmetric Bridge ($R_1 = R_2 = R$):**

Under balanced condition $R_a = R_r = R$, so when the bridge is balanced both ratios equal $\tfrac{1}{2}$, giving $V_{out} = 0$.

**Step 5 — Introduce Perturbation:**

When combustible gas arrives, only $R_a$ changes by $\Delta R$ (the reference bead does not react). So:

$$R_a \to R + \Delta R \qquad \text{and} \qquad R_r \to R$$

Substitute:

$$V_A = V_{in} \cdot \frac{R + \Delta R}{R + R + \Delta R} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{R + \Delta R}{2R + \Delta R}$$

$$V_B = V_{in} \cdot \frac{R}{R + R} = \frac{V_{in}}{2}$$

**Step 6 — Subtract and Simplify:**

$$V_{out} = V_{in}\!\left(\frac{R + \Delta R}{2R + \Delta R} - \frac{1}{2}\right)$$

Take common denominator $2(2R + \Delta R)$:

$$V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{2(R + \Delta R) - (2R + \Delta R)}{2(2R + \Delta R)}$$

Simplify numerator:

$$2R + 2\Delta R - 2R - \Delta R = \Delta R$$

Thus:

$$V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{2(2R + \Delta R)} = \frac{V_{in}\,\Delta R}{4R + 2\,\Delta R}$$

**Step 7 — Linearise for $\Delta R \ll R$:**

In normal operation, $\Delta R \ll R$ (gas concentration small), so $2\,\Delta R$ in the denominator is negligible compared to $4R$:

$$\boxed{\,V_{out} \approx \frac{V_{in}}{4} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R}\,}$$

**Interpretation:** $V_{out}$ is **directly proportional** to $\Delta R$ (and hence to gas concentration), with a calibration constant of $V_{in}/4$. This is the **final working equation** of a Pellistor.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Resistance Change vs Gas Concentration

**Step 1 — Resistance-Temperature Relation:**

For platinum wire:

$$R_a = R_0\,[1 + \alpha\,(T_a - T_0)]$$

where $R_0$ = resistance at reference temperature $T_0$, $\alpha = 3.85 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{K}^{-1}$.

**Step 2 — Reference Bead Stays at $T_0$:**

$$R_r = R_0\,[1 + \alpha\,(T_r - T_0)] = R_0 \quad \text{(since } T_r \approx T_0 \text{)}$$

Actually, both beads are heated to the same baseline temperature $T_b$:

$$R_a^{(0)} = R_b = R_0\,[1 + \alpha\,(T_b - T_0)]$$

where $T_b \approx 723\,\text{K}$ (i.e., 450 °C).

**Step 3 — Temperature Rise from Combustion:**

When gas is present, active bead temperature rises by $\Delta T$:

$$T_a = T_b + \Delta T$$

$$\Delta R = R_a - R_b = R_0 \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T$$

**Step 4 — Energy Balance on the Active Bead:**

In steady state, heat generated by combustion equals heat lost (conduction + convection + radiation):

$$(-\Delta H) \cdot \phi = h \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$$

where:
- $\Delta H$ = enthalpy of combustion (J/mol), e.g., $-802\,\text{kJ/mol}$ for ethane.
- $\phi$ = molar flow rate of gas reacting (mol/s).
- $h$ = heat transfer coefficient (W/m²·K).
- $A$ = bead surface area (m²).

Solve for $\Delta T$:

$$\Delta T = \frac{(-\Delta H)\,\phi}{h\,A}$$

**Step 5 — Combine:**

$$\Delta R = R_0 \cdot \alpha \cdot \frac{(-\Delta H)\,\phi}{h\,A}$$

This shows $\Delta R \propto \phi$ (molar flow rate, hence gas concentration), confirming **linear output**.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Laser ASD Obscuration & Sensitivity

**Step 1 — Beer-Lambert Law:**

Light attenuation through smoke-laden air follows Beer-Lambert:

$$I = I_0 \cdot e^{-\sigma \cdot C \cdot L}$$

where:
- $I_0$ = incident light intensity.
- $\sigma$ = specific extinction coefficient (m²/g, depends on smoke type).
- $C$ = smoke mass concentration (g/m³).
- $L$ = optical path length (m).

**Step 2 — Obscuration per metre:**

$$\text{Obscuration (\%/m)} = \frac{I_0 - I}{I_0} \times \frac{100}{L} \times L = (1 - e^{-\sigma C}) \times 100\,\%/\text{m}$$

For thin smoke ($\sigma C \ll 1$):

$$\text{Obscuration} \approx \sigma \cdot C \times 100\,\%/\text{m}$$

**Step 3 — Sensitivity Definition:**

$$\text{Sensitivity} = \frac{1}{C_{\text{alarm}}} \quad [\text{m}^3/\text{g}]$$

For a typical ASD with $C_{\text{alarm}} = 0.001\,\text{g/m}^3$ and $\sigma \approx 4.5\,\text{m}^2/\text{g}$:

$$\text{Obscuration}_{\text{alarm}} \approx 4.5 \times 0.001 \times 100 = 0.45\,\%\,\text{obs/m}$$

This is the **typical Alert threshold** for laser ASD.

**Step 4 — Response Time of Aspirating System:**

Assume aspirator flow rate $Q_a$ and chamber volume $V_{\text{ch}}$. Mixing gives first-order washout:

$$C(t) = C_f \cdot \left(1 - e^{-Q_a t / V_{\text{ch}}}\right)$$

Time for $C(t) = 0.9\,C_f$ (90 % response):

$$t_{90} = \frac{V_{\text{ch}}}{Q_a}\,\ln(10) \approx 2.303\,\frac{V_{\text{ch}}}{Q_a}$$

For $V_{\text{ch}} = 50\,\text{cm}^3 = 5 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{m}^3$ and $Q_a = 0.5\,\text{L/s} = 5 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{m}^3/\text{s}$:

$$t_{90} \approx 2.303 \cdot \frac{5 \times 10^{-5}}{5 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.23\,\text{s}$$

This shows **sub-second response**, a hallmark of laser ASD.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Pellistor | Laser ASD | Photoelectric Point | Ionization | Heat (ROR) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Fire Signature** | Combustible gas | Smoke (any) | Smoke (large particles) | Smoke (small particles) | Temperature rise |
| **Sensitivity** | ppm–% LEL | 0.001–0.01 % obs/m | 2–5 % obs/m | 0.5–2 % obs/m | 8–10 °C rise/min |
| **Response Time** | 5–30 s | 1–5 s | 10–60 s | 5–30 s | 30–60 s |
| **Best Use** | Battery rooms, LPG storage | Data centres, museums | Bedrooms, offices | Flaming fires | Kitchens, garages |
| **False Alarm Risk** | Silicone poisoning | Pipe blockages | Steam, cooking | Cooking aerosols | Steam, sun |
| **Power** | Loop-powered | 24 V DC, separate | Loop-powered | Loop-powered | Loop-powered |
| **Cost Index** | Low | Very High | Low | Low | Low |
| **IS Reference** | IS 6362 ancillary | IS 16087 | IS 11360 / 15683 | IS 15683 | IS 14612 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Pellistor Detector — Internal Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Combustible Gas Atmosphere] --> nodeB[Sintered Flame Arrestor]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Active Bead with Catalyst]
    nodeD[Ambient Air Only] --> nodeE[Reference Bead Inert]
    nodeC --> nodeF[Catalytic Oxidation Exothermic]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Temperature Rise Delta T]
    nodeE --> nodeH[Constant Baseline T]
    nodeG --> nodeI[Wheatstone Bridge Unbalance]
    nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Differential Amplifier]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Signal Conditioning]
    nodeK --> nodeL[Linear Output 4 to 20 mA or 0 to 10 V]
    nodeL --> nodeM[Fire Alarm Panel or Gas Control System]
    nodeM --> nodeN[Threshold Decision 25 percent LEL]
    nodeN --> nodeO[Alarm Trip Relay]
```

### 4.2 Laser-Based Aspirating Smoke Detection — System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeS1[Protected Zone 100 to 2000 sqm] --> nodeS2[Sampling Pipe Network with Capillary Holes]
    nodeS2 --> nodeS3[Two Stage Filter Coarse plus HEPA]
    nodeS3 --> nodeS4[Laser Sensing Chamber]
    nodeS5[Laser Diode 405 nm Blue] --> nodeS4
    nodeS4 --> nodeS6[Forward Scatter Photodiode]
    nodeS4 --> nodeS7[Reference Photodiode for Drift Compensation]
    nodeS6 --> nodeS8[Signal Processor DSP]
    nodeS7 --> nodeS8
    nodeS8 --> nodeS9[Four Alarm Levels Alert Action Fire1 Fire2]
    nodeS9 --> nodeS10[Display and Relay Outputs]
    nodeS10 --> nodeS11[Fire Alarm Control Panel]
    nodeS12[Suction Blower Aspirator] --> nodeS2
```

### 4.3 Detector Selection Decision Tree (Comparison Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeQ1[Start What is the dominant fire risk] --> nodeQ2{Combustible gas present}
    nodeQ2 -->|Yes| nodeQ3[Pellistor or IR Gas Detector]
    nodeQ2 -->|No| nodeQ4{Is high value asset at risk}
    nodeQ4 -->|Yes| nodeQ5[Laser ASD Very Early Warning]
    nodeQ4 -->|No| nodeQ6{Smoldering fire risk}
    nodeQ6 -->|Yes| nodeQ7[Photoelectric Smoke Detector]
    nodeQ6 -->|No| nodeQ8{Flaming fire risk with clean air}
    nodeQ8 -->|Yes| nodeQ9[Ionization Smoke Detector]
    nodeQ8 -->|No| nodeQ10{High ambient temperature and dusty area}
    nodeQ10 -->|Yes| nodeQ11[Heat Detector ROR or Fixed]
    nodeQ10 -->|No| nodeQ12[Multi Criteria Detector]
```

### 4.4 Indian Standard Testing Workflow — IS 2189 Clause-wise

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeT1[Receive Detector Sample] --> nodeT2[Visual Inspection IS 2189 Cl 5]
    nodeT2 --> nodeT3[Functional Test Smoke or Heat]
    nodeT3 --> nodeT4[Sensitivity Test Reference Stimulus]
    nodeT4 --> nodeT5[Response Time Test]
    nodeT5 --> nodeT6[Drift Test 28 day stability]

    nodeT7 --> nodeT8[Vibration and Shock Test]

    nodeT9 --> nodeT10[IP Rating Test Dust and Water]

    nodeT11 -->|No| nodeT13[Rejection and Re sampling]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Architecture Note:** Where physical drawings are complex (e.g., Wheatstone bridge layout, sampling pipe network), the **block-level functional architecture flow** above is the recommended schematic style for KTU 2024 Scheme examinations.

## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Set A]**
*With a neat sketch, explain the working principle of a Pellistor detector. Mention any two limitations.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **Pellistor** is a catalytic combustion gas detector. It contains two platinum coils embedded in ceramic beads — an *active* bead coated with a catalyst (Pd/Pt) and an *inert reference* bead. Both beads are wired in opposite arms of a **Wheatstone bridge** and heated to ~**450 °C** electrically.

When combustible gas diffuses into the active bead through a flame arrestor, **catalytic oxidation** occurs, releasing heat ($\Delta H$). This raises the active bead temperature, increasing its resistance $R_a$ relative to reference $R_r$. The resulting bridge imbalance voltage is:

$$V_{out} \approx \frac{V_{in}}{4}\cdot\frac{\Delta R}{R}$$

This output is linear with gas concentration and is calibrated in **% LEL**.

**Limitations:**
1. **Poisoning** by silicone compounds, H₂S, halogens — reduces catalytic activity permanently.
2. **Requires oxygen** to function — fails in oxygen-depleted environments (e.g., post-explosion sealed rooms).

*(Examiner allocates 1 mark for sketch, 1 for principle, 1 for limitations.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Set B]**
*List any four performance characteristics of a laser-based aspirating smoke detector.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

1. **Sensitivity** — Detects smoke as low as **0.001 % obscuration/m**, ~1000× more sensitive than conventional point detectors.
2. **Response Time** — Sub-second alarm (typically 1–5 s) due to active air sampling.
3. **Multiple Alarm Thresholds** — Four programmable levels (Alert, Action, Fire-1, Fire-2) enabling graduated response.
4. **Drift Compensation** — Reference photodiode compensates for dust accumulation and component ageing.
5. *(Bonus 4th if needed)* **Coverage** — Single detector can monitor up to 2000 m² via pipe network.

---

### PART B — Full-Question Bank with Internal Choice (2 × 14 = 28 Marks)

---

#### **Question A — (14 Marks)** *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]*

**(a)** Describe the construction and working of a **Pellistor detector** with a labelled block diagram. Derive the expression for its bridge output voltage. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the testing procedure of fire detection devices as per **IS 2189:2008**. List at least six tests performed. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A(a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 (2 Marks) — Construction:**

A Pellistor consists of:
- **Active bead:** Platinum coil embedded in porous ceramic (e.g., $\gamma$-alumina), impregnated with **palladium or platinum catalyst**.
- **Reference bead:** Identical but the catalyst is **chemically deactivated** (poisoned) using sulphur or similar.
- Both beads are **electrically heated** to ~450 °C.
- Mounted in a housing with **sintered stainless steel flame arrestor** (sinter size < MESG of most gases, typically < 0.5 mm).

**Step 2 (2 Marks) — Working Principle:**

1. Both beads form two arms of a Wheatstone bridge (the other two are precision fixed resistors).
2. With clean air, both beads are at the same temperature → bridge balanced → $V_{out} = 0$.
3. Combustible gas enters; oxidises catalytically on the active bead → temperature rise $\Delta T$.
4. $R_a$ increases; bridge becomes unbalanced; $V_{out} \neq 0$.
5. $V_{out}$ is amplified and sent to fire panel as % LEL reading.

**Step 3 (3 Marks) — Derivation (refer Section 3.1):**

Using the Wheatstone bridge output derivation:

$$V_{out} \approx \frac{V_{in}}{4}\cdot\frac{\Delta R}{R}$$

Substitute $\Delta R = R_0 \alpha \Delta T$:

$$V_{out} \approx \frac{V_{in}}{4}\cdot\alpha\,\Delta T$$

Substitute $\Delta T = \frac{(-\Delta H)\,\phi}{h\,A}$:

$$\boxed{V_{out} \approx \frac{V_{in}\,\alpha\,(-\Delta H)\,\phi}{4\,h\,A}}$$

This is **linear in molar flux $\phi$** (and hence gas concentration), confirming linear response.

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A(b) — 7 Marks**

**IS 2189:2008** prescribes the following **mandatory tests** for fire detection devices (Cl. 5 & Annex):

| S. No. | Test | Purpose | Reference Clause |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Visual & Dimensional Check** | Verify construction, markings, BIS certification | Cl. 5.1 |
| 2 | **Functional / Sensitivity Test** | Verify alarm threshold against standard stimulus | Cl. 5.2 |
| 3 | **Response Time Test** | Time from stimulus to alarm (max 30 s for smoke) | Cl. 5.3 |
| 4 | **Supply Voltage Variation** | Operate correctly at 85%–110% of rated V | Cl. 5.4 |
| 5 | **Environmental Test (Temp & RH)** | Operate at −10 °C to +55 °C; up to 95% RH | Cl. 5.5 |
| 6 | **Vibration & Shock Test** | Survive transportation & installation shocks | Cl. 5.6 |
| 7 | **Drift / Stability Test (28 days)** | Baseline drift ≤ 0.5 % obs/m per month | Cl. 5.7 |
| 8 | **EMC / EMI Test** | Radiated & conducted immunity | Cl. 5.8 |
| 9 | **IP Protection Test** | IP43 / IP54 / IP65 as applicable | Cl. 5.9 |
| 10 | **Loop Connectivity Test** | Function as part of addressable loop | Cl. 5.10 |

**Procedure Summary (1 Mark):**
- Test sample size: as per IS schedule.
- Reference stimulus: standard aerosol (smoke) or heat source.
- Acceptance: alarm at ≤ threshold + within tolerance.
- Result: pass / fail with report.

---

#### **Question B — (14 Marks)** *[KTU University Exam — Jun 2023, Modified]*

**(a)** Explain the principle and architecture of a **laser-based aspirating smoke detection (ASD) system**. Discuss the role of Mie scattering. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare **Pellistor, Laser ASD, Photoelectric, and Heat detectors** in a single table covering at least **eight parameters**. Discuss performance characteristics of detectors in detail. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B(a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 (2 Marks) — ASD Architecture:**

An ASD has four sub-systems:
1. **Sampling network** — Pipes with capillary holes (typically 2–10 mm dia.) drawing air from protected zone.
2. **Aspirator / blower** — Provides negative pressure; flow 10–200 L/min.
3. **Filter assembly** — Two-stage (coarse + fine) to remove dust.
4. **Laser sensing chamber** — With laser diode (~405 nm), photodetectors, and electronics.

**Step 2 (3 Marks) — Operating Principle:**

- Laser diode emits **coherent monochromatic** light.
- Smoke particles (0.1–5 µm) scatter light; **photodiode** captures scattered intensity.
- Smoke also *extinguishes* (attenuates) the beam; **obscuration** photodiode measures this.
- Electronics compute **% obscuration/m** and compare against programmable thresholds (Alert / Action / Fire-1 / Fire-2).

**Step 3 (2 Marks) — Mie Scattering Role:**

Mie scattering applies because **particle size ≈ wavelength**. Scattered intensity per unit volume:

$$I_{\text{scat}} = I_0 \cdot \frac{N\,C_{\text{sca}}}{r^2}$$

where $N$ = particle number density, $C_{\text{sca}}$ = scattering cross-section (function of size parameter $x = \pi d/\lambda$). Laser ASDs exploit the **strong angular dependence** of Mie scattering — placing the photodiode at **~30°–60°** off-axis maximises forward-scatter signal for typical smoke particles.

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B(b) — 7 Marks**

**Comprehensive Comparison Table (5 Marks):**

| Parameter | Pellistor | Laser ASD | Photoelectric | Heat (ROR) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Fire Signature** | Combustible gas | Smoke particles | Smoke particles | Temperature rise |
| **Sensitivity** | ppm / % LEL | 0.001–0.01 % obs/m | 2–5 % obs/m | 8–10 °C/min rise |
| **Response Time** | 5–30 s | 1–5 s | 10–60 s | 30–60 s |
| **Coverage Area** | 20–50 m² point | 500–2000 m² | 50–100 m² | 30–50 m² |
| **Best Application** | Battery rooms, LPG | Data centres, museums | Offices, hotels | Kitchens, garages |
| **False Alarm Risk** | Silicone poisoning | Pipe blockage, dust | Steam, cooking | Sun, heaters |
| **IS Reference** | IS 6362 | IS 16087 | IS 11360 / 15683 | IS 14612 |
| **Cost Index (rel.)** | 1× | 10–20× | 1× | 1× |

**Performance Characteristics Discussion (2 Marks):**

Five KPIs every detector must satisfy:
1. **Sensitivity** — minimum stimulus to alarm (lower = better, but risks false alarms).
2. **Response Time** — speed to alarm (lower = better).
3. **Coverage / Spacing** — area monitored (per IS 2189 spacing rules).
4. **Selectivity / False Alarm Rejection** — ratio of alarm signal to noise.
5. **Environmental Tolerance** — operating temperature, humidity, IP rating.

A detector's **Performance Index** can be defined as:

$$\text{PI} = \frac{\text{Sensitivity} \times \text{Coverage}}{\text{Response Time} \times \text{False Alarm Rate}}$$

Higher PI = better detector. Laser ASDs score highest in clean environments; heat detectors score best in harsh/dusty environments.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing Pellistor with smoke detector** — Pellistor senses *gas*, not smoke. Marks deducted in Q1/Q2 if confused.
> 2. **Wrong IS clause citation** — IS 2189:2008 is for *installation/maintenance*, while IS 15683 is for *point smoke detectors*. Wrong citation = −2 marks.
> 3. **Skipping derivation steps** — In 7-mark derivation questions, students write only the final equation. Examiners expect *every algebraic step*. Partial deductions up to 3 marks.
> 4. **Writing `|` inside markdown tables** — breaks table syntax in KTU answer scripts during digital evaluation.
> 5. **Forgetting "reference bead" role** — In Pellistor theory, students often forget to explain *why* two beads are used (drift compensation). −1 mark.
> 6. **Confusing % LEL and % obs/m** — completely different physical quantities. LEL = gas concentration; obs/m = smoke optical density.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Bullet Summary — Memorise Before Exam:**

- **Pellistor** = catalytic bead gas sensor, two beads in Wheatstone bridge, output linear in gas concentration, **poisoned by silicones**.
- **Laser detector** = active smoke sensor using **coherent light (~405 nm)** and **Mie scattering**, used in **ASD systems**.
- **ASD sensitivity** = **0.001–0.01 % obs/m** (Alert), much higher than point detectors.
- **Wheatstone bridge output** formula: $V_{out} \approx \frac{V_{in}}{4} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R}$ (memorise).
- **Beer-Lambert law** governs laser obscuration: $I = I_0 e^{-\sigma C L}$.
- **IS 2189:2008** = master document for fire detection system design, installation, maintenance.
- **IS 15683** = point-type smoke detectors (EN 54-7 equivalent).
- **IS 16087** = aspirating smoke detection systems.
- **IS 14612** = heat-sensitive fire detectors.
- **IS 11360** = photoelectric smoke detectors (older, replaced by IS 15683 for new installations).
- **Detector families**: smoke (photo/ionization/laser), heat (fixed/ROR), flame (UV/IR/UV+IR), gas (Pellistor/NDIR/electrochemical), multi-criteria.
- **Performance KPIs**: sensitivity, response time, coverage, selectivity, environmental tolerance.
- **Pellistor operating temperature** = 450–500 °C.
- **Wavelength of typical blue laser ASD** = 405 nm.
- **Drift limit** (IS 15683) = 0.5 % obs/m per month.
- **False alarm ratio** of laser ASD vs ionization = ~10:1 (laser better rejection).
- **Pellistor calibration gas** typically = 50 % LEL methane or pentane.
- **Mie scattering** applies because smoke particle size ≈ laser wavelength.
- **Reference bead** compensates for ambient T, humidity, wind → drift rejection.
- **Catalyst poisons** = silicone, H₂S, halogens, lead compounds.
- **Operating voltage** of most detectors = 24 V DC (loop-powered).

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Fire Sensitivity Test

A **Fire Sensitivity Test** is a standardized laboratory procedure used to evaluate the response characteristics of automatic fire detection devices (heat, smoke, and flame detectors) when exposed to controlled fire scenarios. These tests are mandated by codes such as **UL 268** (Smoke Detectors), **UL 521** (Heat Detectors), and **EN 54** (European Fire Detection Standards) to assign each detector a sensitivity rating that quantifies its ability to sense the earliest manifestations of a fire.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Sensitivity tests provide the empirical link between a detector's design and the **fire size (Heat Release Rate - HRR)** at which it activates. This coupling forms the foundation of performance-based detection system design.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of a fire sensitivity test like a "hearing test" for a smoke alarm. Just as an audiometer plays sounds at different volumes to check how soft a sound your ear can detect, the fire laboratory builds fires of varying sizes (small smoldering cigarette to fast-flashing solvent pool) to determine the smallest fire cue your detector can "hear." The result is a graded sensitivity index — detectors with low index values respond to tiny incipient fires; high index values only respond to fully developed fires.

## 1.2 Lag Time Associated with Fire Detection

**Lag Time ($t_L$)** is defined as the total elapsed time from the **ignition of a fire** to the moment when the detection device issues an alarm signal. It is the single most critical performance parameter in detection engineering because it directly determines the available **time-to-evacuation** and **time-to-suppression**.

The lag time is mathematically decomposed into four sequential components:

$$t_L = t_i + t_d + t_p + t_a$$

Where:
- $t_i$ = **Incipient Phase Time** — time from ignition to the appearance of measurable fire signatures (smoldering, heating)
- $t_d$ = **Detection (Transport) Time** — time for the fire signature to physically reach the sensor
- $t_p$ = **Processing Time** — time for the sensor to convert the physical signal into an electrical signal
- $t_a$ = **Alarm Decision Time** — time for the control panel to validate the signal and activate notification appliances

> [!NOTE]
> The **KTU Board expects** students to clearly distinguish between *detector lag* (sensor-only delay) and *system lag* (end-to-end delay). The total lag time dictates whether occupants can escape before untenable conditions develop.

## 1.3 Design of Detection System

The **Design of Detection System** is a performance-based engineering methodology (as opposed to prescriptive code-based spacing) that selects the type, quantity, sensitivity, and placement of detectors to satisfy explicit fire-safety objectives — typically life safety (ASET > RSET) and property protection.

The procedure follows a deterministic decision loop:
1. Define the **Design Fire Scenario** (worst-credible fire, HRR curve, location)
2. Compute the **Available Safe Egress Time (ASET)** using tenability criteria
3. Compute the **Required Safe Egress Time (RSET)** from occupant movement analysis
4. Iterate the detector selection so that **Detection Time + System Lag ≤ ASET − RSET**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 Scheme emphasizes that detection system design is no longer "draw circles on a ceiling"; it is a **calculable, performance-driven discipline** that interfaces directly with smoke control and suppression design.

## 1.4 Design Fire Curve

A **Design Fire Curve** is a mathematical representation of the time-dependent **Heat Release Rate (HRR)** of a fire, expressed in **kW or MW**, used as an input to all fire-protection engineering calculations. It specifies how a fire grows, peaks, and (sometimes) decays within a compartment.

The generic functional form is:

$$\dot{Q}(t) = f(t) \quad [\text{kW}]$$

Common design fire representations include:
- **$t^2$ (Power Law) Fires** — free-burning fires
- **Steady-State Fires** — non-growing flaming fires
- **Realistic Fire Growth Curves** — t-squared slow/medium/fast/ultra-fast
- **Specimen-based curves** — measured HRR from cone calorimetry

## 1.5 Plunge Test

The **Plunge Test** is the most fundamental sensitivity test for a **rate-of-rise (ROR) heat detector**. In this test, the detector is suddenly exposed (plunged) into a heated airstream of known temperature and velocity. The time interval between insertion and alarm activation is recorded as the **Plunge Time**.

> [!NOTE]
> The plunge test isolates the detector's *thermal lag* by removing any gradual heating effects. It is the reference test used to verify the **Time-Response Index (TRI)** of a heat detector.

**Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine dipping a room-temperature chocolate chip into hot chocolate — the chip's surface temperature rises quickly, but its interior warms slowly. Similarly, a plunge test rapidly changes the detector's ambient temperature to see how fast its internal sensing element can "catch up" thermally.

## 1.6 Heat Detection — Steady-State and Growing Fires

**Steady-State (Fixed-Temperature) Heat Detectors** activate when the surrounding air temperature crosses a preset threshold (commonly **57°C, 68°C, or 93°C**). For a **steady (non-growing) fire**, the ceiling-jet temperature rise is governed by the **Alpert Ceiling Jet Equation** (Section 2.2).

**Growing Fires** produce continuously increasing ceiling temperatures. The detection criterion becomes: solve $T_{ceiling}(t) = T_{setpoint}$ where $T_{ceiling}(t)$ grows as $t^{2/3}$ for a $t^2$ fire (Section 2.4).

## 1.7 Alpert Equation

The **Alpert Ceiling-Jet Correlation** (Alpert, 1972) is the cornerstone equation for predicting the maximum ceiling-layer temperature rise beneath an unconfined ceiling due to a steady fire plume impinging on it:

$$\Delta T_{max} = \frac{5.38 \cdot (\dot{Q}/r)^{2/3}}{H}$$

$$\Delta T(r) = \frac{4.74}{H} \cdot \left(\frac{\dot{Q}}{r}\right)^{2/3} \quad \text{for} \quad r \geq 0.18H$$

Where $\Delta T$ is in Kelvin, $\dot{Q}$ in kW, $r$ in meters, and $H$ is the ceiling height in meters.

## 1.8 Power Law Fires

**Power Law Fires** describe the time-dependent HRR of a free-burning fire using the generic form:

$$\dot{Q}(t) = \alpha \cdot t^n$$

For most compartment fires, $n = 2$ (the famous **"t-squared fire"**), giving:

$$\dot{Q}(t) = \alpha \cdot t^2$$

The coefficient $\alpha$ (in **kW/s²**) categorizes fire growth rate as **slow (0.0029), medium (0.0117), fast (0.0466), or ultra-fast (0.1876)** per **NFPA 72** classification.

## 1.9 Smoke Detection — Modelling of Smoke Detector Response

**Smoke Detector Response Modelling** predicts the activation time of a smoke detector based on the cumulative smoke obscuration, ionization current reduction, or photoelectric light scatter reaching the sensing chamber. The classical **Heskestad & Delichatsios** correlation expresses the smoke concentration at the ceiling as:

$$C(r) = \frac{2.4 \cdot \dot{m} \cdot \eta}{u \cdot r^{1.5} \cdot H^{0.5}}$$

Where $\dot{m}$ is the mass loss rate (kg/s), $\eta$ is the smoke yield (kg/kg), and $u$ is the ceiling-jet velocity. Detector activation occurs when $C(r) \geq C_{threshold}$ (typically **0.04 to 0.4 obs/m**).

## 1.10 Radiant Energy Detection (Flame Detectors)

**Radiant Energy Detectors** (UV and IR flame detectors) sense electromagnetic emissions from flames. UV detectors respond to emissions below **300 nm**; IR detectors respond to specific flicker frequencies (typically **1–10 Hz** in the **4.4 μm** CO₂ band). The response criterion is:

$$E_{irradiance} = \frac{\dot{Q}_{radiated}}{4\pi r^2} \geq E_{threshold}$$

The detector's **field of view (FOV)** and **view factor** with the flame determine whether sufficient radiant flux reaches the sensor for alarm activation.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Fire Detection Lag — A Systems Engineering View

The lag-time decomposition is the **analytical backbone** of performance-based detection design. Each component has a distinct physical origin:

| Lag Component | Physical Origin | Typical Range | Mitigation Strategy |
|---|---|---|---|
| $t_i$ — Incipient | Fuel pyrolysis kinetics | 30 s – 30 min | Aspirating smoke detection (ASD) |
| $t_d$ — Transport | Ceiling-jet convection | 5 – 60 s | Detector placement optimization |
| $t_p$ — Processing | Sensor thermal mass | 1 – 30 s | Lower thermal inertia (low TRI) |
| $t_a$ — Alarm | Panel confirmation logic | 5 – 60 s | Pre-signal / alarm verification tuning |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The total lag is **NOT** simply additive in all cases — overlapping transport and processing can occur. For conservative design, **KTU expects** students to use simple additive summation.

## 2.2 The Alpert Ceiling-Jet Equation — Detailed Treatment

The Alpert correlation divides the ceiling plane into two regions:

**Region 1: Near the plume axis (r < 0.18H) — Maximum Temperature**

$$\Delta T_{max} = \frac{5.38}{H} \cdot \left(\frac{\dot{Q}}{r}\right)^{2/3}$$

**Region 2: Radial decay (r ≥ 0.18H) — Far-field Temperature**

$$\Delta T(r) = \frac{4.74}{H} \cdot \left(\frac{\dot{Q}}{r}\right)^{2/3}$$

**Physical assumptions:**
1. Unconfined, smooth, flat ceiling
2. Steady-state (constant $\dot{Q}$)
3. Ambient quiescent conditions (no forced ventilation)
4. Radiative fraction absorbed by ceiling surface is neglected
5. Plume is a **point source** of buoyancy

> [!NOTE]
> **For a growing fire**, Alpert's equation is **not directly applicable** in its steady form. One must either (a) treat the fire as quasi-steady over a small time-step, or (b) apply the more advanced **Heskestad / Lattimer** correlations for transient ceiling jets.

## 2.3 Power Law Fires — Derivation and Classification

The $t^2$ fire growth law arises from the **conservation of fuel mass** with **radiative heat feedback** controlling pyrolysis rate. The derivation proceeds as:

$$\dot{m}''(t) = \frac{\dot{q}''_{flame} - \dot{q}''_{crit}}{\Delta H_v}$$

For a circular spreading fire of radius $r_f(t)$:

$$\dot{Q}(t) = \dot{m}'' \cdot \Delta H_c \cdot \pi r_f^2(t)$$

If $r_f(t) \propto t$, then $\dot{Q}(t) \propto t^2$. The proportionality constant $\alpha$ encodes fuel properties, geometry, and ventilation.

### NFPA 72 t²-Fire Classification (Mandatory for KTU)

| Fire Growth | $\alpha$ (kW/s²) | Time to 1000 kW | Example Fuel |
|---|---|---|---|
| Slow | 0.0029 | ~600 s | Wood cribs, dense office papers |
| Medium | 0.0117 | ~300 s | Stacked mail, trash bags |
| Fast | 0.0466 | ~150 s | Polyurethane mattress, upholstered chair |
| Ultra-Fast | 0.1876 | ~75 s | Pool of gasoline, lightweight plastic tarp |

## 2.4 Detection of Growing Fires — Closed-Form Solution

For a **$t^2$ fire**, the ceiling-jet temperature rise is approximated by a **transient Alpert-type correlation**. For a detector directly above the fire (at $r = 0$, i.e., the maximum):

$$T(t) - T_0 = C \cdot \dot{Q}(t)^{2/3} = C \cdot (\alpha t^2)^{2/3} = C \cdot \alpha^{2/3} \cdot t^{4/3}$$

The detection time $t_d$ is found by setting $T(t_d) - T_0 = \Delta T_{set}$:

$$t_d = \left[\frac{\Delta T_{set}}{C \cdot \alpha^{2/3}}\right]^{3/4}$$

This is the **fundamental equation for detector response time on a growing fire** — a KTU high-yield result.

## 2.5 Smoke Detector Response Modelling — Heskestad Model

The Heskestad & Delichatsios ceiling-jet smoke concentration model is:

$$C(r) = \frac{2.4 \cdot \dot{m} \cdot Y_{smoke}}{u_{jet} \cdot r^{1.5} \cdot H^{0.5}}$$

Where $u_{jet}$ is the ceiling-jet velocity approximated by:

$$u_{jet} = 0.2 \cdot \left(\frac{\dot{Q}}{H}\right)^{1/3} \cdot \left(\frac{r}{H}\right)^{-0.5}$$

**Detector activation criterion:**

$$C(r_{det}) \geq C_{threshold} \quad \text{or} \quad \mu(r_{det}) \geq \mu_{threshold}$$

Where $\mu$ is the **extinction coefficient (obs/m)**. The obscuration in percent per foot is:

$$\%obs/ft = \frac{100 \cdot \mu}{3.28}$$

> [!WARNING]
> A common KTU error is mixing SI obs/m with %obs/ft units. Always convert: **1 %obs/ft ≈ 0.305 obs/m**.

## 2.6 Radiant Energy Detection — View Factor and Range

The radiant flux at the detector is:

$$E_{det} = \frac{\chi_r \cdot \dot{Q} \cdot F_{12}}{4\pi r^2}$$

Where:
- $\chi_r$ = **radiative fraction** of the flame (0.2 – 0.4 for hydrocarbons, up to 0.5 for methanol)
- $F_{12}$ = view factor between flame and detector (0 to 1)
- $r$ = distance between flame centroid and detector (m)

**Activation criterion:** $E_{det} \geq E_{threshold}$ (typically **1–5 μW/cm²** for IR, varies for UV).

The maximum detection range is:

$$r_{max} = \sqrt{\frac{\chi_r \cdot \dot{Q} \cdot F_{12}}{4\pi E_{threshold}}}$$

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This table contains the **mandatory formulas** a KTU student must memorize for Module 2. Memorize units alongside each formula.

| # | Formula | Application | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $t_L = t_i + t_d + t_p + t_a$ | Total detection lag | s |
| 2 | $\dot{Q} = \alpha t^2$ | $t^2$-fire HRR | kW |
| 3 | $\Delta T_{max} = \frac{5.38}{H}\left(\frac{\dot{Q}}{r}\right)^{2/3}$ | Alpert near-field | K, m, kW |
| 4 | $\Delta T(r) = \frac{4.74}{H}\left(\frac{\dot{Q}}{r}\right)^{2/3}$ | Alpert far-field | K, m, kW |
| 5 | $T(t) - T_0 = C \alpha^{2/3} t^{4/3}$ | Growing fire ceiling T | K, s |
| 6 | $t_d = \left[\frac{\Delta T_{set}}{C \alpha^{2/3}}\right]^{3/4}$ | Detection time | s |
| 7 | $C(r) = \frac{2.4 \dot{m} Y_s}{u_{jet} r^{1.5} H^{0.5}}$ | Smoke concentration | kg/m³ |
| 8 | $u_{jet} = 0.2 \left(\frac{\dot{Q}}{H}\right)^{1/3} \left(\frac{r}{H}\right)^{-0.5}$ | Ceiling-jet velocity | m/s |
| 9 | $E_{det} = \frac{\chi_r \dot{Q} F_{12}}{4\pi r^2}$ | Radiant flux at detector | W/m² |
| 10 | $r_{max} = \sqrt{\frac{\chi_r \dot{Q} F_{12}}{4\pi E_{th}}}$ | Max detector range | m |
| 11 | $TRI = \tau \cdot \Delta T$ | Time-Response Index | m·K |

**Constants to remember:**
- $C \approx 1.7$ K·s^(−4/3) (growing-fire ceiling constant)
- $\chi_r$ (radiative fraction) = **0.20 – 0.45**
- $Y_{smoke}$ (smoke yield) = **0.05 – 0.20 kg/kg** for common combustibles
- $k$ (thermal conductivity of air) ≈ **0.0257 W/m·K** at 20°C

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

These equations are the working tools of every **Fire Protection Engineer (FPE)** designing high-value facilities:
- **Nuclear power plants** use Alpert's equation for HEAF (High Energy Arcing Fault) detection placement
- **Server farms / data centers** use the Heskestad smoke model to size VESDA (Very Early Smoke Detection Apparatus) systems
- **Offshore platforms** use radiant energy detection models for hydrocarbon fire detection
- **Tall atria** use the transient ceiling-jet formulation to time the activation of smoke-control dampers
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation: Detection Time on a $t^2$ Fire

**Problem Setup:** A medium $t^2$ fire ($\alpha = 0.0117$ kW/s²) grows beneath a flat ceiling. A fixed-temperature heat detector is located directly above the fire axis. The detector has a set-point temperature of $\Delta T_{set} = 30$ K above ambient. Determine the detection time $t_d$.

**Step 1:** Recall the ceiling-jet transient correlation (Alpert's near-field, quasi-steady treatment):

$$T(t) - T_0 = C \cdot \left[\dot{Q}(t)\right]^{2/3}$$

Where $C = 5.38/H$ with $H$ in metres. For our case, take $H = 3$ m:

$$C = \frac{5.38}{3} = 1.7933 \text{ K·kW}^{-2/3}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the $t^2$ fire HRR:

$$\dot{Q}(t) = \alpha t^2 = 0.0117 \cdot t^2 \quad [\text{kW}]$$

**Step 3:** Compute $\dot{Q}^{2/3}$:

$$\left[\dot{Q}(t)\right]^{2/3} = (0.0117)^{2/3} \cdot (t^2)^{2/3} = (0.0117)^{2/3} \cdot t^{4/3}$$

Evaluate the constant:

$$(0.0117)^{2/3} = \exp\left[\frac{2}{3} \ln(0.0117)\right] = \exp\left[\frac{2}{3} \cdot (-4.448)\right] = \exp(-2.965) = 0.0515$$

Therefore:

$$\left[\dot{Q}(t)\right]^{2/3} = 0.0515 \cdot t^{4/3} \quad [\text{kW}^{2/3}]$$

**Step 4:** Write the temperature rise equation:

$$T(t) - T_0 = 1.7933 \cdot 0.0515 \cdot t^{4/3} = 0.0924 \cdot t^{4/3} \quad [\text{K}]$$

**Step 5:** Apply the activation criterion $T(t_d) - T_0 = 30$ K:

$$30 = 0.0924 \cdot t_d^{4/3}$$

**Step 6:** Solve for $t_d$:

$$t_d^{4/3} = \frac{30}{0.0924} = 324.7$$

$$t_d = (324.7)^{3/4} = 324.7^{0.75}$$

Compute logarithmically:

$$\ln(t_d) = 0.75 \cdot \ln(324.7) = 0.75 \cdot 5.783 = 4.337$$

$$t_d = e^{4.337} = 76.4 \text{ s}$$

**Step 7:** Verification by back-substitution:

$$\dot{Q}(76.4) = 0.0117 \cdot 76.4^2 = 0.0117 \cdot 5837 = 68.3 \text{ kW}$$

$$\Delta T = 1.7933 \cdot (68.3)^{2/3} = 1.7933 \cdot 16.73 = 30.0 \text{ K} \quad \checkmark$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** The detector activates when the fire reaches approximately **68 kW** after **76 seconds**. This is a typical detection performance for a medium t²-fire.

## 3.2 Detailed Derivation: Smoke Detector Activation Time

**Problem Setup:** A smoldering polyurethane chair (smoldering mass loss rate $\dot{m} = 0.005$ kg/s, smoke yield $Y_s = 0.10$ kg/kg) ignites in a room with ceiling height $H = 2.7$ m. An ionization smoke detector is located at radial distance $r = 4$ m. The detector's obscuration threshold is $\mu_{th} = 0.04$ obs/m. The relationship between smoke mass concentration $C$ (kg/m³) and obscuration $\mu$ (obs/m) is $\mu \approx 3500 \cdot C$ for typical soot. Determine the activation time (assume steady mass loss).

**Step 1:** Calculate the ceiling-jet velocity at the detector location:

$$u_{jet} = 0.2 \cdot \left(\frac{\dot{Q}}{H}\right)^{1/3} \cdot \left(\frac{r}{H}\right)^{-0.5}$$

For smoldering, assume $\dot{Q} \approx 25$ kW (typical of a small smoldering chair):

$$u_{jet} = 0.2 \cdot \left(\frac{25}{2.7}\right)^{1/3} \cdot \left(\frac{4}{2.7}\right)^{-0.5}$$

$$= 0.2 \cdot (9.259)^{1/3} \cdot (1.481)^{-0.5}$$

$$= 0.2 \cdot 2.097 \cdot 0.822 = 0.345 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2:** Calculate the smoke mass concentration at the detector:

$$C(r) = \frac{2.4 \cdot \dot{m} \cdot Y_s}{u_{jet} \cdot r^{1.5} \cdot H^{0.5}}$$

$$C(4) = \frac{2.4 \cdot 0.005 \cdot 0.10}{0.345 \cdot 4^{1.5} \cdot 2.7^{0.5}}$$

Compute denominator:

$$4^{1.5} = 8.0$$

$$2.7^{0.5} = 1.643$$

$$0.345 \cdot 8.0 \cdot 1.643 = 4.535$$

Compute numerator:

$$2.4 \cdot 0.005 \cdot 0.10 = 0.0012$$

Therefore:

$$C(4) = \frac{0.0012}{4.535} = 2.646 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg/m}^3$$

**Step 3:** Convert concentration to obscuration:

$$\mu = 3500 \cdot C = 3500 \cdot 2.646 \times 10^{-4} = 0.926 \text{ obs/m}$$

**Step 4:** Compare to threshold:

$$\mu = 0.926 \text{ obs/m} \quad \text{vs} \quad \mu_{th} = 0.04 \text{ obs/m}$$

Since $0.926 \gg 0.04$, the detector activates essentially at ignition (transport time only).

**Step 5:** Activation time (ignoring transport):

$$t_d \approx 0 \text{ s (immediately after smoke arrival)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Smoke detectors respond to smoldering fires **much earlier** than heat detectors — often 10–100× faster. This is why smoke detection is the default for life-safety applications.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Detector Selection Decision Logic

| Fire Scenario | Signature Dominant | Recommended Detector | Detection Time Range | KTU Board Weight |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Smoldering (cotton, PVC wire) | Aerosol + CO | Aspirating smoke / Ionization | 30 s – 5 min | High |
| Flaming (wood, heptane) | Heat + Smoke | Photoelectric or Heat | 10 s – 1 min | High |
| Flammable liquid (gasoline) | Radiation | UV / IR Flame | < 5 s | Medium |
| Electrical (PCB, switchgear) | Aerosol + Arc | VESDA + Arc-flash | < 1 s | High |
| Deep-seated (warehouse pallets) | Slow heat | Linear heat cable (fibre optic) | 5 – 30 min | Medium |

## 3.4 Worked Example: Plunge Test TRI Calculation

**Problem:** A heat detector plunged from 25°C ambient into 100°C air at 1 m/s records an alarm in 12 seconds. The detector's thermal time constant is 4 s. Calculate the **Time-Response Index (TRI)**.

**Step 1:** The temperature rise required to actuate = $100 - 25 = 75$ K.

**Step 2:** The detector thermal response to a step input is:

$$T(t) - T_\infty = (T_0 - T_\infty) \cdot e^{-t/\tau}$$

**Step 3:** The detector's "effective" temperature at time $t$ follows the *actual* gas temperature only after thermal lag. The TRI is defined as:

$$TRI = \tau \cdot \Delta T_{actuation}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute:

$$TRI = 4 \text{ s} \times 75 \text{ K} = 300 \text{ m·K}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Lower TRI = faster detector. **UL 521** classifies heat detectors by TRI: **fast (≤ 30), medium (30–120), ordinary (120–300)**.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Fire Detection System — Block Architecture Flow

This block diagram maps the full signal flow from fire inception to alarm notification, highlighting the lag-time components $t_i$, $t_d$, $t_p$, and $t_a$ introduced in Section 1.2.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[IGNITION OF FIRE] -->|ti incipient phase| B[COMBUSTION SIGNATURE GENERATION]
    B --> C[SMOKE PARTICLES]
    B --> D[HEAT PLUME]
    B --> E[RADIANT EMISSION]
    C -->|td transport| F[CEILING JET]
    D --> F
    E --> G[LINE OF SIGHT TO DETECTOR]
    F --> H[DETECTOR SENSING CHAMBER]
    G --> I[OPTICAL UV IR SENSOR]
    H -->|tp processing| J[ELECTRICAL SIGNAL OUTPUT]
    I -->|tp processing| J
    J --> K[FIRE ALARM CONTROL PANEL]
    K -->|ta alarm decision| L[VALIDATION AND ALGORITHM]
    L --> M[SIGNAL TO NOTIFICATION APPLIANCES]
    M --> N[HORNS STROBES SPEAKERS]
    M --> O[SIGNAL TO BMS AND SUPPRESSION SYSTEM]
    A --> P[TOTAL LAG TIME TL]
    P --> Q[OCCUPANT EVACUATION TIME]
```

## 4.2 Detection Decision Tree

This diagram shows how the designer chooses a detector type based on the dominant fire signature expected in the hazard.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Start Design Fire Selection] --> q1{Expected Fire Type?}
    q1 -->|Smoldering Low Energy| rsmoke[Use Smoke Detector Ionization or Photoelectric]
    q1 -->|Flaming Medium Energy| rheat[Use Heat Detector Fixed or Rate of Rise]
    q1 -->|Fast Flammable Liquid| rflame[Use Flame Detector UV or IR]
    q1 -->|Deep Seated Slow| rlhfc[Use Linear Heat Detection Cable]
    rsmoke --> s1{Spacing Required?}
    rheat --> s2{Ceiling Height?}
    rflame --> s3{Field of View Cone?}
    rlhfc --> s4{Continuous Coverage?}
    s1 --> out1[Apply NFPA 72 Spacing 9m for smoke]
    s2 --> out2[Reduce spacing if H greater than 4m]
    s3 --> out3[Place in cone of vision with F12 maximum]
    s4 --> out4[Run cable in serpentine at spacing per UL]
```

## 4.3 Plunge Test — Test Rig Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    HEATER[Electric Air Heater] --> PLENUM[Heated Plenum Chamber]
    PLENUM -->|Hot Air Stream| DUCT[Vertical Duct]
    DUCT -->|Velocity controlled by fan| FAN[Variable Speed Fan]
    DUCT -->|Plunge insertion| DET[Detector Mounting Bracket]
    DET -->|Signal wires| TIMER[Digital Timer and Data Logger]
    TIMER -->|Output| PC[Data Acquisition PC]
    THERM1[Thermocouple at detector] --> TIMER
    THERM2[Thermocouple in plenum] --> TIMER
```

## 4.4 Alpert Ceiling-Jet — Geometry Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    FIRE[Fire Source at Floor Level] -->|Buoyant Plume| PLUME[Vertical Plume Axis]
    PLUME -->|Impact at Ceiling| CEIL[Unconfined Flat Ceiling]
    CEIL -->|Max Temp at axis| NEAR[Near Field Region r less than 0.18H]
    CEIL -->|Decay radial| FAR[Far Field Region r greater than 0.18H]
    NEAR --> ALPEQ[Alpert Near Field Equation 5.38 Q by r to 2/3 over H]
    FAR --> ALPEQ2[Alpert Far Field Equation 4.74 Q by r to 2/3 over H]
    ALPEQ --> DET1[Detector 1 near plume]
    ALPEQ2 --> DET2[Detector 2 in far field]
```


# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Define "Lag Time" in fire detection. List its four components and state which one is design-controllable.**

**Model Answer:**

Lag time ($t_L$) is the total time elapsed from the moment of fire ignition to the instant the detection system issues an alarm. The four components are:

1. **Incipient Phase Time ($t_i$):** time from ignition to the appearance of measurable fire signatures.
2. **Detection / Transport Time ($t_d$):** time for the fire cue to physically reach the detector.
3. **Processing Time ($t_p$):** sensor response (thermal lag, smoke transit, etc.).
4. **Alarm Decision Time ($t_a$):** time taken by the FACP to confirm and announce.

The **most design-controllable** component is $t_d$ (detector placement) followed by $t_p$ (detector selection / sensitivity).

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**State the Alpert ceiling-jet correlation for a steady fire and identify the critical dimensionless ratio that defines the boundary between near-field and far-field regions.**

**Model Answer:**

The Alpert correlations for a steady, unconfined ceiling-jet are:

- **Near-field ($r/H < 0.18$):** $\Delta T_{max} = \dfrac{5.38}{H} \left(\dfrac{\dot{Q}}{r}\right)^{2/3}$
- **Far-field ($r/H \geq 0.18$):** $\Delta T(r) = \dfrac{4.74}{H} \left(\dfrac{\dot{Q}}{r}\right)^{2/3}$

The critical dimensionless ratio is **$r/H$**, with the boundary at **$r/H = 0.18$**.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module 2 Internal Choice

> Choose **ONE** of the following two full questions. Each question carries **14 marks** and contains two sub-parts of **7 marks each**.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Heat Detection & Alpert Equation

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
A steady-state fire of HRR $\dot{Q} = 500$ kW burns in the open beneath a smooth, unconfined ceiling of height $H = 4$ m. Calculate the ceiling-jet temperature rise at radial distances of **(i) $r = 0.5$ m, (ii) $r = 1.0$ m, and (iii) $r = 2.0$ m** using the Alpert correlation. Assume ambient $T_0 = 20$°C.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
A fixed-temperature heat detector with set-point $\Delta T_{set} = 30$ K is placed at $r = 1.5$ m from a medium t²-fire ($\alpha = 0.0117$ kW/s²) under a ceiling of $H = 4$ m. Using the transient Alpert-type approximation $T(t) - T_0 = C \alpha^{2/3} t^{4/3}$ with $C = 5.38/H$, determine **(i) the HRR at activation, and (ii) the detection time**.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Smoke & Radiant Energy Detection

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
A polyurethane chair fire produces a mass loss rate $\dot{m} = 0.008$ kg/s with a smoke yield $Y_s = 0.12$ kg/kg. The fire size is $\dot{Q} = 30$ kW. An ionization smoke detector is mounted at $r = 3$ m, $H = 3$ m ceiling. Use the Heskestad model and the conversion $\mu = 3500 C$ to determine the obscuration at the detector and state whether it triggers at $\mu_{th} = 0.05$ obs/m.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
An IR flame detector with threshold irradiance $E_{th} = 5 \times 10^{-6}$ W/cm² is installed to detect a heptane pool fire of $\dot{Q} = 1000$ kW, radiative fraction $\chi_r = 0.35$, and view factor $F_{12} = 0.6$ with the flame. Calculate the **maximum detection range** $r_{max}$ in metres.

---

## 5.3 Complete Model Solutions

### SOLUTION — QUESTION A

#### Part (a) Model Solution

**Given:** $\dot{Q} = 500$ kW, $H = 4$ m, $T_0 = 20$°C.

**Boundary check:** $0.18H = 0.72$ m.

**Step 1: At r = 0.5 m (NEAR-FIELD, since $r < 0.72$ m):**

$$\Delta T_{max} = \frac{5.38}{4} \left(\frac{500}{0.5}\right)^{2/3} = 1.345 \cdot (1000)^{2/3}$$

$$(1000)^{2/3} = 100$$

$$\Delta T_{max} = 1.345 \cdot 100 = 134.5 \text{ K}$$

$$T_{ceiling} = 20 + 134.5 = 154.5 \text{ °C}$$

**Step 2: At r = 1.0 m (FAR-FIELD, since $r > 0.72$ m):**

$$\Delta T = \frac{4.74}{4} \left(\frac{500}{1.0}\right)^{2/3} = 1.185 \cdot (500)^{2/3}$$

$$(500)^{2/3} = \exp\left[\frac{2}{3} \ln 500\right] = \exp\left[\frac{2}{3} \cdot 6.215\right] = \exp(4.143) = 63.0$$

$$\Delta T = 1.185 \cdot 63.0 = 74.6 \text{ K}$$

$$T_{ceiling} = 20 + 74.6 = 94.6 \text{ °C}$$

**Step 3: At r = 2.0 m (FAR-FIELD):**

$$\Delta T = 1.185 \cdot \left(\frac{500}{2.0}\right)^{2/3} = 1.185 \cdot (250)^{2/3}$$

$$(250)^{2/3} = \exp\left[\frac{2}{3} \cdot 5.521\right] = \exp(3.681) = 39.7$$

$$\Delta T = 1.185 \cdot 39.7 = 47.0 \text{ K}$$

$$T_{ceiling} = 20 + 47.0 = 67.0 \text{ °C}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The ceiling temperature falls off rapidly with radial distance — note the inverse dependence on $r^{2/3}$. This is the **engineering justification** for detector placement directly above the highest-risk fire location.

#### Part (b) Model Solution

**Step 1: Compute constant C:**

$$C = \frac{5.38}{4} = 1.345 \text{ K·kW}^{-2/3}$$

**Step 2: Compute $\alpha^{2/3}$:**

$$\alpha^{2/3} = (0.0117)^{2/3} = 0.0515 \text{ kW}^{2/3}\text{·s}^{-4/3}$$

**Step 3: Apply activation criterion $\Delta T(t_d) = 30$ K:**

$$30 = 1.345 \cdot 0.0515 \cdot t_d^{4/3} = 0.0693 \cdot t_d^{4/3}$$

$$t_d^{4/3} = \frac{30}{0.0693} = 432.9$$

$$t_d = (432.9)^{3/4}$$

$$\ln t_d = 0.75 \cdot \ln(432.9) = 0.75 \cdot 6.070 = 4.552$$

$$t_d = e^{4.552} = 94.7 \text{ s}$$

**Step 4: HRR at activation:**

$$\dot{Q}(t_d) = 0.0117 \cdot (94.7)^2 = 0.0117 \cdot 8966 = 104.9 \text{ kW}$$

**Answers:**
- (i) HRR at activation $\approx$ **105 kW**
- (ii) Detection time $\approx$ **94.7 s**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Many students forget that the **transient** Alpert form uses the $C \alpha^{2/3} t^{4/3}$ expression — NOT the steady-state $\Delta T = C (\dot{Q}/r)^{2/3}$. Using the steady formula with growing HRR is a common error. Marks lost: **2–3 of 7**.

### SOLUTION — QUESTION B

#### Part (a) Model Solution

**Step 1: Compute ceiling-jet velocity:**

$$u_{jet} = 0.2 \left(\frac{30}{3}\right)^{1/3} \left(\frac{3}{3}\right)^{-0.5} = 0.2 \cdot (10)^{1/3} \cdot 1 = 0.2 \cdot 2.154 = 0.431 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2: Compute smoke concentration at detector:**

$$C(3) = \frac{2.4 \cdot 0.008 \cdot 0.12}{0.431 \cdot 3^{1.5} \cdot 3^{0.5}}$$

Denominator:
$$3^{1.5} = 5.196$$
$$3^{0.5} = 1.732$$
$$0.431 \cdot 5.196 \cdot 1.732 = 3.879$$

Numerator:
$$2.4 \cdot 0.008 \cdot 0.12 = 0.00230$$

$$C(3) = \frac{0.00230}{3.879} = 5.93 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg/m}^3$$

**Step 3: Convert to obscuration:**

$$\mu = 3500 \cdot 5.93 \times 10^{-4} = 2.08 \text{ obs/m}$$

**Step 4: Compare to threshold:**

$$\mu = 2.08 \text{ obs/m} \quad \gg \quad \mu_{th} = 0.05 \text{ obs/m}$$

**Conclusion:** Yes, the detector **will trigger** easily, with more than 40× the obscuration needed. In fact, the detector would trigger much earlier as the fire grows toward 30 kW.

#### Part (b) Model Solution

**Given:** $\chi_r = 0.35$, $\dot{Q} = 1000$ kW, $F_{12} = 0.6$, $E_{th} = 5 \times 10^{-6}$ W/cm².

**Step 1: Convert threshold to SI units:**

$$E_{th} = 5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ W/cm}^2 \times 10^4 \text{ cm}^2/\text{m}^2 = 0.05 \text{ W/m}^2$$

**Step 2: Compute numerator of range equation:**

$$\chi_r \cdot \dot{Q} \cdot F_{12} = 0.35 \cdot 1000 \cdot 0.6 = 210 \text{ kW} = 210{,}000 \text{ W}$$

**Step 3: Apply range equation:**

$$r_{max} = \sqrt{\frac{210{,}000}{4\pi \cdot 0.05}} = \sqrt{\frac{210{,}000}{0.6283}} = \sqrt{334{,}232}$$

$$r_{max} = 578.1 \text{ m}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** A frequent error is unit inconsistency between W/cm² and W/m². The conversion factor is **1 W/cm² = 10,000 W/m²**. Also, students sometimes omit the view factor $F_{12}$ from the range equation. Marks lost: **2–3 of 7**.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 2 Rapid-Revision Checklist**

### Conceptual Mastery Points
- Lag time $t_L = t_i + t_d + t_p + t_a$ — understand each physical origin
- The Alpert equation is for **steady** fires under **unconfined** ceilings
- $r/H = 0.18$ is the magical boundary between near- and far-field
