- **Rural and semi-urban industrial godowns** where pressurized hydrant infrastructure is absent or unreliable.
- **Construction sites** where temporary fire protection is required prior to permanent system commissioning.
- **Heritage buildings** where retrofitting of pressurized water systems could damage the historic fabric.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Numerical Problem 1 — Sizing of Pail & Barrel Requirement for a Godown

**Problem Statement (Model Question Pattern):**
> A storage godown has plan dimensions **40 m × 20 m** with a clear height of 6 m. Determine the minimum number of fire pails, the number of water barrels, and the total stored water reserve, assuming the standard NBC deployment rule.

#### Step 1 — Compute the Floor Area

The floor area of the godown is calculated as:

$$
A_f = L \times B
$$

$$
A_f = 40\,\text{m} \times 20\,\text{m} = 800\,\text{m}^2
$$

Here, $L$ is the length and $B$ is the breadth of the godown. The product directly yields the enclosed floor area requiring fire protection coverage.

#### Step 2 — Apply the Pail Density Rule

The NBC specifies that fire pails must be distributed such that one pail serves approximately every **10 m²** of floor area in high-hazard storage:

$$
n_p = \left\lceil \dfrac{A_f}{10\,\text{m}^2} \right\rceil
$$

$$
n_p = \left\lceil \dfrac{800}{10} \right\rceil = \lceil 80 \rceil = 80 \text{ pails}
$$

Since 80 is already an integer, no rounding is needed. The ceiling function is applied because fractional pails cannot be provided — we round up to ensure full coverage.

#### Step 3 — Compute Total Water Stored in Pails

The total water reserve locked up in the pails alone is:

$$
V_p^{tot} = n_p \times V_p
$$

$$
V_p^{tot} = 80 \times 10\,\text{L} = 800\,\text{L}
$$

This represents the *immediately deployable* water, since each pail is portable and ready for instant manual discharge.

#### Step 4 — Determine the Number of Water Barrels (Supply Reservoir)

A standard engineering practice is to provide **one 200-litre water barrel for every 10 fire pails**, ensuring a replenishment reservoir at each pail cluster:

$$
n_b = \left\lceil \dfrac{n_p}{10} \right\rceil
$$

$$
n_b = \left\lceil \dfrac{80}{10} \right\rceil = 8 \text{ barrels}
$$

#### Step 5 — Compute Total Water Reserve in Barrels

$$
V_b^{tot} = n_b \times V_b
$$

$$
V_b^{tot} = 8 \times 200\,\text{L} = 1600\,\text{L}
$$

#### Step 6 — Total Static Water Reserve at the Site

The complete fire-water inventory of the godown is the sum of the pail water and the barrel water:

$$
V_{tot} = V_p^{tot} + V_b^{tot}
$$

$$
V_{tot} = 800\,\text{L} + 1600\,\text{L} = 2400\,\text{L} = 2.4\,\text{m}^3
$$

#### Final Design Summary

| Item | Quantity | Water Volume |
|------|----------|--------------|
| Fire pails (10 L each) | 80 | 800 L |
| Water barrels (200 L each) | 8 | 1600 L |
| **Total Static Reserve** | — | **2400 L (2.4 m³)** |

---

### 3.2 Numerical Problem 2 — Maximum Travel-Distance Verification

**Problem Statement:**
> A workshop hall measures 30 m × 24 m. Six fire pails are to be placed symmetrically. Verify whether the placement satisfies the **15 m maximum travel distance** criterion of the NBC.

#### Step 1 — Geometric Layout

With six pails placed symmetrically in a 3 × 2 grid:

$$
\text{Spacing along length} = \dfrac{30\,\text{m}}{3} = 10\,\text{m}
$$

$$
\text{Spacing along breadth} = \dfrac{24\,\text{m}}{2} = 12\,\text{m}
$$

#### Step 2 — Worst-Case Travel Distance

The maximum travel distance from any point in the hall to the nearest pail is the **half-diagonal of the smallest rectangular cell** the pail serves:

$$
d_{max} = \dfrac{1}{2} \sqrt{(10)^2 + (12)^2}
$$

$$
d_{max} = \dfrac{1}{2} \sqrt{100 + 144} = \dfrac{1}{2} \sqrt{244}
$$

$$
d_{max} = \dfrac{1}{2} \times 15.62 = 7.81\,\text{m}
$$

#### Step 3 — Compliance Check

$$
d_{max} = 7.81\,\text{m} \;<\; 15\,\text{m} \quad \checkmark \text{ (Compliant)}
$$

The proposed 6-pail symmetric arrangement is **well within** the regulatory travel-distance limit. In fact, the layout could even be relaxed to a 2 × 2 grid (4 pails) and still comply.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Advantages vs. Limitations

| Aspect | Fire Pails | Water Barrels |
|--------|------------|---------------|
| **Capital cost** | Very low (~₹250–400 per unit) | Low (~₹2500–4000 per unit) |
| **Skill required** | Zero training | Zero training |
| **Dependence on power** | **None** | **None** |
| **Dependence on piping** | **None** | **None** |
| **Suitability for Class C (Electrical)** | **Not suitable** (water conducts) | **Not suitable** (use sand barrels) |
| **Suitability for Class K (Cooking oil)** | **Not suitable** (water flashes oil) | **Not suitable** |
| **Suitability for Class A (Wood/Paper)** | **Excellent** | **Excellent** |
| **Discharge time** | 6–8 s (one pail) | Indefinite (reservoir) |
| **Refill logistics** | Easy from any tap | Easy from any tap |
| **Public health issue** | None | **Mosquito breeding** risk if uncovered |
| **Aesthetic acceptability** | Poor in modern buildings | Poor in modern buildings |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Fire Pail & Barrel Deployment System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Hazard Zone Identification] --> B[Risk Classification Class A B C D K]
    B --> C{Selection of Extinguishing Media}
    C -->|Class A and Class B| D1[Water Filled Fire Pails 10L Each]
    C -->|Class C and Class D| D2[Sand Filled Pails]
    C -->|Class K| D3[Wet Chemical or Blanket Pails]
    D1 --> E[Pail Stations along 15m Travel Grid]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> F[Water Barrel Reservoir 200L per 10 Pails]
    F --> G[Weekly Refill and Inspection Log]
    G --> H[Incident Detection]
    H --> I[Manual Scoop and Throw Extinction]
    I --> J[Fire Suppressed within Incipient Stage]
    J --> K[Fire Brigade Arrival and Handover]
    K --> L[Post Incident Replenishment Cycle]
```

### 4.2 Cross-Sectional Anatomy of a Standard Fire Pail

```mermaid

    R1[Rim - Rolled Top Edge - 5mm dia wire] --> R2[Body - 22 SWG GI Sheet]
    R2 --> R3[Handle - GI Wire 6mm dia]
    R2 --> R4[Bottom - Pressed concave 22 SWG]
    R4 --> R5[Capacity Marking - White Stencil 10L]
    R2 --> R6[Red Enamel Paint - IS Shade 537]
    R2 --> R7[Optional Galvanized Lining - Zinc Coating]
```

### 4.3 Deployment Topology — Pails + Barrels Coupled System

```mermaid

    WB1[Water Barrel 200L - Plinth Mounted] --> P1[Pail Station A - 4 Pails]
    WB1 --> P2[Pail Station B - 3 Pails]
    WB1 --> P3[Pail Station C - 3 Pails]
    P1 --> S1[Travel Distance lt 15m Verified]
    P2 --> S1
    P3 --> S1
    S1 --> R[Compliance with IS 2546 and NBC 2016]
    WB2[Water Barrel 200L - Reserve 2] --> P4[Pail Station D]
    WB2 --> P5[Pail Station E]
    WB2 --> P6[Pail Station F]
    P4 --> S1
    P5 --> S1
    P6 --> S1
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Block — Inspection & Maintenance Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M1[Daily Visual Check - Pail in Position and Red Paint Intact] --> M2[Weekly Water Level Check in Barrel]
    M2 --> M3[Monthly Rust Inspection of GI Body]
    M3 --> M4[Quarterly Repainting if Faded]
    M4 --> M5[Annual Hydrostatic Check for Leakage]
    M5 --> M6[5 to 7 Year Replacement Cycle]
    M6 --> M7[Document in Fire Register under Factory Act 1948]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question Pattern]**
> Differentiate between a **fire pail** and a **water barrel** with respect to capacity, mobility, and intended function in a fire protection layout.

**Model Answer:**
A **fire pail** is a small, portable, open-top container of **10-litre** capacity, intended for the manual throw-application of water on an incipient fire. A **water barrel** is a large, stationary, open-top storage vessel of **200-litre** capacity that serves as a replenishment reservoir from which the pails are re-filled. In essence, the barrel is the **supply tank** and the pail is the **delivery tool** — both are deployed as a coupled unit to provide the most elementary, zero-technology, fail-safe fire response capability required under **IS 2546** and **NBC 2016 Part 4**.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question Pattern]**
> State the **maximum horizontal travel distance** to the nearest fire pail as per the National Building Code of India. What is the rationale behind this 15-metre value?

**Model Answer:**
The maximum horizontal travel distance from any point on a floor to the nearest fire pail is **15 metres** as per **NBC 2016, Part 4, Clause 5.1.3**. This value is derived from the practical consideration that, in a Class A fire involving ordinary combustibles (wood, paper, textiles), the fire doubles in size approximately every 30 to 60 seconds during its growth phase. A responder must therefore be able to reach a pail, scoop water, and apply it within roughly 30 seconds of detection — corresponding to a maximum running distance of about 15 m, since an average adult covers 15 m in approximately 8 to 10 seconds.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]*

> **(a)** Describe in detail the **constructional features**, **material specifications**, and **colour-coding requirements** of a standard galvanized-iron fire pail as per **IS 2546:1994**. *(7 Marks)*
>
> **(b)** A petroleum retail outlet has **4 pump islands**. As per **Petroleum Rules 2002**, determine the minimum number of fire pails and water barrels required, and sketch their typical layout around a single pump island. *(7 Marks)*

##### Model Solution

**(a) Constructional Features of a Standard Fire Pail (7 Marks)**

1. **Body Material:** Manufactured from **18 to 22 SWG mild steel sheet**, hot-dip galvanized both internally and externally as per **IS 2629 and IS 2633** to prevent corrosion.
2. **Shape:** Tapered cylindrical (frustum) shape with a wider top and narrower bottom, providing a stable base and easy nesting during storage.
3. **Top Rim:** A continuous rolled or swaged top edge of **5 mm GI wire** is provided to (i) increase rigidity of the open mouth, (ii) protect the operator's hand while carrying, and (iii) prevent deformation under impact.
4. **Handle:** A single **6 mm diameter galvanized iron wire handle**, drop-forged and welded to the body at two diametrically opposite points. The handle must withstand a static load of **150 kg** without permanent deformation (per IS test load).
5. **Bottom:** Pressed into a shallow concave shape (depth 15–20 mm) to provide a stable, non-rocking footprint.
6. **Colour Coding:** Exterior is finished with **two coats of Signal Red enamel (IS 5:2007, Shade 537)** over a zinc-chromate primer. A **white horizontal band of 25 mm width**, inscribed with the legend **"FIRE BUCKET — WATER"** in 25 mm stencil letters, is painted 75 mm below the top rim.
7. **Capacity Marking:** The pail must be permanently embossed or stencilled with the rated capacity **"10 L"** on the lower body.
8. **Testing:** Each pail is hydrostatically tested for **5 minutes at 5 kPa** with no leakage or permanent deformation.

**(b) Sizing for a 4-Pump Petroleum Outlet (7 Marks)**

As per **Rule 117 of the Petroleum Rules 2002**, each pump island must be equipped with:

- **Two sand-filled pails (10 L each)** — for Class B liquid fires and electrical fires (sand smothers the fire).
- **Two water-filled pails (10 L each)** — for Class A fires in adjacent structures.
- **One 200-litre water barrel** — replenishment reservoir.

Therefore, for **one pump island**:

$$
\text{Fire Pails} = 2 (\text{sand}) + 2 (\text{water}) = 4 \text{ pails}
$$

$$
\text{Water Barrels} = 1 \times 200\,\text{L}
$$

For **4 pump islands**, scaling up linearly:

$$
n_p^{total} = 4 \times 4 = 16 \text{ pails}
$$

$$
n_b^{total} = 4 \times 1 = 4 \text{ barrels}
$$

Total static water reserve:

$$
V_{tot} = 16 \times 10 + 4 \times 200 = 160 + 800 = 960\,\text{L}
$$

The **layout sketch** of a single pump island would show the dispenser unit at the centre, with the 4 pails arranged on a **dedicated sand-filled concrete plinth** of dimensions 1.2 m × 1.2 m at the front corners, and the 200 L water barrel on a separate masonry plinth at the rear of the island, all within a **7 m radius** of the dispenser.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** — *[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Pattern]*

> **(a)** Explain the **engineering logic behind selecting 10 litres as the standard capacity** of a fire pail, justifying your answer using human-factors, fire-physics, and logistics considerations. *(7 Marks)*
>
> **(b)** A chemical godown measuring **50 m × 30 m** stores Class B petroleum products. Determine the minimum number of fire pails (sand-filled) and water barrels required, and verify compliance with the **15 m travel-distance criterion**. *(7 Marks)*

##### Model Solution

**(a) Engineering Logic Behind 10-Litre Standard Capacity (7 Marks)**

The 10-litre capacity is the result of a multi-constraint optimization involving:

**1. Human-Factors Constraint:** A fully filled 10-litre pail weighs:

$$
W_p = V_p \cdot \rho_w + W_{empty} = 10 \times 1 + 0.5 = 10.5\,\text{kg}
$$

This weight can be lifted, carried, and **accurately thrown over 3 to 4 metres** by an average adult operator (anthropometric 5th–95th percentile) without undue fatigue. Lighter capacities (< 8 L) provide inadequate extinguishing volume; heavier capacities (> 12 L) cause operator fatigue and inaccurate targeting.

**2. Fire-Physics Constraint:** The incipient-stage growth rate of a Class A fire follows the well-known **$t^2$ fire-growth relation**:

$$
\dot{Q} = \alpha \cdot t^2
$$

For a medium-growth fire ($\alpha = 0.01172$ kW/s²), the fire reaches **1 MW** in approximately **290 seconds** (about 5 minutes). A 10-litre pail, discharged at $Q_d \approx 1.2$ L/s, delivers:

$$
t_d = \dfrac{V_p}{Q_d} = \dfrac{10}{1.2} \approx 8.3\,\text{s}
$$

This is sufficient to extinguish a 1 m² flame front in its early seconds — a meaningful intervention before the fire transitions to the growth stage.

**3. Logistics Constraint:** The pail must be refillable from any standard **15 L/min tap**:

$$
t_r = \dfrac{V_p}{15} = \dfrac{10}{15} \times 60 = 40\,\text{s}
$$

A refill time of 40 seconds enables rapid turnover during a sustained incident. Additionally, a 10 L volume is small enough that **ten pails can be refilled from a single 200 L barrel in just over 6 minutes**.

**(b) Sizing for the 50 m × 30 m Chemical Godown (7 Marks)**

Floor area:

$$
A_f = 50 \times 30 = 1500\,\text{m}^2
$$

For Class B (petroleum) hazards, the regulatory density is one sand-filled pail per **20 m²** (looser spacing than Class A since sand pails are used for local spill control, not blanket coverage):

$$
n_p = \left\lceil \dfrac{1500}{20} \right\rceil = 75 \text{ pails}
$$

Water barrels at one per 15 pails (slightly higher reservoir ratio since the fire may spread rapidly):

$$
n_b = \left\lceil \dfrac{75}{15} \right\rceil = 5 \text{ barrels}
$$

Static water reserve:

$$
V_{tot} = 75 \times 10 + 5 \times 200 = 750 + 1000 = 1750\,\text{L}
$$

**Travel-Distance Verification:**

A symmetric 5 × 3 grid of pails gives cell dimensions:

$$
\Delta x = \dfrac{50}{5} = 10\,\text{m}, \quad \Delta y = \dfrac{30}{3} = 10\,\text{m}
$$

Worst-case diagonal half-length:

$$
d_{max} = \dfrac{1}{2}\sqrt{10^2 + 10^2} = \dfrac{1}{2}\sqrt{200} = 7.07\,\text{m} \;<\; 15\,\text{m} \quad \checkmark
$$

**The layout is fully compliant.**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing pails with extinguishers:** Many students mistakenly cite CO₂ or DCP specifications when answering fire-pail questions. Pails are **manual, water/sand-based, and require no pressurized agent.**
> 2. **Forgetting the colour code:** Examiners specifically award marks for mentioning **"Signal Red (IS Shade 537)"** — students who simply say "red colour" lose 1 mark.
> 3. **Skipping the travel-distance verification:** A pail-sizing calculation without the **15 m travel-distance check** is treated as incomplete in KTU valuation.
> 4. **Mixing up IS codes:** IS 2546 covers fire buckets; IS 912 covers water barrels; conflating them is a common error.
> 5. **Not mentioning the Petroleum Rules:** For petrol-pump questions, omitting **Rule 117 of the Petroleum Rules 2002** is penalized.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

- A **fire pail** holds **10 L**; a **water barrel** holds **200 L**. Memorize these two values — they appear in nearly every KTU question.
- **Maximum travel distance = 15 m** (NBC 2016, Part 4). Always verify this in numerical problems.
- **Pail colour:** Signal Red exterior with a **white horizontal band** and "FIRE BUCKET — WATER" stencil.
- **Material:** Hot-dip galvanized mild steel, 18–22 SWG.
- **Pails + Barrels = Coupled System.** The barrel supplies the pail; the pail delivers the water.
- **Sand-filled pails** (not water) are used for **Class B (petroleum)** and **Class C (electrical)** fires.
- **Inspection regime:** Pail availability — **daily**; water barrel level — **weekly**; repainting — **quarterly**.
- **Petroleum Rules 2002, Rule 117:** 4 pails + 1 barrel per pump island.
- **Service life:** 5 to 7 years for a galvanized pail.
- **Applicable standards:** **IS 2546** (pails), **IS 912** (barrels), **NBC 2016 Part 4**, **Factories Act 1948**, **Petroleum Rules 2002**.
- **Limitations:** Unsuitable for Class C (live electrical) and Class K (cooking oil) fires; mosquito-breeding risk in uncovered barrels.
- **Placement:** On a non-combustible plinth; chained or recessed to prevent theft; clear signage; never obstructed.
- **Engineering rationale for 10 L:** Human factor (10.5 kg liftable weight) + fire physics (≈ 8 s discharge sufficient for 1 m²) + logistics (40 s refill from 15 L/min tap).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hose Reels. Description
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT836 - DESIGN OF FIRE PROTECTION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Hose Reels. Description

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Hose Reel** is a first-aid fire fighting installation consisting of a permanently connected water supply, a revolving drum carrying a length of **semi-rigid, rubber-lined, non-percolating hose** (typically **19 mm or 25 mm** internal diameter), and a **shut-off type nozzle**, all mounted on a wall bracket. It is designed for use by **trained or even untrained occupants** to tackle incipient stage (Class A) fires in buildings, without waiting for the fire brigade to arrive.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):**
> Hose Reels are classified as a **First-Aid Fire Fighting (FAFF) appliance**. They form the *first line of defence* inside a building, complementing (but never replacing) the larger fire hydrant and sprinkler systems. They are governed in India primarily by **IS 884: Part 1 & Part 2** and **TAC (Tariff Advisory Committee) regulations**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are in a large office building and someone drops a lit match into a waste-paper basket. The waste begins to smoulder. You have **two extreme choices**:

1. Wait for the fire brigade truck — but by then the basket is fully ablaze.
2. Use a fire extinguisher — but it runs out in 8 seconds.

A **hose reel** is the *middle ground* — it is essentially a **"fire hose on a wall-mounted fishing reel"**. Just like pulling thread off a spool, you pull the rubber hose off the drum, walk up to the fire, open the nozzle valve, and spray water **continuously for as long as the building's water supply lasts** (hours, not seconds). Think of it as a **"permanent, plumbing-connected garden hose locked inside every floor of a building"**, ready for immediate use.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following standards are **bolded because they are board-exam favourites**:

- **Hose internal diameter:** 19 mm (preferred for short reels) or 25 mm
- **Standard hose length:** 30 m (commonly); 36 m in some high-rise layouts
- **Minimum working pressure at the nozzle:** 3.5 bar (≈ **0.35 MPa**)
- **Minimum flow rate:** 24 LPM (**litres per minute**) per reel
- **Maximum length of throw:** 6–8 m effective water jet
- **Hose reel drum material:** Mild steel / stainless steel / heavy-duty polymer
- **Nozzle type:** Three-position (Jet – Spray – Shut-off), normally a **6 mm** bore

> [!NOTE]
> The **6 mm nozzle bore × ~3.5 bar pressure** combination is what produces the canonical **24 LPM** discharge. This is an *empirical benchmark*, not a derived number — memorise it.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step

1. **Water enters** the building via a rising main / down-comer system from the fire tank + pump.
2. **A dedicated branch pipe** (usually 25 mm or 32 mm GI) drops from the riser to each hose reel landing.
3. **An isolation globe valve** (the *hose reel stop valve*) allows maintenance isolation without affecting the rest of the riser.
4. **The drum rotates** on a horizontal axis when the hose is pulled — there is **no need for re-coiling during fire fighting**.
5. **The operator** walks up to ~6 m of the fire, opens the nozzle shut-off valve, and water discharges in either a **straight jet** (penetrative) or **spray** (cooling/blanketing) pattern.
6. **After use**, the hose is rewound manually (some reels are spring-loaded for auto-rewind) and the stop valve closed.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Design Choice

- **Semi-rigid hose (not fabric):** Allows it to be dragged across floors, around corners, and over debris *without kinking* — critical because untrained personnel may operate it.
- **Permanent water connection (not a stored-pressure extinguisher):** Provides an *unlimited duration* discharge — limited only by the fire pump running.
- **Drum mounting (not wall cabinet):** Ensures **rapid deployment** — one pull and the hose is available; no uncoiling by hand.
- **Shut-off nozzle:** Lets the operator **advance toward the fire progressively** without wasting water during repositioning.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Symbol | Formula / Value | Unit | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Discharge / flow rate | $Q$ | $Q = 24 \text{ LPM} = 0.4 \text{ L/s}$ | L/min | TAC / IS minimum |
| 2 | Nozzle outlet velocity | $v$ | $v = C_v \sqrt{2 g H}$ | m/s | From Bernoulli |
| 3 | Discharge (theoretical) | $Q_{th}$ | $Q_{th} = A \cdot v = A \cdot C_v\sqrt{2gH}$ | m³/s | $A$ = nozzle area |
| 4 | Discharge coefficient | $C_v$ | $0.95 \text{ to } 0.98$ | – | For standard nozzle |
| 5 | Nozzle bore area | $A$ | $A = \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4}$ | m² | $d$ = nozzle diameter |
| 6 | Frictional head loss (Hagen–Poiseuille) | $h_f$ | $h_f = \dfrac{32 \mu L v}{\rho g D^2}$ | m | Laminar (rarely used) |
| 7 | Frictional loss (Hazen–Williams) | $h_f$ | $h_f = \dfrac{10.67 \cdot L \cdot Q^{1.852}}{C^{1.852} \cdot D^{4.87}}$ | m | Preferred for water |
| 8 | Equivalent length for fittings | $L_e$ | $L_e = k \cdot D / f$ | m | For bends, valves |
| 9 | Required pressure at hose reel | $P_{req}$ | $P_{req} = P_{nozzle} + h_f + h_{elevation}$ | bar | Design check |
| 10 | Number of reels per floor | $N$ | $N = \dfrac{A_{floor}}{500}$ (rounded up) | – | TAC guideline |
| 11 | Coverage radius per reel | $R$ | $R = 30 \text{ m}$ (hose) $\times \sin(\theta)$ | m | $\theta$ ≈ 60°–120° |
| 12 | Total water demand | $V_{total}$ | $V_{total} = N \times 24 \text{ LPM}$ | L/min | Sum across building |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Vertical pipe (`|` symbol) handling:** In exam sheets, write $\vert x \vert$ for absolute value or $a \mid b$ for divisibility. NEVER write a bare `|` inside a table cell — it breaks markdown. Always use `\vert` or `\mid`.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Hose reels are **mandatory** under:
- **National Building Code (NBC 2016), Part 4 – Fire and Life Safety**
- **TAC (Tariff Advisory Committee)** recommendations for insurance-rated buildings
- **IS 884** – Specification for Fire Hose Reels
- **IS 636** – Non-percolating fire hose
- **IS 903** – Fire fighting vehicles (auxiliary spec)

Hose reels are typically installed in: **corridors, lift lobbies, stairwell landings, car parks, electrical rooms, generator rooms, and kitchen back-of-house areas**. They are *always paired* with portable extinguishers (CO₂ for electrical, ABC powder for general).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Theoretical Discharge from a Hose Reel Nozzle

We derive the discharge $Q$ from first principles (Bernoulli's equation between the upstream pipe and the nozzle exit).

**Step 1 — State Bernoulli between two points**

Let point 1 be *just inside the nozzle inlet* (still in the pipe, where velocity is small) and point 2 be *at the nozzle exit* (atmospheric).

$$
\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{v_1^2}{2g} + z_1 \;=\; \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{v_2^2}{2g} + z_2 + h_L
$$

**Step 2 — Apply assumptions**

The nozzle is nearly horizontal, so $z_1 \approx z_2$. Inside the pipe, $v_1 \approx 0$ (reservoir / large pipe assumption). $P_2 = P_{atm}$ (gauge → 0). Combining:

$$
\frac{P_1}{\rho g} \;=\; \frac{v_2^2}{2g} + h_L
$$

**Step 3 — Introduce velocity coefficient $C_v$**

Real nozzles lose energy; introduce $C_v$:

$$
v_2 \;=\; C_v \sqrt{2g H}
$$

where $H = \dfrac{P_1}{\rho g} - h_L$ is the *net head* available at the nozzle.

**Step 4 — Convert to discharge**

$$
Q_{th} \;=\; A \cdot v_2 \;=\; \frac{\pi d^2}{4} \cdot C_v \sqrt{2 g H}
$$

**Step 5 — Numerical check (KTU-favourite number: 24 LPM @ 3.5 bar)**

Given $d = 6 \text{ mm} = 0.006 \text{ m}$, $C_v = 0.97$, $H = 35 \text{ m of water}$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$:

$$
A = \frac{\pi (0.006)^2}{4} = 2.827 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
v_2 = 0.97 \cdot \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 35} = 0.97 \times 26.20 = 25.41 \text{ m/s}
$$

$$
Q_{th} = 2.827 \times 10^{-5} \times 25.41 = 7.183 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

$$
Q_{th} = 0.7183 \text{ L/s} = 43.10 \text{ LPM}
$$

This is the **theoretical maximum**. The **practical/empirical figure used in IS 884** is **24 LPM**, which accounts for real hose friction losses across 30 m of 19 mm rubber hose. The **design safety factor** built into the standard is therefore:

$$
\text{Safety factor} = \frac{Q_{theoretical}}{Q_{specified}} = \frac{43.1}{24.0} \approx 1.80
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU exams:** Examiners love asking *"Why is the design flow only 24 LPM and not the theoretical max?"* The answer: **head loss in 30 m of small-bore semi-rigid hose is very high** — typically 15–20 m of water column — leaving a residual nozzle pressure of only ~15 m (≈ 1.5 bar), which produces ≈ 24 LPM in practice.

### 3.2 Derivation — Number of Hose Reels Required per Floor (TAC Method)

The Tariff Advisory Committee prescribes coverage based on **floor plate area**:

$$
N_{reels} \;=\; \left\lceil \frac{A_{floor}}{A_{coverage}} \right\rceil
$$

where $A_{coverage} = 500 \text{ m}^2$ per reel for **ordinary hazard** occupancies, and $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ denotes the ceiling function.

**Worked Example:** A commercial floor of 1850 m² in a high-rise.

$$
N_{reels} = \left\lceil \frac{1850}{500} \right\rceil = \lceil 3.70 \rceil = 4 \text{ reels}
$$

**Total simultaneous demand:**

$$
Q_{building} = 4 \times 24 = 96 \text{ LPM} = 1.6 \text{ L/s}
$$

This is added to the **sprinkler + hydrant demand** to size the fire pump.

### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use Which Diameter

| Criterion | 19 mm Hose Reel | 25 mm Hose Reel |
|---|---|---|
| Hose length | 30 m | 30–36 m |
| Friction loss | High | Moderate |
| Operator effort | Low (lighter hose) | Higher (heavier) |
| Reach of jet | 6–7 m | 8–10 m |
| Recommended for | Internal floors, offices | Car parks, industrial, basements |
| KTU exam default | ✅ | – |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Hose Reel System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Source["Water Source"]
        A1["Fire Water Tank"]
        A2["Fire Pump - Electric + Diesel"]
    end

    subgraph Riser["Vertical Distribution"]
        B1["Riser - Wet Riser / Downcomer"]
    end

    subgraph Floor["Per Floor Assembly"]
        C1["Branch Take-off 32 mm GI"]
        C2["Isolation Globe Valve - Stop Valve"]
        C3["Hose Reel Drum - 30 m Semi-Rigid Hose"]
        C4["Three-Position Nozzle - Jet or Spray or Off"]
    end

    subgraph Operator["Operator Trajectory"]
        D1["Pull hose off drum"]
        D2["Walk to fire up to 6 m"]
        D3["Open nozzle shut-off"]
        D4["Discharge water on fire"]
    end

    A1 --> A2
    A2 --> B1
    B1 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> C3
    C3 --> C4
    C4 --> D1
    D1 --> D2
    D2 --> D3
    D3 --> D4
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Schematic — Hose Reel Drum Components

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1["Inlet Pipe - from Riser"]
    V1["Stop Valve - 25 mm Globe"]
    D1["Drum Hub - rotating on shaft"]
    H1["Hose - 30 m - 19 mm - Semi-rigid rubber"]
    G1["Guide Roller - smooth payout"]
    N1["Nozzle - 6 mm - Shut-off - Three position"]
    B1["Wall Bracket - MS Powder-coated"]
    S1["Shaft and Bearings - sealed for life"]

    P1 --> V1
    V1 --> D1
    D1 --> H1
    H1 --> G1
    G1 --> N1
    D1 --- S1
    D1 --- B1
```

### 4.3 Coverage Geometry — Single Hose Reel Reach

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    W["Wall Mounted Reel - W"]
    O["Operator Position - O"]
    F["Fire Source - F"]

    W -- "30 m hose pulls out" --> O
    O -- "effective jet 6 m" --> F

    style W fill:#cfe8ff,stroke:#036
    style O fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#a80
    style F fill:#ffd0d0,stroke:#a00
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The 30 m hose gives a **positional** reach of 30 m from the drum, but the **effective fire-fighting reach** is only **6 m** of usable water jet beyond the nozzle. Designers therefore space reels so that *every point on the floor is within 6 m of either the hose tip OR a nozzle position*. Practically, this translates to the **500 m² / reel** rule.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define a fire hose reel. List any four components.**

**Model Answer:**
A fire hose reel is a first-aid fire-fighting installation consisting of a revolving drum, a permanently connected water supply, a length of semi-rigid rubber-lined non-percolating hose (typically 19 mm bore, 30 m long), and a shut-off type nozzle, all mounted on a wall bracket.

Four components:
1. Revolving drum with hose
2. Shut-off nozzle (3-position)
3. Isolation stop valve
4. Wall mounting bracket with guide rollers

*(Full marks for crisp definition + correct list.)*

---

#### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**What is the standard discharge rate and working pressure specified by IS 884 for a hose reel?**

**Model Answer:**
As per IS 884:
- **Discharge rate:** 24 LPM minimum
- **Working pressure at nozzle:** 3.5 bar minimum
- **Test pressure:** 14 bar (hydraulic test)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full ESE Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Modified]*

**(a)** Describe in detail the construction, components, and operational features of a fire hose reel system. Highlight the relevant Indian Standards governing its design. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A fire hose reel is a permanently installed, first-aid fire-fighting appliance designed to deliver a continuous supply of water to incipient-stage (Class A) fires within a building. It enables even untrained occupants to take immediate action before the fire brigade arrives.

**1. Construction & Components:**

- **Drum Assembly:** The drum is the central rotating element, manufactured from mild steel (powder-coated) or stainless steel. It rotates on a horizontal shaft supported by sealed, self-lubricating bearings. The drum's axis is mounted on a sturdy wall bracket that anchors it firmly to the building structure.
- **Hose:** The drum carries **30 m (standard) of 19 mm internal diameter semi-rigid, rubber-lined, non-percolating hose**, conforming to **IS 636**. The semi-rigid nature allows it to be dragged across floors, around corners, and over minor obstacles without kinking — an essential feature for untrained operators.
- **Nozzle:** A **three-position shut-off nozzle** (bore ≈ 6 mm) is fitted at the hose end. The three positions are: (i) **Shut-off** (water off — for repositioning), (ii) **Jet** (straight stream for reach and penetration), and (iii) **Spray** (cone pattern for cooling and blanketing larger areas).
- **Stop Valve (Isolation Valve):** A 25 mm globe valve installed on the inlet pipe, between the building riser and the drum. This isolates the reel for maintenance without interrupting supply to other reels on the same riser.
- **Guide Rollers / Swivel Arm:** Mounted at the drum mouth to ensure smooth, kink-free payout of the hose.
- **Inlet Pipe Connection:** Typically a 25 mm or 32 mm GI branch tee from the wet riser or down-comer.

**2. Operational Features:**

- Hose is paid out simply by pulling; no manual uncoiling required.
- Water supply is **permanent and pressurised** — discharge duration is limited only by the fire pump running, not by any stored charge.
- Discharge rate: **24 LPM minimum** at the nozzle; effective jet reach: **6–8 m**.
- After use, hose is manually rewound (or auto-rewind in premium models), stop valve closed, and the reel is ready for re-use.

**3. Indian Standards Governing Design:**

| Standard | Title / Scope |
|---|---|
| **IS 884 (Part 1 & Part 2)** | Specification for Fire Hose Reels |
| **IS 636** | Non-percolating fire hose (hose specification) |
| **IS 903** | Fire fighting vehicles (auxiliary reference) |
| **NBC 2016, Part 4** | National Building Code – Fire & Life Safety |
| **TAC Regulations** | Tariff Advisory Committee – insurance-grade installation rules |

*(Examiner reward: a labelled sketch / block diagram adds 1–2 marks. Always mention the standards — it's a guaranteed half-mark.)*

---

**(b)** A commercial office floor of plan area **1850 m²** is to be protected by hose reels. Determine (i) the number of hose reels required, (ii) the total simultaneous water demand, and (iii) the minimum number of hose reels located near the **two staircase landings**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** Floor area $A_{floor} = 1850 \text{ m}^2$. Standard TAC coverage per reel = **500 m²**.

**(i) Number of hose reels:**

$$
N_{reels} = \left\lceil \frac{A_{floor}}{A_{coverage}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{1850}{500} \right\rceil = \lceil 3.70 \rceil = 4 \text{ reels}
$$

**(ii) Total simultaneous water demand:**

$$
Q_{total} = N_{reels} \times Q_{each} = 4 \times 24 \text{ LPM} = 96 \text{ LPM} = 1.60 \text{ L/s}
$$

**(iii) Distribution near staircases:**
Per NBC 2016 + TAC, **at least one hose reel must be installed on every staircase landing**. With **2 staircase landings** on this floor:
- Reels at staircases = **2** (one per landing)
- Additional reels in corridor / lobby = $4 - 2 = \mathbf{2}$ reels

**Final answer summary table:**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Number of hose reels | **4** |
| Total water demand | **96 LPM (1.6 L/s)** |
| Reels at stair landings | **2 (mandatory)** |
| Reels in corridors | **2 (remaining)** |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - ❌ **Mistake 1:** Writing $N = 1850/500 = 3.7$ and rounding to 3. **Always round UP** — partial coverage is non-compliant.
> - ❌ **Mistake 2:** Forgetting to enforce "one reel per staircase landing". Many students calculate only on area basis and miss this mandatory requirement.
> - ❌ **Mistake 3:** Using 25 mm hose diameter in flow-rate calculation. The **standard 24 LPM** is specified for **19 mm** bore; do not adjust unless the problem explicitly states a different diameter.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified]*

**(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram the working principle of a hose reel system, from the fire water tank to the nozzle discharge. Identify the role of each component in ensuring **continuous, reliable** operation. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Working Principle (Block Flow):**

Fire Water Tank (capacity typically 1–4 hours of combined demand) → Fire Pump (electric + diesel jockey) → Riser (vertical wet riser / down-comer) → Branch Tee (at each floor) → Stop Valve → Hose Reel Drum → Semi-Rigid Hose → Three-Position Nozzle → Discharge onto fire.

**Role of each component in reliability:**

1. **Fire Water Tank:** Provides *uninterrupted water source* even during pump failure or mains failure. Sized per NBC for 1–4 hours of combined sprinkler + hydrant + hose reel demand.
2. **Fire Pump (Electric + Diesel + Jockey):**
   - *Jockey pump* maintains system pressure at 7–10 bar continuously (compensates small leaks).
   - *Electric pump* starts on pressure drop (~5 bar).
   - *Diesel pump* is the **standby** — starts on power failure. This **redundancy** is what makes the system *reliable*.
3. **Riser:** The vertical spine. Always *pressurised* (wet riser); maintains water column to upper floors.
4. **Branch Tee + Stop Valve:** Allows isolation of one reel without affecting the rest — supports **maintainability**.
5. **Hose Reel Drum:** Provides *instant deployment* — one pull and the hose is in operation, no uncoiling.
6. **Semi-Rigid Hose:** *Kink-resistant* even when bent sharply — essential for untrained operators.
7. **Three-Position Nozzle:** Allows *flow control on demand* (jet / spray / off) — supports tactical flexibility.
8. **Pressure Gauges & Test Cock:** Mounted at each landing to allow *periodic performance verification* per IS 3844.

> Reliability is achieved through **redundancy (pumps)**, **standardisation (IS)**, and **simplicity of operation (no training required)**.

---

**(b)** A hose reel system uses a **19 mm bore hose of 30 m length**, with a nozzle of **6 mm diameter** and a coefficient of velocity $C_v = 0.97$. The available head at the nozzle inlet is **30 m of water**. Compute the theoretical discharge in **LPM** and compare it with the **IS 884 specified value of 24 LPM**. Comment on the difference. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Nozzle diameter $d = 6 \text{ mm} = 0.006 \text{ m}$
- Coefficient of velocity $C_v = 0.97$
- Head at nozzle $H = 30 \text{ m}$ of water
- $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

**Step 1 — Nozzle exit area:**

$$
A = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.006)^2}{4} = 2.827 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Theoretical exit velocity (Torricelli + $C_v$):**

$$
v_2 = C_v \sqrt{2 g H} = 0.97 \times \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 30}
$$

$$
v_2 = 0.97 \times \sqrt{588.6} = 0.97 \times 24.26 = 23.53 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 3 — Theoretical discharge:**

$$
Q_{th} = A \times v_2 = 2.827 \times 10^{-5} \times 23.53 = 6.652 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to LPM:**

$$
Q_{th} = 6.652 \times 10^{-4} \times 60 \times 1000 = 39.91 \text{ LPM}
$$

**Step 5 — Compare with IS 884 specified value (24 LPM):**

$$
\text{Difference} = 39.91 - 24.00 = 15.91 \text{ LPM}
$$

$$
\text{Percentage shortfall} = \frac{15.91}{39.91} \times 100 \approx 39.9 \%
$$

**Comment on the difference:**

The theoretical discharge (≈ 40 LPM) is significantly higher than the IS 884 specified value (24 LPM). The difference is **not a discrepancy** — it reflects **real-world energy losses** that are not captured in the idealised Bernoulli equation:

1. **Frictional head loss** in 30 m of 19 mm semi-rigid hose (≈ 10–15 m of water column).
2. **Local losses** at bends, couplings, and the stop valve (≈ 2–3 m).
3. **Nozzle internal losses** (already partially accounted for by $C_v = 0.97$ rather than 1.0).

When these losses are subtracted from the available 30 m head, the *residual head* at the nozzle is only **~12–15 m**, which yields ≈ **24 LPM** in practice — matching IS 884. This is precisely why the standard prescribes **24 LPM** as the design figure rather than the theoretical maximum.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - ❌ **Unit trap:** Forgetting to convert m³/s → L/s → LPM. Always do this in **one explicit line**.
> - ❌ **Using $d = 19 \text{ mm}$ (hose) instead of $d = 6 \text{ mm}$ (nozzle).** This is the most common error. The **6 mm is the nozzle bore**, not the hose diameter.
> - ❌ **Forgetting $C_v$** — without it, the answer will be ≈ 41 LPM, and you'll lose the "compare and comment" sub-part.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ A **hose reel** is a **first-aid fire-fighting** appliance using **semi-rigid rubber hose** permanently connected to the building water supply.
- ✅ **Standard hose:** 19 mm bore × 30 m length (per IS 884).
- ✅ **Standard nozzle:** 6 mm three-position shut-off (Jet / Spray / Off).
- ✅ **Design flow rate:** **24 LPM minimum** at nozzle.
- ✅ **Design pressure at nozzle:** **3.5 bar minimum**.
- ✅ **Coverage rule:** 1 reel per **500 m²** of floor area (TAC).
- ✅ **Mandatory placement:** at least **one reel per staircase landing**.
- ✅ **Discharge equation (Bernoulli + $C_v$):**
$$
Q = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} \cdot C_v \sqrt{2 g H}
$$
- ✅ **Why 24 LPM, not 40 LPM?** Friction + local losses in 30 m of small-bore hose consume the difference.
- ✅ **Reels are NOT a substitute for sprinklers or hydrants** — they are the *first responder's tool*, while hydrants serve the fire brigade.
- ✅ **Maintenance check frequency:** Weekly visual, monthly operational test (per IS 3844).
- ✅ **High-rise trigger:** Buildings > 15 m require **wet riser + hose reels at every floor**; > 24 m additionally require **fire lifts + refuge floors**.
- ✅ **Test pressure vs working pressure:** Test = **2 × working** (e.g., 14 bar test for 7 bar working) — a frequent viva question.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Working Principle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT836 - DESIGN OF FIRE PROTECTION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Working Principle

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Working Principle of a Fire Detection & Alarm System (FDAS)** refers to the systematic sequence of physical, chemical, electrical, and electronic processes by which a fire-initiated phenomenon (smoke, heat, flame, or gas) is sensed, converted into an electrical signal, processed by a control unit, and translated into a human-perceivable alarm and/or an automated suppression command.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, the **Working Principle** module covers the operational mechanics of the four primary fire signatures — *smoke, heat, radiation, and combustion gases* — and how transducers convert these phenomena into measurable electrical quantities. It also encompasses the logic architecture of the **Fire Alarm Control Panel (FACP)**, signal transmission protocols, and notification appliance networks defined under **IS 2189:2008**, **NFPA 72**, and **TAC (Technical Advisory Committee) guidelines** adopted by the Kerala Fire & Rescue Services.

### The Four Fire Signatures (The Fire Tetrahedron Sensors)

| Fire Signature | Sensor Type | Physical Quantity Measured |
|---|---|---|
| Smoke (aerosol particles) | Smoke Detector | Light scatter / ion current |
| Heat | Heat Detector | Temperature / Rate of temperature rise |
| Flame / Radiation | Flame Detector | UV / IR wavelength intensity |
| Combustion Gases | Gas Detector | CO, CO₂ concentration (ppm) |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Human Nose and Skin:**
> Imagine you are sleeping in a house. You cannot *see* the fire yet, but three things happen almost simultaneously: (1) you **smell** the smoke (like a *smoke detector* sensing aerosol particles), (2) you **feel** the heat radiating from the room (like a *heat detector* measuring temperature rise), and (3) you **shout** to wake everyone up (like the *sounder/horn* — a notification appliance). The brain that decided to shout is the **Fire Alarm Control Panel (FACP)**.

**Plain-English Decomposition:**
1. **Sense** — A physical/chemical phenomenon of fire reaches the sensor head.
2. **Transduce** — The sensor converts it into a tiny electrical signal (microvolts to millivolts).
3. **Transmit** — The signal travels through a wire (Initiating Device Circuit, IDC) back to the panel.
4. **Decide** — The FACP's microcontroller compares the signal against preset thresholds.
5. **Actuate** — If threshold is exceeded, the panel energizes Notification Appliance Circuits (NACs), activates suppression relays, and signals the Fire Station via a **transponder**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Definition — Initiating Device Circuit (IDC):** A supervised electrical pathway connecting automatic and manual fire sensors (Initiating Devices) to the FACP. "Supervised" means a small end-of-line (EOL) current flows continuously so that any break, short, or ground fault is detected even when no fire exists.

## 1.3 Fundamental Physical Constants & Standards

- **Speed of light:** $c = 3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$
- **Stefan-Boltzmann constant:** $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}^4$
- **CO alarm threshold (UL 2034):** **70 ppm for 60 minutes** (typical residential)
- **Standard test fire temperature (TF1, TF2):** **57 °C and 90 °C** (EN 54 Part 5, IS 11360)
- **Standard smoke obscuration trigger:** Obscuration $\geq$ **5 %/m to 20 %/m** depending on detector class

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Operational Logic of a Generic FDAS

The working principle can be broken down into **five logical stages**, each tied to a specific physical law:

### Stage 1 — Phenomenon Generation
Fire releases combustion byproducts: solid soot/aerosols (smoke), thermal energy (heat), electromagnetic radiation (UV/IR/visible), and gases (CO, CO₂, HCN, NOx). Each byproduct carries quantitative information.

### Stage 2 — Detection (Transduction)
A sensor element reacts to the specific byproduct:

- **Ionization Chamber:** Two americium-241 ($\alpha$-source) plates ionize air → a steady ion current flows. Smoke particles attach to ions → current drops measurably.
- **Photoelectric Chamber:** An LED emits light into a sensing chamber. Under quiescent conditions, light does not strike the photodiode. Smoke aerosols scatter photons onto the photodiode → photodiode output rises.
- **Rate-of-Rise (ROR) Heat Detector:** A thermistor or differential pressure element compares ambient temperature vs. an inert gas-trapped chamber. A rate $> 8.3 \,^\circ\text{C/min}$ triggers.
- **Fixed Temperature Heat Detector:** A eutectic solder, bimetallic strip, or fusible link melts/disconnects at **57 °C, 68 °C, 90 °C, or 141 °C** (per IS 2175 / EN 54-5 classes A1, A2, B, C).
- **UV Flame Detector:** A phototube sensitive to **185–260 nm** wavelengths detects the UV emission from hydrocarbon flames.
- **IR Flame Detector:** A photodiode or thermopile senses **4.3 μm** CO₂ emission bands or flicker frequencies in the 1–10 Hz range.

### Stage 3 — Signal Conditioning
The raw transducer signal is passed through:
- Amplification (op-amp gain stage)
- Filtering (band-pass to reject noise; e.g., IR flame detectors band-pass at 4.3 μm ± 0.2 μm)
- Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC, typically 10-bit or 12-bit)
- Threshold comparison using a microcontroller (e.g., PIC, ARM Cortex)

### Stage 4 — Decision Logic (FACP)
The FACP runs a **state machine**. Inputs from all IDCs are checked against:
- Alarm threshold
- Pre-alarm threshold (often 70 % of alarm level)
- Drift compensation (long-term contamination correction for smoke detectors)
- Voting logic (e.g., "2-of-N" coincidence for high-rises)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept — Coincidence Detection:** In high-rise and high-sensitivity environments (e.g., server rooms, hospitals), **"2-out-of-N" voting logic** is used to avoid false alarms. Any single detector's alarm is logged as a "pre-alarm"; only when **two detectors** in the same zone trigger does the panel command evacuation and suppression release.

### Stage 5 — Notification & Actuation
Upon confirmation, the FACP energizes:
- **Notification Appliance Circuits (NACs)** — Sounders, strobes, speakers
- **Auxiliary relays** — Fire dampers, AHU shutdown, elevator recall, pressurization fans
- **Suppression release solenoids** — FM-200, Novec 1230, sprinkler deluge valves
- **Remote signaling** — GSM/TCP-IP transponder to Fire Station and BMS

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates every quantitative relationship required for numerical questions on the working principle of detectors.

| Concept | Formula / Equation | Variables & Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Stefan-Boltzmann Radiative Heat Transfer | $Q = \varepsilon \sigma A (T_s^4 - T_a^4)$ | $Q$ = W; $\varepsilon$ = emissivity; $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}^4$; $A$ = m²; $T$ = K | IR flame detector sensitivity & fixed-temp detector sizing |
| Obscuration (smoke density) | $O = \dfrac{1}{L} \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{I_0}{I}\right)$ | $O$ = %/m; $L$ = path length (m); $I_0$ = incident light; $I$ = transmitted light | Photoelectric detector calibration |
| Beer-Lambert (ionization current drop) | $I = I_0 \, e^{-k n d}$ | $I$ = current (A); $k$ = constant; $n$ = smoke density; $d$ = gap (m) | Ionization detector threshold |
| Rate of Temperature Rise (ROR) | $\dfrac{dT}{dt} = \dfrac{T(t) - T_0}{t}$ | $T$ = °C; $t$ = minutes | ROR detector trigger |
| CO Threshold Logic (UL 2034) | $C \cdot t \geq K$ | $C$ = ppm; $t$ = min; $K$ = 70·60 constant | CO detector compliance |
| Wheatstone Bridge (heat sensor) | $V_{out} = V_{ex}\!\left(\dfrac{R_1}{R_1+R_2} - \dfrac{R_3}{R_3+R_4}\right)$ | $V$ = V; $R$ = Ω | RTD/thermistor signal conditioning |
| Inverse Square Law (Radiant Intensity) | $E = \dfrac{I}{d^2}$ | $E$ = W/m²; $I$ = W/sr; $d$ = m | UV/IR detector distance |
| Detector Coverage Area (Spot Type) | $A_c = \pi r^2 \quad$ or $\quad A_c = S^2$ | $r$ = radius (m); $S$ = spacing (m) per IS 2189 Table | Detector placement design |
| EOL Supervision Current | $I_{EOL} = \dfrac{V_{sup}}{R_{EOL}}$ | $V$ = V; $R$ = Ω | IDC design current |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Real-World Utility:** The Stefan-Boltzmann law directly governs the **siting distance** of IR flame detectors in oil & gas refineries. The inverse square law defines the **cone of detection**. KTU expects you to know which equation governs which detector — this is a common 3-mark question.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Photoelectric Smoke Detector Response

The photoelectric (light-scatter) detector's working principle is governed by **Mie scattering theory**. For smoke particles in the **0.1–1 μm range** (typical combustion aerosol), the scattered intensity at angle $\theta$ is:

$$I_{scat} = I_0 \cdot \frac{\lambda^2}{8\pi^2 r^2} \left(1 + \cos^2\theta\right) \cdot \left(\frac{n^2 - 1}{n^2 + 2}\right)^2 \cdot \left(\frac{2\pi r}{\lambda}\right)^6$$

**Where:**
- $I_{scat}$ = scattered light intensity at photodiode (W/m²)
- $I_0$ = incident LED intensity (W/m²)
- $\lambda$ = wavelength of LED (typically 880 nm infrared)
- $r$ = smoke particle radius (m)
- $n$ = refractive index of aerosol (~1.45)
- $\theta$ = scattering angle (designed $\sim$ 90° for chamber geometry)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Interpretation:** Because the scattered intensity scales with $\left(\dfrac{2\pi r}{\lambda}\right)^6$, **doubling the particle size increases scattered light by a factor of 64** (Rayleigh scattering limit). This is why photoelectric detectors are highly sensitive to **large, smoldering aerosols** (e.g., polyurethane foam fires) and less sensitive to **small, invisible particles** (where ionization excels).

### Numerical Example — Detector Threshold Calculation

**Given:**
- LED intensity $I_0 = 10 \text{ mW/m}^2$
- $\lambda = 880 \text{ nm} = 880 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}$
- Particle radius $r = 0.5 \text{ μm} = 0.5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}$
- $n = 1.45$, $\theta = 90°$
- Photodiode threshold $I_{th} = 50 \text{ μW/m}^2$

**Find:** Does the detector trigger?

**Step 1 — Compute the bracketed terms.**

$$\frac{n^2 - 1}{n^2 + 2} = \frac{(1.45)^2 - 1}{(1.45)^2 + 2} = \frac{2.1025 - 1}{2.1025 + 2} = \frac{1.1025}{4.1025} = 0.2687$$

$$\frac{2\pi r}{\lambda} = \frac{2\pi \cdot 0.5 \times 10^{-6}}{880 \times 10^{-9}} = \frac{3.1416 \times 10^{-6}}{8.8 \times 10^{-7}} = 3.5699$$

$$\left(\frac{2\pi r}{\lambda}\right)^6 = (3.5699)^6 = 5043.5$$

**Step 2 — Plug into the equation.**

$$I_{scat} = 10 \times 10^{-3} \cdot \frac{(880 \times 10^{-9})^2}{8\pi^2 (0.5 \times 10^{-6})^2} \cdot (1 + \cos^2 90°) \cdot (0.2687)^2 \cdot 5043.5$$

$$I_{scat} = 10^{-2} \cdot \frac{7.744 \times 10^{-13}}{8 \cdot 9.8696 \cdot 2.5 \times 10^{-13}} \cdot (1 + 0) \cdot 0.0722 \cdot 5043.5$$

$$I_{scat} = 10^{-2} \cdot \frac{7.744 \times 10^{-13}}{1.974 \times 10^{-12}} \cdot 364.1$$

$$I_{scat} = 10^{-2} \cdot 0.3923 \cdot 364.1 = 10^{-2} \cdot 142.86 = 1.4286 \text{ W/m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Compare against threshold.**

$$I_{scat} = 1.4286 \text{ W/m}^2 = 1428.6 \text{ mW/m}^2 \gg 50 \text{ μW/m}^2 = I_{th}$$

**Conclusion:** The detector triggers alarm — the smoke density produces a scattered signal three to four orders of magnitude above the photodiode threshold. In practice, the detector triggers at smoke obscuration levels far below the saturated value computed above.

## 3.2 Derivation — Rate-of-Rise (ROR) Detector Time-to-Alarm

A differential air-pressure ROR detector compares air pressure in an open chamber vs. a sealed reference chamber through a calibrated leak.

The pressure differential follows the **ideal gas law applied differentially:**

$$\Delta P = P_0 \cdot \frac{\Delta T}{T_0} \cdot k_{leak}$$

**Where:**
- $P_0$ = atmospheric pressure (Pa)
- $\Delta T$ = temperature rise above ambient (K)
- $T_0$ = ambient absolute temperature (K)
- $k_{leak}$ = calibration factor (0 to 1) accounting for the leak rate

The detector triggers when the **rate** exceeds the calibrated threshold. The standard EN 54 threshold is:

$$\frac{dT}{dt} \geq 8.3 \text{ °C/min}$$

**Numerical Example — Time to Alarm:**

**Given:**
- Fire grows from $T_0 = 27 \,^\circ\text{C}$
- $P_0 = 101325 \text{ Pa}$
- $k_{leak} = 0.85$
- Alarm threshold: $\Delta P_{th} = 50 \text{ Pa}$

**Find:** $\Delta T$ required.

$$\Delta T_{th} = \frac{\Delta P_{th} \cdot T_0}{P_0 \cdot k_{leak}} = \frac{50 \cdot 300}{101325 \cdot 0.85} = \frac{15000}{86126} = 0.174 \text{ K}$$

This temperature difference triggers the diaphragm switch, registering an alarm at:

$$T_{alarm} = 27 + 0.174 \approx 27.17 \,^\circ\text{C}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The ROR detector is a **rate-sensitive device**, not a level-sensitive one. Even a small $\Delta T = 0.174 \,^\circ\text{C}$ is sufficient *if it occurs within one minute*. This is why ROR detectors respond to fast-growing fires (e.g., liquid pool fires) but ignore slow ambient drift (e.g., seasonal temperature changes).

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Detector Working Principles

| Parameter | Ionization Smoke | Photoelectric Smoke | Heat (Fixed) | Heat (ROR) | UV Flame | IR Flame | CO Gas |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Fire Sensitivity** | Fast-flaming | Smoldering | Slow growth | Fast growth | Open flame | Open flame | Smoldering |
| **Response Time** | 30–60 s | 60–120 s | 2–10 min | 30–90 s | 1–10 s | 1–10 s | 5–30 min |
| **Output Signal** | Current drop (μA) | Photocurrent (μA) | Contact closure | Contact closure | Phototube pulse | Photodiode voltage | ppm level |
| **Power Consumption** | ~50 μA standby | ~80 μA standby | Passive | Passive | ~5 mA | ~5 mA | ~100 μA |
| **Typical Location** | Bedrooms, corridors | Living rooms, kitchens | Kitchens, garages | Warehouses, plant rooms | Refineries, turbine halls | Refineries, aircraft hangars | Parking, sleeping areas |
| **KTU Note** | Rare due to radioactive source ban | Most preferred | High false alarm immunity | Standard industrial | Outdoor high-risk | Outdoor high-risk | Used in multi-criteria detectors |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Complete FDAS Working Principle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fire Initiation: Smoke / Heat / Flame / Gas] --> B[Sensor Transduction Element]
    B --> B1[Ionization Chamber]
    B --> B2[Photoelectric Chamber]
    B --> B3[Thermistor / ROR Element]
    B --> B4[UV Phototube]
    B --> B5[IR Photodiode]
    B --> B6[Electrochemical CO Cell]
    B1 --> C[Signal Conditioning: Amplify, Filter, ADC]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    B4 --> C
    B5 --> C
    B6 --> C
    C --> D{Microcontroller Threshold Comparison}
    D -->|Below threshold| E[Standby Mode: Drift Comp + Self-Test]
    D -->|Pre-alarm threshold| F[Pre-Alarm Log: 70 percent level]
    D -->|Above threshold| G[Alarm State: Latch ON]
    G --> H[Fire Alarm Control Panel FACP]
    H --> H1[Notification Appliance Circuits NAC]
    H --> H2[Auxiliary Relay Output]
    H --> H3[Suppression Release Solenoid]
    H --> H4[Remote Transponder to Fire Station]
    H1 --> I1[Horn / Sounder]
    H1 --> I2[Strobe / Visual Alarm]
    H1 --> I3[Voice Evacuation Speaker]
    H2 --> J1[AHU Shutdown / Fire Damper Close]
    H2 --> J2[Elevator Recall to Ground]
    H2 --> J3[Pressurization Fan Start]
    H3 --> K1[Sprinkler Deluge Valve]
    H3 --> K2[FM-200 / Novec 1230 Release]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Schematic — Photoelectric Smoke Detector Internal Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[LED Light Source 880 nm] --> B[Light Trap Chamber]
    B -->|No smoke| C[Light Absorbs at Black Walls]
    B -->|Smoke particles enter| D[Scattered Light Ray]
    D --> E[Photodiode Receiver]
    E --> F[Transimpedance Amplifier]
    F --> G[Comparator and Threshold Logic]
    G -->|Exceeds setpoint| H[Alarm Output Signal]
    C --> H
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Flow — Decision Logic Inside the FACP

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[FACP Power-On Self-Test] --> B{All IDCs Healthy?}
    B -->|No| C[Trouble Signal: Open / Short Circuit]
    B -->|Yes| D[Continuous IDC Monitoring]
    D --> E{Signal Received?}
    E -->|Yes| F[Compare to Alarm Threshold]
    E -->|No| D
    F --> G{Threshold Exceeded?}
    G -->|No| D
    G -->|Yes| H[Activate NACs and Auxiliaries]
    H --> I[Manual Acknowledge or Auto Reset Timer]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Schematic — Heat Detector Fixed-Temperature Fusible Link

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Eutectic Alloy Solder] -->|Heat >= 57 / 68 / 90 / 141 °C| B[Solder Melts]
    B --> C[Spring-Loaded Plunger Releases]
    C --> D[Contact Closure Across IDC]
    D --> E[Current Spike Detected by FACP]
    E --> F[Alarm State Registered]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Explain the working principle of an ionization smoke detector with a neat sketch.**

> **Model Answer:**
>
> An ionization smoke detector uses a small amount of radioactive americium-241 ($\alpha$-emitter) to ionize air molecules between two charged electrodes, creating a steady **microampere-level ion current**. Under normal (smoke-free) conditions, this current flows constantly. When **combustion aerosols** enter the chamber, the smoke particles attach to the ions, reducing their mobility. This causes the **ion current to drop measurably** (typically 10–30 %). The FACP detects this drop and registers an alarm. **The detector is highly sensitive to small (< 1 μm) particles** produced by fast-flaming fires but is being phased out in many jurisdictions due to radioactive material handling concerns.

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between the working principles of a fixed-temperature heat detector and a rate-of-rise (ROR) heat detector.**

> **Model Answer:**
>
> | Aspect | Fixed-Temperature | Rate-of-Rise (ROR) |
> |---|---|---|
> | **Trigger Quantity** | Absolute temperature (e.g., 57 °C, 68 °C, 90 °C) | Rate of temperature rise ≥ 8.3 °C/min |
> | **Sensor Element** | Eutectic solder, bimetallic strip, fusible link | Differential pressure diaphragm, dual thermistor |
> | **Response to Fast Fires** | Delayed (waits for absolute temp) | Fast response (reacts to rate) |
> | **Response to Slow Fires** | Eventually triggers | May miss if rate is low |
> | **False Alarm Risk** | Very low | Higher (e.g., from sudden HVAC warm-up) |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]** 

**(a)** Describe the working principle of a **photoelectric smoke detector**. Derive the relationship between scattered light intensity and smoke particle size. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An office space of dimensions **20 m × 15 m × 3.5 m** requires photoelectric smoke detector coverage as per **IS 2189**. Determine the **number of detectors required**, the **spacing**, and the **mounting height** restrictions. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a)** Refer to Section 3.1 of these notes. The photoelectric detector works on the principle of **light scatter (Tyndall effect / Mie scattering)**. The derivation yields:

$$I_{scat} \propto \left(\frac{2\pi r}{\lambda}\right)^6$$

This shows that scattered intensity is highly sensitive to particle radius. For $\lambda = 880 \text{ nm}$ LED, particles of radius $0.5 \text{ μm}$ produce strong scatter. The detector chamber is designed so that, under no-smoke conditions, the LED light is trapped in a black-walled labyrinth and does not reach the photodiode. When smoke enters, the scattered light strikes the photodiode and generates an alarm.

**(b) Step-by-step:**

**Step 1 — Reference IS 2189:2008 Table 2 (Spot-type smoke detector coverage):**

For a **smooth ceiling** with height **≤ 3.5 m** and **no air movement > 1 m/s**, the **maximum coverage area per detector = 80 m²**.

**Step 2 — Compute total floor area:**

$$A_{floor} = 20 \times 15 = 300 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Number of detectors:**

$$N = \frac{A_{floor}}{A_{cov}} = \frac{300}{80} = 3.75 \implies \lceil 3.75 \rceil = 4 \text{ detectors}$$

**Step 4 — Compute spacing:**

For a square layout with 4 detectors in a $2 \times 2$ grid:

$$\text{Side spacing } S = \frac{L}{\sqrt{N}} = \frac{20}{2} = 10 \text{ m} \quad ; \quad \frac{15}{2} = 7.5 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5 — Verify spacing ≤ √(80 m²) = 8.94 m.** The 10 m side spacing **exceeds** 8.94 m, so a **$2 \times 3$ layout (6 detectors)** is preferred:

$$S_x = \frac{20}{3} = 6.67 \text{ m} \quad ; \quad S_y = \frac{15}{2} = 7.5 \text{ m}$$

Both are $\leq 8.94$ m ✔. **Answer: 6 detectors** at $6.67 \text{ m} \times 7.5 \text{ m}$ grid.

**Mounting height restriction:** Photoelectric detectors are permitted only up to **10.5 m** ceiling height per IS 2189. Our ceiling is 3.5 m — well within limits.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #1:** Students often forget to **round up** the detector count (3.75 → **4**, not 3). Losing 1 mark. Other students miss the **spacing constraint** check and recommend a 2×2 grid that violates IS 2189. Losing 2 marks. Always verify both count *and* spacing.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** With a functional block diagram, explain the **working principle of a Fire Alarm Control Panel (FACP)**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A warehouse stores **Class B (flammable liquid)** materials. Compare the suitability of **UV, IR, and dual-flame detectors** for this application. Recommend one and justify. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) FACP Working Principle — Block Diagram:**

Refer to Section 4.3 of these notes. The FACP works on the following functional sequence:

1. **Power Supply Section:** Mains 230 V AC → transformer → rectifier → regulated 24 V DC; battery backup (typically 2 × 12 V, 65 Ah sealed lead-acid) provides 24-hour standby + 15-minute alarm.
2. **Initiating Device Circuit (IDC) Interface:** Supervised loops with EOL resistors (typically 4.7 kΩ). Continuously monitors for alarm, open-circuit, and short-circuit.
3. **Microcontroller / Processor Unit:** Runs the state machine; checks ADC inputs from addressable analog sensors, applies voting logic, drift compensation, and time delays.
4. **Notification Appliance Circuit (NAC) Interface:** Switches 24 V DC to horns, strobes, speakers; typically polarized for supervision.
5. **Auxiliary Relay Output:** Dry contacts rated 2 A / 24 V DC for AHU, elevator, damper interlocks.
6. **Communication Port:** RS-485, BACnet, or Modbus to BMS; GSM/IP module to Fire Station.

**(b) Flame Detector Comparison for Class B Warehouse:**

| Criterion | UV Detector | IR Detector | Dual (UV + IR) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Detection Range** | 10–15 m | 20–30 m | 25–40 m |
| **False Alarm from Sunlight** | Very low (sun has little UV-C) | High (IR is abundant in sunlight) | **Very low** (logic AND requires both) |
| **False Alarm from Arc Welding** | High | Moderate | Low (time-coded coincidence) |
| **Response to Hydrocarbon Flame** | Excellent (CO/OH bands) | Excellent (4.3 μm CO₂ band) | **Best** |
| **Response to Non-Hydrocarbon (e.g., hydrogen)** | Low | Good | Moderate |
| **Cost** | Low | Moderate | High |

**Recommendation:** For a **Class B flammable liquid warehouse**, the optimal choice is a **Dual UV/IR Flame Detector** because:

- It eliminates false alarms from sunlight, welding, and lightning (which are common in industrial yards).
- It provides the longest detection range (25–40 m), critical for **high-ceiling warehouses** (typically 8–12 m).
- The "AND logic" ensures that only a real flame signature triggers alarm, satisfying the **low false-alarm requirement of NFPA 72**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #2:** Students often confuse the **scatter chamber geometry** of photoelectric detectors with the **refractometer chamber** of light-beam (projected-beam) detectors. They are different! Projected-beam detectors are used for **large open spaces > 80 m²** (atriums, warehouses), while spot-type detectors cover ≤ 80 m². The working principle differs — projected-beam uses **obscuration**, not **scatter**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 3, Working Principle**

- ✅ The **Fire Alarm Control Panel (FACP)** is the brain; detectors are the senses; notification appliances are the voice.
- ✅ **Two detector types to remember by signature:** *Ionization + Photoelectric* for smoke; *Fixed + ROR* for heat; *UV + IR* for flame.
- ✅ **Mie scattering equation** $\left(\dfrac{2\pi r}{\lambda}\right)^6$ governs photoelectric detector sensitivity — bigger particles = stronger scatter.
- ✅ **Ionization current drops** when smoke enters; **photoelectric current rises** — opposite signs.
- ✅ **Standard alarm temperatures:** **57 °C (A2S)** and **90 °C (CS)** per EN 54-5 / IS 11360.
- ✅ **ROR threshold:** $\geq \mathbf{8.3 \,^\circ\text{C/min}}$.
- ✅ **Smoke coverage (spot type):** **80 m² max** per IS 2189 Table 2, smooth ceiling, height ≤ 3.5 m.
- ✅ **Spacing rule:** $S \leq \sqrt{A_{coverage}}$ — and you **must check both** X and Y spacings.
- ✅ **Flame detector** at **4.3 μm** is the gold standard for hydrocarbon fires; UV is for open flames.
- ✅ **CO threshold (UL 2034):** 70 ppm for 60 minutes; 400 ppm for 4 minutes.
- ✅ **IDC must be supervised** with EOL resistor; otherwise the panel cannot detect a wire break.
- ✅ **Coincidence logic (2-out-of-N):** Required in high-rises and high-sensitivity environments (NFPA 72).
- ✅ **Stefan-Boltzmann law** $Q = \varepsilon \sigma A T^4$ is the key formula for IR detector sensitivity design.
- ✅ **Inverse square law** $E = I/d^2$ defines the **detection cone** of UV/IR detectors — **double the distance, one-fourth the signal**.
- ✅ **Eutectic solder melting point** determines fixed-temperature detector class — remember **57, 68, 90, 141 °C**.
- ✅ **Remember the four steps always:** *Sense → Transduce → Decide → Actuate*.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Alignment:** This topic maps to **Course Outcome CO3** — "Design and analyze fire detection and alarm system components for various occupancy types." Apply Bloom's Taxonomy levels *Understand (L2)* for definitions, *Apply (L3)* for numerical sizing, and *Analyze (L4)* for detector selection logic in design questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Method of Operation of Different Types of Portable Fire Extinguishers - Water Type
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT836 - DESIGN OF FIRE PROTECTION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Method of Operation of Different Types of Portable Fire Extinguishers - Water Type

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> A *Water Type Portable Fire Extinguisher* is a cylindrical, manually operated fire suppression device that uses **water as the primary extinguishing agent** to combat **Class A fires** (involving ordinary combustibles such as wood, paper, cloth, rubber, and certain plastics). It functions by exploiting the **cooling effect** (heat absorption) of water during its conversion from liquid to steam, thereby removing one leg of the classical **Fire Triangle** — namely, the *Heat Element*.

The water-based extinguishers are classified under **IS 940:1989** and **IS 15683:2018** (Indian Standards) and are rated for use against Class A fires only. They are strictly **prohibited** for **Class B** (flammable liquids), **Class C** (electrical/energized), **Class D** (combustible metals), and **Class K** (cooking oils) fires.

### 1.2 The Fire Triangle — Conceptual Foundation

$$\text{Fire} = \text{Fuel (F)} + \text{Heat (H)} + \text{Oxygen (O)}$$

Water extinguishers primarily attack the **Heat** component by absorbing approximately **2,257 kJ/kg** of *latent heat of vaporization*. When one litre of water evaporates, it absorbs roughly **2,257 kJ** of energy, dramatically cooling the fuel surface below its *ignition temperature*.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Bucket vs. The Bomb"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:**
> Imagine you are standing in a hot kitchen where a pan of oil has caught fire. If you throw a bucket of water, the water rapidly cools the surroundings and smothers the heat. However, if you mistakenly throw water on a chip-pan fire (Class K), the water instantly vaporizes into steam, expanding ~1,700 times, sending a fireball upward — a catastrophic explosion. This is precisely why **water type extinguishers are engineered ONLY for Class A fires**. They are the "bucket brigade" of firefighting — simple, reliable, and effective against ordinary combustibles.

### 1.4 Classification of Water Type Fire Extinguishers

Based on the **method of expelling water** and the **internal pressurization mechanism**, water type extinguishers are categorized into the following four sub-types:

| Sub-Type | Pressurization Method | Common Application |
|----------|----------------------|--------------------|
| **Soda-Acid Type** | Internal chemical reaction (CO₂ generation) | Industrial, warehouses |
| **Gas Pressure / Cartridge Type** | External CO₂/N₂ gas cartridge | Offices, commercial buildings |
| **Stored Pressure Type** | Pre-charged propellant gas in same vessel | Modern buildings, residential |
| **Water Pump Type** | Manual hand-pump action | Rural areas, construction sites |
| **Anti-Freeze Type** | Chemical additives (CaCl₂) lower freezing point | Cold storage, outdoor use |

---

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:**
> In board examinations, students frequently confuse **Stored Pressure** with **Gas Pressure (Cartridge)** type. The key distinction is:
> - *Stored Pressure* → Propellant gas and water share the **same chamber**.
> - *Gas Pressure (Cartridge)* → Propellant gas is in a **separate cartridge** punctured on activation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle — Heat Removal Mechanism

Water extinguishes fire via three simultaneous physical phenomena:

1. **Cooling (Sensible Heat Absorption):** Water absorbs heat as it warms from ambient to boiling point (~100 °C). Specific heat capacity: **$c_p = 4.186 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$**.
2. **Cooling (Latent Heat Absorption):** Water absorbs heat during phase change from liquid to steam at boiling point. Latent heat: **$L_v = 2,257 \text{ kJ/kg}$**.
3. **Smothering (Steam Blanketing):** Generated steam displaces oxygen in the immediate fire zone, contributing to limited oxygen suppression.

The total heat absorbed per kilogram of water can be expressed as:

$$\boxed{Q_{\text{total}} = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T + m \cdot L_v}$$

Where:
- $Q_{\text{total}}$ = Total heat absorbed (kJ)
- $m$ = Mass of water (kg)
- $c_p$ = Specific heat capacity of water = **4.186 kJ/kg·K**
- $\Delta T$ = Temperature rise of water from ambient to boiling point (K)
- $L_v$ = Latent heat of vaporization = **2,257 kJ/kg**

### 2.2 Detailed Operational Methods (Sub-Type Analysis)

#### A. Soda-Acid Type Extinguisher

**Construction:**
- Cylindrical mild-steel body (~9 L capacity)
- Internal solution: **Sodium bicarbonate (NaHCO₃)** dissolved in water (typically 8–10% concentration)
- Glass/Plastic vial suspended at the top containing concentrated **Sulphuric acid (H₂SO₄)**
- Discharge nozzle with a lever-operated squeeze-grip

**Method of Operation:**

1. Carry the extinguisher to the fire location (within ~6 m range).
2. Remove the safety pin/cap.
3. **Invert the extinguisher** — this is the critical step.
4. The acid vial breaks; H₂SO₄ mixes with NaHCO₃ solution.
5. Chemical reaction generates CO₂ gas internally.
6. CO₂ pressurizes the water column.
7. Water is expelled through the discharge hose and nozzle.

**Chemical Reaction:**

$$2\text{NaHCO}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \rightarrow \text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 2\text{CO}_2 \uparrow$$

**Generated Pressure:** Typically **8–10 bar (800–1,000 kPa)**.

#### B. Gas Pressure (Cartridge) Type Extinguisher

**Construction:**
- Main body filled with water only (no internal chemical).
- Separate **CO₂ or N₂ gas cartridge** (typically 60–120 g) housed in the head/cap assembly.
- Piercing mechanism actuated by a striker lever.

**Method of Operation:**

1. Carry the extinguisher to the fire.
2. Pull the safety pin.
3. Strike the top lever/knob → this drives a piercing pin into the gas cartridge.
4. The high-pressure gas rushes into the water chamber.
5. Pressurized water is discharged via the squeeze-grip nozzle.

**Pressure Regulation (Gas Expansion):**

$$P_1 V_1 = P_2 V_2$$

Where:
- $P_1, V_1$ = Initial pressure and volume of gas in cartridge
- $P_2, V_2$ = Final (working) pressure and total water chamber volume

#### C. Stored Pressure Type Extinguisher

**Construction:**
- Single chamber containing both water and a permanent propellant gas (air or N₂) pre-charged at **10–15 bar**.
- Built-in **pressure gauge** (colour-coded: green = operational, red = needs servicing).
- Continuous, uninterrupted discharge.

**Method of Operation:**

1. Carry to fire.
2. Check the pressure gauge (must be in the green zone).
3. Pull safety pin.
4. Aim nozzle at the **base of the flames**.
5. Squeeze the handle → water discharges in a steady stream.
6. Sweep side-to-side until fire is extinguished.

#### D. Water Pump Type Extinguisher

**Construction:**
- Open or semi-closed cylindrical tank (~10–15 L).
- Manual piston pump attached to the top.
- No pressurization gas required.

**Method of Operation:**

1. Fill with clean water.
2. Manually pump the handle — this creates mechanical pressure.
3. Direct the discharge nozzle at the fire base.

> This type is the **simplest** and is favoured in **rural/agricultural** settings and **construction sites** where pressurized cylinders pose maintenance issues.

#### E. Anti-Freeze Type Extinguisher

**Construction:**
- Similar to soda-acid or gas pressure type.
- Water is mixed with **Calcium Chloride (CaCl₂)** or **Ethylene Glycol**.
- These additives depress the freezing point to approximately **−30 °C**.

**Method of Operation:**
Identical to either soda-acid or gas-pressure type, depending on the pressurization mechanism chosen by the manufacturer.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Typical Value | Use Case |
|---|-------------------|---------------|---------------|----------|
| 1 | Total heat removal | $Q = mc_p\Delta T + mL_v$ | $L_v = 2{,}257 \text{ kJ/kg}$ | Sizing of extinguisher capacity |
| 2 | Soda-acid reaction | $2\text{NaHCO}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \rightarrow \text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 2\text{CO}_2$ | Generates ~10 bar | Pressurization chemistry |
| 3 | Gas law | $P_1V_1 = P_2V_2$ | $P_1 = 50 \text{ bar (cartridge)}$ | Cartridge pressure decay |
| 4 | Discharge time | $t_d = V / Q_r$ | 60–90 s | Operational planning |
| 5 | Effective range | $R_e$ | 6–10 m | Tactical deployment |
| 6 | Standard capacity | $V_{\text{std}}$ | **9 litres** | IS 940 compliance |
| 7 | Freezing point depression | $\Delta T_f = K_f \cdot m \cdot i$ | Down to −30 °C | Anti-freeze formulation |
| 8 | Steam expansion ratio | $V_{\text{steam}}/V_{\text{water}}$ | ~1,700 | Hazard warning |

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> The $Q = mc_p\Delta T + mL_v$ equation is the foundation for **fire load calculations** in building design. A KTU graduate working as a Fire Safety Engineer will routinely use these calculations to determine the *minimum number of extinguishers* required per floor area as per **TAC (Tariff Advisory Committee)** and **NBC 2016** guidelines.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Total Heat Absorption by Water Extinguisher

**Problem Statement:**
A 9-litre water-type fire extinguisher discharges its entire contents onto a wood fire at 25 °C ambient temperature. Calculate the **total heat energy absorbed** by the water from the fire.

**Given Data:**
- Mass of water: $m = 9 \text{ kg}$ (since density of water = 1 kg/L)
- Initial water temperature: $T_i = 25 \text{ °C} = 298 \text{ K}$
- Final water temperature (boiling): $T_f = 100 \text{ °C} = 373 \text{ K}$
- Specific heat capacity: $c_p = 4.186 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$
- Latent heat of vaporization: $L_v = 2{,}257 \text{ kJ/kg}$
- Acceleration due to gravity: $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
