> **Reading tip for KTU students:** Every time a question asks *"Explain the working of a dry powder system,"* this is the diagram you draw — start with the **propellant cylinder**, trace the path through the **actuator → manifold → pipe → nozzle → fire**, and close the loop with the **detector → control panel** chain.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 Dry Powder Type — Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> All questions below are **written in the KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Evaluation) style**. Marks are tagged per the official KTU Bloom's Taxonomy mapping. **Model answers are complete, step-by-step, and written in the tone of a topper's answer script** that the Board examiner would award full marks to.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper Set B, Q1.a]**

### Question A1 (3 Marks)

> *"With the help of a neat sketch, explain the working of a stored-pressure type dry powder fire extinguisher. List any **three** important dry chemical agents used."*

#### Model Answer (3 Marks Allocation)

**Introduction (1 mark):** A stored-pressure dry powder extinguisher is a **self-contained fire suppression device** in which the propellant gas (N₂) and the extinguishing powder are stored in the **same cylinder**, separated only by a floating piston or a free gas-powder interface. The cylinder is permanently pressurised to **15 bar** at 21 °C and is fitted with a **pressure gauge**, a **discharge valve**, a **siphon tube**, and a **discharge hose with nozzle**.

**Working Principle (1.5 marks):** When the **discharge lever** is pressed, the valve opens. The pressurised N₂ pushes the powder up the **siphon tube** and out through the **hose**. The powder exits the **nozzle** as a high-velocity cloud, interrupting the **combustion chain reaction** in the flame zone. The discharge lasts **8–30 seconds** depending on cylinder size.

**Three Agents (0.5 mark — 1 sentence each):**
1. **Monoammonium Phosphate (NH₄H₂PO₄)** — for ABC class fires.
2. **Sodium Bicarbonate (NaHCO₃)** — for BC class fires.
3. **Potassium Bicarbonate (KHCO₃)** — for higher-efficiency BC applications.

**Sketch (essential, even if ASCII):**

```
    [Pressure Gauge]
          |
   _______V________
  |                |  <-- 15 bar N2 above powder
  |  ============= |  <-- Free gas-powder interface
  |  ///////////// |
  |  // POWDER //  |  <-- Monoammonium Phosphate
  |  ///////////// |
  |  |  |       |  |  <-- Siphon tube
  |__|__|_______|__|
            |
        [Valve] ---- [Hose] ---- [Nozzle] ----> FIRE
```

---

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Q1.c]**

### Question A2 (3 Marks)

> *"Compare ABC and BC type dry chemical powders in terms of their **active chemical agent**, **classes of fire tackled**, and **colour identification band** as per IS 14609."*

#### Model Answer (in tabular form, 3 marks)

| Property | ABC Powder | BC Powder |
|:---------|:-----------|:----------|
| **Active Agent** | Monoammonium Phosphate (NH₄H₂PO₄) | Sodium Bicarbonate (NaHCO₃) or Potassium Bicarbonate (KHCO₃) |
| **Fires Tackled** | Class A (solid fuels), Class B (flammable liquids), Class C (flammable gases), **and electrical** | Class B and Class C only — **NOT** effective on Class A |
| **Colour Band** | **Cream / Beige** band on the red body | **No band** (plain red body) |
| **Application Density** | **1.0 kg/m²** | **0.65 kg/m²** |
| **Discharge Time** | **60 s** | **30 s** |

> **Conclusion (1 mark worth of inference):** ABC powder is the *universal* agent and is preferred when the hazard inventory includes ordinary combustibles (wood, paper, cloth, plastic). BC powder is the *specialist* agent, used where liquid or gas fires dominate and a faster knock-down is required.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **INTERNAL CHOICE:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Q7.a — Modified]**

> *"A dry powder protection system is to be designed for a **diesel generator (DG) room** in a hospital. The DG room floor area is **6 m × 5 m** with a ceiling height of **4 m**. The available powder cylinder is rated at **25 kg of ABC powder**, pressurised with N₂ at **15 bar**. The design application density is **1.0 kg/m²**, and the discharge must complete in **60 s**.*
>
> **(a)** Calculate the **total powder mass** and the **number of cylinders** required. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Draw a **labelled line diagram** of the system and explain the **sequence of operations** from fire detection to fire extinction. **(7 marks)**"

---

#### Model Answer — Question A

### (a) Numerical Design (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Floor area.** (0.5 mark)

$$
A = 6 \text{ m} \times 5 \text{ m} = 30 \text{ m}^{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Powder mass using Formula F-1.** (1 mark)

$$
M_{p} = A \times q = 30 \text{ m}^{2} \times 1.0 \text{ kg/m}^{2} = 30 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 3 — Number of cylinders using Formula F-3.** (1 mark)

$$
N_{cyl} = \dfrac{30}{25} = 1.2
$$

**Step 4 — Round UP.** (0.5 mark)

$$
N_{cyl} = 2 \text{ cylinders (redundancy = 20 kg = 67 % margin)}
$$

**Step 5 — Required mass flow rate using Formula F-4.** (1 mark)

$$
\dot{m}_{p} = \dfrac{M_{p}}{t_{d}} = \dfrac{30}{60} = 0.50 \text{ kg/s}
$$

**Step 6 — Nozzle sizing — assume a single flooding nozzle of $d_n = 10$ mm.** (1 mark)

$$
A_{n} = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(0.010)^{2} = 7.854 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^{2}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{p,actual} = 0.7 \times 7.854 \times 10^{-5} \times \sqrt{2 \times 900 \times (15-1)\times 10^{5}}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{p,actual} = 5.498 \times 10^{-5} \times \sqrt{2.52 \times 10^{9}} = 5.498 \times 10^{-5} \times 5.02 \times 10^{4} \approx 2.76 \text{ kg/s}
$$

**Step 7 — Final verdict.** (1 mark)

$$
\boxed{N_{cyl} = 2, \quad \dot{m}_{p,actual} = 2.76 \text{ kg/s} \gg 0.5 \text{ kg/s required. Design is OVER-DESIGNED and SAFE.}}
$$

> **Conclusion:** Two cylinders will provide **50 kg** of powder, which is 67 % more than the minimum 30 kg required. The single 10 mm nozzle will discharge 2.76 kg/s, well above the 0.5 kg/s required, ensuring **60-second rapid knock-down**.

---

### (b) Labelled Diagram and Sequence of Operations (7 Marks)

**Line Diagram (4 marks):**

```
   [Smoke Detector]  [Heat Detector]
          \\              //
           \\            //
            V          V
       [CONTROL PANEL — 24V DC]
              |
              V
    [Solenoid Actuator]  <-- triggered
              |
              V
   [N₂ Cylinder @ 15 bar]  ---> pressurises
              |
              V
   [ABC Powder Cylinder 25 kg x 2]
              |
              V
   [Manifold with Check Valve]
              |
              V
   [DN 25 Pipe, 12 m run, 1 bend, 1 valve]
              |
              V
   [Flooding Nozzle — 120 deg cone, 10 mm]
              |
              V
   [DG Room Hazard — 6 m x 5 m]
              |
              V
   [Pressure Switch] ---> confirms ---> [Remote Alarm]
```

**Sequence of Operations (3 marks — to be written as a numbered list):**

1. **Detection:** Smoke/heat detector senses the incipient fire (e.g., from fuel-line leak in DG).
2. **Confirmation:** Control panel cross-verifies signal using **voting logic** (1-out-of-2 or 2-out-of-2) to avoid false actuation.
3. **Pre-discharge alarm:** Hooter and strobe sound. **30-second evacuation timer** begins. DG room ventilation fans are shut down to prevent powder loss.
4. **Solenoid energisation:** At t = 30 s, the **24 V DC solenoid** receives the actuation pulse, opening the cylinder valve.
5. **Propellant expansion:** N₂ from the pressurising cylinder rushes into the powder cylinder, fluidising the powder bed.
6. **Two-phase flow:** Powder + N₂ mixture flows through the manifold, check valve, and pipe network.
7. **Nozzle atomisation:** The mixture exits the nozzle as a **120° conical cloud** of fine particles (10–75 μm).
8. **Extinction:** The cloud reaches the fire in **< 2 seconds**. **Chain-breaking** (KH⁺, PO₄³⁻ radicals) and **smothering** extinguish the flame in **5 seconds**.
9. **Hold time:** Powder cloud remains suspended for **5–10 minutes**, preventing re-ignition.
10. **Post-event:** Pressure switch confirms discharge. Hospital fire station is alerted. System is recharged within 24 hours.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Q8 — Modified]**

> *"Explain in detail the **design philosophy**, **components**, and **selection criteria** of a fixed dry powder suppression system for a **petrol pump installation**. Your answer must include:*
>
> **(a)** A **classification of dry chemical powders** and the **justification** for the choice of powder type for a petrol pump. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A **detailed description of the system components** and a **discussion of the advantages and disadvantages** of dry powder over foam for this application. **(7 marks)**"

---

#### Model Answer — Question B

### (a) Classification and Justification (7 Marks)

**Classification Table (3 marks):**

| Class | Active Agent | Fires Tackled | Notes |
|:-----:|:-------------|:--------------|:------|
| **ABC** | Monoammonium Phosphate | A, B, C | Universal; sticky residue on metals |
| **BC (Standard)** | Sodium Bicarbonate | B, C | Most common; cheap |
| **BC (Super-K)** | Potassium Bicarbonate | B, C | 1.5–2× more efficient than NaHCO₃ |
| **D** | NaCl / Cu powder | D (metals) | Not used for hydrocarbon fires |
| **Monnex** | Potassium phosphates | B (high-performance) | Used in military aviation |

**Justification for Petrol Pump (4 marks):**

- The **primary hazard** at a petrol pump is **Class B** (spilled petrol, kerosene, diesel). Spilled fuel vapours ignite rapidly and form a **pool fire** with **radiative heat flux > 25 kW/m²** at 5 m distance.
- **BC powder (NaHCO₃)** is selected because:
  1. It is **most effective on Class B** (knock-down in 5–10 seconds).
  2. It is **non-conductive** — safe around the **electrical pump dispenser** (230 V / 415 V).
  3. It is **economical** — half the cost of ABC powder per kg.
- **ABC powder is NOT preferred** for petrol pumps because the *Class A* capability is unnecessary, and the **sticky phosphate residue** clogs fuel filters if discharged near dispensers.
- **Application density:** $q = 0.65 \text{ kg/m}^{2}$ (BC standard).
- **Discharge time:** $t_d = 30 \text{ s}$ (BC requirement).
- **Design per IS 14609 + TAC guidelines** (Tariff Advisory Committee, India) for **petrol pump protection**.

---

### (b) System Components and Comparative Analysis (7 Marks)

**System Components (4 marks — write a paragraph for each):**

1. **Detection Subsystem:** Linear heat-detection cable along the dispenser canopy, plus **flame detectors (IR/UV)** for fast response (< 5 s). Manual pull stations at every pump island.
2. **Control Panel:** Microprocessor-based, with **cross-zoned detection** logic, **30-second countdown timer**, **abort switch** (for false alarms), and **battery backup (24 h)**.
3. **Agent Storage:** **Two 50 kg cylinders** of NaHCO₃ powder, pressurised by **N₂ at 15 bar**, fitted with **pressure gauges, safety relief burst discs (set at 20 bar)**, and **solenoid valves** (24 V DC).
4. **Piping:** **Galvanised MS pipe, DN 40** (for 50 kg systems), Sch 40, with **long-radius bends** and **flanged joints** for ease of maintenance.
5. **Nozzles:** **Flooding nozzles** with **120° cone pattern**, mounted at **2.5 m height** on the canopy underside.
6. **Auxiliary:** **Pressure switch** for discharge confirmation; **gas detector** for vapour leak pre-warning.

**Advantages of Dry Powder over Foam at Petrol Pumps (1.5 marks):**

1. **Faster knock-down** — powder extinguishes in 5–10 s; foam needs 30–60 s to spread.
2. **No foaming agent storage** — eliminates the maintenance headache of foam concentrate degradation.
3. **Easier recharge** — a 50 kg cylinder can be swapped in 15 min; foam tanks need complete refill.
4. **Operates at any orientation** — no risk of foam siphoning in tilted cylinders.

**Disadvantages of Dry Powder (1.5 marks — must be admitted!):**

1. **Severe post-discharge contamination** — powder coats vehicles, dispensers, and the forecourt; clean-up takes 4–8 hours.
2. **Visibility zero during discharge** — fire-fighters cannot enter the area until the cloud settles.
3. **No cooling** — once the powder settles, the *hot metal* of the dispenser can re-ignite spilled fuel if not manually cooled.
4. **Not suitable for underground tank fires** — powder cannot penetrate a 6 m depth of fuel; foam is mandatory for that scenario.

> **Conclusion (1 mark worth of summary):** Dry powder is the **optimal choice for surface dispenser fires** at petrol pumps due to its speed, simplicity, and electrical safety. However, for **bulk storage tank fires**, **foam systems are mandatory**, and KTU Module 3 covers this complementary system in the next unit.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Lose Marks (Common KTU 2024 Pitfalls):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to round UP the cylinder count.** If $N_{cyl} = 1.6$, writing "$1.6$ cylinders" is a **2-mark penalty** — the correct answer is **2 cylinders**.
> 2. **Confusing kg/m² with kg/m³.** ABC powder on a *floor* uses kg/m²; on a *volumetric flood* (paint booth) uses kg/m³. Mixing them gives **0 marks** in part (a).
> 3. **Omitting the 30-second evacuation delay.** The countdown timer is a *legal* requirement. Drawings without the timer are marked down **1 mark**.
> 4. **Not stating the colour band** of the extinguisher when asked for "identification." IS 14609 mandates the **cream band for ABC**; the KTU examiner will deduct **1 mark** for omitting it.
> 5. **Forgetting the safety relief burst disc.** A dry powder cylinder without a burst disc is an **explosion hazard**. Examiners look for this in the diagram.
> 6. **Writing "dry powder" instead of "dry chemical powder"** in the answer. The two are *not* identical — "dry powder" often refers to talcum-like inert powders used in explosion suppression, not fire suppression. KTU penalises this **terminology confusion**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary — Read this 30 minutes before the exam.**

### ✅ Must-Memorise Constants

- Design application density: **q = 0.65 kg/m²** (BC) ; **q = 1.00 kg/m²** (ABC)
- Volumetric density: **q_v = 0.40 kg/m³** (ABC for enclosures)
- Cylinder pressure: **15 bar (N₂)**
- Nozzle pressure: **≥ 10 bar**
- Discharge time: **30 s (BC)** ; **60 s (ABC)**
- Nozzle discharge coefficient: **C_d = 0.7**
- Bulk density of powder: **900 kg/m³**

### ✅ Must-Know Components

- **Powder cylinder** (ABC/BC/D powder)
- **N₂ propellant cylinder** (stored-pressure) or **CO₂ cartridge** (cartridge-operated)
- **Solenoid actuator** (24 V DC)
- **Manifold with check valve**
- **DN 25 / DN 40 Sch 40 pipe**
- **Flooding nozzle** (120° cone) / **Jet nozzle** (60°) / **Flat-fan nozzle** (180°)
- **Pressure switch** (for discharge confirmation)
- **30-second evacuation timer**
- **Burst disc** (set at 20 bar)

### ✅ Must-Know Mechanisms

- **Chemical chain-breaking** (K, P, NH₄ radicals mop up OH•, H•)
- **Thermal cooling** (latent heat of decomposition)
- **Physical smothering** (cloud blanket on fuel surface)

### ✅ Must-Know Selection Criteria

| Hazard | Powder |
|:-------|:------:|
| Petrol pump | BC |
| Transformer | ABC |
| Paint booth | ABC (volumetric) |
| Magnesium workshop | D |
| DG room | ABC |
| LPG godown | ABC |

### ✅ Must-Know Standards

- **IS 14609** — Specification for dry chemical powder
- **IS 15105** — Fixed extinguishing systems — Dry powder
- **NFPA 17** — Standard for dry chemical extinguishing systems
- **TAC India** — Petrol pump protection guidelines

### ✅ Two Formulae to Tattoo on Your Brain

$$
M_{p} = A \times q \quad \quad \quad \quad N_{cyl} = \left\lceil \dfrac{M_{p}}{C_{cyl}} \right\rceil
$$

> **Final Revision Tip:** The KTU 2024 board has historically asked the *same 3 sub-questions* on dry powder for three years running:
> 1. "Explain the working with a diagram" (7 marks).
> 2. "Calculate the powder mass and number of cylinders" (7 marks).
> 3. "Classify dry powders and justify the choice" (5 marks).
>
> **If you can write these three answers cold, you have secured ≥ 12 / 14 marks on any dry powder question.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: CO2 Type
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT836 - DESIGN OF FIRE PROTECTION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** CO2 Type

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **CO₂ (Carbon Dioxide) Type Fire Protection System** is a **gaseous total flooding or local application fire suppression system** that extinguishes Class A, Class B, and Class C fires by physically displacing the oxygen in the protected enclosure or around the hazard, thereby interrupting the **fire tetrahedron's** combustion-sustaining element. It is regulated primarily by **NFPA 12: Standard on Carbon Dioxide Extinguishing Systems** and recognized under **IS 6382** for Indian installations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** CO₂ systems are classified as *clean agent alternatives* (though not technically "clean" by Halon-replacement standards) and are used where water damage must be avoided — such as **electrical rooms, server halls, transformer bays, paint spray booths, and flammable liquid storage rooms**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a room where a candle is burning. If you gently pour an invisible, heavy blanket of gas (CO₂ is ~1.5× denser than air) over the flame, the blanket **smothers** it by pushing the oxygen away. That is exactly what a CO₂ system does — but in **seconds**, and at industrial scale.

- CO₂ is stored as a **liquid under high pressure** in cylinders.
- When released, it rapidly **expands into gas**, **displacing air (and its oxygen)**.
- The flame is choked of oxygen within **60 seconds** (standard discharge time).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why CO₂ and not water?** Water conducts electricity and damages electronics. CO₂ is electrically non-conductive, leaves **zero residue**, evaporates cleanly, and works on **live electrical fires** (Class C).

### 1.3 Key Physical Properties of CO₂ as an Agent

| Property | Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Molecular Weight | **44.01 g/mol** | 1.5× heavier than air → settles to bottom |
| Boiling Point at 1 atm | **−78.5 °C (194.7 K)** | Rapid phase change absorbs heat |
| Critical Temperature | **31.04 °C** | Above this, CO₂ cannot be liquefied by pressure alone |
| Critical Pressure | **1070 psia (7.38 MPa)** | Storage design boundary |
| Vapor Density at STP | **1.522 kg/m³** | Heavier than air (1.225 kg/m³) |
| Extinguishing Mechanism | Primarily **Oxygen Displacement** | Reduces O₂ below ~15% (from 21%) |
| ODP (Ozone Depletion Potential) | **0** | Environmentally acceptable |
| GWP (Global Warming Potential) | **1** | Acceptable (vs 1300 for HFC-227ea) |
| Electric Conductivity | **Non-conductive** | Safe for live electrical equipment |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mark-Worthy Fact:** CO₂ is **the only gaseous extinguishing agent approved by NFPA for deep-seated Class A fires** (such as in coal piles, baled cotton, or paper storage) when applied as a local application system.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Science: How CO₂ Extinguishes Fire

Fire requires four elements (the **Fire Tetrahedron**): **Fuel, Oxygen, Heat, Chemical Chain Reaction**. CO₂ attacks the **Oxygen** leg primarily, with a minor **Heat Removal (Cooling)** leg.

#### Step-by-Step Mechanism:

1. **Liquid CO₂ → Gas Expansion**: Stored at ~**57.6 bar (835 psi) at 21 °C**, liquid CO₂ is released through **differential pressure** or **pilot-operated valves**.
2. **Adiabatic Expansion Cooling**: The rapid Joule-Thomson expansion produces **CO₂ "snow"** (dry ice particles) at **−78.5 °C**, absorbing significant latent heat.
3. **Oxygen Dilution**: The released CO₂ mixes with air, diluting O₂ concentration. Most flaming combustion ceases when O₂ drops below **15%** (normal air = 21%).
4. **Sustained Inertion**: CO₂ concentration is **maintained** in the enclosure for a specified **HOLD time** (typically 10–20 minutes) to prevent re-ignition.

### 2.2 Classification of CO₂ Systems (KTU Board Focus)

#### A. By Storage Pressure

| Type | Storage Pressure | Storage Condition | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **High Pressure (HP)** | **57.6 bar (835 psi)** at 21 °C | Liquid + vapor at room temp | Small enclosures (< 500 m³) |
| **Low Pressure (LP)** | **20.7 bar (300 psi)** at −18 °C | Refrigerated bulk tank | Large enclosures, industrial |

#### B. By Application Method

- **Total Flooding System**: CO₂ released to fill an entire enclosed volume uniformly. Used for rooms, vaults, gensets.
- **Local Application System**: CO₂ directed at a specific hazard (e.g., a paint dip tank, quench tank). Not required to flood the entire room.
- **Hand Hose Line System**: Manual CO₂ hose reels for fire brigade use.

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Reference:** KTU board questions frequently ask for **Total Flooding Quantity Calculations** using the **NFPA 12 specific volume method**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Equations

| # | Formula | Description / Use | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $W = \dfrac{V}{S}$ | Quantity of CO₂ required (mass basis) | kg |
| 2 | $W = \dfrac{V \cdot C}{(100 - C) \cdot S_C}$ | Refined NFPA 12 form using concentration | kg |
| 3 | $V_{net} = V_{room} - V_{non-permeable}$ | Adjusted enclosure volume after deducting impermeable objects | m³ |
| 4 | $V_{equiv} = V_{net} + V_{perm} \cdot f$ | Adjusted volume accounting for permeable objects | m³ |
| 5 | $T_{discharge} \leq 60\ s$ | Total flooding discharge time (NFPA 12 limit) | s |
| 6 | $Q_{nozzle} = C_d \cdot A_n \sqrt{2 \cdot \Delta P / \rho_{CO_2}}$ | Nozzle mass flow rate | kg/s |
| 7 | $P_{cyl} = f(T)$ | Cylinder pressure as function of temperature (vapor pressure curve) | bar |
| 8 | $A_{orifice} = \dfrac{Q_{total}}{N \cdot Q_{per\, nozzle}}$ | Orifice area per nozzle | mm² |
| 9 | $N_{cyl} = \dfrac{W}{w_{cyl}}$ | Number of cylinders required | integer |
| 10 | $C_{min}$ | Minimum design concentration (typically **34%** for surface fires, **50%** for deep-seated) | % |

Where:
- $W$ = Total weight of CO₂ required
- $V$ = Net protected volume (m³)
- $S$ = Specific volume of CO₂ at design concentration (m³/kg, from NFPA 12 tables)
- $C$ = Design concentration by volume (%)
- $S_C$ = Specific volume of CO₂ at concentration $C$
- $C_d$ = Discharge coefficient (~0.7 for standard nozzles)
- $w_{cyl}$ = CO₂ mass per cylinder (typically **45 kg** standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Conversion:** Always use $1 \text{ m}^3/kg = 1000 \text{ L/kg}$ and remember that NFPA 12 tables for $S$ give values in **ft³/lb** — convert using $1 \text{ ft}^3/\text{lb} = 0.0624 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$.

### 2.4 Design Concentration Standards (NFPA 12 Reference)

| Fuel Hazard | Minimum Design Concentration $C_{min}$ |
|---|---|
| Class A — Surface (paper, wood) | **34%** |
| Class A — Deep-seated (bales, coal) | **50%** |
| Class B — Heptane, gasoline | **34%** |
| Class B — Acetone, alcohol (polar solvents) | **50%** |
| Class C — Electrical (minimum) | **34%** |
| Paint spray booths (NFPA 33) | **34%** minimum |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Trap:** Many students incorrectly use **65%** (the IDLH for humans) as the design concentration. The correct **engineering design concentration** is based on fuel flammability — typically **34%**, which is **fatal to humans**. The system includes **pre-discharge alarms and time delays** to ensure evacuation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the CO₂ Quantity Formula (NFPA 12 Specific Volume Method)

The governing principle: **The mass of CO₂ released must displace enough air to lower the oxygen concentration to the design concentration $C$.**

**Given:**
- Enclosure volume $V$ (m³)
- Design concentration $C$ (%, by volume of CO₂ in the protected space)
- Specific volume $S$ of CO₂ vapor at 21 °C at concentration $C$ (m³/kg)

**Derivation:**

**Step 1:** Define the protected volume by the molar conservation at the final mixture state.

At equilibrium, the enclosure contains a mixture of CO₂ and air. The volume of CO₂ (as gas at atmospheric conditions) required to achieve concentration $C$ is:

$$
V_{CO_2} = \frac{C}{100} \cdot V
$$

**Step 2:** Relate the gas-phase volume of CO₂ to its **mass** via the **specific volume** $S$:

$$
m_{CO_2} = \frac{V_{CO_2}}{S}
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute $V_{CO_2}$:

$$
m_{CO_2} = \frac{C \cdot V}{100 \cdot S}
$$

**Step 4:** Re-arrange to match the canonical NFPA 12 form. Using the identity $\dfrac{C}{100 - C}$ to account for **air displaced** (the air pushed out is partially replaced by CO₂, so the effective mixing ratio needs correction):

$$
W = \frac{V \cdot C}{(100 - C) \cdot S_C}
$$

where $S_C$ is the **specific volume of CO₂ at the design temperature** — but NFPA 12 simplifies by tabulating $S$ directly at the design concentration.

**Step 5:** Therefore, the **final design equation** is:

$$
\boxed{W = \frac{V}{S}}
$$

where $V$ is the **adjusted (net) volume** of the enclosure, and $S$ is read directly from the **NFPA 12 specific volume table** for the chosen concentration and ambient temperature.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Total Flooding Quantity

**Problem:** A server room measures **10 m × 8 m × 4 m** (length × width × height). The hazard is **Class C electrical equipment (surface fire)**. Design temperature is **21 °C**. Standard high-pressure CO₂ cylinders hold **45 kg each**.

**Find:** (a) Adjusted volume; (b) Mass of CO₂ required; (c) Number of cylinders; (d) Verification of discharge time.

---

**Step (a): Adjusted Volume**

$$
V = L \times W \times H = 10 \times 8 \times 4
$$

$$
V = 320 \text{ m}^3
$$

Assume 8 m³ of impermeable equipment (server racks) →

$$
V_{net} = 320 - 8
$$

$$
V_{net} = 312 \text{ m}^3
$$

---

**Step (b): Mass of CO₂**

For Class C surface fire, $C = 34\%$. From NFPA 12 Table, $S = 0.6597 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$ at 21 °C, $C = 34\%$.

$$
W = \frac{V_{net}}{S} = \frac{312}{0.6597}
$$

$$
W \approx 472.93 \text{ kg}
$$

Add **20% safety factor** per NFPA 12 for unclosable openings:

$$
W_{design} = 1.20 \times 472.93
$$

$$
W_{design} \approx 567.5 \text{ kg}
$$

---

**Step (c): Number of Cylinders**

$$
N_{cyl} = \frac{W_{design}}{w_{cyl}} = \frac{567.5}{45}
$$

$$
N_{cyl} = 12.61 \rightarrow 13 \text{ cylinders (rounded up)}
$$

---

**Step (d): Discharge Time Verification**

NFPA 12 mandates discharge in ≤ 60 seconds. Total flow rate:

$$
\dot{m} = \frac{W_{design}}{60} = \frac{567.5}{60}
$$

$$
\dot{m} \approx 9.46 \text{ kg/s}
$$

Per nozzle flow at 57.6 bar (HP system), standard 5 mm nozzle delivers ~1.2 kg/s:

$$
N_{nozzles} = \frac{\dot{m}}{\dot{m}_{nozzle}} = \frac{9.46}{1.2} \approx 7.88 \rightarrow 8 \text{ nozzles}
$$

✅ All parameters verified.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — HP vs LP CO₂ Systems

| Parameter | High Pressure (HP) | Low Pressure (LP) |
|---|---|---|
| Storage pressure | **57.6 bar** | **20.7 bar** |
| Storage temp | Ambient (~21 °C) | Refrigerated (−18 °C) |
| Storage form | Liquid in cylinders | Bulk refrigerated liquid |
| Typical capacity | 22.5 – 45 kg/cylinder | Up to several tonnes |
| Installation cost | Lower for small systems | Lower for large systems |
| Footprint | Multiple cylinders → larger | Single bulk tank → smaller |
| Refilling logistics | Cylinder exchange | On-site recharging |
| Best for | < 500 kg CO₂ systems | > 1000 kg CO₂ systems |
| KTU exam weight | **High** (more common) | Moderate |

### 3.4 Engineering Decision Logic — When to Choose CO₂

| Selection Criterion | Choose CO₂? |
|---|---|
| Hazard is **live electrical** | ✅ Yes |
| Hazard is **flammable liquid** | ✅ Yes |
| Enclosure is **occupied continuously** | ❌ No (use FM-200 / Novec 1230) |
| Hazard is **deep-seated Class A** | ✅ Yes (local application) |
| Environmental concerns | ✅ (ODP = 0) |
| Cost-sensitive, large space | ✅ Yes (cheaper than clean agents) |
| Clean residue requirement | ✅ Yes |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Pressure CO₂ System Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Manual Pull Station] --> B[Control Panel]
    C[Smoke Detector] --> B
    D[Heat Detector] --> B
    B --> E[Abort Switch - Optional]
    B --> F[Pre-Discharge Alarm 30s]
    F --> G[Master Valve Activation]
    H[HP CO2 Cylinders 57.6 bar] --> G
    G --> I[Manifold and Check Valves]
    I --> J[Distribution Piping Network]
    J --> K1[Nozzle 1 - Ceiling]
    J --> K2[Nozzle 2 - Ceiling]
    J --> K3[Nozzle 3 - Ceiling]
    J --> K4[Nozzle 4 - Floor Perimeter]
    L[Protected Enclosure] --> M[Pressure Relief Vent]
    K1 --> L
    K2 --> L
    K3 --> L
    K4 --> L
    L --> N[Hold Time - 10 to 20 min]
```

### 4.2 CO₂ Fire Suppression Mechanism — Process Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1[Step 1: Detection - Smoke or Heat Sensor Trips] --> S2[Step 2: Alarm - Horns and Strobes Activate]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Pre-Discharge Delay - 30 seconds for evacuation]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Master Valve Opens - CO2 Released from Cylinders]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Distribution - Piping Network and Nozzles]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Flooding - Enclosure Filled to Design Concentration]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7: Oxygen Reduced Below 15 percent]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8: Combustion Ceases - Fire Suppressed]
    S8 --> S9[Step 9: Hold Period Maintained for 10 to 20 minutes]
    S9 --> S10[Step 10: Manual Reset and System Recharge]
```

### 4.3 Functional Architecture — HP vs LP Storage Comparison

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph HP[High Pressure System]
        HP1[Cylinder Bank 45 kg each] --> HP2[Pressure Reducing Valve]
        HP2 --> HP3[Piping Network]
        HP3 --> HP4[Multiple Nozzles]
    end

    subgraph LP[Low Pressure System]
        LP1[Bulk Refrigerated Tank -18 °C] --> LP2[Pressure Regulating Valve]
        LP2 --> LP3[Piping Network]
        LP3 --> LP4[Multiple Nozzles]
    end
```

### 4.4 System Layout Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Protected Volume V] --> B[Volume Adjustment]
    B --> B1[Subtract Non-Permeable Objects]
    B --> B2[Add Permeable Object Factor]
    B --> C[Calculate Design Concentration Cmin]
    C --> D[Look Up Specific Volume S]
    D --> E[Calculate CO2 Quantity W]
    E --> F[Select Cylinder Quantity N]
    F --> G[Design Piping Network]
    G --> H[Select Nozzle Type and Area]
    H --> I[Verify Discharge Time 60 seconds]
    I --> J[Issue Construction Drawings]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on Diagrams:** The above Mermaid diagrams illustrate the **functional architecture** and **control logic** of CO₂ systems. Physical nozzle placement is typically symmetric — perimeter or ceiling arrays depending on hazard location.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, May 2024]**  
**List any three properties of CO₂ that make it suitable as a fire extinguishing agent. State one major hazard of using CO₂ systems.**

**Model Answer:**

**Suitable Properties:**
1. **Electrically non-conductive** — safe for use on Class C live electrical fires.
2. **Leaves no residue** — does not damage electronic equipment, machinery, or stored goods.
3. **Zero Ozone Depletion Potential (ODP = 0)** — environmentally acceptable per Montreal Protocol.
4. **High vapor density (1.522 kg/m³)** — 1.5× heavier than air, providing effective smothering by oxygen displacement.

**Major Hazard:**
- CO₂ at extinguishing concentrations (**> 15%**) is **lethal to humans** within minutes. Asphyxiation risk in occupied spaces is the primary hazard. Hence NFPA 12 mandates **pre-discharge alarms, time delays, and lock-out electrical permits** before activation.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**  
**Differentiate between Total Flooding and Local Application CO₂ systems.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Total Flooding System | Local Application System |
|---|---|---|
| **Coverage** | Entire enclosed volume | Specific hazard zone only |
| **Concentration** | Uniform throughout room | Applied directly on hazard surface |
| **Discharge time** | ≤ 60 seconds | May be extended (1–4 minutes) |
| **Enclosure integrity** | Must be relatively airtight | Enclosure may be open |
| **Typical application** | Server rooms, electrical vaults, gensets | Paint dip tanks, quench tanks, 3D printers |
| **Design basis** | NFPA 12 — Volume × concentration | NFPA 12 — Surface area + discharge rate |
| **Example** | CO₂ flooding in a battery room | CO₂ discharge onto a flammable liquid spill |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A — Total Flooding Design Calculation (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024]**  

A **diesel generator room** has dimensions **12 m × 6 m × 5 m (L × W × H)**. The room contains a generator set of volume **18 m³** (impermeable) and cable trays occupying **12 m³** (permeable, factor 0.25). Design concentration is **34%** for Class A surface fire. Design temperature is **21 °C**. High-pressure CO₂ cylinders of **45 kg capacity** are to be used. Specific volume of CO₂ at 34% concentration and 21 °C is **0.6597 m³/kg**.

**Find:**
**(a)** The adjusted protected volume of the room. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** The mass of CO₂ required, number of cylinders, and verification of 60-second discharge. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a): Adjusted Protected Volume**

Gross volume:

$$
V_{gross} = 12 \times 6 \times 5
$$

$$
V_{gross} = 360 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 1:** Subtract impermeable volume (generator):

$$
V_1 = V_{gross} - V_{imp} = 360 - 18
$$

$$
V_1 = 342 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 2:** Add equivalent volume for permeable objects (cable trays with factor 0.25):

$$
V_{perm\,equiv} = 12 \times 0.25
$$

$$
V_{perm\,equiv} = 3 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 3:** Final adjusted volume:

$$
V_{adj} = V_1 + V_{perm\,equiv} = 342 + 3
$$

$$
\boxed{V_{adj} = 345 \text{ m}^3}
$$

---

**Part (b): CO₂ Quantity, Cylinders, Discharge Verification**

**Step 1:** Calculate CO₂ mass:

$$
W = \frac{V_{adj}}{S} = \frac{345}{0.6597}
$$

$$
W = 522.96 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply 20% safety factor for enclosure leakage / unclosable openings:

$$
W_{design} = 1.20 \times 522.96
$$

$$
W_{design} = 627.55 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 3:** Number of 45 kg cylinders:

$$
N_{cyl} = \frac{627.55}{45}
$$

$$
N_{cyl} = 13.95 \rightarrow 14 \text{ cylinders}
$$

**Step 4:** Discharge time verification.

Total required flow rate for 60-second discharge:

$$
\dot{m} = \frac{627.55}{60} \approx 10.46 \text{ kg/s}
$$

Using **standard 8 mm HP nozzle** with discharge coefficient $C_d \approx 0.7$ delivering ~1.5 kg/s each:

$$
N_{nozzles} = \frac{10.46}{1.5} \approx 6.97 \rightarrow 7 \text{ nozzles}
$$

✅ Discharge achievable in 60 seconds with 7 standard nozzles.

---

#### **Question B — Conceptual + Schematic Design (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**  

**(a)** Explain the **mechanism of fire extinguishing by CO₂** with reference to the fire tetrahedron. Why is CO₂ particularly effective on **Class B (flammable liquid)** and **Class C (electrical)** fires? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat functional block diagram, describe the **components and working sequence** of a High-Pressure CO₂ Total Flooding System for an electrical switchgear room. Mention the role of the **pre-discharge delay timer**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a): Mechanism of Extinguishing**

The **Fire Tetrahedron** consists of four components: **Fuel, Oxygen, Heat, and Chemical Chain Reaction**. A fire is sustained only when all four are simultaneously present.

**CO₂ attacks the oxygen leg of the tetrahedron:**

1. **Storage Release**: Liquid CO₂ stored at 57.6 bar in cylinders is released through a master valve.
2. **Adiabatic Expansion**: Upon release, liquid CO₂ expands rapidly (Joule-Thomson effect), forming a cold gas-snow mixture at **−78.5 °C**. This absorbs latent heat, providing a minor **cooling** effect (~6% of total extinguishing power).
3. **Oxygen Displacement**: The dense CO₂ gas (1.522 kg/m³ vs air 1.225 kg/m³) **settles** and **displaces air**, reducing the O₂ concentration from 21% to below the **flame extinction threshold of ~15%** (achieved at 34% CO₂ concentration).
4. **Maintenance of Inertion**: The concentration is **held** for 10–20 minutes (HOLD time) to prevent re-ignition of hot surfaces or smoldering fuel.

**Why CO₂ is effective on Class B and C:**

- **Class B (Flammable Liquids)**: Surface fires depend on vapor-phase fuel mixing with air. Removing O₂ below the **LOC (Limiting Oxygen Concentration)** of the vapor (typically 10–12%) prevents continued combustion. CO₂ achieves this rapidly and leaves no residue that could react with the fuel.
- **Class C (Electrical)**: Water cannot be used due to electrocution risk. CO₂ is **electrically non-conductive** and does not damage insulation, PCBs, or contacts.

---

**Part (b): System Components and Sequence**

The block diagram (shown in **Section 4.1** of these notes) illustrates the sequence. The key components are:

| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| **Detection** (Smoke / Heat Detectors) | Senses fire signature, triggers control panel |
| **Control Panel** | Logic brain — receives signal, activates alarms, releases agent |
| **Pre-Discharge Alarm** | Audible + visual warning; 30-second delay begins |
| **Abort Switch** | Allows manual override before discharge (safety for personnel) |
| **Master Valve (Solenoid/Pneumatic)** | Opens cylinder bank, initiates CO₂ release |
| **HP Cylinders (57.6 bar)** | Storage of liquid CO₂ |
| **Manifold + Check Valves** | Distributes agent, prevents backflow |
| **Piping Network** | Conveys CO₂ to nozzles |
| **Nozzles** | Atomize and direct CO₂ into the enclosure |
| **Pressure Relief Vent** | Prevents enclosure over-pressurization during discharge |
| **Hold Timer** | Maintains concentration for required duration |

**Role of Pre-Discharge Delay Timer:**

The **30-second pre-discharge delay** serves three critical functions:

1. **Personnel Evacuation**: Audible/visual alarms warn occupants to leave the protected enclosure before CO₂ reaches lethal concentration (>10%).
2. **System Integrity Check**: Allows manual abort via the abort switch if a false alarm has occurred.
3. **Process Shutdown**: In industrial settings, allows controlled shutdown of equipment (e.g., closing fuel valves on a genset) before agent release.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - ❌ **Forgetting the 20% safety factor** on adjusted volume → loses 2 marks.
> - ❌ **Confusing HP pressure** (57.6 bar / 835 psi) with LP pressure (20.7 bar / 300 psi) → loses 1 mark.
> - ❌ **Using 65% (human IDLH)** as the design concentration instead of 34% (fuel-based) → wrong design entirely.
> - ❌ **Omitting the pre-discharge delay** explanation in Question B → loses 2 marks.
> - ❌ **Not rounding up cylinder count** (always round to the next higher integer) → loses 1 mark.
> - ❌ **Forgetting to subtract impermeable volumes** in volume adjustment → cascading errors, loses 3+ marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

✅ **CO₂ is a gaseous fire suppression agent** that extinguishes primarily by **oxygen displacement** (smothering), with minor cooling from Joule-Thomson expansion.

✅ **NFPA 12** is the governing design standard; **IS 6382** is the Indian equivalent.

✅ **Two storage types**: High Pressure (HP, 57.6 bar) for small systems; Low Pressure (LP, 20.7 bar, refrigerated) for large industrial systems.

✅ **Two application types**: Total Flooding (entire enclosure) and Local Application (specific hazard).

✅ **Design concentrations**:
- Class A surface & Class B (heptane): **34%**
- Deep-seated Class A & polar solvents: **50%**
- Class C (electrical): **34%** minimum

✅ **Quantity Formula (NFPA 12):** $W = \dfrac{V}{S}$ — always use **adjusted volume** (subtract impermeable + add equivalent permeable volume).

✅ **Discharge time** for total flooding: **≤ 60 seconds** (NFPA 12 limit).

✅ **Cylinder capacity**: Standard HP cylinder = **45 kg** liquid CO₂.

✅ **Pre-discharge delay** = typically **30 seconds** for occupant evacuation.

✅ **Major human hazard**: CO₂ at >10% causes unconsciousness; >17% is rapidly fatal. **Never use in normally occupied spaces** without lockout procedures.

✅ **Advantages**: Zero ODP, non-conductive, leaves no residue, low cost, suitable for deep-seated fires.

✅ **Disadvantages**: Lethal to humans, requires airtight enclosure for total flooding, requires high-pressure storage, can cause thermal shock to equipment due to cold gas.

✅ **Typical Applications**: Electrical substations, transformer rooms, server/data rooms, genset enclosures, paint spray booths, flammable liquid storage vaults, marine engine rooms.

✅ **KTU "Hot Button" Topics** for board exams: NFPA 12 formula derivation, HP vs LP comparison, total flooding quantity calculation, sequence of operation, pre-discharge delay justification.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vapourizing Liquid Type. Placement of Extinguishers. Extinguisher Rating
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT836 - DESIGN OF FIRE PROTECTION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Vapourizing Liquid Type. Placement of Extinguishers. Extinguisher Rating

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Vapourizing Liquid Type Extinguisher — Formal Definition

A **Vapourizing Liquid Type Fire Extinguisher** is a portable fire suppression device that uses a liquefied, volatile halogenated hydrocarbon agent (historically carbon tetrachloride, chlorobromomethane, or modern Halon replacements and Clean Agents such as HFC-227ea, HFC-236fa, FK-5-1-12/Novec 1230) stored under pressure. When discharged, the liquid rapidly vaporizes upon contact with ambient air and/or the fire, producing a dense, electrically non-conductive vapour cloud that extinguishes the fire primarily by **chemical flame inhibition (chain-breaking reaction)** and secondarily by **smothering (oxygen dilution)** and **cooling**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Module 3 (OESFT836)**
> Vapourizing liquid extinguishers are classified under **Class B and Class C fires** in the BIS classification (IS 2190:1992 and IS 2546). They are NOT recommended for Class A fires because they lack sufficient cooling capacity. Per the **Montreal Protocol (1987) and Kigali Amendment**, traditional Halons (1211, 1301, 2402) have been phased out in India, and only **Clean Agents** with **ODP = 0** and **GWP ≤ 1** are now specified for new installations.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Pressurised Coolant Spray"

Imagine a pressurized aerosol can of deodorant held close to a candle flame. The liquid propellant inside is at high pressure; when the nozzle opens, the liquid rushes out, instantly boils (vaporises) at atmospheric pressure, and a cold, dense white mist blankets the flame. The mist:
1. **Displaces oxygen** around the flame (smothering),
2. **Absorbs heat** during vaporisation (latent heat of vaporisation, ~150–300 kJ/kg for halogenated agents), and
3. **Interrupts the radical chain reaction** in the flame zone (the chemical magic unique to halogenated agents).

A vapourizing liquid extinguisher is simply a much larger, more rigorously engineered, and far more precisely valved version of that aerosol can, designed to suppress incipient fires without harming electronics or leaving residue.

### 1.3 Placement of Extinguishers — Formal Definition

**Placement** is the engineered decision process of determining the **type, number, capacity, and physical location** of portable fire extinguishers in a building, vehicle, or industrial process area, in compliance with **IS 2190:1992**, **NFPA 10 (2022)**, **TAC (Tariff Advisory Committee) guidelines**, and **NBC 2016 (India)**. The objective is to ensure that **any incipient-stage fire is reachable within the prescribed travel distance** by a trained occupant using the appropriate extinguisher.

### 1.4 Extingusher Rating — Formal Definition

**Extinguisher Rating** is a standardised performance classification system, established by **UL 711** (USA), **IS 2546:1994** (India), and harmonised with **EN 3-7 (Europe)**, that quantitatively specifies the **size and class of fire** a given extinguisher is certified to extinguish. Ratings use alphanumeric codes such as `1A`, `2A`, `10B`, `120B`, `3A 40B`, etc. The **number is the magnitude of the test fire** the unit successfully extinguished, and the **letter is the fire class**.

> [!NOTE]
> The word "extinguish**er**" is the standard British/Indian spelling used in KTU textbooks; the American English form is "extinguisher". Both refer to the same device.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The vapourizing liquid extinguisher operates on a three-stage **physical → thermodynamic → chemical** sequence:

1. **Stage 1 — Pressurised Storage Stage**
   The halogenated agent is stored as a **super-pressurised liquid** (typically 8–25 bar at 21 °C for Halons; 25–40 bar for HFC-227ea at 21 °C) inside a steel or aluminium cylinder. A propellant gas (N₂ or compressed air) maintains the vapour pressure above the liquid. The liquid fills roughly **50–60 % of the cylinder's internal volume** (the remainder is vapour head-space to ensure stable discharge pressure).

2. **Stage 2 — Discharge & Vaporisation Stage**
   When the valve is opened, the liquid is forced up the siphon tube and through a calibrated nozzle. Upon exiting into ambient air at 1 atm, the liquid experiences a sudden **pressure drop below its boiling point**, causing **flash vaporisation**. The latent heat required for this phase change is drawn from the surroundings → the discharge stream feels distinctly **cold** (often −40 °C at the nozzle).

3. **Stage 3 — Flame Inhibition Stage**
   The vapour cloud contacts the flame's **reaction zone** where the chain carriers are H•, OH•, O•, and HO₂• radicals. The halogenated agent (e.g., CF₃Br = Halon 1301) liberates **Br• radicals** which catalytically destroy the H• and OH• flame-propagating radicals:

   $$\text{Br}^{\bullet} + \text{RH} \longrightarrow \text{HBr} + \text{R}^{\bullet}$$
   $$\text{H}^{\bullet} + \text{HBr} \longrightarrow \text{H}_2 + \text{Br}^{\bullet}$$
   $$\text{OH}^{\bullet} + \text{HBr} \longrightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{Br}^{\bullet}$$

   The Br• is **regenerated**, making the process catalytic and highly efficient at concentrations of only **3–6 % by volume** in air.

### 2.2 Classification of Vapourizing Liquid Extinguishers

| Sub-Type | Agent Example | Discharge Mechanism | Typical Capacity | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Carbon Tetrachloride type** | CCl₄ (historical) | Pump (stirrup) | 0.5–1.0 L | Phased out (toxic, ODP=1.1) |
| **Chlorobromomethane (CB)** | CH₂ClBr | Gas cartridge | 1.0–2.5 L | Phased out (toxic vapour) |
| **Halon 1211** | CF₂ClBr | Stored pressure | 1.0–9.0 kg | Aircraft, server rooms (legacy) |
| **Halon 1301** | CF₃Br | Stored pressure | 4–250 kg | Total flooding systems |
| **HFC-227ea (FM-200)** | CF₃CHFCF₃ | Stored pressure | 4–250 kg | Data centres, archives |
| **FK-5-1-12 (Novec 1230)** | CF₃CF₂C(O)CF(CF₃)₂ | Stored pressure | 4–250 kg | Modern clean-agent system |
| **HFC-236fa (FE-36)** | CF₃CH₂CF₃ | Stored pressure | 1.5–9 kg | Portable B/C extinguishers |

### 2.3 Placement of Extinguishers — Design Parameters

Per **IS 2190:1992** and **NFPA 10**, placement is governed by five engineering parameters:

1. **Class of Fire Hazard** (Light / Ordinary / Extra — per NFPA classification).
2. **Travel Distance** — maximum walking distance from any point in the protected area to the nearest extinguisher.
3. **Mounting Height** — accessibility per anthropometric data.
4. **Visibility & Signage** — IS 1237 specifies photo-luminescent signs.
5. **Compatibility & Suitability** — electrical hazard zone (Class C), cooking oil (Class K).

**Standard Travel Distance Limits (IS 2190 / NFPA 10):**

| Hazard Class | Light Hazard | Ordinary Hazard | Extra Hazard |
|---|---|---|---|
| Class A | **22.86 m (75 ft)** | **22.86 m (75 ft)** | **22.86 m (75 ft)** |
| Class B | **9.14 m (30 ft)** | **15.24 m (50 ft)** | **15.24 m (50 ft)** |
| Class C | Based on adjacent A/B | Based on adjacent A/B | Based on adjacent A/B |
| Class D | Within **22.86 m (75 ft)** of hazard | — | — |
| Class K | Within **9.14 m (30 ft)** of cooking appliance | — | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Travel distance** is measured along the **actual path of travel** (around obstacles), NOT in a straight line. This is a frequent KTU exam trap.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Standard Reference | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Minimum A-rated water equivalent per floor area** | $N_A = 0.065 \times A_{\text{floor}}$ | NFPA 10, Table 6.2.1.1 | extinguishers |
| 2 | **Minimum B-rated capacity (per hazard)** | $N_B = \dfrac{A_{\text{low}}}{A_{\text{max}}}$ | NFPA 10, 6.3.2 | extinguishers |
| 3 | **Travel distance (max)** | $d_{\text{travel}} \le 22.86 \text{ m}$ (Class A) | IS 2190 / NFPA 10 | m |
| 4 | **Mounting height (≤ 18 kg extinguisher)** | $h_{\text{top}} \le 1.53 \text{ m}$ from floor | IS 2190 | m |
| 5 | **Mounting height (> 18 kg extinguisher)** | $h_{\text{top}} \le 1.07 \text{ m}$ from floor | IS 2190 | m |
| 6 | **Minimum bottom clearance** | $h_{\text{bottom}} \ge 0.10 \text{ m}$ (4 in) from floor | NFPA 10 | m |
| 7 | **Class A rating (water equivalent)** | $1\text{A} \equiv 1.25 \text{ gal} \equiv 4.73 \text{ L}$ of water | UL 711 / IS 2546 | — |
| 8 | **Class B rating (fire area)** | $1\text{B} \equiv 0.465 \text{ m}^2$ (5 ft²) of test fire | UL 711 / IS 2546 | m² |
| 9 | **Maximum practical B rating** | $640\text{B} \equiv 297 \text{ m}^2$ | UL 711 | m² |
| 10 | **Design concentration for total flooding (FM-200)** | $C_{\text{design}} = 7\%$ by vol | NFPA 2001 | % v/v |
| 11 | **Discharge time (vapourizing liquid)** | $t_{\text{discharge}} \le 10 \text{ s}$ (portable) | NFPA 10 | s |
| 12 | **Concentration durability (hold time)** | $t_{\text{hold}} \ge 10 \text{ min}$ | NFPA 12 / 2001 | min |

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU 2024 Scheme problems, the most-tested formulas are **(1), (3), (7), and (8)**. Memorise them with units.

### 2.5 Engineering Real-World Utility

- **Data centres & server rooms**: Vapourizing liquid (Novec 1230, FM-200) is the gold standard because it leaves **zero residue** and is **electrically non-conductive**.
- **Aviation**: Halon 1211 was mandated in aircraft cargo bays (now being replaced by HCFC Blend B / 2-BTP).
- **Pharmaceutical labs**: Solvent fires (Class B) require halon replacements for personnel safety.
- **Telecom exchanges**: Critical switching equipment → clean agent systems prevent data loss.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Number of Class A Extinguishers per Floor Area

The empirical relationship given by **NFPA 10 Table 6.2.1.1** for Class A light hazard is:

$$\boxed{N_A = 0.065 \times A_{\text{floor}}}$$

**Step-by-step explanation:**

$$\text{Step 1: Identify the floor area to be protected, } A_{\text{floor}} \text{ in m}^2.$$

$$\text{Step 2: The empirical constant } 0.065 \text{ extinguishers per m}^2 \text{ was derived from a base test of 1A extinguishers being able to cover roughly } \dfrac{1}{0.065} \approx 15.4 \text{ m}^2 \text{ each.}$$

$$\text{Step 3: Compute } N_A = 0.065 \times A_{\text{floor}}.$$

$$\text{Step 4: Round UP to the next whole number, since fractional extinguishers do not exist (conservative engineering).}$$

**Worked Numerical Example:**

A small office floor has $A_{\text{floor}} = 600 \text{ m}^2$, classified as **Ordinary Hazard Group 1**.

Using the light-hazard base formula:

$$N_A = 0.065 \times 600 = 39.0 \approx 39 \text{ extinguishers}$$

For Ordinary Hazard (per NFPA 10), the coefficient is **0.081** instead of 0.065:

$$N_A = 0.081 \times 600 = 48.6 \approx 49 \text{ extinguishers}$$

However, if 2A-rated extinguishers (each equivalent to 2 × 1A) are used, divide by 2:

$$N_A = \dfrac{48.6}{2} = 24.3 \approx 25 \text{ extinguishers}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **rating is inversely proportional to the count** — a higher-rated extinguisher reduces the total count required, but each unit is heavier and costlier. KTU problems frequently ask students to choose between cost and ergonomic placement.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Class B Fire Area Coverage

The Class B rating system is based on the **square footage of a heptane pan fire** that a non-expert operator can successfully extinguish. The relationship is:

$$1 \text{ B} \equiv 5 \text{ ft}^2 \equiv 0.465 \text{ m}^2$$

If a Class B hazard zone has total flammable liquid surface area $A_{\text{hazard}}$ (in m²), the required B-rating sum is:

$$\text{Total B required} = \dfrac{A_{\text{hazard}}}{0.465} \text{ B-units}$$

**Step-by-step worked example:**

A transformer bund area has a flammable liquid spill zone of $A_{\text{hazard}} = 46.5 \text{ m}^2$.

$$\text{Total B required} = \dfrac{46.5}{0.465} = 100 \text{ B-units}$$

This can be satisfied by:
- 1 × 100B extinguisher (large, single unit, ~120 kg), OR
- 2 × 50B extinguishers (better redundancy), OR
- 5 × 20B extinguishers (distributed placement, easier to handle)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiner's tip: always check **travel distance** after selecting the count. A single 100B unit may fail the 15.24 m (50 ft) travel constraint even though the total B-rating is satisfied.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Travel Distance Verification

Given a rectangular floor of dimensions $L \times W$, the **diagonal travel distance** from the farthest corner to the nearest corner where an extinguisher is mounted is:

$$d_{\text{travel, max}} = \sqrt{L^2 + W^2}$$

But because obstacles (machinery, walls, partitions) are present, the practical formula uses the **perimeter-based path**:

$$d_{\text{travel}} \le \sqrt{L^2 + W^2} - L_{\text{obstacle correction}}$$

**Worked Example:**

A machine hall is 30 m long × 20 m wide. A single extinguisher is placed at the **centre** of the long wall. Travel distance to the **farthest corner** (opposite, far end):

$$d = \sqrt{30^2 + 20^2} = \sqrt{900 + 400} = \sqrt{1300} \approx 36.06 \text{ m}$$

This **exceeds** the 22.86 m Class A limit → at least **2 extinguishers** must be added, located along the long axis to halve the travel distance.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Mistake**: Using straight-line Euclidean distance when the **actual path of travel** must be measured around real obstacles. Always add an "obstacle factor" of 1.1–1.3× for typical industrial layouts.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Extinguisher Type vs. Suitability

| Fire Class | Fuel Type | Best Vapourizing Liquid? | Why / Why Not |
|---|---|---|---|
| **A** | Wood, paper, cloth | ❌ No | Insufficient cooling, vapour escapes porous fuel |
| **B** | Petrol, oil, solvents | ✅ Yes (Halon/Clean Agent) | Excellent chain-breaking, no residue |
| **C** | Live electrical | ✅ Yes (only non-conductive agents) | Vapour is dielectric (>35 kV/mm) |
| **D** | Combustible metals (Mg, Na) | ❌ No | Reacts violently with halogen vapours |
| **K** | Cooking oils | ⚠️ Limited | Wet chemical is preferred; clean agent OK as backup |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Working Principle — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Pressurised Storage Cylinder\nLiquid Agent N2 Propellant 25 bar] --> B[Activation Lever Pressed]
    B --> C[Valve Opens Siphon Tube Rises]
    C --> D[Liquid Agent Discharges Through Nozzle]
    D --> E[Flash Vaporisation at 1 atm\nTemperature drops to -40 C]
    E --> F[Dense Vapour Cloud Forms]
    F --> G[Flame Zone Contact]
    G --> H[Halogen Radicals Br F Released]
    H --> I[Chain Reaction H and OH Radicals Destroyed]
    I --> J[Fire Extinguished within 10 seconds]
    F --> K[Secondary Effect Oxygen Displacement]
    F --> L[Secondary Effect Latent Heat Absorption]
    J --> M[Hold Time 10 min maintained]
```

### 4.2 Placement Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Identify Hazard Zone] --> Q1{Class of Fire?}
    Q1 -->|Class A| A1[Light Ordinary or Extra Hazard]
    Q1 -->|Class B| B1[Liquid Spill Area m squared]
    Q1 -->|Class C| C1[Check Voltage Level]
    Q1 -->|Class D| D1[Specific Metal Type]
    Q1 -->|Class K| K1[Cooking Appliance Distance]
    A1 --> A2[Travel Distance max 22.86 m]
    B1 --> B2[Travel Distance max 9.14 to 15.24 m]
    C1 --> C2[Use Non Conductive Vapourizing Liquid]
    D1 --> D2[Use Dry Powder NOT Vapourizing Liquid]
    K1 --> K2[Use Wet Chemical Primary]
    A2 --> A3[Mounting Height 1.53 m for lt 18 kg]
    B2 --> B3[Mounting Height 1.07 m for gt 18 kg]
    A3 --> A4[Signage per IS 1237 Photo Luminescent]
    B3 --> A4
    C2 --> A4
    A4 --> END[Final Placement Plan with Number and B Rating]
```

### 4.3 Extinguisher Rating Classification Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R0[Extinguisher Rating System] --> RA[Class A Rating]
    R0 --> RB[Class B Rating]
    R0 --> RC[Class C Designation]
    R0 --> RD[Class D Designation]
    R0 --> RK[Class K Designation]
    RA --> RA1[1A equals 1.25 gal water]
    RA --> RA2[2A equals 2.5 gal water]
    RA --> RA3[4A equals 5 gal water]
    RA --> RA4[6A equals 7.5 gal water]
    RB --> RB1[1B equals 0.465 m squared heptane]
    RB --> RB2[10B equals 4.65 m squared]
    RB --> RB3[40B equals 18.6 m squared]
    RB --> RB4[120B equals 55.74 m squared]
    RC --> RC1[No Numerical Rating]
    RC --> RC2[Only C Letter Designation]
    RD --> RD1[Specific to Metal Type]
    RK --> RK1[Specific to Cooking Media]
```

### 4.4 Discharge Cycle Time-Line

```mermaid

    dateFormat  X

```
