<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)**

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]** *List any three halogenated agents used in vapourizing liquid type extinguishers and state their environmental impact in terms of ODP and GWP.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Three halogenated agents used in vapourizing liquid type extinguishers are:

1. **Halon 1211 (CF₂ClBr)** — ODP = 3.0, GWP = 1300. Banned under Montreal Protocol; in service only for aviation and military.
2. **HFC-227ea (FM-200, CF₃CHFCF₃)** — ODP = 0, GWP = 3500. Modern replacement; widely used in data centres.
3. **FK-5-1-12 (Novec 1230, C₆F₁₂O)** — ODP = 0, GWP = 1. Latest generation clean agent; lowest global warming impact.

*(1 mark for each correct agent with ODP/GWP. Total 3 marks.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *Define "Extinguisher Rating". What does the designation "3A 40B" signify?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Extinguisher Rating** is a standardised performance classification that quantitatively specifies the size and class of fire a portable extinguisher is certified to extinguish, as per UL 711 and IS 2546.

The designation **"3A 40B"** signifies:
- **3A** = The extinguisher has a Class A rating of 3, meaning it has the water-equivalent suppression capacity of 3 × 1.25 = 3.75 gallons (≈ 14.2 L) of water, sufficient for an ordinary Class A fire (wood, paper).
- **40B** = The extinguisher has a Class B rating of 40, meaning it can extinguish a flammable liquid fire covering 40 × 0.465 = 18.6 m² (≈ 200 ft²) of heptane pool.

*(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for decoding 3A 40B.)*

---

### **Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks each)**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]

**(a)** *Explain the operating principle of a vapourizing liquid type fire extinguisher with a neat block diagram. Discuss why it is preferred for Class B and C fires but not for Class A fires.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *A small chemical laboratory has a Class B hazard (solvent spill area) of 18.6 m². Calculate the total B-rating required. Suggest the number of extinguishers if (i) 10B units are used, (ii) 20B units are used, and (iii) 40B units are used. State the travel distance limit applicable as per IS 2190.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Operating Principle (7 marks):**

A vapourizing liquid extinguisher consists of a **pressurised cylinder** containing a liquefied halogenated agent (e.g., HFC-227ea), a **propellant gas (N₂)** to maintain head pressure, a **siphon tube**, a **valve assembly with discharge lever**, a **pressure gauge**, and a **discharge nozzle/hose**. *(2 marks — components)*

When the lever is pressed, the valve opens and the pressurised liquid is forced up the siphon tube and out through the nozzle. Upon exiting to atmospheric pressure (1 atm), the liquid undergoes **flash vaporisation** — its boiling point is below ambient, so it rapidly boils, absorbing its **latent heat of vaporisation** from the surroundings. This produces a cold, dense vapour cloud. *(2 marks — flash vaporisation)*

The vapour enters the flame zone and **chemically interrupts combustion** by releasing halogen radicals (Br•, F•, Cl•) that catalytically destroy the flame-propagating H• and OH• radicals in a chain-breaking mechanism. Secondary effects include **oxygen dilution** (smothering) and **cooling**. *(2 marks — chemical mechanism)*

**Why preferred for B and C, not for A:** Class B fires (flammable liquids) are best attacked at the flame's radical chain, where halogen agents are extremely efficient. Class C fires (electrical) require a non-conductive medium, and halogen vapours are excellent dielectrics. Class A fires (solid combustibles like wood) require deep cooling and soaking, which halogen vapours cannot provide because they are gases and do not penetrate the porous fuel mass. *(1 mark — suitability)*

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

Given: $A_{\text{hazard}} = 18.6 \text{ m}^2$

**Step 1: Total B-rating required**
$$\text{Total B} = \dfrac{A_{\text{hazard}}}{0.465} = \dfrac{18.6}{0.465} = 40 \text{ B-units}$$ *(2 marks)*

**Step 2: Number of extinguishers for each option**

(i) Using 10B units:
$$N = \dfrac{40}{10} = 4 \text{ extinguishers}$$ *(1 mark)*

(ii) Using 20B units:
$$N = \dfrac{40}{20} = 2 \text{ extinguishers}$$ *(1 mark)*

(iii) Using 40B units:
$$N = \dfrac{40}{40} = 1 \text{ extinguisher}$$ *(1 mark)*

**Step 3: Travel distance limit per IS 2190**
For Class B hazard, the maximum travel distance is **9.14 m (30 ft) for Light Hazard** and **15.24 m (50 ft) for Ordinary/Extra Hazard**. *(1 mark)*

**Recommendation**: Two 20B extinguishers provide optimum balance of redundancy and travel distance compliance for a typical laboratory.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]

**(a)** *Discuss the various factors governing the placement of portable fire extinguishers in a building as per IS 2190. State the maximum travel distance for Class A and Class B hazards with mounting height limits.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *An office floor of 50 m × 30 m (Area = 1500 m²) is classified as Light Hazard. Calculate the number of 2A-rated water extinguishers required per NFPA 10. Verify whether 2 extinguishers placed at opposite corners satisfy the travel distance criterion.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Factors Governing Placement (7 marks):**

The placement of portable fire extinguishers as per **IS 2190:1992** is governed by the following factors:

1. **Class of Fire Hazard** — Light, Ordinary, or Extra hazard classification determines spacing and rating. *(1 mark)*
2. **Travel Distance** — Maximum walking distance from any point to the nearest extinguisher. For Class A: 22.86 m; for Class B: 9.14 m (Light) to 15.24 m (Ordinary/Extra). *(1 mark)*
3. **Mounting Height** — For extinguishers ≤ 18 kg gross weight, top must not exceed 1.53 m from floor; for > 18 kg, top must not exceed 1.07 m. Bottom clearance ≥ 0.10 m (4 in). *(1 mark)*
4. **Visibility and Signage** — Per IS 1237, photo-luminescent signs above extinguishers; not blocked by stock, machinery, or partitions. *(1 mark)*
5. **Accessibility** — Located along normal paths of egress (e.g., near exits, stairways, corridors); not inside locked cabinets. *(1 mark)*
6. **Environmental Suitability** — Temperature range, corrosive atmospheres, vibration zones. *(1 mark)*
7. **Hazard Compatibility** — Class C in electrical rooms; Class K near kitchens; Class D near combustible metals. *(1 mark)*

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

Given: $L = 50 \text{ m}$, $W = 30 \text{ m}$, $A_{\text{floor}} = 1500 \text{ m}^2$, Light Hazard, 2A-rated extinguishers.

**Step 1: Base formula for Light Hazard (per NFPA 10 Table 6.2.1.1)**
$$N_A = 0.065 \times A_{\text{floor}} = 0.065 \times 1500 = 97.5$$
Round up: $N_A = 98$ A-units required. *(2 marks)*

**Step 2: Convert to 2A-rated extinguishers**
Since each extinguisher is 2A:
$$N = \dfrac{98}{2} = 49 \text{ extinguishers}$$ *(1 mark)*

**Step 3: Travel distance check with 2 extinguishers at opposite corners**

Distance between opposite corners (diagonal):
$$d = \sqrt{50^2 + 30^2} = \sqrt{2500 + 900} = \sqrt{3400} \approx 58.31 \text{ m}$$

Maximum travel distance from a corner to the nearest extinguisher (at the opposite corner, walking around the perimeter) is essentially the **diagonal path ≈ 58.31 m**, which **violates** the 22.86 m (75 ft) Class A limit. *(2 marks)*

**Step 4: Conclusion**

2 extinguishers are grossly insufficient — both in **count (49 required)** and **travel distance (58.31 m > 22.86 m)**. The extinguishers must be distributed such that no point in the floor is more than 22.86 m from one. With 49 units, average spacing ≈ $\sqrt{1500/49} \approx 5.5$ m, well within the limit. *(2 marks)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on This Topic:**
> 1. **Confusing ODP and GWP**: Students often swap the values. ODP (Ozone Depletion Potential) applies to ozone layer damage (CFC, Halons); GWP (Global Warming Potential) applies to climate change (HFCs). Halons have high BOTH; Novec 1230 has zero ODP and ~1 GWP.
> 2. **Forgetting the rounding-up rule**: Always round UP the count of extinguishers. A fractional extinguisher does not exist.
> 3. **Euclidean vs. Path travel distance**: Using straight-line distance instead of actual walking path. Always use the **obstacle-inclusive path**.
> 4. **Mixing up Class A and B rating bases**: 1A = 1.25 gal of water, but 1B = 0.465 m² (5 ft²) of fire. These are NOT directly comparable.
> 5. **Ignoring Montreal Protocol context**: In KTU answers, always mention that traditional Halons (1211, 1301) are **phased out** in India under the 1992 Montreal Protocol ratification.
> 6. **Mounting height confusion**: 1.53 m limit applies ONLY to extinguishers ≤ 18 kg. Heavier ones must be mounted lower (1.07 m) for ergonomic accessibility.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Vapourizing Liquid Extinguisher** = halogenated agent (CCl₄, CB, Halons, HFCs, Novec 1230) that vaporises on discharge and extinguishes by **chemical chain inhibition**.
- [x] Three extinguish modes: **chemical (primary), smothering, cooling (secondary)**.
- [x] Suitable for **Class B and C** fires; **not for A, D, or K** as primary agent.
- [x] Modern preference: **Novec 1230 (ODP=0, GWP=1)** > FM-200 (ODP=0, GWP=3500) > Halons (banned).
- [x] Placement per **IS 2190** considers: fire class, travel distance, mounting height, signage, accessibility, environmental conditions.
- [x] Maximum **Class A travel distance = 22.86 m**; Class B = **9.14 m (Light)** or **15.24 m (Ord/Extra)**.
- [x] Mounting: top ≤ **1.53 m** for ≤18 kg; ≤ **1.07 m** for >18 kg; bottom ≥ 0.10 m clearance.
- [x] **1A ≡ 1.25 gal (4.73 L) water**; **1B ≡ 0.465 m² (5 ft²) heptane fire**.
- [x] Maximum B rating: **640B (297 m²)**.
- [x] Formula $N_A = 0.065 \times A_{\text{floor}}$ for Light Hazard; coefficient rises to 0.081 for Ordinary.
- [x] Discharge time ≤ **10 s**; hold time ≥ **10 min** for total-flooding systems.
- [x] Always **round UP** the number of extinguishers; consider both **count** and **travel distance** constraints.
- [x] **Diagonal travel** check: $d = \sqrt{L^2 + W^2} \le 22.86 \text{ m}$ for Class A.
- [x] Halons phased out under **Montreal Protocol (1987)** and **Kigali Amendment (2016)**; only clean agents with ODP=0 are specified for new KTU-aligned designs.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Care
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT836 - DESIGN OF FIRE PROTECTION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Care

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Fire Protection Engineering** as prescribed by the **KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned)** syllabus for the Open Elective course *OESFT836 – Design of Fire Protection Systems*, the term **"Care"** refers to the systematic, scheduled, and documented regime of **inspection, testing, maintenance, and upkeep** of all installed fire protection systems and equipment so that they remain in *fully functional and code-compliant operational readiness* throughout their design service life.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Examiner Accepted):**
> *Care of Fire Protection Systems* is the engineering discipline and management practice that ensures the continued reliability, availability, and performance of fire detection, alarm, suppression, and evacuation systems through **routine inspection, preventive maintenance, corrective maintenance, and record-keeping** in accordance with the relevant Indian Standards (**IS codes**), **National Building Code of India (NBC 2016)**, **TAC (Tariff Advisory Committee)** guidelines, and **NFPA** best practices.

The scope of "Care" under Module 3 generally covers the following fire protection subsystems:

1. **Care of Fire Hydrant Systems** (internal and external)
2. **Care of Automatic Sprinkler Systems** (wet, dry, deluge, pre-action)
3. **Care of Fire Extinguishers** (portable and wheeled)
4. **Care of Fire Detection and Alarm Systems** (conventional and addressable)
5. **Care of Fixed Fire Suppression Systems** (FM-200, CO₂, foam, water-mist)
6. **Care of Passive Fire Protection** (firestops, compartmentation, fire doors)

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Simple Analogy: The Car Service Analogy**
> Imagine you bought a brand-new car with the best safety features — airbags, ABS brakes, reverse sensors. Would you simply leave it untouched for 10 years and expect it to save your life in a crash? Of course not! You regularly service it, check the brakes, replace worn brake pads, top up brake fluid, and inspect the airbags. **Fire protection systems are the "safety brakes" of a building.** The "Care" module teaches you how to *service those brakes* — so that on the day a real fire breaks out, the system actually works.

The three pillars of **Care** can be remembered as the **"AAR" model**:

| Pillar | Meaning | Engineering Equivalent |
|--------|---------|----------------------|
| **A** – Audit | Periodic physical inspection | Visual + functional checks |
| **A** – Assurance | Testing & verification | Hydrostatic, flow, alarm tests |
| **R** – Record | Documentation | Log books, certificates, test reports |

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following **IS / NBC / NFPA** benchmark metrics are central to the *Care* module:

- **Hydrostatic Test Pressure** for hydrants: **$1.5 \times$ working pressure** (held for **2 hours**)
- **Sprinkler system working pressure**: **$1.0 \text{ bar}$ to $12 \text{ bar}$** (typical range)
- **Fire extinguisher recharge pressure** (CO₂): **$55 \text{ bar}$ to $60 \text{ bar}$** at **$21^{\circ}\text{C}$**
- **Acceptable leakage** in hydrant networks: **$\le 1.5 \text{ L/min}$ per joint**
- **Standard inspection frequency** (NBC 2016 Part 4): **Monthly** for extinguishers, **Weekly** for pumps, **Annually** for comprehensive testing

---

## 1.4 Learning Outcomes Alignment (KTU 2024 CO Mapping)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Course Outcomes addressed in this topic:**
> - **CO3:** *Understand the inspection, testing, and maintenance procedures for various fire protection systems.*
> - **CO4:** *Apply preventive maintenance schedules and documentation practices as per Indian Standards.*
> - **Bloom's Levels:** L2 (Understand), L3 (Apply), L4 (Analyse)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Framework — The Five Pillars of "Care"

The discipline of *Care* of fire protection systems is built on **five engineering pillars**. Every KTU question on this topic tests one or more of these.

### Pillar 1 — Inspection (Visual + Functional)

**Why:** Visual inspection catches **80% of deficiencies** before functional failure (industry-accepted benchmark from **NFPA 25** and **IS 15105**).

**How:**
- **Daily/Weekly:** Pump house log, valve tamper switches, pressure gauges
- **Monthly:** Hydrant valve lubrication, hose inspection, nozzle cleanness
- **Quarterly:** Flow switch testing, alarm verification
- **Annually:** Full system flush, hydrostatic tests, head-by-head sprinkler inspection

### Pillar 2 — Testing (Performance Verification)

**Why:** Confirms the system *actually works* under simulated fire conditions.

**How:**
- **Hydrostatic test** — pressurises piping to **$1.5 \times$ working pressure**
- **Flow test** — confirms the *rated discharge* at the *remote-most* hydrant
- **Pump performance test** — verifies **flow $\ge 100\%$ rated** and **pressure $\ge 65\%$ of rated shutoff head**
- **Detector response test** — uses *smoke pens* / *heat guns* for addressable detectors

### Pillar 3 — Maintenance (Preventive + Corrective)

**Why:** Prevents *deferred failure* — when components silently decay over years.

**How:**
- **Preventive Maintenance (PM):** Scheduled, planned, calendar-based
- **Corrective Maintenance (CM):** Reactive, post-failure
- **Predictive Maintenance (PdM):** Vibration analysis on pumps, thermography of electrical panels

### Pillar 4 — Documentation

**Why:** Documentation is the **legal proof of due diligence** under the **Indian Penal Code §304A**, **Factories Act 1948**, and **Kerala Fire & Rescue Services Rules**.

**How:**
- **Log Book**, **Test Certificates**, **Maintenance Register**, **Asset Register**, **As-Built Drawings**

### Pillar 5 — Training & Drills

**Why:** A perfectly maintained system fails if operators don't know how to use it.

**How:**
- **Fire Drills** — **twice a year** (NBC 2016)
- **Mock evacuation**
- **Pump operator training** under **IS 6360**

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas are in metric SI units. Memorise the *form*, not the digits.**

### Table 2.1 — Hydrostatic & Pressure Test Formulas

| Sl. | Parameter | Formula | Units | KTU Frequency |
|-----|-----------|---------|-------|---------------|
| 1 | Hydrostatic Test Pressure | $P_{test} = 1.5 \times P_{working}$ | bar | ★★★★★ |
| 2 | Maximum Allowable Leakage Rate | $Q_{leak} \le 1.5 \text{ L/min per joint}$ | L/min | ★★★★ |
| 3 | Total Test Volume Loss | $V_{loss} = Q_{leak} \times t_{test}$ | L | ★★★ |
| 4 | Pressure Drop Acceptance | $\Delta P \le 0.5 \text{ bar in 2 hours}$ | bar | ★★★★ |
| 5 | Pump Rated Flow Check | $Q_{actual} \ge 1.0 \times Q_{rated}$ | L/min | ★★★★★ |
| 6 | Pump Pressure at Rated Flow | $P_{@Q} \ge 0.65 \times P_{shutoff}$ | bar | ★★★★★ |
| 7 | Sprinkler Spacing (Light Hazard) | $S_{max} = 4.5 \text{ m}$ | m | ★★★ |
| 8 | Sprinkler Spacing (Ordinary Hazard) | $S_{max} = 4.0 \text{ m}$ | m | ★★★ |
| 9 | Extinguisher Discharge Time (Water) | $t \ge 30 \text{ s}$ | s | ★★★ |
| 10 | Extinguisher Discharge Time (CO₂) | $t \ge 12 \text{ s}$ | s | ★★★ |

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The "Care" framework is not academic — it is **directly applicable** in:

- **High-rise residential compliance audits** (Kerala RERA + KSBC rules)
- **Industrial safety audits** under **TAC** (now merged into IRDAI norms)
- **Insurance premium reduction** — Insurers offer **5–15% rebate** on Property Insurance for buildings with documented PM schedules
- **Hospital NABH accreditation**
- **IT park and SEZ fire NOC renewals** by Kerala Fire & Rescue Services

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Hydrostatic Test Pressure Calculation

This is the **most-asked numerical in KTU Module 3 exams**. Let us derive it step-by-step.

### Given (Typical KTU Numerical):
A wet riser system in a high-rise building has a **working pressure of $8 \text{ bar}$** at the top-most hydrant. Determine the **hydrostatic test pressure**, the **maximum permissible leakage**, and the **test duration**, as per **IS 6360** and **NFPA 14** standards.

### Step 1 — Recall the Code Factor

Per **IS 6360:1991** and **NFPA 14**:
$$
P_{test} = k \times P_{working}
$$

where the code factor $k = 1.5$ for hydrostatic acceptance tests on new or modified hydrant systems.

### Step 2 — Substitute Working Pressure

$$
P_{test} = 1.5 \times 8 \text{ bar}
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{test} = 12.0 \text{ bar}}
$$

### Step 3 — Apply the Leakage Criterion

Per **NFPA 25**, the **maximum permissible leakage** in a hydrant network hydrostatic test is:
$$
Q_{leak, max} = 1.5 \text{ L/min per joint}
$$

### Step 4 — Compute Total Permissible Loss over Test Duration

The test is held for **$t = 2 \text{ hours} = 120 \text{ minutes}$**.

$$
V_{loss, max} = Q_{leak, max} \times t
$$

$$
V_{loss, max} = 1.5 \text{ L/min} \times 120 \text{ min}
$$

$$
\boxed{V_{loss, max} = 180 \text{ L per joint}}
$$

### Step 5 — Pressure Drop Check

Per **IS 15105**, the gauge pressure should **NOT drop by more than $0.5 \text{ bar}$** during the 2-hour hold:
$$
\Delta P_{observed} \le 0.5 \text{ bar}
$$

If $\Delta P_{observed} > 0.5 \text{ bar}$, the system is **deemed failed**, and all joints must be re-torqued and re-tested.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Fire Pump Performance Verification

This is a **frequently asked 7-mark question**.

### Given:
A **diesel-driven fire pump** is rated at:
- **Rated flow** $Q_{rated} = 2850 \text{ L/min}$ (≈ $750 \text{ GPM}$)
- **Rated shutoff head** $H_{shutoff} = 80 \text{ m}$ (≈ $7.85 \text{ bar}$)

During an annual acceptance test, the following were observed:
- **Actual flow** $Q_{actual} = 2880 \text{ L/min}$
- **Head at rated flow** $H_{@Q} = 56 \text{ m}$

**Verify whether the pump meets acceptance criteria as per NFPA 20.**

### Step 1 — Verify Flow Criterion

$$
Q_{actual} \ge 1.0 \times Q_{rated}
$$

$$
2880 \text{ L/min} \ge 1.0 \times 2850 \text{ L/min}
$$

$$
2880 \ge 2850 \quad \checkmark \text{ (PASS)}
$$

### Step 2 — Verify Pressure Criterion at Rated Flow

$$
H_{@Q} \ge 0.65 \times H_{shutoff}
$$

$$
0.65 \times 80 \text{ m} = 52 \text{ m}
$$

$$
H_{@Q} = 56 \text{ m} \ge 52 \text{ m} \quad \checkmark \text{ (PASS)}
$$

### Step 3 — Efficiency Check (Optional Advanced)

$$
\eta_{pump} = \frac{\rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H}{P_{input}}
$$

Substituting (SI):
$$
\eta_{pump} = \frac{1000 \times 9.81 \times (2880/60) \times 56}{P_{input} \text{ (W)}}
$$

For the pump to pass, $\eta_{pump}$ should be **$\ge 65\%$** (typical fire pump acceptance benchmark).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Inspection Frequencies

> [!NOTE]
> **This matrix is the most-reproduced table in KTU Module 3 solutions.**

| Equipment / System | Daily | Weekly | Monthly | Quarterly | Annually | 5-Yearly |
|--------------------|:-----:|:------:|:-------:|:---------:|:--------:|:--------:|
| Fire Pump (Diesel/Electric) | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | — |
| Hydrant Valves | — | — | ✓ | — | ✓ | — |
| Hydrant Hoses | — | — | ✓ | — | ✓ | Hydro-test |
| Sprinkler Heads (visual) | — | — | — | — | ✓ | — |
| Sprinkler Heads (sample test) | — | — | — | — | — | 20-yr replace |
| Portable Extinguishers | — | — | ✓ | — | ✓ | Hydro-test 5y |
| CO₂ cylinders | — | — | — | — | ✓ | Hydro-test 10y |
| Smoke / Heat Detectors | — | — | — | ✓ | ✓ | — |
| Fire Alarm Panel | — | ✓ | — | ✓ | ✓ | — |
| Water Storage Tank | — | — | ✓ | — | ✓ | Clean 3y |
| Fire Doors | — | — | ✓ | — | ✓ | — |

---

## 3.4 Derivation 3 — Extinguisher Discharge Time Verification

### Given:
An **ABC powder extinguisher** has the following ratings:
- **Capacity** $V = 6 \text{ kg}$
- **Discharge rate** $r_d = 1.2 \text{ kg/s}$

### Step 1 — Compute Theoretical Discharge Time

$$
t = \frac{V}{r_d} = \frac{6 \text{ kg}}{1.2 \text{ kg/s}} = 5.0 \text{ s}
$$

### Step 2 — Compare with IS 15683 Standard

| Type | Min. Discharge Time |
|------|--------------------:|
| Water (9 L) | $\ge 30 \text{ s}$ |
| Foam (9 L) | $\ge 30 \text{ s}$ |
| CO₂ (4.5 kg) | $\ge 12 \text{ s}$ |
| ABC Powder (6 kg) | $\ge 9 \text{ s}$ |

### Step 3 — Conclusion

$$
t_{actual} = 5.0 \text{ s} < 9.0 \text{ s}
$$

**FAIL** — The extinguisher must be recharged or replaced. This is a *typical real-world failure* due to nozzle clogging or propellant leakage.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Care Lifecycle (Block-Level Flow Diagram)

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **complete Care lifecycle** of a fire protection system — from installation through decommissioning. This is a **functional architecture flow** showing the cyclic nature of maintenance activities.

```mermaid
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E

    G3 --> H

    I --> D2
    I --> D3
    I --> D4
    I --> D5
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution Note (KTU 2024 Scheme ESE):**
> - Part A: 3 questions × **3 marks each** = 9 marks (answer any 2 typically)
> - Part B: Internal choice — answer ONE of two 14-mark questions
> - Each 14-mark question typically has **part (a) — 7 marks** and **part (b) — 7 marks**

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**List and explain any three routine inspection checks to be carried out on a fire hydrant system as per IS 6360.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Visual Check of Hydrant Valves (1 mark):** Inspect for corrosion, leaks at the stem, missing caps, and ensure the valve is in *open* or *closed* position as required.

2. **Pressure Gauge Verification (1 mark):** Check that the system pressure gauge reads **within $\pm 5\%$** of the design static pressure and that the gauge glass is intact.

3. **Hose and Nozzle Inspection (1 mark):** Hoses must be free from cuts, abrasions, mildew; nozzles must be unobstructed; couplings must be free-swivelling.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**What is the significance of hydrostatic testing in fire protection systems? Mention the standard test pressure.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Significance (2 marks):** Hydrostatic testing verifies the **integrity and leak-tightness** of piping systems under pressure, ensuring that joints, welds, and valves can withstand **$1.5 \times$ the working pressure** without failure. It is mandatory before commissioning and after any major repair.
- **Standard Test Pressure (1 mark):** $P_{test} = 1.5 \times P_{working}$, held for a **minimum of 2 hours**, with pressure drop $\le 0.5 \text{ bar}$.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### **Question A `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]`**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Describe in detail the maintenance schedule for a portable fire extinguisher as per IS 15683. Include details on visual inspection, refilling intervals, and hydrostatic testing.**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Monthly Visual Inspection (2 marks):**
- Verify pressure gauge pointer in *green zone*
- Check for physical damage, corrosion, denting
- Confirm safety pin and tamper seal intact
- Verify accessibility and signage

**2. Annual Maintenance (2 marks):**
- Discharge test for partially discharged units
- Replace O-rings and valve gaskets
- Re-powder or refill to rated capacity
- Repressurise to $8 \text{ bar}$ for stored pressure type

**3. Hydrostatic Test (2 marks):**
- Conducted every **5 years** for water/foam/powder extinguishers
- Conducted every **10 years** for CO₂ cylinders
- Test pressure = **$1.5 \times$ working pressure**
- Discard if permanent deformation exceeds **$1\%$** of total volume

**4. Record-Keeping (1 mark):**
- Tag the extinguisher with date of last service
- Maintain register with serial number, location, last refill, next due date

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**A wet sprinkler system in a commercial complex has 500 sprinkler heads, working pressure of 6 bar at the system riser. Calculate the hydrostatic test pressure and outline the step-by-step test procedure.**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Calculate Test Pressure (2 marks):**
$$
P_{test} = 1.5 \times P_{working} = 1.5 \times 6 \text{ bar} = 9.0 \text{ bar}
$$

**Step 2 — Test Preparation (2 marks):**
- Isolate system from town mains and pump
- Drain the system completely
- Blank all branch lines with test blanks
- Connect hydrostatic pump to system test connection

**Step 3 — Pressurisation (1 mark):**
- Pressurise gradually in **3 stages** — $3$ bar → $6$ bar → $9$ bar
- Hold each stage for **10 minutes** for observation

**Step 4 — Hold Test (1 mark):**
- Maintain $9.0 \text{ bar}$ for **2 hours**
- Monitor pressure drop; acceptable $\Delta P \le 0.5 \text{ bar}$

**Step 5 — Acceptance / Rectification (1 mark):**
- If passed → issue **hydrostatic test certificate**
- If failed → locate leaks via soap solution, rectify, retest

---

### **Question B `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]`**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the inspection and testing procedure for a fire alarm system as per IS 2189 and NBC 2016.**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Daily Inspection (1 mark):** Check that the fire alarm panel shows *normal status* with no fault/trouble indicators; verify mains supply and battery health LEDs.

**2. Weekly Test (1 mark):** Operate **one manual call point** on a rotating basis to confirm the alarm sounds and signal reaches the panel.

**3. Monthly Inspection (2 marks):**
- Test **at least one detector of each type** (smoke, heat, multi-sensor)
- Verify **battery voltage** — should be within $\pm 10\%$ of nominal
- Check **sounders** — should produce $\ge 65 \text{ dB(A)}$ at $3 \text{ m}$

**4. Quarterly Inspection (1 mark):**
- Test **$25\%$ of detectors** using smoke pen / heat gun
- Verify **cause-and-effect matrix** with linked equipment (HVAC, lifts, doors)

**5. Annual Inspection (2 marks):**
- Test **$100\%$ of detectors**
- Calibrate addressable detectors
- Verify cable insulation resistance $\ge 1 \text{ M}\Omega$
- Test all interface relays to BMS / fire station

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Discuss the care and maintenance of a diesel-driven fire pump. Include weekly, monthly, and annual checks.**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Weekly Checks (2 marks):**
- Run pump for **30 minutes** at no-load to keep it primed
- Check fuel level $\ge 75\%$
- Inspect batteries, electrolyte level, terminal voltage
- Verify oil pressure and coolant temperature gauges

**2. Monthly Checks (2 marks):**
- Run pump at **rated flow for 1 hour** through test bypass
- Log suction and discharge pressures
- Lubricate bearings as per OEM schedule
- Inspect vibration isolators and flexible couplings

**3. Annual Checks (2 marks):**
- **Flow test** to verify $Q_{actual} \ge Q_{rated}$
- **Pressure test** at rated flow — head should be $\ge 65\%$ of shutoff head
- Replace lubricating oil and oil filter
- Calibrate pressure gauges and switches
- Inspect exhaust and intake louvers

**4. Documentation (1 mark):**
- Maintain logbook with: date, run hours, fuel consumed, anomalies, corrective action taken
- Submit quarterly report to Local Fire Officer

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ Common Mark-Deduction Traps in the "Care" Module:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the code reference** — Students lose **1 mark** if they write "test pressure is 1.5 times" without citing **IS 6360 / NFPA 14 / NFPA 25**.
> 2. **Confusing hydrostatic test with pneumatic test** — Pneumatic tests use **1.1 × working pressure**, not 1.5×. Mismatch = **−2 marks**.
> 3. **Not mentioning record-keeping** — Every Care answer must end with a line about **documentation/logbook**. Examiners specifically test this. Missing = **−1 mark**.
> 4. **Wrong frequency for extinguisher hydrotest** — Students often write "annual" — it is **5 years** (water/foam/powder) or **10 years** (CO₂).
> 5. **Skipping the "why"** — Examiners reward *justification*, not just procedure. Always add one line: *"This is necessary because..."*

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist (Print and pin on your wall!)**

- ✅ **Care = Inspection + Testing + Maintenance + Documentation + Training**
- ✅ **Hydrostatic test pressure** = $1.5 \times P_{working}$
- ✅ **Hydrostatic hold time** = 2 hours minimum
- ✅ **Acceptable leakage** = $\le 1.5 \text{ L/min per joint}$
- ✅ **Acceptable pressure drop** = $\le 0.5 \text{ bar}$
- ✅ **Fire pump acceptance:** $Q_{actual} \ge Q_{rated}$ AND $H_{@Q} \ge 0.65 \times H_{shutoff}$
- ✅ **Extinguisher hydrotest:** **5 yr** (water/foam/powder), **10 yr** (CO₂)
- ✅ **Fire drill frequency:** **twice a year** per NBC 2016
- ✅ **Weekly pump test:** 30 min no-load (diesel); monthly = 1 hr at rated flow
- ✅ **Document everything** — logbook, certificates, as-built drawings
- ✅ **Cite the code** — IS 6360, IS 2189, IS 15105, IS 15683, NBC 2016, NFPA 25
- ✅ **Always end your answer with documentation/training** — examiners reward it

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inspection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT836 - DESIGN OF FIRE PROTECTION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Inspection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Inspection** in the context of Fire Protection Engineering refers to the **systematic, periodic, and documented physical examination** of all fire protection installations—including detection, alarm, suppression, and evacuation systems—to verify their operational readiness, code compliance, and structural integrity against the approved design and applicable standards (e.g., **IS 15105, NFPA 25, NFPA 72, TAC, NBC 2016**).

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, inspection is the *proactive verification process* carried out by competent authorities (such as the **Fire and Rescue Department**, **Chartered Fire Safety Engineer**, or **Third-Party Certifying Agency**) at three statutory stages:

1. **Pre-construction Design Review** — verification of drawings and hydraulic calculations.
2. **During-construction Rough-in Inspection** — verification of concealed piping, hangers, and embedding.
3. **Post-construction / Periodic Inspection** — verification of installed systems during commissioning and throughout the operational life of the building (typically **annual**, **semi-annual**, **quarterly**, or **monthly** based on system criticality).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Health Check-up" Model

Think of a fire protection system exactly like a **human body**:

- A doctor doesn't just check your body once when you're born—they perform periodic check-ups (**annual physicals**), emergency visits (**breakdown inspections**), and specialized diagnostics (**hydrostatic tests, flow tests**).
- Similarly, a building's sprinkler system, hydrant network, and alarm panels need **routine clinical examinations** because internal corrosion, valve seizure, or pump failure can occur silently.
- Just as a skipped health check-up can lead to a sudden medical emergency, a skipped fire system inspection can lead to catastrophic loss of life and property when an actual fire occurs.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Inspection is **NOT** the same as **Testing** or **Maintenance**, though the three are interrelated:
> - **Inspection** = Visual and physical examination to identify defects.
> - **Testing** = Operational verification (e.g., running the pump, sounding the alarm).
> - **Maintenance** = Corrective action to restore the system to working order.
>
> Together, they form the **ITM Triad** (Inspection, Testing, Maintenance) — a heavily tested concept in KTU examinations.

## 1.3 Why Inspection is a Mandatory Engineering Function

| Reason | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|
| **Life Safety** | Ensures active fire systems activate within design parameters (e.g., sprinkler opens in **≤ 60 seconds**). |
| **Code Compliance** | Mandatory under **NBC 2016 (Part 4, Fire & Life Safety)**, **TAC** rules, and local **Kerala State Fire & Rescue** rules. |
| **Insurance Validity** | Insurance companies **void claims** if inspection records are not produced. |
| **Asset Protection** | Early detection of corrosion, leakage, and obstruction. |
| **Legal Liability** | Building owner/occupier has **statutory duty of care** under **Section 14 of the Kerala Fire Force Act, 2020**. |

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Key numerical thresholds used during fire protection system inspection:
> - **Hydrostatic test pressure** for sprinkler systems = **13.8 bar (200 psi)** for **2 hours**.
> - **Fire pump churn pressure** = Shut-off head + **~10%** margin tolerance.
> - **Standpipe flow test residual pressure** = **≥ 4.5 bar (65 psi)** at the hydraulically most remote outlet.
> - **Annual sprinkler obstruction investigation** mandatory when ≥ **50 heads** are present, or **≥ 20 heads** in a single riser.
> - **Alarm audibility level** = **≥ 65 dBA at the pillow level** in sleeping areas, or **≥ 75 dBA** for common areas.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework of Inspection

The theoretical basis of inspection rests on **three foundational principles** derived from **Reliability Engineering** and **Risk-Based Inspection (RBI)** methodology:

### Principle 1: Degradation Over Time
All mechanical and electrical components degrade according to a **bathtub curve**:

- **Infant Mortality Phase** (0–1 year): Manufacturing/Installation defects surface.
- **Useful Life Phase** (1–15 years): Stable, predictable wear.
- **Wear-out Phase** (15+ years): Increasing failure rate due to corrosion, fatigue, obsolescence.

### Principle 2: Defect Visibility Hierarchy
Defects exist on a **visibility spectrum** that dictates the inspection technique:

| Visibility Level | Defect Type | Inspection Technique |
|---|---|---|
| **Surface-visible** | External corrosion, missing signage | **Visual inspection** (eyes-on) |
| **Subsurface** | Pipe wall thinning, MIC (Microbial Influenced Corrosion) | **Ultrasonic thickness gauging** |
| **Internal obstruction** | Sludge, biofouling in sprinklers | **Internal obstruction investigation** |
| **Functional** | Pump failure, valve seizure | **Performance/flow testing** |
| **Electrical** | Detector sensor drift, battery failure | **Sensitivity testing, load testing** |

### Principle 3: Frequency-Determinant Logic
Inspection frequency is governed by:

$$f_{insp} = f_{base} \times k_{env} \times k_{occ} \times k_{crit}$$

Where:
- $f_{base}$ = Baseline frequency (e.g., **1 year** for sprinklers).
- $k_{env}$ = Environmental factor (coastal/corrosive = **0.5**, i.e., halve the interval).
- $k_{occ}$ = Occupancy factor (high-hazard = **0.5**).
- $k_{crit}$ = Criticality factor (life-safety system = **0.5**).

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Inspection Stage

- **Visual Inspection (Why):** Catches **80%** of deficiencies (per NFPA defect databases) at minimal cost and zero system downtime.
- **Functional Testing (Why):** Verifies that the *system* — not just individual components — operates as a unified chain (detection → notification → suppression).
- **Internal Inspection (Why):** Identifies **Microbial Influenced Corrosion (MIC)** and **obstructions** like zebra mussels, rust flakes, or mineral scale that are invisible from outside.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master Reference Table for KTU Numerical Problems on Inspection**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Standard Value / Unit | Source / Standard |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Hydrostatic Test Pressure (Sprinkler) | $P_{test} = 1.5 \times P_{work} + 5\,\text{bar}$ (or **200 psi**) | **13.8 bar** held for **2 hours** | NFPA 13, IS 15105 |
| 2 | Pressure Gauge Accuracy | $\pm\,\dfrac{1}{2}\text{ bar}$ of test pressure | Class II or better | IS 3624 |
| 3 | Annual Flow Test Discharge (Hydrant) | $Q_{test} = \dfrac{A \times v}{K}$ where $A$ = nozzle area | **≥ 900 LPM** for high-rise | IS 3844, TAC |
| 4 | Velocity Pressure (Pitot Gauge) | $v = K\sqrt{2gh_p}$ | $h_p$ in m of water | Hydraulic Eq. |
| 5 | Available Residual Pressure | $P_r = P_s \times \left(\dfrac{Q_r}{Q_s}\right)^{1.85}$ | $\geq$ **4.5 bar** | Hazen-Williams |
| 6 | Pump Acceptance Test Duration | $t = $ rated time at churn/peak flow | **30 min** churn, **5 min** at 150% flow | NFPA 20 |
| 7 | Alarm Bell Sound Level at 3m | $L_p = 20\log_{10}\left(\dfrac{p}{p_0}\right)$ | **≥ 90 dB** at 3 m | IS 2189 |
| 8 | Battery Backup Test | $t_{discharge} \geq 1.5 \times t_{rated}$ | **24 hr** for voice systems | NFPA 72 |
| 9 | Detector Sensitivity Test | $Obscuration\,\vert_{ft^{-1}} \in [0.5,\,4.0]$ | Within listed range | IS 11360 |
| 10 | Inspection Frequency Modifier | $f_{insp} = f_{base} \cdot \prod k_i$ | Range: **0.25 to 1.0** | RBI Method |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** In the table above, I have used `\vert` instead of the vertical pipe `|` to avoid breaking markdown table syntax. The same rule applies to absolute value notation in your own exam answers.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility of Inspection Theory

In actual engineering practice, RBI (Risk-Based Inspection) reduces maintenance costs by **30–40%** compared to fixed-interval inspection, while improving reliability. The **American Petroleum Institute (API) RP 580/581** framework — although developed for oil & gas — is increasingly used in fire protection of high-hazard industrial occupancies. For B.Tech-level applications, the simplified multiplier formula above is what KTU examiners expect.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Available Residual Pressure at the Most Remote Hydrant

This is a **classic KTU 14-mark derivation** that combines Hazen-Williams head loss with the discharge-flow relationship.

**Given:**
- Static (no-flow) pressure at the hydrant: $P_s$
- Total flow during test: $Q_s$ at the pump
- Desired test flow: $Q_r$ (e.g., 900 LPM)
- Hazen-Williams coefficient: $C$

**To Find:** Residual pressure $P_r$ available at the most remote hydrant at test flow.

### Step 1 — Start with Hazen-Williams head loss equation

$$h_f = \dfrac{10.67 \cdot L \cdot Q^{1.85}}{C^{1.85} \cdot D^{4.87}}$$

Where:
- $h_f$ = head loss in meters
- $L$ = pipe length in meters
- $Q$ = flow in $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$
- $D$ = internal diameter in meters

### Step 2 — Recognize that the *test* and *design* flows share the same pipe geometry

Because the pipe length $L$, diameter $D$, and roughness coefficient $C$ are **constant**, we can form a ratio of friction losses at the two different flow rates:

$$\dfrac{h_{f,r}}{h_{f,s}} = \left(\dfrac{Q_r}{Q_s}\right)^{1.85}$$

### Step 3 — Convert the static condition (zero flow) to a flow condition

The pressure drop from static to residual is **friction loss** between the supply (pump) and the test hydrant. So:

$$P_s - P_r = h_{f,r} \cdot \rho g \quad \text{(in Pa, divide by }\rho g\text{ for head)}$$

For the *supply* side at flow $Q_s$:

$$P_s - P_{r,s} = h_{f,s} \cdot \rho g$$

where $P_{r,s}$ is the residual pressure at the *same hydrant* when the system delivers $Q_s$.

### Step 4 — Combine the relations

Assuming a constant-supply source (city main or pump with flat curve approximation), the change in residual pressure between two flow conditions is:

$$P_{r} = P_{r,s} + (P_s - P_{r,s}) \cdot \left(\dfrac{Q_r}{Q_s}\right)^{1.85}$$

**This is the simplified linear-with-friction correction used in KTU board problems.**

### Step 5 — Numerical Worked Example (KTU Style)

> **Problem:** A fire pump delivers **1500 LPM** at a static (no-flow) pressure of **8 bar**. A test flow of **900 LPM** is recorded, and the residual pressure at the most remote hydrant is found to be **6.5 bar**. Determine the available residual pressure if the design demand rises to **2250 LPM**.

**Solution:**

Given:
- $P_s = 8$ bar
- $Q_s = 1500$ LPM, $P_{r,s} = 6.5$ bar
- New flow $Q_r = 2250$ LPM

$$\Delta P = P_s - P_{r,s} = 8 - 6.5 = 1.5\,\text{bar}$$

$$\dfrac{Q_r}{Q_s} = \dfrac{2250}{1500} = 1.5$$

$$P_r = P_s - \Delta P \cdot \left(\dfrac{Q_r}{Q_s}\right)^{1.85}$$

$$P_r = 8 - 1.5 \times (1.5)^{1.85}$$

$$(1.5)^{1.85} = e^{1.85 \cdot \ln 1.5} = e^{1.85 \times 0.4055} = e^{0.7502} \approx 2.117$$

$$P_r = 8 - (1.5 \times 2.117) = 8 - 3.176 = 4.824\,\text{bar}$$

**Conclusion:** The available residual pressure at design flow is **≈ 4.82 bar**, which **meets the NFPA minimum of 4.5 bar (65 psi)**. ✅ System passes inspection.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Inspection Frequencies by System Type

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The matrix below is heavily tested in KTU Part A (3-mark) questions.**

| System Component | Inspection Frequency | Specific Test Performed | Acceptance Criterion |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Sprinkler heads (visible)** | **Monthly** | Visual check for corrosion, paint, loading | No obstruction; no paint; **180°F / 68°C** orange bulbs intact |
| **Sprinkler control valves** | **Weekly** (sealed) | Visual + seal check | Seal intact, valve **fully open** |
| **Fire hydrants (above ground)** | **Semi-annually** | Flow test, lubrication | **≥ 4.5 bar residual**; smooth operation |
| **Fire hydrants (below ground)** | **Annually** | Flow test | **≥ 1.7 bar** at 950 LPM |
| **Fire pump (electric/diesel)** | **Weekly** (start), **Annually** (certified) | Run test, performance test | Starts in **≤ 30 sec**; rated flow & pressure |
| **Fire extinguishers (visual)** | **Monthly** | Pressure gauge, seal, accessibility | Gauge in green; seal intact; signage visible |
| **Fire extinguishers (maintenance)** | **Annually** | Discharge test / recharge | Pressure holds; nozzle clear |
| **Smoke detectors (functional)** | **Annually** | Smoke test / sensitivity check | Activates within **≤ 30 sec** |
| **Heat detectors (functional)** | **Annually** | Heat source test | Activates at **rated temperature ± 5°C** |
| **Manual call points (MCPs)** | **Semi-annually** | Activation test | Triggers FACP within **≤ 3 sec** |
| **Fire alarm control panel (FACP)** | **Annually** | Lamp test, battery load | All LEDs functional; battery lasts **24 hr** |
| **Sprinkler system (obstruction investigation)** | **Every 5 years** (or sooner) | Internal pipe inspection | **≤ 1/3 pipe diameter** obstruction |
| **Water storage tanks (fire)** | **Annually** (level), **5 years** (cleaning) | Level, structural, water quality | **Full capacity**; no sediment |

## 3.3 Worked Example: Inspection Frequency for a Coastal Hotel

**Scenario:** A 5-star resort hotel in **Kovalam, Kerala** (coastal, high-humidity, Group A-2 occupancy).

Determine the inspection frequency for the sprinkler system.

**Step 1 — Baseline frequency** $f_{base} = 1$ year (per NFPA 25 / IS 15105).

**Step 2 — Environmental factor** (coastal = corrosive salt atmosphere):
$$k_{env} = 0.5$$

**Step 3 — Occupancy factor** (high-rise, sleeping risk = critical):
$$k_{occ} = 0.5$$

**Step 4 — Criticality factor** (life-safety, evacuation of 500+ guests):
$$k_{crit} = 0.5$$

**Step 5 — Combined frequency**:
$$f_{insp} = 1 \times 0.5 \times 0.5 \times 0.5 = 0.125\,\text{year}$$

$$f_{insp} = 0.125 \times 12 = 1.5\,\text{months} \approx \textbf{every 6 weeks}$$

**Conclusion:** Sprinkler visual inspection should be performed **approximately every 6 weeks**, with internal obstruction investigation every **2.5 years** instead of 5. ✅

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: The Inspection Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Trigger Event: Scheduled Date or Deficiency Report] --> B{Initial Desk Review}
    B --> C[Verify Previous Inspection Records]
    B --> D[Check Approved Design Documents]
    C --> E[Field Inspection Mobilization]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Visual Inspection Phase]
    F --> G[Components Checked: Pipes Valves Heads Signage]
    F --> H[Environmental Check: Corrosion Blockage Tampering]
    G --> I[Functional Testing Phase]
    H --> I
    I --> J[System Activation Test]
    I --> K[Flow and Pressure Measurement]
    I --> L[Alarm and Detector Sensitivity Test]
    J --> M[Deficiency Classification]
    K --> M
    L --> M
    M --> N{Deficiency Found?}
    N --> O[Yes: Tag as Critical Impairment or Non-Critical]
    N --> P[No: Issue Compliance Certificate]
    O --> Q[Notify Owner and Authority Having Jurisdiction]
    Q --> R[Schedule Corrective Maintenance]
    R --> S[Re-Inspection After Rectification]
    S --> T{Compliance Achieved?}
    T --> U[Yes: Close Out and Archive Records]
    T --> V[No: Escalate to Enforcement Action]
    P --> U
    U --> W[Update Asset Management Database]
    W --> X[Schedule Next Inspection Cycle]
```

## 4.2 Inspection Decision Tree — Deficiency Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Deficiency Identified During Inspection] --> B{Does it prevent system activation?}
    B -- YES --> C[CRITICAL IMPAIRMENT]
    B -- NO --> D{Does it reduce system reliability below 90 percent?}
    D -- YES --> E[MAJOR NON-CRITICAL DEFICIENCY]
    D -- NO --> F[MINOR DEFICIENCY OR OBSERVATION]
    C --> G[Immediate Shutdown or Red-Tag Notice]
    G --> H[Rectify within 24 to 48 hours]
    H --> I[Re-Inspect before Re-energizing]
    E --> J[Rectify within 30 days]
    F --> K[Rectify within 90 days or next scheduled service]
    J --> L[Update Compliance Log]
    K --> L
    I --> L
```

## 4.3 System-Wide Inspection Coverage Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DETECT["DETECTION SUBSYSTEM"]
        D1[Smoke Detectors]
        D2[Heat Detectors]
        D3[Manual Call Points]
        D4[Flame Detectors]
    end
    subgraph NOTIFY["NOTIFICATION SUBSYSTEM"]

        N2[Hooters and Bells]

    end

        S1[Sprinkler Network]
        S2[Fire Hydrants]
        S3[Fire Pumps]
        S4[Water Storage Tanks]
    end
    subgraph EVAC["EVACUATION SUBSYSTEM"]
        E1[Exit Signage]
        E2[Emergency Lighting]
        E3[Fire Doors]
        E4[Stairwell Pressurization]
    end
    INSP[FIRE PROTECTION INSPECTION AUTHORITY] --> DETECT
    INSP --> NOTIFY
    INSP --> SUPPRESS
    INSP --> EVAC
    DETECT --> NOTIFY
    NOTIFY --> SUPPRESS
    SUPPRESS --> EVAC
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** These Mermaid block diagrams use purely alphanumeric node IDs (e.g., `DETECT`, `SUPPRESS`) and avoid reserved keywords like `end` or `graph` as standalone labels, ensuring smooth rendering on GitHub, VS Code preview, and Mermaid Live Editor.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper Set A]
**Define the term "Inspection" as applied to fire protection systems. Differentiate it from "Testing" and "Maintenance" with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Inspection** is the systematic physical examination of fire protection installations to determine their condition and operational readiness.
>
> - **Inspection:** Visual and physical check, e.g., checking that sprinkler control valves are **fully open** and seals are intact.
> - **Testing:** Operational verification of performance, e.g., conducting an **annual flow test** on a fire hydrant to verify discharge ≥ 900 LPM at ≥ 4.5 bar residual pressure.
> - **Maintenance:** Corrective action to restore function, e.g., **replacing a corroded** sprinkler head or **recharging** a discharged fire extinguisher.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Retest Paper]
**List any six components that must be visually inspected during a routine sprinkler system inspection.**

**Model Answer (any six):**
1. Sprinkler heads — for corrosion, paint, and obstruction.
2. Control valves — for open position and unbroken seal.
3. Pipe hangers and supports — for damage or detachment.
4. Pressure gauges — for normal system pressure reading.
5. Water flow alarm devices — for free movement.
6. Identification signage — for visibility and accuracy.
7. Spare sprinkler cabinet — for correct quantity and temperature rating.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern, students answer **one full question from Module 3**, with internal choice between **Question A and Question B**. Both are provided below.

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Jul 2024, Hypothetical Paper]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **inspection procedure for a fire hydrant system** as per **IS 3844 / NFPA 25**. Include the frequency, items checked, and acceptance criteria. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A water-based fire protection system is installed in a **coastal IT park in Thiruvananthapuram**. Using a **Risk-Based Inspection (RBI) approach**, calculate the **revised inspection frequency** for the sprinkler system, given baseline $f_{base} = 12$ months and the following factors: environmental $k_{env} = 0.5$ (corrosive coastal climate), occupancy $k_{occ} = 0.75$ (medium-hazard Group B), and criticality $k_{crit} = 0.5$ (life-safety essential). Comment on whether the result satisfies NBC 2016 requirements. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Fire Hydrant Inspection Procedure:**

1. **Frequency:** Semi-annual full inspection; **monthly** visual check; **annual** flow test.
2. **Items Inspected:**
   - Above-ground hydrant: Body, valves, couplings, caps, chains.
   - Below-ground hydrant: Pit condition, valve seat, top section alignment.
3. **Acceptance Criteria:**
   - Static pressure: As per design (typically **7–10 bar**).
   - Flow test: **≥ 900 LPM at 4.5 bar residual** for high-rise.
   - Outlet threads: Free of damage; matching local fire service couplings (**63 mm instantaneous**, as used by Kerala Fire Force).
4. **Documentation:** Inspection tag, test certificate, and water-flow test graph.

**Part (b) — RBI Frequency Calculation:**

$$f_{insp} = f_{base} \times k_{env} \times k_{occ} \times k_{crit}$$

$$f_{insp} = 12 \times 0.5 \times 0.75 \times 0.5$$

$$f_{insp} = 12 \times 0.1875 = 2.25\,\text{months}$$

**Final Answer:** The sprinkler system must be inspected **every 2.25 months (≈ 9 weeks)**.

**Comment:** This revised frequency satisfies NBC 2016 Part 4 Clause 4.6, which mandates *"periodic inspection at intervals not exceeding 3 months for high-risk occupancies"*. ✅

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2023, Retest Paper]**

**(a)** Describe the **internal obstruction investigation** procedure for a sprinkler system. Under what conditions is it **mandated**? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A fire pump is tested during annual inspection. The following data is recorded:
- Churn (no-flow) pressure: **12 bar**
- Rated flow: **1800 LPM** at **10.5 bar**
- Test flow during inspection: **1500 LPM** at **11.0 bar** residual pressure at the most remote hydrant.
- Static pressure at the hydrant: **12.5 bar**

Determine the **available residual pressure** at the **design demand of 2250 LPM**. State whether the system passes inspection as per NFPA 25. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Internal Obstruction Investigation:**

The procedure involves physically opening the system at strategic points (e.g., end-of-line fittings, inspector's test connections) to inspect pipe interiors for:
- Rust, scale, or sediment buildup.
- Microbiologically Influenced Corrosion (MIC).
- Foreign materials (construction debris, gravel, etc.).

**Conditions for Mandate (NFPA 25 §14.3.1):**
- Sprinkler system ≥ **50 years old** AND not previously investigated.
- Presence of **raw water sources** (well, pond, river).
- History of **pin-hole leaks** in the system.
- Obstruction of **two or more sprinklers** with foreign material.
- Whenever the **pump or water supply** has been out of service for **> 1 year**.

**Part (b) — Residual Pressure Calculation:**

Using the simplified relation from Section 3.1:

$$P_r = P_s - (P_s - P_{r,s}) \cdot \left(\dfrac{Q_r}{Q_s}\right)^{1.85}$$

Given:
- $P_s = 12.5$ bar
- $P_{r,s} = 11.0$ bar at $Q_s = 1500$ LPM
- $Q_r = 2250$ LPM

$$\Delta P = 12.5 - 11.0 = 1.5\,\text{bar}$$

$$\dfrac{Q_r}{Q_s} = \dfrac{2250}{1500} = 1.5$$

$$(1.5)^{1.85} = 2.117 \quad \text{(as derived earlier)}$$

$$P_r = 12.5 - 1.5 \times 2.117 = 12.5 - 3.176 = 9.32\,\text{bar}$$

**Conclusion:** Available residual pressure at 2250 LPM = **9.32 bar**, which is **well above the 4.5 bar NFPA minimum**. ✅ The system **passes inspection**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Inspection with Testing:** Many students use these interchangeably. Always state clearly: Inspection is *observation*, Testing is *operation*, Maintenance is *correction*.
> 2. **Forgetting Units:** Pressure in **bar** vs **psi** vs **kPa** is a frequent 1-mark deduction. Use SI units (bar) for KTU papers.
> 3. **Missing RBI Formula Application:** Examiners award full marks only when the product of all **three** factors is explicitly shown.
> 4. **No Code Reference:** Always cite the relevant **IS / NFPA / NBC** standard number in your answer. This fetches 1–2 grace marks.
> 5. **Skipping Hazen-Williams Exponent:** The exponent is **1.85** (not 2.0). Using 2.0 (Darcy-Weisbach approximation) loses 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Inspection in Fire Protection Systems**

- ✅ **ITM Triad:** Inspection → Testing → Maintenance (3 distinct activities).
- ✅ **Three statutory stages:** Design review → Rough-in → Periodic.
- ✅ **Baseline frequency:** Weekly (valves), Monthly (sprinklers), Quarterly (alarms), Annually (flow tests, hydrostatic), 5-yearly (obstruction investigation).
- ✅ **NFPA 25** is the global gold standard for water-based fire system inspection.
- ✅ **Hazen-Williams exponent = 1.85** — memorize it.
- ✅ **Hydrostatic test = 200 psi (13.8 bar) for 2 hours** for sprinkler systems.
- ✅ **Residual pressure at most remote hydrant ≥ 4.5 bar (65 psi)** at design flow.
- ✅ **RBI formula:** $f_{insp} = f_{base} \times k_{env} \times k_{occ} \times k_{crit}$.
- ✅ **Deficiency hierarchy:** Critical Impairment → Major Non-Critical → Minor/Observation.
- ✅ **Documentation is mandatory** — Inspection tag, certificate, and test report are legal records.
- ✅ **Coastal Kerala = corrosive environment** — always halve inspection intervals.
- ✅ **Authority Having Jurisdiction (AHJ)** = typically the District Fire Officer under the Kerala Fire & Rescue Services.
- ✅ **Audibility standard:** **65 dBA** at pillow in sleeping rooms; **75 dBA** in public areas.
- ✅ **Battery backup for FACP:** Must sustain **24 hours** (voice systems) or **15 minutes** (general alarm) on loss of mains.
- ✅ **Internal obstruction investigation** is mandatory if: ≥ 50 heads exist, raw water supply, MIC suspected, or 2+ blocked sprinklers found.
- ✅ **NBC 2016, Part 4** governs inspection obligations for all occupancies in India.
- ✅ **Deficiency rectification windows:** Critical = 24–48 hours; Major = 30 days; Minor = 90 days.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: and Maintenance of Portable Extinguishers as per Relevant Indian Standards Specifications. Automatic Water Sprinkler System - Risk Categories
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OESFT836 - DESIGN OF FIRE PROTECTION SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** and Maintenance of Portable Extinguishers as per Relevant Indian Standards Specifications. Automatic Water Sprinkler System - Risk Categories # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Portable Fire Extinguishers — Inspection & Maintenance Framework ### Formal Definition A **Portable Fire Extinguisher** is a first-aid fire-fighting device, manually operated, having a mass not exceeding **23 kg** when charged, intended to be carried and used by a single operator to combat incipient-stage fires. Inspection and maintenance refer to the systematic set of checks, tests, and servicing procedures prescribed under the **Indian Standard IS 2190:2010** (Selection, Installation and Maintenance of Portable First-Aid Fire Extinguishers) and the **TAC (Tariff Advisory Committee) guidelines**, which together constitute the legal and technical compliance framework for extinguisher upkeep in India. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** For board examinations, students must remember the exact IS code numbers, the three-tier maintenance schedule (Monthly, Quarterly, Annual, and Pressure Test), and the discharge duration limits for each extinguisher class. ### Conceptual Analogy — The "First Aid Kit" for Buildings Imagine a hospital's first-aid kit. It is *portable*, *immediately accessible*, *simple enough for a layperson to use*, and *designed only for the first few minutes of a medical emergency* — not for full surgery. A portable fire extinguisher is exactly this for a building. Just as the hospital nurse inspects the first-aid kit every month to ensure the bandages are sterile and the medicines are within their expiry date, the **fire and safety officer** inspects extinguishers on a strict schedule so that the moment a fire breaks out, the device works flawlessly. The Indian Standards (IS 2190, IS 2546) act as the **"doctor's prescription"** dictating what to check, how often, and what to do if something is wrong. ### Key Terminology & Standard Metrics - **Charged Extinguisher:** An extinguisher filled with the extinguishing medium and pressurized with the propellant (typically **Nitrogen** at 10–15 bar). - **Discharge Duration:** The minimum time for which an extinguisher must continuously discharge, ranging from **8 seconds (1 kg CO₂)** to **60 seconds (50 kg trolley)**. - **Effective Discharge Range:** The horizontal distance the jet covers, between **1.5 m (1 kg CO₂)** to **5 m (water/foam)**. - **Hydrostatic Pressure Test:** Conducted at **1.5 times the maximum working pressure** (or 30 bar for CO₂ cylinders) every 5 years. --- ## 1.2 Automatic Water Sprinkler System — Risk Categories ### Formal Definition An **Automatic Water Sprinkler System** is a network of pipes installed at the ceiling level of a building, fitted with thermally-sensitive **sprinkler heads** that open automatically at a predetermined temperature, discharging water over the fire area to achieve simultaneous detection, alarm, and suppression. The **Risk Categories** are classifications of the protected occupancy based on the fire load, commodity type, and storage height, defined under **IS 15105:2002 (equivalent to NFPA 13)** and the **TAC Fire Insurance Regulations**. > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Mandate:** Students must clearly distinguish the four primary risk categories — **Light Hazard (LH), Ordinary Hazard Group 1 (OH1), Ordinary Hazard Group 2 (OH2), and Extra High Hazard (EHH)** — and recall their respective design densities and area of operation. ### Conceptual Analogy — The "Nervous System" of a Building Think of a skyscraper as a human body. The fire alarm pull stations are the **pain receptors** (you must touch them to know you're hurt). The smoke detectors are the **nose** (smelling trouble). But a sprinkler system? It is the **immune system on autopilot** — distributed everywhere, ever-vigilant, and triggered *only* at the exact location of injury. Now imagine the immune system deciding how aggressively to fight. A small cut (paper fire in an office) needs a gentle white-blood-cell response — that's **Light Hazard**. A deep infection (flammable liquid warehouse) needs a massive, full-body immune response — that's **Extra High Hazard**. The **Risk Category** is essentially the building's *medical threat level* that pre-determines how much water, how many sprinklers, and what pipe sizes are needed. ### Key Terminology & Standard Metrics - **Light Hazard (LH):** Design density **2.25 mm/min** (0.05 gpm/ft²) over **72 m²**. - **Ordinary Hazard Group 1 (OH1):** Design density **5.0 mm/min** (0.10 gpm/ft²) over **72 m²**. - **Ordinary Hazard Group 2 (OH2):** Design density **5.0 mm/min** (0.10 gpm/ft²) over **144 m²**. - **Extra High Hazard (EHH):** Design density **12.5 mm/min** (0.25 gpm/ft²) or higher over **260 m²** (process) / **260–300 m²** (storage). - **Sprinkler Temperature Rating:** Standard ratings are **57°C, 68°C, 79°C, 93°C, 141°C, 182°C, 227°C, 260°C** (color-coded glass bulbs). # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Portable Extinguisher Inspection & Maintenance — Theoretical Breakdown The maintenance philosophy is built on a **time-tiered inspection hierarchy** combined with **performance verification tests**. Each tier addresses a different failure mode. ### Tier 1: Monthly Visual Inspection - **Why:** Catches the most common failures (vandalism, missing seals, pressure loss) before an emergency. - **How:** - Verify the extinguisher is in its designated location, unobstructed, and visible. - Check the **pressure gauge** needle is in the green zone. - Inspect for physical damage, corrosion, leakage, or clogged nozzle. - Verify the **tamper seal** and safety pin are intact. - Read the **inspection tag** for the previous month. ### Tier 2: Quarterly Maintenance Inspection - **Why:** Detects slow-developing issues (powder settling, valve sluggishness) that monthly checks cannot. - **How:** - Invert the extinguisher to ensure powder flow. - Check the discharge hose and nozzle for blockages. - Weigh CO₂ extinguishers (if weight loss > 10%, refill). - Verify the **discharge duration** meets the standard. ### Tier 3: Annual Maintenance - **Why:** Required by IS 2190 to maintain manufacturer warranty and ensure functional reliability. - **How:** - Complete discharge and refill (or replace as applicable). - Repressurize with Nitrogen. - Replace the **sealing washer (gasket)**. - Internal inspection of the cylinder for corrosion or pitting. - Reissue the **inspection card** with date, agency, and signature. ### Tier 4: Periodic Pressure Test - **Why:** Every pressure vessel is subject to metal fatigue, wall thinning, and stress corrosion. - **How:** - **CO₂ cylinders (seamless):** Hydraulic test every **5 years** at **250 bar** (test pressure) with a 30-second hold. - **Dry powder / Foam cylinders (welded):** Hydraulic test every **3 years**. - **Water-type extinguishers (stored pressure):** Hydraulic test every **5 years**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Coaching Tip:** Examiners often frame a question as: *"An ABC powder extinguisher shows green pressure gauge but no discharge. Diagnose."* — Answer: The powder has likely **caked/compacted** at the bottom. This is why **quarterly shaking/inversion** is mandatory. --- ## 2.2 Sprinkler System Risk Categories — Theoretical Breakdown The risk classification determines **five interlinked design parameters**: water density, area of operation, pipe sizing, water supply duration, and sprinkler spacing. ### Light Hazard (LH) - **Typical Occupancies:** Offices, schools, hospitals, libraries, museums, residential dwellings. - **Fire Load:** Low — predominantly Class A fuels (paper, wood, fabric). - **Design Goal:** Suppress the fire until evacuation is complete. - **System Type:** Typically **wet pipe** with small orifice sprinklers. ### Ordinary Hazard Group 1 (OH1) - **Typical Occupancies:** Bakeries, canneries, dairies, electronics manufacturing, laundries, restaurant service areas. - **Fire Load:** Moderate — limited quantities of flammable liquids or plastics. - **Design Goal:** Suppress and control with moderate water application. ### Ordinary Hazard Group 2 (OH2) - **Typical Occupancies:** Automobile showrooms, machine shops, printing presses, repair garages, woodworking shops. - **Fire Load:** Moderate-to-high — larger amounts of combustible stock. - **Design Goal:** Higher area of operation; longer duration of supply. ### Extra High Hazard (EHH) - **Subdivisions:** EHH-1 (process), EHH-2 (storage), EHH-3 (baled cotton), EHH-4 (roll paper). - **Typical Occupancies:** Petrochemical plants, paint stores, warehouse storage of Class IV commodities, plastic storage. - **Fire Load:** Very high — rapid fire growth, high heat release rates. - **Design Goal:** Suppress large, fast-developing fires; may require **ESFR (Early Suppression Fast Response)** sprinklers. ### Design Density Determination — The Core Concept The **design density** is *not* a measurement of the actual fire; it is a *prescribed* value the system must be capable of delivering. The principle is: $$\text{Water Flow Rate} = \text{Design Density} \times \text{Area of Operation}$$ The hydrant system and tank capacity must then sustain this flow for the required duration (typically **60–90 minutes**). --- ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | **Parameter** | **Formula / Standard** | **Units** | **Applicable Standard** | |---|---|---|---| | Extinguisher discharge duration (water) | $t \geq 30$ s for $\le 9$ L; $t \geq 60$ s for trolley | seconds | IS 2546 | | CO₂ cylinder hydrostatic test pressure | $P_{test} = 1.5 \times P_{work}$ (typically **250 bar**) | bar | IS 7285 | | Dry Powder test interval | Every **3 years** | years | IS 2190 | | CO₂ / Water / Foam test interval | Every **5 years** | years | IS 2190 | | Sprinkler Design Density (LH) | $d = 2.25$ mm/min | mm/min | IS 15105 / TAC | | Sprinkler Design Density (OH1/OH2) | $d = 5.0$ mm/min | mm/min | IS 15105 / TAC | | Sprinkler Design Density (EHH) | $d = 12.5$ mm/min or higher | mm/min | IS 15105 / TAC | | Total Water Demand (Sprinkler) | $Q = d \times A$ | L/min | Derived | | Maximum Sprinkler Spacing (LH) | $S_{max} = 4.5$ m | m | IS 15105 | | Maximum Sprinkler Spacing (OH/EHH) | $S_{max} = 4.0$ m (or 3.0 m for high rack) | m | IS 15105 | | Water Supply Duration (LH/OH) | $T = 60$ minutes | minutes | TAC | | Water Supply Duration (EHH) | $T = 90$ minutes | minutes | TAC | | Tank Capacity | $V = Q \times T$ | litres | Derived | > [!NOTE] > **Conversion Note:** $1 \text{ mm/min} = 2.36 \text{ gpm/ft}^2$ and $1 \text{ m}^2 \approx 10.76 \text{ ft}^2$. In exams, the SI unit is the standard. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked-Out Numerical: Sprinkler System Water Demand Calculation ### Problem Statement A **2-storey automobile service garage** is to be protected by an automatic wet-pipe sprinkler system. The protected floor area of one storey is **1500 m²** (Length = 50 m, Width = 30 m). Using the **TAC / IS 15105** risk classification: 1. Identify the correct risk category. 2. Determine the design density and the hydraulically most remote area of operation. 3. Calculate the total water flow rate required from the sprinkler system. 4. Determine the required duration and minimum **fire water storage tank capacity** in litres. ### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1 — Risk Classification Identification** An automobile service garage stores motor oils, rubber, plastics, and petroleum products in moderate quantities. Per IS 15105 / TAC: - It is **not** Light Hazard (involves flammable liquids). - It fits **Ordinary Hazard Group 2 (OH2)** criteria. Therefore: **Risk Category = OH2** **Step 2 — Design Parameters for OH2** From the standard (IS 15105 / TAC table): - Design density: $d = 5.0 \text{ mm/min}$ - Area of operation (hydraulically remote area): $A = 144 \text{ m}^2$ **Step 3 — Water Flow Rate Calculation** The fundamental formula relating flow, density, and area is: $$Q = d \times A$$ Substituting the values: $$ \begin{aligned} Q &= 5.0 \text{ mm/min} \times 144 \text{ m}^2 \\ Q &= 720 \text{ mm} \cdot \text{m}^2 / \text{min} \end{aligned} $$ Converting millimetres to litres (since 1 mm over 1 m² = 1 litre): $$Q = 720 \text{ L/min}$$ **Step 4 — Duration Determination** For OH2, the standard water supply duration per TAC is: $$T = 60 \text{ minutes}$$ **Step 5 — Tank Capacity Calculation** The required water storage volume is: $$V = Q \times T$$ Substituting: $$ \begin{aligned} V &= 720 \text{ L/min} \times 60 \text{ min} \\ V &= 43200 \text{ litres} \\ V &= 43.2 \text{ kL} \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > In practice, an additional **Hose Stream Demand** of approximately **500–1000 L/min** must be added for the fire brigade. For KTU board exams, the standard is to state: *"Plus 2 hose streams of 5 m³/h each for fire brigade use."* So, the practical tank capacity becomes **~50 kL** to be safe. --- ## 3.2 Worked-Out Numerical: Extinguisher Pressure Test Verification ### Problem Statement A **stored-pressure water (gas cartridge) type extinguisher** of capacity **9 litres** has a working pressure of **10 bar**. As per IS 2190, what is the hydrostatic test pressure, and how often must the test be performed? ### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1 — Identify Extinguisher Type and Test Interval** - Type: Water (stored pressure or gas cartridge). - Test Interval per IS 2190: **Every 5 years** for water-type extinguishers. **Step 2 — Determine Test Pressure** The standard formula is: $$P_{test} = 1.5 \times P_{work}$$ Substituting: $$ \begin{aligned} P_{test} &= 1.5 \times 10 \text{ bar} \\ P_{test} &= 15 \text{ bar} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Interpretation** - The cylinder is filled with water and pressurized to **15 bar** at the test house. - Pressure is held for **30 seconds** with no permanent deformation or leakage. - The cylinder is then **stamped** with the test date, agency code, and next due date. - If any leak, drop in pressure > 5%, or visible deformation is observed, the cylinder is **condemned**. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Risk Categories at a Glance | **Criterion** | **Light Hazard (LH)** | **OH1** | **OH2** | **EHH** | |---|---|---|---|---| | Design Density (mm/min) | **2.25** | **5.0** | **5.0** | **12.5 or more** | | Area of Operation (m²) | **72** | **72** | **144** | **260–300** | | Sprinkler Spacing (m²) | Up to **21** | Up to **15** | Up to **12** | Up to **9** (or 7.5) | | Water Supply Duration (min) | **60** | **60** | **60** | **90** | | Typical Pipe Class | **Class 1 (Light)** | **Class 2 (Medium)** | **Class 3 (Heavy)** | **Class 4 (Extra Heavy)** | | Common Occupancy | Office, School | Bakery, Dairy | Garage, Workshop | Petrochemical, Warehouse | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner Insight:** A very common board question is to give an occupancy (e.g., *"a textile mill"*) and ask for the risk category. The trick is that **textile mills with high storage of finished goods = EHH**, but a **small tailoring shop = OH1**. Always look at *fire load* and *storage height*, not just the type of business. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture — Portable Extinguisher Inspection Workflow This diagram maps the operational hierarchy of inspection activities as mandated by IS 2190, showing how each tier feeds into the next. ```mermaid graph TD A[IS 2190 Maintenance Regime] --> B[Tier 1 Monthly Visual] A --> C[Tier 2 Quarterly Check] A --> D[Tier 3 Annual Service] A --> E[Tier 4 Hydrostatic Test] B --> B1[Check pressure gauge] B --> B2[Verify tamper seal and pin] B --> B3[Inspect physical damage] B --> B4[Log in inspection register] C --> C1[Shake or invert powder type] C --> C2[Check hose and nozzle] C --> C3[Weigh CO2 cylinders] D --> D1[Complete discharge and refill] D --> D2[Replace gaskets] D --> D3[Internal cylinder inspection] D --> D4[Repressurize with nitrogen] E --> E1{Cylinder type?} E1 -->|CO2 seamless| E2[Test every 5 years at 250 bar] E1 -->|Powder welded| E3[Test every 3 years at 1.5x Pwork] E1 -->|Water stored pressure| E4[Test every 5 years at 1.5x Pwork] ``` --- ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Sprinkler System Risk Classification Flow This diagram represents the decision logic an engineer follows to classify an occupancy into the correct risk category under IS 15105 / TAC. ```mermaid graph TD Start([New Project]) --> Q1{What is the
combustible load?} Q1 -->|Low - paper, wood, fabric| Q2{Is occupancy
residential or office?} Q1 -->|Moderate - limited liquids| Q3{Manufacturing or
process work?} Q1 -->|High - bulk storage or chemicals| EHH[Extra High Hazard EHH] Q2 -->|Yes| LH[Light Hazard LH] Q2 -->|No - commercial| OH1[Ordinary Hazard Group 1 OH1] Q3 -->|Light process - bakery, laundry| OH1 Q3 -->|Heavier process - garage, printing| Q4{Storage height
and density?} Q4 -->|Up to 3.6 m, moderate| OH2[Ordinary Hazard Group 2 OH2] Q4 -->|Tall racks, high density| EHH LH --> D1[Design density 2.25 mm/min
Area 72 sq m] OH1 --> D2[Design density 5.0 mm/min
Area 72 sq m] OH2 --> D3[Design density 5.0 mm/min
Area 144 sq m] EHH --> D4[Design density 12.5 mm/min
Area 260 to 300 sq m] ``` --- ## 4.3 Schematic — Sprinkler Head Anatomy (Functional Block) ```mermaid graph TD S[Sprinkler Head Assembly] --> Def[Deflector Plate] S --> Frame[Brass or Lead Frame Body] S --> Bulb[Glass Bulb Heat Sensor] S --> Orifice[Orifice and Seal] S --> Thread[Threaded Connection to Pipe] Bulb -->|Rated 57 to 260 deg C| Trigger[Heat Absorption] Trigger -->|Glass shatters| Open[Orifice Opens] Open --> Jet[Water Jet Strikes Deflector] Jet --> Spray[Uniform Spray Pattern] ``` --- ## 4.4 Schematic — Fire Water Storage Tank Sizing Logic ```mermaid graph LR A[Risk Category Selection] --> B[Lookup Design Density d] A --> C[Lookup Area of Operation A] B --> D[Compute Flow Q = d x A] C --> D D --> E[Lookup Duration T = 60 or 90 min] E --> F[Compute Tank Volume V = Q x T] F --> G[Add Hose Stream Demand] G --> H[Final Tank Capacity in kL] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Similar]` **List and briefly explain the four tiers of maintenance prescribed by IS 2190 for portable fire extinguishers.** **Model Answer:** The four-tier maintenance schedule under **IS 2190:2010** is as follows: 1. **Monthly Visual Inspection:** Conducted by the building's fire warden or trained employee. Checks the pressure gauge (green zone), tamper seal, safety pin, accessibility, and physical condition. Logged in the inspection register. 2. **Quarterly Maintenance Check:** A more detailed examination by a competent person. Includes inverting the extinguisher to prevent powder compaction, checking hose and nozzle for blockages, weighing CO₂ cylinders to detect leakage (>10% weight loss mandates refill). 3. **Annual Servicing:** Performed by an authorized agency. Complete discharge, internal inspection, gasket replacement, recharging with the extinguishing medium, and repressurization with nitrogen. A new inspection card is issued. 4. **Periodic Hydrostatic Pressure Test:** Mandatory every **3 years** for welded cylinders (dry powder/foam) and every **5 years** for seamless cylinders (CO₂) and stored-pressure water types. Test pressure = **1.5 × working pressure**, held for 30 seconds. Cylinder is stamped with the test date if it passes. --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Similar]` **What is the difference between Light Hazard and Extra High Hazard sprinkler classifications? State one example occupancy for each.** **Model Answer:** The difference between Light Hazard (LH) and Extra High Hazard (EHH) sprinkler classifications is summarized below: - **Light Hazard (LH):** Low fire load, primarily Class A combustibles (paper, wood, fabric). The system is designed to support occupant evacuation only, requiring a lower water density. **Design density = 2.25 mm/min**, area of operation = **72 m²**, water supply duration = **60 min**. *Example: Office building, school, hospital, library.* - **Extra High Hazard (EHH):** Very high fire load, involving flammable liquids, high-stack storage, plastics, or combustible liquids. The system must suppress large, rapidly growing fires. **Design density = 12.5 mm/min or higher**, area of operation = **260–300 m²**, water supply duration = **90 min**. *Example: Petrochemical plant, high-rack warehouse storing Class IV commodities, paint manufacturing facility.* The fundamental distinction lies in the **fire load**, **combustion rate**, and the **expected heat release rate**, which determine the volume of water the system must deliver to the most demanding area. --- ## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) ### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Model 2024 Scheme]` **An old warehouse storing cotton bales to a height of 6 m is to be protected by an automatic sprinkler system.
