The protected area is 2000 m² (50 m × 40 m).** **(a)** Identify the risk category under IS 15105 / TAC and justify your selection. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Calculate the design water flow rate (L/min) and the minimum required fire water storage tank capacity (litres). Add 2 hose streams of 5 m³/h each for fire brigade use. **(7 marks)** #### Part (a) Model Solution The warehouse stores **cotton bales to a height of 6 m**. This is a high-stack storage scenario with combustible Class A materials. - It is **not Light Hazard** (storage height and fire load are too high). - It is **not Ordinary Hazard** (storage exceeds typical OH limits). - Per **IS 15105 / TAC** classification, baled cotton storage falls under **Extra High Hazard (EHH)** — specifically EHH-3 (baled cotton). For EHH-3 (Baled Cotton), the standard design parameters are: - **Design density:** $d = 12.5 \text{ mm/min}$ (this is the minimum for EHH, but for high racks, increased densities up to 20–25 mm/min may be specified; we use the prescribed minimum for design). - **Area of operation:** $A = 260 \text{ m}^2$ (for process / EHH-1; EHH storage may require 300 m²). - **Water supply duration:** $T = 90 \text{ minutes**. Justification: Cotton bales have a **high fire load density (in MJ/m²)**, are prone to **deep-seated smouldering fires**, and the **6 m storage height** produces rapid vertical fire spread. The system must therefore deliver a high water density over a large area for an extended duration to penetrate and suppress the fire. #### Part (b) Model Solution **Step 1 — Sprinkler flow rate:** $$ \begin{aligned} Q_{sprinkler} &= d \times A \\ Q_{sprinkler} &= 12.5 \text{ mm/min} \times 260 \text{ m}^2 \\ Q_{sprinkler} &= 3250 \text{ L/min} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Hose stream demand:** $$ \begin{aligned} Q_{hose} &= 2 \times 5 \text{ m}^3/\text{h} \\ Q_{hose} &= 10 \text{ m}^3/\text{h} \\ Q_{hose} &= 10000 \text{ L/h} \\ Q_{hose} &= \frac{10000}{60} \text{ L/min} \\ Q_{hose} &= 166.67 \text{ L/min} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Total flow rate:** $$ \begin{aligned} Q_{total} &= Q_{sprinkler} + Q_{hose} \\ Q_{total} &= 3250 + 166.67 \\ Q_{total} &= 3416.67 \text{ L/min} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Tank capacity (using 90-minute duration):** $$ \begin{aligned} V &= Q_{total} \times T \\ V &= 3416.67 \text{ L/min} \times 90 \text{ min} \\ V &= 307500 \text{ litres} \\ V &= 307.5 \text{ kL} \end{aligned} $$ **Final Answer:** The sprinkler system requires **3250 L/min**, and the **fire water storage tank must be approximately 3,07,500 litres (308 kL)** including hose stream demand. --- ### Question B (Alternative for Internal Choice) `[KTU University Exam - Model 2024 Scheme]` **(a)** Explain in detail the inspection and maintenance procedure of **CO₂ type portable fire extinguishers** as per IS 2190 and IS 15222. Mention the hydrostatic test schedule clearly. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Differentiate between **stored-pressure and gas-cartridge (external pressure) type** water extinguishers. State which one is preferred for modern installations and why. **(7 marks)** #### Part (a) Model Solution **CO₂ Extinguisher Maintenance Procedure:** 1. **Identification:** The extinguisher body is colour-coded **signal red** (IS 15222 specifies the body colour, with a **black band** indicating CO₂). Capacity: 1 kg, 2 kg, 3 kg, 4.5 kg, 6.5 kg, 9 kg, 22.5 kg, and 45 kg (trolley). 2. **Monthly Inspection:** - Verify the extinguisher is in the designated location and accessible. - Inspect the horn (discharge nozzle) for cracks or blockages. - Check the high-pressure cylinder for any external damage, corrosion, or pitting. - Verify the **safety pin and tamper seal** are intact. - **Weight check:** Weigh the extinguisher and compare with the stamped weight on the cylinder. A weight loss exceeding **10%** indicates leakage and mandates immediate refill. 3. **Quarterly Inspection:** - Discharge a small quantity (1–2 seconds) into a test environment to verify the valve mechanism functions smoothly. - Check the flexible hose (if present) for cracks. - Verify the **horn and expansion nozzle** are not blocked with ice (which can form during discharge due to adiabatic expansion). 4. **Annual Maintenance:** - Complete discharge of the CO₂ into the open atmosphere (NOT indoors — CO₂ is an asphyxiant). - Internal inspection of the cylinder valve. - Replacement of the **valve washer (gasket)** and O-rings. - Recharging with fresh, high-purity CO₂ (≥ 99.5% as per IS 7285). - Re-pressurization is not required since CO₂ itself is stored in liquid form at its own vapour pressure (~58 bar at 21°C). 5. **Hydrostatic Test (per IS 7285):** - **Frequency:** Every **5 years** for seamless CO₂ cylinders. - **Test Pressure:** **250 bar** (held for 30 seconds). - **Procedure:** Cylinder is filled with water, pressurized using a hydraulic pump, and held. No drop in pressure, no permanent expansion, and no visible leakage = PASS. - **Stamping:** Cylinder is stamped with the test date, agency code, and the next due date. Cylinders that fail are **condemned and destroyed** (cannot be reused). 6. **Disposal:** Cylinders older than **20 years** or those that fail the pressure test are condemned as per the Gas Cylinder Rules. #### Part (b) Model Solution **Comparison Table:** | **Criterion** | **Stored-Pressure Type** | **Gas-Cartridge Type** | |---|---|---| | Pressure Source | Propellant (N₂) gas + extinguishing medium mixed in the same chamber | Separate **CO₂ cartridge** inserted at the top; pressurizes the medium on demand | | Pressure Gauge | Required (visible gauge at all times) | Not required (pressure is only when cartridge is pierced) | | Visual Indication of Charge | Yes — gauge needle | Indirect — must weigh the cartridge or check expiry | | Inspection Difficulty | Easy — instant visual check via gauge | Difficult — must physically inspect the cartridge | | Risk of Leakage | Higher (continuous pressure) | Lower (no pressure until activated) | | Discharge Mechanism | Press lever → valve opens → gas pressure expels medium | Press lever → cartridge plunger pierces CO₂ bulb → gas rushes into chamber → medium expelled | | Use in Harsh Environments | Less suitable (gauge damage, pressure loss) | **More suitable** for cold storage, outdoor sites | | Recharging | Requires compressor, regulated gas cylinder, weighing scale | Requires a new CO₂ cartridge (cheap and easy) | | Modern Preference | **YES — preferred** for office, commercial, and public spaces | Used in industrial / marine / military applications | **Preferred Type:** The **stored-pressure type** is preferred for modern installations because: - It allows **instant visual verification** of pressure status (gauge reading), which is critical for monthly IS 2190 inspections. - It has a **faster response** (no cartridge piercing delay). - It is **simpler to use** under panic conditions (single lever operation). - It is **easier to train** laypersons to operate and inspect. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark-Loss Areas:** > 1. Students often confuse the test interval for CO₂ cylinders. **Remember: 5 years for CO₂, but 3 years for welded dry powder / foam cylinders.** A 14-mark question may explicitly test this. > 2. In numerical problems, students forget to **convert mm/min to L/min** correctly. 1 mm over 1 m² = 1 litre, so $d \times A$ directly gives L/min. > 3. When asked for "minimum tank capacity", students sometimes forget to **add hose stream demand**. Always read the question carefully. > 4. In risk classification, students incorrectly classify any warehouse as EHH. A small, low-storage warehouse is **OH2**, not EHH. Always check **storage height** and **commodity type**. > 5. For CO₂ extinguishers, students state the colour as "red" only. Per IS 15222, the body is **signal red with a black band** identifying it as CO₂. > 6. Annual maintenance does **not** mean monthly. Annual = once every 12 months by an authorized agency. Monthly = in-house visual check by trained staff. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - 🔹 **IS 2190** is the master code for selection, installation, and maintenance of portable first-aid fire extinguishers in India. - 🔹 **IS 2546** specifies the design and construction requirements for portable extinguishers. - 🔹 **IS 15222** defines the colour-coding scheme: signal red with bands (black for CO₂, cream for foam, French blue for dry powder, etc.). - 🔹 **IS 7285** governs CO₂ seamless cylinder manufacture and testing. - 🔹 **IS 15105** is the Indian equivalent of NFPA 13 for automatic sprinkler systems. - 🔹 The **four maintenance tiers** are: Monthly visual → Quarterly check → Annual service → Hydrostatic test. - 🔹 **Hydrostatic test pressure = 1.5 × working pressure**, held for 30 seconds. - 🔹 **Test intervals:** CO₂ = 5 years; Welded powder/foam = 3 years; Water = 5 years. - 🔹 CO₂ cylinder weight loss > 10% = refill immediately. - 🔹 **Risk Categories:** LH (2.25 mm/min, 72 m²), OH1 (5.0, 72), OH2 (5.0, 144), EHH (12.5, 260–300). - 🔹 **Water supply duration:** LH/OH = 60 min; EHH = 90 min. - 🔹 The formula $Q = d \times A$ is the heart of sprinkler hydraulic design. - 🔹 Tank capacity: $V = Q \times T$ (plus hose stream demand). - 🔹 **Sprinkler head temperature ratings** (color-coded bulbs): 57°C (orange), 68°C (red), 79°C (yellow), 93°C (green), 141°C (blue), 182°C (mauve), 227°C (black), 260°C (black). - 🔹 **ESFR (Early Suppression Fast Response)** sprinklers are now standard in modern EHH warehouses. - 🔹 Always cite the **relevant IS standard** in your answer to fetch full marks. - 🔹 The **hydraulically most remote area** is used for design — not the largest area of the building.

topics.title: Requirement and Source of Water Supply
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT836 - DESIGN OF FIRE PROTECTION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Requirement and Source of Water Supply

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Water Supply Requirement (as per KTU 2024 / NBC 2016 & IS 13039):**
> The quantified volumetric demand of water — expressed in **litres per minute (LPM)** or **cubic metres per hour (m³/hr)** — that must be reliably available at adequate residual pressure to operate all fire-fighting installations (sprinklers, hydrants, hose reels, monitors) simultaneously for a stipulated minimum duration, without compromising domestic or process consumption.

In fire protection engineering, the **water requirement** is **not** a single number; it is a *function* of:

$$\text{Water Demand} = f(\text{Occupancy Hazard}, \text{Built-up Area}, \text{Number of Simultaneous Operations}, \text{Duration})$$

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Bank Account of Fire Water"

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine your fire-water tank is a **"piggy bank" reserved only for emergencies**.
> - **Deposit (Source):** You fill it from a municipal line, a bore-well, a river, or a reservoir.
> - **Withdrawal Rate (Demand):** During a fire, hydrants and sprinklers will *withdraw* water at a fixed high rate (e.g., 4500 LPM).
> - **Time Lock (Duration):** The fire may last 2–4 hours. The "piggy bank" must hold enough money to survive this *without going broke*.
> - **Rule:** Deposit ≥ Withdrawal × Duration. Otherwise, the fire "bankrupts" you.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Standard Value (NBC / TAC) |
| :--- | :--- |
| **1 US Gallon** | **3.785 litres** |
| **1 m³ of water** | **1000 litres** ≈ **264.17 gallons** |
| **Static Pressure at Hydrant** | **≥ 5.5 bar (80 psi)** minimum |
| **Residual Pressure at Hydrant** | **≥ 3.5 bar (50 psi)** minimum |
| **Standard Sprinkler Discharge** | **60 LPM (≈ 15 GPM) per head** |
| **Hose Stream (Standard 63 mm)** | **450–900 LPM** |
| **Standard Duration of Supply** | **2 hours (low hazard) to 4 hours (high hazard)** |
| **Fire Pump Capacity Range** | **900 LPM to 7500 LPM** |

### 1.4 Categories of Water Demand in a Building

A fire-protection engineer must calculate three *parallel* water demands:

1. **Fire-fighting demand** (Hydrants + Sprinklers + Monitors)
2. **Domestic demand** (drinking, washing, flushing)
3. **Process / Industrial demand** (cooling, manufacturing)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The *fire water storage tank is exclusively reserved for fire fighting*. Under no circumstances is fire-water used for domestic supply — this is mandated by **NBC 2016 Part 4, Clause 4.6** and **TAC (Tariff Advisory Committee)** regulations.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Occupancies by Water Demand

KTU follows the **TAC / IS 9668** classification which categorises buildings into **three hazard groups**:

| Hazard Class | Examples | Sprinkler Density (LPM/m²) | Hose Stream (LPM) | Duration |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Light Hazard (LH)** | Offices, Hospitals, Schools, Hotels | **2.5** | **250** | **1–2 hrs** |
| **Ordinary Hazard Group 1 (OH-1)** | Bakeries, Garages, Laundries | **5.0** | **500** | **2 hrs** |
| **Ordinary Hazard Group 2 (OH-2)** | Showrooms, Workshops, Libraries | **5.0** | **750** | **2 hrs** |
| **Ordinary Hazard Group 3 (OH-3)** | Paint shops, Plastic processing | **5.0–7.5** | **1000** | **2 hrs** |
| **Extra Hazard (EH)** | Chemical plants, Solvent storage | **12.5–25.0** | **1500** | **3–4 hrs** |

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Demand Calculation

**Step 1 — Identify the Hazard Classification** based on occupancy and stored commodities.

**Step 2 — Calculate Sprinkler Demand:**
$$\text{Sprinkler Demand} = \text{Area of Operation} \times \text{Density}$$

**Step 3 — Add Hose Stream Allowance** (typically 250–1500 LPM based on hazard).

**Step 4 — Add Hydrant Demand:**
$$\text{Hydrant Demand} = n_{\text{hydrants}} \times q_{\text{per hydrant}}$$

**Step 5 — Add Standby Demand** (the *largest* of the above plus 250 LPM for "unknown unknowns").

**Step 6 — Multiply by Duration** to obtain **storage volume.**

### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (HIGH-YIELD)

> [!NOTE]
> Master these equations — they constitute **~70% of numerical questions** in KTU ESE for this module.

| # | Formula | Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $V_{\text{tank}} = Q_{\text{total}} \times t$ | Storage volume (L) = Total flow (LPM) × Duration (min) |
| 2 | $Q_{\text{sprinkler}} = A_{\text{design}} \times d$ | Sprinkler flow (LPM) = Design area (m²) × Density (LPM/m²) |
| 3 | $Q_{\text{hydrant}} = N \times 450$ | Hydrant flow = Number × 450 LPM per hydrant |
| 4 | $H_f = \dfrac{4.52 \cdot Q^{1.85}}{C^{1.85} \cdot d^{4.87}} \cdot L$ | Hazen-Williams head loss (SI units: Q in LPM, d in cm, L in m) |
| 5 | $P_{\text{residual}} = P_{\text{static}} - H_f$ | Pressure drop equation |
| 6 | $Q = A \times v$ | Continuity equation (pipe flow) |
| 7 | $P = \rho g h$ | Hydrostatic pressure |
| 8 | $\text{Reynolds} = \dfrac{v d}{\nu}$ | Flow regime check |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Municipal Corporations** use these calculations to size **underground static water tanks** for high-rise townships.
- **Industrial Safety Officers (CISF / Fire Services)** verify these designs during **statutory audits** before granting operating licenses.
- **Insurance Premiums** are *directly proportional* to the adequacy of water supply — better supply ⇒ lower premium.
- **Architects & MEP Consultants** co-ordinate **plumbing, structural, and electrical** design based on tank capacity.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Sprinkler Demand Calculation

> **Problem:** An office building is classified as **Light Hazard**. The sprinkler system is designed to cover an *area of operation* of **72 m²** with a **density of 2.5 LPM/m²**. Calculate:
> (a) Sprinkler demand in LPM
> (b) Total demand including hose stream allowance of 250 LPM
> (c) Effective storage tank capacity for a **2-hour** fire.

#### Part (a) — Sprinkler Demand

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_{\text{sprinkler}} &= A_{\text{design}} \times d \\
Q_{\text{sprinkler}} &= 72 \, \text{m}^2 \times 2.5 \, \text{LPM/m}^2 \\
Q_{\text{sprinkler}} &= 180 \, \text{LPM}
\end{aligned}$$

*Explanation:* Each square metre of the design area requires 2.5 litres of water every minute. For 72 m², we multiply the area by the density.

#### Part (b) — Total Demand

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_{\text{total}} &= Q_{\text{sprinkler}} + Q_{\text{hose}} \\
Q_{\text{total}} &= 180 + 250 \\
Q_{\text{total}} &= 430 \, \text{LPM}
\end{aligned}$$

*Explanation:* Sprinklers and hose streams operate *simultaneously*. The hose stream allowance is added to the sprinkler demand.

#### Part (c) — Storage Volume

$$\begin{aligned}
V &= Q_{\text{total}} \times t \\
V &= 430 \, \text{LPM} \times (2 \times 60) \, \text{min} \\
V &= 430 \times 120 \\
V &= 51\,600 \, \text{litres} \\
V &= 51.6 \, \text{m}^3
\end{aligned}$$

*Explanation:* Storage volume is the rate of withdrawal multiplied by the time the fire is expected to last. Since the duration is in hours, we convert to minutes (2 hours = 120 min).

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — High-Rise Hydrant Network

> **Problem:** A 12-storey commercial complex requires a hydrant system. Assume:
> - Number of landing valves = **12** (one per floor)
> - Discharge per hydrant = **450 LPM**
> - Simultaneously operating hydrants = **3** (top 3 floors worst case)
> - Hose stream allowance = **250 LPM**
> - Duration = **2 hours**

#### Solution

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_{\text{hydrant}} &= N \times q_{\text{per hydrant}} \\
Q_{\text{hydrant}} &= 3 \times 450 \\
Q_{\text{hydrant}} &= 1350 \, \text{LPM}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_{\text{total}} &= Q_{\text{hydrant}} + Q_{\text{hose}} \\
Q_{\text{total}} &= 1350 + 250 \\
Q_{\text{total}} &= 1600 \, \text{LPM}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{tank}} &= 1600 \times (2 \times 60) \\
V_{\text{tank}} &= 1600 \times 120 \\
V_{\text{tank}} &= 192\,000 \, \text{L} \\
V_{\text{tank}} &= 192 \, \text{m}^3
\end{aligned}$$

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Hazen-Williams Head Loss in Ring Main

> **Problem:** A **150 mm diameter** ring main supplies water at a rate of **2250 LPM**. The pipe material is **Cast Iron** with Hazen-Williams coefficient **C = 100**. Length of pipe = **500 m**. Calculate the head loss.

Using the SI-form Hazen-Williams formula:

$$\begin{aligned}
H_f &= \dfrac{4.52 \cdot Q^{1.85}}{C^{1.85} \cdot d^{4.87}} \cdot L \\
H_f &= \dfrac{4.52 \times (2250)^{1.85}}{(100)^{1.85} \times (15)^{4.87}} \times 500
\end{aligned}$$

Now evaluate stepwise:

$$\begin{aligned}
(2250)^{1.85} &\approx 1\,938\,150 \\
(100)^{1.85} &\approx 7\,080 \\
(15)^{4.87} &\approx 1\,120\,000
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
H_f &= \dfrac{4.52 \times 1\,938\,150}{7\,080 \times 1\,120\,000} \times 500 \\
H_f &= \dfrac{8\,760\,438}{7\,929\,600\,000} \times 500 \\
H_f &\approx 0.001105 \times 500 \\
H_f &\approx 0.55 \, \text{metres}
\end{aligned}$$

*Explanation:* This low head loss confirms the chosen pipe diameter is **adequate**. If $H_f$ had exceeded **3–4 m**, we would need to upsize the main.

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Selecting a Water Source

| Criterion | Municipal Supply | Bore Well | River/Lake | Overhead Tank | UG Sump |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Reliability** | Medium | Medium | Medium | High (local) | High (gravity) |
| **Cost** | Low | Medium | High (pumping) | Low | Low |
| **Capacity** | Unlimited (if pipe size OK) | 10–100 m³/day | Unlimited | 5–50 m³ typical | 50–500 m³ typical |
| **Quality** | Treated | Hard water | Filter needed | Stored | Stored |
| **Preferred For** | Towns, offices | Rural industries | Large plants | Daily top-up | **Primary fire tank** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Source-to-Nozzle Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Primary Water Sources] --> B[Underground Static Tank]
    B1[Municipal Main] --> B
    B2[Bore Well] --> B
    B3[River or Lake] --> B
    B4[Rainwater Harvest Pit] --> B
    B --> C[Fire Pumps - Diesel and Electric]
    C --> C1[Jockey Pump - Pressure Maintenance]
    C --> C2[Electric Motor Pump - Main Supply]
    C --> C3[Diesel Engine Pump - Standby]
    C --> D[Ring Main Distribution Loop]
    D --> E[Sprinkler Riser]
    D --> F[Hydrant Riser]
    D --> G[Monitor Riser]
    E --> E1[Individual Sprinkler Heads]
    F --> F1[Landing Valves per Floor]
    G --> G1[Monitors at Terrace]
    H[Overhead Tank Domestic] -.top up.-> B
    I[Fire Services Inlet] --> D
```

### 4.2 Typical Fire-Water Storage Hierarchy (Block Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LEVEL_A[Level A - Primary Source]
        S1[Municipal 150 mm CI Main]
        S2[Bore Well 10 HP Submersible]
        S3[Lake or Reservoir Intake]
    end
    subgraph LEVEL_B[Level B - Storage]
        T1[Underground Static Tank Capacity 192 m3]
    end
    subgraph LEVEL_C[Level C - Pumping]
        P1[Jockey Pump 450 LPM at 30 m head]
        P2[Main Electric Pump 1600 LPM at 70 m head]
        P3[Diesel Standby Pump 1600 LPM at 70 m head]
        P4[Booster Pump at Terrace if above 60 m]
    end
    subgraph LEVEL_D[Level D - Distribution]
        D1[150 mm Ring Main Around Building]
        D2[Sprinkler Risers 100 mm]
        D3[Hydrant Risers 150 mm]
        D4[Monitor 63 mm at Terrace]
    end
    S1 --> T1
    S2 --> T1
    S3 --> T1
    T1 --> P1
    T1 --> P2
    T1 --> P3
    P1 --> D1
    P2 --> D1
    P3 --> D1
    D1 --> D2
    D1 --> D3
    D1 --> D4
    D1 --> P4
```

### 4.3 Reliability Redundancy Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Fire Event Begins] --> Q1{Pressure Drops Below 4 Bar?}
    Q1 -->|No| M1[System Stays in Standby]
    Q1 -->|Yes| A1[Jockey Pump Starts Immediately]
    A1 --> Q2{Pressure Restored?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| M1
    Q2 -->|No after 10 sec| A2[Main Electric Pump Auto Start]
    A2 --> Q3{Electricity Available?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| R1[Supply at 1600 LPM]
    Q3 -->|No| A3[Diesel Pump Auto Start within 30 sec]
    A3 --> R1
    R1 --> Q4{Fire Demand Higher than Pump Capacity?}
    Q4 -->|Yes| X1[Activate Standby Pump or Additional Source]
    Q4 -->|No| Z[Fire Suppression Proceeds]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1: List any four sources of water supply for fire-fighting installations. **[3 Marks]**
`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> The four principal sources of fire-water supply are:
> 1. **Municipal water supply** — public piped network from corporation/council.
> 2. **Private wells / bore wells** — dedicated for industrial premises.
> 3. **Natural sources** — rivers, lakes, ponds, canals (treated with strainers and silt pits).
> 4. **Stored water** — underground sumps, overhead tanks, reservoirs exclusively reserved for fire fighting.
>
> *Auxiliary sources* include sea water (coastal refineries), rainwater harvesting pits, and reclaimed/recycled treated water.

---

#### Question 2: What is the standard duration of water supply for (i) Light Hazard and (ii) Extra Hazard occupancies? **[3 Marks]**
`[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> As per **TAC guidelines** and **NBC 2016, Part 4**:
> - **(i) Light Hazard** (offices, hospitals, schools): **Minimum duration = 1 to 2 hours**
> - **(ii) Extra Hazard** (chemical plants, oil refineries): **Minimum duration = 3 to 4 hours**
>
> The duration is selected based on the time required for **fire brigade arrival**, **evacuation**, and **complete extinguishment**.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]`

> **(a)** Explain in detail the **classification of occupancies** as per TAC and the corresponding **water supply requirements** for each category. **[7 Marks]**
>
> **(b)** A warehouse of total floor area **3000 m²** is classified as **Ordinary Hazard Group 2**. The sprinkler system is to be designed for an area of operation of **72 m²** with density **5.0 LPM/m²**. The hose stream allowance is **750 LPM** and the duration of supply is **2 hours**.
> Calculate:
> (i) Sprinkler demand
> (ii) Total water demand
> (iii) Effective storage tank capacity required
> **[7 Marks]**

---

##### **Model Solution — Part (a)**

The **Tariff Advisory Committee (TAC)** and **IS 9668** classify buildings based on the *combustibility* and *quantity of goods stored* into:

| Class | Description | Examples |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Light Hazard (LH)** | Low combustible load, mainly Class A | Offices, Hospitals, Schools |
| **Ordinary Hazard 1 (OH-1)** | Moderate combustible | Bakeries, Garages, Canteens |
| **Ordinary Hazard 2 (OH-2)** | Higher combustible, some Class B | Workshops, Libraries, Showrooms |
| **Ordinary Hazard 3 (OH-3)** | Significant Class B liquids | Paint shops, Plastic units |
| **Extra Hazard (EH)** | High risk, flammable liquids / gases | Refineries, Solvent tanks |

**Water Requirements (TAC norms):**

| Hazard | Sprinkler Density (LPM/m²) | Hose Stream (LPM) | Duration (hr) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| LH | 2.5 | 250 | 1 |
| OH-1 | 5.0 | 500 | 2 |
| OH-2 | 5.0 | 750 | 2 |
| OH-3 | 7.5 | 1000 | 2 |
| EH | 12.5 to 25.0 | 1500 | 3 to 4 |

The **total water demand** governs the sizing of pumps, storage tank, ring mains, and risers. Higher hazard classes require **larger diameters**, **higher pump heads**, and **longer fire-fighting durations**.

##### **Model Solution — Part (b)**

**(i) Sprinkler Demand:**

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_{\text{sprinkler}} &= A_{\text{design}} \times d \\
Q_{\text{sprinkler}} &= 72 \times 5.0 \\
Q_{\text{sprinkler}} &= 360 \, \text{LPM}
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) Total Water Demand:**

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_{\text{total}} &= Q_{\text{sprinkler}} + Q_{\text{hose stream}} \\
Q_{\text{total}} &= 360 + 750 \\
Q_{\text{total}} &= 1110 \, \text{LPM}
\end{aligned}$$

**(iii) Storage Tank Capacity:**

$$\begin{aligned}
V &= Q_{\text{total}} \times t \\
V &= 1110 \times (2 \times 60) \\
V &= 1110 \times 120 \\
V &= 133\,200 \, \text{litres} \\
V &= 133.2 \, \text{m}^3
\end{aligned}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students often forget to **multiply the area of operation (72 m²) — NOT the total floor area (3000 m²) — by the density**. The 3000 m² is a distractor! Only the *most hydraulically remote* design area (usually 72–139 m²) governs the sprinkler demand. Also remember to convert duration from **hours to minutes** before multiplying.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
`[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]`

> **(a)** Describe in detail the **various sources of water supply** for fire-protection systems. Discuss the merits and demerits of each. **[7 Marks]**
>
> **(b)** An industrial building has the following loads:
> - Sprinkler demand = **1500 LPM**
> - Hydrant demand (4 hydrants simultaneously) = **1800 LPM**
> - Monitor demand = **900 LPM**
> Calculate the **maximum fire-water demand** if all systems can operate simultaneously, and the **minimum storage tank capacity** required for a **3-hour** duration. **[7 Marks]**

---

##### **Model Solution — Part (a)**

The four major sources of fire-water supply are:

**1. Municipal / Public Water Supply**
- *Merits:* Continuous, treated, unlimited supply; minimal infrastructure cost.
- *Demerits:* Pressure fluctuations during peak hours; can fail during earthquakes/floods; reliant on corporation.

**2. Bore Wells / Private Wells**
- *Merits:* Independent of municipal failure; suitable for remote sites.
- *Demerits:* Yield may deplete over years; requires periodic testing; quality may be hard.

**3. Natural Sources (Rivers, Lakes, Ponds)**
- *Merits:* Unlimited capacity; ideal for large industrial plants.
- *Demerits:* Requires intake wells, strainers, silt pits; year-round availability not guaranteed; pumping cost is high.

**4. Stored Water (Underground Sump / Overhead Tank)**
- *Merits:* Always available, gravity-fed when elevated, sized per requirement.
- *Demerits:* Needs large footprint; quality must be maintained (algae, stagnation); refilling system must be reliable.

> **Hybrid arrangement:** Most modern projects use a *combination* — underground static tank as primary, municipal line as replenishment, and a natural lake/river as **emergency tertiary backup**.

##### **Model Solution — Part (b)**

**Step 1 — Maximum Fire-Water Demand (simultaneous operation):**

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_{\text{total}} &= Q_{\text{sprinkler}} + Q_{\text{hydrant}} + Q_{\text{monitor}} \\
Q_{\text{total}} &= 1500 + 1800 + 900 \\
Q_{\text{total}} &= 4200 \, \text{LPM}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Minimum Storage Tank Capacity:**

$$\begin{aligned}
V &= Q_{\text{total}} \times t \\
V &= 4200 \times (3 \times 60) \\
V &= 4200 \times 180 \\
V &= 756\,000 \, \text{litres} \\
V &= 756 \, \text{m}^3
\end{aligned}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Two common mistakes in this type of problem:
> 1. **Assuming only one system operates at a time** — fire codes mandate *worst-case simultaneous* operation.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert hours to minutes.** Since $Q$ is in LPM, the duration *must* also be in minutes. Writing $V = 4200 \times 3$ gives $12\,600$ litres — a 60× error!
> 3. **Not stating the simultaneous assumption** — always write "Assuming simultaneous operation as per TAC guidelines."

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Storage Volume Formula:** $V = Q \times t$ (with consistent units: LPM × minutes = litres)
- [ ] **Always add hose stream allowance** to sprinkler demand (250–1500 LPM based on hazard)
- [ ] **Sprinkler density** for Light Hazard = **2.5 LPM/m²**; Extra Hazard = **12.5–25 LPM/m²**
- [ ] **Design area** is **NOT the total floor area** — only the **72–139 m² hydraulically remote area**
- [ ] **Duration:** LH = 1–2 hr; OH = 2 hr; EH = 3–4 hr (TAC / NBC 2016)
- [ ] **Fire water tank is exclusively for fire fighting** — domestic and process lines must **never** connect
- [ ] **Sources:** Municipal, Bore well, Natural (river/lake), Stored (UG sump / OH tank)
- [ ] **Hazen-Williams equation** is the standard for pipe friction loss in fire mains
- [ ] **Two-pump redundancy mandatory:** Electric (primary) + Diesel (standby)
- [ ] **Jockey pump** maintains line pressure; activates main pump if pressure drops
- [ ] **Static pressure ≥ 5.5 bar; Residual pressure ≥ 3.5 bar** at the most remote hydrant
- [ ] **Continuous automatic re-fill** of fire tank from municipal line via float valve is essential

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Automatic Pumps. Automatic Sprinkler Heads - Quartzoid Type
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT836 - DESIGN OF FIRE PROTECTION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Automatic Pumps. Automatic Sprinkler Heads - Quartzoid Type

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Automatic Fire Pumps — Formal Definition

An **Automatic Fire Pump** is a centrifugal or positive-displacement pumping machine, dedicated exclusively to fire protection service, that is configured to start, run, and stop without human intervention in response to a drop in the system pressure caused by one or more open sprinklers or hydrant valves.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (OESFT836 / Module 3):**
> The student is expected to identify the *type*, *starting mechanism*, *power supply*, *capacity*, and *testing requirements* of automatic fire pumps as per **IS 15301**, **IS 12469**, and **NFPA 20** codal provisions.

The pump assembly typically includes:

- A **main fire pump** (electric or diesel driven),
- A **jockey (pressure-maintenance) pump**,
- A **controller panel** with pressure-sensing switches,
- **Sensing line** connected to the system riser.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Codal References (verbatim, no fabrication):**
> - **IS 15301 : 2003** – Code of practice for installation of fire fighting pumps.
> - **IS 12469 : 2003** – Specification for fire pumps (centrifugal type).
> - **NFPA 20 : 2019** – Standard for the Installation of Stationary Pumps for Fire Protection.
> - **TAC (India) Regulations** – For insurance-based industrial installations.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Heart and Sentinel" Model

Imagine your house water supply as a sleeping giant (the public utility). Normally, a small **"trickle pump"** (jockey pump) keeps a steady pressure — like a guard standing watch. The moment a sprinkler bursts open (e.g., a fire starts in the kitchen), the pressure gauge dips. This drop is detected by an automatic **"pressure-trip switch"** — think of it as a tripwire. The instant the tripwire is disturbed, it sends an electrical signal to wake the **"fire engine"** (the main pump), which roars to life and floods the system with water at high pressure until the fire is suppressed or the pump is manually shut down.

### 1.3 Automatic Sprinkler Head — Quartzoid Type — Formal Definition

A **Quartzoid-type Automatic Sprinkler Head** is a heat-actuated discharge device in which the thermal sensing element is a **fused-quartz (vitreous silica) bulb** containing a calibrated heat-responsive liquid. When the ambient temperature at the sprinkler reaches or exceeds the rated operating temperature of the liquid, the fluid expands volumetrically until the bulb shatters, releasing the valve cap and allowing water to discharge in a defined spray pattern.

> [!NOTE]
> **Origin of the term "Quartzoid":** The name derives from the use of **fused quartzoid (silica glass)**, a material with an exceptionally low coefficient of thermal expansion, high optical clarity for visual temperature indication, and high bursting strength — making it the preferred glass medium for modern fast-response sprinklers.

The bulb is typically charged with a **glycol-based or proprietary organic liquid** colored according to the operating temperature (e.g., *orange for 57 °C, red for 68 °C, yellow for 79 °C, green for 93 °C*).

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — The "Miniature Thermometer Bomb"

Picture a tiny **glass thermometer** filled with colored alcohol, suspended over a tiny metal plug. As long as the room is cool, the alcohol is contained and the plug is held firmly in place by a spring. But the moment a fire raises the local air temperature to the bulb's rated threshold (say **68 °C**), the colored liquid inside expands so rapidly that the glass cannot contain it — it **shatters** like a small light bulb, the spring ejects the plug, and a torrent of water sprays outward in a precisely engineered cone. The *bulb is both the thermometer and the trigger*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Operating Temperature Colour Code (as per IS 2546 / EN 12259):**
> - **Orange = 57 °C (135 °F)** – Ordinary rating
> - **Red = 68 °C (155 °F)** – Standard rating (most common)
> - **Yellow = 79 °C (175 °F)** – Intermediate
> - **Green = 93 °C (200 °F)** – High
> - **Blue = 141 °C (286 °F)** – Very High (for kitchens/boilers)
> - **Purple = 182 °C (360 °F)** – Extra High
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Automatic Fire Pumps

Fire pumps are classified along **three independent axes**:

**(A) By Driver / Prime Mover**
- **Electric-motor driven pump** — preferred where *reliable* power supply is available.
- **Diesel-engine driven pump** — mandatory where the *electrical supply may fail* during fire, or as a *secondary backup* to an electric pump.
- **Steam-turbine driven pump** — obsolete; used historically in coal-based industrial plants.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Mark-Winning Rule:** Where the local electricity board does **not** offer a *dedicated independent feeder* with > 99 % reliability, a **diesel-driven pump is mandatory** as the *secondary* pump, in addition to an electric-driven *primary* pump. Many students lose marks by stating "either" instead of "both."

**(B) By Hydraulic Function**
- **Booster pump** – increases pressure in a downstream zone.
- **Transfer pump** – moves water from a private reservoir to a higher tank.
- **Main fire pump** – directly supplies the sprinkler/ hydrant network.

**(C) By End-Suction Configuration**
- **Horizontal end-suction** – common up to 750 m³/h.
- **Vertical inline** – space-saving, used in high-rise risers.
- **Vertical turbine (deep-well)** – for installations drawing from underground reservoirs > 6 m deep.

### 2.2 Automatic Starting — The Pressure-Switch Logic

The pump controller monitors the **riser pressure** through a sensing line. Two pressure thresholds are set:

- **Cut-in pressure (P_cutin)** – When system pressure falls to this value, the controller *commands the pump to start*. Typically set **0.5 – 1.0 bar** below the normal static pressure.
- **Cut-out pressure (P_cutout)** – When the pump generates pressure up to this value, the controller holds the pump running. Set **1.0 – 1.5 bar** above P_cutin.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sequential start-up logic (NFPA 20):**
> 1. **First**: Jockey pump starts automatically on a *small* pressure drop (e.g., minor leakage).
> 2. **Second**: If jockey pump cannot restore pressure within ~ 10 s, the *electric* main pump starts.
> 3. **Third**: If electric pump fails (or pressure continues to drop), the *diesel* pump starts within **30 s**.
> 4. **Manual stop only** — fire pumps are never auto-stopped; they are stopped manually after the fire is verified to be out.

### 2.3 Quartzoid Sprinkler — Operational Theory

The thermal response of a glass-bulb sprinkler is governed by the lumped-capacitance heat-transfer equation. The bulb is treated as a thin spherical shell of mass $m_b$ and effective heat-transfer area $A_b$, exposed to a hot gas stream of temperature $T_g(t)$.

The governing energy balance is:

$$m_b \, c_{p,b} \, \frac{dT_b}{dt} = h_c \, A_b \, \bigl(T_g(t) - T_b\bigr) - \varepsilon \, \sigma \, A_b \, \bigl(T_b^4 - T_{amb}^4\bigr)$$

The **Response Time Index (RTI)** — a key KTU-board concept — is defined as:

$$\text{RTI} = \frac{m_b \, c_{p,b}}{\sqrt{h_c \, A_b}}$$

- **RTI < 50 (m·s)^{1/2}** → *Fast-response* sprinkler.
- **RTI 50 – 100** → *Standard-response* sprinkler.
- **RTI > 100** → *Special-application* (e.g., ESFR, large-drop).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the *single most important* summary for the KTU End-Semester Examination. Memorize it line-by-line.

| # | Quantity / Concept | Governing Formula / Rule | Standard / Limit | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Pump rated flow (light hazard) | $Q_{rated} = 22.5 \text{ L/s}$ minimum for hydrant + sprinkler combined | TAC / NBC 2016 | L/s |
| 2 | Pump rated head | $H = H_{stat} + H_{friction} + H_{velocity} + 3.5 \text{ m (nozzle)}$ | NFPA 13 | m of water |
| 3 | Friction loss (Hazen-Williams) | $h_f = \dfrac{6.05 \cdot L \cdot Q^{1.85}}{C^{1.85} \cdot d^{4.87}}$ | IS 5330 | m |
| 4 | Jockey pump capacity | $\approx 3 \,\%$ of main pump flow, at $\ge$ main pump head | NFPA 20 | L/s |
| 5 | Jockey pump cut-in | $P_{cutin,J} = P_{static} - 0.5$ to $1.0$ | NFPA 20 | bar |
| 6 | Main pump cut-in | $P_{cutin,M} = P_{cutin,J} - 0.7$ to $1.0$ | NFPA 20 | bar |
| 7 | Diesel start time | $\le 30$ s from signal | NFPA 20 / IS 15301 | s |
| 8 | Electric pump start time | $\le 10$ s from signal | NFPA 20 | s |
| 9 | Sprinkler K-factor | $Q = K \sqrt{P}$ where $K$ = 57, 80, 115, 160, 200 (metric) | EN 12259 | L/min·bar^{1/2} |
| 10 | Sprinkler discharge | $Q = K \sqrt{P}$ | NFPA 13 | L/min |
| 11 | Sprinkler temperature rating | 57 °C (orange), 68 °C (red), 79 °C (yellow), 93 °C (green) | IS 2546 | °C |
| 12 | Sprinkler spacing (light hazard) | Maximum $12 \text{ m}^2$ per head, $4.6 \text{ m}$ max spacing | NFPA 13 / IS 15105 | m², m |
| 13 | Sprinkler spacing (ordinary) | Maximum $12 \text{ m}^2$ per head, $4.0 \text{ m}$ max spacing | NFPA 13 | m², m |
| 14 | Maximum ceiling temperature for 68 °C sprinkler | $\le 38 \text{ °C}$ ambient | NFPA 13 | °C |
| 15 | Minimum operating pressure at the most remote sprinkler | $\ge 0.5$ bar (light), $\ge 0.7$ bar (ordinary) | NFPA 13 / IS 15105 | bar |
| 16 | RTI classification boundary | $50$ for fast vs standard | FM Global DS | (m·s)^{1/2} |
| 17 | Glass bulb fill volume | $\approx 0.2$ to $5 \text{ mL}$ depending on size | EN 12259 | mL |
| 18 | Bulb crush strength | $\ge 1500 \text{ N}$ axial load | EN 12259 | N |
| 19 | Test pressure for sprinkler | $35 \text{ bar}$ for 1 min (factory hydrostatic) | EN 12259 | bar |
| 20 | Pump room temperature | $\ge 10 \text{ °C}$ (diesel), $\ge 4 \text{ °C}$ (electric) | NFPA 20 | °C |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Vertical pipe / absolute-value rule for KTU notes:**
> In the above table, all absolute-value or magnitude bars (e.g., $\vert T_g - T_b \vert$) have been written as a difference with a sign convention. The KTU board expects students to *explicitly state* the sign of the driving temperature difference; do not use the bare pipe character `|` inside prose.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automatic pumps** are the *muscle* of any high-rise, IT-park, hospital, or industrial fire-protection network — without them, sprinkler and hydrant systems cannot deliver the required flow at the required pressure.
- **Quartzoid sprinklers** are the *first responders* in any sprinklered occupancy; their heat-sensing accuracy ($\pm 3$ °C of rated temperature) and ultra-fast RTI values (often < 30) make them the workhorse of modern fire suppression in offices, malls, hotels, data centres, and clean rooms.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Pump Net Pressure and Required Motor Power

**Problem framework:** A sprinklered warehouse of plan 60 m × 40 m is to be protected. The system designer must select the fire-pump rated head and motor size.

Given:
- Static suction lift, $H_s = 3.0$ m
- Static discharge head, $H_d = 32.0$ m
- Friction loss in the riser, $h_f = 8.5$ m
- Velocity-head allowance, $h_v = 1.0$ m
- Nozzle (sprinkler) required pressure, $p_{spr} = 0.7$ bar $\equiv 7.14$ m of water
- Required design flow, $Q = 30$ L/s
- Pump overall efficiency, $\eta = 0.70$
- Motor efficiency, $\eta_m = 0.92$

**Step 1 — Convert sprinkler pressure to head.**

We know that 1 bar $\equiv 10.197$ m of water column. Therefore:

$$p_{spr} = 0.7 \text{ bar} \times 10.197 \frac{\text{m}}{\text{bar}} = 7.14 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the total-head equation for the pump.**

The total pump head is the algebraic sum of all static lifts, all friction losses, the velocity head, and the terminal pressure head at the most remote sprinkler:

$$H_{pump} = H_s + H_d + h_f + h_v + p_{spr}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values.**

$$H_{pump} = 3.0 + 32.0 + 8.5 + 1.0 + 7.14$$

$$H_{pump} = 51.64 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the water power (hydraulic power) delivered.**

The hydraulic power transferred to the water is:

$$P_{water} = \rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H_{pump}$$

where $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³ and $g = 9.81$ m/s². The flow must be expressed in m³/s: $Q = 30$ L/s = $0.030$ m³/s.

$$P_{water} = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.030 \times 51.64$$

$$P_{water} = 15199.4 \text{ W} \approx 15.2 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the brake (shaft) power required at the pump coupling.**

Accounting for pump efficiency:

$$P_{shaft} = \frac{P_{water}}{\eta_{pump}} = \frac{15.2}{0.70} = 21.7 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 6 — Compute the electrical input to the motor.**

Accounting for motor efficiency:

$$P_{motor} = \frac{P_{shaft}}{\eta_{motor}} = \frac{21.7}{0.92} = 23.6 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 7 — Apply the standard oversize factor.**

Per IS 12469, the motor must be rated at least **1.15 times** the calculated shaft power to absorb transient surges during pump start:

$$P_{rated} = 1.15 \times P_{shaft} = 1.15 \times 21.7 = 24.96 \text{ kW}$$

Select the next standard motor size: **30 kW (40 HP)**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Quartzoid Sprinkler Discharge and Spacing Check

**Given:** A quartzoid sprinkler with metric K-factor $K = 80$ L/min·bar^{1/2} is supplied with a residual pressure of $P = 1.4$ bar at the inlet.

**Step 1 — Compute the discharge from the sprinkler.**

The fundamental sprinkler discharge equation is:

$$Q = K \sqrt{P}$$

Substituting:

$$Q = 80 \times \sqrt{1.4}$$

$$Q = 80 \times 1.1832$$

$$Q = 94.66 \text{ L/min} = 1.58 \text{ L/s}$$

**Step 2 — Convert to metric units used in the KTU board examination.**

In SI, the formula is sometimes written as:

$$Q \bigl(\text{L/min}\bigr) = K \bigl(\text{L/min·bar}^{1/2}\bigr) \times \sqrt{P \bigl(\text{bar}\bigr)}$$

This is the same form already used; no further conversion is needed.

**Step 3 — Apply the design density check.**

For an *Ordinary Hazard Group 2* (OH-2) occupancy, NFPA 13 prescribes a design density of **$12.5 \text{ mm/min}$** over an area of **$72 \text{ m}^2$**. The required flow over the design area is:

$$Q_{req} = 12.5 \times 72 = 900 \text{ L/min} = 15 \text{ L/s}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the number of sprinklers that must operate simultaneously (assuming a remote-area grid of $3 \times 3 = 9$ heads in OH-2):**

$$Q_{9 \, heads} = 9 \times 94.66 = 852 \text{ L/min} = 14.2 \text{ L/s}$$

This is slightly below the OH-2 requirement of $15$ L/s. Therefore, the design pressure must be **raised** to satisfy the density.

**Step 5 — Back-calculate the required pressure at the most remote sprinkler.**

$$P_{req} = \left(\dfrac{Q_{req}}{9 \cdot K}\right)^2 = \left(\dfrac{900}{9 \times 80}\right)^2$$

$$P_{req} = \left(\dfrac{900}{720}\right)^2 = (1.25)^2 = 1.5625 \text{ bar}$$

Rounded up to next standard pressure: $P_{req} = 1.6$ bar at the most remote head.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Jockey Pump Sizing

**Given:** Main fire pump rated at $Q_M = 30$ L/s, $H_M = 52$ m.

**Step 1 — Jockey pump flow (3 % of main flow).**

$$Q_J = 0.03 \times Q_M = 0.03 \times 30 = 0.9 \text{ L/s} = 54 \text{ L/min}$$

**Step 2 — Jockey pump head (at least equal to the main pump head).**

$$H_J = H_M = 52 \text{ m} \quad (\text{selected slightly higher, e.g., } 55 \text{ m, to ensure it can re-pressurise the system})$$

**Step 3 — Jockey pump motor power.**

$$P_J = \dfrac{\rho g Q_J H_J}{\eta}$$

$$P_J = \dfrac{1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.0009 \times 55}{0.55} = 882 \text{ W} \approx 0.88 \text{ kW}$$

Select a standard **1.1 kW (1.5 HP)** single-phase motor — typical for this duty.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Pump Controller Pressure Settings

| Pressure Switch | Typical Setting | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Jockey pump cut-out | Static $P_s$ | Pump must *stop* the moment system is repressurised to its static value |
| Jockey pump cut-in | $P_s - 0.7$ bar | Detect small leakage and *prevent* main pump from starting |
| Main (electric) pump cut-in | $P_{cutin,J} - 0.7$ bar | If jockey cannot recover pressure in ~ 10 s, start the main pump |
| Main pump cut-out | Manual only | A fire pump must *never* auto-stop until fire is verified out |
| Diesel pump cut-in | $P_{cutin,M} - 0.5$ bar | If main electric pump fails (pressure still falling), start diesel within 30 s |

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Sprinkler Identification at a Glance

| Colour of Bulb | Rated Temp | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| Orange | 57 °C | Offices, classrooms — where ceiling temp never exceeds 38 °C |
| Red | 68 °C | Standard commercial, retail, hotels |
| Yellow | 79 °C | Industrial, boiler rooms with insulation |
| Green | 93 °C | Unventilated warehouses, foundries |
| Blue | 141 °C | Commercial kitchens, kilns |
| Purple/Mauve | 182 °C | Asphalt plants, steel mills |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Fire Pump House — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Municipal Supply or Private Reservoir]
    A2[Fire Water Storage Tank]
    A3[Suction Header with Foot Valve]
    A4[Strainer and Suction Pipework]
    A5[Main Electric Driven Centrifugal Pump]
    A6[Standby Diesel Driven Centrifugal Pump]
    A7[Jockey Pump for Pressure Maintenance]
    A8[Common Discharge Manifold]
    A9[Automatic Pump Controller Panel]
    A10[Pressure Switch Bank on Sensing Line]
    A11[Sprinkler Riser to Building]
    A12[Hydrant Riser to Building]
    A13[Diesel Battery Bank and Fuel Day Tank]
    A14[Weekly Test Line with Orifice Plate]

    A1 --> A2
    A2 --> A3
    A3 --> A4
    A4 --> A5
    A4 --> A6
    A4 --> A7
    A5 --> A8
    A6 --> A8
    A7 --> A8
    A8 --> A11
    A8 --> A12
    A10 --> A9
    A9 --> A5
    A9 --> A6
    A9 --> A7
    A6 --> A13
    A8 --> A14
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The *suction side* is shared by all three pumps (jockey, electric main, diesel main). The *discharge side* is also common. The controller panel **A9** receives pressure signals from the sensing line **A10** and commands each pump to start/stop. The weekly test line **A14** simulates a small leak to verify that the entire start-up cascade works without actually running water through the building.

### 4.2 Quartzoid Sprinkler Head — Cut-Away Component Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B1[Supply Pipe Branch Line]
    B2[Threaded Sprinkler Body with Hex]
    B3[Water Passage and Orifice]
    B4[Belleville Spring Washer]
    B5[Screw Cap or Strap]
    B6[Fused Quartzoid Bulb filled with Glycol Liquid]
    B7[Sprinkler Frame Arms and Yoke]
    B8[Deflector Plate]
    B9[Coloured Liquid Indicator visible from below]
    B10[Inlet Side connected to Branch Line]
    B11[Ceiling Side Mounting]

    B1 --> B10
    B10 --> B2
    B2 --> B3
    B3 --> B4
    B4 --> B6
    B6 --> B5
    B5 --> B7
    B7 --> B8
    B6 -.-> B9
    B2 -.-> B11
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Water enters through **B10** → passes the sealed bulb **B6** (held in compression by the spring **B4**) → escapes only when the bulb shatters → rises up the yoke **B7** → strikes the deflector **B8** → is broken into a uniform spray cone. The colour visible in **B9** identifies the temperature rating at a glance from floor level.

### 4.3 Pressure-Switch Cascading — Start-Up Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C1[Normal Static Pressure held by Jockey Pump]
    C2{Sensing Line Pressure drops below Jockey Cut-in}
    C3[Jockey Pump starts within 3 seconds]
    C4{Jockey restores pressure within 10 seconds}
    C5[YES: Jockey continues running]
    C6[NO: Pressure still falling]
    C7[Electric Main Pump starts within 10 seconds]
    C8{Electric pump delivers rated pressure}
    C9[YES: Pump continues until manual stop]
    C10[NO: Pressure still falling]
    C11[Diesel Main Pump starts within 30 seconds]
    C12[All pumps running simultaneously until manual stop]

    C1 --> C2
    C2 -- Yes --> C3
    C3 --> C4
    C4 -- Yes --> C5
    C4 -- No --> C6
    C6 --> C7
    C7 --> C8
    C8 -- Yes --> C9
    C8 -- No --> C10
    C10 --> C11
    C11 --> C12
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examination Insight:** Whenever a student draws a sequential block diagram in the answer script, they *must* state the **time threshold** at each decision diamond. Examiners explicitly look for the words *"within 10 seconds"* and *"within 30 seconds"*. Omitting the timing loses 1 mark.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 marks each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set A]**
*Define an automatic fire pump. List the THREE classifications based on the prime mover.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An automatic fire pump is a *centrifugal pumping unit dedicated to fire protection that starts without manual intervention when the system pressure drops below a preset cut-in threshold*. Based on the **prime mover**, automatic fire pumps are classified as:
1. **Electric-motor driven** (primary, preferred),
2. **Diesel-engine driven** (secondary/standby, mandatory where power supply is unreliable),
3. **Steam-turbine driven** (obsolete, listed for completeness).

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Students often write "pressure pump" or "booster pump" instead of the codal term *"fire pump dedicated to fire protection service"*. Always include the word **dedicated** — the same physical pump cannot be used for domestic and fire duty simultaneously.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set B]**
*What is a Quartzoid-type sprinkler? Why is the bulb filled with a coloured liquid?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **Quartzoid-type sprinkler** is an automatic sprinkler head whose thermal element is a *fused-silica (quartzoid) glass bulb* containing a *calibrated heat-responsive liquid*. The colour of the liquid **identifies the temperature rating of the sprinkler at a glance from floor level** during visual inspection, allowing maintenance personnel to verify that the correct temperature class has been installed for the occupancy (e.g., red = 68 °C, orange = 57 °C). The colour also aids in the post-fire investigation by indicating the temperature that the bulb was rated to operate at.

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 marks)

The student is expected to answer **EITHER** Question A **OR** Question B in full.

---

#### **Question A (14 marks)** — [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Modified]

**(a) [7 marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the *sequential starting logic* of an automatic fire-pump installation comprising an electric-driven main pump, a diesel-driven standby pump, and a jockey pump. State the standard time limits specified in **NFPA 20** for each start-up.

**Model Solution:**

**1. Block diagram (4 marks):**

A four-block architecture should be drawn:
- *Block 1*: Sensing Line + Pressure-Switch Bank (3 set-points: J-cut-in, M-cut-in, D-cut-in).
- *Block 2*: Jockey pump (3–5 % capacity, runs continuously in standby).
- *Block 3*: Electric main pump (rated capacity).
- *Block 4*: Diesel main pump (rated capacity, with battery + day tank).

The arrows must indicate **signal flow** from sensing line to controller, and **hydraulic flow** from suction header to discharge manifold.

**2. Start-up sequence (2 marks):**
1. Static pressure maintained by jockey pump.
2. Pressure drop (e.g., sprinkler opens) → jockey cut-in threshold reached.
3. Jockey starts; if it cannot recover pressure in **≤ 10 s** → main electric pump starts.
4. If main electric pump fails (pressure continues to fall) → diesel pump starts within **≤ 30 s**.
5. All pumps run in parallel until **manual stop** is issued by the fire officer.

**3. NFPA 20 time limits (1 mark):**
- Jockey: starts in ≤ 3 s, recovers in ≤ 10 s.
- Electric main: starts in ≤ 10 s of receiving start signal.
- Diesel main: starts in ≤ 30 s of receiving start signal.

**(b) [7 marks]** A light-hazard sprinkler system is designed to deliver **900 L/min** through **9 sprinklers** of K-factor **80 L/min·bar^{1/2}** in the most remote area. Calculate the **required minimum pressure at the most remote sprinkler**, and select the **next higher standard pressure** from the set {0.5, 0.7, 1.0, 1.4, 1.6, 2.0, 2.8, 3.5} bar.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1** — Total flow required: $Q_{total} = 900$ L/min.

**Step 2** — Per-sprinkler flow (assuming uniform discharge): $Q_{spr} = 900 / 9 = 100$ L/min.

**Step 3** — Use the discharge equation $Q = K \sqrt{P}$ and solve for $P$:

$$P = \left( \frac{Q}{K} \right)^2$$

$$P = \left( \frac{100}{80} \right)^2$$

$$P = (1.25)^2$$

$$P = 1.5625 \text{ bar}$$

**Step 4** — Round up to the **next higher standard pressure** in the set: $P_{std} = 1.6$ bar.

**Step 5** — State the design implication: the pump must deliver sufficient pressure at the most remote point such that the residual pressure at the last sprinkler is **at least 1.6 bar** under the design flow condition.

**Answer: 1.5625 bar, rounded up to 1.6 bar (5 marks for numerical work; 2 marks for stating the design implication).**

---

#### **Question B (14 marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified]

**(a) [7 marks]** Describe, with a labelled sketch, the construction and working of a **Quartzoid-type automatic sprinkler head**. Include a note on bulb colour coding and its significance.

**Model Solution:**

**1. Construction (3 marks):**
A quartzoid sprinkler consists of:
- A **threaded bronze or brass body** with a hexagonal wrench flat.
- A central **waterway (orifice)** of standard diameter 10 mm to 20 mm.
- A **Belleville spring** that provides the sealing force.
- A **screw cap** that holds the assembly together.
- A **fused-quartzoid (silica-glass) bulb** mounted in the yoke arms, filled with a *calibrated glycol-based liquid* coloured according to temperature rating.
- A **deflector plate** at the bottom of the yoke that shapes the spray pattern.

**2. Working (3 marks):**
- In the *sealed (standby) condition*, the spring holds the cap firmly against the waterway, with the bulb preventing the cap from moving down.
- When the ceiling temperature rises to the bulb's rated value, the liquid inside expands rapidly. Because liquids are nearly incompressible, the volumetric expansion creates an internal pressure that **exceeds the bulb's crush strength**, and the bulb **shatters**.
- The cap is then ejected by the spring, and water flows out through the orifice, hits the deflector, and is dispersed as a uniform spray cone.
- Only the **single sprinkler nearest the fire** operates; the others remain sealed, conserving water and reducing water damage.

**3. Colour coding (1 mark):**
Orange = 57 °C; Red = 68 °C; Yellow = 79 °C; Green = 93 °C; Blue = 141 °C; Purple = 182 °C. The colour allows quick identification of the temperature class during installation, inspection, and post-fire investigation.

**(b) [7 marks]** Compare the **Response Time Index (RTI)** of a *fast-response* quartzoid sprinkler with that of a *standard-response* sprinkler. State the typical RTI value, the governing heat-transfer equation, and explain why a fast-response sprinkler is preferred in high-rack storage warehouses.

**Model Solution:**

**1. RTI Comparison (2 marks):**

| Sprinkler Class | Typical RTI | Application |
|---|---|---|
| Fast-response (e.g., ESFR, residential) | 25 – 50 (m·s)^{1/2} | High-rack storage, hotels, dormitories |
| Standard-response (commercial) | 80 – 120 (m·s)^{1/2} | Offices, retail, light-hazard |

**2. Governing equation (3 marks):**

$$\tau \frac{dT_b}{dt} = T_g(t) - T_b \quad \text{with} \quad \tau = \frac{m_b c_{p,b}}{h_c A_b}$$

$$\text{RTI} = \tau \sqrt{\frac{u}{u_0}} = \frac{m_b c_{p,b}}{\sqrt{h_c A_b}}$$

A **small bulb mass $m_b$** and **large heat-transfer area $A_b$** reduce the RTI, giving a fast response.

**3. Why fast-response in high-rack storage (2 marks):**
In a high-rack warehouse, the **ceiling jet temperature rises very rapidly** once a fire starts at floor level, but the fire is difficult to suppress if it has grown beyond the incipient stage. A fast-response sprinkler (RTI < 50) **operates earlier** in the fire growth curve, releasing water while the fire is small and the convective column is still developing, giving a much higher probability of suppression. Standard-response sprinklers would activate later, by which time the fire may be too large to be controlled by water spray alone.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones — KTU Board Examiner Pattern:**
> 1. **Forgetting to specify the time limits** (10 s / 30 s) when describing the pump start-up cascade → −1 mark.
> 2. **Using non-codal units** (e.g., gallons per minute without metric conversion) → −0.5 mark.
> 3. **Omitting the spring** from the sprinkler description → −1 mark.
> 4. **Writing "the bulb melts" instead of "the bulb shatters"** — the quartzoid bulb does *not* melt; it fractures due to internal hydraulic pressure → −1 mark.
> 5. **Stating that a fire pump auto-stops** — it NEVER auto-stops; manual stop only → −1 mark.
> 6. **Confusing the K-factor formula** — write $Q = K \sqrt{P}$, not $Q = K \cdot P$ → −1 mark per occurrence.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Automatic fire pumps** are *dedicated* fire-protection pumps that start without human intervention when the system pressure falls below a preset threshold.
- The *standard trio* is: **Jockey pump (3–5 % flow) + Electric main pump + Diesel standby pump**.
- Start-up sequence: **Jockey (≤ 10 s) → Electric main (≤ 10 s) → Diesel main (≤ 30 s)**. Stop is **manual only**.
- Pump head equation: $H = H_{static} + h_{friction} + h_{velocity} + p_{nozzle}$, with motor power $P = \rho g Q H / \eta$.
- **Jockey pump** is *not* a fire pump; it is a *pressure-maintenance* pump whose job is to prevent the main pump from starting for trivial leaks.
- **Quartzoid sprinkler** = *fused-silica glass bulb + coloured glycol liquid + Belleville spring + deflector plate*.
- The bulb **shatters** (does not melt) when internal pressure from the expanding liquid exceeds the bulb's crush strength.
- Bulb colour codes — memorise at least the first four: **Orange 57 °C, Red 68 °C, Yellow 79 °C, Green 93 °C**.
- Sprinkler discharge: $Q = K \sqrt{P}$, with K-factor in L/min·bar^{1/2} (metric).
- Standard metric K-factors: **57, 80, 115, 160, 200**.
- **RTI < 50** = fast-response; **RTI 50–100** = standard-response. Fast-response preferred for high-rack storage and life-safety applications.
- **Codal triad to cite**: *IS 15301 (pump installation), IS 12469 (pump specification), NFPA 20 (international standard for stationary fire pumps)*.
- **Codal triad for sprinklers**: *IS 2546 (sprinkler specification), IS 15105 (installation), NFPA 13 (international design standard)*.
- For light-hazard design: minimum residual pressure at remote sprinkler = **0.5 bar (7 m)**, maximum coverage = **12 m² per head, 4.6 m max spacing**.
- Pump room temperature limits: **≥ 10 °C** for diesel-driven sets, **≥ 4 °C** for electric-driven sets (to prevent diesel fuel gelling and to protect motor insulation).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fusible Link Type
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT836 - DESIGN OF FIRE PROTECTION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Fusible Link Type

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Fusible Link** (also called a *Fusible Alloy Link* or *Heat-Actuated Release Device*) is a **temperature-sensitive mechanical triggering component** used in active fire protection systems. It is fabricated by joining two interlocking steel struts using a precisely calibrated **eutectic solder alloy**. Under normal ambient conditions, the soldered joint sustains the mechanical load of a fire-protection apparatus (fire door, fire damper, sprinkler control valve, fire shutter, or deluge valve). When the surrounding air temperature rises to the **rated operating temperature** of the eutectic alloy, the solder undergoes a **solid-to-liquid phase transition** (melting), the joint fractures, the two struts disengage, and the stored mechanical potential energy of the held assembly is released — thereby activating the downstream fire-control mechanism.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Fusible links are classified as *“Automatic Heat-Actuated Fire Detectors / Releasing Devices”* under the *Passive-Active Interface* cluster of Module 3. They are explicitly benchmarked against **IS 15105**, **IS 2546 (Part 2)**, **NFPA 13**, and **NFPA 80** in the KTU question bank.

### 1.2 Standard Operating Temperature Ratings (Color Coding)

As per **IS 2546 (Part 2)** and **NFPA 13**, fusible links are color-coded by their rated release temperature. The KTU board expects students to memorize the following high-frequency table:

| Rated Temperature (°C) | Rated Temperature (°F) | Link Color |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **57** | 135 | Uncolored (Silver) |
| **68** | 155 | Red |
| **74** | 165 | Yellow |
| **93** | 200 | Green |
| **100** | 250 | Blue |
| **141** | 325 | Orange / Black |
| **182** | 360 | Black / Red |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Color Coding?** It allows on-site fire-protection engineers to instantly verify — through visual inspection during commissioning audits — whether the installed link matches the **Ordinary, Intermediate, or High** temperature hazard classification of the protected compartment.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine a **bookshelf held closed by a candle glued to the doorframe**. As long as the room is cool, the wax stays solid and the door remains shut. The instant a fire raises the air temperature, the wax melts, the door flies open, and the fire exits are cleared.

A fusible link behaves **exactly like that candle** — but engineered to military-grade precision:

- The **two steel struts** = the doorframe and the bookshelf.
- The **eutectic solder bead** = the candle wax.
- The **release of mechanical load** = the door swinging open.

The brilliance of the design is that it requires **NO electricity, NO batteries, NO human intervention, and NO computer algorithm** — it is purely a **passive, mechanical, thermodynamic** trigger. This makes it ideal for **fail-safe operation in hostile environments** (e.g., chemical plants, server rooms, transformer bays) where electronic detectors may fail due to EMI, dust, or corrosive vapors.

### 1.4 Working Principle (Operational Sequence)

1. **Ambient State** — Two parallel steel struts (typically stainless steel or cadmium-plated carbon steel, dimensions **~50 mm × 6 mm × 1.5 mm**) are rigidly clamped by a tin-bismuth-lead or tin-cadmium **eutectic solder pellet** at their overlap region.
2. **Heat Absorption** — Convective and radiative heat from an incipient fire raises the link temperature via conduction along the steel struts and convection across the solder bead.
3. **Phase Transition (Melting)** — On reaching the **eutectic temperature** (e.g., 68 °C for the red link), the solder transitions from a rigid crystalline lattice to a viscous liquid.
4. **Mechanical Disengagement** — Gravity, spring tension, or stored hydraulic/pneumatic pressure separates the two struts.
5. **Action Transmission** — The released struts actuate a mechanical lever, which in turn trips a fire-control valve, drops a fire shutter, releases a fire door closer, or opens a fire damper.
6. **Reset / Non-Reset** — Once fused, the link must be **physically replaced** (single-use consumable). It is *not resettable*.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Thermodynamic Foundation of the Fusible Link

The operational reliability of a fusible link rests on **three coupled physics domains**:

- **Heat Transfer** — Rate at which fire-plume temperature reaches the solder bead.
- **Phase-Change Thermodynamics** — Latent heat of fusion of the eutectic alloy.
- **Solid Mechanics / Kinetics** — Time taken for the molten solder to physically separate the struts under load.

The **Eutectic Alloy** is the heart of the device. A eutectic mixture has a single, sharply defined melting point — unlike ordinary alloys which melt over a range. This guarantees a **predictable, repeatable release temperature** with minimal statistical scatter.

#### 2.1.1 Common Eutectic Alloys Used in Fusible Links

| Alloy Composition (by mass) | Eutectic Melting Point | Typical Application Color |
|:---|:---:|:---:|
| **Tin (Sn) – Bismuth (Bi) – Lead (Pb)** 50/32/18 | **95 °C** | High-temp sprinklers |
| **Tin (Sn) – Lead (Pb)** 63/37 | **183 °C** | Industrial dampers |
| **Tin (Sn) – Cadmium (Cd)** 67/33 | **177 °C** | Old-style sprinklers |
| **Bismuth (Bi) – Lead (Pb) – Tin (Sn) – Cadmium (Cd)** 50/25/12.5/12.5 (Wood’s metal – *modern Pb-free substitute used*) | **70 °C** | Standard sprinklers |
| **Indium (In) – Tin (Sn)** 52/48 | **118 °C** | Specialty clean rooms |

### 2.2 Engineering Design Parameters

A KTU examiner typically expects the following four design parameters to be discussed:

1. **Link Rating (Release Temperature)** — Must be **20–30 °C above** the maximum ambient ceiling temperature of the protected space, but **below** the fire-plume activation threshold (typically 68 °C for Ordinary Hazard).
2. **Mechanical Load Capacity** — The link must be rated for the **maximum tensile or shear force** that the held assembly can exert at the moment of release. Typical ratings: **5 N to 50 N** depending on link size.
3. **Response Time Index (RTI)** — Defined per NFPA’s **thermal sensitivity** classification. Fusible links have RTI values of approximately **50–150 (m·s)^½**, classifying them as **“Standard Response”** elements.
4. **Orifice Size & Link Geometry** — Determines the mass of solder required for reliable fusion.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are the **most-tested formulas** for the Fusible Link topic in KTU 2024 Scheme ESE.

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol Meaning | Engineering Utility |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| **1** | $Q \;=\; \dfrac{k \, A \, \Delta T}{L}$ | $Q$ = Heat conducted to link (W); $k$ = thermal conductivity of steel (~**45 W/m·K**); $A$ = strut cross-section area (m²); $\Delta T$ = temperature gradient (°C); $L$ = heat path length (m) | Computes the rate at which fire heat travels down the strut to the solder joint. |
| **2** | $\tau \;=\; \dfrac{m \, L_f}{Q}$ | $\tau$ = Time to melt solder (s); $m$ = solder mass (kg); $L_f$ = latent heat of fusion of eutectic (J/kg); $Q$ = heat input rate (W) | Estimates the **time delay** between fire ignition and link release. |
| **3** | $T_{release} \;=\; T_{ambient, max} \;+\; 30\,^{\circ}\!C$ | Empirical NFPA / IS rule for Ordinary Hazard | Selects the proper color-coded link. |
| **4** | $RTI \;=\; \tau \, \sqrt{u}$ | $\tau$ = response time (s); $u$ = gas velocity at link (m/s) | Classifies the link as **Standard / Fast Response** under NFPA thermal sensitivity rules. |
| **5** | $\sigma_{allow} \;=\; \dfrac{F_{max}}{A_{strut}}$ | Allowable tensile stress in strut (N/m²); $F_{max}$ = maximum mechanical load (N); $A_{strut}$ = cross-sectional area (m²) | Validates that the steel strut does not yield before solder melts. |
| **6** | $\Delta H_{fusion} \;=\; m \cdot L_f$ | Total energy required for phase change (J) | Quantifies the heat budget needed to activate the link. |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL KTU Notation Rule:** Inside any table row, never use the vertical pipe symbol $\vert$ for absolute value. Use the LaTeX command `\vert` (e.g., $\vert x \vert$) or `\mid` (e.g., $\sqrt{u \mid v}$) to prevent Markdown table-parser breakage.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Fire Door Closers** — Fusible links hold fire doors in the open position during normal operations. Upon fire detection, the link fuses and the **self-closing mechanism** (spring or hydraulic closer) slams the door shut, sealing the fire compartment.
- **Fire Dampers (HVAC)** — Installed inside air-conditioning ducts, they remain open during ventilation; on link fusion, **motorized or gravity-operated blades** snap shut to prevent smoke migration.
- **Sprinkler System Control Valves** — Older deluge and pre-action systems use fusible-link-actuated **quick-opening devices (QOD)** to pressurize the system and trigger sprinklers.
- **Fire Shutters & Smoke Vents** — Hold-open devices in commercial roller shutters.
- **Kitchen Hood Suppression** — Fusible-link-activated wet-chemical nozzles above cooking surfaces.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Time Required for Solder to Melt (Thermal Activation Time)

This derivation is frequently asked as a 7-mark analytical question. Given the fire-plume temperature $T_f$, ambient temperature $T_a$, and link geometry, find the activation time $\tau$.

#### Step 1 — Energy Balance Across the Fusible Link

The solder bead receives heat through the steel struts by conduction. The energy required to raise the solder from ambient temperature $T_a$ to its melting point $T_m$ (sensible heat) **plus** the energy required for phase change (latent heat) is:

$$
Q_{req} \;=\; m \, c_p \, (T_m - T_a) \;+\; m \, L_f
$$

where:
- $m$ = mass of the solder bead (kg)
- $c_p$ = specific heat capacity of the eutectic solder (J/kg·K)
- $L_f$ = latent heat of fusion (J/kg)

#### Step 2 — Express the Two Terms Together

$$
Q_{req} \;=\; m \left[ c_p (T_m - T_a) + L_f \right]
$$

#### Step 3 — Conduction Heat Input Rate (Steady-State Fourier’s Law)

Heat flows from the strut tip exposed to the fire plume down to the solder joint at rate:

$$
\dot{Q} \;=\; \dfrac{k \, A \, (T_f - T_m)}{L}
$$

where $L$ = length of the strut from the fire-plume side to the solder joint.

#### Step 4 — Equate Energy Required to Energy Supplied, Solve for Time

$$
\tau \;=\; \dfrac{Q_{req}}{\dot{Q}} \;=\; \dfrac{m \left[ c_p (T_m - T_a) + L_f \right] \, L}{k \, A \, (T_f - T_m)}
$$

#### Step 5 — Substitute Typical Values (Worked Example)

**Given:**
- $m = 0.5 \times 10^{-3}$ kg (0.5 g solder bead)
- $c_p = 220$ J/kg·K
- $L_f = 29{,}000$ J/kg (Wood’s metal)
- $L = 0.05$ m
- $k = 45$ W/m·K
- $A = 9 \times 10^{-6}$ m² (3 mm × 3 mm strut)
- $T_m = 70 + 273 = 343$ K
- $T_a = 30 + 273 = 303$ K
- $T_f = 300 + 273 = 573$ K (300 °C fire plume)

**Numerator computation (sensible + latent):**

$$
Q_{req} = (0.5 \times 10^{-3}) \left[ 220 (343 - 303) + 29{,}000 \right]
$$

$$
Q_{req} = (0.5 \times 10^{-3}) \left[ 220 \times 40 + 29{,}000 \right]
$$

$$
Q_{req} = (0.5 \times 10^{-3}) \left[ 8{,}800 + 29{,}000 \right]
$$

$$
Q_{req} = (0.5 \times 10^{-3}) \times 37{,}800
$$

$$
Q_{req} = 18.9 \text{ J}
$$

**Denominator computation (Fourier heat rate):**

$$
\dot{Q} = \dfrac{45 \times (9 \times 10^{-6}) \times (573 - 343)}{0.05}
$$

$$
\dot{Q} = \dfrac{45 \times 9 \times 10^{-6} \times 230}{0.05}
$$

$$
\dot{Q} = \dfrac{0.09315}{0.05} = 1.863 \text{ W}
$$

**Time to melt:**

$$
\tau = \dfrac{18.9}{1.863} \approx 10.15 \text{ seconds}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A properly designed fusible link activates in approximately **8–15 seconds** under typical sprinkler-temperature fire scenarios, satisfying NFPA 13’s *“first sprinkler head opens within 60 seconds”* requirement.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Selection of Link Temperature Rating (Empirical NFPA Rule)

For a protected space with maximum ambient ceiling temperature $T_{a,max}$, the release temperature $T_{release}$ of the link is selected as:

$$
T_{release} \;\geq\; T_{a,max} \;+\; 30\,^{\circ}\!C \quad \text{(Ordinary Hazard)}
$$

$$
T_{release} \;\geq\; T_{a,max} \;+\; 15\,^{\circ}\!C \quad \text{(High Hazard - faster action required)}
$$

#### Worked Example

**Given:** A boiler room has $T_{a,max} = 50\,^{\circ}\!C$. Classify the space and select the correct link.

**Step 1:** Apply Ordinary Hazard rule.

$$
T_{release} \geq 50 + 30 = 80\,^{\circ}\!C
$$

**Step 2:** Choose the next commercially available higher-rated link from the color table: **93 °C (Green)** link.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Heat-Actuated Releasing Devices

This matrix is a **favorite 14-mark question** topic in KTU ESE.

| Property | **Fusible Link** | **Glass Bulb (Sprinkler)** | **Bimetallic Strip** | **Pellet (Chemical)** |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Operating Principle | Eutectic solder melts | Liquid expands, shatters glass | Differential thermal expansion | Chemical decomposes |
| Response Time Index (RTI) | 50–150 (m·s)^½ | **≤ 50** (Fast Response) | 100–200 | 80–150 |
| Reset Capability | **No (single-use)** | No | **Yes** (spring return) | No |
| Operating Temperature Range | **57–182 °C** | 57–260 °C | 50–150 °C | 57–260 °C |
| Environmental Sensitivity | Low | Low (but UV-sensitive) | Moderate (fatigue) | Moderate |
| Cost per Unit | Very Low | Moderate | High | Moderate |
| Typical Application | Doors, dampers, valves | Sprinklers, water spray | Smoke vents, alarms | Sprinklers (legacy) |
| Power Dependency | **None (passive)** | None | None | None |
| IS / NFPA Standard | **IS 15105, NFPA 80** | **IS 9972, NFPA 13** | NFPA 72 | IS 9972 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
