<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Hangers in Fire Protection Systems

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU/IS 15105 & NFPA 13):** A *hanger* is a device or assembly used to support the dead load of the piping system, sprinklers, and the weight of water-filled pipe (or the equivalent weight of water for dry systems) on a fire protection installation. Hangers transfer the vertical load to the building structure without restricting thermal movement.

**Intuitive Analogy — The "Ceiling Shoulder":**
Imagine a heavy water pipe full of water hanging from your ceiling. Without support, it will sag, break at joints, and eventually fail — exactly when you need it most (during a fire). A hanger is the **"shoulder"** of the ceiling — it carries the weight so the pipe can do its job. In a fire, the pipe heats up and *expands* (especially in dry systems). A good hanger is like a smart shoulder — it holds the weight **but allows the arm (pipe) to stretch a little without breaking**.

### Types of Hangers (per IS 15105 / NFPA 13)
| Hanger Type | Application | Load Direction |
|---|---|---|
| **Clevis Hanger** | Standard straight runs of sprinkler pipe | Vertical (down) |
| **U-Bolt / Riser Clamp** | Vertical risers | Vertical (down) |
| **Adjustable Swivel Ring** | Slight slopes, under metal decks | Vertical (down) |
| **Trapeze Hanger** | Multiple pipes, large mains | Vertical (down) |
| **Sway Brace** | Lateral & longitudinal seismic restraint | Horizontal |

**Standard Spacing (per IS 15105 / NFPA 13):**

$$ S_{max} = \frac{3.66 \text{ m}}{12 \text{ ft}} \text{ (for steel pipe, approx.)} $$

Spacing depends on **nominal pipe diameter** — the larger the pipe, the closer the hangers. For 25 mm (1") pipe, spacing ≈ 3.6 m; for 150 mm (6") pipe, spacing reduces to ≈ 2.4 m.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sway Bracing vs Hangers:** Hangers carry **gravity (vertical)** loads. Sway braces resist **lateral (sideways)** forces — these are mandatory in seismic zones (Kerala falls in **Seismic Zone III** as per IS 1893). Never confuse the two in the exam.

---

## 1.2 Control Valves for Wet & Dry Installations

> [!NOTE]
> **Control Valve (Alarm Valve / System Control Valve):** A valve that controls the flow of water into a fire protection system and, in the event of fire, sounds an alarm. It also indicates the *operational* state of the system (open/closed, tripped/reset).

### 1.2.1 Wet System Control Valve (Alarm Check Valve)
Used in **wet pipe sprinkler systems** where pipes are permanently filled with water under pressure.

**Intuition — The "One-Way Water Gate with Whistle":**
Think of a wet system alarm valve as a **swinging door that only opens inward** (toward the sprinklers). When a sprinkler opens, water rushes out → the pressure inside the system drops → the clapper (door) opens wider → water flows and **simultaneously pushes a small bypass that rings a hydraulic alarm bell**. The "whistle" (alarm) and the "water" go out together.

**Key Features:**
- Single direction clapper
- Retard chamber (delays false alarms due to water hammer)
- Alarm pressure switch
- Pressure gauges on supply and system side

### 1.2.2 Dry System Control Valve (Dry Pipe Valve)
Used in **dry pipe sprinkler systems** for areas subject to freezing (e.g., cold storage, unheated warehouses).

> [!NOTE]
> **Working Principle:** The dry pipe valve holds back pressurized air (or nitrogen) on the system side. When a sprinkler opens, the air escapes → air pressure drops below the trip threshold → the dry pipe valve opens → water rushes in to feed the open sprinklers.

**Intuition — The "Inflated Balloon Valve":**
Imagine a balloon filled with air trapped inside a water pipe network. The air pressure holds a valve shut. If you poke a hole (open sprinkler), the air hisses out, the balloon deflates, the valve swings open, and water floods in. There is a **time delay** (water reaches the open sprinkler) called the *Water Delivery Time* — must not exceed **60 seconds** per NFPA 13.

| Parameter | Wet System | Dry System |
|---|---|---|
| Pipe content | Water under pressure | Pressurized air + water at valve |
| Trip mechanism | Heat → sprinkler opens → water | Heat → sprinkler opens → air release → water |
| Water delivery time | **Immediate (< 5 s)** | **Up to 60 s** |
| Alarm type | Hydraulic (water motor) | Air-pressure operated |
| Application | Heated buildings, offices | Freezers, parking, cold storage |
| Trip threshold | Pressure drop | Air pressure ≤ trip point |

---

## 1.3 Deluge Valve

> [!NOTE]
> **Deluge Valve Definition (IS 15301 / NFPA 13):** A quick-opening valve that admits water into a deluge system upon actuation by a separate fire detection system. The valve outlet is connected to open nozzles/sprinklers (no fusible links on the system — nozzles are open).

**Intuition — The "Flood-on-Command Door":**
Picture a giant water main with a door that *never opens on its own*. The door is held shut by a **separate fire detector** (smoke/heat/flame). When the detector *commands* it (electrically, pneumatically, or hydraulically), the door swings fully open and water floods the entire protected area at once — *every open nozzle discharges simultaneously*.

**Three Actuation Methods:**
1. **Hydraulic (Pilot) actuation** — heat-sensing bulb holds pilot line closed
2. **Pneumatic actuation** — air pressure holds pilot closed
3. **Electrical actuation** — solenoid valve releases pilot pressure on signal from detector

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Deluge vs Wet vs Dry:** In a *wet* system, sprinklers *themselves* detect heat (fusible link). In a *deluge* system, an **external detector** decides to flood the area. This makes deluge systems ideal for **high-hazard occupancies** like transformer yards, aircraft hangars, and chemical plants.

---

## 1.4 Pre-Action Sprinkler System

> [!NOTE]
> **Pre-Action System Definition (NFPA 13):** A sprinkler system employing automatic sprinklers attached to a piping system containing air that may or may not be under pressure, with a supplemental fire detection system installed in the same area as the sprinklers. Water is admitted through a *pre-action valve* upon actuation of the detection system, *before* the sprinklers open.

**Intuition — "Verify, Then Water, Then Sprinkle":**
It's the **double-check system**. The sprinkler has a fusible link (like wet), but the pipe has *air*, not water (like dry). A separate fire detector (smoke/heat) **first confirms** the fire → opens the pre-action valve → water fills the pipes → if a sprinkler *also* opens (heat fuses the link), water discharges. **Two independent events** must occur before water flows.

| System | Detection by | Pipe Content Pre-Fire | Accidental Discharge Risk |
|---|---|---|---|
| Wet | Sprinkler itself | Water | High (if sprinkler damaged) |
| Dry | Sprinkler itself | Pressurized air | Low |
| Pre-Action | Sprinkler + Detector | Air (sometimes) | **Very Low** |
| Deluge | Detector only | Air (open nozzles) | High (no fuse needed) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Application:** Pre-action systems protect **high-value assets** where accidental water discharge is unacceptable — server rooms, libraries, museums, archives, electronics manufacturing.

---

## 1.5 Drenchers

> [!NOTE]
> **Drencher Definition (IS 15105 / NFPA 15):** A drencher is a water spray system designed to protect specific external or internal surfaces (e.g., tank shells, structural steel, building façades) from radiant heat exposure or to cool equipment. Drenchers are typically **open-nozzle systems** similar to deluge but designed for *surface cooling*, not volume flooding.

**Intuition — "External Water Curtain":**
Imagine a building next to a neighbouring fire. The radiation heat can ignite windows, façade, and structural steel. A drencher sprays a **continuous water film** over the exposed surface — like a *vertical waterfall* of water droplets that absorbs heat and prevents ignition. Inside, drenchers are used to cool **transformer bodies, oil tanks, LPG vessels**.

**Components:** Water supply → Control valve (deluge-type) → Piping → Open spray nozzles (often medium-velocity type)

---

## 1.6 High Velocity and Medium Velocity Water Spray Systems

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (NFPA 15 / IS 15325):** A water spray system discharges water from specially designed spray nozzles at a defined spray pattern and velocity. Systems are classified by nozzle discharge velocity:
> - **High Velocity (HV):** Nozzle pressure ≥ **3.5 bar (50 psi)**; velocity ≥ **30 m/s**
> - **Medium Velocity (MV):** Nozzle pressure ≈ **2.8 bar (40 psi)**; velocity ≈ **15–22 m/s**

**Intuition — "Targeted Extinguishing Shower":**
- **Medium velocity** = a **heavy rain** over a surface — good for **cooling** tanks and structures.
- **High velocity** = a **pressure jet** that atomizes water into very fine droplets — these droplets penetrate a fire's flame zone, cool the fuel, and extinguish the fire *rapidly* (especially for flammable liquid fires).

| Feature | Medium Velocity (MV) | High Velocity (HV) |
|---|---|---|
| Nozzle pressure | 2.8 – 3.5 bar | ≥ 3.5 bar |
| Droplet size | Larger | Very fine (atomized) |
| Application | Cooling of tanks, structures | **Flammable liquid fires, transformers** |
| Density | 10.2 L/min/m² (typical) | 20.4 L/min/m² (typical) |
| Range | ~3–6 m | ~6–9 m |

---

## 1.7 Principles of Water Sprinkler System Design (IS 15105 / NFPA 13)

**Core Design Objectives (in order of priority):**
1. **Life Safety** — protect occupants
2. **Property Protection** — limit fire spread
3. **Continuity of Operations** — minimize downtime

**Key Design Parameters (per IS 15105):**
- **Design Density** (L/min/m²) — water applied per unit area
- **Area of Operation** (m²) — design area assumed on fire
- **Duration of Supply** (minutes) — water supply duration
- **Sprinkler Spacing** (m²) — coverage per sprinkler
- **Hazard Classification** — LH (Light), OH (Ordinary Grp 1–4), HH (High)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU/IS Hazard Classification (IS 15105):**
> - **LH** (Light Hazard): Offices, churches — density 2.25 L/min/m²
> - **OH-1 to OH-4** (Ordinary Hazard): Workshops, parking — 5–12.5 L/min/m²
> - **HH** (High Hazard): Cotton mills, chemical storage — 12.5–25 L/min/m²
> - **Storage (HS-1 to HS-4):** Warehouses with stored commodities

---

## 1.8 Hydraulic Calculations — The Heart of Sprinkler Design

> [!NOTE]
> **Hydraulic Calculation (IS 15105 / NFPA 13):** A method of sizing the water supply and piping network for a sprinkler system by balancing the available water pressure against pipe friction losses, elevation changes, and required sprinkler discharge pressures.

**Intuition — "Water's Energy Budget":**
Water has a fixed amount of pressure energy coming from the pump/tank. This energy must be distributed to overcome:
- **Friction loss** in pipes (energy lost to pipe walls)
- **Elevation head** (energy used to lift water to upper floors)
- **Nozzle pressure** (energy needed at each sprinkler to discharge water)

If **Available Pressure ≥ Total Demand + Friction + Elevation**, the system works.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Detailed Operational Analysis

### 2.1.1 Deluge Valve Operation — Step by Step
1. **At-Rest Position:** Water supply at full city/main pressure pushes against the deluge valve clapper. The clapper is held CLOSED by water pressure on a *small auxiliary chamber* (cover chamber), with the auxiliary chamber pressure maintained through a **pilot line**.
2. **Detection Event:** A heat sensor / smoke detector / flame detector (electric or pneumatic) senses fire.
3. **Solenoid / Pilot Release:** The detector sends a signal that **releases the pressure** in the auxiliary chamber (solenoid opens, or pilot sprinkler fuses, or pneumatic actuator releases).
4. **Pressure Imbalance:** Now the water pressure on the *main* clapper (larger area) is greater than on the auxiliary chamber (smaller area). The clapper **snaps open**.
5. **Water Floods System:** Water rushes through the open deluge valve into the open-nozzle system → all nozzles discharge simultaneously.
6. **Alarm:** A flow switch (pressure switch) on the alarm line triggers the fire alarm.

### 2.1.2 Pre-Action Valve Operation — Three States
- **State 1 — Standby (Interlock):** Piping has air at low pressure; valve closed; **detector armed**.
- **State 2 — Detection (Pre-Action):** Detector trips → pre-action valve opens → water fills pipes. **But sprinklers are still closed** (no water discharge).
- **State 3 — Sprinkler Operation:** If a sprinkler fuses from heat, water discharges at that sprinkler **only**.

This **two-stage logic** prevents water damage from a single fault.

### 2.1.3 Hydraulic Calculation — Darcy-Weisbach & Hazen-Williams

The **Hazen-Williams equation** is the most common in sprinkler hydraulics (per IS 15105 / NFPA 13):

$$ P_f = \frac{4.52 \cdot Q^{1.85}}{C^{1.85} \cdot d^{4.87}} \cdot L $$

where:
- $P_f$ = friction loss in **bar** (when using SI units)
- $Q$ = flow in **L/min**
- $C$ = Hazen-Williams coefficient (cast iron = 100, steel = 120, cement-lined = 130, plastic = 150)
- $d$ = internal pipe diameter in **mm**
- $L$ = equivalent length of pipe in **m**

**Equivalent length** includes straight pipe + fittings (each elbow, tee, valve adds length based on the *L/D* ratio from friction loss tables).

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use the formulas below in all hydraulic calculation problems on the exam. Memorize the sprinkler discharge formula — it carries the most marks.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = K \sqrt{P}$ | Sprinkler discharge (K-factor method) | Q in L/min, P in bar |
| 2 | $P_f = \dfrac{4.52 \cdot Q^{1.85}}{C^{1.85} \cdot d^{4.87}} \cdot L$ | Hazen-Williams friction loss (SI) | bar, L/min, m, mm |
| 3 | $P_e = 0.098 \cdot h$ | Elevation pressure head (water) | bar, m |
| 4 | $P_{total} = P_{sprinkler} + P_{friction} + P_{elevation}$ | Total required pressure at pump | bar |
| 5 | $A_{design} = N_{s} \cdot S_s$ | Design area = no. of sprinklers × spacing | m² |
| 6 | $Q_{total} = N_{s} \cdot q_{avg}$ | Total flow for design area | L/min |
| 7 | $V_{tank} = Q_{total} \cdot t$ | Tank volume (duration) | L, t in min |
| 8 | $S_{hanger,max} = f(d_{nominal})$ | Hanger max spacing (table) | m |
| 9 | $P_{dry-trip} = P_{water} \cdot \dfrac{A_{clapper}}{A_{aux}} - \Delta P$ | Dry valve trip condition | bar |
| 10 | $P_{alarm,min} = 0.5$ bar | Min alarm pressure for water motor gong | bar |

**Conversion Note:**
- **1 bar = 14.5 psi = 10.2 m of water head**
- **1 L/min ≈ 0.264 GPM**
- **1 m² ≈ 10.76 ft²**

### Hazard Classification Quick Reference (IS 15105)

| Hazard Class | Design Density (L/min/m²) | Design Area (m²) | Duration (min) | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| LH | 2.25 | 72 | 30 | Offices, hospitals |
| OH-1 | 5.0 | 72 | 60 | Car parks, bakeries |
| OH-2 | 5.0 | 144 | 60 | Workshops |
| OH-3 | 5.0 | 216 | 60 | Mercantile |
| OH-4 | 5.0 | 360 | 60 | Paper mills |
| HHS-1 | 12.5 | 260 | 90 | Cotton storage |
| HHS-2 | 16.0 | 260 | 90 | Wood, paper storage |
| HHS-3 | 25.0 | 260 | 120 | Chemical storage |

### Hanger Spacing Quick Reference (Steel Pipe, IS 15105)

| Pipe Diameter (mm) | Max Spacing (m) |
|---|---|
| 25 | 3.6 |
| 32 | 3.6 |
| 40 | 3.6 |
| 50 | 3.6 |
| 65 | 3.6 |
| 80 | 3.0 |
| 100 | 3.0 |
| 150 | 2.4 |
| 200 | 1.8 |

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| System | Real Application |
|---|---|
| Deluge | LPG storage spheres, transformer yards, aircraft hangars |
| Pre-Action | Server data centers, semiconductor fabs, museums |
| Drencher | External façade protection, transformer cooling |
| HV Spray | Oil-cooled transformers, chemical reactors |
| MV Spray | Tank shell cooling, structural steel protection |
| Wet Alarm Valve | Office buildings, hotels, hospitals |
| Dry Valve | Cold storage, parking garages, unheated warehouses |
| Hangers & Sway Bracing | All installations — especially in seismic zones (Kerala Zone III) |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of Sprinkler Discharge Equation (K-Factor)

The sprinkler nozzle discharge is derived from **Torricelli's Law**, which relates fluid velocity to pressure head.

**Step 1 — Velocity from Pressure Head (Torricelli's Law):**

$$ v = \sqrt{2 \cdot g \cdot h} $$

where:
- $v$ = exit velocity (m/s)
- $g$ = acceleration due to gravity = **9.81 m/s²**
- $h$ = pressure head (m of water)

**Step 2 — Convert Pressure Head to Pressure:**
For water, $P = \rho \cdot g \cdot h$, so:

$$ h = \frac{P}{\rho \cdot g} $$

Substituting into Torricelli's Law:

$$ v = \sqrt{2 \cdot g \cdot \frac{P}{\rho \cdot g}} = \sqrt{\frac{2P}{\rho}} $$

**Step 3 — Flow Rate from Velocity and Orifice Area:**

$$ Q = C_d \cdot A \cdot v $$

where:
- $C_d$ = discharge coefficient (typically 0.7–0.9)
- $A$ = orifice area (m²)
- $Q$ = volumetric flow (m³/s)

**Step 4 — Combine:**

$$ Q = C_d \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2P}{\rho}} $$

**Step 5 — Group Constants into K-factor:**
Define $K = C_d \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2}{\rho}}$, which gives:

$$ Q = K \cdot \sqrt{P} $$

**Step 6 — Apply SI Unit Conversion (P in bar, Q in L/min):**
With proper conversion, the **standard sprinkler discharge formula** is:

$$ \boxed{\;Q = K \cdot \sqrt{P}\;} \quad [\text{Q in L/min, P in bar}] $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Common K-factors (metric):**
> - K-80 → orifice 11 mm (small)
> - K-115 → orifice 15 mm (standard residential)
> - K-160 → orifice 20 mm (standard commercial)
> - K-200 → large orifice

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — Hydraulic Calculation for a Sprinkler Grid

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is a typical KTU university problem. Master the four-step method: (1) Q at sprinkler, (2) Friction loss, (3) Branch summation, (4) Total pressure at pump.**

**Problem Statement:**
A wet pipe sprinkler system is to be designed for a **High Hazard Storage (HHS-1)** occupancy. The most remote sprinkler has a **K-160** factor. Required design pressure at the remote sprinkler = **0.7 bar**. Pipe diameters and equivalent lengths are given in the table below. Determine the **total pressure required at the riser**.

| Segment | Diameter (mm) | Q (L/min) | Equivalent Length (m) |
|---|---|---|---|
| A → B (most remote) | 25 | 200 (1 sprinkler) | 5.0 |
| B → C (branch cross) | 32 | 400 (2 sprinklers) | 4.5 |
| C → D (riser) | 50 | 1200 (6 sprinklers) | 12.0 |
| D → Pump | 80 | 3600 (18 sprinklers) | 8.0 |

Assume C = 120 (steel), elevation = 0 (no height head).

### Step 1 — Verify the Remote Sprinkler Pressure

$$ Q_1 = K \sqrt{P_1} $$

$$ 200 = 160 \cdot \sqrt{P_1} $$

$$ \sqrt{P_1} = \frac{200}{160} = 1.25 $$

$$ P_1 = 1.5625 \text{ bar} $$

This is the **actual pressure** at sprinkler A, which must be ≥ 0.7 bar. ✓ Satisfies minimum.

### Step 2 — Friction Loss in Segment A → B

Using Hazen-Williams (SI):

$$ P_f = \frac{4.52 \cdot Q^{1.85}}{C^{1.85} \cdot d^{4.87}} \cdot L $$

Substituting Q = 200, C = 120, d = 25, L = 5.0:

$$ P_{f,AB} = \frac{4.52 \cdot (200)^{1.85}}{(120)^{1.85} \cdot (25)^{4.87}} \cdot 5.0 $$

Compute step-by-step:

- $200^{1.85} \approx 22{,}627$
- $120^{1.85} \approx 9{,}521$
- $25^{4.87} \approx 5.07 \times 10^6$

$$ P_{f,AB} = \frac{4.52 \cdot 22{,}627}{9{,}521 \cdot 5.07 \times 10^6} \cdot 5.0 $$

$$ P_{f,AB} = \frac{102{,}274}{4.827 \times 10^{10}} \cdot 5.0 \approx 0.0106 \text{ bar} $$

### Step 3 — Friction Loss in Segment B → C (Q = 400 L/min, d = 32 mm)

- $400^{1.85} \approx 102{,}627$
- $32^{4.87} \approx 1.55 \times 10^7$

$$ P_{f,BC} = \frac{4.52 \cdot 102{,}627}{9{,}521 \cdot 1.55 \times 10^7} \cdot 4.5 $$

$$ P_{f,BC} = \frac{463{,}874}{1.476 \times 10^{11}} \cdot 4.5 \approx 0.0141 \text{ bar} $$

### Step 4 — Friction Loss in Segment C → D (Q = 1200 L/min, d = 50 mm)

- $1200^{1.85} \approx 622{,}600$
- $50^{4.87} \approx 1.91 \times 10^8$

$$ P_{f,CD} = \frac{4.52 \cdot 622{,}600}{9{,}521 \cdot 1.91 \times 10^8} \cdot 12.0 $$

$$ P_{f,CD} = \frac{2.814 \times 10^6}{1.819 \times 10^{12}} \cdot 12.0 \approx 0.0186 \text{ bar} $$

### Step 5 — Friction Loss in Segment D → Pump (Q = 3600 L/min, d = 80 mm)

- $3600^{1.85} \approx 4.65 \times 10^6$
- $80^{4.87} \approx 2.50 \times 10^9$

$$ P_{f,DP} = \frac{4.52 \cdot 4.65 \times 10^6}{9{,}521 \cdot 2.50 \times 10^9} \cdot 8.0 $$

$$ P_{f,DP} = \frac{2.10 \times 10^7}{2.38 \times 10^{13}} \cdot 8.0 \approx 0.0071 \text{ bar} $$

### Step 6 — Total Pressure at Pump

$$ P_{pump} = P_{sprinkler} + \sum P_{friction} $$

$$ P_{pump} = 1.5625 + 0.0106 + 0.0141 + 0.0186 + 0.0071 $$

$$ \boxed{P_{pump} \approx 1.61 \text{ bar}} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Note:** Real sprinkler systems typically require 5–10 bar at the pump for commercial installations. The example uses small pipes (low friction) and few sprinklers, hence low pressure. In practice, pipe diameters are increased at the riser to keep velocity ≤ 6 m/s and friction reasonable.

### Step 7 — Sizing the Pump Flow

For HHS-1 with 18 sprinklers in the design area:

$$ Q_{total} = N_s \cdot q_{avg} = 18 \cdot 200 = 3600 \text{ L/min} $$

This matches our segment D → Pump flow. ✓

### Step 8 — Tank Sizing (90 min duration for HHS-1)

$$ V_{tank} = Q_{total} \cdot t = 3600 \cdot 90 = 324{,}000 \text{ L} = 324 \text{ m}^3 $$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparing All Control Valves

| Parameter | Wet Alarm Valve | Dry Pipe Valve | Deluge Valve | Pre-Action Valve |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Pipe condition** | Water-filled | Air-filled (pressurized) | Air (open nozzles) | Air (low pressure) |
| **Detection by** | Sprinkler fuse | Sprinkler fuse | External detector | Detector + sprinkler |
| **Trip signal** | Pressure drop | Air pressure drop | Detector signal | Detector signal |
| **Trip mechanism** | Hydraulic | Pneumatic | Solenoid / Pilot | Solenoid / Pilot |
| **Water delivery** | Immediate | Up to 60 s | Immediate (open) | Pre-fills then discharges |
| **Accidental discharge** | Possible | Unlikely | Possible | Very unlikely |
| **Application** | Heated buildings | Freezing areas | High-hazard deluge | High-value assets |
| **Alarm type** | Water motor | Pressure switch | Pressure switch | Pressure switch |
| **IS Standard** | IS 15105 | IS 15105 | IS 15301 | IS 15301 |
| **NFPA Reference** | NFPA 13 Ch. 7 | NFPA 13 Ch. 8 | NFPA 13 Ch. 11 | NFPA 13 Ch. 9 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block Architecture — Wet Pipe Sprinkler System

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[City Water Supply] --> B[Fire Pump Room]
    B --> C[Check Valve]
    C --> D[Wet Alarm Control Valve]
    D --> E[Riser Pipe]
    E --> F[Main Cross]
    F --> G[Branch Line 1]
    F --> H[Branch Line 2]
    F --> I[Branch Line 3]
    G --> J1[Sprinkler Head 1]
    G --> J2[Sprinkler Head 2]
    G --> J3[Sprinkler Head 3]
    H --> K1[Sprinkler Head 4]
    H --> K2[Sprinkler Head 5]
    I --> L1[Sprinkler Head 6]
    D --> M[Retard Chamber]
    M --> N[Water Motor Gong]
    M --> O[Alarm Pressure Switch]
    O --> P[Fire Alarm Panel]
    D --> Q[Drain Valve]
    D --> R[Pressure Gauge Supply Side]
    D --> S[Pressure Gauge System Side]
```

**Block Logic:** City water enters through the fire pump, passes the check valve (prevents backflow), is monitored by the wet alarm valve, and rises vertically through the riser. The riser feeds horizontal branch lines carrying individual sprinklers. The retard chamber suppresses false alarms (water hammer) and triggers both the mechanical (water motor gong) and electrical (pressure switch → fire alarm panel) alarm signals. Pressure gauges on either side of the valve verify valve position.

---

## 4.2 Block Architecture — Deluge System

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Water Supply Main] --> B[Deluge Valve]
    B --> C[Cover Chamber Auxiliary]
    C --> D[Pilot Line]
    D --> E[Heat / Smoke / Flame Detector]
    E --> F[Solenoid Release Valve]
    F --> G[Pressure Release to Drain]
    E --> H[Fire Alarm Panel]
    H --> F
    B --> I[Deluge Riser]
    I --> J[Open Spray Nozzle Bank 1]
    I --> K[Open Spray Nozzle Bank 2]
    I --> L[Open Spray Nozzle Bank 3]
    I --> M[Open Spray Nozzle Bank 4]
    B --> N[Alarm Pressure Switch]
    N --> O[Fire Alarm Bell]
    B --> P[Drain Valve]
    B --> Q[Pressure Gauges]
```

**Block Logic:** Water is held back by the deluge valve, pressurized against a small cover chamber. A pilot line maintains this auxiliary pressure. A separate fire detection system (heat, smoke, or flame sensor) trips the solenoid release valve, which vents the cover chamber to drain. The pressure imbalance opens the deluge valve, flooding the open nozzle banks simultaneously. The alarm pressure switch sounds the fire alarm.

---

## 4.3 Block Architecture — Dry Pipe / Pre-Action System

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Water Supply] --> B[Dry or Pre-Action Valve]
    B --> C[Air Compressor or Nitrogen Cylinder]
    C --> D[Air Pressure Maintenance]
    B --> E[Riser Pipe]
    E --> F[Branch Line]
    F --> G1[Sprinkler with Fusible Link 1]
    F --> G2[Sprinkler with Fusible Link 2]
    F --> G3[Sprinkler with Fusible Link 3]
    B --> H[Air Pressure Switch]
    H --> I[Fire Alarm Panel Low Pressure]
    I --> J[Solenoid Pre-Action Release]
    J --> B
    B --> K[Quick Opening Device]
    K --> L[Drain]
    B --> M[Drain Valve]
    B --> N[Pressure Gauges]
```

**Block Logic:** The piping is filled with pressurized air (no water). The dry/pre-action valve holds water back. The air compressor maintains system pressure. A drop in air pressure (caused by an open sprinkler) is detected by the air pressure switch, which signals the fire panel. In a *dry system*, this directly admits water. In a *pre-action system*, the fire panel triggers a solenoid that opens the pre-action valve, allowing water to fill the pipes — but actual discharge only occurs when a sprinkler fuse melts from heat.

---

## 4.4 Hydraulic Calculation Flow — Pressure Summation

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Start: Design Density and Area] --> B[Determine Number of Sprinklers in Design Area]
    B --> C[Compute Required Flow per Sprinkler Q = K sqrt P]
    C --> D[Branch Line Flow Summation]
    D --> E[Cross Main Flow Summation]
    E --> F[Riser Flow Summation]
    F --> G[Compute Friction Loss per Pipe Segment Hazen Williams]
    G --> H[Sum Total Friction Loss]
    H --> I[Add Elevation Head Loss P = 0.098 h]
    I --> J[Add Required Sprinkler Pressure]
    J --> K[Total Required Pressure at Pump]
    K --> L[Check Against Available Water Supply]
    L --> M{Available Pressure Sufficient?}
    M -- No --> N[Resize Pipe Diameters or Add Pump]
    N --> G
    M -- Yes --> O[Final System Approved]
```

**Block Logic:** Hydraulic calculations start at the most remote sprinkler (the worst-case point with the lowest pressure) and proceed backward to the water supply. At each pipe segment, friction loss is calculated using the Hazen-Williams equation. The total pressure at the pump must be balanced against the available water supply. If insufficient, pipe diameters are increased (to reduce friction) or a fire pump is added.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Question 1: Define a "Drencher System" and state two applications. `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> A drencher system is an open water spray system designed to protect specific external or internal surfaces (such as tank shells, structural steel, or building façades) from radiant heat exposure or to cool process equipment. Unlike a deluge system, drenchers are oriented toward *surface cooling and heat absorption* rather than volume flooding.
>
> **Applications (any two):**
> 1. External façade cooling to prevent ignition from a neighbouring fire
> 2. Transformer body cooling in electrical substations
> 3. LPG / oil storage tank shell cooling
> 4. Protection of structural steel in industrial buildings

**Marks Distribution (KTU pattern):**
- 1 mark — Correct definition
- 1 mark — Two applications listed
- 1 mark — Distinction from deluge

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between Pre-Action and Deluge Sprinkler Systems. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
| Parameter | Pre-Action | Deluge |
|---|---|---|
| Detection by | Sprinkler + External detector (two events needed) | External detector only (one event) |
| Nozzle type | Closed (with fusible link) | Open |
| Pipe content | Air (low pressure) → fills with water on detection | Air (open to nozzles) |
| Discharge | Only at sprinklers that open (post-detection) | All nozzles simultaneously |
| Accidental discharge risk | Very low | Higher |

**Marks Distribution:**
- 1 mark — Detection distinction
- 1 mark — Nozzle/pipe content
- 1 mark — Accidental discharge / application

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the construction and working of a **Deluge Valve**. State its applications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A wet pipe sprinkler system protects an **OH-2** (Ordinary Hazard Group 2) occupancy. The most remote sprinkler is **K-160** with a minimum required pressure of **0.7 bar**. Compute the **flow at the most remote sprinkler** and the **friction loss** in the connecting pipe (25 mm dia, 6 m equivalent length, C = 120). Also calculate the **design density** and **total water supply required** for a design area of 144 m², 60 min duration. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution A(a) — Deluge Valve (7 marks)

**Definition (1 mark):**
A deluge valve is a quick-opening, hydraulically-operated diaphragm or clapper valve that admits water into a deluge system (open-nozzle system) upon actuation of a separate fire detection system. It is held closed by water pressure in a small auxiliary cover chamber.

**Construction (2 marks):**
The deluge valve has the following key components:
1. **Body** — large waterway with flanged inlet/outlet
2. **Main Clapper / Diaphragm** — covers the waterway
3. **Cover Chamber** — small auxiliary chamber above the clapper, fed by a pilot line
4. **Diaphragm Plate** — separates cover chamber from main waterway
5. **Solenoid Valve** (or pilot sprinkler) — releases cover chamber pressure
6. **Drain Trim** — vents cover chamber to drain
7. **Alarm Pressure Switch** — mounted on the alarm line

**Working (3 marks):**

| Step | Action |
|---|---|
| 1 | At-rest: water supply pressure pushes against the clapper. Cover chamber pressure (from pilot line) holds clapper CLOSED (differential area design). |
| 2 | Fire detection: heat/smoke/flame detector senses fire and signals solenoid release. |
| 3 | Solenoid opens → cover chamber pressure drops to drain. |
| 4 | Main waterway pressure now exceeds cover chamber pressure → clapper snaps open. |
| 5 | Water floods the open-nozzle deluge system → all nozzles discharge simultaneously. |
| 6 | Alarm pressure switch trips → fire alarm activates. |

**Applications (1 mark):**
- Aircraft hangars
- Transformer yards
- LPG storage spheres
- Flammable liquid chemical plants
- High-rack storage warehouses

---

### Model Solution A(b) — Hydraulic Calculation (7 marks)

**Given:**
- Sprinkler K-factor: K = 160
- Required sprinkler pressure: P = 0.7 bar
- Pipe: d = 25 mm, L_eq = 6 m, C = 120

**Step 1 — Flow at Most Remote Sprinkler (2 marks):**

$$ Q = K \sqrt{P} $$

$$ Q = 160 \cdot \sqrt{0.7} $$

$$ Q = 160 \cdot 0.8367 $$

$$ \boxed{Q \approx 133.87 \text{ L/min} \approx 134 \text{ L/min}} $$

**Step 2 — Friction Loss in Pipe (2 marks):**

$$ P_f = \frac{4.52 \cdot Q^{1.85}}{C^{1.85} \cdot d^{4.87}} \cdot L $$

With Q = 134:
- $Q^{1.85} = 134^{1.85} \approx 11{,}870$
- $C^{1.85} = 120^{1.85} \approx 9{,}521$
- $d^{4.87} = 25^{4.87} \approx 5.07 \times 10^6$

$$ P_f = \frac{4.52 \cdot 11{,}870}{9{,}521 \cdot 5.07 \times 10^6} \cdot 6.0 $$

$$ P_f = \frac{53{,}652}{4.83 \times 10^{10}} \cdot 6.0 $$

$$ P_f = 1.11 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 6.0 \approx 0.0067 \text{ bar} $$

**Step 3 — Design Density (1 mark):**
For OH-2 per IS 15105:
$$ \rho_{design} = 5.0 \text{ L/min/m}^2 $$

**Step 4 — Total Water Supply Required (2 marks):**

$$ Q_{total} = \rho_{design} \cdot A_{design} = 5.0 \cdot 144 = 720 \text{ L/min} $$

$$ V_{tank} = Q_{total} \cdot t = 720 \cdot 60 = 43{,}200 \text{ L} $$

$$ \boxed{V_{tank} = 43.2 \text{ m}^3} $$

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks): Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the construction and working of a **Dry Pipe Valve**. State its trip pressure relationship and the maximum permissible water delivery time. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A wet pipe sprinkler system in a **High Hazard Storage (HHS-2)** area has **15 sprinklers** in the design area. Each sprinkler has **K = 160** and the most remote sprinkler pressure is **0.85 bar**. Calculate:
   (i) Flow at the most remote sprinkler
   (ii) Friction loss in a 32 mm pipe segment carrying 3 sprinklers (L = 8 m, C = 120)
   (iii) Total flow at the riser if each sprinkler discharges 200 L/min
   (iv) Tank capacity for 90 min duration **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution B(a) — Dry Pipe Valve (7 marks)

**Definition (1 mark):**
A dry pipe valve is an automatic valve that admits water into a dry pipe sprinkler system when the air pressure in the system falls below a set threshold (after a sprinkler opens).

**Construction (2 marks):**
1. **Body** — flanged cast iron body
2. **Clapper** — pivoting clapper with differential area design
3. **Intermediate Chamber** — air pressure chamber
4. **Air Supply Connection** — from compressor or nitrogen cylinder
5. **Water Supply Connection** — from city main / fire pump
6. **Quick-Opening Device** — accelerates water entry
7. **Drain Trim & Pressure Gauges**

**Working (2 marks):**
- At-rest: air pressure in intermediate chamber + system side holds clapper closed against water pressure.
- A sprinkler opens → air pressure drops → clapper trips open → water enters system.

**Trip Pressure Relationship (1 mark):**

$$ P_{trip} = P_{water} \cdot \frac{A_{clapper}}{A_{intermediate}} - \Delta P_{margin} $$

Typically, system air pressure is maintained at **2.5–3.5 bar**, with water supply at **5–8 bar**.

**Water Delivery Time (1 mark):**
Maximum permissible time for water to reach the most remote sprinkler is **60 seconds** per NFPA 13 (and IS 15105). For high-hazard areas, **30 seconds** is preferred.

---

### Model Solution B(b) — HHS-2 Calculation (7 marks)

**(i) Flow at most remote sprinkler (2 marks):**

$$ Q = K \sqrt{P} = 160 \cdot \sqrt{0.85} = 160 \cdot 0.9220 = 147.5 \text{ L/min} $$

**Verification against problem data:** The problem states *each sprinkler discharges 200 L/min*. This means **Q_actual = 200 L/min** at the design point (since hydraulic calculations start from the most remote). So we use Q = 200 L/min for friction loss.

**(ii) Friction loss in 32 mm pipe with Q = 3 × 200 = 600 L/min (2 marks):**

$$ P_f = \frac{4.52 \cdot (600)^{1.85}}{(120)^{1.85} \cdot (32)^{4.87}} \cdot 8.0 $$

- $600^{1.85} \approx 191{,}500$
- $32^{4.87} \approx 1.55 \times 10^7$

$$ P_f = \frac{4.52 \cdot 191{,}500}{9{,}521 \cdot 1.55 \times 10^7} \cdot 8.0 $$

$$ P_f = \frac{865{,}580}{1.476 \times 10^{11}} \cdot 8.0 \approx 0.0469 \text{ bar} $$

**(iii) Total flow at the riser (1 mark):**

$$ Q_{riser} = N_s \cdot q_{sprinkler} = 15 \cdot 200 = 3000 \text{ L/min} $$

**(iv) Tank capacity for 90 min (2 marks):**

$$ V_{tank} = Q_{riser} \cdot t = 3000 \cdot 90 = 270{,}000 \text{ L} = 270 \text{ m}^3 $$

$$ \boxed{V_{tank} = 270 \text{ m}^3} $$

---

## ⚠️ KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING / PITFALL CALLOUT

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Points in Hydraulic Calculation Questions (Examiner's Perspective):**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units** — Using psi instead of bar, or GPM instead of L/min without conversion. Always declare units explicitly.
> 2. **Wrong value of K-factor** — K = 80 ≠ K = 160. The orifice size matters. State the K-factor in your diagram.
> 3. **Missing the elevation head** — $P_e = 0.098 \cdot h$ is often forgotten. If the problem has any height, add 0.098 × h_bar to the total.
> 4. **Adding friction loss in only ONE segment** — For a multi-segment network, **sum ALL segments** from the most remote sprinkler to the pump.
> 5. **Confusing density and flow** — Density is per unit area (L/min/m²); flow is total (L/min). Don't multiply by duration for the *pump* requirement.
> 6. **Forgetting tank duration** — Use 30 min for LH, 60 min for OH, 90 min for HHS, 120 min for HHS-3 (per IS 15105).
> 7. **Mixing up Pre-Action and Deluge** — Pre-action has *closed* sprinklers; deluge has *open* sprinklers. State this clearly.
> 8. **In dry-pipe valve, the trip is from AIR pressure drop, not heat directly** — The detector is the sprinkler head, but the trip signal is the air pressure change.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Memorize for KTU Exam)**

### Core Valve Distinctions
- **Wet Alarm Valve** → water in pipe, immediate discharge, heat-fuse sprinkler detection
- **Dry Pipe Valve** → air in pipe, sprinkler opens → air drops → valve trips, max 60 s delivery
- **Deluge Valve** → open nozzles, external detector only, immediate full-area flooding
- **Pre-Action Valve** → air in pipe, requires BOTH detector AND sprinkler opening to discharge
- **Drencher** → open nozzles, focused on **surface cooling** (not volume flood)

### Spray System Differences
- **MV (Medium Velocity):** 2.8 bar nozzle pressure, larger droplets, surface cooling
- **HV (High Velocity):** 3.5+ bar, atomized droplets, fast extinguishment of liquid fires

### Hydraulic Calculation Essentials
- **Sprinkler discharge:** $Q = K\sqrt{P}$
- **Friction loss:** $P_f = \dfrac{4.52 \cdot Q^{1.85}}{C^{1.85} \cdot d^{4.87}} \cdot L$
- **Elevation head:** $P_e = 0.098 \cdot h$
- **Total pressure at pump** = $P_{sprinkler} + \Sigma P_{friction} + P_{elevation}$
- **Hazen-Williams C-value:** Steel = 120, Cast Iron = 100, Cement-lined = 130, Plastic = 150
- **Standard design densities (L/min/m²):** LH = 2.25, OH = 5.0, HHS-1 = 12.5, HHS-2 = 16, HHS-3 = 25

### Hanger Rules
- Hangers carry **vertical (gravity)** load
- Sway braces carry **lateral** load (seismic / wind)
- Spacing depends on pipe diameter (25 mm → 3.6 m, 150 mm → 2.4 m)
- For dry systems, support pipe at both sides of the dry valve

### Key Standards
- **IS 15105** — Automatic Sprinkler Systems (Indian Standard, equivalent to NFPA 13)
- **IS 15301** — Deluge and Pre-Action Systems
- **IS 15325** — Water Spray Systems
- **NFPA 13** — Standard for the Installation of Sprinkler Systems (International)
- **NFPA 15** — Standard for Water Spray Fixed Systems

### Final Memory Hooks
- **Kerala = Seismic Zone III** → sway bracing is mandatory
- **Hazard classes increase:** LH < OH < HHS (in both density and water demand)
- **Always work backward** in hydraulics: from most remote sprinkler → to pump
- **Add equivalent lengths** of fittings (elbows, tees) to straight pipe length
- **Tank duration:** LH=30, OH=60, HHS-1/2=90, HHS-3=120 minutes (per IS 15105)
- **Conversion:** 1 bar = 14.5 psi = 10.2 m of water head

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Area-Density Curve. Sprinkler Design Considerations for Warehouse Storage in a Manufacturing Plant. Fire Protection of Specialised Occupancies - Computer Rooms - Communication Centre - Airport
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT836 - DESIGN OF FIRE PROTECTION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Area-Density Curve. Sprinkler Design Considerations for Warehouse Storage in a Manufacturing Plant. Fire Protection of Specialised Occupancies - Computer Rooms - Communication Centre - Airport

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Area-Density Curve (The Foundation of Sprinkler Design)

**Formal Definition (KTU/NFPA 13 Terminology):**
The **Area-Density Curve** is a graphical and mathematical relationship prescribed by **NFPA 13 (Standard for the Installation of Sprinkler Systems)** that correlates the **minimum design area of sprinkler operation** (in square feet or square metres) with the corresponding **required water discharge density** (in GPM/ft² or mm/min) for a given occupancy hazard classification.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Principle:** The Area-Density Curve embodies an inverse (hyperbolic) relationship — as the design area increases, the required water density decreases. This reflects a fundamental fire engineering trade-off: *either concentrate more water over a smaller area OR spread moderate water over a larger area to control the same fire load.*

**Conceptual Analogy — "The Watering Can Principle":**
Imagine you have a fixed total amount of water (your water supply). You must decide how to distribute it on a burning field (the fire area):
- *Concentrate it:* A small, dense puddle on the hottest spot → less area covered but intense cooling.
- *Spread it out:* A thin, even film across the whole field → larger area covered but each spot gets less water.

The Area-Density Curve tells you the *optimal balance point* for each type of occupancy (office, warehouse, factory, etc.) based on empirical fire test data.

---

### 1.2 Sprinkler Design Considerations for Warehouse Storage in a Manufacturing Plant

**Formal Definition:**
Warehouse sprinkler design is a specialised branch of fire protection engineering that addresses the unique challenges of **high-piled storage**, **diverse commodity classifications**, **rack storage configurations**, and **large unobstructed ceiling heights**. Design parameters are derived from **NFPA 13, Chapter 20 (Protection of High-Piled and Rack Storage)** and **FM Global Data Sheets (e.g., DS 8-9)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Design Drivers:**
> 1. **Commodity Classification** (Class I → Class IV → Group A Plastics)
> 2. **Storage Height** (low-pile < 12 ft vs. high-pile > 12 ft)
> 3. **Storage Configuration** (palletized, rack, bin-box, on-floor)
> 4. **Aisle Width** (minimum 8 ft / 2.4 m for manual firefighting)
> 5. **Ceiling Height** (affects sprinkler spray pattern)
> 6. **Encapsulation** (pallet wrap drastically increases fire severity)

**Conceptual Analogy — "The Library Fire Problem":**
Think of a warehouse as a giant library storing millions of books. A small fire on the top shelf can:
- Spread upward through convection (the **chimney effect** in rack flue spaces)
- Spread horizontally along the ceiling (the **ceiling jet**)
- Penetrate deep into the rack via **horizontal fire spread**

A warehouse sprinkler system must therefore fight fires on **three fronts simultaneously** — ceiling level, intermediate rack levels (via *in-rack sprinklers*), and the floor level.

---

### 1.3 Fire Protection of Specialised Occupancies

#### A. Computer Rooms
**Definition:** Critical electronic equipment spaces housing servers, mainframes, and data storage requiring **continuous operation** and **zero water damage tolerance**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Computer rooms are protected by a dual-layered defence:**
> 1. **Primary:** Clean Agent Gas Suppression (FM-200, Novec 1230, Inert Gas — IG-541)
> 2. **Secondary:** Pre-Action Sprinkler System (water only as last resort)

**Analogy:** Computer rooms are like a **museum full of priceless paintings** — the first response is a *gentle, non-damaging gas blanket* that smothers the fire without harming the artwork (servers).

#### B. Communication Centres
**Definition:** Telecommunications hubs (telephone exchanges, data centres, broadcast studios) requiring **99.999% uptime (Five-Nines reliability)** and protection against both fire and **equipment-generated heat/electrical faults**.

#### C. Airports
**Definition:** Large-volume, high-traffic transportation facilities encompassing **terminals, hangars, fuel farms, baggage handling areas, and control towers**, each with unique fire risks (jet fuel, large crowds, structural steel, aircraft fuselages).

> [!NOTE]
> **Airport Fire Protection Triad:**
> 1. **High-Expansion Foam** — for hangars & fuel spills
> 2. **Deluge Sprinklers** — for terminals (fast response, open nozzles)
> 3. **AR-AFFF (Alcohol-Resistant AFFF)** — for jet fuel fires

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Area-Density Curve — Deep Dive

#### 2.1.1 The Underlying Physics

The Area-Density Curve is derived from **large-scale fire tests** conducted by the **Fire Test Council (FTC)** and **FM Global Research**. The empirical relationship for curve generation follows the inverse power law:

$$ q = \frac{K}{A^n} $$

where:
- $q$ = required water density (mm/min or GPM/ft²)
- $A$ = design area of operation (m² or ft²)
- $K$ = occupancy constant (depends on hazard class)
- $n$ = curve shape exponent (typically 0.5 to 1.0)

#### 2.1.2 NFPA 13 Hazard Classifications (The Four Tiers)

| Hazard Class | Density (mm/min) | Density (GPM/ft²) | Design Area (m²) | Design Area (ft²) | Typical Occupancy |
|--------------|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|---|
| **Light Hazard (LH)** | **2.5** | 0.05 | 74 – 139 | 800 – 1500 | Offices, Classrooms, Hospitals |
| **Ordinary Hazard Group 1 (OH-1)** | **5.0** | 0.10 | 74 – 139 | 1500 | Parking, Bakeries |
| **Ordinary Hazard Group 2 (OH-2)** | **5.0** | 0.10 | 139 – 186 | 1500 – 2000 | Libraries, Restaurants |
| **Ordinary Hazard Group 3 (OH-3)** | **5.0** | 0.10 | 186 – 232 | 2000 – 2500 | Manufacturing, Workshops |
| **Extra Hazard Group 1 (EH-1)** | **12.5** | 0.30 | 232 | 2500 | Aircraft hangars, Plastics |
| **Extra Hazard Group 2 (EH-2)** | **12.5** | 0.30 | 279 | 3000 | Flammable liquids storage |

> [!NOTE]
> The **density is FIXED** for a hazard class. The **design AREA** is what you read off the curve based on the *worst-case sprinkler spacing × coverage*.

#### 2.1.3 The Curve Shape — Why Inverse?

The curve is **inverse** (hyperbolic) because of the **Heat Release Rate (HRR) vs. Control Time** physics:
- A small fire over a large area = **each sprinkler does less work**, but more sprinklers operate.
- A large fire over a small area = **each sprinkler does more work**, but fewer operate.

Both scenarios achieve **equivalent fire control** by balancing the **Rate of Heat Release (RHR)** against the **Rate of Heat Absorption by Water Spray**.

---

### 2.2 Warehouse Storage Design Theory

#### 2.2.1 Commodity Classification (NFPA 13 Chapter 20)

| Class | Description | Examples | Fire Severity |
|-------|-------------|----------|:---:|
| **Class I** | Non-combustible product on wooden pallets | Metal parts, glass bottles | Low |
| **Class II** | Class I + limited combustible packaging | Metal parts in cardboard | Low-Medium |
| **Class III** | Wood, paper, natural fibre clothing, Group B plastics | Furniture, books, toys | Medium |
| **Class IV** | Class III + significant Group B plastics | Electronics, appliances | High |
| **Group A Plastics** | ABS, Acrylic, Nylon, Polyethylene, Polypropylene | Toys, foam packaging | **Very High** |
| **Exposed Group A Plastics** | Uncartoned Group A plastics | Loose plastic pellets | **Extreme** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Plastic Rule:** Any commodity containing **> 5% Group A plastics by weight** OR any **exposed Group A plastic** jumps to the **highest commodity class**, requiring the most aggressive sprinkler protection.

#### 2.2.2 ESFR Sprinklers (Early Suppression Fast Response)

**Definition:** ESFR sprinklers are **suppression-mode** sprinklers designed to **extinguish** (not just control) high-challenge fires at the **ceiling level only**, eliminating the need for in-rack sprinklers.

| Parameter | Conventional Sprinkler | ESFR Sprinkler |
|-----------|:---:|:---:|
| **RTI (Response Time Index)** | > 50 (m·s)½ | **< 28 (m·s)½** |
| **Operating Element** | Glass bulb or fusible link | **Fast-response glass bulb** |
| **Mode** | Control | **Suppression** |
| **K-factor** | 5.6, 8.0 | **14.0, 16.8, 22.4, 25.2** |
| **Min. Operating Pressure** | 7 – 25 psi | **25 – 75 psi** |
| **Max. Storage Height** | Limited | **40+ ft (12+ m)** |

#### 2.2.3 In-Rack Sprinkler Placement Logic

For rack storage **> 12 ft (3.7 m)** with **ordinary sprinklers**, NFPA 13 mandates **horizontal barriers** (draft curtains) and **in-rack sprinklers** at intermediate levels:

- **Level 1:** At the top of the first tier (6 ft / 1.8 m above floor)
- **Intermediate levels:** At every 10 ft (3.0 m) of vertical spacing
- **Top level:** At the top of the highest tier (within 10 ft of ceiling)

> [!NOTE]
> **The Flue Space Rule:** A minimum **6 in. (150 mm) transverse flue** and **12 in. (300 mm) longitudinal flue** must be maintained in rack storage to allow water penetration.

---

### 2.3 Specialised Occupancy — Deep Technical Analysis

#### 2.3.1 Computer Room Fire Protection Philosophy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 4-Tier Hierarchy of Computer Room Protection (per NFPA 75):**
> 1. **Tier 1 — Detection:** Aspirating Smoke Detection (VESDA / FAAST) at 0.001% obscuration/ft
> 2. **Tier 2 — Pre-Action Suppression:** Double-interlocked pre-action sprinkler (dry pipe → supervised → water)
> 3. **Tier 3 — Gas Suppression:** Clean agent (FM-200, Novec 1230, Inergen) flooding at 8 – 10% concentration
> 4. **Tier 4 — Water Mist:** High-pressure water mist as final backup

#### 2.3.2 Clean Agent Design Formula

The required clean agent quantity is calculated as:

$$ W = \frac{V}{S} \times \left(\frac{C}{100 - C}\right) $$

where:
- $W$ = agent weight required (kg)
- $V$ = room volume (m³)
- $S$ = specific volume of agent at design temperature (m³/kg)
- $C$ = design concentration (% by volume)

| Agent | Formula | ODP | GWP | Design Conc. |
|-------|---------|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **FM-200 (HFC-227ea)** | CF₃CHFCF₃ | 0 | 3220 | **7.0 – 8.5%** |
| **Novec 1230 (FK-5-1-12)** | CF₃CF₂C(O)CF(CF₃)₂ | 0 | **1** | **4.5 – 6.0%** |
| **Inergen (IG-541)** | N₂ + Ar + CO₂ | 0 | 0 | **38 – 40%** |
| **Halon 1301** *(phased out)* | CBrClF₃ | 12 | 7100 | 5.0% |

#### 2.3.3 Airport Fire Protection — Terminal vs. Hangar

**Terminals:**
- Deluge sprinkler systems with **K-25.2 ESFR sprinklers**
- Smoke management via **mechanical smoke exhaust (10 – 12 air changes/hr)**
- Voice evacuation systems (per **NFPA 72**)

**Hangars:**
- **High-Expansion Foam** (per **NFPA 11**) — expansion ratio 500:1 to 1000:1
- Foam submergence time: **< 4 minutes** for aircraft fuel fires
- **AFFF (Aqueous Film-Forming Foam)** at **6.5 L/min/m²** application rate

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULAS — MEMORISE THESE FOR KTU EXAM**

| # | Formula | Variables | Units | Application |
|---|---------|-----------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | $Q = q \times A$ | $Q$=flow, $q$=density, $A$=area | L/min, L/min/m², m² | Total water demand |
| 2 | $q = \dfrac{K}{A^n}$ | Empirical curve relation | mm/min, m² | Area-Density Curve |
| 3 | $P = \dfrac{Q^2}{K^2}$ | $P$=pressure, $Q$=flow, $K$=K-factor | psi, GPM, GPM/psi½ | Sprinkler outlet pressure |
| 4 | $H_f = \dfrac{4.52 \times Q^{1.85}}{C^{1.85} \times d^{4.87}}$ | Hazen-Williams friction loss | m, L/s, m | Pipe friction loss |
| 5 | $P_2 = P_1 + Z - H_f$ | Branch line pressure calc | psi, ft | Hydraulic calc balance |
| 6 | $W_{agent} = \dfrac{V}{S} \times \dfrac{C}{100-C}$ | Clean agent weight | kg, m³, m³/kg, % | Gas suppression design |
| 7 | $N_{sprinklers} = \dfrac{A_{design}}{A_{sprinkler}}$ | Number of sprinklers in design area | – | Sprinkler count |
| 8 | $t_{discharge} \geq 10$ s (ESFR), $\geq 30$ s (OH) | Minimum water supply duration | sec, min | Duration check |
| 9 | $Q_{pump} = 1.15 \times Q_{max}$ | Pump sizing safety factor | L/min | Fire pump capacity |
| 10 | $V_{smoke} = A_{floor} \times 6$ (imperial) | Smoke reservoir volume | ft³ | Smoke management |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Area-Density Curve method is the **global industry standard** because it:
- Enables **cost-effective** sprinkler design (avoids over-design)
- Provides a **uniform risk-based** approach across all building types
- Allows **performance-based verification** via hydraulic calculations
- Is **legally enforceable** under KTU-approved codes (NFPA 13 adopted by NBC 2016, IS 15105, and Kerala State Fire Service Rules)

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Total Water Demand from Area-Density Curve

**Problem Statement:** A manufacturing plant has an **Ordinary Hazard Group 2 (OH-2)** classification. The most hydraulically demanding area has **16 sprinklers** in the design area, each with a **K-factor of 80** (metric, L/min/√bar). Determine the required **pump capacity** and **tank sizing**.

**Step 1: Identify Hazard Class Parameters (from NFPA 13)**

For **OH-2**:
- Design density: $q = 5.0$ mm/min
- Design area: $A = 139$ m² (worst-case 1500 ft²)

**Step 2: Calculate Total Water Flow Required**

$$ Q_{total} = q \times A $$

$$ Q_{total} = 5.0 \, \frac{L}{min \cdot m^2} \times 139 \, m^2 $$

$$ Q_{total} = 695 \, L/min $$

**Step 3: Account for Hose Stream Demand**

Per **NFPA 13 Table 19.3.3.1.2**, OH-2 requires an additional **250 GPM (≈ 950 L/min)** hose allowance.

$$ Q_{demand} = Q_{total} + Q_{hose} $$

$$ Q_{demand} = 695 + 950 = 1645 \, L/min $$

**Step 4: Apply Pump Safety Factor (1.15)**

$$ Q_{pump} = 1.15 \times Q_{demand} $$

$$ Q_{pump} = 1.15 \times 1645 = 1892 \, L/min \approx 1900 \, L/min $$

**Step 5: Calculate Minimum Water Tank Capacity**

For OH-2, **minimum duration** = **60 minutes** (per IS 15105).

$$ V_{tank} = Q_{pump} \times t $$

$$ V_{tank} = 1900 \, \frac{L}{min} \times 60 \, min $$

$$ V_{tank} = 114{,}000 \, L = 114 \, m^3 $$

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Number of Sprinklers in Design Area (Warehouse Example)

**Problem Statement:** Design a sprinkler system for a **Class IV commodity** stored on **double-row racks** to a height of **25 ft (7.6 m)** in a **30 ft (9.1 m) high warehouse**. Ceiling sprinklers have a **spacing of 100 ft² (9.3 m²)** each.

**Step 1: Determine the Design Density and Area**

Per **NFPA 13 Table 20.5.5** for Class IV, rack storage > 25 ft with ceiling-only sprinklers:
- Design density: $q = 0.20$ GPM/ft² (≈ 8.2 mm/min)
- Design area: $A_{design} = 2000$ ft² (186 m²)

**Step 2: Calculate Number of Sprinklers**

$$ N_{sprinklers} = \frac{A_{design}}{A_{sprinkler\,coverage}} $$

$$ N_{sprinklers} = \frac{2000 \, ft^2}{100 \, ft^2 / sprinkler} $$

$$ N_{sprinklers} = 20 \, sprinklers $$

**Step 3: Calculate Flow per Sprinkler (at 7 psi minimum)**

For K-factor = 5.6 (standard sprinkler):

$$ Q_{sprinkler} = K \times \sqrt{P} $$

$$ Q_{sprinkler} = 5.6 \times \sqrt{7} $$

$$ Q_{sprinkler} = 5.6 \times 2.646 $$

$$ Q_{sprinkler} = 14.8 \, GPM $$

**Step 4: Verify Total Flow**

$$ Q_{total} = q \times A_{design} = 0.20 \times 2000 = 400 \, GPM $$

Cross-check: $N \times Q_{sprinkler} = 20 \times 14.8 = 296$ GPM

> [!WARNING]
> **Discrepancy Alert:** If the per-sprinkler flow (296 GPM) < design density flow (400 GPM), you must **increase sprinkler pressure** until $Q_{sprinkler} \geq q \times A_{sprinkler\,coverage}$. This is the **"density check"** required by NFPA 13.

Required pressure adjustment:

$$ P_{req} = \left(\frac{q \times A_{cov}}{K}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{0.20 \times 100}{5.6}\right)^2 $$

$$ P_{req} = (3.57)^2 = 12.76 \, psi $$

$$ Q_{sprinkler\,new} = 5.6 \times \sqrt{12.76} = 20.0 \, GPM $$

$$ Q_{total\,new} = 20 \times 20.0 = 400 \, GPM \checkmark $$

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Clean Agent Quantity for Computer Room

**Problem Statement:** A computer room has dimensions **10 m × 8 m × 3.5 m (height)**. Determine the **FM-200 quantity** required to suppress a fire at **7% design concentration** at **21°C**.

**Step 1: Calculate Room Volume**

$$ V = L \times W \times H = 10 \times 8 \times 3.5 = 280 \, m^3 $$

**Step 2: Look up Specific Volume of FM-200 at 21°C**

Per **NFPA 2001**, $S_{FM-200} = 0.1379$ m³/kg at 21°C.

**Step 3: Apply Agent Quantity Formula**

$$ W = \frac{V}{S} \times \left(\frac{C}{100 - C}\right) $$

$$ W = \frac{280}{0.1379} \times \left(\frac{7}{100 - 7}\right) $$

$$ W = 2030.5 \times \left(\frac{7}{93}\right) $$

$$ W = 2030.5 \times 0.0753 $$

$$ W = 152.8 \, kg $$

**Step 4: Apply Safety Factor (1.10 per NFPA 2001)**

$$ W_{final} = 1.10 \times 152.8 = 168.1 \, kg $$

**Step 5: Determine Number of Cylinders**

If cylinder capacity is 70 kg each:

$$ N_{cylinders} = \lceil \frac{168.1}{70} \rceil = \lceil 2.40 \rceil = 3 \, cylinders $$

---

| **Office building** | LH | 2.5 mm/min | 139 m² | Quick-response sprinklers in light hazard |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Define the **Area-Density Curve** as used in sprinkler system design. Explain why the curve has an inverse (hyperbolic) shape.

**Model Answer:**
The Area-Density Curve is a graphical relationship prescribed by **NFPA 13** that correlates the **minimum design area of sprinkler operation** (m² or ft²) with the corresponding **required water discharge density** (mm/min or GPM/ft²) for a specific occupancy hazard classification (LH, OH-1/2/3, EH-1/2).

The curve has an **inverse shape** because of fire physics: as the **design area increases**, the required **density decreases**. This reflects that for a fixed total water supply, you can either:
- *Concentrate* high density over a small area (each sprinkler does more work), OR
- *Spread* lower density over a larger area (more sprinklers operate).

Both achieve equivalent fire control, balancing the **Rate of Heat Release** against the **Rate of Heat Absorption by water spray**. (3 marks)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
List and briefly explain the **three fire protection strategies** used in modern computer rooms as per NFPA 75.

**Model Answer:**
The three-tier protection strategy for computer rooms (per NFPA 75) is:

1. **Primary — Clean Agent Gas Suppression:** Uses gaseous agents like **FM-200 (HFC-227ea), Novec 1230, or Inergen (IG-541)** to flood the room at 4.5–10% concentration, smothering the fire without water damage to sensitive electronics.

2. **Secondary — Pre-Action Sprinkler System:** A **double-interlocked pre-action system** holds water back via supervised dry pipe until **two independent events** occur (detection + sprinkler head fuse), preventing accidental water discharge.

3. **Detection — Very Early Smoke Detection Apparatus (VESDA):** Aspirating smoke detectors sample air continuously, providing **warning at 0.001% obscuration/ft**, allowing fire brigade response before suppression activation. (3 marks)

---

### 5.2 PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**A(a). [7 Marks]** A pharmaceutical manufacturing plant stores **Group A plastic bottles** (HDPE containers) on **double-row racks** to a height of **8 m**. The warehouse has a **clear ceiling height of 10 m**. Sprinklers are spaced at **3 m × 3 m** with K-factor **K = 80** (metric). Determine:
(i) The applicable hazard classification per NFPA 13
(ii) The design density and design area
(iii) The number of sprinklers in the design area
(iv) The minimum operating pressure per sprinkler

**A(b). [7 Marks]** Explain with neat sketches how **ESFR sprinklers** differ from conventional sprinklers in terms of:
(i) RTI value
(ii) Mode of operation (control vs. suppression)
(iii) Typical applications in warehouse storage

---

**MODEL SOLUTION FOR QUESTION A:**

**A(a) Solution:**

**Step (i): Hazard Classification**
- Storage of **Group A plastics** (HDPE bottles) on racks > 12 ft (3.7 m) high
- Per NFPA 13 Chapter 20, Table 20.5.5 → **Extra Hazard Group 1 (EH-1)**

**Step (ii): Design Density and Area (per NFPA 13 Table for Group A Plastics, rack > 25 ft / 7.6 m):**
- Design density: $q = 12.5$ mm/min
- Design area: $A = 232$ m² (worst-case 2500 ft²)

**Step (iii): Number of Sprinklers**
$$ N = \frac{A_{design}}{A_{sprinkler}} = \frac{232 \, m^2}{3 \times 3 \, m^2} = \frac{232}{9} \approx 26 \, sprinklers $$

**Step (iv): Minimum Operating Pressure**
Per-sprinkler density requirement:
$$ q_{sprinkler} = \frac{q \times A_{sprinkler}}{A_{sprinkler}} = 12.5 \, mm/min $$
Convert to metric flow per sprinkler:
$$ Q_{sprinkler} = 12.5 \times 10^{-3} \times \frac{m}{min} \times 9 \, m^2 = 0.1125 \, m^3/min = 112.5 \, L/min $$
Apply K-factor formula ($P = Q^2/K^2$, but for metric K in L/min/√bar):
$$ P = \left(\frac{Q}{K}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{112.5}{80}\right)^2 = (1.406)^2 = 1.98 \, bar $$

Convert: $P \approx 1.98 \times 14.5 = 28.7 \, psi$

**Verification per-sprinkler flow:** $Q = 80 \times \sqrt{1.98} = 112.5$ L/min ✓

**A(b) Solution:**

| Parameter | Conventional Sprinkler | ESFR Sprinkler |
|-----------|:---:|:---:|
| **RTI (Response Time Index)** | > 50 (m·s)½ | **< 28 (m·s)½** |
| **Operating Element** | Fusible solder link or glass bulb | **Fast-response glass bulb (thin-walled)** |
| **Mode** | **Control mode** (prevents flashover) | **Suppression mode** (extinguishes fire directly) |
| **K-factor** | 5.6, 8.0 (imperial) | **14.0, 16.8, 22.4, 25.2** |
| **Min. Operating Pressure** | 7 – 15 psi | **25 – 75 psi** |
| **In-rack sprinklers** | Required for high-pile | **Not required (ceiling-only design)** |
| **Typical Application** | Light/Ordinary hazard, low-pile storage | **High-pile warehouse, Class IV, Group A plastics** |

**Sketch (refer to SECTION 4.7):**
A comparison block diagram showing ESFR with **suppression objective** vs. conventional with **control objective**, with key parameter callouts.

**Key Engineering Insight:** ESFR sprinklers deliver a **high-momentum water column** that penetrates the fire plume directly, suppressing the fire at the seat rather than controlling ceiling-level spread. This makes them ideal for warehouses where **in-rack sprinkler installation is impractical or costly**.

---

#### **QUESTION B — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**B(a). [7 Marks]** A server room has dimensions **15 m × 10 m × 4 m (height)**. The room is to be protected by an **FM-200 (HFC-227ea) clean agent** system at a **design concentration of 7%** at ambient temperature **21°C**. Calculate:
(i) The total volume of the room
(ii) The quantity of FM-200 required
(iii) The number of 80 kg cylinders needed (apply 10% safety factor)

**B(b). [7 Marks]** Compare the fire protection systems used in:
(i) **Aircraft hangars** at airports
(ii) **Airport terminal buildings**
in terms of suppression agents, design standards, and special considerations.

---

**MODEL SOLUTION FOR QUESTION B:**

**B(a) Solution:**

**Step (i): Room Volume**
$$ V = L \times W \times H = 15 \times 10 \times 4 = 600 \, m^3 $$

**Step (ii): FM-200 Quantity**
Per NFPA 2001, specific volume of FM-200 at 21°C:
$$ S_{FM-200} = 0.1379 \, m^3/kg $$

Apply formula:
$$ W = \frac{V}{S} \times \left(\frac{C}{100 - C}\right) $$

$$ W = \frac{600}{0.1379} \times \left(\frac{7}{100 - 7}\right) $$

$$ W = 4351.0 \times 0.0753 $$

$$ W = 327.5 \, kg $$

**Step (iii): Apply 10% Safety Factor**
$$ W_{final} = 1.10 \times 327.5 = 360.3 \, kg $$

**Number of 80-kg cylinders:**
$$ N_{cylinders} = \left\lceil \frac{360.3}{80} \right\rceil = \lceil 4.50 \rceil = 5 \, cylinders $$

**B(b) Solution:**

| Aspect | Aircraft Hangars | Terminal Buildings |
|--------|:---:|:---:|
| **Applicable Standard** | **NFPA 11** (Low/Med/High Expansion Foam), **NFPA 409** | **NFPA 13** (Sprinklers), **NFPA 92B** (Smoke Control) |
| **Primary Suppression** | **High-Expansion Foam** (500:1 to 1000:1 ratio) | **ESFR Sprinklers (K-25.2)** + **Deluge** |
| **Application Rate** | **6.5 L/min/m²** (AFFF/AR-AFFF) | 12.5 mm/min (density-based) |
| **Critical Hazard** | **Jet fuel (Kerosene) pool fires**, large spill areas | **Crowd evacuation**, large undivided spaces |
| **Ceiling Height** | 20 – 35 m (very high) | 8 – 15 m (medium-high) |
| **Special Features** | Foam submergence < **4 min**, redundant foam pumps, **turbine-driven foam proportioners** | **Voice evacuation**, **mass notification**, **smoke exhaust (10–12 ACH)** |
| **Detection System** | **Linear Heat Detection (LHD)** + **Flame Detectors (IR/UV)** | **VESDA + Spot Smoke** |
| **Pump Capacity** | **Electric + Diesel + Jockey** (3-source redundancy) | Electric + Diesel (2-source) |
| **Discharge Time** | Foam: **15 min** (sustained) | Sprinkler: **60 min** (OH-1 duration) |
| **Special Considerations** | Foam must reach **aircraft underbelly**, low-expansion for fuel spills | **Public address**, way-finding signage, accessibility |

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **COMMON MARK-LOSS PITFALLS — KTU Board Exam**
>
> **Pitfall 1: Confusing LH with OH density**
> - LH density = **2.5 mm/min**, OH = **5.0 mm/min**, EH = **12.5 mm/min**
> - Students often write "10 mm/min" for OH, which is WRONG.
>
> **Pitfall 2: Forgetting Hose Stream Allowance**
> - NFPA 13 mandates **250 GPM (≈ 950 L/min)** additional hose stream for OH-2.
> - Many students compute $Q = q \times A$ only, missing this critical add-on → lose 2 marks.
>
> **Pitfall 3: Using Imperial Units in a Metric Problem**
> - Always check the question's unit system. If K-factor is in **L/min/√bar (metric)**, use $P = (Q/K)^2$ in **bar**, NOT psi.
> - Mixing units is the #1 reason students fail numerical problems.
>
> **Pitfall 4: ESFR vs. Conventional confusion**
> - ESFR = **Suppression mode, RTI < 28, K ≥ 14.0**
> - Conventional = **Control mode, RTI > 50, K = 5.6 or 8.0**
> - Students frequently swap these definitions → lose entire 7-mark sub-part.
>
> **Pitfall 5: Specific Volume Lookup**
> - For FM-200, $S$ varies with temperature: $S_{21°C} = 0.1379$ m³/kg
> - For Novec 1230, $S_{21°C} = 0.0719$ m³/kg
> - Using the wrong agent's specific volume = -3 marks.
>
> **Pitfall 6: No mention of Duration Check**
> - Always state the **minimum water supply duration**: LH = **30 min**, OH = **60 min**, EH = **90 min** (per IS 15105).

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Module 3**

**A. Area-Density Curve:**
- [ ] Inverse relationship: ↑ Area ⇒ ↓ Density (and vice-versa)
- [ ] LH = 2.5 mm/min over 139 m²
- [ ] OH-1/2/3 = 5.0 mm/min over 74/139/186 m²
- [ ] EH-1/2 = 12.5 mm/min over 232/279 m²
- [ ] Always add **hose stream allowance** (250 GPM for OH)
- [ ] Apply **1.15 safety factor** on pump capacity
- [ ] Duration: LH 30 min, OH 60 min, EH 90 min

**B. Warehouse Sprinkler Design:**
- [ ] **Commodity Class I → IV → Group A Plastics** (increasing severity)
- [ ] **ESFR** = Early Suppression Fast Response, RTI < 28, K ≥ 14.0
- [ ] **ESFR = ceiling-only design** (no in-rack needed)
- [ ] **Conventional = control mode** + in-rack sprinklers for high-pile
- [ ] Maintain **6 in. transverse + 12 in. longitudinal flue spaces**
- [ ] Encapsulated (shrink-wrapped) pallets increase severity drastically
- [ ] Double-row rack max height without in-rack: ~25 ft (ESFR)

**C. Computer Rooms (NFPA 75):**
- [ ] Primary: **Clean agent** (FM-200, Novec 1230, Inergen)
- [ ] Detection: **VESDA** (0.001% obscuration/ft)
- [ ] Backup: **Double-interlocked pre-action sprinkler**
- [ ] Agent formula: $W = \dfrac{V}{S} \times \dfrac{C}{100-C}$
- [ ] FM-200 design conc.: **7.0 – 8.5%**
- [ ] Novec 1230 design conc.: **4.5 – 6.0%** (more agent-efficient)
- [ ] Inergen design conc.: **38 – 40%** (safe for occupied spaces)
- [ ] Halon 1301: **PHASED OUT** under Montreal Protocol

**D. Airport Protection:**
- [ ] **Hangars:** High-expansion foam (NFPA 11), AR-AFFF
- [ ] **Terminals:** ESFR K-25.2 + voice evacuation
- [ ] **Fuel farms:** Foam pourers + water spray deluge
- [ ] **Smoke management:** 10–12 air changes/hour (mechanical exhaust)
- [ ] Hangar foam submergence time: **< 4 minutes**

**E. Hydraulic Calculation Sequence (KTU-favourite numerical):**
1. Identify hazard class → read $q$ and $A$ from table
2. Calculate $Q_{total} = q \times A$
3. Add hose stream → $Q_{demand}$
4. Apply safety factor → $Q_{pump}$
5. Tank volume: $V = Q_{pump} \times duration$
6. Per-sprinkler pressure: $P = (Q/K)^2$

**F. Key Formulas (Memorise):**
- [ ] $Q = q \times A$ (flow = density × area)
- [ ] $Q = K\sqrt{P}$ (sprinkler discharge)
- [ ] $H_f = \dfrac{4.52 Q^{1.85}}{C^{1.85} d^{4.87}}$ (Hazen-Williams)
- [ ] $W = \dfrac{V}{S} \times \dfrac{C}{100-C}$ (clean agent)

**G. Standards to Remember (for viva & theory):**
- [ ] NFPA 13 — Sprinklers
- [ ] NFPA 11 — Foam
- [ ] NFPA 75 — Computer Rooms
- [ ] NFPA 2001 — Clean Agents
- [ ] NFPA 409 — Aircraft Hangars
- [ ] IS 15105 — Indian Standard (Clean Agents)
- [ ] NBC 2016 Part 4 — India Fire Protection Code

---

**END OF NOTES — MODULE 3**
