topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT836 - DESIGN OF FIRE PROTECTION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Fixed Firefighting System using CO2, Dry Chemical Powder, and Foam - Concept of Total Flooding and Local Application, Advantages and Disadvantages of Each System. Basic System Components. Design Principles of Fixed Firefighting Systems for Total Flooding and for Local Application as per Relevant Standards (ISI). Fire Alarm System - Classification of Alarm System as per NBC. Manually Operated System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Fixed Firefighting Systems — Formal Definition

A **Fixed Firefighting System** is a permanently installed, engineered fire suppression arrangement integrated into a building or hazard enclosure. It is designed to detect, control, and extinguish fire automatically (or via manual initiation) using a stored extinguishing medium distributed through a fixed piping network. The system remains in a state of continuous readiness, with the extinguishing agent held under pressure in dedicated storage containers and delivered through purpose-designed discharge nozzles upon actuation.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**
> Fixed systems are broadly classified by the extinguishing medium: **Gaseous (CO₂)**, **Powder (DCP)**, and **Foam (AFFF/Protein)**. Each is governed by specific Indian Standards (BIS) and National Building Code (NBC 2016) provisions.

### 1.2 The Three Primary Extinguishing Media — Conceptual Overview

| Medium | State at Discharge | Primary Mechanism | Typical Hazard |
|---|---|---|---|
| **CO₂** | Gas (cryogenic/liquefied) | Oxygen dilution + cooling | Electrical, flammable liquid (Class B) |
| **Dry Chemical Powder (DCP)** | Micronized solid cloud | Chain reaction inhibition | Flammable liquids, gases, electrical (Class B/C) |
| **Foam (AFFF/FP/AR)** | Aerated aqueous blanket | Smothering + cooling | Hydrocarbon & polar solvent fires (Class A/B) |

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Three Firefighters"

Imagine a kitchen fire emergency with three different specialists arriving:

- **CO₂** is like an **invisible suffocator** who removes the oxygen from the room (smothers without leaving residue — perfect for server rooms where residue would destroy electronics).
- **DCP** is like a **chemical saboteur** who throws a fine chemical dust that interrupts the flame's chain reaction at the molecular level (extremely fast knock-down, but leaves a corrosive mess).
- **Foam** is like a **fire blanket specialist** who lays a thick, floating blanket over burning liquid to seal it from air (only useful where liquids are involved).

### 1.4 Total Flooding vs Local Application — The Two Application Philosophies

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Total Flooding (TF)**: The extinguishing agent is discharged into an **enclosed space** (room, cabinet, enclosure) to achieve a uniform concentration throughout the volume. Designed to extinguish fire anywhere within the protected enclosure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Local Application (LA)**: The extinguishing agent is discharged **directly onto a specific hazard or equipment** (open-face application) without requiring an enclosed room. Used where total enclosure is impractical.

### 1.5 Physical & Engineering Constants

- **Design concentration of CO₂ for Class B fires**: **34 % minimum**, typically **50 %** for surface fires (per **IS 6382**).
- **Design concentration of CO₂ for deep-seated Class A fires**: **65 %**.
- **DCP design density**: **4.0–6.0 kg/m²** of floor area (per **IS 13849**).
- **Foam application rate (hydrocarbon)**: **4.0 L/min per m²** (per **IS 4989**).
- **NBC 2016 reference standard**: **Part 4 — Fire and Life Safety**.
- **Minimum CO₂ discharge time**: **60 seconds for surface fires**, **7 minutes for deep-seated** (per IS 6382).
- **Specific heat of CO₂ at constant pressure (Cp)**: **0.839 kJ/kg·K**.

### 1.6 Classification of Fixed Firefighting Systems (Per Module Scope)

- **Gaseous Systems**: CO₂ (high-pressure or low-pressure)
- **Powder Systems**: DCP — stored pressure or cartridge-operated
- **Foam Systems**: AFFF, Protein (FP), Film Forming FluoroProtein (FFFP), Alcohol-Resistant (AR-AFFF)
- **Combined Systems**: Foam-Water Spray, Dual-Agent (CO₂ + DCP)

### 1.7 Course Outcome (CO) Mapping

| Topic Element | KTU CO | Bloom's Level |
|---|---|---|
| CO₂ / DCP / Foam systems — definitions | CO4 | Remember (L1) |
| Total Flooding vs Local Application concepts | CO4 | Understand (L2) |
| Advantages/Disadvantages comparison | CO4 | Analyze (L4) |
| Design calculations per ISI | CO4, CO5 | Apply / Evaluate (L3, L5) |
| NBC Alarm classification | CO4, CO5 | Understand / Apply (L2, L3) |

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 CO₂ Fixed Firefighting System — Operational Analysis

**CO₂** (Carbon Dioxide) is a colourless, odourless, electrically non-conductive gas stored as a liquid under high pressure (≈ **5.86 MPa at 21 °C** in high-pressure cylinders) or at low pressure (≈ **2.07 MPa, −18 °C**) in refrigerated bulk tanks.

**Mechanism of Extinguishment (3-fold action):**
1. **Oxygen Displacement (Smothering)** — Reduces O₂ concentration below the **combustion threshold (≈ 15 %)**.
2. **Cooling Effect** — The rapid expansion of liquid CO₂ absorbs latent heat (latent heat of vaporization ≈ **571 kJ/kg** at 0 °C).
3. **Thermal Radiation Attenuation** — The high specific heat of CO₂ reduces radiant heat transfer.

#### 2.1.1 Application Modes of CO₂

**A. Total Flooding (TF) with CO₂:**
- Applicable to **enclosed hazards**: electrical rooms, switchgear rooms, generator enclosures, paint stores, server rooms.
- The protected volume must be **reasonably gas-tight** (leakage area allowances per IS 6382).
- Design concentration: **50 %** for surface liquid/gas fires, **65 %** for deep-seated solids.

**B. Local Application (LA) with CO₂:**
- Applicable to **specific hazards** without total enclosure: printing presses, dip tanks, quench tanks, oil baths.
- Discharge is aimed at the hazard surface from fixed nozzles.
- Discharge rate based on **hazard surface area**, not room volume.

#### 2.1.2 Components of a CO₂ Fixed System

1. **Storage Cylinders / Bank** — High-pressure seamless cylinders (68 L water capacity, holding ≈ 45 kg CO₂) arranged in a manifold.
2. **Master/Selector Valve** — Pneumatic, mechanical, or electric release valve; directs CO₂ to the protected zone.
3. **Actuation System** — Pneumatic head, solenoid actuator, or manual lever.
4. **Distribution Piping** — Schedule 40/80 black steel; sized per hydraulic flow calculations.
5. **Discharge Nozzles** — Full-cone or hollow-cone, sized to deliver the required flow rate.
6. **Detection & Control Panel** — Initiates release on confirmed fire signal with **time delay (≥ 30 s)** for personnel evacuation.
7. **Pressure Switches, Pressure Gauges, Safety Relief Valves**.
8. **Warning Signs & Audible/Visual Alarms** — Pre-discharge alarm.

### 2.2 Dry Chemical Powder (DCP) Fixed System — Operational Analysis

**DCP** is a finely divided solid (particle size **10–75 μm**) based primarily on **Monoammonium Phosphate (MAP)** for ABC rating or **Sodium Bicarbonate** for BC rating.

**Mechanism of Extinguishment:**
- **Chain-Breaking (Negative Catalysis)** — Powder particles decompose in the flame to form free radicals (PO, PO₂) that interrupt the combustion chain reaction.
- **Smothering** — Powder cloud displaces oxygen locally.
- **Radiation Shielding** — Powder cloud blocks radiant heat from reaching fuel.

#### 2.2.1 DCP System Variants

- **ABC Powder**: MAP-based; suitable for Class A, B, C fires.
- **BC Powder**: Sodium Bicarbonate or Potassium Bicarbonate; Class B, C only.
- **D Class Powder**: Sodium Chloride or Copper powder; for combustible metal fires (Na, Mg, etc.).

#### 2.2.2 DCP System Configurations

- **Stored Pressure Type** — Expellant gas (N₂) and powder in same cylinder.
- **Cartridge-Operated Type** — Powder in main cylinder, N₂ in separate cartridge that punctures on actuation.

#### 2.2.3 Components of DCP Fixed System

1. **Powder Cylinder(s)** — Mild steel pressure vessel (typically **50–500 kg capacity**).
2. **Expellant Gas Cylinder (N₂)** — At **1.4–1.6 MPa** working pressure.
3. **Actuator (Solenoid/Pneumatic)** — Releases N₂ into powder cylinder.
4. **Powder Piping** — Schedule 40 steel, dry and pressurized with N₂ to prevent moisture ingress.
5. **Discharge Nozzles** — Specifically designed for powder (different from water/foam nozzles).
6. **Pressure Regulators, Check Valves, Manifolds**.
7. **Detection & Control System** with abort and hold switches.

### 2.3 Foam Fixed System — Operational Analysis

**Fire-fighting foam** is a homogeneous aggregation of air-filled bubbles (lower density than oil/gasoline) formed by mechanically mixing foam concentrate with water and air.

**Types of Foam Concentrate (per IS 4989 & IS 15519):**
- **Protein Foam (FP)** — From hydrolyzed animal protein; for hydrocarbon fires.
- **Aqueous Film-Forming Foam (AFFF)** — Synthetic, fast knock-down.
- **Film-Forming FluoroProtein (FFFP)** — Combines FP and AFFF.
- **Alcohol-Resistant Foam (AR-AFFF)** — For polar solvents (methanol, acetone).
- **Class A Foam** — For wildland/structural Class A fires.

**Mechanism:**
- **Smothering** — Foam blanket excludes oxygen.
- **Cooling** — Water content cools the fuel.
- **Fuel Vapor Suppression** — AFFF forms an aqueous film on hydrocarbon surfaces.
- **Radiation Shielding** — Foam blanket blocks re-radiation.

#### 2.3.1 Components of Foam Fixed System

1. **Foam Concentrate Storage Tank** — Stainless steel or HDPE.
2. **Proportioner** — Balances foam concentrate to water at **1 %, 3 %, or 6 %** ratio (bladder tank, in-line eductor, balanced pressure proportioner).
3. **Water Supply Piping**.
4. **Foam Maker / Foam Pourer** — Aspirates air to produce foam.
5. **Aspirating Nozzles** — For low/medium expansion foam.
6. **High-Expansion Foam Generators** — For enclosed spaces (sub-station basements, aircraft hangars).
7. **Hose Reels, Monitors, Fixed Foam Pourers**.

### 2.4 Comparative Advantages & Disadvantages

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Comparison Table — Frequently Asked in KTU Exams**

| Aspect | CO₂ | DCP | Foam |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Residue** | None | Heavy corrosive powder | Wet, may damage equipment |
| **Electrical Safety** | Excellent (non-conductive) | Excellent (dry, non-conductive) | Poor (water-based) |
| **Class A (Solids)** | Limited (use 65 %) | Good (ABC) | Good (Class A foam) |
| **Class B (Liquids)** | Excellent (50 %) | Excellent | Excellent |
| **Class C (Gases)** | Limited | Excellent | Not suitable |
| **Class D (Metals)** | Not suitable | Special D powder | Not suitable |
| **Re-ignition Risk** | High (no cooling of hot surfaces) | Low–Moderate | Low |
| **Enclosure Required** | Yes (TF mode) | No (LA mode) | No (LA mode) |
| **Toxicity Hazard** | High (asphyxiation risk) | Low (nuisance dust) | Low |
| **Cost (per kg)** | High | Moderate | Moderate |
| **Cleanup** | None (ventilate) | Extensive (vacuum) | Moderate (water flush) |
| **3D / Deep-seated fires** | Poor (TF requires concentration hold) | Moderate | Moderate |
| **Storage Stability** | Unlimited (sealed) | Unlimited (dry) | Limited (shelf life 15–25 yr) |

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Formula Table — All Key Equations for KTU 2024 Exams**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Reference |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | CO₂ Total Flooding Quantity | $Q = V \cdot k \cdot C$ | kg | IS 6382 |
| 2 | CO₂ Volume Required | $V = A \cdot H$ | m³ | IS 6382 |
| 3 | CO₂ Required for LA | $Q = q \cdot A_s$ | kg | IS 6382 |
| 4 | DCP Total Quantity | $Q = d \cdot A$ | kg | IS 13849 |
| 5 | DCP Nozzle Flow | $Q_n = C_d \cdot A_n \sqrt{2 \Delta P / \rho}$ | kg/s | Hydraulic |
| 6 | Foam Solution Rate | $Q_s = R_a \cdot A$ | L/min | IS 4989 |
| 7 | Foam Concentrate Quantity | $Q_c = Q_s \cdot C_f$ | L | IS 4989 |
| 8 | Expansion Ratio | $E_r = V_{foam} / V_{soln}$ | — | NFPA 11 |
| 9 | Pipe Sizing (Hagen-Poiseuille) | $\Delta P = \frac{128 \mu L Q}{\pi D^4}$ | Pa | Hydraulic |
| 10 | Hazen-Williams Friction Loss | $h_f = \frac{4.52 \cdot L \cdot Q^{1.85}}{C^{1.85} \cdot d^{4.87}}$ | m | Hydraulic |
| 11 | Discharge Time (CO₂) | $t_d \geq 60 \text{ s (surface)}, \geq 7 \text{ min (deep-seated)}$ | s/min | IS 6382 |
| 12 | Enclosure Leakage Factor (k) | $k = 1 + (A_L / 100)$ | — | IS 6382 |

**Where:**
- $V$ = protected volume (m³)
- $A_s$ = hazard surface area (m²) for LA
- $k$ = leakage/enclosure factor
- $C$ = specific CO₂ quantity per m³ (kg/m³) for the design concentration
- $R_a$ = foam application rate (L/min·m²)
- $C_f$ = foam concentrate percentage (e.g., 0.03 for 3 %)
- $C_d$ = discharge coefficient
- $A_n$ = nozzle throat area
- $A_L$ = total leakage area (cm²)

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Server Rooms / Data Centres**: CO₂ total flooding (no damage to electronics).
- **Petrol Refineries**: Foam pourers + DCP monitors for tanker loading bays.
- **LPG Bottling Plants**: DCP local application (powder cloud is non-conductive and instantly effective).
- **Transformer Yards**: High-expansion foam + water spray deluge.
- **Aircraft Hangars**: High-expansion foam (AFFF) — typical application rate 4 L/min·m².
- **Paint Manufacturing**: CO₂ total flooding (prevents solvent vapour explosion).

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 CO₂ Total Flooding — Exhaustive Design Derivation

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example (KTU Exam-Style)**
> A 6 m × 5 m × 3.5 m high electrical switchgear room is to be protected by a CO₂ total flooding system. The enclosure has a total leakage area of 200 cm². Design concentration required: 50 % by volume. Determine the CO₂ quantity required.

**Step 1 — Compute the gross protected volume:**

$$
V_{gross} = L \times B \times H = 6.0 \times 5.0 \times 3.5 = 105.0 \; \text{m}^3
$$

This represents the **gross geometric volume** of the enclosure. The next step determines how much CO₂ escapes through leaks before achieving the design concentration.

**Step 2 — Determine the specific CO₂ quantity ($C$) from IS 6382 tables:**

For a **50 % design concentration** at an assumed **ambient temperature of 21 °C**, the specific CO₂ quantity is:

$$
C = 2.65 \; \text{kg/m}^3
$$

This value is read from **Table 1 of IS 6382**, which relates design concentration (by volume) to the mass of liquid CO₂ required per cubic metre of free volume at a reference temperature.

**Step 3 — Apply the leakage factor ($k$):**

The leakage factor compensates for CO₂ that escapes through openings (cable penetrations, door undercuts, ventilation grilles) before the design concentration is fully established.

$$
k = 1 + \frac{A_L}{100} = 1 + \frac{200}{100} = 1 + 2.0 = 3.0
$$

Where $A_L$ is the total equivalent leakage area expressed in cm² (or as the equivalent in cm²/m³ per IS 6382). The factor $1/100$ originates from empirical tests correlating a 1 cm² leak to 1 % additional CO₂ demand.

**Step 4 — Compute the Net Protected Volume (considering submergence allowance for floor area):**

For floor-level hazards, the CO₂ must submerge the lower **0.6 m** of the room at the design concentration. However, since $V_{gross}$ already encompasses the hazard, we apply the **subtraction method**:

$$
V_{net} = V_{gross} - V_{non-flooded} = 105.0 - 0 = 105.0 \; \text{m}^3
$$

(If the room is fully enclosed with no permanent openings above the design flood level, no subtraction is needed.)

**Step 5 — Compute the CO₂ mass required:**

$$
Q = V_{net} \cdot k \cdot C
$$

$$
Q = 105.0 \times 3.0 \times 2.65
$$

$$
Q = 315.0 \times 2.65
$$

$$
Q = 834.75 \; \text{kg}
$$

**Step 6 — Convert to number of standard cylinders:**

A standard 68 L high-pressure CO₂ cylinder contains **45 kg** of liquid CO₂ (nominal fill ratio 0.67).

$$
N = \frac{Q}{45} = \frac{834.75}{45} = 18.55
$$

Round up: **N = 19 cylinders** (with one standby, total 20 cylinders).

**Step 7 — Verify discharge time:**

Minimum discharge time for surface fires per IS 6382 = **60 s**.

$$
t_d = \frac{Q}{Q_{rate}} = \frac{834.75}{Q_{rate}}
$$

With a 19-cylinder manifold, the combined flow rate is approximately 14 kg/s. Therefore:

$$
t_d = \frac{834.75}{14} \approx 59.6 \; \text{s}
$$

This is **marginally below 60 s**, so we add one cylinder to provide:

$$
t_d = \frac{879.75}{14.7} \approx 59.9 \; \text{s}
$$

**Final Design: 20 cylinders (19 active + 1 standby) gives 879.75 kg, satisfying the 50 % design concentration and ≥ 60 s discharge time.**

---

### 3.2 DCP System — Quantity Calculation (IS 13849)

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example**
> A 4 m × 3 m gasoline storage room requires DCP protection. Design density = 5 kg/m². Determine powder quantity and number of cylinders.

**Step 1 — Floor area:**

$$
A = 4 \times 3 = 12.0 \; \text{m}^2
$$

**Step 2 — DCP mass required:**

$$
Q_{DCP} = d \times A = 5.0 \times 12.0 = 60.0 \; \text{kg}
$$

**Step 3 — Number of cylinders (50 kg capacity each):**

$$
N = \frac{60.0}{50} = 1.2 \Rightarrow N = 2 \; \text{cylinders}
$$

**Step 4 — N₂ expellant gas quantity:**

Standard expellant requirement: **1 L (NTP) of N₂ per kg of powder at 1.5 MPa**.

$$
V_{N_2} = 60.0 \times 1.0 = 60.0 \; \text{L (NTP)}
$$

This requires one **7 L N₂ cylinder** pressurized to **15 MPa** (gives 105 L NTP).

**Step 5 — Nozzle selection:**

DCP nozzles are typically rated at **1.5–3.0 kg/s**. For 60 kg in 20 seconds:

$$
Q_{nozzle} = \frac{60.0}{20.0} = 3.0 \; \text{kg/s}
$$

Select **2 nozzles at 1.5 kg/s each**, strategically placed to cover the entire hazard area.

---

### 3.3 Foam System — Hydraulic & Quantity Design (IS 4989)

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example**
> A 100 m² hydrocarbon bund (diesel storage) is to be protected with AFFF foam. Application rate = 4 L/min·m² (per IS 4989 for hydrocarbon). Foam concentrate 3 %. Application time 15 minutes. Determine concentrate quantity and foam solution flow rate.

**Step 1 — Foam solution flow rate:**

$$
Q_s = R_a \times A = 4.0 \times 100 = 400 \; \text{L/min}
$$

**Step 2 — Foam concentrate flow rate:**

$$
Q_c = Q_s \times C_f = 400 \times 0.03 = 12 \; \text{L/min}
$$

**Step 3 — Total water consumption:**

$$
Q_w = Q_s - Q_c = 400 - 12 = 388 \; \text{L/min}
$$

**Step 4 — Total concentrate quantity (15 min application):**

$$
Q_{ct} = Q_c \times t = 12 \times 15 = 180 \; \text{L}
$$

**Step 5 — Total foam solution quantity:**

$$
Q_{st} = Q_s \times t = 400 \times 15 = 6000 \; \text{L} = 6.0 \; \text{m}^3
$$

**Step 6 — Foam volume produced (expansion ratio 8:1 for AFFF):**

$$
V_{foam} = Q_{st} \times E_r = 6000 \times 8 = 48000 \; \text{L} = 48.0 \; \text{m}^3
$$

This is the foam blanket volume. Verify it covers the bund area to a depth of at least **100 mm**:

$$
\text{Depth} = \frac{V_{foam}}{A} = \frac{48.0}{100} = 0.48 \; \text{m} = 480 \; \text{mm} \;\; \text{(acceptable)}
$$

**Step 7 — Hydraulic check — Pipe friction loss (Hazen-Williams):**

For a 65 mm nominal diameter steel pipe ($d = 0.065$ m), $C = 120$, $L = 50$ m, $Q = 400$ L/min = 0.00667 m³/s.

$$
h_f = \frac{4.52 \times L \times Q^{1.85}}{C^{1.85} \times d^{4.87}}
$$

Converting to compatible units: $Q = 6.67$ L/s, $L = 50$ m, $d = 65$ mm, $C = 120$.

$$
h_f = \frac{4.52 \times 50 \times (6.67)^{1.85}}{(120)^{1.85} \times (65)^{4.87}}
$$

Compute step by step:

$$
(6.67)^{1.85} \approx 33.16
$$

$$
(120)^{1.85} \approx 5878
$$

$$
(65)^{4.87} \approx 6.45 \times 10^{8}
$$

$$
h_f = \frac{4.52 \times 50 \times 33.16}{5878 \times 6.45 \times 10^{8}} = \frac{7494.16}{3.79 \times 10^{12}} \approx 1.98 \times 10^{-9} \; \text{m}
$$

**This indicates an order-of-magnitude miscalculation in unit selection.** Correctly using SI units throughout:

For $Q$ in m³/s and $d$ in m, the metric Hazen-Williams form is:

$$
h_f = \frac{10.67 \cdot L \cdot Q^{1.852}}{C^{1.852} \cdot d^{4.87}}
$$

With $Q = 0.00667$ m³/s, $d = 0.065$ m:

$$
h_f = \frac{10.67 \times 50 \times (0.00667)^{1.852}}{(120)^{1.852} \times (0.065)^{4.87}}
$$

$$
(0.00667)^{1.852} \approx 1.13 \times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
(120)^{1.852} \approx 5830
$$

$$
(0.065)^{4.87} \approx 1.91 \times 10^{-6}
$$

$$
h_f = \frac{10.67 \times 50 \times 1.13 \times 10^{-4}}{5830 \times 1.91 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{0.0603}{0.01113} \approx 5.42 \; \text{m of water}
$$

**This friction loss must be added to the nozzle pressure (typically 7 bar) to determine the required pump pressure.**

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — System Selection by Hazard

| Hazard Type | Recommended System | Application Mode | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Server Room** | CO₂ | Total Flooding | No residue; no electronics damage |
| **Generator Enclosure** | CO₂ | Total Flooding | Smothers without residue |
| **Petrol Pump** | DCP + Foam | Local Application | Fast knock-down + blanket |
| **LPG Bottling** | DCP | Local Application | Non-conductive; rapid discharge |
| **Aircraft Hangar** | High-Exp Foam | Local Application | Large area, polar solvent risk |
| **Transformer Yard** | Water Spray + DCP | Local Application | Cooling + chemical suppression |
| **Paint Store** | CO₂ or Foam | Total Flooding | Enclosed; vapour explosion risk |
| **Compressor Room** | DCP or CO₂ | Local Application | Open hazard; oil mist fire |

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Generic Fixed Firefighting System Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    A1[Fire Initiation Source] --> B1[Detection Sensors Heat Smoke Flame]
    A2[Manual Call Point] --> B1
    B1 --> C1[Fire Alarm Control Panel FACP]
    C1 --> D1[Audible Visual Alarm Pre-discharge]
    C1 --> D2[Time Delay 30 seconds]
    C1 --> D3[Confirmation Logic]
    D3 --> E1[Release Actuator Solenoid Pneumatic]
    E1 --> F1[Agent Storage Bank CO2 DCP Foam]
    F1 --> G1[Distribution Manifold]
    G1 --> H1[Discharge Piping Network]
    H1 --> I1[Nozzles Local Application]
    H1 --> I2[Room Volume Total Flooding]
    C1 --> J1[Emergency Shutdown Hold Abort]
    C1 --> K1[Remote Signaling BMS Fire Station]
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- Detection occurs via **automatic sensors** (B1) or **manual call points** (A2).
- The **FACP** processes signals through **confirmation logic** (D3) — typically requiring two sensor confirmations to avoid false discharge.
- A **30-second pre-discharge alarm** (D2) allows personnel evacuation.
- The **release actuator** (E1) triggers the **agent storage bank** (F1) to discharge through the **piping network** (H1).
- Discharge is delivered as either **local application via nozzles** (I1) or **total flooding into the room** (I2).
- A **hold/abort** switch (J1) allows authorized personnel to delay discharge if false alarm is verified.

---

### 4.2 CO₂ Total Flooding System — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    A1[CO2 Cylinder Bank 19 plus 1] --> B1[Manifold Header]
    B1 --> C1[Master Valve]
    C1 --> D1[Pneumatic Actuator]
    D1 --> E1[Check Valve]
    E1 --> F1[Distribution Pipe]
    F1 --> G1[Room Nozzle 1]
    F1 --> G2[Room Nozzle 2]
    F1 --> G3[Room Nozzle N]
    H1[Detection Panel] --> I1[Solenoid Valve]
    I1 --> D1
    H1 --> J1[Pre-Discharge Alarm]
    H1 --> K1[Time Delay Unit]
    H1 --> L1[Pressure Switch Monitor]
    L1 --> M1[Confirmation to FACP]
```

**Operating Sequence:**
1. Detection Panel (H1) confirms fire.
2. Pre-Discharge Alarm (J1) sounds; Time Delay (K1) starts (30 s).
3. After delay, Solenoid Valve (I1) opens.
4. Pneumatic Actuator (D1) opens the Master Valve (C1).
5. CO₂ discharges from cylinders through the Manifold Header (B1).
6. The Pressure Switch (L1) confirms flow and signals the FACP.
7. CO₂ floods the protected volume through the Room Nozzles (G1, G2, G3).

---

### 4.3 DCP Local Application System — Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    A1[DCP Cylinder 50 kg] --> B1[Powder Manifold]
    C1[N2 Cylinder 1.5 MPa] --> D1[Pressure Regulator]
    D1 --> B1
    B1 --> E1[Solenoid Release Valve]
    E1 --> F1[Powder Piping]
    F1 --> G1[Powder Nozzle 1]
    F1 --> G2[Powder Nozzle 2]
    H1[Fire Detection] --> I1[Control Panel]
    I1 --> J1[Abort Hold Switch]
    I1 --> E1
    I1 --> K1[Visual Audible Alarm]
    I1 --> L1[Remote Annunciation]
```

**Operating Sequence:**
1. Detection (H1) triggers Control Panel (I1).
2. Alarm (K1) sounds; operator has 30 s to abort if false.
3. Solenoid Release Valve (E1) opens.
4. N₂ from C1 enters DCP Cylinder A1 via Pressure Regulator D1.
5. Powder fluidizes and is pushed through Powder Manifold B1 → Powder Piping F1 → Nozzles G1/G2.
6. The hazard area is rapidly blanketed with DCP cloud.

---

### 4.4 Foam System — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    A1[Water Supply Pump Hydrant] --> B1[Proportioner Bal Bladder Tank]
    A2[Foam Concentrate Tank] --> B1
    B1 --> C1[Foam Solution Manifold]
    C1 --> D1[Foam Maker Pourer]
    C1 --> D2[Aspirating Nozzle]
    C1 --> D3[High-Expansion Generator]
    E1[Detection Panel] --> F1[Control Logic]
    F1 --> G1[Deluge Valve]
    A1 --> G1
    G1 --> B1
    F1 --> H1[Alarm Annunciation]
    F1 --> I1[Manual Release]
```

**Operating Sequence:**
1. Detection (E1) sends signal to Control Logic (F1).
2. Deluge Valve (G1) opens, allowing water to flow.
3. Proportioner (B1) draws foam concentrate from A2 at the specified ratio (3 % or 6 %).
4. The foam solution flows to foam-generating devices (D1, D2, D3).
5. As air is aspirated, foam is produced and discharged onto the hazard.

---

### 4.5 Fire Alarm System — Classification Block Diagram (Per NBC 2016)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A1[Fire Alarm System Classification] --> B1[By Initiation]
    A1 --> C1[By Detection Technology]
    A1 --> D1[By Signalling Method]
    A1 --> E1[By Coverage]
    B1 --> B2[Manual System]
    B1 --> B3[Automatic System]
    B2 --> B21[Manual Call Points MCP]
    B2 --> B22[Pull Stations]
    B2 --> B23[Break Glass Units]
    B3 --> B31[Heat Detectors]
    B3 --> B32[Smoke Detectors]
    B3 --> B33[Flame Detectors]
    B3 --> B34[Gas Detectors]
    C1 --> C2[Conventional Zoned]
    C1 --> C3[Addressable]
    C1 --> C4[Analogue Addressable]
    C1 --> C5[Intelligent]
    D1 --> D2[Wired]
    D1 --> D3[Wireless]
    D1 --> D4[Hybrid]
    E1 --> E2[L1 Life Safety Full]
    E1 --> E3[L2 Property Protection]
    E1 --> E4[L3 Pre-action Partial]
    E1 --> E5[L4 Local Alarm]
    E1 --> E6[L5 Standalone]
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- **By Initiation**: Manual (MCP, pull stations) vs. Automatic (sensors).
- **By Detection Technology**: Heat, smoke (ionization/photoelectric), flame (IR/UV), gas (CO, hydrocarbon).
- **By Signalling Method**: Conventional (zoned), Addressable (each device has unique ID), Intelligent (analogue values reported).
- **By Coverage (L-categories per BS 5839 & NBC)**:
  - **L1**: Full protection with automatic detection throughout.
  - **L2**: Automatic detection in high-risk areas only.
  - **L3**: Pre-action; detection only in escape routes.
  - **L4**: Manual alarm only.
  - **L5**: Standalone (single detector + sounder).

---

### 4.6 Manually Operated System — Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    A1[Manual Initiator] --> A2[Pull Station MCP]
    A1 --> A3[Manual Lever]
    A1 --> A4[Break Glass Unit]
    A2 --> B1[FACP]
    A3 --> B1
    A4 --> B1
    B1 --> C1[Sounder Hooter]
    B1 --> C2[Visual Strobe]
    B1 --> C3[Release of Agent]
    B1 --> C4[Manual Call to Fire Brigade]
    B1 --> D1[Hydrant System]
    B1 --> D2[Fire Extinguisher Discharge]
    B1 --> D3[Water Monitor Activation]
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The system is activated **only by human intervention** — no automatic sensors.
- Common initiators: **Pull stations**, **manual levers**, **break-glass units**.
- The FACP triggers **audible/visual alarms** and may release pre-determined agents.
- Manual systems are **paired with hydrant networks and extinguishers** for first-aid response.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
**Differentiate clearly between Total Flooding and Local Application fire protection systems. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Total Flooding (1.5 marks):** A system in which the extinguishing agent is discharged into an **enclosed three-dimensional volume** to establish a uniform concentration that extinguishes fire anywhere within the enclosure. *Example:* CO₂ flooding of a sealed server room at 50 % concentration.

- **Local Application (1.5 marks):** A system in which the extinguishing agent is discharged **directly onto a specific hazard surface or piece of equipment** without requiring total enclosure. *Example:* DCP nozzles aimed at a gasoline pump island; foam pourer over an open hydrocarbon bund.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**List any three advantages and two disadvantages of using CO₂ as a firefighting medium in fixed systems.**

**Model Answer (5 points / 3 marks):**

**Advantages (any 3 × 0.5 = 1.5 marks):**
- No residue, no cleanup, no damage to equipment (ideal for electronics).
- Electrically non-conductive — safe for live electrical hazards.
- High penetration into obstructed spaces (gaseous state).
- Unlimited storage stability (sealed cylinders).

**Disadvantages (any 2 × 0.75 = 1.5 marks):**
- **Asphyxiation risk** — displaces oxygen; personnel must be evacuated.
- **Ineffective for deep-seated Class A fires** unless high concentration (65 %) held for extended time.
- **Requires enclosed, gas-tight room** for total flooding.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — 2 Sub-Parts**

**[KTU University Exam — July 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Describe in detail the **basic components of a CO₂ fixed firefighting system** for total flooding applications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **6 m × 5 m × 3.5 m** electrical switchgear room with a leakage area of **200 cm²** is to be protected by a CO₂ total flooding system at **50 %** design concentration. Calculate the **CO₂ quantity** required and the **number of 45 kg cylinders** needed. Assume ambient temperature 21 °C. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to (a) — 7 Marks:**

A CO₂ fixed total flooding system consists of the following **essential components** (each 1 mark ≈):

1. **CO₂ Storage Cylinders (1 mark):** High-pressure seamless steel cylinders (68 L water capacity) filled to 0.67 fill ratio with food-grade liquid CO₂ at 5.86 MPa (at 21 °C). Cylinders are manifolded in a bank with a 10 % standby reserve.

2. **Master Valve and Actuator (1 mark):** A mechanically, pneumatically, or electrically actuated release valve that opens the cylinder(s) upon command. A pneumatic head uses pilot gas from a small N₂ cylinder.

3. **Distribution Piping Network (1 mark):** Schedule 40 black steel pipe, sized using hydraulic flow calculations to ensure each nozzle receives the required flow rate with maximum 10 % imbalance.

4. **Discharge Nozzles (1 mark):** Full-cone or hollow-cone brass nozzles, specifically designed for CO₂, sized to deliver the agent at sonic velocity. Number and placement designed for uniform 3D concentration.

5. **Detection and Control System (1 mark):** Smoke/heat detectors in a coincidence (cross-zoned) configuration feeding the Fire Alarm Control Panel (FACP), which initiates the release sequence with a **30-second time delay**.

6. **Warning Alarms (1 mark):** Pre-discharge audible horn and flashing strobe, plus a "DO NOT ENTER — CO₂ DISCHARGED" sign at the entrance. An abort switch allows manual delay.

7. **Auxiliary Devices (1 mark):** Pressure gauges, safety relief valves, pressure switches (to confirm discharge), manual release lever, and remote signaling to BMS/Fire Station.

---

**Model Solution to (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Given:**
- Room dimensions: $L = 6$ m, $B = 5$ m, $H = 3.5$ m
- Leakage area: $A_L = 200$ cm²
- Design concentration: 50 % (surface fire)
- Cylinder capacity: 45 kg
- Temperature: 21 °C

**Step 1 — Gross volume (1 mark):**
$$
V = 6 \times 5 \times 3.5 = 105 \; \text{m}^3
$$

**Step 2 — Specific CO₂ quantity at 50 % (1 mark):**
From IS 6382, Table 1: $C = 2.65 \; \text{kg/m}^3$ at 21 °C.

**Step 3 — Leakage factor (1 mark):**
$$
k = 1 + \frac{A_L}{100} = 1 + 2.0 = 3.0
$$

**Step 4 — CO₂ required (2 marks):**
$$
Q = V \times k \times C = 105 \times 3.0 \times 2.65 = 834.75 \; \text{kg}
$$

**Step 5 — Number of cylinders (1 mark):**
$$
N = \frac{834.75}{45} = 18.55 \Rightarrow 19 \text{ cylinders (active)}
$$

**Step 6 — Final count with 1 standby (1 mark):**
$$
N_{total} = 19 + 1 = 20 \; \text{cylinders}
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — 2 Sub-Parts (Alternative Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam — July 2024 Alternative Model]**

**(a)** Explain the **classification of Fire Alarm Systems as per NBC 2016**. Describe **L1, L2, L3, L4, and L5** categories in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **advantages and disadvantages of Dry Chemical Powder (DCP)** as an extinguishing medium in fixed firefighting systems. Compare briefly with **CO₂** for Class B fires. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to (a) — 7 Marks:**

The **National Building Code of India (NBC 2016), Part 4 — Fire and Life Safety** classifies fire alarm systems based on the **degree of automation and the extent of coverage**. The five categories are derived from BS 5839 and adopted in Indian practice. (1 mark for citation)

**L1 — Maximum Life Safety / Full Automatic Protection (1.5 marks):**
- Automatic fire detection (smoke and heat) installed in **all rooms, voids, and circulation areas**.
- Manual call points at all exits and stairwells.
- Sounder/visual alarm devices throughout the building.
- Required for hospitals, high-rise buildings, and assembly occupancies.

**L2 — Property Protection (1.5 marks):**
- Automatic detection in **high-risk areas only** (e.g., plant rooms, kitchens, electrical rooms).
- Manual call points at exits.
- Suitable for offices, hotels, and commercial buildings.

**L3 — Pre-Action / Escape Route Detection (1 mark):**
- Automatic detection only in **escape corridors and staircases**.
- Manual call points elsewhere.
- Used where early warning of escape route compromise is critical.

**L4 — Local Alarm / Manual Only (1 mark):**
- **No automatic detection**; relies entirely on manual call points.
- Sounders activate only on manual operation.
- Common in small offices, warehouses, and ancillary buildings.

**L5 — Standalone / Self-Contained (1 mark):**
- Single detector (typically smoke) connected to a self-contained sounder.
- Battery-powered, no central panel.
- Used in small residential dwellings and individual rooms.

---

**Model Solution to (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Advantages of DCP (4 marks, each 0.5 mark):**
1. **Extremely fast knock-down** of flame (within 1–2 seconds of contact).
2. **Effective on Class B, C, and (with ABC) Class A fires** — broad applicability.
3. **Non-conductive** (dry powder) — safe for live electrical fires.
4. **Effective in open / unenclosed hazards** (local application mode).
5. **Long storage life** with no degradation; insensitive to temperature.
6. **Non-toxic** at application concentrations (mild respiratory irritation only).
7. **Suitable for 3D / obstructed fires** — powder cloud penetrates spaces.

**Disadvantages of DCP (1.5 marks):**
1. **Leaves heavy corrosive residue** requiring extensive cleanup and equipment replacement.
2. **No cooling effect** — risk of re-ignition from hot surfaces.
3. **Limited visibility** during discharge (occupants disoriented).
4. **Hygroscopic** — moisture causes caking; system must be kept dry.
5. **Visibility impairment** during discharge hinders rescue operations.

**Comparison with CO₂ for Class B Fires (1.5 marks):**
- **Knock-down speed:** DCP is faster (milliseconds vs. seconds).
- **Residue:** DCP leaves residue; CO₂ leaves none.
- **Re-ignition risk:** DCP lower (due to inhibition); CO₂ higher (no cooling).
- **Suitability for enclosed rooms:** CO₂ preferred (no cleanup); DCP would damage electronics.
- **Suitability for outdoor hazards:** DCP preferred (CO₂ disperses rapidly in open air).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU 2024 Exams**
>
> 1. **Confusing Total Flooding with Local Application:** Students frequently interchange these in definitions. **TF = enclosed volume; LA = specific hazard surface.** Mixing up costs 1–2 marks.
>
> 2. **Omitting the leakage factor ($k$):** In CO₂ TF calculations, the leakage factor is **mandatory** per IS 6382. Skipping it yields an under-designed system and loses **2 marks**.
>
> 3. **Using wrong specific quantity ($C$):** The value of $C$ depends on both the **design concentration** AND the **ambient temperature**. Using 2.0 kg/m³ instead of 2.65 kg/m³ (at 50 %, 21 °C) is a frequent error.
>
> 4. **Ignoring standby cylinder requirement:** A working design must include 10–25 % standby reserve. Examiners explicitly check for this.
>
> 5. **Wrong units in hydraulic calculations:** Using L/s vs. m³/s inconsistently in Hazen-Williams causes order-of-magnitude errors. Always check units **before** substituting.
>
> 6. **Mixing up DCP powder types:** ABC (MAP) for Class A/B/C; BC (sodium bicarbonate) for B/C only; D (sodium chloride) for metals. Confusing them is a structural error.
>
> 7. **Forgetting the 30-second pre-discharge delay** in CO₂ systems — this is a **safety requirement**, not optional.
>
> 8. **In NBC classification, writing "L1 to L5" without explaining the L-category** loses the descriptive marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Module 4**

- [x] **CO₂** is stored as **liquid at 5.86 MPa** (HP) or **−18 °C at 2.07 MPa** (LP).
- [x] **CO₂ design concentration**: **50 %** surface, **65 %** deep-seated.
- [x] **CO₂ discharge time**: **≥ 60 s** (surface), **≥ 7 min** (deep-seated).
- [x] **CO₂ formula**: $Q = V \cdot k \cdot C$ (Total Flooding).
- [x] **CO₂ leakage factor**: $k = 1 + A_L/100$.
- [x] **DCP design density**: **4–6 kg/m²** of floor area (IS 13849).
- [x] **DCP is hygroscopic** — keep piping dry with N₂ pressurization.
- [x] **DCP mechanism**: chain-breaking via PO/PO₂ free radicals.
- [x] **Foam application rate (hydrocarbon)**: **4 L/min·m²** (IS 4989).
- [x] **Foam expansion ratios**: 6:1 to 12:1 (low), 50:1 to 200:1 (high).
- [x] **AR-AFFF** is for **polar solvents** (alcohols, ketones).
- [x] **AFFF** forms a **thin aqueous film** on hydrocarbon surfaces.
- [x] **Total Flooding (TF)** requires **enclosed, gas-tight** room.
- [x] **Local Application (LA)** targets **specific hazard surface**.
- [x] **Pre-discharge alarm delay**: **30 seconds minimum** (life safety).
- [x] **CO₂ extinguishment threshold O₂**: **< 15 %** (atmospheric = 21 %).
- [x] **NBC 2016 Part 4** governs fire alarm classification in India.
- [x] **L1 to L5 categories** describe detection coverage levels.
- [x] **Manual systems** rely on **Pull Stations, MCPs, Break-Glass Units**.
- [x] **Conventional systems** = zoned; **Addressable systems** = each device has unique ID.
- [x] **Cross-zoning (coincidence detection)** prevents false discharge.
- [x] **ISI Standards**: **IS 6382** (CO₂), **IS 13849** (DCP), **IS 4989** (Foam).
- [x] **Cylinder fill ratio for CO₂** = 0.67 (not 1.0).
- [x] **High-expansion foam** is used for **enclosed spaces** (basements, hangars).
- [x] **Class D fires** (combustible metals) require **special DCP** (NaCl or Cu powder).
- [x] **Smoke detectors** have two main types: **ionization** (fast-flaming) and **photoelectric** (smouldering).
- [x] **Heat detectors**: **fixed temperature** (e.g., 57 °C) and **rate-of-rise** (8–10 °C/min).
- [x] **Abort switch** must be **sealed and supervised** to prevent unauthorized use.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Automatic Alarm System. Component and Features of Local System, Auxiliary System, Remote Station System, Central Station System and Proprietary System. False Alarm, Designing Fire Alarm Audibility and Visibility
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT836 - DESIGN OF FIRE PROTECTION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Automatic Alarm System. Component and Features of Local System, Auxiliary System, Remote Station System, Central Station System and Proprietary System. False Alarm, Designing Fire Alarm Audibility and Visibility

---

# 🔔 Automatic Fire Alarm (AFA) Systems — Module 4 Master Notes

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Automatic Fire Alarm (AFA) System** is a network of interconnected detection, notification, control, and power components designed to automatically identify the unwanted presence of fire through the physical phenomena of smoke, heat, flame, or combustion by-products, and to subsequently alert the building occupants, the fire service, and/or a supervising station so that life-safety and property-protection actions may be initiated in accordance with **NFPA 72 (National Fire Alarm and Signaling Code)**, **IS 2189**, and **TAC (TAC-India) Guidelines**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The Automatic Fire Alarm System is the *nerve centre* of a modern fire-protection strategy. As per the 2024 Scheme, students are expected to identify the *components*, classify the *five system architectures*, analyse *false alarm* phenomena, and **design for both audibility AND visibility** per codified thresholds.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Five Senses of a Building"

Imagine a building as a living organism:

| Body Part | Building Equivalent | Function |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 👁️ **Eyes** | Smoke, Flame, Gas Detectors | *See* the fire signature |
| 🖐️ **Skin** | Heat / Thermal Detectors | *Feel* abnormal temperature |
| 👂 **Ears (Mouth)** | Horns, Speakers, Bells | *Announce* the danger |
| 👀 **Visual** | Strobes, Beacons | *Show* the danger |
| 🧠 **Brain** | Fire Alarm Control Panel (FACP) | *Decide* & *coordinate* response |

> [!NOTE]
> A building without an alarm system is like a sleeping giant — *unaware, vulnerable, and unable to react*. The AFA System acts as the giant's alarm clock that wakes it instantly upon sensing danger.

### 1.3 Governing Codes & Standards

The numbers **15 dBA**, **65 dBA**, **75 dBA**, and **110 candela** are *not arbitrary* — they are codified **NFPA 72 thresholds** that any KTU graduate must memorize for viva and design questions.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook for Audibility:** "**65-70-75**" → Sleeping area, general occupancy, mechanical rooms.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Major Components of an Automatic Alarm System

The system comprises four functional blocks:

#### A. Initiating Devices (Input Side)
- **Smoke Detectors** — Ionization, Photoelectric, Aspirating (VESDA), Beam
- **Heat Detectors** — Fixed Temperature (57 °C / 68 °C), Rate-of-Rise (ROR)
- **Flame Detectors** — UV, IR, Dual-spectrum
- **Gas Detectors** — CO, CO₂, LPG detectors
- **Manual Pull Stations** — Single-action, Double-action
- **Water-flow Switches** & **Tamper Switches** (sprinkler integration)

#### B. Fire Alarm Control Panel (FACP) — The Brain
- Microprocessor-based, addressable or conventional
- Receives signals → processes logic → activates notification
- Houses **Primary Power (230 V AC)** + **Secondary Power (Sealed Lead-Acid battery, 24-hr standby + 5-min alarm)**

#### C. Notification Appliances (Output Side)
- **Audible:** Horns, Bells, Speakers (Voice Evacuation)
- **Visible:** Xenon Strobes, LED Beacons
- **Combination:** Horn-Strobe, Speaker-Strobe

#### D. Pathways (Wiring)
- **Initiating Device Circuit (IDC)** — Class A / B
- **Notification Appliance Circuit (NAC)** — Class A / B / X
- **Signaling Line Circuit (SLC)** — for addressable loops

---

### 2.2 The Five System Architectures (NFPA 72 Classification)

| # | System Type | Alarm Sounded At | Signal Transmitted To | Personnel at Receiver |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Local System** | Protected premises only | None (internal only) | Occupants |
| 2 | **Auxiliary System** | Protected premises + public fire alarm box | Municipal Fire Dept. via Master Box | Public Fire Service |
| 3 | **Remote Station System** | Protected premises | Remote location (off-site) | Third-party monitoring service |
| 4 | **Central Station System** | Protected premises + remote | UL-listed Central Station | Trained Operators (24/7) |
| 5 | **Proprietary System** | Protected premises | Owner's remote station | Owner's trained personnel |

#### Detailed Feature Analysis

**(i) Local Alarm System**
- Simplest, lowest cost
- Sounders/horns in the same building
- No off-site communication
- Suitable for small offices, shops

**(ii) Auxiliary Alarm System**
- Property owner-owned alarm system connected to a **Master Fire Alarm Box** at the street
- Triggers the public fire service via municipal circuits
- Older technology, declining use

**(iii) Remote Station System**
- Alarm transmitted over **telephone lines, cellular, IP, or radio** to a remote monitoring station
- The remote station is *not* the same as the property owner
- Operators notify the fire department

**(iv) Central Station System**
- Monitored by a **UL-listed or FM-approved Central Station**
- Central station operator verifies, dispatches, and provides **runner service**
- Strict requirements on retransmission, logging, record-keeping

**(v) Proprietary System**
- Used by **large property owners** (universities, airports, industrial complexes)
- The remote station is *owned and operated by the same entity*
- Requires **trained, in-house personnel** available 24/7

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Trap:** A *Proprietary* system is **NOT** the same as a *Central Station* system. The receiver is the **owner's** staff, not a third party.

---

### 2.3 False Alarm Engineering

A **False/Unwanted Alarm** is any alarm signal that is not the result of an actual fire emergency. NFPA classifies them as:

| Category | Cause | Mitigation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Unwanted Alarm** | Cooking aerosols, steam, dust | Use photoelectric over ionization near kitchens |
| **Malicious Alarm** | Manual pull prank | Pull station covers, video surveillance |
| **Equipment Alarm** | Detector contamination, electrical fault | Regular maintenance, sensitivity testing |
| **Environmental Alarm** | Humidity, insects, RF interference | Proper detector selection, sealing |

> [!WARNING]
> **NFPA Fact:** Approximately **93–95 %** of all fire alarm activations in the US are false alarms — a major *cost and response-burden* problem that drives modern detector design (e.g., multi-criteria detectors).

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Audibility & Visibility

| Parameter | Symbol | Formula / Threshold | Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Sound Pressure Level at distance | $L_p$ | $L_p = L_w - 20 \log_{10}(r) - 11$ | dB |
| Sound Pressure Level (point source) | $L_p$ | $L_p = L_w - 20 \log_{10}(r) - 8$ (hemispherical) | dB |
| Required level over ambient | $\Delta L$ | $\Delta L = L_{alarm} - L_{ambient} \ge \mathbf{15}$ | dBA |
| Sleeping area requirement | $L_{sleep}$ | $L_p \ge \mathbf{65}$ dBA at pillow | dBA |
| General occupied area | $L_{gen}$ | $L_p \ge \mathbf{70}$ dBA | dBA |
| Mechanical equipment room | $L_{mech}$ | $L_p \ge \mathbf{75}$ dBA | dBA |
| Candela intensity (sleeping room) | $I_{sleep}$ | $\mathbf{110}$ cd minimum | candela |
| Candela intensity (public space) | $I_{pub}$ | $\mathbf{15}$ – $\mathbf{177}$ cd | candela |
| Effective intensity (multi-candela) | $I_{eff}$ | $\sum I_i \cdot \omega_i / \sum \omega_i$ | candela |
| Wall-mounting height | $h_w$ | $1.5 \le h_w \le 2.4$ m | metres |
| Ceiling-mounting height | $h_c$ | Full-room coverage if $h_c \le 9.1$ m | metres |
| Polarity coherence | — | All strobes in field of view must flash *simultaneously* | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the constant "11" in the equation?** It comes from spherical spreading: $20 \log_{10}(4\pi) \approx 11.0$ dB. For a *hemispherical* source on a wall, the constant becomes **8** since $20 \log_{10}(2\pi) \approx 8.0$ dB.

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Hospital** → Voice evacuation + 110 cd strobes in every patient room
- **Hotel** → Sleeping-area audibility & strobe coverage are critical for ADA compliance
- **Data Centre** → Aspirating (VESDA) detection + gaseous suppression interface
- **Mall** → Speakers for voice instructions, 75 cd strobes in public areas
- **Industrial** → Flame detectors + manual pull + explosion-proof notification

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Sound Pressure Level at Distance

The fundamental relationship between acoustic power and pressure is derived from conservation of energy over a sphere.

#### Step 1: Acoustic Intensity Definition
Acoustic intensity $I$ is the power per unit area:

$$I = \frac{W}{4\pi r^{2}}$$

where:
- $W$ = acoustic power (W)
- $r$ = radial distance (m)

#### Step 2: Sound Pressure Level
The sound pressure level $L_p$ is referenced to $p_0 = 20\ \mu\text{Pa}$:

$$L_p = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p_{rms}}{p_0}\right) \quad \text{[dB]}$$

#### Step 3: Relating Intensity to Pressure
Since $I = p_{rms}^{2} / (\rho c)$, and Sound Power Level $L_w = 10 \log_{10}(W / W_0)$:

$$L_p = L_w - 20 \log_{10}(r) - 11$$

#### Step 4: For a Hemispherical Source (wall or ceiling mount)
When a sounder radiates into a half-sphere (e.g., mounted on a wall), the denominator becomes $2\pi r^{2}$:

$$L_p = L_w - 20 \log_{10}(r) - 8$$

#### Step 5: Design Verification
For the alarm to be **perceived**, the alarm level must exceed ambient by at least **15 dBA**:

$$L_{p,\text{alarm}} \ge L_{p,\text{ambient}} + 15 \quad \text{[dBA]}$$

---

### 3.2 Worked Example — Audibility Verification

> **Problem (KTU-Style):**
> A horn sounder is mounted on a wall of a hotel guest room. The manufacturer's datasheet specifies a Sound Power Level of $L_w = 100$ dB. The ambient noise level in the room is $L_{ambient} = 45$ dBA. Verify audibility compliance at the pillow, located at $r = 3.5$ m from the sounder. Sleeping-area criterion: **65 dBA at pillow**.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Calculate Sound Pressure Level at pillow** (wall = hemispherical source, use constant 8):

$$L_p = L_w - 20 \log_{10}(r) - 8$$

$$L_p = 100 - 20 \log_{10}(3.5) - 8$$

$$L_p = 100 - (20)(0.5441) - 8$$

$$L_p = 100 - 10.88 - 8 = \mathbf{81.12\ dBA}$$

**Step 2 — Verify against sleeping-area threshold:**

$$L_p = 81.12 \ge 65 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 3 — Verify 15-dB margin over ambient:**

$$\Delta L = 81.12 - 45 = 36.12 \ge 15 \quad \checkmark$$

> ✅ **Result:** The sounder is *acceptable* on both counts — significantly above the sleeping-area minimum and well above the 15-dB ambient margin.

---

### 3.3 Visibility Design — Candela Selection Algorithm

#### Step 1 — Identify Room Use Category
- Sleeping room → minimum **110 cd**
- Public space (corridor, lobby) → **15 / 30 / 75 / 110 / 177 cd** based on room size

#### Step 2 — Verify Wall-Mounted Strobe Spacing
NFPA Table 18.5.5.4.1 spacing for wall-mounted strobes (rectangular room):

$$\text{Spacing} \le 30\ \text{m along wall} \quad \text{and} \quad \text{Spacing} \le 15\ \text{m from end wall}$$

#### Step 3 — Verify Ceiling-Mounted Strobe
For ceiling heights $h_c \le 9.1$ m, use a single ceiling-mounted strobe with a coverage radius determined by candela rating:

| Candela | Coverage Radius |
| :--- | :--- |
| 15 cd | 6.1 m radius |
| 30 cd | 7.6 m radius |
| 75 cd | 10.7 m radius |
| 110 cd | 12.2 m radius |

> [!NOTE]
> **Higher candela = larger coverage circle** = fewer devices needed. A 110 cd ceiling strobe can protect a **12.2 m radius** circle — a major cost optimization.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — System Selection

| Selection Criterion | Local | Auxiliary | Remote Station | Central Station | Proprietary |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Off-site notification | ✗ | ✓ (public FD) | ✓ (private) | ✓ (private) | ✓ (owner) |
| UL-listed central station | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ | ✗ |
| 24/7 trained operators | ✗ | ✗ | varies | ✓ | ✓ |
| Capital cost | Lowest | Low | Medium | Medium-High | Highest |
| Code requirement | Light hazard | Historical | Common | Common | Large campus |
| Typical user | Small shop | Old buildings | Small commercial | Most commercial | Airports, IT parks |

---

### 3.5 False Alarm Rate (FAR) Calculation

$$\text{FAR} = \frac{\text{Number of False Alarms}}{\text{Total Number of Alarms}} \times 100\ \text{[\%]}$$

> [!TIP]
> An FAR $> 50\%$ is a *red flag* — indicates the need for detector-type re-evaluation or maintenance overhaul.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 System Architecture — Five Alarm Systems Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph fireA["Fire Event at Protected Premises"]
        detA["Initiating Device: Smoke/Heat/Manual Pull"]
        facpA["FACP - Fire Alarm Control Panel"]
    end

    subgraph sysTypes["Five System Architectures"]
        locA["Local System"]
        auxA["Auxiliary System"]
        remA["Remote Station System"]
        cenA["Central Station System"]
        propA["Proprietary System"]
    end

    subgraph receivers["Receivers / Notification Targets"]
        occA["Occupants Only"]
        pubA["Public Fire Dept via Master Box"]
        rmsA["Remote Monitoring Service"]
        ulA["UL Central Station Operators"]
        ownA["Owner's 24x7 Trained Staff"]
    end

    facpA --> locA --> occA
    facpA --> auxA --> pubA
    facpA --> remA --> rmsA
    facpA --> cenA --> ulA
    facpA --> propA --> ownA
    detA --> facpA
```

### 4.2 Component Block Diagram — Complete AFA System

```mermaid
    smk --> slc

```

### 4.3 Decision Flow — Choosing the Right System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA["Building requires AFA System"]
    q1A{"Need off-site notification?"}
    localA["Use Local System"]
    q2A{"Connection to public fire dept?"}

    q3A{"Owner wants 24/7 in-house monitoring?"}
    propA["Use Proprietary System"]
    q4A{"UL-listed central station required?"}

    remoteA["Use Remote Station System"]

    startA --> q1A
    q1A -- "No" --> localA
    q1A -- "Yes" --> q2A
    q2A -- "Yes" --> auxA
    q2A -- "No" --> q3A
    q3A -- "Yes" --> propA
    q3A -- "No" --> q4A
    q4A -- "Yes" --> centA
    q4A -- "No" --> remoteA
```

### 4.4 Audibility & Visibility Design Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    sA["Start: Identify Room Type"]
    ambA["Measure Ambient Noise L_amb"]
    selA["Select Sounder with L_w rating"]
    calA["Calculate L_p = L_w - 20log r - 8"]
    chkA["L_p >= L_amb + 15 dBA?"]
    chkB["L_p >= 65 dBA at pillow?"]
    chkC["Strobe I_cd selected per room use?"]
    passA["Design ACCEPTED"]
    failA["REJECT - Increase L_w or add sounders"]

    sA --> ambA --> selA --> calA --> chkA
    chkA -- "No" --> failA
    chkA -- "Yes" --> chkB
    chkB -- "No" --> failA
    chkB -- "Yes" --> chkC
    chkC -- "Yes" --> passA
    chkC -- "No" --> failA
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> *Differentiate between a Local Alarm System and a Central Station Alarm System.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Local Alarm System | Central Station Alarm System |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Notification destination | Within the protected premises only | Off-site UL-listed Central Station |
| Personnel | Occupants of the building | Trained operators at central station |
| Off-site communication | None | Yes (telephone, GSM, IP) |
| Regulatory compliance | Basic NFPA 72 | NFPA 72 + UL 827 + runner service rules |
| Typical use | Small shops, residences | Hospitals, malls, high-rises |

(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for differences, 1 mark for examples) — **Total: 3 marks**

---

#### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> *List the four major components of an Automatic Fire Alarm System.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Initiating Devices** — Smoke, heat, flame, gas detectors, manual pull stations (1 mark)
2. **Fire Alarm Control Panel (FACP)** — The brain, processes signals (1 mark)
3. **Notification Appliances** — Horns, bells, strobes, speakers (0.5 marks)
4. **Power Supply** — Primary 230 V AC + Secondary 24 V DC battery backup (0.5 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (Choice 1) [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the **five types of fire alarm systems** as classified by NFPA 72, highlighting the key feature that distinguishes each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A horn sounder with a sound power level of **102 dB** is wall-mounted in a hotel corridor. The ambient noise level is **55 dBA**. Calculate the sound pressure level at a point **6 m** away from the sounder and verify whether it satisfies the **70 dBA general-occupancy criterion** and the **15 dBA margin** requirement. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Seven-mark breakdown:**

1. **Local System** (1.4 m) — Alarms sound only inside the protected premises; no off-site communication. Used in small buildings.
2. **Auxiliary System** (1.4 m) — Connected to a public Master Fire Alarm Box; transmits signal to municipal fire department.
3. **Remote Station System** (1.4 m) — Signals are sent to a remote monitoring station that notifies the fire department.
4. **Central Station System** (1.4 m) — UL-listed central station with trained operators, retransmission rules, runner service, and complete logging.
5. **Proprietary System** (1.4 m) — Owner-operated remote station with own trained personnel (e.g., university campus).

**(b) Solution (7 marks):**

Given: $L_w = 102$ dB, $r = 6$ m, wall-mounted (hemispherical, constant = 8), $L_{amb} = 55$ dBA

**Step 1 — Calculate SPL at 6 m** (1 mark)

$$L_p = L_w - 20 \log_{10}(r) - 8$$

$$L_p = 102 - 20 \log_{10}(6) - 8$$

$$L_p = 102 - (20)(0.7782) - 8$$

$$L_p = 102 - 15.56 - 8 = \mathbf{78.44\ dBA}$$

**Step 2 — Check 70 dBA criterion** (1.5 marks)

$$78.44 \ge 70 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 3 — Check 15 dBA margin** (1.5 marks)

$$\Delta L = 78.44 - 55 = 23.44 \ge 15 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion** (1 mark)

> ✅ Both criteria are satisfied with comfortable margins. The sounder design is acceptable.

**Step 5 — Reliability/Code remark** (1 mark)
- Code requires alarm level also ≥ 15 dB above maximum sound level having duration > 60 s.
- Battery backup must support 15 min of alarm signalling.

---

#### **Question B (Choice 2) [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Discuss the **causes and mitigation measures for false fire alarms** in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design the **visibility (strobe) layout** for a 40 m × 8 m corridor in a public building, given ceiling height = 3 m. Specify the candela rating and minimum number of strobes per NFPA 72. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Seven-mark breakdown:**

1. **Definition of false alarm** (1 m) — Any alarm not resulting from actual fire; per NFPA, *unwanted alarm*.

2. **Types & causes** (3 m):
   - *Unwanted* — Cooking vapours, steam (ionization detector near kitchen)
   - *Malicious* — Manual pull without fire
   - *Equipment failure* — Detector contamination, dirty chamber, electrical fault
   - *Environmental* — Insects, dust, humidity, RF interference

3. **Mitigation** (2 m):
   - Use *photoelectric* near kitchens (less sensitive to aerosols)
   - Use *multi-criteria* detectors combining smoke + heat + CO
   - Regular cleaning & sensitivity testing (per NFPA 72 Chapter 14)
   - Pull station covers, CCTV monitoring for malicious cases
   - Addressable systems with *verification delay* (60–120 s pre-alarm)

4. **NFPA target** (1 m) — Modern designs aim for FAR $< 1$ per 1000 detectors/year.

**(b) Solution — Strobe layout for 40 m × 8 m corridor (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Room classification** (1 m)
- Corridor in a *public building* = 15 to 30 cd typically sufficient; use **15 cd wall-mounted** (since $h_c = 3$ m which is within standard range).

**Step 2 — Wall-mount spacing** (2 m)
- Per NFPA 72, wall-mounted strobes in corridors: max spacing 30 m apart, with max 15 m from end wall.
- Effective intensity must be visible from anywhere in the corridor.

**Step 3 — Quantity calculation** (2 m)
- Place first strobe at 15 m from one end, second at 15 m from the other end.
- Total: **2 strobes of 15 cd** is the minimum to cover a 40 m corridor with the 15-m end-zone rule.
- However, to avoid end-of-corridor gap, add a third strobe at 5 m from the middle.
- **Final design: 3 × 15 cd wall-mounted strobes** spaced 13.3 m apart along the 40 m length.

**Step 4 — Mounting height** (1 m)
- Wall mounting: $1.5 \le h_w \le 2.4$ m (centre of lens).

**Step 5 — Synchronization rule** (1 m)
- Per NFPA 72, all strobes *within the field of view* must flash simultaneously (polarity synchronization).

---

### 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks (most common pitfalls):**
> 1. ❌ **Confusing hemispherical (8) with spherical (11) constant** in $L_p$ equation — *deduct 1 mark* if wrong constant used.
> 2. ❌ **Citing only "Local" and "Central Station"** — Examiner expects **all five** system types to be discussed.
> 3. ❌ **Skipping the 15 dBA margin check** — Even if absolute SPL is fine, the *margin* must be verified.
> 4. ❌ **Strobes not synchronized** — A serious *code violation* per NFPA 72 — *deduct 1 mark*.
> 5. ❌ **False alarm types confused with false alarm *causes*** — Examiner expects categorization.
> 6. ❌ **Battery backup requirements (24 hr + 5 min alarm)** often forgotten when listing components.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **AFA System = Initiating Devices + FACP + Notification + Power + Pathways**
- ✅ **Five System Types:** Local, Auxiliary, Remote Station, Central Station, Proprietary
- ✅ **Key Distinction:** Central Station = *third-party UL-listed*; Proprietary = *owner's own staff*
- ✅ **Audibility Constants:** **11** for spherical source, **8** for wall (hemispherical) source
- ✅ **Audibility Thresholds:** **65 dBA (sleeping)**, **70 dBA (general)**, **75 dBA (mechanical)**
- ✅ **Margin Requirement:** Alarm level ≥ Ambient + **15 dBA**
- ✅ **Strobe Intensity:** **110 cd** in sleeping rooms, **15–177 cd** in public areas
- ✅ **Wall-mount strobe height:** **1.5 – 2.4 m**, **30 m max spacing**
- ✅ **Synchronization:** All strobes in view must flash *simultaneously*
- ✅ **Power:** Primary 230 V AC + Secondary 24 V DC battery (24-hr standby + 5-min alarm)
- ✅ **False Alarm Categories:** Unwanted, Malicious, Equipment, Environmental
- ✅ **NFPA Reference:** All designs governed by **NFPA 72**; in India, **IS 2189** and **TAC** are followed
- ✅ **Code Memo:** "**65-70-75-110**" → Sleeping dB, General dB, Mechanical dB, Sleeping candela
- ✅ **FAR target:** < 1 per 1000 detectors/year in modern designs
- ✅ **Class A vs Class B wiring:** Class A = redundant loop (fault-tolerant); Class B = single path

> **Final Note for KTU 2024 Aspirants:** The question paper *almost always* asks at least one design calculation involving the $L_p$ equation. Memorize the formula and the **three thresholds (65/70/75)** — they appear in 80% of ESE papers.
